Boolean values are 32-bit (`0` for false, `1` for true). **Mixing booleans with numerics is illegal.** **Example:** ```arm IF (%a > %b) AND (%c == %d) THEN ... ``` Result: A single 32-bit boolean (0 or 1). --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q: Consider the following ARM assembly expression: `'7' * 10 + 5 - '0'`.** - **(i) Tokenize the expression and identify the type of each token.** - **(ii) Evaluate the expression step-by-step.** - **(iii) Write an ARM code snippet that computes this value and stores the result in R0.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Tokenization:** | Token | Type | Numeric Value | Width | |:-----:|:----:|:-------------:|:-----:| | `'7'` | Character (numeric context) | 55 (0x37) | 32-bit (after promotion) | | `*` | Multiplicative operator | — | — | | `10` | Decimal numeric | 10 | 32-bit | | `+` | Additive operator | — | — | | `5` | Decimal numeric | 5 | 32-bit | | `-` | Additive operator | — | — | | `'0'` | Character (numeric context) | 48 (0x30) | 32-bit (after promotion) | **(ii) Step-by-Step Evaluation:** Precedence: Multiplication (`*`) before addition (`+`) and subtraction (`-`). **Step 1: Multiplication first.** $$ \begin{aligned} '7' \times 10 &= 55 \times 10 = 550 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Addition.** $$ \begin{aligned} 550 + 5 &= 555 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Subtraction.** $$ \begin{aligned} 555 - '0' &= 555 - 48 = 507 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result:** **507** (0x1FB), 32-bit numeric. **(iii) ARM Code Snippet:** ```arm AREA MixTypesDemo, CODE, READONLY ENTRY MOV R0, #'7' ; R0 = 0x37 (55) MUL R0, R0, #10 ; R0 = 55 * 10 = 550 ADD R0, R0, #5 ; R0 = 550 + 5 = 555 SUB R0, R0, #'0' ; R0 = 555 - 48 = 507 STOP B STOP ; Halt END ``` **Real-world interpretation:** This expression effectively converts the character `'7'` into its **integer value 7**, then multiplies by 10 and adds 5 (giving 75), then... wait — note that the standard integer conversion would be `'7' - '0' = 7`, but the given expression uses `* 10 + 5 - '0'`, which yields 507. This is a stylized expression designed to **test the student's grasp of operator precedence** and **type promotion** — both critical KTU evaluation points. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Mixing Types Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting operator precedence:** Multiplicative operators (`*`, `/`, `:AND:`) are evaluated **before** additive operators (`+`, `-`, `:OR:`). Students who evaluate left-to-right without precedence lose **2–3 marks**. > > 2. **Confusing character width:** A character literal is **8 bits at source**, but the assembler **promotes to 32 bits** during expression evaluation. Students who answer "8-bit result" lose marks. > > 3. **Mixing booleans with numerics:** The expression `%TRUE + 1` is **illegal** in ARM assembler. Students who claim "this is valid and gives 2" are penalized **2 marks**. > > 4. **Ignoring ASCII conversion:** When a character is mixed with a numeric, students often forget to **convert the character to its ASCII value first**. Always state the conversion explicitly: `'A' = 65 = 0x41`. > > 5. **Not showing intermediate steps:** KTU examiners award **partial marks** for correct intermediate computations. A direct answer of "507" without showing `'7' * 10 = 550`, `550 + 5 = 555`, `555 - 48 = 507` will lose **up to 2 marks**. > > 6. **Wrong width in final answer:** Always state the **width** (8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit) and **type** (numeric, string, boolean) of the final result explicitly. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mixing Types in ARM Assembly** - ✅ **Definition:** Combining different data types (numeric, string, boolean) in a single expression or instruction. - ✅ **Three Main Types:** Numeric (32-bit), String (8-bit/char), Boolean (32-bit, `%` prefix). - ✅ **Width Dominance Rule:** Result width = **MAX** of all operand widths. - ✅ **Character Promotion:** Single characters are promoted to 32-bit numeric when mixed with numeric operators. - ✅ **Operator Precedence:** Multiplicative (`*`, `/`, `MOD`, `AND`) > Additive (`+`, `-`, `OR`, `EOR`) > Shifts (`LSL`, `LSR`, `ASR`, `ROR`). - ✅ **ASCII Magic Number 32:** Lowercase → Uppercase conversion offset is **32**. - ✅ **Hex-Decimal Equivalence:** Both are numeric; no type promotion needed between them. - ✅ **Boolean Isolation:** Booleans **cannot** mix with numerics or strings. - ✅ **Label as Numeric:** Memory labels are numeric (their address) and can be mixed freely with integers. - ✅ **Common Pattern:** `MOV R0, #'a'` and `SUB R0, R0, #32` converts lowercase to uppercase. - ✅ **Type Tracking Table:** Always document **type-before** and **type-after** for each token in a complex expression. - ✅ **KTU 2024 Bloom Level:** This topic maps to **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** in Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. --- > **End of Notes — Mixing Types | PEECT525 Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Manipulating Bits in Memory and IO Ports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Manipulating Bits in Memory and IO Ports ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bit Manipulation** in the context of ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers refers to the systematic use of bitwise logical instructions (`AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `BIC`, `ORN`, `LSL`, `LSR`, `ROR`) and memory-mapped I/O techniques to **set, clear, toggle, and read individual bits** within Special Function Registers (SFRs), General Purpose Memory (SRAM), and Peripheral I/O port registers without disturbing the state of neighboring bits. In ARM Cortex-M3/M4 processors, there are **two complementary mechanisms** for accessing/manipulating bits: 1. **Software Bit-Banding** – Read-Modify-Write (RMW) using bitwise operators and load/store instructions. 2. **Hardware Bit-Band Alias Region** – A dedicated memory alias (32 MB) that maps every individual bit of the 1 MB bit-band region to a full 32-bit word, enabling **single-instruction atomic bit access**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mailbox Wall" > [!NOTE] > Imagine a long corridor of **1024 mailboxes** (1 MB memory), each containing a tiny note pad with **8 bits** (1 byte). Normally, to change just one bit (say, bit 5 of mailbox 47), you must: > 1. Open the mailbox (read the byte), > 2. Wipe and rewrite the note (modify the byte), > 3. Close the mailbox (write the byte back). > > This is **Read-Modify-Write (RMW)** — slow, and risky if an interrupt fires between read and write (race condition). > > Now imagine there is a **second parallel corridor** (the **bit-band alias**) where **each bit of every byte in the original corridor has its own dedicated, full-sized mailbox** (a 32-bit word). To flip bit 5 of mailbox 47, you simply walk to the second corridor and drop a note into a *single, unique* mailbox. **One instruction. Atomic. No risk.** > > This second corridor is exactly what ARM calls the **bit-band alias region**. ### 1.3 Why Bit Manipulation Matters in ARM | Application Domain | Bit Used | Purpose | |---|---|---| | GPIO Output | ODR bit `n` | Drive pin HIGH/LOW | | GPIO Input | IDR bit `n` | Read pin state | | Peripheral Enable | RCC_AHBxENR bit | Clock gating | | Interrupt Control | ISER/ICER bit | Enable/Clear IRQ | | Status Flags | SR/TIFR bit | Check event | | Configuration | CR1/CR2 bits | Mode select | > [!TIP] > **Key takeaway for KTU**: In ARM, *every* peripheral register lives in the same 4 GB address space as SRAM. There is **no separate I/O bus** (unlike x86 `IN`/`OUT`). Therefore, "I/O port" in ARM is just a **memory address**, and bit manipulation is simply a **read or write to a specific memory-mapped bit**. ### 1.4 ARM Memory-Mapped I/O Address Map (Cortex-M4) | Region | Start Address | End Address | Size | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---| | Code / SRAM (bit-band) | `0x20000000` | `0x200FFFFF` | **1 MB** | Bit-band SRAM | | SRAM bit-band alias | `0x22000000` | `0x23FFFFFF` | **32 MB** | Atomic bit access | | Peripheral (bit-band) | `0x40000000` | `0x400FFFFF` | **1 MB** | Bit-band peripherals | | Peripheral bit-band alias | `0x42000000` | `0x43FFFFFF` | **32 MB** | Atomic bit access | | NVIC | `0xE000E000` | `0xE000EFFF` | 4 KB | System control | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Bit-Band Alias Address Formula (★ Most Important for KTU) For every byte address `A` in the bit-band region and every bit position `n` (where `0 ≤ n ≤ 7`), the corresponding **alias word address** is computed as: $$ \boxed{\text{Alias\_Addr} = \text{Alias\_Base} + (A - \text{Region\_Base}) \times 32 + n \times 4} $$ Where the constants depend on the region: $$ \text{For SRAM: } \text{Alias\_Base} = 0x22000000, \quad \text{Region\_Base} = 0x20000000 $$ $$ \text{For Peripherals: } \text{Alias\_Base} = 0x42000000, \quad \text{Region\_Base} = 0x40000000 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The factor **32** arises because each source byte (8 bits) is expanded to 8 word-sized aliases (8 × 4 = 32 bytes). The factor **4** arises because each alias is a 32-bit aligned word. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. ### 2.2 Read-Modify-Write (RMW) Logic Steps The traditional (non-bit-band) approach to modify a single bit follows these stages: 1. **LDR** — Load the entire 32-bit register value into a general-purpose register `R_t`. 2. **Bitwise Op** — Apply `BIC` (clear), `ORR` (set), or `EOR` (toggle) with a mask. 3. **STR** — Store the modified value back to the same memory address. 4. **Memory Barrier (DMB/DSB)** — On multi-core or shared-bus systems, ensure ordering. > [!WARNING] > The classic RMW sequence is **non-atomic**. If an ISR fires between the LDR and STR, the ISR's writes to the same register will be **overwritten** when the original code resumes. This is the **race condition** that bit-band aliasing is designed to eliminate. ### 2.3 Core Bitwise Truth Table (Applied to Bits) | Operation | Symbol (C) | Symbol (ARM asm) | Effect on bit `x` | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | AND | `&` | `AND` | `x & 1 = x` | Mask / test | | OR | `\|` | `ORR` | `x \| 1 = 1` | Set a bit | | XOR | `^` | `EOR` | `x ^ 1 = ~x` | Toggle a bit | | AND-NOT | `& ~` | `BIC` | `x & ~1 = 0` | Clear a bit | | OR-NOT | `\| ~` | `ORN` | `x \| ~1 = 1` | Force HIGH | | Shift Left | `<<` | `LSL` | Position bit | Build mask | | Shift Right | `>>` | `LSR` | Extract bit | Decode field | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (No vertical pipes in math columns) | Concept | Formula / Expression | Constant / Unit | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | Bit-band alias addr (SRAM) | $\text{Alias} = 0x22000000 + (A-0x20000000)\cdot 32 + n\cdot 4$ | bytes, bits | ★★★★★ | | Bit-band alias addr (Peri) | $\text{Alias} = 0x42000000 + (A-0x40000000)\cdot 32 + n\cdot 4$ | bytes, bits | ★★★★★ | | Alias region size | $2^{25} = 33\,554\,432$ bytes (32 MB) | bytes | ★★★★ | | Bit-band region size | $2^{20} = 1\,048\,576$ bytes (1 MB) | bytes | ★★★★ | | Expansion factor | $32 = 2^{5}$ | unitless | ★★★ | | Word size of alias | $4$ bytes (32 bits) | bytes | ★★★ | | Bit shift cost | $n$ positions = $2^{n}$ in mask value | bits | ★★★★★ | | Set bit | $x \mathrel{{+}{=}} (1 \ll n)$ | mask | ★★★★★ | | Clear bit | $x \mathrel{{&}{=}} \sim(1 \ll n)$ | mask | ★★★★★ | | Toggle bit | $x \mathrel{{}^{\wedge}{=}} (1 \ll n)$ | mask | ★★★★★ | | Test bit | $(x \gg n) \mathrel{{&}{=}} 1$ | bool | ★★★★★ | > [!TIP] > In C, the `` types `uint32_t` and `volatile uint32_t *` are mandatory for SFR bit access. The `volatile` qualifier tells the compiler **not** to optimize away the read/write — critical for memory-mapped I/O. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Bare-metal firmware** — Every GPIO, UART, SPI, ADC initialization in Cortex-M is built on these primitives. * **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)** — Bit-band is used to make peripheral flags accessible in O(1) without disabling interrupts. * **IoT & Sensor Drivers** — Setting/clearing the *Data Ready* bit of a sensor's status register is a single alias write. * **RTOS Porting** — PendSV and SysTick priority bits are manipulated via alias words for atomicity. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bit-Band Alias Address (Full Expansion) Let byte address $A = 0x20000010$ (in SRAM) and bit number $n = 3$ (i.e., bit 3 of that byte). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alias\_Addr} &= \text{Alias\_Base} + (A - \text{Region\_Base}) \times 32 + n \times 4 \\ &= 0x22000000 + (0x20000010 - 0x20000000) \times 32 + 3 \times 4 \\ &= 0x22000000 + (0x10) \times 32 + 12 \\ &= 0x22000000 + 512 + 12 \quad \text{(since } 0x10 = 16 \text{ and } 16 \times 32 = 512) \\ &= 0x22000000 + 0x200 + 0xC \\ &= 0x2200020C \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation**: A 32-bit write to `0x2200020C` will set/clear **only** bit 3 of the byte at `0x20000010`, leaving all other 31 bits in the byte and the rest of memory untouched. The value written (0 or 1) becomes the new bit value; bits 1–0 and 31–4 of the written word are **ignored**. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bit Set, Clear, Toggle in ARM Assembly Assume `R0` holds the value of GPIOA->ODR. We wish to set bit 5. **Set bit 5 (ORR approach):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mask} &= 1 \text{ LSL } 5 = 0x20 \\ \text{R0} &\leftarrow \text{R0 OR 0x20} \\ \text{STR R0, [R1]} &\quad \text{(R1 = \&GPIOA->ODR)} \end{aligned} $$ ARM assembly: ```armasm LDR R0, [R1] ; Read ODR ORR R0, R0, #0x20 ; Set bit 5 STR R0, [R1] ; Write back ``` **Clear bit 5 (BIC approach):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mask} &= 1 \text{ LSL } 5 = 0x20 \\ \text{~Mask} &= 0xFFFFFFDF \\ \text{R0} &\leftarrow \text{R0 AND NOT (0x20)} \end{aligned} $$ ARM assembly: ```armasm LDR R0, [R1] BIC R0, R0, #0x20 ; Clear bit 5 STR R0, [R1] ``` **Toggle bit 5 (EOR approach):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{R0} &\leftarrow \text{R0 XOR (1 LSL 5)} \end{aligned} $$ ARM assembly: ```armasm LDR R0, [R1] EOR R0, R0, #0x20 ; Toggle bit 5 STR R0, [R1] ``` ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bit-Band Alias Address in C (STM32 Example) STM32 GPIOA ODR register is at `0x40020014`. To set bit 5 using the bit-band alias: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alias} &= 0x42000000 + (0x40020014 - 0x40000000) \times 32 + 5 \times 4 \\ &= 0x42000000 + 0x20014 \times 32 + 20 \\ &= 0x42000000 + 0x400280 + 0x14 \\ &= 0x42400294 \end{aligned} $$ C code: ```c #define GPIOA_ODR_BIT5_ALIAS (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x42400294UL) GPIOA_ODR_BIT5_ALIAS = 1; // Atomic set of PA5 GPIOA_ODR_BIT5_ALIAS = 0; // Atomic clear of PA5 ``` ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Test-Bit Logic To check whether bit `n` of register `REG` is set or not: $$ \text{result} = \frac{\text{REG}}{2^{n}} \bmod 2 = \bigl(\text{REG} \gg n\bigr) \mathbin{\&} 1 $$ For `REG = 0xA5` (binary `1010 0101`) and `n = 2`: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{REG} \gg 2 &= 0x29 \ (\text{binary } 0010\,1001) \\ (0x29) \mathbin{\&} 0x01 &= 0x01 \quad \text{(bit is set)} \end{aligned} $$ For `n = 1`: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{REG} \gg 1 &= 0x52 \ (\text{binary } 0101\,0010) \\ (0x52) \mathbin{\&} 0x01 &= 0x00 \quad \text{(bit is clear)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What | Scenario | Recommended Method | Reason | |---|---|---| | Setting a single bit in ODR | `ORR` (asm) or `\|=` (C) | Single instruction, RMW acceptable if ISR doesn't touch same bit | | Toggling a bit on every loop | Bit-band alias write | Atomic, no RMW race | | Writing a multi-bit field | Mask + shift, single STR | Cannot use bit-band | | Reading a single bit | `LDR` + `UBFX` or `TST` | Bit-band read also valid | | Disabling interrupts briefly | `CPSID i` | Architecture-level | | Multi-bit mask | Read, `BIC` cleared bits, `ORR` set bits, write | Field manipulation | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Bit-Band Alias Memory Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Byte at 0x20000000
bits 7 to 0"] --- B["Byte at 0x20000001
bits 7 to 0"] B --- C["Byte at 0x20000002
bits 7 to 0"] C --- D["..."] D --- E["Byte at 0x200FFFFF
bits 7 to 0"] A1["Word at 0x22000000
bit 0 of 0x20000000"] --- A2["Word at 0x22000004
bit 1 of 0x20000000"] A2 --- A3["Word at 0x22000008
bit 2 of 0x20000000"] A3 --- A4["..."] A4 --- A5["Word at 0x2200001C
bit 7 of 0x20000000"] B1["Word at 0x22000020
bit 0 of 0x20000001"] --- B2["Word at 0x22000024
bit 1 of 0x20000001"] B2 --- B3["Word at 0x22000028
bit 2 of 0x20000001"] B3 --- B4["..."] B4 --- B5["Word at 0x2200003C
bit 7 of 0x20000001"] A --- A1 A --- A2 A --- A3 A --- A4 A --- A5 B --- B1 B --- B2 B --- B3 B --- B4 B --- B5 ``` ### 4.2 Read-Modify-Write (RMW) Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Start
Need to modify bit n of address A"] --> S2["Step 2: LDR Rt, [A]
Read 32-bit word into register"] S2 --> S3{"Step 3: Is the
operation atomic?"} S3 -- "Yes, use alias" --> P1["Step 4a: STR Rt, [Alias_A, n]
Single atomic write"] P1 --> S5["Step 5: Bit n updated
in one bus cycle"] S3 -- "No, use RMW" --> P2["Step 4b: BIC ORR or EOR
with mask 1 LSL n"] P2 --> S4["Step 5: STR Rt, [A]
Write back modified value"] S4 --> S6{"Step 6: Did an ISR fire
between LDR and STR?"} S6 -- "Yes" --> S7["Step 7: DATA LOST
Race condition occurred"] S6 -- "No" --> S5 S5 --> END1["End: Bit n is the
desired new value"] S7 --> END1 ``` ### 4.3 Bit Manipulation Instruction Set (Cortex-M4) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Source Register Rn
32-bit value"] --> O1["AND Rd, Rn, Rm
Mask bits"] A --> O2["ORR Rd, Rn, Rm
Set bits"] A --> O3["EOR Rd, Rn, Rm
Toggle bits"] A --> O4["BIC Rd, Rn, Rm
Clear bits where Rm = 1"] A --> O5["ORN Rd, Rn, Rm
Set bits where Rm = 0"] A --> O6["LSL LSR ROR
Position the mask"] A --> O7["TST Rn, Rm
Test bits, update flags only"] M["Mask Register Rm
e.g. 0x00000020"] --> O1 M --> O2 M --> O3 M --> O4 M --> O5 M --> O6 M --> O7 O1 --> R["Result Rd
manipulated value"] O2 --> R O3 --> R O4 --> R O5 --> R O6 --> R O7 --> F["APSR flags
N Z C V updated"] ``` ### 4.4 Memory-Mapped I/O vs Isolated I/O Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph M1["ARM Cortex-M Memory-Mapped I/O"] A1["4 GB Linear Address Space"] --> B1["SRAM Region"] A1 --> C1["Code Region"] A1 --> D1["Peripheral SFRs
accessed via LDR and STR"] D1 --> E1["Same MOV LDR STR instructions
for memory and I/O"] end subgraph M2["Legacy x86 Isolated I/O"] A2["Separate 64K I/O Space"] --> B2["IN and OUT instructions"] B2 --> C2["Address via DX register
or immediate"] end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar Pattern]** **Q1. (3 Marks)** *Explain the concept of "bit-band alias region" in ARM Cortex-M3/M4. Why is it useful?* **Model Answer:** The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 processors provide a **bit-band alias region** — a 32 MB block of memory that maps every individual bit of a 1 MB bit-band region to a full 32-bit aligned word. There are two bit-band regions: * **SRAM bit-band region** at `0x20000000` to `0x200FFFFF` (1 MB) → aliased to `0x22000000` to `0x23FFFFFF` (32 MB). * **Peripheral bit-band region** at `0x40000000` to `0x400FFFFF` (1 MB) → aliased to `0x42000000` to `0x43FFFFFF` (32 MB). **Usefulness:** 1. **Atomicity** — A single store (`STR`) to an alias address sets or clears exactly one bit without a read-modify-write sequence, eliminating race conditions in ISR-shared registers. 2. **Speed** — One instruction instead of three (LDR + logical op + STR). 3. **Compiler-friendly** — Standard C pointer dereference works, no assembly needed. 4. **Determinism** — Critical for real-time embedded systems. --- **Q2. (3 Marks)** *Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and isolated I/O. Which method is used in ARM Cortex-M?* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O | |---|---|---| | Address space | Peripherals share the same address space as memory | Peripherals have a separate 64 K I/O space | | Instructions | Uses standard `LDR` / `STR` | Uses special `IN` / `OUT` (x86) | | Flexibility | High — any instruction that accesses memory can access I/O | Limited — only `IN`/`OUT` family | | Address range | Full 4 GB on 32-bit ARM | Restricted to 64 K ports | | Protection | MPU can guard peripheral regions | No fine-grained MPU support for I/O space | | Used in | **ARM Cortex-M**, MIPS, RISC-V, 68K | x86 (legacy mode), 8086 | **ARM Cortex-M uses memory-mapped I/O** — all peripherals are accessible via the standard `LDR` and `STR` instructions at fixed addresses defined in the device header file (e.g., STM32, LPC, Tiva C). --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Pattern]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) (7 Marks)** *With a neat diagram and example, explain the bit-band alias address computation for ARM Cortex-M4. Compute the alias address for bit 7 of the byte located at `0x20000100` in SRAM.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Concept:** The bit-band alias region provides a unique 32-bit word for every single bit of the bit-band region. Writing `0x00000001` to the alias word sets the bit, and writing `0x00000000` clears it. Reading the alias returns either `0x01` or `0x00`. **Step 2 — Formula:** $$ \text{Alias\_Addr} = \text{Alias\_Base} + (A - \text{Region\_Base}) \times 32 + n \times 4 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values** for SRAM bit 7 of byte at `0x20000100`: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alias\_Base} &= 0x22000000 \\ A &= 0x20000100 \\ \text{Region\_Base} &= 0x20000000 \\ n &= 7 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alias\_Addr} &= 0x22000000 + (0x20000100 - 0x20000000) \times 32 + 7 \times 4 \\ &= 0x22000000 + 0x100 \times 32 + 28 \\ &= 0x22000000 + 0x2000 + 0x1C \\ &= 0x2200201C \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — C implementation:** ```c #define SRAM_BB_BIT7_OF_0x20000100 (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x2200201CUL) SRAM_BB_BIT7_OF_0x20000100 = 1; // atomic SET SRAM_BB_BIT7_OF_0x20000100 = 0; // atomic CLEAR ``` **Step 5 — Memory map diagram:** ``` Bit-band SRAM: 0x20000000 ───────────── 0x200FFFFF (1 MB = 1024 KB = 2^20 bytes) │ alias mapping factor 32x ▼ Bit-band Alias: 0x22000000 ───────────── 0x23FFFFFF (32 MB = 2^25 bytes) ``` The factor 32 = 8 bits × 4 bytes/word; this expansion is what allows each bit to be addressed individually as a full 32-bit word. **Marks distribution:** Formula (2), Substitution (2), Arithmetic (2), Code/diagram (1) = **7 marks**. --- **(b) (7 Marks)** *Compare the Read-Modify-Write (RMW) technique and the Bit-Band Alias technique for setting bit 5 of a GPIO output data register at address `0x40020014`. Provide ARM assembly code for both.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Problem setup:** We want to set bit 5 of the ODR register at `0x40020014` (STM32-style GPIOA ODR). The current value in the register is unknown. **Step 2 — Bit-Band Alias Address Computation** (peripheral region): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alias} &= 0x42000000 + (0x40020014 - 0x40000000) \times 32 + 5 \times 4 \\ &= 0x42000000 + 0x20014 \times 32 + 20 \\ &= 0x42000000 + 0x400280 + 0x14 \\ &= 0x42400294 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — ARM assembly using RMW (ORR technique):** ```armasm LDR R1, =0x40020014 ; R1 = address of ODR LDR R0, [R1] ; R0 = current ODR value ORR R0, R0, #0x20 ; Set bit 5 (mask = 1 << 5) STR R0, [R1] ; Write back ``` This requires **3 instructions** and is **non-atomic** — an ISR can corrupt the operation. **Step 4 — ARM assembly using Bit-Band Alias:** ```armasm LDR R1, =0x42400294 ; R1 = alias address of ODR bit 5 MOV R0, #1 ; R0 = value to set the bit STR R0, [R1] ; Atomic write — bit 5 is now 1 ``` This requires **2 instructions** and is **fully atomic**. **Step 5 — Comparison table:** | Aspect | RMW (ORR) | Bit-Band Alias | |---|---|---| | Number of instructions | 3 (LDR, ORR, STR) | 2 (MOV, STR) | | Atomicity | No (race condition possible) | Yes (hardware guaranteed) | | Clock cycles (M4) | ~3 + flash wait | ~2 | | Code size | Slightly larger | Smaller | | Read of register needed | Yes | No | | Suitable for ISR-shared registers | No | Yes | | Multi-bit manipulation | Yes | No (one bit per write) | **Step 6 — Conclusion:** For setting/clearing/toggling a single bit, bit-band alias is superior in safety and speed. For modifying multiple bits or a bit-field simultaneously, RMW is unavoidable. **Marks distribution:** Alias calc (2), RMW code (2), Bit-band code (1), Comparison (2) = **7 marks**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative* **(a) (7 Marks)** *Explain the various bitwise operations supported by ARM Cortex-M4 for bit manipulation. Write C code snippets to (i) set, (ii) clear, (iii) toggle, and (iv) test bit 3 of a register `REG` defined as `volatile uint32_t`. Show the equivalent ARM Thumb-2 assembly for each.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Bitwise operators in C and ARM:** | C operator | Equivalent ARM instruction | Truth on bit | Use | |---|---|---|---| | `&` (AND) | `AND` | `x & 1 = x` | Mask | | `\|` (OR) | `ORR` | `x \| 1 = 1` | Set bit | | `^` (XOR) | `EOR` | `x ^ 1 = ~x` | Toggle bit | | `& ~` (AND-NOT) | `BIC` | `x & ~1 = 0` | Clear bit | | `~` (NOT) | `MVN` | `~x` | Invert all bits | | `<<` `>>` | `LSL` `LSR` | position | Build mask | **Step 2 — Define `REG`:** ```c #define REG (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40000000UL) ``` **Step 3 — (i) SET bit 3:** ```c REG |= (1U << 3); // C ``` Thumb-2 assembly: ```armasm LDR R1, =0x40000000 LDR R0, [R1] ORR R0, R0, #0x8 ; 1 << 3 = 0x8 STR R0, [R1] ``` **Step 4 — (ii) CLEAR bit 3:** ```c REG &= ~(1U << 3); // C ``` Thumb-2 assembly: ```armasm LDR R1, =0x40000000 LDR R0, [R1] BIC R0, R0, #0x8 STR R0, [R1] ``` **Step 5 — (iii) TOGGLE bit 3:** ```c REG ^= (1U << 3); // C ``` Thumb-2 assembly: ```armasm LDR R1, =0x40000000 LDR R0, [R1] EOR R0, R0, #0x8 STR R0, [R1] ``` **Step 6 — (iv) TEST bit 3:** ```c if ((REG >> 3) & 1U) { // C — direct test /* bit is set */ } ``` Thumb-2 assembly: ```armasm LDR R1, =0x40000000 LDR R0, [R1] LSRS R0, R0, #3 ; logical shift right by 3 BICS R0, R0, #1 ; test low bit, update flags BEQ bit_clear ; branch if zero ; bit is set bit_clear: ``` **Step 7 — Why `volatile`?** The compiler is free to cache register values in CPU registers and skip subsequent reads. For a memory-mapped peripheral register, the value can change **outside** the compiler's knowledge (hardware, DMA, ISR). The `volatile` qualifier forces every access to be a real load/store. **Marks distribution:** Operator table (2), Set/clear code (2), Toggle/test code (2), `volatile` explanation (1) = **7 marks**. --- **(b) (7 Marks)** *With a block diagram, describe the memory map of ARM Cortex-M4 highlighting the bit-band and bit-band alias regions. Why is the alias region 32 times larger than the bit-band region? Show the calculation.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Memory Map (textual block diagram):** ``` 0xFFFFFFFF ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Vendor-specific / System │ 0xE0000000 ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ System Control Space │ │ (NVIC, SysTick, MPU, etc.) │ 0xE0000000 └─────────────────────────────────┘ 0x43FFFFFF ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Peripheral bit-band ALIAS │ 32 MB │ 0x42000000 – 0x43FFFFFF │ 0x42000000 ├═════════════════════════════════┤ │ Mapped bit-by-bit to ↓ │ 0x400FFFFF ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Peripheral bit-band REGION │ 1 MB │ 0x40000000 – 0x400FFFFF │ 0x40000000 ├═════════════════════════════════┤ │ (GPIO, UART, TIM, ADC, etc.) │ 0x23FFFFFF ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ SRAM bit-band ALIAS │ 32 MB │ 0x22000000 – 0x23FFFFFF │ 0x22000000 ├═════════════════════════════════┤ │ Mapped bit-by-bit to ↓ │ 0x200FFFFF ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ SRAM bit-band REGION │ 1 MB │ 0x20000000 – 0x200FFFFF │ 0x20000000 └─────────────────────────────────┘ 0x1FFFFFFF ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ Code / Default │ 0x00000000 └─────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Step 2 — Why 32× larger? Derivation:** Each source byte in the bit-band region contains 8 bits. Each of these 8 bits must be mapped to a **unique 32-bit word** in the alias region. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alias size} &= \text{Region size} \times \text{bits per byte} \times \text{bytes per word} \\ &= 1\ \text{MB} \times 8 \times 4 \\ &= 32\ \text{MB} \end{aligned} $$ Or equivalently: $$ \frac{\text{Alias size}}{\text{Region size}} = 8 \times 4 = 32 $$ **Step 3 — Address arithmetic proof:** For a byte at offset `Δ` within the bit-band region and bit `n`, the alias word offset is: $$ \text{Offset} = \Delta \times 32 + n \times 4 $$ For the maximum case `Δ = 0xFFFFF` and `n = 7`: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Offset} &= 0xFFFFF \times 32 + 7 \times 4 \\ &= 0x1FFFFE0 + 0x1C \\ &= 0x1FFFFFC \end{aligned} $$ This is **≤ 0x1FFFFFF**, fitting exactly into a 32 MB alias region. ✓ **Step 4 — Engineering significance:** The 32× expansion is the **hardware price** for atomic single-bit access. Designers accepted this memory cost (cheap on-chip) to gain race-free peripheral programming. **Marks distribution:** Memory map (3), Derivation (3), Significance (1) = **7 marks**. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes (from Board Examiner's Perspective):** > > 1. **Wrong constant in the alias formula** — Students frequently write `× 8` instead of `× 32`. Remember: it is *bits per byte* × *bytes per word* = 8 × 4 = 32. > 2. **Wrong alias base address** — SRAM is `0x22000000` and **peripheral is `0x42000000`**, not `0x22000000` for both. > 3. **Forgetting `volatile`** in C pointer declarations for SFRs — guaranteed 0.5–1 mark loss in code-based questions. > 4. **Confusing RMW with bit-band** — RMW is **software-based** (3 instructions, non-atomic); bit-band is **hardware-based** (1 alias write, atomic). > 5. **Decimal vs hexadecimal** — When the question says "bit 7 of `0x20000100`", compute `0x100 × 32 = 0x2000`, not `256 × 32 = 8192` and then re-convert. Stay in hex throughout. > 6. **Writing `0x20000000 + 0x100 × 32`** without parentheses — operator precedence may give the wrong answer in calculators that parse left-to-right. > 7. **Forgetting that bit-band reads return 0 or 1, not the original byte** — alias reads do **not** return the parent byte; they return a 0x00000000 or 0x00000001 word. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **ARM uses memory-mapped I/O** — there is no separate I/O space; SFRs are normal memory addresses accessed via `LDR`/`STR`. - [x] **Two bit-band regions exist**: SRAM (`0x20000000`–`0x200FFFFF`) and Peripheral (`0x40000000`–`0x400FFFFF`), each **1 MB**. - [x] **Two alias regions exist**: `0x22000000`–`0x23FFFFFF` and `0x42000000`–`0x43FFFFFF`, each **32 MB**. - [x] **Alias address formula** (memorise verbatim): - SRAM: `0x22000000 + (A − 0x20000000) × 32 + n × 4` - Peripheral: `0x42000000 + (A − 0x40000000) × 32 + n × 4` - [x] **Expansion factor = 32** because 8 bits/byte × 4 bytes/word. - [x] **ARM bitwise instructions**: `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR`, `BIC`, `ORN`, `LSL`, `LSR`, `ROR`, `TST`, `MVN`. - [x] **Set bit** → `ORR` (asm) / `|=` (C); **Clear bit** → `BIC` / `&= ~`; **Toggle bit** → `EOR` / `^=`. - [x] **Read-Modify-Write (RMW) is non-atomic** — vulnerable to ISR race conditions. - [x] **Bit-band alias writes are atomic** — single `STR` sets/clears one bit, ISR-safe. - [x] **Bit-band reads return 0 or 1**, not the parent byte's value. - [x] **`volatile` qualifier is mandatory** for any pointer to a memory-mapped peripheral register. - [x] **Multi-bit field manipulation** cannot use bit-band; use mask + shift + RMW. - [x] **Cortex-M3 and Cortex-M4** support bit-band; **Cortex-M0/M0+ do not** (use RMW only). - [x] **Real-world usage**: GPIO, NVIC ISER/ICER, RCC clock-enable registers, peripheral status flags. - [x] **KTU favourite 14-mark question pattern**: "Compute alias address for bit n of address A" — practise with at least 5 different numerical examples. - [x] **Avoid in answers**: `|` (vertical pipe) inside math expressions in tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) for absolute value notation.

topics.title: Accessing Memory Mapped IO Using Pointers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Accessing Memory Mapped IO Using Pointers ## SECTION 1 : CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO)** is a method of performing input/output operations in embedded systems where the **hardware peripheral registers** are mapped into the **same address space** as the main program memory (RAM/ROM). In the ARM Cortex-M architecture (LPC1768, STM32, etc.), every peripheral control register, data register, and status register is assigned a **unique 32-bit memory address**, allowing the CPU to manipulate them using standard load and store instructions, executed through **C pointers**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In ARM Cortex-M3/M4, there is **NO separate I/O address space** (unlike the x86 IN/OUT instructions). All I/O is memory-mapped, and the Single-cycle `LDR` (Load Register) and `STR` (Store Register) instructions are used to communicate with peripherals. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Number" Metaphor Imagine a city where: - **Memory (RAM)** is one huge neighborhood, with every house having a unique address (e.g., House #1 to #10000). - **Peripheral Devices** (UART, GPIO, ADC, Timer) are also houses, but they are *interleaved* into the same city at specific addresses (e.g., House #5000 is the GPIO controller, House #6000 is the UART). A **pointer** is simply a piece of paper with a house number written on it. To "talk" to the device at House #5000 (GPIO), you: 1. Write `5000` on the paper (declare pointer). 2. Go to that address (dereference the pointer). 3. Either read what is there or write a new value. This is exactly how `*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40000000` works in C. ### 1.3 Isolated I/O vs Memory-Mapped I/O | Feature | Isolated I/O (x86 style) | Memory-Mapped I/O (ARM) | |---|---|---| | Address Space | Separate (IN/OUT instructions) | Unified with memory | | Instructions | Special (`IN`, `OUT`) | Normal `LDR`/`STR` | | Address Size | Smaller (8/16 bit) | Full 32-bit (4 GB) | | Used in ARM? | **No** | **Yes** | | Flexibility | Limited | High (can use pointers, arrays, structures) | > [!NOTE] > In the KTU syllabus (PEECT525), we focus **exclusively** on Memory-Mapped I/O because ARM Cortex-M processors use a **Von Neumann / Modified Harvard unified address map** where peripherals, SRAM, and Flash all coexist in a 4 GB linear address space starting from `0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`. ## SECTION 2 : DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of MMIO Pointer Access #### Pillar 1 — Address Casting (The "Where") The compiler must be told the **exact numeric address** of a peripheral register. Since the address is a numeric constant, it must be **cast** to a pointer of the correct type matching the register's width (usually `uint32_t` for 32-bit ARM). ```c #define GPIO_PORT0_DIR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028008) ``` The cast `(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028008` tells the compiler: *"Treat the number `0xE0028008` as a pointer to a volatile 32-bit unsigned integer."* #### Pillar 2 — The `volatile` Qualifier (The "Don't Optimize") The `volatile` keyword is **non-negotiable** in embedded C. It instructs the compiler: - **DO NOT** cache the value in a CPU register. - **DO NOT** optimize away repeated reads/writes. - **EVERY** access must touch the actual hardware. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why `volatile` is critical:** The compiler may see `while(GPIO_FLAG == 0);` and think *"This is an infinite loop, optimize it away!"* Without `volatile`, the compiler caches the value in an internal register and never re-reads the hardware flag, causing the program to hang forever. #### Pillar 3 — Bitwise Manipulation (The "Which Bit") Peripheral registers control multiple features in a single 32-bit word. Each bit (or group of bits) controls a specific function. We use **bitwise operators** (`&`, `|`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`) to manipulate individual bits without disturbing others. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — The Bit Manipulation Cheat Table | Operation | C Syntax | Purpose | Real Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Set a Bit** | `reg \|= (1 << n)` | Force bit `n` to **1** | Turn ON an LED, Enable a peripheral | | **Clear a Bit** | `reg &= ~(1 << n)` | Force bit `n` to **0** | Turn OFF an LED, Disable a peripheral | | **Toggle a Bit** | `reg ^= (1 << n)` | Flip bit `n` | Blink LED, Toggle output | | **Test a Bit** | `(reg >> n) & 1` | Read bit `n` | Check button status, Read flag | | **Mask n bits** | `reg & MASK` | Keep only selected bits | Read specific field | | **Write Field** | `reg = (reg & ~MASK) \vert VALUE` | Modify a multi-bit field | Set baud rate, Set prescaler | > [!NOTE] > The vertical separator symbol `\vert` is used in tables above to avoid breaking KTU markdown table syntax (per protocol). ### 2.3 Engineering Utility In real-world embedded systems, MMIO via pointers is used for: - **Automotive ECUs** (ECU = Engine Control Unit reads sensors via MMIO). - **Medical Devices** (Pacemakers, MRI machines access ADC registers via pointers). - **IoT Devices** (Smart sensors toggle GPIO bits to communicate). - **Robotics** (Motor controllers set PWM duty cycles through MMIO). ### 2.4 Memory Map of LPC1768 (KTU Reference Board) The **LPC1768** (ARM Cortex-M3) memory map is a key KTU exam topic: | Address Range | Region | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `0x0000 0000` – `0x0007 FFFF` | Flash Memory | Program code storage | | `0x1000 0000` – `0x1000 7FFF` | SRAM (32 KB) | Variables, stack, heap | | `0x2000 0000` – `0x2000 FFFF` | Local SRAM (64 KB) | Additional RAM | | `0x4000 0000` – `0x400F FFFF` | APB0 Peripherals | UART0, UART1, Timer0/1, PWM | | `0x5000 0000` – `0x500F FFFF` | GPIO | Ports 0–4 | | `0xE000 0000` – `0xE00F FFFF` | System Control | NVIC, SysTick, SCB | | `0xE002 8000` – `0xE002 8FFF` | GPIO Specific Registers | FIO0DIR, FIO0SET, FIO0CLR, FIO0PIN | ## SECTION 3 : STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Toggling an LED (LPC1768 GPIO Port 0.22) **Problem Statement:** Write a C program to blink LED connected to P0.22 using memory-mapped pointers. #### Step 1: Identify the Hardware Registers From the LPC1768 User Manual: $$ \text{FIO0DIR} = 0xE0028008 \quad \text{(Direction Register)} $$ $$ \text{FIO0SET} = 0xE0028018 \quad \text{(Set Register — write 1 to set pin HIGH)} $$ $$ \text{FIO0CLR} = 0xE002801C \quad \text{(Clear Register — write 1 to set pin LOW)} $$ $$ \text{FIO0PIN} = 0xE0028014 \quad \text{(Pin Value Register — read current state)} $$ #### Step 2: Define Symbolic Names Using Pointers ```c #define FIO0DIR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028008) #define FIO0SET (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028018) #define FIO0CLR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE002801C) ``` **Explanation of the magic:** - `volatile uint32_t *` → pointer to a 32-bit value that must be re-read every time. - `0xE0028008` → hardware address of the register. - The `*` outside the parentheses → when used on the left of `=`, it **dereferences** the pointer, writing to hardware. #### Step 3: Set Pin P0.22 as Output Bit 22 of `FIO0DIR` controls the direction of pin 0.22. Writing `1` makes it output. ```c FIO0DIR |= (1 << 22); // Set bit 22 of FIO0DIR ``` **Step-by-step trace:** - `1 << 22` shifts a `1` to the 22nd position: binary `0000 0000 0100 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000`. - `FIO0DIR |=` performs a **read-modify-write**: reads current DIR, ORs with the mask, writes back. - Result: Bit 22 becomes `1` → Pin P0.22 is now an **output**. #### Step 4: Blink the LED ```c int main(void) { FIO0DIR |= (1 << 22); // Step A: Configure P0.22 as output while(1) { FIO0SET = (1 << 22); // Step B: Turn LED ON delay_ms(500); FIO0CLR = (1 << 22); // Step C: Turn LED OFF delay_ms(500); } } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Note that writing `FIO0SET = (1 << 22)` is **atomic** (single instruction). We do NOT use `FIO0SET |= (1 << 22)` because the SET register is a "write-1-to-set" type — only the bits written as `1` take effect, so a direct assignment is correct and safer (no read-modify-write race condition). #### Step 5: Mathematical Analysis of the Bit Operation For a generic register `REG` and bit position `n`: $$ \text{Set bit } n: \quad \text{REG}_{\text{new}} = \text{REG}_{\text{old}} \lor (1 \ll n) $$ $$ \text{Clear bit } n: \quad \text{REG}_{\text{new}} = \text{REG}_{\text{old}} \land \neg(1 \ll n) $$ $$ \text{Test bit } n: \quad \text{result} = (\text{REG} \gg n) \land 1 $$ For **n = 22** (our LED pin): $$ 1 \ll 22 = 2^{22} = 4\,194\,304 $$ So `FIO0SET = 4194304` turns on the LED. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Reading a Push Button with Debouncing **Problem Statement:** Read the state of a switch connected to P0.10 (configured as input) and mirror it on LED at P0.22. ```c #define FIO0DIR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028008) #define FIO0PIN (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028014) #define FIO0SET (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028018) #define FIO0CLR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE002801C) int main(void) { FIO0DIR |= (1 << 22); // LED pin as OUTPUT FIO0DIR &= ~(1 << 10); // Button pin as INPUT while(1) { if ((FIO0PIN >> 10) & 1) { // Test bit 10 FIO0SET = (1 << 22); // LED ON if button pressed } else { FIO0CLR = (1 << 22); // LED OFF if button released } } } ``` **Logic Trace for Button Test:** - `FIO0PIN` is read → returns 32-bit value of all pin states. - `>> 10` shifts bit 10 to position 0 (LSB). - `& 1` masks all other bits, keeping only the LSB. - Result: `1` if pressed, `0` if released. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Structure Pointer Access (CMSIS Style) Modern ARM development uses **structures** mapped over the peripheral memory block. The compiler computes each register's address as an **offset** from the base. ```c typedef struct { volatile uint32_t PIN; // Offset 0x00 volatile uint32_t SET; // Offset 0x04 volatile uint32_t DIR; // Offset 0x08 (Note: example order) volatile uint32_t CLR; // Offset 0x0C } GPIO_TypeDef; #define GPIO0_BASE (0xE0028000UL) #define GPIO0 ((GPIO_TypeDef *) GPIO0_BASE) // Usage: GPIO0->DIR |= (1 << 22); // Equivalent to FIO0DIR |= (1<<22) GPIO0->SET = (1 << 22); ``` **Why offsets matter:** - The compiler calculates: `address = GPIO0_BASE + (element_index * 4)` for each `uint32_t` field. - This is identical to the manual `#define` approach, but **type-safe** and **readable**. **Offset calculation proof:** $$ \text{Address of SET} = \text{Base} + (\text{offsetof}(SET)) $$ $$ \text{Address of SET} = 0xE0028000 + 0x04 = 0xE0028004 $$ (Assuming SET is the second member; actual LPC1768 has DIR at 0x08. The principle remains identical.) --- ### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Manual `#define` vs Structure Pointer | Aspect | Manual `#define` | Structure Pointer | |---|---|---| | Readability | Low (cryptic addresses) | High (named fields) | | Type Safety | None (always `uint32_t`) | Strong (compiler checks) | | Offset Calculation | Manual | Automatic | | Industry Standard | Beginner / Academic | CMSIS, HAL, Professional | | KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | Moderate | ## SECTION 4 : STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 LPC1768 Memory Map — Unified Address Space ```mermaid flowchart TB A["0x0000 0000 - FLASH 512KB
Program Code"]:::mem B["0x1000 0000 - SRAM 32KB
Static Variables Stack"]:::mem C["0x2000 0000 - LOCAL SRAM 64KB
Heap Large Buffers"]:::mem D["0x4000 0000 - APB0 PERIPHERALS
UART0 UART1 TIMER0 PWM"]:::periph E["0x5000 0000 - GPIO PORTS
FIO0DIR FIO0SET FIO0CLR"]:::gpio F["0xE000 0000 - SYSTEM CONTROL
NVIC SYSTICK SCB"]:::sys G["0xE002 8000 - GPIO0 BASE
Specific Register Block"]:::gpio A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F E -.contains.-> G classDef mem fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef periph fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gpio fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sys fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Pointer Dereferencing Flow — Writing to GPIO ```mermaid flowchart LR A["C Code
FIO0SET = 1<<22"]:::code B["Compiler Generates
STR r0, r1, #0x18"]:::asm C["Address Bus
0xE0028018"]:::bus D["Memory Decoder
Identifies as GPIO0"]:::dec E["GPIO Hardware
Sets Pin 22 HIGH"]:::hw F["LED Turns ON"]:::led A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F classDef code fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 classDef asm fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#1A237E,color:#000 classDef bus fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 classDef dec fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,color:#000 classDef hw fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef led fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Volatile vs Non-Volatile — Compiler Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NV["WITHOUT volatile"] NV1["Code: while(REG == 0);"]:::codeNV NV2["Compiler reads REG ONCE
Stores in CPU register r3"]:::opt1 NV3["Compares r3 with 0
INFINITE LOOP HANG"]:::hang NV1 --> NV2 --> NV3 end subgraph W["WITH volatile"] W1["Code: while(REG == 0);"]:::codeV W2["Compiler MUST reload REG
Every iteration from memory"]:::opt2 W3["Sees hardware change
Loop exits correctly"]:::exit W1 --> W2 --> W3 end classDef codeNV fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 classDef codeV fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef opt1 fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000 classDef opt2 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 classDef hang fill:#EF5350,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#FFF classDef exit fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#FFF ``` ### 4.4 Bit Manipulation Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A["32-bit Register Value
0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000"]:::reg B["Mask: 1 << 22
0000 0000 0100 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000"]:::mask C["OR Operation
Set Bit 22 to 1"]:::op D["Result
0000 0000 0100 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000"]:::result E["Pin P0.22 driven HIGH"]:::hwout A --> C B --> C C --> D D --> E classDef reg fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000 classDef mask fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 classDef op fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000 classDef result fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef hwout fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#4527A0,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]** *Explain the concept of Memory Mapped I/O. How does it differ from I/O Mapped I/O? Why is it preferred in ARM processors?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Memory Mapped I/O is a scheme in which peripheral device registers are assigned addresses within the same address space as the main memory. The CPU uses standard data transfer instructions (`LDR`/`STR` in ARM) to read from or write to these devices by accessing their memory-mapped addresses. **Key Differences:** - In **I/O Mapped I/O** (used in x86), a separate address space and special instructions (`IN`, `OUT`) are required. - In **Memory Mapped I/O** (used in ARM), peripherals and memory share the same address space; normal load/store instructions work on both. **Why ARM prefers MMIO:** The ARM Cortex-M architecture has a unified 4 GB linear address map. MMIO allows the use of a full 32-bit address space, supports all addressing modes (including pointers and structures), simplifies compiler design, and enables high-speed single-cycle peripheral access using `LDR`/`STR`. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** *What is the significance of the `volatile` keyword when accessing memory-mapped peripheral registers using pointers in C?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `volatile` keyword prevents the C compiler from applying optimizations (such as register caching or dead-code elimination) on the variable. Since hardware registers can change asynchronously — for example, when a peripheral sets a status flag or an external interrupt modifies a value — the compiler must read the register from its actual memory address on every access, not from a CPU register copy. **Example:** ```c volatile uint32_t *status = (volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028014; while((*status & 0x01) == 0); // Wait for flag ``` Without `volatile`, the compiler may cache the value of `*status` and the loop becomes infinite. Hence, `volatile` is **mandatory** for all memory-mapped peripheral access. --- ### PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### ✅ CHOICE A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question — Module 1]** **(a) [7 Marks]** *With neat sketches, explain the Memory Mapped I/O addressing scheme of the LPC1768 ARM Cortex-M3 microcontroller. List the addresses of at least three peripheral registers.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *Write a C program to configure pin P0.15 of LPC1768 as an output and continuously toggle it with a delay. Use memory-mapped pointer access. Show the bit manipulation calculations for setting and clearing the pin.* ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — Solution:** The LPC1768 has a **unified 4 GB memory map** (`0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`). All peripherals are memory-mapped at fixed addresses defined in the device header file. A simplified block diagram of the memory map is shown in the previous section. **Three Important Register Addresses (LPC1768):** | Register | Address | Function | |---|---|---| | `FIO0DIR` | `0xE0028008` | Configure port 0 pins as input/output | | `FIO0SET` | `0xE0028018` | Write 1 to drive pin HIGH | | `FIO0CLR` | `0xE002801C` | Write 1 to drive pin LOW | When the CPU executes `LDR R0, [R1]` where `R1 = 0xE0028008`, the memory decoder recognizes the address falls in the GPIO block and routes the data to the GPIO hardware instead of RAM. (7 marks: 3 for diagram explanation, 2 for listing addresses, 2 for working mechanism) --- **Part (b) — Solution:** **Step 1: Define pointer-mapped register addresses** ```c #define FIO0DIR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028008UL) #define FIO0SET (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE0028018UL) #define FIO0CLR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0xE002801CUL) ``` **Step 2: Bit position for P0.15** Bit position $n = 15$. Therefore, the mask value is: $$ 1 \ll 15 = 2^{15} = 32\,768 $$ **Step 3: Configure P0.15 as output** To make bit 15 an output, we must set it to `1` in `FIO0DIR`: ```c FIO0DIR |= (1 << 15); // Set bit 15 ``` Mathematical equivalent: $$ \text{FIO0DIR}_{\text{new}} = \text{FIO0DIR}_{\text{old}} \lor 2^{15} $$ **Step 4: Toggle the pin continuously** ```c int main(void) { FIO0DIR |= (1 << 15); // Configure P0.15 as output while(1) { FIO0SET = (1 << 15); // Drive pin HIGH delay_ms(500); FIO0CLR = (1 << 15); // Drive pin LOW delay_ms(500); } } ``` **Step 5: Verification of logic** - After `FIO0SET = 32768` → Pin P0.15 is at logical HIGH (3.3 V). - After `FIO0CLR = 32768` → Pin P0.15 is at logical LOW (0 V). - A 500 ms ON / 500 ms OFF cycle produces a **1 Hz square wave** with 50 % duty cycle. (7 marks: 1 for #define, 2 for direction config, 2 for toggle logic, 2 for explanation and timing) --- #### ✅ CHOICE B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question — Module 1]** **(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe the role of pointers in accessing memory-mapped peripherals in ARM-based systems. Explain why the `volatile` qualifier is essential with a suitable example.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *Consider the UART0 Transmit Holding Register (`U0THR`) of LPC1768 at address `0xE0000000` and the Line Status Register (`U0LSR`) at `0xE0000014`. Write a C function `UART0_SendChar(char c)` that uses memory-mapped pointers to transmit a character byte, waiting until the transmitter is ready (Transmitter Empty bit = bit 5 of U0LSR is 1).* ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — Solution:** Pointers play a central role in MMIO because peripheral registers do not have variable names in C — they exist only at fixed hardware addresses. A pointer provides the mechanism to: 1. **Hold the hardware address** as a numeric constant. 2. **Dereference it** to read from or write to the register. 3. **Be cast to the correct type** (`uint8_t`, `uint16_t`, `uint32_t`) to match the register width. **Why `volatile` is essential:** Hardware registers are *external* to the CPU. Their values can change without any action by the C program — for example, a UART sets a "transmit complete" flag when it finishes sending a byte. The compiler, however, has no way to know this; it assumes that if a variable is not modified by the program, its value is constant and may be cached in a CPU register. The `volatile` keyword disables this optimization, forcing the compiler to re-read the memory location on every access. **Example:** ```c volatile uint32_t *U0LSR = (volatile uint32_t *)0xE0000014; while((*U0LSR & (1 << 5)) == 0); // Wait for Transmitter Empty ``` Without `volatile`, the compiler may optimize this to an infinite loop, hanging the entire system — a classic KTU exam pitfall. (7 marks: 3 for pointer role, 2 for volatile explanation, 2 for example) --- **Part (b) — Solution:** **Step 1: Define register addresses** ```c #define U0THR (*(volatile uint8_t *)0xE0000000UL) // 8-bit data register #define U0LSR (*(volatile uint8_t *)0xE0000014UL) // 8-bit status register ``` Note: The `uint8_t` cast is used here because UART data and status registers are 8 bits wide. **Step 2: Bit position of Transmitter Empty (TEMT) in U0LSR** According to the LPC1768 User Manual, the **TEMT** bit is at position **5** of `U0LSR`. **Step 3: C function implementation** ```c void UART0_SendChar(char c) { // Wait until Transmitter Empty bit (bit 5) becomes 1 while((U0LSR & (1 << 5)) == 0) { ; // Busy wait } // Load the character into the Transmit Holding Register U0THR = c; } ``` **Step 4: Mathematical proof of the wait condition** The expression `U0LSR & (1 << 5)` performs an AND with mask `0b00100000`: $$ \text{Mask} = 1 \ll 5 = 2^{5} = 32 = 0x20 $$ If bit 5 is `1` → the result is `32` (non-zero) → the `while` loop exits and the character is transmitted. If bit 5 is `0` → the result is `0` → the loop continues spinning. This is a standard **polling-based** UART transmission technique. (7 marks: 1 for address defs, 2 for wait loop, 2 for transmit step, 2 for bit-mask calculation) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Loss Areas:** > 1. **Missing `volatile` qualifier** → 1–2 mark deduction. Examiners actively scan for this. > 2. **Confusing FIO0SET with FIO0PIN** → FIO0SET/FIO0CLR use *write-1-to-set* semantics; do NOT use `|=` on them. > 3. **Wrong address** → Always cross-verify with the LPC1768 User Manual. A single wrong hex digit costs 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting `UL` suffix** on large hex constants → May cause compiler warnings on some systems. > 5. **Confusing bit 0 with bit 1** → LEDs on LPC1768 are typically on P0.22 or P0.15; check the board schematic. > 6. **Using `FIO0SET |= (1<> n) & 1` — for polling flags and reading inputs. - ✅ **LPC1768 GPIO0 base**: `0xE0028000`; key offsets: DIR=0x08, PIN=0x14, SET=0x18, CLR=0x1C. - ✅ **`volatile` is mandatory** — without it, compiler optimizations break hardware communication. - ✅ **CMSIS structure pointers** (`GPIO0->DIR`) are the modern, type-safe equivalent of raw `#define` pointers. - ✅ **Always calculate the mask**: `1 << n` where `n` is the bit position (0–31 for 32-bit registers). - ✅ **Polling pattern**: `while((reg & MASK) == 0);` is the standard "wait for hardware event" idiom. - ✅ **LPC1768** uses little-endian byte ordering and a 4 GB unified address space (`0x00000000`–`0xFFFFFFFF`).

topics.title: Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Structures # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Structure** in the C programming language is a user-defined, heterogeneous aggregate data type that logically groups one or more variables (called **members** or **fields**) of potentially different data types under a single name. The declaration of a structure creates a custom template (often called a derived, composite, or record type) that can subsequently be used to allocate memory for any number of instances, each instance being a logically contiguous but non-contiguous-in-memory block (subject to alignment padding) holding its own copy of the members. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Board Definition:** A `struct` in C is a *derived data type* that bundles dissimilar data items, contrasting with arrays which are homogeneous. In embedded ARM Cortex-M (Keil MDK / LPC2148) programming, structures are heavily used to define **Memory-Mapped Register (SFR) layouts** and **Hardware Descriptor Blocks (HDBs)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a structure as a **"Student ID Card"**: - The card itself is the *structure variable* (one card = one instance). - The card contains a **Name** (a `char` array), a **Roll Number** (an `int`), a **CGPA** (a `float`), and a **Blood Group** (a `char`). - Each piece of data on the card is of a different type, yet all are physically attached to that single card. - One template ("Student ID") can produce thousands of cards — this is exactly how a `struct` template generates many *structure variables*. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** An *array* is a row of identical boxes (homogeneous). A *structure* is a single cabinet with different-sized drawers (heterogeneous). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Property | Typical ARM C Standard | |---|---| | **Minimum size of `struct`** | **1 byte** (compiler-defined, but practically the size of the largest member after padding) | | **Default storage class** | `auto` (stack) unless declared `static` or `extern` | | **Alignment unit** | **4 bytes** on ARM (Cortex-M word-aligned access) | | **End-of-struct alignment** | Padded to the size of the largest member's alignment boundary | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structured Theoretical Breakdown ### Step 1 — Structure Template Declaration A structure *template* is defined using the `struct` keyword. It does **not** consume memory; only *instantiation* does. ```c struct Student { int rollNo; // 4 bytes char name[20]; // 20 bytes float cgpa; // 4 bytes }; ``` ### Step 2 — Variable Declaration (Memory Allocation) After the template, we create actual variables (instances): ```c struct Student s1, s2; // s1 and s2 are two independent instances ``` ### Step 3 — Member Access Operators (Why & How) - **`.` (Dot / Member Operator)** — used with structure **variables**. - **`->` (Arrow / Pointer-to-Member Operator)** — used with **pointers to structures**. > [!NOTE] > **Why two operators?** A pointer `p` stores an *address*, not the structure itself, so `p.member` would dereference an address as if it were a struct (illegal). The compiler interprets `p->member` as `(*p).member`. ### Step 4 — Initialization Three legal styles: 1. **Designated initialization (C99 / KTU preferred):** `struct Student s1 = {.rollNo=101, .cgpa=9.2};` 2. **Positional (in declaration order):** `struct Student s1 = {101, "Anu", 9.2};` 3. **Runtime assignment:** `s1.rollNo = 101;` ### Step 5 — Size Calculation (with Padding) $$S_{struct} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(m_i) + P_{padding}$$ where $P_{padding}$ is the number of bytes inserted by the compiler to satisfy **natural alignment** constraints. On a 32-bit ARM core, members are typically aligned to **4-byte boundaries**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Notes | |---|---|---| | Template | `struct Tag { members; };` | Allocates **0 bytes** at this point | | Variable | `struct Tag var;` | Allocates memory | | Access by variable | `var.member` | Dot operator | | Access by pointer | `ptr->member` ≡ `(*ptr).member` | Arrow operator | | Size | `sizeof(struct Tag)` | Includes padding | | Nested struct | `struct Outer { struct Inner x; };` | Access: `o.x.field` | | Array of struct | `struct Tag arr[10];` | Access: `arr[3].member` | | Self-referential | `struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; };` | Used in linked lists | | Bit-fields | `unsigned int flag : 1;` | Saves memory in SFRs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** On ARM Cortex-M, hardware registers (e.g., `GPIO0->FIODIR`, `UART0->LCR`) are accessed via structures that mirror the **System Control Space (SCS)** memory map. The CMSIS standard uses `typedef struct { __IO uint32_t MODER; ... } GPIO_TypeDef;` to map every register. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Size of a Padded Structure Consider the structure template used in many KTU board questions: ```c struct Sample { char a; // 1 byte int b; // 4 bytes char c; // 1 byte double d; // 8 bytes (on ARM with FPU) }; ``` We must compute `sizeof(struct Sample)` assuming a 32-bit ARM with 8-byte double alignment. ### Step-by-Step Memory Layout (Exhaustive) | Offset (bytes) | Member | Size | Padding Inserted | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | `a` (char) | 1 | 0 | Placed at offset 0; no alignment needed. | | 1–3 | — | — | 3 | `b` (int, 4-byte aligned) cannot start at 1. Compiler inserts 3 padding bytes. | | 4–7 | `b` (int) | 4 | 0 | Aligned at offset 4 (multiple of 4). | | 8 | `c` (char) | 1 | 0 | Occupies 1 byte. | | 9–15 | — | — | 7 | `d` (double, 8-byte aligned) must start at an 8-byte boundary. The next such boundary after 9 is 16. So 7 bytes of padding are inserted. | | 16–23 | `d` (double) | 8 | 0 | Aligned at offset 16. | | 24–31 | — | — | 8 | **Trailing padding** to make the total size a multiple of the largest alignment (8), so arrays of this struct remain aligned. | $$ \begin{aligned} S_{struct} &= \text{sizeof}(a) + \text{pad} + \text{sizeof}(b) + \text{sizeof}(c) + \text{pad} + \text{sizeof}(d) + \text{trailpad} \\ &= 1 + 3 + 4 + 1 + 7 + 8 + 8 \\ &= 32 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Logical Deduction:** Reordering members as `{double d; int b; char a; char c;}` reduces the size to **16 bytes** — a common KTU question on how to optimize memory in embedded systems. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Access Time & Memory Trade-offs | Access Type | Syntax | Indirection Levels | Typical Clock Cycles (ARM Cortex-M3) | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | Direct on stack | `s.rollNo` | 0 | 1 cycle (LDR) | Local variables | | Pointer dereference | `p->rollNo` | 1 | 1 cycle (LDR) + address load | Dynamic memory, hardware regs | | Nested | `o.inner.field` | 0 (or 1 if pointer) | 2 cycles | Complex data | | Array index | `arr[i].field` | 1 | Base + index*sizeof + offset | Tables of records | | Bit-field | `s.flag : 1` | 0 | 1–3 (read-modify-write) | Hardware flags | ## 3.3 Derivation: Pointer-to-Member Equivalence The C language specification defines the arrow operator as a syntactic shorthand: $$ \begin{aligned} p \rightarrow m &\equiv (*p).m \\ &\equiv \text{Read memory at address } (p + \text{offset}_m) \\ \text{where } \text{offset}_m &= \text{offsetof}(struct\ Type, m) \end{aligned} $$ This is why ARM CMSIS headers use `->` extensively — it generates efficient pointer-relative addressing. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Layout Block Diagram of a Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph StructLayout["Memory Layout: sizeof = 24 bytes (illustrative)"] direction LR A["Offset 0
char id
1 byte"]:::mem B["Offset 1-3
PADDING
3 bytes"]:::pad C["Offset 4-7
int value
4 bytes"]:::mem D["Offset 8-15
double price
8 bytes"]:::mem E["Offset 16-19
char tag
1 byte"]:::mem F["Offset 20-23
PADDING
4 bytes"]:::pad A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F end classDef mem fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef pad fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Structure Access Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Program needs member access]) --> Q{Is access via
variable or pointer?} Q -- "Variable (s)" --> Dot["Use . operator
s.member"] Q -- "Pointer (p)" --> Arrow["Use -> operator
p -> member"] Dot --> Compile["Compiler computes
base + offset"] Arrow --> Deref["Compiler emits:
LDR r0, [r1, #offset]"] Compile --> Result([Member value retrieved]) Deref --> Result style Start fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style Result fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style Dot fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style Arrow fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nested Structure with Pointer ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OuterStruct["struct Outer (Size 12 bytes on ARM)"] O1["int type
offset 0"]:::fld O2["struct Inner payload
offset 4 (4-byte aligned)"]:::fld end subgraph InnerStruct["struct Inner"] I1["char code
offset 0"]:::inner I2["float val
offset 4"]:::inner end Ptr["struct Outer *p
(Holds base address)"]:::ptr O2 -.contains.-> InnerStruct Ptr -- "p -> payload.code" --> I1 Ptr -- "p -> payload.val" --> I2 classDef fld fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd classDef inner fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 classDef ptr fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457 ``` ## 4.4 Self-Referential Structure (Linked List Node) ```mermaid flowchart LR N1["Node 1
data=10
next --->"]:::node N2["Node 2
data=20
next --->"]:::node N3["Node 3
data=30
next = NULL"]:::node N1 -- "next ptr" --> N2 N2 -- "next ptr" --> N3 style N1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style N2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style N3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Define a structure in C. How is it different from an array?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A structure is a user-defined data type that groups variables of **dissimilar data types** under one name. It is declared using the `struct` keyword. **Difference from array:** - **Array:** Collection of *homogeneous* elements accessed by index; size is fixed at compile time; name represents the base address. - **Structure:** Collection of *heterogeneous* members accessed by name (dot/arrow operator); may contain arrays, pointers, or other structures; name represents the entire block. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** *What is the difference between the dot (`.`) and arrow (`->`) operators in structures?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Dot operator (`.`):** Used to access a member of a *structure variable* directly. Example: `s.rollNo`. - **Arrow operator (`->`):** Used to access a member through a *pointer to a structure*. Example: `p->rollNo`. It is equivalent to `(*p).rollNo`. The compiler translates `p->m` into a load from the address `p + offset_of(m)`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model] **(a)** Explain the concept of a *nested structure* in C with a suitable example. Write a program to store and display the details of **5 students** where each student record contains `rollNo`, `name`, `dateOfBirth` (a nested structure with `day`, `month`, `year`), and `cgpa`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss *structure padding* and *alignment* in ARM-based embedded C. Compute `sizeof` of the following structure on a 32-bit ARM system and justify your answer. **(7 marks)** ```c struct Data { char ch; int num; char tag; float val; }; ``` #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) A **nested structure** is a structure defined as a member of another structure. It is used to model real-world hierarchical data. **Program:** ```c #include struct Date { int day; int month; int year; }; struct Student { int rollNo; char name[30]; struct Date dateOfBirth; // Nested structure float cgpa; }; int main(void) { struct Student s[5]; int i; for (i = 0; i < 5; i++) { printf("Enter roll, name, DOB(d m y), cgpa for student %d: ", i+1); scanf("%d %s %d %d %d %f", &s[i].rollNo, s[i].name, &s[i].dateOfBirth.day, &s[i].dateOfBirth.month, &s[i].dateOfBirth.year, &s[i].cgpa); } printf("\n--- Student Records ---\n"); for (i = 0; i < 5; i++) { printf("Roll:%d Name:%s DOB:%02d-%02d-%04d CGPA:%.2f\n", s[i].rollNo, s[i].name, s[i].dateOfBirth.day, s[i].dateOfBirth.month, s[i].dateOfBirth.year, s[i].cgpa); } return 0; } ``` **Key points (valuation ready):** - The inner `struct Date` is declared first. - Access pattern: `s[i].dateOfBirth.day` (drill down using dots). - Memory: `s` is an array of 5 `struct Student`; size = `5 * sizeof(struct Student)`. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Padding & Alignment Theory:** On a 32-bit ARM core, the compiler aligns members to their *natural boundary* to enable single-cycle word-aligned loads/stores. If a member's natural alignment is not satisfied at its current offset, the compiler inserts **padding bytes**. **Computation for `struct Data` on 32-bit ARM:** | Offset | Member | Size | Padding | Cumulative Offset | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | `ch` (char, align=1) | 1 | 0 | 1 | | 1–3 | — | — | 3 | 4 | | 4–7 | `num` (int, align=4) | 4 | 0 | 8 | | 8 | `tag` (char) | 1 | 0 | 9 | | 9–11 | — | — | 3 | 12 | | 12–15 | `val` (float, align=4) | 4 | 0 | 16 | | 16–19 | trailing padding | — | 4 | 20 | $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sizeof(struct Data)} &= 1 + 3 + 4 + 1 + 3 + 4 + 4 \\ &= 20 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Justification:** The total is rounded up to a multiple of the largest alignment (4 bytes) to guarantee that arrays of `struct Data` remain properly aligned. Reordering the members as `{int num; float val; char ch; char tag;}` would reduce the size to **12 bytes**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **memory layout of a self-referential structure** used to implement a singly linked list. Write a C program to insert a node at the *beginning* and *end* of the list, each node holding an integer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain **bit-fields** in C structures. How are they used to model ARM Cortex-M **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** such as the `GPIO PORT` direction register? Give an example. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) A *self-referential* structure contains a pointer to another instance of the same structure. ```c struct Node { int data; // payload struct Node *next; // pointer to next node }; ``` **Memory Layout (each node):** ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Node["struct Node (Size = 8 bytes on 32-bit ARM)"] D["int data
Offset 0, Size 4"]:::m N["struct Node *next
Offset 4, Size 4"]:::m end classDef m fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b ``` **Program:** ```c #include #include struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; void insertBegin(struct Node **head, int val) { struct Node *n = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); n->data = val; n->next = *head; *head = n; } void insertEnd(struct Node **head, int val) { struct Node *n = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); n->data = val; n->next = NULL; if (*head == NULL) { *head = n; return; } struct Node *t = *head; while (t->next != NULL) t = t->next; t->next = n; } void display(struct Node *head) { while (head != NULL) { printf("%d -> ", head->data); head = head->next; } printf("NULL\n"); } int main(void) { struct Node *head = NULL; insertEnd(&head, 10); insertEnd(&head, 20); insertBegin(&head, 5); display(head); // 5 -> 10 -> 20 -> NULL return 0; } ``` **Key points:** - `**head` is used (pointer to pointer) so head-pointer modifications in callee reflect in caller. - Each node is dynamically allocated on the heap (`malloc`). - `->` operator is used for member access via pointer. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Bit-fields** allow packing of boolean/numeric members into a specified number of bits within a single storage unit (usually `unsigned int`). They save memory and map cleanly onto hardware registers. **Generic syntax:** ```c struct { unsigned int FIELD_A : N1; unsigned int FIELD_B : N2; }; ``` **Example — Modelling an ARM GPIO Direction Register (8 pins per port):** ```c typedef struct { unsigned int PIN0 : 1; // bit 0 unsigned int PIN1 : 1; // bit 1 unsigned int PIN2 : 1; unsigned int PIN3 : 1; unsigned int PIN4 : 1; unsigned int PIN5 : 1; unsigned int PIN6 : 1; unsigned int PIN7 : 1; unsigned int RSV : 24; // reserved upper 24 bits } GPIO_DIR_Type; #define GPIO0_DIR (*(volatile GPIO_DIR_Type *)0xE0028008) void SetPinsAsOutput(unsigned char mask) { GPIO0_DIR.PIN0 = (mask >> 0) & 1; GPIO0_DIR.PIN1 = (mask >> 1) & 1; // ... etc } ``` **Key concepts:** - Bit-fields let you *symbolically* read or write individual bits without manual masking. - The `volatile` qualifier prevents the compiler from optimizing away repeated hardware reads. - Reading a bit-field wider than the bus may require read-modify-write (handled by the compiler). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing `(*p).member` with `*p.member`** — the latter is a syntax error. > 2. **Forgetting trailing padding** — many compute `1+3+4+1+3+4 = 16` and forget to round to 20. KTU key insists on *total size = multiple of largest alignment*. > 3. **Using `.` instead of `->`** through a pointer — common runtime error. > 4. **Not initialising nested members** — only the outer struct's first member may be partially initialised in C90. > 5. **Ignoring `volatile`** in SFR-mapped structures — ARM compiler may cache register values, breaking I/O. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Structure** = heterogeneous, user-defined, aggregate data type. - Template vs Variable: Declaration creates a *template* (0 bytes); variable creation allocates memory. - **Access operators:** `.` (variable), `->` (pointer). They are **not interchangeable** in syntax but equivalent in effect via `(*p).m`. - **Size with padding:** Always equal to `sum of member sizes + internal padding + trailing padding` rounded up to the largest member's alignment. - **Nested structures** model hierarchical real-world data (e.g., student → DOB). - **Self-referential structures** are the foundation of linked lists, trees, and dynamic data structures. - **Arrays of structures** allow indexed collections of records (e.g., student database). - **Bit-fields** are essential for SFR mapping in ARM Cortex-M firmware. - **`typedef`** is widely used with structures to avoid writing `struct` repeatedly (e.g., `typedef struct { ... } GPIO_TypeDef;`). - **CMSIS convention** uses structures to overlay memory-mapped peripherals. - **Padding optimisation** by member reordering is a KTU favourite in Part B questions. - **Memory hierarchy on ARM:** Stack (auto) < Heap (malloc) < Global/Static (BSS/DATA). - Always use **`volatile`** when mapping a struct onto a hardware register address.

topics.title: Packed Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Packed Structures # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Packed Structure** in the context of ARM Architecture and C programming is a user-defined aggregate data type in which the compiler is explicitly instructed to **suppress the natural byte-alignment padding** that is normally inserted between data members. By invoking compiler-specific attributes (such as `__attribute__((packed))` in GCC/ARM GCC) or preprocessor directives (such as `#pragma pack(1)`), the structure is laid out in memory as a **contiguous, byte-tight sequence** with no inserted filler bytes. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, packed structures are classified under the *Advanced C Constructs for Embedded Programming* cluster of Module 1, and they are treated as a **hardware-oriented memory optimization technique** essential for designing deterministic, register-compatible data layouts on ARM Cortex-M and ARM Cortex-A based target systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEECT525, Module 1):** > Packed structures are listed as a sub-topic under *"Advanced C Data Types for ARM Programming"*. The expected outcome (mapped to **CO1: Apply C constructs for ARM-based embedded application development**) is that the student must be able to *declare, analyse, and deploy packed structures for hardware-register mapping and communication protocol framing*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tetris Block" Intuition Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a journey: - A **normal (non-packed) structure** behaves like a traveler who is *allergic to wasted space but still insists on organizing shirts and shoes in neat rectangular blocks*. Even if a small item (say, a `char`, which is only **1 byte**) could fit snugly after a previous item, the compiler will not let it occupy the leftover gap. Instead, it inserts invisible "air-bubble" padding bytes just to keep every member starting at a "round" address. The suitcase is technically not full, but you cannot squeeze anything more in. - A **packed structure**, on the other hand, behaves like a **Tetris master** who rotates and slides every piece (no matter how small or oddly shaped) into the smallest possible gap. There are no air-bubbles, no wasted corners. The suitcase becomes *completely filled* with zero gaps. The trade-off? It took the Tetris master longer to arrange the pieces, and pulling out one piece might shake the others. Likewise, on the ARM processor, packed structures are **slower to access** and can cause **unaligned memory access faults** on certain ARM cores (notably the strict-aligned ARMv7-M profile). ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Values The following bold values represent the canonical numbers you must memorize for KTU examinations: - **1 byte** = **8 bits** = the smallest addressable unit on virtually all ARM cores. - **Natural alignment** of a member = `sizeof(member)` rounded **up to the next power of two** boundary (e.g., a 4-byte `int` is naturally aligned on a 4-byte boundary, i.e., addresses divisible by 4). - **Alignment of a packed structure** = **1 byte** (forced unaligned packing). - **GCC attribute token**: `__attribute__((packed))`. - **Keil/ARMCC pragma**: `#pragma pack(1)`. > [!NOTE] > **Core takeaway:** Packed structures trade *memory compactness* and *hardware register compatibility* for *potential unaligned access penalties* and *possible bus faults* on ARM cores that enforce strict alignment. The engineer must consciously choose packing only when the protocol or hardware register map demands it. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Background: Why Does Padding Exist at All? ARM processors — like all modern RISC architectures — fetch data from memory in **word-sized (4-byte) or halfword-sized (2-byte) chunks** for efficiency. When a 4-byte `int` is placed at a memory address that is **not a multiple of 4**, the CPU must perform **two memory bus cycles** to read it: one for the lower half, one for the upper half. This is called an **unaligned access**. To avoid this slowdown, the C compiler (when compiling for ARM by default) **inserts padding bytes** so that every multi-byte member begins on its natural alignment boundary. ## 2.2 Operational Steps — What Happens When You Mark a Structure as Packed Here is the structured logic that the ARM C compiler follows when it encounters a packed structure declaration: 1. **Lexical Analysis & Attribute Recognition:** The compiler scans the `struct` declaration. It detects either the GCC attribute `__attribute__((packed))` placed after the closing brace, or a preceding `#pragma pack(1)` directive. 2. **Alignment Override:** The compiler sets the structure's effective alignment to **1 byte**, overriding the default natural alignment. 3. **Sequential Memory Allocation:** Members are placed back-to-back in memory. The offset of each member equals the sum of the sizes of all preceding members (no padding is inserted). 4. **No Tail Padding:** Unlike standard structs, packed structs do not pad the total size to a multiple of the largest member's alignment.
