# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the General Purpose Registers in ARM Cortex-M and explain the role of R13, R14, and R15.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The ARM Cortex-M provides **13 General Purpose Registers**: **R0 to R12**, each of **32 bits**.
- **R13 (SP — Stack Pointer):** It is *banked* into two physical registers — **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** and **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)**. The MSP is used after reset and during exception handling; the PSP is used by user (unprivileged) applications.
- **R14 (LR — Link Register):** Holds the **return address** after a function call (BL/BLX) or stores the special **EXC_RETURN** value on exception entry.
- **R15 (PC — Program Counter):** Holds the address of the **next instruction to fetch**. It is always **Thumb-aligned (even)** and reads back as `current + 4` due to pipelining.
- (1 mark for listing, 1 mark for SP, 1 mark for LR/PC.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is xPSR? List the sub-registers of xPSR and state the significance of the N, Z, C, V flags.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **xPSR (Program Status Register)** is a **32-bit composite register** that combines the application, interrupt, and execution status of the processor.
- It has **three sub-registers**:
  - **APSR (Application PSR)** — contains condition flags.
  - **IPSR (Interrupt PSR)** — contains the current exception number.
  - **EPSR (Execution PSR)** — contains the IT/ICI state and the Thumb (T) bit.
- The four main condition flags are:
  - **N (Negative)** — set when the result's MSB is 1.
  - **Z (Zero)** — set when the result is zero.
  - **C (Carry)** — set on unsigned overflow or borrow-not.
  - **V (Overflow)** — set on signed overflow.
- These flags drive **conditional branching** in assembly and influence **compiler code generation** decisions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Draw the programmer's model of the ARM Cortex-M3 processor and explain the role of each register group. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between MSP and PSP. Under what condition does a Thread-mode program use the PSP? Describe the role of the CONTROL register in selecting between them. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Programmer's Model of Cortex-M3 (7 Marks):**

| Register | Role |
|---|---|
| **R0 – R12** | General Purpose Registers for data, addresses, and temporaries. R0–R3 hold arguments; R4–R11 are callee-saved. |
| **R13 (SP)** | Stack Pointer — banked as MSP and PSP. |
| **R14 (LR)** | Link Register — stores subroutine return address / EXC_RETURN. |
| **R15 (PC)** | Program Counter — points to the next instruction. |
| **xPSR** | Composite Program Status Register (APSR + IPSR + EPSR). |
| **PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI** | Exception masking registers. |
| **CONTROL** | Selects active SP and sets Thread-mode privilege. |

A clean labelled block diagram should accompany this answer. (Award 1 mark for the diagram, 1 mark for GPRs, 1 mark for SP, 1 mark for LR/PC, 1 mark for xPSR, 2 marks for special registers and overall organization.)

**(b) MSP vs PSP and the CONTROL Register (7 Marks):**

- **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** is the **default** Stack Pointer after reset. It is used in **Handler mode** (all exceptions) and optionally in Thread mode.
- **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** is intended for **application threads** in Thread mode. Using the PSP allows an RTOS to **isolate each task's stack** for safety and debugging.
- A Thread-mode program uses the PSP when the **CONTROL[1]** bit (also called **SPSEL**) is set to **1**.
- The **CONTROL register** has the following structure:
  - **CONTROL[0]** (nPRIV) — When set, Thread mode is **unprivileged**.
  - **CONTROL[1]** (SPSEL) — When set, **PSP** is used in Thread mode; when cleared, **MSP** is used.
- In **Handler mode**, CONTROL[1] is **always 0** (MSP is forced) regardless of software writes — this is enforced by hardware.
- The processor automatically saves and restores the active SP and LR during exception entry/exit using the **EXC_RETURN** mechanism, so the application context is preserved seamlessly.

(Award 2 marks for MSP/PSP definition, 2 marks for the use condition, 3 marks for the CONTROL register details.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of the bit-band region in ARM Cortex-M3/M4. Derive the formula to compute the alias address for a given bit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A peripheral register is located at address `0x4002_103C`. Compute the bit-band alias address for **bit 6** of this register. If you write `0x00000001` to the computed alias address, what is the effect on the peripheral register? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bit-Band Concept and Formula Derivation (7 Marks):**

- **Bit-banding** is a memory-mapping technique where every **single bit** in a designated **1 MB bit-band region** is mapped to a unique **32-bit word** in a 32 MB **alias region**.
- The two bit-band regions in Cortex-M3/M4 are:
  - **SRAM region:** `0x2000_0000 – 0x200F_FFFF` ↔ Alias `0x2200_0000 – 0x23FF_FFFF`
  - **Peripheral region:** `0x4000_0000 – 0x400F_FFFF` ↔ Alias `0x4200_0000 – 0x43FF_FFFF`
- **Derivation of the alias-address formula:**
  - Let the target address be $A_{target}$ in the bit-band region.
  - The byte offset from the region base is $B = A_{target} - A_{base}$.
  - Each byte holds 8 bits, and each bit maps to a 32-bit (4-byte) word. So the bit at position $N$ in byte $B$ consumes the address range $A_{base} + (B \times 32) + (N \times 4)$ to $A_{base} + (B \times 32) + (N \times 4) + 3$.
  - Therefore, the **master formula** is:

$$
A_{alias} = A_{alias\_base} + (B \times 32) + (N \times 4)
$$

  - For peripheral bit-band, $A_{alias\_base} = 0x4200\,0000$.
- **Engineering benefit:** A single store instruction atomically sets or clears a bit, eliminating the classic read-modify-write race condition in multi-tasking environments.

(Award 2 marks for concept, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for engineering benefit.)

**(b) Numerical Computation and Effect (7 Marks):**

- **Step 1: Compute byte offset B.**

$$
B = 0x4002\,103C - 0x4000\,0000 = 0x0002\,103C = 135228
$$

- **Step 2: Apply formula with $N = 6$.**

$$
A_{alias} = 0x4200\,0000 + (135228 \times 32) + (6 \times 4)
$$
$$
A_{alias} = 0x4200\,0000 + 4\,327\,296 + 24
$$
$$
A_{alias} = 0x4200\,0000 + 4\,327\,320 = 0x4242\_0018
$$

- **Step 3: Effect of writing `0x00000001` to the alias.**

  - The bit-band alias mechanism causes the bus matrix to translate the write to the original bit-band address. Writing `0x00000001` to `0x4242_0018` will **atomically set bit 6** of the peripheral register at `0x4002_103C` to **1**, leaving all other bits unchanged.
  - Writing `0x00000000` would atomically clear bit 6 to 0.

(Award 2 marks for offset, 3 marks for formula substitution, 2 marks for explaining the atomic set/clear effect.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic (Insider View):**
> 1. **Conflating "operating mode" with "privilege level."** They are independent dimensions — a frequent 2-mark trap. Memorize: *Handler mode is always privileged; Thread mode can be either.*
> 2. **Forgetting that PC reads as `current + 4`.** Students who state "PC points to the currently executing instruction" lose the mark.
> 3. **Mixing up R0–R3 (argument/return) and R4–R11 (callee-saved).** In AAPCS questions, misnaming a register's role costs 1 mark each.
> 4. **Incorrect bit-band base.** The peripheral bit-band alias starts at `0x4200_0000`, **not** `0x4200_0000 + region_offset`. Use the formula as-is.
> 5. **Stating that the SP and LR are "banked" in Handler mode.** Only the **SP** is banked (MSP/PSP). The LR in Handler mode stores **EXC_RETURN**, not a return address.
> 6. **Ignoring the 8-byte stack alignment** in AAPCS proofs. Even if the math is right, missing the alignment constraint loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cortex-M core** has **16 core registers** (R0–R15), all **32 bits** wide.
- **R0–R12** = **General Purpose Registers** (13 total).
- **R13 (SP)** is **banked** into **MSP** (reset default) and **PSP** (application).
- **R14 (LR)** = return address or **EXC_RETURN** constant.
- **R15 (PC)** = next instruction address; reads as `current + 4`; always Thumb-aligned.
- **xPSR** is the union of **APSR** (flags: N, Z, C, V, Q), **IPSR** (exception number), and **EPSR** (ICI/IT, T-bit).
- **PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI** are the three exception-masking registers.
- **CONTROL** register has 2 functional bits: **nPRIV (CONTROL[0])** and **SPSEL (CONTROL[1])**.
- **Two modes** — **Thread** and **Handler**; **Two privilege levels** — **Privileged** and **Unprivileged**.
- After **reset** → Thread mode, Privileged, MSP active, all flags cleared.
- **AAPCS rule** — R0–R3 are **caller-saved** (arguments), R4–R11 are **callee-saved**.
- **Bit-band formula** — $A_{alias} = A_{alias\_base} + (B \times 32) + (N \times 4)$ — applies to both SRAM and peripheral bit-band regions.
- **Stack alignment** — SP must be **8-byte aligned** at public function entry.
- **NVIC** can support up to **240 external interrupts** with **3 to 8 priority bits**.
- **EXC_RETURN values** — `0xFFFFFFFE` (return to Thread/MSP), `0xFFFFFFFD` (return to Thread/PSP), `0xFFFFFFF1` (return to Handler — rare).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Pipelining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Instruction Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture whereby multiple instructions are overlapped in execution. A pipeline is a set of data-processing elements (stages) connected in series, where the output of one stage is the input of the next. The execution of an instruction is decomposed into a sequence of well-defined sub-operations (stages), and successive instructions enter the pipeline before the previous ones have completed, so multiple instructions are in different stages of execution simultaneously.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, pipelining is treated as the central performance-enhancing mechanism exploited by all ARM cores. The ARM architecture evolved from a simple **3-stage pipeline** (ARM7TDMI) to a **5-stage pipeline** (ARM9) to a **13-stage superscalar pipeline** (ARM11/Cortex), making the study of pipeline dynamics essential.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key KTU Definitions to Memorize:**
> - **Latency (L):** Time taken to complete one instruction from start to end.
> - **Throughput (TP):** Number of instructions completed per unit time.
> - **Cycle Per Instruction (CPI):** Average clock cycles required per instruction.
> - **Speedup Factor (S):** Ratio of execution time without pipelining to execution time with pipelining.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Laundry Pipeline

Imagine you have **4 loads of laundry** and 4 stages: **Wash → Dry → Fold → Stow**.

- **Non-Pipelined Approach (Sequential):** You wash load 1, dry it, fold it, stow it → then start load 2. Total time = 4 × (Wash+Dry+Fold+Stow) = 4T per load × 4 loads = inefficient.
- **Pipelined Approach:** As soon as load 1 finishes washing, you put it in the dryer **AND** simultaneously put load 2 in the washer. The "pipeline" keeps all 4 machines busy.

This is exactly how an instruction pipeline works — while one instruction is being **Fetched**, the next is being **Decoded**, and another is being **Executed** in parallel.

## 1.3 Physical Performance Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Standard Pipeline Metrics (Bold for Board Emphasis):**
> - **Ideal CPI = 1** (one instruction completes per clock cycle in a perfect pipeline).
> - **Maximum Speedup = k**, where k is the number of pipeline stages (Amdahl's bound).
> - **Pipeline Cycle Time (τ)** = Maximum stage delay + Register delay.
> - **Pipeline Register Delay (d)** = Flip-flop setup/hold time between stages.

## 1.4 Why Pipelining Matters in ARM

ARM processors are **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** machines. RISC philosophy mandates:
1. Fixed-length instructions (32-bit in ARM).
2. Simple addressing modes.
3. Register-to-register operations.

These properties make ARM **ideally suited** for pipelining because every instruction has a predictable, uniform format. If instructions were variable-length (like x86), pipeline stages would be highly irregular and overlapping would be inefficient.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of a Standard Pipeline

A classic 5-stage RISC pipeline decomposes instruction execution as follows:

| Stage | Mnemonic | Function | Hardware Resources |
|:------|:---------|:---------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **IF** (Instruction Fetch) | Read instruction from memory using PC | Instruction Memory, PC |
| 2 | **ID** (Instruction Decode) | Decode opcode, read register file | Decoder, Register File |
| 3 | **EX** (Execute) | ALU operation, effective address calc | ALU |
| 4 | **MEM** (Memory Access) | Read/Write data memory | Data Memory |
| 5 | **WB** (Write Back) | Write result back to register file | Register File |

> [!NOTE]  
> **ARM7TDMI (3-Stage):** IF → ID → EX  
> **ARM9 (5-Stage):** IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB  
> **ARM Cortex-A8 (13-Stage):** IF1-IF4 → ID1-ID3 → EX1-EX6  

## 2.2 The Theory of Operation

1. **Temporal Overlap:** While instruction N is in the EX stage, instruction N+1 is in ID, and N+2 is in IF. They occupy different physical resources simultaneously.
2. **Pipeline Registers:** Latches/flip-flops separate stages. Data + control signals are passed forward each clock edge.
3. **Steady State:** After the pipeline fills (after k cycles), **one instruction completes per cycle** → CPI = 1.
4. **Drain Phase:** After the last instruction enters, k-1 more cycles are required to drain the pipeline.

## 2.3 Pipeline Hazards (The "Why" Behind Stalls)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> A **hazard** is a condition that prevents the next instruction from executing in the designated clock cycle. Hazards force the pipeline to **stall (insert bubbles)**, increasing effective CPI above 1.

### A. Structural Hazards
- **Cause:** Hardware resource conflict (two stages need the same resource in the same cycle).
- **Example:** Single memory port accessed by IF and MEM in the same cycle.
- **Solution:** Duplicate resources (Harvard architecture: separate I-cache and D-cache — **this is exactly why ARM uses Harvard architecture**).

### B. Data Hazards
Occur when instructions depend on the results of previous instructions still in the pipeline.

| Type | Name | Description | Example |
|:-----|:-----|:------------|:--------|
| **RAW** | Read After Write (True Dependency) | Instruction j tries to read a source before instruction i writes it | ADD R1, R2, R3 ; SUB R4, R1, R5 |
| **WAR** | Write After Read (Anti-Dependency) | Instruction j writes a register before i reads it | Impossible in in-order pipelines |
| **WAW** | Write After Write (Output Dependency) | Two instructions write the same register | Impossible in single-issue pipelines |

### C. Control Hazards (Branch Hazards)
- **Cause:** PC update depends on a branch outcome that hasn't been computed yet.
- **Penalty:** Usually **1–3 clock cycles** wasted.
- **Solutions:**
  1. **Branch Prediction** (Static/Dynamic).
  2. **Delayed Branch** (compiler inserts useful instruction in delay slot).
  3. **Speculative Execution.**
  4. **Branch Target Buffer (BTB).**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Memorize the following equations — they appear in 14-mark numericals every year.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:------------|
| 1 | Execution Time (Non-pipelined) | $T_{np} = n \times k \times \tau$ | n instructions, k stages, τ stage delay |
| 2 | Execution Time (Pipelined) | $T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \times \tau$ | k cycles to fill + (n-1) cycles in steady state |
| 3 | Speedup | $S = \dfrac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{n \times k}{k + n - 1}$ | Ratio of times |
| 4 | Maximum Speedup (n → ∞) | $S_{max} = k$ | Theoretical upper bound |
| 5 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ | Fraction of time stages are useful |
| 6 | Throughput | $TP = \dfrac{n}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{1}{\tau \left(1 + \dfrac{k-1}{n}\right)}$ | Instructions completed per cycle |
| 7 | Effective CPI | $CPI_{eff} = 1 + \sum(\text{stalls per instr.})$ | With hazards |
| 8 | CPU Time | $T_{CPU} = I \times CPI \times \tau_{clock}$ | Standard CPU time formula |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems:** ARM Cortex-M pipelining enables real-time DSP for audio/codec processing.
- **Mobile Computing:** Branch prediction in ARM Cortex-A series reduces wasted cycles in smartphone OS scheduling.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Low-power pipelined MCUs balance throughput vs. dynamic power ($P \propto C \times V^2 \times f$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pipeline Speedup

**Given:** A processor has **k** pipeline stages, each taking equal time **τ** (assuming balanced stages). We want to execute **n** instructions.

### Step 1: Time without Pipelining
Each instruction must pass through all **k** stages sequentially, so one instruction takes $k \times \tau$ time. For **n** instructions with no overlap:

$$T_{np} = n \times k \times \tau$$

### Step 2: Time with Pipelining
- **Fill phase:** The first instruction must traverse all **k** stages → takes $k \times \tau$.
- **Steady-state phase:** Once the pipeline is full, one new instruction completes every cycle. So the remaining **n − 1** instructions each add only **τ** time.
- **Drain phase:** Already included in the steady-state counting.

$$T_{p} = k \times \tau + (n - 1) \times \tau$$

$$\boxed{T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \times \tau}$$

### Step 3: Speedup Factor

$$S = \dfrac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{n \times k \times \tau}{(k + n - 1) \times \tau}$$

$$S = \dfrac{n \times k}{k + n - 1}$$

### Step 4: Maximum Speedup (Limit as n → ∞)

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \times k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{k}{\dfrac{k}{n} + 1 - \dfrac{1}{n}} = \dfrac{k}{0 + 1 - 0} = k$$

$$\boxed{S_{max} = k}$$

### Step 5: Efficiency

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful time}}{\text{Total time}} = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n \times k / (k + n - 1)}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1: Basic Speedup

**Question:** Consider a non-pipelined processor with a clock cycle time of **25 ns**. It has **4 stages** (IF, ID, EX, WB) with equal delays. We want to execute **100 instructions**. Now we convert it to a **4-stage pipelined processor** with a 1 ns pipeline register delay.

**(a)** Calculate the time to execute 100 instructions without pipelining.
**(b)** Calculate the time with pipelining.
**(c)** Find the speedup factor.
**(d)** Find the efficiency and throughput.

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $k = 4$ stages
- $n = 100$ instructions
- Non-pipelined clock cycle = 25 ns
- Stage delay in pipelined processor: 25/4 = 6.25 ns
- Pipeline register delay: $d = 1$ ns
- Effective cycle time: $\tau = 6.25 + 1 = 7.25$ ns

#### (a) Non-Pipelined Time

$$T_{np} = n \times k \times \tau_{non-pipe}$$

$$T_{np} = 100 \times 4 \times 25 \text{ ns}$$

$$\boxed{T_{np} = 10000 \text{ ns} = 10 \text{ µs}}$$

#### (b) Pipelined Time

$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \times \tau$$

$$T_{p} = (4 + 100 - 1) \times 7.25 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{p} = 103 \times 7.25$$

$$\boxed{T_{p} = 746.75 \text{ ns} \approx 0.747 \text{ µs}}$$

#### (c) Speedup

$$S = \dfrac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{10000}{746.75}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 13.39}$$

#### (d) Efficiency and Throughput

$$\eta = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1} = \dfrac{100}{103} = 0.9709 = 97.09\%$$

$$TP = \dfrac{n}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{100}{746.75 \text{ ns}} = 1.339 \times 10^{8} \text{ instr/sec} \approx 134 \text{ MIPS}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2: Hazards Impact

**Question:** A **5-stage** pipelined ARM processor runs at **100 MHz**. Of all instructions, **20% are branch instructions** and **30% of branches are mispredicted**. Each misprediction causes a **2-cycle stall**. Data hazards cause an average of **0.4 stalls per instruction**.

**(a)** Find the base CPI (ideal pipeline).
**(b)** Calculate the effective CPI with hazards.
**(c)** Calculate the MIPS rating.

### Solution:

#### (a) Base CPI

For an ideal pipeline with no hazards:

$$\boxed{CPI_{base} = 1}$$

#### (b) Effective CPI

Branch stall cycles per instruction:
$$\text{Branch stalls} = 0.20 \times 0.30 \times 2 = 0.12 \text{ cycles/instr}$$

Data hazard stalls per instruction:
$$\text{Data stalls} = 0.4 \text{ cycles/instr}$$

Total stall cycles per instruction:
$$\text{Total stalls} = 0.12 + 0.4 = 0.52$$

$$CPI_{eff} = 1 + 0.52 = \boxed{1.52}$$

#### (c) MIPS Rating

Clock frequency: $f = 100 \text{ MHz}$

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI_{eff} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{6}}{1.52 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MIPS} \approx 65.79}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | ARM7 (3-Stage) | ARM9 (5-Stage) | ARM11 (8-Stage) |
|:--------|:---------------|:---------------|:-----------------|
| **Max Clock** | ~133 MHz | ~300 MHz | ~1 GHz |
| **CPI (Ideal)** | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| **Branch Penalty** | 2 cycles | 3 cycles | 4-5 cycles |
| **Memory Ports** | 1 (Unified) | 2 (Harvard) | 2+ |
| **Power Efficiency** | High | Medium | Lower (but faster) |
| **Typical Use** | Embedded/MCU | Mobile baseband | Smartphones |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic 5-Stage Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IF Stage\nInstruction Fetch\nPC + 4"]:::stage1
    B["ID Stage\nInstruction Decode\nRead Registers"]:::stage2
    C["EX Stage\nALU Operation\nBranch Resolve"]:::stage3
    D["MEM Stage\nData Memory\nRead or Write"]:::stage4
    E["WB Stage\nWrite Back\nTo Register File"]:::stage5

    A ==> B
    B ==> C
    C ==> D
    D ==> E

    PR1["Pipeline\nRegister 1"]:::reg
    PR2["Pipeline\nRegister 2"]:::reg
    PR3["Pipeline\nRegister 3"]:::reg
    PR4["Pipeline\nRegister 4"]:::reg

    A -.-> PR1 -.-> B
    B -.-> PR2 -.-> C
    C -.-> PR3 -.-> D
    D -.-> PR4 -.-> E

    classDef stage1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stage5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram (Space-Time Chart)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Instruction Pipeline Timing Diagram (k=5, n=6)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Instruction I1
    IF1 :a1, 0, 1
    ID1 :a2, 1, 1
    EX1 :a3, 2, 1
    MEM1 :a4, 3, 1
    WB1 :a5, 4, 1

    section Instruction I2
    IF2 :b1, 1, 1
    ID2 :b2, 2, 1
    EX2 :b3, 3, 1
    MEM2 :b4, 4, 1
    WB2 :b5, 5, 1

    section Instruction I3
    IF3 :c1, 2, 1
    ID3 :c2, 3, 1
    EX3 :c3, 4, 1
    MEM3 :c4, 5, 1
    WB3 :c5, 6, 1

    section Instruction I4
    IF4 :d1, 3, 1
    ID4 :d2, 4, 1
    EX4 :d3, 5, 1
    MEM4 :d4, 6, 1
    WB4 :d5, 7, 1

    section Instruction I5
    IF5 :e1, 4, 1
    ID5 :e2, 5, 1
    EX5 :e3, 6, 1
    MEM5 :e4, 7, 1
    WB5 :e5, 8, 1

    section Instruction I6
    IF6 :f1, 5, 1
    ID6 :f2, 6, 1
    EX6 :f3, 7, 1
    MEM6 :f4, 8, 1
    WB6 :f5, 9, 1
```

> [!NOTE]  
> Observe how **Cycle 0–4** is the fill phase (one instruction completes only at cycle 4). From **cycle 5 onwards**, one instruction completes per cycle — this is the **steady state**.

## 4.3 Pipeline Hazards Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HAZ["Pipeline Hazards"]:::root

    SH["Structural Hazards\n(Hardware Conflict)"]:::h1
    DH["Data Hazards\n(Data Dependency)"]:::h1
    CH["Control Hazards\n(Branch/Jump)"]:::h1

    RAW["RAW: Read After Write\nTrue Dependency"]:::h2
    WAR["WAR: Write After Read\nAnti-Dependency"]:::h2
    WAW["WAW: Write After Write\nOutput Dependency"]:::h2

    HAZ --> SH
    HAZ --> DH
    HAZ --> CH
    DH --> RAW
    DH --> WAR
    DH --> WAW

    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef h1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef h2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 ARM Pipeline Evolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["ARM7TDMI\n3-Stage"]:::a7
    A2["ARM9\n5-Stage"]:::a9
    A3["ARM11\n8-Stage"]:::a11
    A4["Cortex-A8\n13-Stage\nDual Issue"]:::ca8

    A1 -->|"+2 stages"| A2
    A2 -->|"+3 stages"| A3
    A3 -->|"Superscalar"| A4

    A1 -.- NOTE1["IF → ID → EX\nSingle Mem Port"]:::note
    A2 -.- NOTE2["+ MEM + WB\nHarvard I/D Cache"]:::note
    A3 -.- NOTE3["+ Issue + Write\n+ Prefetch stages"]:::note
    A4 -.- NOTE4["Out-of-Order\nSpeculative"]:::note

    classDef a7 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef a9 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef a11 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ca8 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef note fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#888,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

## 4.5 Data Hazard Forwarding Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ID["ID Stage\nReads R1, R2"]:::s1
    EX["EX Stage\nALU Computes A + B"]:::s2
    MEM["MEM Stage"]:::s3
    WB["WB Stage\nWrites R1"]:::s4

    FWD["Forwarding Mux\n(Selects ALU result)"]:::fwd

    EX -- "ALU result\n(before WB)" --> FWD
    FWD -- "Forwarded\nto EX input" --> EX

    classDef s1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef s2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef s3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef s4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fwd fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Forwarding (Bypassing)** is the primary hardware technique to eliminate RAW data hazards without stalling. The ALU result is routed **back** to the EX stage input of the next instruction, eliminating the need to wait for WB.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define instruction pipelining. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Instruction pipelining is a technique of decomposing the execution of an instruction into a sequence of well-defined sub-stages (such as IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) and overlapping the execution of multiple instructions so that each stage operates on a different instruction simultaneously. It improves instruction throughput without reducing the execution time of individual instructions.

**Advantages (1 Mark, any two):**
1. **Increased Throughput:** Multiple instructions complete per cycle, raising MIPS rating.
2. **Lower Latency per Instruction:** Once the pipeline is full, ideally one instruction finishes per clock.
3. **Better Resource Utilization:** All functional units remain active continuously.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between structural hazard and data hazard in pipelining.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Structural Hazard | Data Hazard |
|:-------|:------------------|:------------|
| **Cause** | Hardware resource conflict between overlapping instructions | True data dependency between instructions |
| **Example** | Single memory port accessed by IF and MEM simultaneously | ADD R1, R2, R3 followed by SUB R4, R1, R5 |
| **Solution** | Duplicate hardware (e.g., separate I-cache & D-cache) | Forwarding/bypassing or operand interleaving |
| **Frequency** | Less common in modern Harvard ARM | Most common hazard type |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the formula for speedup of **n** instructions in a **k-stage** pipeline with the simple ratio of stage counts. The full expression $S = \dfrac{nk}{k+n-1}$ must be shown explicitly. **(Lose 2 marks if skipped)**
> - **Always convert** time units (ns → µs) consistently — wrong units are the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks in numericals.
> - **State assumptions** (e.g., balanced stages, equal stage delay) before substituting values. **(Lose 1 mark if missing)**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the 5 stages of a RISC pipeline with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of pipeline registers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pipelined processor has **5 stages** with stage delays of **30 ns, 25 ns, 35 ns, 28 ns, and 32 ns** respectively. Pipeline register delay is **5 ns**. For executing **50 instructions**, calculate: **(i)** Clock cycle time **(ii)** Total execution time **(iii)** Speedup vs. non-pipelined equivalent **(iv)** Throughput. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a): Five Stages of RISC Pipeline (7 Marks)

The **5-stage RISC pipeline** is the standard architecture adopted in ARM9TDMI and is foundational to modern processors. The five stages are:

**1. Instruction Fetch (IF) — 1 Mark**
- Reads the next instruction from instruction memory using the **Program Counter (PC)**.
- PC is incremented by 4 (since ARM instructions are 32-bit = 4 bytes).
- Output → IR (Instruction Register) and PC+4.

**2. Instruction Decode (ID) — 1 Mark**
- Decodes the opcode to identify the operation.
- Reads source operand values from the **register file** (R0–R15).
- Performs **sign extension** on immediate values.
- Output → Decoded control signals and operand values.

**3. Execute (EX) — 1 Mark**
- The **ALU** performs the required operation (ADD, SUB, AND, etc.).
- Computes the **effective address** for load/store instructions.
- Resolves **branch conditions** (sets flags in CPSR for ARM).
- Output → ALU result and branch target address.

**4. Memory Access (MEM) — 1 Mark**
- For **load** instructions → reads data from data memory using the effective address.
- For **store** instructions → writes data to data memory.
- For other instructions → this stage is a **NOP (no operation)**.

**5. Write Back (WB) — 1 Mark**
- Writes the result (from ALU or memory) back to the **destination register** in the register file.
- Completes the instruction.

**Role of Pipeline Registers — 2 Marks:**
Pipeline registers are the **flip-flops placed between consecutive stages** that:
- **Store intermediate results** at the end of each stage.
- **Isolate stages** so they operate independently and concurrently.
- **Carry control signals and data** (PC+4, register values, ALU opcode, etc.) to the next stage.
- **Synchronize** the entire pipeline to the global clock.
- **Hold the instruction** while it is being processed by the next stage.

Without pipeline registers, the output of one stage would disturb the input of the next, breaking the parallel operation.

**Block Diagram (Refer Section 4.1 of these notes):**

```
[IF] → [PR1] → [ID] → [PR2] → [EX] → [PR3] → [MEM] → [PR4] → [WB]
```

#### Part (b): Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Stage delays: 30, 25, 35, 28, 32 ns
- Pipeline register delay: $d = 5$ ns
- $k = 5$ stages, $n = 50$ instructions

##### (i) Clock Cycle Time (2 Marks)

The clock cycle time is determined by the **slowest stage + register delay**.

$$\text{Stage delay} = 35 \text{ ns (maximum)}$$

$$\tau = \text{Max stage delay} + d = 35 + 5$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 40 \text{ ns}}$$

##### (ii) Total Execution Time (2 Marks)

$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \times \tau$$

$$T_{p} = (5 + 50 - 1) \times 40 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{p} = 54 \times 40$$

$$\boxed{T_{p} = 2160 \text{ ns} = 2.16 \text{ µs}}$$

##### (iii) Speedup (2 Marks)

Non-pipelined execution time: In a non-pipelined design, each instruction takes the sum of all stage delays plus register delay per stage.

$$T_{np} = n \times \sum(\text{stage delays} + d)$$

$$T_{np} = 50 \times (30 + 25 + 35 + 28 + 32 + 5 \times 5)$$

$$T_{np} = 50 \times (150 + 25) = 50 \times 175$$

$$T_{np} = 8750 \text{ ns} = 8.75 \text{ µs}$$

$$S = \dfrac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{8750}{2160}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 4.05}$$

##### (iv) Throughput (1 Mark)

$$TP = \dfrac{n}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{50}{2160 \text{ ns}} = 23.15 \times 10^{6} \text{ instr/sec}$$

$$\boxed{TP \approx 23.15 \text{ MIPS}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain **structural, data, and control hazards** in pipelining with examples. How does **forwarding** resolve data hazards? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a **4-stage pipelined** ARM processor (IF, ID, EX, WB) with a clock cycle of **20 ns**. A program has **200 instructions**, of which **25% are branches**. Branch misprediction rate is **15%** and each misprediction causes a **1-cycle stall**. Data hazards cause an average of **0.3 stalls per instruction**. Calculate: **(i)** Effective CPI **(ii)** Total execution time **(iii)** Speedup if the same program were run on a non-pipelined processor with average instruction time of 75 ns. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a): Pipeline Hazards Explained (7 Marks)

**1. Structural Hazards (2 Marks)**
These occur when two pipeline stages need the **same hardware resource** in the same cycle. 
- **Example:** In a processor with unified instruction and data memory, the IF stage and MEM stage both need memory access in the same cycle (when a load instruction is in MEM and the next instruction is in IF).
- **Solution:** Use **Harvard architecture** with separate instruction and data caches, allowing simultaneous access.

**2. Data Hazards (2.5 Marks)**
These occur when an instruction's execution depends on the result of a **previous instruction still in the pipeline**.
- **Example:** 
  ```
  ADD R1, R2, R3    ; Writes R1
  SUB R4, R1, R5    ; Reads R1 — but R1 is not yet written!
  ```
  This is a **RAW (Read After Write)** hazard — the most common type.

- **Types:** RAW (True), WAR (Anti), WAW (Output) — explained in Section 2.3.

**3. Control Hazards (1.5 Marks)**
These are caused by **branch and jump instructions**. The PC may change based on a condition that hasn't been evaluated yet.
- **Example:** A conditional branch is fetched in cycle 1, but its outcome is known only after the EX stage (cycle 3). The instructions fetched in cycles 2 and 3 may be from the wrong path and must be discarded.
- **Penalty:** Typically 1–3 wasted cycles.
- **Solutions:** Branch prediction, delayed branching, speculative execution.

**4. Forwarding (Bypassing) (1 Mark)**
Forwarding is a hardware technique that **routes the output of one pipeline stage directly to the input of an earlier stage**, bypassing the need to wait for the value to be written back to the register file. The ALU result from the EX stage is fed directly back to the EX stage input of the next instruction, eliminating RAW stalls.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $k = 4$, $n = 200$, $\tau = 20$ ns
- Branch instructions: 25%
- Misprediction rate: 15%
- Misprediction penalty: 1 cycle
- Data hazard stalls: 0.3 per instruction

##### (i) Effective CPI (3 Marks)

Branch stall cycles per instruction:
$$\text{Branch stalls} = 0.25 \times 0.15 \times 1 = 0.0375$$

Data hazard stalls per instruction:
$$\text{Data stalls} = 0.3$$

$$CPI_{eff} = 1 + 0.0375 + 0.3 = \boxed{1.3375}$$

##### (ii) Total Execution Time (2 Marks)

$$T_{p} = n \times CPI_{eff} \times \tau$$

$$T_{p} = 200 \times 1.3375 \times 20 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{p} = 200 \times 26.75 \text{ ns} = 5350 \text{ ns}$$

$$\boxed{T_{p} = 5350 \text{ ns} = 5.35 \text{ µs}}$$

##### (iii) Speedup (2 Marks)

$$T_{np} = n \times 75 \text{ ns} = 200 \times 75 = 15000 \text{ ns} = 15 \text{ µs}$$

$$S = \dfrac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{15000}{5350}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 2.80}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often compute $T_{p}$ using $(k+n-1) \times \tau$ even when hazards are present. **The correct formula with hazards is $T_{p} = n \times CPI_{eff} \times \tau$**, since each instruction now takes $CPI_{eff}$ cycles, not 1 cycle. **(Lose 2 marks here if formula is wrong)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- ⭐ **Pipelining definition:** Decompose + overlap multiple instructions across stages.
- ⭐ **Standard 5 stages:** IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB. (ARM7 = 3 stages; ARM9 = 5 stages).
- ⭐ **Pipelined time formula:** $T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \times \tau$ — **do not confuse with non-pipelined**.
- ⭐ **Non-pipelined time formula:** $T_{np} = n \times k \times \tau$ (or sum of stage delays per instruction).
- ⭐ **Speedup formula:** $S = \dfrac{nk}{k+n-1}$; Maximum speedup = **k** (number of stages).
- ⭐ **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{n}{k+n-1}$; ranges from 0 to 1, always < 1.
- ⭐ **Three hazard types:** Structural (resource), Data (dependency), Control (branch).
- ⭐ **Three data dependencies:** RAW (true, most common), WAR (anti), WAW (output).
- ⭐ **Forwarding (bypassing):** Routes ALU output back to ALU input to eliminate RAW stalls.
- ⭐ **Branch prediction:** Reduces control hazard penalty; static or dynamic.
- ⭐ **Effective CPI with hazards:** $CPI = 1 + \text{stall cycles per instruction}$.
- ⭐ **ARM Harvard architecture:** Separate I-cache and D-cache eliminates structural hazards.
- ⭐ **Pipeline registers:** Mandatory between stages; cause register delay $d$ added to clock cycle.
- ⭐ **Cycle time rule:** $\tau = \text{Max(stage delay)} + d$ — determined by slowest stage.
- ⭐ **Clock period in deep pipelines:** Smaller stage delay = higher clock = more power consumption.
- ⭐ **Real-world ARM pipeline depth:** Cortex-A8 has 13 stages; Cortex-A76 has 15+ stages.
- ⭐ **Unit conversions:** 1 µs = 1000 ns; 1 ms = 10⁶ ns. **Always state units in the answer.**
- ⭐ **For numericals:** Always identify $k$, $n$, $\tau$ first, then plug into the right formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Memory Model** of an ARM processor defines the complete logical organization of the addressable memory space, including the address ranges assigned to code (Flash/ROM), data (SRAM), peripherals, and external buses, along with the rules governing data placement, access (alignment, endianness, bit-band operations), and ordering of memory accesses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (Board Exam Favorite):**
> The ARM Cortex-M memory model is a **unified 4 GB linear address space** (`0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`) where code, data, and peripherals share a common address map but reside in architecturally defined regions with specific access permissions and bus behaviors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **gigantic 4-floor office building** that represents the entire 4 GB address space of the ARM Cortex-M processor:
- **Ground Floor (0x00000000 – 0x1FFFFFFF)** → The **"Workshop Area"** (Code/Flash memory) — where programs (instructions) are stored and executed.
- **First Floor (0x20000000 – 0x3FFFFFFF)** → The **"Working Desk Area"** (SRAM) — temporary scratchpad where variables and the stack live while programs run.
- **Second Floor (0x40000000 – 0x5FFFFFFF)** → The **"Control Room"** (On-chip Peripherals) — where registers of UART, timers, GPIO, etc. sit at fixed addresses.
- **Third Floor (0x60000000 – 0x7FFFFFFF) & Beyond** → The **"External Warehouses"** (External RAM/ROM/Device) — connected through the External Bus Interface (EBI).

Just like each floor in the building has a **dedicated purpose and access policy**, each region in the ARM memory map has a specific role, bus width, and access behavior. The processor can directly reach **any byte** in this 4 GB space through a single pointer.

### 1.3 Fundamental Memory Types in ARM Systems

| Memory Type | Full Form | Key Property | Typical Use in KTU Labs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ROM/Flash** | Read-Only Memory / Flash | Non-volatile, byte/half-word/word read | Storing program code (firmware) |
| **SRAM** | Static Random-Access Memory | Volatile, fast R/W | Variables, stack, heap |
| **Peripheral Registers** | Memory-Mapped I/O | Read/Write side effects | Controlling UART, GPIO, ADC |
| **External Memory** | Off-chip memory | Accessed via EBI/FSMC | LCD buffers, large data arrays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** ARM Cortex-M processors use a **Modified Harvard Architecture** with a unified address space. Although physically separate buses exist for instruction and data (Harvard), the programmer sees **one linear 4 GB address space** (Von Neumann-style visibility).

### 1.4 The Load-Store Paradigm

ARM is a **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** architecture. This means:

- The processor **CANNOT** directly operate on data stored in memory.
- Data must first be **loaded** from memory into a register.
- Arithmetic/Logic operations are performed on the **register**.
- The result must then be **stored** back to memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (Board Exam Tip):**
> **LDR** = **L**oa**D** from memory to **R**egister.
> **STR** = **S**ore from **R**egister to memory.
> All other instructions (ADD, SUB, AND, ORR) work **only on registers** (R0–R12, R13=SP, R14=LR, R15=PC).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 4 GB Memory Map of ARM Cortex-M3/M4

The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 memory map is divided into **8 primary regions**, each of size **512 MB**. The address decoding logic in the processor uses the upper 3 bits (A[31:29]) to select the region.

| Region | Address Range (Hex) | Size | Memory Type | Bus Access | Cacheable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Code** | `0x00000000 – 0x1FFFFFFF` | **512 MB** | Flash/ROM (Code) | I-Code / D-Code | Yes (M7/M4) |
| **SRAM** | `0x20000000 – 0x3FFFFFFF` | **512 MB** | Static RAM | System Bus | Yes |
| **Peripheral** | `0x40000000 – 0x5FFFFFFF` | **512 MB** | On-Chip Peripherals | System Bus | No (Device) |
| **External RAM** | `0x60000000 – 0x7FFFFFFF` | **512 MB** | External SRAM/PSRAM | External Bus | Yes |
| **External Device** | `0x80000000 – 0x9FFFFFFF` | **512 MB** | External Device | External Bus | No (Device) |
| **System** | `0xE0000000 – 0xFFFFFFFF` | **512 MB** | System/PPB/ROM Table | System Bus | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two key regions for **Bit-Banding** are:
> - **Bit-band region (alias):** `0x22000000 – 0x23FFFFFF` → 1 MB alias for SRAM bit-band
> - **Bit-band region (alias):** `0x42000000 – 0x43FFFFFF` → 1 MB alias for Peripheral bit-band

### 2.2 Endianness — The Byte-Ordering Policy

**Endianness** defines the byte order in which a multi-byte value (half-word = 2 bytes, word = 4 bytes) is stored in memory.

#### (a) Little-Endian (Default in ARM Cortex-M)
The **least significant byte (LSB)** is stored at the **lowest memory address**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example: Storing the 32-bit value `0x12345678` at address `0x20000000` in Little-Endian:**
>
> | Address | `0x20000000` | `0x20000001` | `0x20000002` | `0x20000003` |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Byte | `0x78` (LSB) | `0x56` | `0x34` | `0x12` (MSB) |

#### (b) Big-Endian
The **most significant byte (MSB)** is stored at the **lowest memory address**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example: Storing `0x12345678` at `0x20000000` in Big-Endian:**
>
> | Address | `0x20000000` | `0x20000001` | `0x20000002` | `0x20000003` |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Byte | `0x12` (MSB) | `0x34` | `0x56` | `0x78` (LSB) |

### 2.3 Bit-Band Aliasing (Cortex-M3/M4 Exclusive Feature)

Bit-banding allows **atomic** (indivisible) read-modify-write operations on a single bit in the SRAM or Peripheral regions by mapping each bit to a **full word (32 bits)** in an alias region. This eliminates the need for read-modify-write sequences (which are non-atomic in multi-tasking environments).

#### KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory for Numericals)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Address Space** | $2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}$ | 32-bit address bus |
| 2 | **Bit-band Alias Address** | $\text{AliasAddr} = \text{AliasBase} + ((\text{Addr} - \text{BitBandBase}) \times 32) + (\text{Bit} \times 4)$ | All in **bytes** |
| 3 | **Bit-band Region for SRAM** | Base: `0x20000000`, Alias Base: `0x22000000` | 1 MB SRAM → 32 MB alias |
| 4 | **Bit-band Region for Peripherals** | Base: `0x40000000`, Alias Base: `0x42000000` | 1 MB peripheral → 32 MB alias |
| 5 | **Little-Endian Byte Order** | LSB @ Lowest Address | ARM Cortex-M default |
| 6 | **Aligned Word Access** | Address must be divisible by **4** | Misaligned access → Fault |
| 7 | **Aligned Half-Word Access** | Address must be divisible by **2** | Required for LDRH/STRH |
| 8 | **Stack Pointer Registers** | MSP (`R13` / Banked) and PSP | MSP after reset |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Numerical Problems:** Students often forget the **unit conversion (bytes to words)** in the Bit-Band formula. The factor $\times 32$ is correct because **1 bit = 4 bytes (1 word) in the alias region**, and there are 8 bits in a byte, giving $8 \times 4 = 32$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Firmware (KTU Lab):** Understanding the memory map is **essential** for linker scripts (placing code in Flash, variables in SRAM, stack setup).
- **Bare-Metal Programming:** Every GPIO register, UART data register, and Timer register is accessed by writing to its **memory-mapped address** (e.g., `GPIO_PORT0 = (1<<3);`).
- **RTOS Development:** Bit-banding is used in real-time operating systems for **lock-free communication** between tasks and interrupt handlers.
- **IoT/Automotive:** The system region contains **NVIC, SysTick, and ROM table** — critical for interrupt-driven firmware.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bit-Band Alias Address Calculation

**Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):**
In an ARM Cortex-M3 processor, calculate the bit-band alias address for **bit 7 of the byte located at SRAM address `0x20000020`**. Verify the byte offset and word offset.

#### Given Data
- SRAM Bit-Band Base: $\text{Base}_{SRAM} = \text{0x20000000}$
- SRAM Alias Base: $\text{Alias}_{SRAM} = \text{0x22000000}$
- Target Address: $\text{Addr} = \text{0x20000020}$
- Target Bit: $\text{Bit} = 7$

#### Formula
$$\text{AliasAddr} = \text{AliasBase} + ((\text{Addr} - \text{BitBandBase}) \times 32) + (\text{Bit} \times 4)$$

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the byte offset of the target address from the bit-band base.

$$\text{ByteOffset} = \text{Addr} - \text{BitBandBase} = \text{0x20000020} - \text{0x20000000} = \text{0x20}$$

This means the target byte is at **byte offset 0x20** (= 32 bytes) from the start of the bit-band region.

**Step 2:** Convert the byte offset to a word offset (each byte maps to 8 words in the alias region).

$$\text{WordOffset} = \text{ByteOffset} \times 8 = 0\text{x}20 \times 8 = 0\text{x}100 \text{ (words)}$$

In bytes, this is:
$$\text{WordOffset}_{\text{bytes}} = \text{ByteOffset} \times 32 = 0\text{x}20 \times 32 = 0\text{x}400 \text{ (bytes)}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the byte offset contribution from the specific bit number.

$$\text{BitOffset}_{\text{bytes}} = \text{Bit} \times 4 = 7 \times 4 = 28 = \text{0x1C}$$

**Step 4:** Combine all components in the alias region.

$$\text{AliasAddr} = \text{0x22000000} + \text{0x400} + \text{0x1C}$$

Let us perform the addition carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{0x22000000} \\
+\quad \text{0x00000400} \\
+\quad \text{0x0000001C} \\
\hline
= \text{0x2200041C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** The bit-band alias address is $\text{0x2200041C}$.

**Verification (Logical Check):**
- Bit 7 of `0x20000020` is the **highest bit (MSB)** of that byte.
- Setting the alias address `0x2200041C` to `1` will set bit 7 of `0x20000020`.
- Setting it to `0` will clear bit 7 — all in a **single atomic bus transaction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful in RTOS?** In a multi-tasking system, two tasks accessing the same byte can cause race conditions. With bit-banding, the alias write is **atomic** — the bus master never releases the bus between the read and write, eliminating the race condition.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Endianness — Reconstructing a Word from Bytes

**Problem:**
A debugger in **Little-Endian** ARM system reads the following 4 bytes from SRAM at addresses `0x20000010` to `0x20000013`:

| Address | Byte Value |
|---|---|
| `0x20000010` | `0xA1` |
| `0x20000011` | `0xB2` |
| `0x20000012` | `0xC3` |
| `0x20000013` | `0xD4` |

**Find the 32-bit word value stored at `0x20000010`**.

#### Solution

**Step 1:** Recall that in Little-Endian, the **LSB is stored at the lowest address**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte at } \text{0x20000010} &= \text{LSB} = \text{0xA1} \\
\text{Byte at } \text{0x20000011} &= \text{Next Byte} = \text{0xB2} \\
\text{Byte at } \text{0x20000012} &= \text{Next Byte} = \text{0xC3} \\
\text{Byte at } \textx{20000013} &= \text{MSB} = \text{0xD4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Construct the 32-bit word by placing each byte in its proper bit position.

$$W = (\text{MSB} \times 2^{24}) + (\text{Next} \times 2^{16}) + (\text{Next} \times 2^{8}) + (\text{LSB} \times 2^{0})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$W = (\text{0xD4} \times 2^{24}) + (\text{0xC3} \times 2^{16}) + (\text{0xB2} \times 2^{8}) + (\text{0xA1} \times 1)$$

**Step 4:** Convert each term to its byte position.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{0xD4} \times 2^{24} &= \text{0xD4000000} \\
\text{0xC3} \times 2^{16} &= \text{0x00C30000} \\
\text{0xB2} \times 2^{8} &= \text{0x0000B200} \\
\text{0xA1} \times 2^{0} &= \text{0x000000A1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Add all four components.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{0xD4000000} \\
+\quad \text{0x00C30000} \\
+\quad \text{0x0000B200} \\
+\quad \text{0x000000A1} \\
\hline
= \text{0xD4C3B2A1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** The 32-bit word stored at `0x20000010` is $\text{0xD4C3B2A1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** The question may ask "if this were Big-Endian, what would be the word value?" In Big-Endian, the result is the **byte-reversed** word: $\text{0xA1B2C3D4}$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of ARM Memory Architectures

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard | ARM Cortex-M (Modified Harvard) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | Unified (one bus) | Separate I and D | Unified 4 GB view, separate buses |
| **Instruction Fetch + Data Access** | Sequential (one at a time) | Simultaneous | Simultaneous via I-Code & D-Code buses |
| **Programmer's View** | One address space | Two address spaces | One linear 4 GB address space |
| **Performance** | Bottleneck (Von Neumann) | High (Harvard) | High (Modified Harvard) |
| **Example Processors** | Older x86, 8051 (with ext.) | DSPs, classic Harvard | LPC2148, STM32, Tiva-C |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Verifying Memory Alignment Constraint

**Problem:**
An ARM Cortex-M3 attempts to execute `LDR R0, [R1]` where `R1 = 0x20000002`. Explain what happens.

#### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the instruction type. `LDR` (word load) requires a **4-byte aligned** address.

**Step 2:** Check the alignment of `0x20000002`.

$$\text{0x20000002} \mod 4 = 2 \neq 0$$

**Step 3:** The address is **misaligned** for a word access.

**Step 4:** Result: The processor raises a **HardFault exception** (UsageFault → HardFault). On older ARM7TDMI, it would rotate the data; on Cortex-M3/M4, it is **strictly faulted**.

> [!NOTE]
> Half-word access (`LDRH/STRH`) requires address $\mod 2 = 0$. Byte access (`LDRB/STRB`) has no alignment restriction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — ARM Cortex-M 4 GB Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startAddr["0xFFFFFFFF — Top of 4 GB Space"]:::top
    
    subgraph REG_SYS["0xE0000000 - 0xFFFFFFFF (System / PPB)"]
        direction TB
        sysNVIC["NVIC + SysTick + ROM Table"]
        sysPrivate["Private Peripheral Bus"]
    end
    
    subgraph REG_EXT2["0xA0000000 - 0xDFFFFFFF (Reserved)"]
        res1["Unused Region"]
    end
    
    subgraph REG_EXTDEV["0x80000000 - 0x9FFFFFFF (External Device)"]
        extdev["External Bus, Non-cacheable"]
    end
    
    subgraph REG_EXTRAM["0x60000000 - 0x7FFFFFFF (External RAM)"]
        extram["External SRAM / SDRAM"]
    end
    
    subgraph REG_PERI["0x40000000 - 0x5FFFFFFF (Peripherals)"]
        peri["GPIO, UART, TIMER, ADC Registers"]
        periAlias["Bit-band Alias: 0x42000000"]
    end
    
    subgraph REG_SRAM["0x20000000 - 0x3FFFFFFF (SRAM)"]
        sram["On-chip Static RAM (Variables, Stack)"]
        sramAlias["Bit-band Alias: 0x22000000"]
    end
    
    subgraph REG_CODE["0x00000000 - 0x1FFFFFFF (Code)"]
        flash["Flash / ROM (Firmware Code)"]
    end
    
    startAddr --> REG_SYS
    REG_SYS --> REG_EXT2
    REG_EXT2 --> REG_EXTDEV
    REG_EXTDEV --> REG_EXTRAM
    REG_EXTRAM --> REG_PERI
    REG_PERI --> REG_SRAM
    REG_SRAM --> REG_CODE
    REG_CODE --> bottomAddr["0x00000000 — Base of 4 GB Space"]:::bottom
    
    classDef top fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef bottom fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Load-Store Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Memory Address 0x20000004"] -->|LDR R0, addr| B["Processor Initiates Load"]
    B --> C{"Address in\nValid Region?"}
    C -->|Yes - SRAM| D["Read 32-bit value\nfrom SRAM bus"]
    C -->|Yes - Peripheral| E["Read from Peripheral\nregister address"]
    C -->|No - Reserved| F["BusFault / HardFault"]
    D --> G["Data placed in R0"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["ALU operates on R0\n(ADD, AND, ORR, etc.)"]
    H --> I["Result in R1"]
    I -->|STR R1, addr| J["Store back to memory"]
    J --> K["Data written to\n0x20000008"]
    
    classDef valid fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef invalid fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    class C,D,E,G,H,I,J,K valid
    class F invalid
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Bit-Band Mapping Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BITBAND_SRAM["Bit-Band Region (SRAM) — 1 MB"]
        s1["0x20000000: Byte 0, Bit 0 to 7"]
        s2["0x20000001: Byte 1, Bit 0 to 7"]
        s3["0x20000002: Byte 2, Bit 0 to 7"]
        sN["... up to 0x200FFFFF"]
    end
    
    subgraph ALIAS_SRAM["Bit-Band Alias Region — 32 MB"]
        a0["0x22000000: Word for Byte 0, Bit 0"]
        a1["0x22000004: Word for Byte 0, Bit 1"]
        a2["0x22000008: Word for Byte 0, Bit 2"]
        a3["0x2200000C: Word for Byte 0, Bit 3"]
        a4["0x22000010: Word for Byte 0, Bit 4"]
        a5["0x22000014: Word for Byte 0, Bit 5"]
        a6["0x22000018: Word for Byte 0, Bit 6"]
        a7["0x2200001C: Word for Byte 0, Bit 7"]
        aN["... up to 0x23FFFFFF"]
    end
    
    s1 -.-> a0
    s1 -.-> a1
    s1 -.-> a2
    s1 -.-> a3
    s1 -.-> a4
    s1 -.-> a5
    s1 -.-> a6
    s1 -.-> a7
    s2 -.-> a0
    s2 -.-> a1
    
    classDef region fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    class s1,s2,s3,sN,a0,a1,a2,a3,a4,a5,a6,a7,aN region
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Von Neumann vs Harvard vs Modified Harvard

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VN["Von Neumann Architecture"]
```
