<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by a "Load-Store Architecture"? How does ARM implement it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Load-Store Architecture** (also called a **Register-Register Architecture**) is a RISC design philosophy in which:
1. **Data processing instructions** (ADD, SUB, AND, ORR, etc.) operate **only on registers** — never directly on memory operands. *(1 mark)*
2. Separate **Load (LDR/LDRB/LDRH)** and **Store (STR/STRB/STRH)** instructions are used to move data between memory and registers. *(1 mark)*
3. ARM Cortex-M follows this strictly: all ALU operands must be in R0–R12 (or R13=SP, R14=LR, R15=PC). *(1 mark)*

**Example sequence to add two memory variables:**
```c
LDR  R0, [R1]      ; Load first operand from memory to R0
LDR  R2, [R3]      ; Load second operand from memory to R2
ADD  R4, R0, R2    ; Perform addition in registers
STR  R4, [R5]      ; Store result back to memory
```

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the concept of Endianness. Differentiate between Little-Endian and Big-Endian with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Endianness** is the byte-ordering convention used by a processor to store a multi-byte value in memory. *(1 mark)*

| Feature | Little-Endian | Big-Endian |
|---|---|---|
| **LSB Position** | Stored at the **lowest** memory address | Stored at the **highest** memory address |
| **MSB Position** | Stored at the **highest** memory address | Stored at the **lowest** memory address |
| **Default in ARM Cortex-M?** | **Yes** (default) | Selectable via AIRCR.ENDIANNESS bit |
| **Network Standard** | No | Yes (Network Byte Order) |

**Example:** Store the 32-bit word `0x12345678` at address `0x20000000`: *(1 mark)*

| Address | Little-Endian Byte | Big-Endian Byte |
|---|---|---|
| `0x20000000` | `0x78` (LSB) | `0x12` (MSB) |
| `0x20000001` | `0x56` | `0x34` |
| `0x20000002` | `0x34` | `0x56` |
| `0x20000003` | `0x12` (MSB) | `0x78` (LSB) |

Reconstructed 32-bit value in both cases remains `0x12345678` (only the byte order in memory differs).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU 2024 Pattern Notice]:** In Part B, students are given an **Internal Choice** between Question A and Question B. You must attempt **one full question**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete 4 GB memory map of the ARM Cortex-M3/M4 processor**. List all the regions with their address ranges and mention the purpose of each region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Bit-Banding** in ARM Cortex-M3/M4? Derive the formula for calculating the **bit-band alias address** and calculate the alias address for setting **bit 3 of the peripheral register located at `0x40005000`**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Memory Map of ARM Cortex-M3/M4 (7 Marks)**

The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 processor implements a **unified 4 GB linear address space** spanning from `0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`. This address space is divided into **8 architecturally defined regions** of **512 MB each**, determined by the upper 3 bits (A[31:29]) of the address. *(1 mark)*

**Block Diagram of Memory Map:** *(3 marks)*

```
0xFFFFFFFF  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
            │   SYSTEM (NVIC, SysTick, PPB)    │  512 MB
0xE0000000  ├──────────────────────────────────┤
            │   RESERVED (Vendor-specific)      │  512 MB
0xA0000000  ├──────────────────────────────────┤
            │   EXTERNAL DEVICE (Non-cacheable) │  512 MB
0x90000000  ├──────────────────────────────────┤
            │   EXTERNAL RAM (Cacheable)        │  512 MB
0x60000000  ├──────────────────────────────────┤
            │   PERIPHERALS (Memory-mapped I/O) │  512 MB
0x40000000  ├──────────────────────────────────┤
            │   SRAM (Variables, Stack)         │  512 MB
0x20000000  ├──────────────────────────────────┤
            │   CODE (Flash/ROM)                │  512 MB
0x00000000  └──────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Region-wise Description:** *(3 marks)*

| Region | Address Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Code** | `0x00000000 – 0x1FFFFFFF` | Non-volatile Flash/ROM storing executable firmware. Accessed via I-Code and D-Code buses. |
| **SRAM** | `0x20000000 – 0x3FFFFFFF` | Volatile on-chip Static RAM. Used for global/local variables, stack, and heap. Supports bit-banding. |
| **Peripheral** | `0x40000000 – 0x5FFFFFFF` | Memory-mapped registers of on-chip peripherals (GPIO, UART, ADC, TIMER). Bit-band capable. |
| **External RAM** | `0x60000000 – 0x7FFFFFFF` | Off-chip SRAM/PSRAM accessed via External Bus Interface (EBI). Cacheable. |
| **External Device** | `0x80000000 – 0x9FFFFFFF` | Off-chip devices (e.g., LCD controllers, NOR flash). Non-cacheable, strongly ordered. |
| **System** | `0xE0000000 – 0xFFFFFFFF` | Private Peripheral Bus (PPB) — includes NVIC, SysTick, MPU, and ROM table. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The regions `0xA0000000 – 0xDFFFFFFF` are **vendor-specific** and reserved for SoC-specific peripherals (e.g., Ethernet, USB on STM32).

---

#### **Part (b) — Bit-Banding and Alias Address Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Definition of Bit-Banding:** *(2 marks)*

**Bit-banding** is a feature unique to ARM Cortex-M3/M4 processors that maps each individual bit in a designated "bit-band region" (SRAM `0x20000000` and Peripheral `0x40000000`) to a **full 32-bit word** in a corresponding "bit-band alias region" (`0x22000000` and `0x42000000` respectively).

**Advantages:**
- **Atomic** bit-level read-modify-write operations (no race conditions in RTOS).
- Eliminates the need for software `read → modify → write` sequences.
- Simplifies bit manipulation in C using pointer dereferencing.

**Derivation of the Alias Address Formula:** *(3 marks)*

Let:
- $\text{BitBandBase}$ = Start of bit-band region
- $\text{AliasBase}$ = Start of alias region
- $\text{Addr}$ = Address of the byte containing the target bit
- $\text{Bit}$ = Bit position (0–7) within that byte

For a given byte, the **byte offset** from the start of the bit-band region is:

$$\text{ByteOffset} = \text{Addr} - \text{BitBandBase}$$

Each byte contributes **8 bits**, and each bit maps to **1 word (4 bytes)** in the alias region. So the **word offset in bytes** is:

$$\text{WordOffset}_{\text{bytes}} = \text{ByteOffset} \times 8 \text{ bits} \times 4 \text{ bytes/word} = \text{ByteOffset} \times 32$$

The **bit offset in bytes** is:

$$\text{BitOffset}_{\text{bytes}} = \text{Bit} \times 4$$

Combining:

$$\boxed{\text{AliasAddr} = \text{AliasBase} + ((\text{Addr} - \text{BitBandBase}) \times 32) + (\text{Bit} \times 4)}$$

**Numerical Calculation:** *(2 marks)*

**Given:**
- Peripheral Bit-Band Base: $\text{0x40000000}$
- Peripheral Alias Base: $\text{0x42000000}$
- Target Address: $\text{Addr} = \text{0x40005000}$
- Target Bit: $\text{Bit} = 3$

**Step 1:** Byte offset

$$\text{ByteOffset} = \text{0x40005000} - \text{0x40000000} = \text{0x5000}$$

**Step 2:** Word offset in bytes

$$\text{WordOffset} = \text{0x5000} \times 32 = \text{0xA0000}$$

**Step 3:** Bit offset in bytes

$$\text{BitOffset} = 3 \times 4 = \text{0xC}$$

**Step 4:** Final alias address

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AliasAddr} &= \text{0x42000000} + \text{0x000A0000} + \text{0x0000000C} \\
&= \text{0x420A000C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** The bit-band alias address for **bit 3 of `0x40005000`** is $\text{0x420A000C}$.

To set this bit, write `1` to `0x420A000C`. To clear it, write `0`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Von Neumann, Harvard, and Modified Harvard architectures** with neat diagrams. Why is the Modified Harvard architecture preferred in ARM Cortex-M processors? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, explain the concepts of **byte order (Endianness)** and **memory alignment** in ARM Cortex-M. A 32-bit value `0xDEADBEEF` is stored at address `0x20000200` in a Little-Endian system. Show the byte arrangement in memory. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Architecture Comparison (7 Marks)**

**1. Von Neumann Architecture:** *(2 marks)*

In the **Von Neumann architecture**, a **single memory** holds both instructions and data, and a **single shared bus** is used for both instruction fetch and data access.

**Disadvantage:** The single bus becomes a **Von Neumann bottleneck** — instruction fetch and data access cannot occur simultaneously, limiting performance.

```
        ┌──────────┐
        │   CPU    │
        │  +  ALU  │
        └────┬─────┘
             │  Single Address + Data Bus
             ▼
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │  Instruction + Data │
   │      Memory         │
   └─────────────────────┘
```

**2. Harvard Architecture:** *(2 marks)*

The classic **Harvard architecture** uses **physically separate memories** and **separate buses** for instructions and data, allowing **simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access.

**Advantage:** High throughput (no bottleneck).
**Disadvantage:** Complex hardware, programmer must manage two separate address spaces.

```
        ┌──────────┐
        │   CPU    │
        │  +  ALU  │
        └──┬───┬───┘
           │   │
   I-Bus  │   │  D-Bus
           ▼   ▼
  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐
  │Instr.   │  │  Data   │
  │Memory   │  │ Memory  │
  └─────────┘  └─────────┘
```

**3. Modified Harvard (ARM Cortex-M):** *(2 marks)*

The **ARM Cortex-M3/M4** uses a **Modified Harvard architecture** internally with **three buses**:
- **I-Code bus** (32-bit) — Instruction fetch from Code region.
- **D-Code bus** (32-bit) — Data access from Code region.
- **System bus** — Accesses SRAM and Peripheral regions.

Although physically separate buses exist, the **programmer sees a single unified 4 GB address space** (Von Neumann-style visibility), making software development simple.

**Why preferred in ARM Cortex-M?** *(1 mark)*
- **High performance** of Harvard (parallel instruction and data access).
- **Programming simplicity** of Von Neumann (single address space).
- Optimal balance for embedded applications requiring both speed and ease of use.

---

#### **Part (b) — Endianness and Memory Alignment (7 Marks)**

**Endianness Concept:** *(2 marks)*

**Endianness** is the byte-ordering rule used to store multi-byte data (16-bit half-word, 32-bit word) in memory.

**Memory Alignment Concept:** *(2 marks)*

**Memory alignment** requires that the address of a multi-byte data access be a **multiple of the data size**:
- **Byte access** (8-bit) — no alignment restriction.
- **Half-word access** (16-bit) — address must be divisible by **2**.
- **Word access** (32-bit) — address must be divisible by **4**.

On ARM Cortex-M, misaligned word/half-word access causes a **UsageFault** (escalated to HardFault). On older ARM7TDMI, misaligned loads were silently rotated, which was a common source of bugs.

**Numerical Example — Little-Endian Storage of `0xDEADBEEF`:** *(3 marks)*

**Given:**
- Word Value: `0xDEADBEEF`
- Base Address: `0x20000200`
- System: Little-Endian

In Little-Endian, the **LSB is stored at the lowest address**. The 4 bytes of `0xDEADBEEF` are:
- Byte 0 (LSB) = `0xEF`
- Byte 1 = `0xBE`
- Byte 2 = `0xAD`
- Byte 3 (MSB) = `0xDE`

**Memory Layout:**

| Address | Byte Stored | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| `0x20000200` | `0xEF` | LSB (Least Significant Byte) |
| `0x20000201` | `0xBE` | |
| `0x20000202` | `0xAD` | |
| `0x20000203` | `0xDE` | MSB (Most Significant Byte) |

**Verification (Reverse Reconstruction):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Word} &= (\text{0xDE} \times 2^{24}) + (\text{0xAD} \times 2^{16}) + (\text{0xBE} \times 2^{8}) + (\text{0xEF} \times 1) \\
&= \text{0xDE000000} + \text{0x00AD0000} + \text{0x0000BE00} + \text{0x000000EF} \\
&= \text{0xDEADBEEF} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the system were **Big-Endian**, the byte order at the same addresses would be reversed: `0xDE`, `0xAD`, `0xBE`, `0xEF`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Bit-Band Formula Mistakes:**
>    - Forgetting the factor **32** (using 8 instead).
>    - Using the **wrong alias base** (mixing up `0x22000000` for SRAM and `0x42000000` for Peripheral).
>    - Treating the bit number as a byte offset (it is **bit × 4**, not just the bit number).
> 2. **Endianness Confusion:** Writing the MSB at the lowest address for Little-Endian (this is Big-Endian).
> 3. **Memory Map Errors:** Mis-identifying the System region as "External Memory" or vice versa. The System region starts at `0xE0000000`, not `0xA0000000`.
> 4. **Alignment Violation:** Not specifying which exception is raised (Cortex-M raises **UsageFault → HardFault**, not just "an error").
> 5. **Load-Store Confusion:** Claiming that ARM can directly add memory operands (this is a CISC misconception).

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Total Address Space:** ARM Cortex-M uses a **32-bit linear address space = 4 GB** (`0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`).
- ✅ **Memory Regions:** **8 regions of 512 MB each** — Code, SRAM, Peripheral, External RAM, External Device, Reserved (×2), System.
- ✅ **Modified Harvard:** Internally uses separate I-Code, D-Code, and System buses; externally appears as a unified 4 GB space.
- ✅ **Load-Store Architecture:** All data-processing instructions operate **only on registers**; memory access is restricted to LDR/STR family.
- ✅ **Default Endianness:** **Little-Endian** (LSB at lowest address). Big-Endian is selectable via `AIRCR.ENDIANNESS` bit.
- ✅ **Bit-Band Alias Formula:** $\text{AliasAddr} = \text{AliasBase} + ((\text{Addr} - \text{BitBandBase}) \times 32) + (\text{Bit} \times 4)$.
- ✅ **Bit-Band Bases:** SRAM `0x20000000` → Alias `0x22000000`; Peripheral `0x40000000` → Alias `0x42000000`.
- ✅ **Memory Alignment:** Word (4-byte) and half-word (2-byte) accesses must be aligned, else **HardFault** on Cortex-M.
- ✅ **System Region:** Houses **NVIC, SysTick, MPU, and ROM Table** — the heart of interrupt and system control.
- ✅ **Stack Pointer:** ARM Cortex-M uses **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** after reset, switchable to **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** in RTOS.
- ✅ **Memory-Mapped I/O:** Peripheral registers are accessed as memory locations — no special I/O instructions needed.
- ✅ **Code Region is Read-Optimized:** I-Code bus typically prefetches instructions for efficient pipeline operation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bit Banding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bit Banding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-Banding** is a memory-mapping technique in the **ARM Cortex-M3 / Cortex-M4** architecture that maps each individual bit in a designated *bit-band region* to a complete 32-bit word in a corresponding *bit-band alias region*. This enables **atomic**, single-instruction read/write access to a single bit without the need for read-modify-write software sequences.

In the standard ARM Cortex-M memory map, there are exactly **two bit-band regions** (each of size **1 MB = 0x100000 bytes**), and two corresponding **bit-band alias regions** (each of size **32 MB = 0x2000000 bytes**).

| Region | Base Address | Size |
|---|---|---|
| SRAM Bit-Band Region | **0x20000000** | **1 MB** |
| SRAM Bit-Band Alias | **0x22000000** | **32 MB** |
| Peripheral Bit-Band Region | **0x40000000** | **1 MB** |
| Peripheral Bit-Band Alias | **0x42000000** | **32 MB** |

The size expansion factor is exactly **32×** because every single bit is mapped to a 32-bit word in the alias region.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Post Office P.O. Box" System**

Imagine a city has 100 houses (your normal memory bytes). The post office decides that for *two special districts only*, they will create a giant warehouse with **3,200 numbered P.O. boxes** — where each individual bit in the original 100 houses gets its own *private, full-sized locker* in the warehouse.

- **Normal memory** = 100 regular houses, each holding 8 tenants (bits).
- **Bit-band alias** = 3,200 huge lockers (32 bits each), but only 100 of them are "active" — one locker per bit of the original house.

When you want to set a particular tenant's door (a single bit) in a special district, instead of the postmaster going to the house, knocking on the door, asking the tenant to move out, putting a new tenant in, and then locking it (the slow read-modify-write dance), the postmaster just walks to the **specific P.O. Box in the alias warehouse** and stuffs a letter inside. The original tenant's door instantly reflects the new state.

That single, atomic "drop a letter in the box" operation = **one single store instruction** at the alias address.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Magnification factor:** **32** (one bit → 32-bit word in alias)
> - **Bit-band region size:** **1 MB** (0x100000 bytes)
> - **Alias region size:** **32 MB** (0x2000000 bytes)
> - **Number of bit-band regions in Cortex-M3/M4:** **2** (SRAM + Peripheral)
> - **Address alignment required for atomic access:** **Word-aligned (4 bytes)** in the alias

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bit-Banding Exists (The Core Motivation)**

In a typical microcontroller, setting a single bit in a peripheral register requires three instructions:
1. **LDR** — Load the entire 32-bit register.
2. **ORR / BIC** — Modify the target bit using logical OR/AND.
3. **STR** — Store the modified value back to the register.

This sequence is **non-atomic** and can be corrupted by **interrupts (IRQ)** or **multi-threaded contexts** (race conditions). Bit-banding solves this by providing a **dedicated memory-mapped address** for *each individual bit* — writing a `1` or `0` to that alias address sets/clears the bit in **one single, atomic store instruction**.

### 2.1.1 The Mapping Logic

- The Cortex-M bus matrix intercepts any access to the alias region.
- The address decoder computes the original bit-band address and bit position.
- The bus performs a **bit-mask read → modify → write cycle in hardware** (atomic at the bus level).
- From the programmer's view, it is *one* write to the alias word address.

### 2.1.2 Atomicity Guarantee

Because the bus hardware performs the R-M-W cycle and the bus is **non-interruptible** for that specific access, the operation is guaranteed atomic. This is critical for:
- GPIO pin manipulation without glitches
- Semaphores / shared flags in RTOS
- Interrupt-safe flag toggling

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear every year in KTU exams.**

| Symbol | Formula / Description | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Alias Base (SRAM) | $A_{SRAM} = \textbf{0x22000000}$ | bytes |
| Alias Base (Peripheral) | $A_{PERI} = \textbf{0x42000000}$ | bytes |
| Region Base (SRAM) | $R_{SRAM} = \textbf{0x20000000}$ | bytes |
| Region Base (Peripheral) | $R_{PERI} = \textbf{0x40000000}$ | bytes |
| Expansion factor | $N = 32$ | dimensionless |
| Word size | $W = 4$ bytes | bytes |
| Bit-band alias address | $A^* = A_{alias} + (B \cdot 32) + (b \cdot 4)$ | bytes |
| Bit-band byte offset | $B = \text{addr} - R_{region}$ | bytes |
| Bit number | $b \in [0, 7]$ within the byte | dimensionless |

**Memory map size relation:**

$$
\text{Size}_{alias} = \text{Size}_{region} \times 8 \times 4 = 1\,\text{MB} \times 32 = 32\,\text{MB}
$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bare-metal firmware:** Driving individual GPIO pins without disturbing neighboring pins.
- **RTOS kernels:** Implementing spinlocks and semaphores atomically.
- **Communication protocols:** Toggling a single SS/CS line or a TX/RX flag safely.
- **Watchdog / interrupt flag clearing:** Clearing a single WDT flag bit in one instruction.
- **Signal integrity:** Avoids the "read-modify-write hazard" that causes brief glitches on I/O pins.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bit-Band Alias Address Formula

### 3.1.1 Starting Point

Let:
- $\text{addr}$ = the original byte address in the bit-band region.
- $b$ = the bit number within that byte ($0 \le b \le 7$).
- $A_{alias}$ = the alias region base address.
- $R_{region}$ = the bit-band region base address.

**Goal:** Derive the alias address $A^*$ that corresponds to bit $b$ of byte $\text{addr}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute the byte offset from the region base.} \\
B &= \text{addr} - R_{region} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Each byte has 8 bits, each mapped to a 32-bit word. So per byte, the alias "consumes"} \\
&\;\; 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes.} \\
\text{Step 3: The address contribution of the byte offset is therefore:} \\
\text{Offset}_{byte} &= B \times 32 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Within a byte, bit } b \text{ contributes } b \times 4 \text{ bytes to the alias address.} \\
\text{Offset}_{bit} &= b \times 4 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Combine the base address and both offsets.} \\
A^* &= A_{alias} + (B \times 32) + (b \times 4) \\[6pt]
\text{Final Master Formula:} \\
A^* &= A_{alias} + ((\text{addr} - R_{region}) \times 32) + (b \times 4)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Question)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Find the alias address corresponding to **bit 5** of the byte located at **0x20000010** (SRAM bit-band region). Then find the same for a peripheral register at **0x40001000**, **bit 3**.

### 3.2.1 SRAM Example

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{SRAM} &= \text{0x22000000} \\
R_{SRAM} &= \text{0x20000000} \\
\text{addr} &= \text{0x20000010} \\
b &= 5 \\[4pt]
B &= \text{0x20000010} - \text{0x20000000} = \text{0x10} = 16 \text{ (decimal)} \\[4pt]
A^* &= \text{0x22000000} + (16 \times 32) + (5 \times 4) \\
&= \text{0x22000000} + 512 + 20 \\
&= \text{0x22000000} + \text{0x214} \\
&= \text{0x22000214}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Writing to `*(volatile uint32_t *)0x22000214` atomically sets/clears bit 5 of byte at `0x20000010`.

### 3.2.2 Peripheral Example

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{PERI} &= \text{0x42000000} \\
R_{PERI} &= \text{0x40000000} \\
\text{addr} &= \text{0x40001000} \\
b &= 3 \\[4pt]
B &= \text{0x40001000} - \text{0x40000000} = \text{0x1000} = 4096 \text{ (decimal)} \\[4pt]
A^* &= \text{0x42000000} + (4096 \times 32) + (3 \times 4) \\
&= \text{0x42000000} + 131072 + 12 \\
&= \text{0x42000000} + \text{0x2000C} \\
&= \text{0x4202000C}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Exam Sub-Cases

| Case | Address Type | Bit Position | Typical Exam Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| GPIO output set | Peripheral | b = pin number | Toggle a single LED |
| Interrupt flag clear | Peripheral | b = flag bit index | Clear NVIC pending bit |
| SRAM semaphore | SRAM | b = 0 to 7 | Implement lock |
| Peripheral enable | Peripheral | b = module enable bit | Enable UART clock |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cortex-M3/M4 Memory Map — Bit-Band Regions

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["0x00000000 Code Region"]
    A1["0x20000000 SRAM Bit-Band Region 1 MB"]
    A2["0x200FFFFF End of SRAM Bit-Band"]
    A3["0x22000000 SRAM Bit-Band Alias 32 MB"]
    A4["0x23FFFFFF End of SRAM Alias"]
    A5["0x40000000 Peripheral Bit-Band Region 1 MB"]
    A6["0x400FFFFF End of Peripheral Bit-Band"]
    A7["0x42000000 Peripheral Bit-Band Alias 32 MB"]
    A8["0x43FFFFFF End of Peripheral Alias"]
    A9["0xE0000000 System Control Space"]

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9
```

## 4.2 Bit-to-Alias-Word Mapping (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reg[Bit-Band Region 0x20000000 to 0x200FFFFF]:::region
    alias[Bit-Band Alias 0x22000000 to 0x23FFFFFF]:::alias
    bus[ARM Cortex-M3 Bus Matrix with Hardware Decoder]:::bus

    byte0[Byte at 0x20000000 Bits 0 to 7]:::byte
    byte1[Byte at 0x20000001 Bits 0 to 7]:::byte
    byten[Byte at 0x200FFFFF Bits 0 to 7]:::byte

    word0[32-bit Word at 0x22000000 for bit 0]:::word
    word1[32-bit Word at 0x22000004 for bit 1]:::word
    word2[32-bit Word at 0x22000008 for bit 2]:::word
    word3[32-bit Word at 0x22000214 for bit 5 of offset 0x10]:::word

    reg --> byte0
    reg --> byte1
    reg --> byten
    byte0 -- bit 0 --> word0
    byte0 -- bit 1 --> word1
    byte0 -- bit 2 --> word2
    byte1 -- bit 5 of offset 0x10 --> word3
    word0 --> bus
    word1 --> bus
    word2 --> bus
    word3 --> bus
    bus --> alias

    classDef region fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef alias fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bus fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef byte fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef word fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Read-Modify-Write vs Bit-Band Operation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer wants to set bit 5 of register at 0x40000004] --> Q{Use bit-banding?}
    Q -- No Traditional --> B1[LDR load 32-bit word from 0x40000004]
    B1 --> B2[ORR with mask 0x00000020]
    B2 --> B3[STR store back to 0x40000004]
    B3 --> B4[3 instructions - non-atomic - IRQ hazard]

    Q -- Yes Bit-Band --> C1[Compute alias address 0x42000000 plus offset]
    C1 --> C2[STR write 1 to alias address]
    C2 --> C3[1 instruction - atomic - IRQ safe]

    B4 --> Z[Final state bit 5 is set]
    C3 --> Z
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q: Define Bit-Banding in ARM Cortex-M3. Mention the two regions where it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

Bit-banding is a memory-mapping feature of the ARM Cortex-M3 (and M4) processor in which every single bit of a designated *bit-band region* is mapped to a full 32-bit word in a corresponding *bit-band alias region*. This enables atomic, single-instruction bit manipulation.

The **two bit-band regions** are:
1. **SRAM region:** 0x20000000 to 0x200FFFFF (1 MB), aliased to 0x22000000 to 0x23FFFFFF.
2. **Peripheral region:** 0x40000000 to 0x400FFFFF (1 MB), aliased to 0x42000000 to 0x43FFFFFF.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for SRAM region, 1 for peripheral region)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Q: List any three advantages of Bit-Banding over conventional read-modify-write bit manipulation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Atomicity:** Bit-banding performs the read-modify-write in hardware, so the operation cannot be interrupted by IRQs, eliminating race conditions.
2. **Code size reduction:** A single STR instruction replaces the three-instruction LDR-ORR-STR (or LDR-AND-STR) sequence.
3. **Faster execution:** Fewer instructions, fewer clock cycles, and deterministic timing for critical bit operations.
4. *(Bonus, optional)* Cleaner peripheral programming without bit masks.

*(3 marks — 1 mark per advantage)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the Bit-Banding mechanism in ARM Cortex-M3 with a neat diagram. Derive the formula to compute the alias address for any bit in the SRAM bit-band region. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A peripheral register is located at address **0x4000A000**. Calculate the alias address corresponding to **bit 6** of this register. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- Cortex-M3 memory map contains two bit-band regions: SRAM (0x20000000–0x200FFFFF) and Peripheral (0x40000000–0x400FFFFF). [1 mark]
- Each region has an alias (0x22000000 for SRAM, 0x42000000 for Peripheral) 32 times larger. [1 mark]
- Every bit in the bit-band region corresponds to a full 32-bit word in the alias region. [1 mark]
- The bus hardware performs an atomic R-M-W when the alias is accessed, so a single STR sets/clears a bit. [1 mark]

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \text{addr} - \text{0x20000000} \\
A^* &= \text{0x22000000} + (B \times 32) + (b \times 4)
\end{aligned}
$$

(Steps: byte offset → multiply by 32 → add bit offset × 4 → add alias base. Award 1 mark for byte offset, 1 for the 32× factor, 1 for final formula.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{alias} &= \text{0x42000000} \\
R_{region} &= \text{0x40000000} \\
\text{addr} &= \text{0x4000A000} \\
b &= 6 \\[4pt]
B &= \text{0x4000A000} - \text{0x40000000} = \text{0xA000} = 40960 \text{ (decimal)} \\[4pt]
A^* &= \text{0x42000000} + (40960 \times 32) + (6 \times 4) \\
&= \text{0x42000000} + 1310720 + 24 \\
&= \text{0x42000000} + \text{0x140018} \\
&= \text{0x42140018}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark each for: identifying base, computing B, multiplying B×32, multiplying b×4, summing, final answer.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain how bit-banding avoids the read-modify-write hazard in interrupt-driven systems. Compare the number of instructions required with and without bit-banding. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** An LED is connected to **bit 4** of a GPIO output data register located at **0x40010008**. Using bit-banding, write a single C instruction (or equivalent assembly) to turn the LED **ON**. Compute the alias address first. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Hazard Explanation (4 marks):**
- In a conventional sequence (LDR–ORR–STR), an interrupt arriving between the LDR and STR can modify the same register. [1 mark]
- The STR will then overwrite the interrupt's change → **lost update / race condition**. [1 mark]
- Bit-banding hardware does the R-M-W atomically on the bus — IRQs cannot interleave. [1 mark]
- Hence the bit is set/cleared exactly once, deterministically, with no glitches on output pins. [1 mark]

**Instruction Count Comparison (3 marks):**

| Method | Instructions | Atomic? | IRQ-Safe? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional (LDR–ORR–STR) | **3** | No | No |
| Bit-band (STR to alias) | **1** | Yes | Yes |

(1 mark for the table, 1 mark each for atomicity and IRQ safety comments.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute alias address (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{alias} &= \text{0x42000000} \\
R_{region} &= \text{0x40000000} \\
\text{addr} &= \text{0x40010008} \\
b &= 4 \\[4pt]
B &= \text{0x40010008} - \text{0x40000000} = \text{0x10008} = 65544 \text{ (decimal)} \\[4pt]
A^* &= \text{0x42000000} + (65544 \times 32) + (4 \times 4) \\
&= \text{0x42000000} + 2097408 + 16 \\
&= \text{0x42000000} + \text{0x200010} \\
&= \text{0x42200010}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Write the turn-ON instruction (3 marks):**

Conceptual C-style statement (pseudo-code for explanation):

```c
volatile uint32_t *LED = (volatile uint32_t *)0x42200010;
*LED = 1;   // Atomic single-bit SET
```

Assembly equivalent:

```asm
MOV  r0, #0x42200010
MOV  r1, #1
STR  r1, [r0]   ; a single atomic store turns the LED ON
```

(1 mark for correct final address, 1 mark for indicating the destination, 1 mark for showing the write of value 1 — not 0.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the two alias bases** — Students often write 0x22000000 for a peripheral question. Memorize: **2** for SRAM, **4** for Peripheral (the alias base's most-significant nibble mirrors the region's, with a `2` prefix added).
> 2. **Forgetting the ×4 for bit offset** — Each bit occupies 4 bytes in the alias, not 1.
> 3. **Using the wrong magnification factor** — The byte contributes 32 bytes (8 bits × 4 bytes/bit), not 8 bytes.
> 4. **Writing to the alias with a value other than 0 or 1** — The bus only looks at the LSB; writing 0xFF still only sets/clears the single target bit, but examiners may deduct a mark if the student claims it sets multiple bits.
> 5. **Confusing Cortex-M3/M4 with Cortex-M0/M0+** — Bit-banding is **NOT** present in Cortex-M0/M0+; this is a frequent MCQ trap.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Bit-Banding**

- Bit-banding is a **Cortex-M3 / M4** feature (not M0/M0+).
- Two **bit-band regions**, each **1 MB**; two **alias regions**, each **32 MB**.
- Region bases: **0x20000000** (SRAM), **0x40000000** (Peripheral).
- Alias bases: **0x22000000** (SRAM), **0x42000000** (Peripheral).
- Each bit → mapped to a full 32-bit word in alias.
- Master formula:
  $$A^* = A_{alias} + ((\text{addr} - R_{region}) \times 32) + (b \times 4)$$
- Magnification factor: **32** (byte offset multiplier) and **4** (bit offset multiplier).
- **Advantages:** atomic, single-instruction, IRQ-safe, no glitches, smaller code.
- **Disadvantages:** Doubles the SRAM address-space usage for the alias map (only 32 MB is "real" data; rest is decode overhead).
- **Usage:** GPIO control, flag clearing, semaphores, watchdog servicing.
- **Exam tip:** Always show the byte offset calculation explicitly — examiners award step-marks generously.
- **Trap to avoid:** Bit-band alias reads return 0 or 1, not the original byte — to read a single bit, do `(*(volatile uint32_t *)alias_addr)`.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arm Assembly Language Instruction Format and Operands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arm Assembly Language Instruction Format and Operands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Instruction Format (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **ARM assembly language instruction** is a 32-bit, fixed-length word that follows a standardized, predictable syntax recognized by the ARM assembler. In its generic human-readable form, it is written as:
> 
> **`<opcode>{<cond>} {S} <Rd>, <Rn>, <operand2>`**
> 
> The instruction is composed of four fundamental fields: an **opcode** (mandatory operation code), a **condition code** (optional 4-letter suffix that conditionally gates execution on CPSR flags), an **S flag** (optional, decides whether CPSR flags are updated), and one to three **operands** (`Rd`, `Rn`, and the flexible `operand2`).

The operand fields occupy the remaining 20 bits of the instruction word. The flexibility of the second operand (`operand2`) — known as the **Flexible Second Operand** — is one of the most distinctive features of the ARM ISA, allowing it to be a plain register, an immediate value, a register with a shift, or a register with a rotation, all encoded in the same 12-bit field.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Universal Restaurant Order Ticket"**
> 
> Imagine a kitchen where every order ticket must look identical, no matter whether you are ordering a cold drink, a pizza, or a dessert. To achieve this uniformity, the head chef designed a standard ticket with a fixed number of boxes:
> 
> - **Box 1 (Opcode):** What action? (e.g., "FRY", "BOIL", "CHOP")
> - **Box 2 (Condition):** Should it be prepared only if a special event happens? (e.g., "Only if VIP present" — this is the condition code)
> - **Box 3 (S flag):** Should the head chef announce when the dish is ready? (this updates the kitchen's status board — analogous to the CPSR flags)
> - **Box 4, 5, 6 (Rd, Rn, Operand2):** Where to put the result, what main ingredient to use, and how to prepare it.
> 
> Just as the kitchen hardware (the ARM pipeline) is fixed but the recipes (instructions) are flexible, the ARM instruction format gives you a **rigid 32-bit box** that can hold vastly different operations simply by changing the contents of the boxes. The "Flexible Second Operand" is like saying, "use an onion — but chopped, sliced, or whole" — all in a single ticket field.

### 1.3 Physical and Architectural Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Architectural Constants (Memorize These):**
> 
> - **Instruction word width = 32 bits (4 bytes)** — every standard ARM instruction is exactly one word.
> - **Opcode field width = varies** (typically 4–8 bits depending on the instruction class).
> - **Condition code field = 4 bits** (supports 16 condition codes; code `1110` = ALways, code `1111` is reserved/undefined in older ARM).
> - **S flag = 1 bit** (sets CPSR flags when `S=1`).
> - **Flexible Second Operand = 12 bits** (8 bits operand + 4 bits shift specification).
> - **General-purpose registers (GPRs) = 16 visible registers** (`R0`–`R12`, plus `R13=SP`, `R14=LR`, `R15=PC`).
> - **Immediate (literal) value = 8-bit number rotated right by an even number of bit positions**, yielding the full 32-bit immediate constant.

### 1.4 Three Classes of Operands

| Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Immediate operand** | A 12-bit pattern representing a 32-bit constant | `=0x200`, `#&FF` |
| **Register operand** | A direct reference to one of the 16 GPRs | `R0`, `R12`, `PC` |
| **Shifted register operand** | A register whose value is pre-shifted before being used | `R1, ROR #5`; `R2, LSL R3` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The *Flexible Second Operand* is the only operand position in an ARM instruction that permits the use of a *shifted* or *rotated* value. The first operand (`<Rn>`) is always a plain register. This asymmetry is a deliberate RISC design decision that simplifies the hardware datapath.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generic ARM Instruction Format — Field-by-Field Breakdown

The generic ARM instruction, when written in assembly source code, obeys the syntactic template:

$$\text{`<opcode>{<cond>} {S} <Rd>, <Rn>, <operand2>`}$$

The braces `{ }` indicate **optional** fields. Each field is described below in the order it appears on the source line and then mapped to its bit-field location in the 32-bit machine code.

| Field | Required? | Bit-Width in Machine Code | Purpose |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Opcode** | Yes | Variable (encodes instruction class) | Identifies the basic operation (ADD, SUB, AND, MOV, LDR, STR, B, …) |
| **Cond** (condition code) | Optional | **4 bits** | Tests CPSR flags; if false, the instruction is skipped (becomes a NOP) |
| **S** (set flags) | Optional | **1 bit** | If `S=1`, the CPSR N, Z, C, V flags are updated from the ALU result |
| **Rd** (destination reg) | Yes (for data ops) | **4 bits** | Register where the result of the operation is stored |
| **Rn** (first source reg) | Yes (except MOV) | **4 bits** | First source operand (must be a register) |
| **Operand2** | Yes (except CMP/TST) | **12 bits** | Flexible second operand: immediate or shifted/rotated register |

### 2.2 Condition Code Field (4 bits)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The condition code is a **defining feature** of the ARM ISA — virtually every instruction can be made conditional by appending a 2-letter suffix. This eliminates the need for short branch instructions in most cases, dramatically reducing code size and branch-pipeline penalties.

| Code | Suffix | CPSR Flags Tested | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| `0000` | `EQ` | Z=1 | Equal |
| `0001` | `NE` | Z=0 | Not equal |
| `0010` | `CS / HS` | C=1 | Carry set / Unsigned higher or same |
| `0011` | `CC / LO` | C=0 | Carry clear / Unsigned lower |
| `0100` | `MI` | N=1 | Minus (negative) |
| `0101` | `PL` | N=0 | Plus (positive or zero) |
| `0110` | `VS` | V=1 | Overflow |
| `0111` | `VC` | V=0 | No overflow |
| `1000` | `HI` | C=1 AND Z=0 | Unsigned higher |
| `1001` | `LS` | C=0 OR Z=1 | Unsigned lower or same |
| `1010` | `GE` | N=V | Signed greater or equal |
| `1011` | `LT` | N≠V | Signed less than |
| `1100` | `GT` | Z=0 AND N=V | Signed greater than |
| `1101` | `LE` | Z=1 OR N≠V | Signed less or equal |
| `1110` | `AL` | (always true) | Always (default if no suffix) |
| `1111` | (NV) | (special) | Reserved / never (in some cores: unconditional in User mode) |

> [!NOTE]
> **S flag + Condition:** Writing `ADDSEQ` is fully valid: the addition executes only if Z=1, AND if the instruction does execute, the S flag is set to update CPSR.

### 2.3 The Flexible Second Operand (12 bits)

The 12-bit field `operand2` is the most subtle part of the ARM ISA. The assembler decides, by examining the syntax, whether to encode it in **Immediate format** or **Register-shifted format**.

#### Case A — Immediate (literal) format

Used when the source code writes the operand as a numeric constant prefixed with `#` (or `=` for 32-bit constants in some assemblers).

- The 12 bits are split into a **4-bit rotate** field and an **8-bit immediate** field.
- The actual 32-bit value is reconstructed as:

$$V_{32} = \text{imm}_{8} \;\text{rotated right by}\; (2 \times \text{rotate})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** The rotate amount is **twice the value stored in the 4-bit field**, so legal rotations are 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, …, 30. This is why the rotation count is always even. Total reachable 32-bit values = $256 \times 16 = 4096$ distinct constants (one of them is duplicated at rotation 0, so 4095 *unique* useful constants).

**Example:** Suppose the bit field is `rotate=0010`, `imm=00000001`.

$$V_{32} = 0\text{x}01 \;\text{rotated right by}\; (2 \times 2) = 0\text{x}01 \;\text{ROR}\; 4 = 0\text{x}40000000$$

#### Case B — Register-shifted format

Used when the operand is a register optionally combined with a barrel-shifter operation.

- The 12 bits are split into a **4-bit shift code**, a **4-bit shift amount** (or `Rs`), and a **4-bit Rm field** (source register).
- The actual operand value is:

$$V_{32} = \text{Rm} \;\text{shifted by}\; (\text{shift\_amount})\;\text{using}\;(\text{shift\_code})$$

**Five shift codes:**

| Code | Mnemonic | Operation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `0000` | `LSL` | Logical Shift Left (fill with zeros) |
| `0001` | `LSR` | Logical Shift Right (fill with zeros) |
| `0010` | `ASR` | Arithmetic Shift Right (sign-extend) |
| `0011` | `ROR` | Rotate Right (32-bit rotation) |
| `0100` | `RRX` | Rotate Right Extended by 1 bit through the C flag |

### 2.4 Barrel Shifter as a Free Pre-Processor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight (KTU high-yield concept):** The ARM CPU contains a hardware unit called the **barrel shifter** sitting on the second operand's input of the ALU. Because it is a combinational circuit, it can apply any of the 5 shift/rotate operations to `Rm` in a single clock cycle **without using an extra instruction**. This means a single ARM instruction can perform a shift, a rotate, AND a logic/arithmetic operation in one cycle — equivalent to 2–3 instructions on a CISC processor like the 8086.

**Real-world engineering utility:** In a digital filter $y[n] = a \cdot x[n] + b \cdot y[n-1]$, the multiplications by $2^k$ are free shifts. For example, multiplying by 4 can be done by `LSL #2` inside an `ADD` instruction:

$$\text{ADD} \quad \text{R0, R1, R2, LSL \#2} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R0 = R1 + (R2 \times 4)$$

This is heavily exploited in DSP routines, audio codecs, and cryptographic algorithms running on ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Generic syntax:** `<opcode>{<cond>} {S} <Rd>, <Rn>, <operand2>` | Recognize any ARM data-processing instruction. |
| 2 | **Immediate value:** $V_{32} = \text{imm}_{8} \;\text{ROR}\; (2 \times \text{rotate}_{4\text{bit}})$ | Decode the 12-bit immediate field. |
| 3 | **Rotate count** is always **even** (0, 2, …, 30) | Quickly reject illegal encodes in exams. |
| 4 | **Barrel shifter** is a **combinational, single-cycle** pre-stage to the ALU | Justify why `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #3` takes 1 cycle. |
| 5 | **5 shift codes:** LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX | Remember all five — RRX is the most often forgotten. |
| 6 | **Condition code** = 4 bits; default suffix = `AL` | Know that `B` is equivalent to `BAL`. |
| 7 | **S flag** only sets N, Z, C, V when an instruction is **actually executed** (not skipped) | Distinguish from x86 where flags update unconditionally. |
| 8 | **Operand2 width** = **12 bits** | Distinguish from the 4-bit fields of `Rd` and `Rn`. |
| 9 | **PC (R15) reads as current address + 8** in ARM state | $PC = \text{address of current instruction} + 2 \times 4 = +8$ bytes. |
| 10 | **`<Rn>` is always a plain register**; shifting is **only** on `operand2` | The classic exam trap. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Example 1 — Decoding an Immediate Operand (KTU-Type Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An ARM instruction contains the 12-bit immediate field shown below (the field follows the standard `imm_8 : rotate_4` layout, where the lower 8 bits are the immediate and the upper 4 bits are the rotation code). Decode it.
> 
> **Binary field:** `0010 0000 0001`
> 
> - Upper 4 bits (`rotate`) = `0010` = 2 in decimal.
> - Lower 8 bits (`imm_8`) = `0000 0001` = `0x01`.

#### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the rotation amount.**

The 4-bit rotate field encodes a rotation that is always doubled:

$$\text{rotation} = 2 \times \text{rotate}_{4} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ bit positions (ROR 4)}$$

**Step 2 — Write the 8-bit immediate in 32-bit form, left-aligned to a 32-bit register.**

$$\text{imm}_{8} = 0\text{x}01 \;\Longrightarrow\; 0\text{x}00000001$$

**Step 3 — Apply the right-rotation by 4 bits.**

A right rotation of 4 bit positions means the 4 LSBs are moved to the 4 MSB positions:

$$0\text{x}00000001 \;\text{ROR}\; 4 = 0\text{x}10000000$$

Written out as a 32-bit value:

$$V_{32} = \text{0x}10000000 = 268{,}435{,}456 \text{ in decimal}$$

**Step 4 — State the final decoded constant.**

$$\boxed{V_{32} = \text{0x}10000000}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The bit pattern is `0001` followed by twenty-eight `0`s. Right-rotating `0000 0001` (which has a single 1 in the LSB) by 4 places lifts that 1 to bit position 28, which is the 4th bit from the top of a 32-bit word — i.e., $0\text{x}10000000$. ✓

### 3.2 Example 2 — Encoding a Constant as an Immediate

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** You want to write the constant `0xFF000000` as the immediate operand of an `AND` instruction. Find the `rotate_imm` and `imm_8` fields.

#### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Express the constant in binary (32 bits).**

$$0\text{xFF000000 = 1111 1111 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000}$$

**Step 2 — Count how many right-rotation positions are needed to bring this constant back to a form where the 8 LSBs carry the entire 1-pattern.**

The 1-pattern is in the upper 8 bits. We need to rotate right until these 8 ones end up in the lower 8 bits of the word. That requires a right rotation of:

$$\text{rotation} = 32 - 8 = 24 \text{ bit positions}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the 4-bit rotate code.**

$$\text{rotate}_{4} = \frac{\text{rotation}}{2} = \frac{24}{2} = 12 = 0\text{x}C$$

**Step 4 — Write the 8-bit immediate.**

After rotating right by 24, the value $0\text{x}FF000000$ becomes:

$$0\text{x}FF000000 \;\text{ROR}\; 24 = 0\text{x}000000FF = 0\text{x}FF$$

So:

$$\text{imm}_{8} = 0\text{x}FF = 1111 1111$$

**Step 5 — State the final encoded 12-bit field.**

$$\text{rotate}_{4} = 12 = 1100_{2}, \quad \text{imm}_{8} = 1111 1111_{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{12-bit field} = 1100\; 1111\; 1111 = \text{0xCFF}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trick (KTU 2024):** If the constant has a run of more than 8 contiguous ones OR a pattern that is not representable as 8 bits rotated by an even number, the constant is **NOT legal** as an ARM immediate. For instance, `0x12345678` cannot be encoded as an immediate. The assembler will reject it and you must use `MOV` from a literal pool or use `MVN`.

### 3.3 Example 3 — Tracing a Register-Shifted Operand

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Decode the semantics of the following ARM assembly instruction:
> 
> `SUBNES R3, R4, R5, LSR #6`
> 
> Assume CPSR flags are currently `N=0, Z=0, C=1, V=0`.

#### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the opcode.**

`SUB` → subtract. The destination will receive `Rn - operand2`.

**Step 2 — Identify the condition code.**

Suffix `NE` → `Z = 0`. Current Z = 0 → condition is **TRUE**, the instruction executes.

**Step 3 — Identify the S flag.**

The `S` letter is present in `SUBNES`, so CPSR flags will be updated.

**Step 4 — Decode the operands.**

- `Rd` = `R3` (destination).
- `Rn` = `R4` (first source).
- `operand2` = `R5, LSR #6` → second source is R5 **logically shifted right by 6 bit positions** (filled with zeros).

The semantic is:

$$R3 = R4 - (R5 \gg_{\text{logical}} 6)$$

**Step 5 — Trace the LSR pre-shift (if R5 = 0xABCDEF01).**

$$\text{R5} = 0\text{x}ABCDEF01 = 1010\;1011\;1100\;1101\;1110\;1111\;0000\;0001$$

Right-shift logically by 6 positions (insert zeros at MSB):

$$0\text{x}ABCDEF01 \;\text{LSR}\; 6 = 0\text{x}002AF37BC \text{ (hex, with low 6 bits discarded)}$$

Equivalently, in binary, the upper 6 bits become zeros, and we keep the lower 26 bits of R5:

$$\text{operand2} = 0\text{x}00\,2A\,F3\,BC$$

**Step 6 — Final expression of the operation.**

$$\boxed{R3 \leftarrow R4 - 0\text{x}002AF37BC \quad \text{(with CPSR updated)}}$$

### 3.4 Example 4 — RRX (Rotate Right Extended) Decoding

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The instruction is `ROR R1, R2, RRX`. If `R2 = 0x80000000` and `C` flag = 1, compute the new `R1`.

#### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Recall the RRX rule.**

`RRX` is a 1-bit right rotation **through** the C flag. Conceptually, treat the 33-bit value `(C : R2[31:0])` and right-rotate by 1.

$$\text{operand2} = \{C, R2\} \;\text{ROR}\; 1 = \{R2[0], C, R2[31:1]\}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

- `R2[0]` = LSB of 0x80000000 = 0.
- `C` (current) = 1.
- `R2[31:1]` = upper 31 bits of R2 = `0x40000000` (binary `0100 0000 ... 0000`).

After RRX:

- New bit 31 of `R1` ← old bit 0 of `R2` = 0.
- New bit 0 of `R1` ← old `C` = 1.
- All other bits shift right by 1.
- New C flag ← old bit 0 of `R2` = 0.

**Step 3 — Final 32-bit value.**

$$\boxed{R1 = 0\text{x}40000001, \quad C_{\text{new}} = 0}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decoding Cheat Sheet for Operand2

| Source-Code Operand | Encoding | Interpreted As |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `#0x200` | 12-bit field: `imm_8 + rotate_4` | $V = 0\text{x}200 \;\text{ROR}\; 0$ |
| `R5` | 12-bit field: `0000 0000 Rm` | $V = R5$ (no shift) |
| `R5, LSL #2` | 12-bit field: `000 00100 Rm` | $V = R5 \ll 2$ |
| `R5, LSR #5` | 12-bit field: `001 00101 Rm` | $V = R5 \gg_{\text{u}} 5$ |
| `R5, ASR #31` | 12-bit field: `010 11111 Rm` | $V = R5 \gg_{\text{s}} 31$ |
| `R5, ROR #8` | 12-bit field: `011 01000 Rm` | $V = \text{R5 rotated right by 8}$ |
| `R5, RRX` | 12-bit field: `011 00000 Rm` | $V = \{C, R5\} \text{ ROR } 1$ |
| `R5, LSL R6` | 12-bit field: `110 00000 Rm, Rs` | $V = R5 \ll (R6 \bmod 256)$ |
| `R5, LSR R6` | 12-bit field: `110 00010 Rm, Rs` | $V = R5 \gg_{\text{u}} (R6 \bmod 256)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic ARM Data-Processing Instruction Datapath

```mermaid
graph LR
    instrMem[Instruction Memory 32 bit word] --> decoder[Instruction Decoder]
    decoder --> opcode[Opcode Logic]
    decoder --> condChk[Condition Code Checker]
    decoder --> sFlag[S Flag Decoder]
    decoder --> rdF[Rd 4 bit field]
    decoder --> rnF[Rn 4 bit field]
    decoder --> op2F[Operand2 12 bit field]
    condChk --> cpsrFlags{CPSR Flags}
    cpsrFlags -->|if TRUE| enable[Enable Execution]
    cpsrFlags -->|if FALSE| skip[Skip Instruction NOP]
    op2F --> immOrShifter{Is Immediate or Shifted Reg}
    immOrShifter -->|Immediate| rotUnit[Rotate Right Unit 2 x rotate]
    immOrShifter -->|Shifted Register| barrel[Barrel Shifter LSL LSR ASR ROR RRX]
    rnF --> rnReg[Read Rn from Register Bank]
    rdF --> rdReg[Write Rd to Register Bank]
    enable --> aluMain[32 bit ALU]
    rnReg --> aluMain
    rotUnit --> aluMain
    barrel --> aluMain
    aluMain --> resultBus[32 bit Result Bus]
    sFlag -->|S 1| flagUpdate[Update N Z C V in CPSR]
    resultBus --> rdReg
    flagUpdate --> cpsrFlags
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Flexible Second Operand (Operand2) Decoding

```mermaid
graph TD
    op2Field[Operand2 12 bit field] --> bit11{Bit 25 of Instruction}
    bit11 -->|0 Immediate Format| immDec[Decode as imm8 plus rotate4]
    bit11 -->|1 Register Format| regDec[Decode as Rm plus Shift Spec]
    immDec --> rotOp[Rotate Right by 2 times rotate4]
    rotOp --> immOut[32 bit Immediate Constant]
    regDec --> shCode[Shift Code 2 bits]
    regDec --> shAmt[Shift Amount 5 bits or Rs]
    regDec --> rmSel[Source Register Rm 4 bits]
    shCode --> muxSel{Select Shift Operation}
    muxSel -->|00| lslOp[LSL Logical Shift Left]
    muxSel -->|01| lsrOp[LSR Logical Shift Right]
    muxSel -->|10| asrOp[ASR Arithmetic Shift Right]
    muxSel -->|11| rorOp[ROR Rotate Right or RRX]
    shAmt --> lslOp
    shAmt --> lsrOp
    shAmt --> asrOp
    shAmt --> rorOp
    rmSel --> lslOp
    rmSel --> lsrOp
    rmSel --> asrOp
    rmSel --> rorOp
    lslOp --> regOut[32 bit Shifted Register Value]
    lsrOp --> regOut
    asrOp --> regOut
    rorOp --> regOut
    immOut --> finalMux[Operand2 Output to ALU B input]
    regOut --> finalMux
```

### 4.3 Flowchart — How the Assembler Encodes an Operand2

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start Parsing Operand2] --> q1{Is operand a numeric constant with hash}
    q1 -->|YES| immPath[Use Immediate Format]
    immPath --> check1{Can the constant be written as 8 bits rotated by even count}
    check1 -->|YES| encodeImm[Encode imm8 in lower 8 bits and rotate in upper 4 bits]
    check1 -->|NO| rejectImm[Assembler Error Constant too large Use MOV from literal pool]
    q1 -->|NO| q2{Is operand a register name}
    q2 -->|NO| syntaxError[Syntax Error]
    q2 -->|YES| q3{Is there a comma after the register}
    q3 -->|NO| encodePlain[Encode Rm with shift code 000 and shift amount 00000]
    q3 -->|YES| q4{Does the next token start with a shift mnemonic}
    q4 -->|NO| syntaxError
    q4 -->|YES| q5{Is the shift amount a hash number or a register}
    q5 -->|Hash number| encodeImmShift[Encode with 5 bit immediate shift amount]
    q5 -->|Register name Rs| encodeRegShift[Encode with 4 bit Rs in shift amount field and bit 4 set]
    encodePlain --> endOut[Emit 12 bit Operand2]
    encodeImm --> endOut
    encodeImmShift --> endOut
    encodeRegShift --> endOut
```

### 4.4 Concept Map — The 32-bit ARM Data-Processing Instruction Word

```mermaid
graph LR
    word[ARM Instruction Word 32 bits] --> cond[Bits 31 to 28 Condition Code 4 bits]
    word --> f1[Bit 27 to 26 Type Field]
    word --> opcode[Bits 24 to 21 Opcode 4 bits]
    word --> sBit[Bit 20 S flag 1 bit]
    word --> rnBits[Bits 19 to 16 Rn 4 bits]
    word --> rdBits[Bits 15 to 12 Rd 4 bits]
    word --> op2Bits[Bits 11 to 0 Operand2 12 bits]
    cond --> meaning1[Determines whether instruction executes]
    f1 --> meaning2[00 Data Processing 01 Data Processing Imm 10 Load Store 11 Branch]
    opcode --> meaning3[ADD SUB AND ORR EOR MOV CMP TST etc]
    sBit --> meaning4[1 update N Z C V 0 leave unchanged]
    rnBits --> meaning5[First ALU source register]
    rdBits --> meaning6[ALU destination register]
    op2Bits --> meaning7[Second ALU source immediate or shifted register]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams are block-level functional architecture flows that map the data path and decode logic of the ARM instruction format. The "physical drawing" of the 32-bit instruction word is represented as a binary bit-field map; this is the most faithful schematic possible in Mermaid syntax, since the instruction is itself a bit-field structure.
