> The following diagrams use the strict Mermaid node identifier rules: all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (no reserved keywords), and all labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the STR Instruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DECODE STAGE FETCH STR OPCODE] --> B[READ BASE REGISTER RN]
    A --> C[READ SOURCE REGISTER RT]
    B --> D[OFFSET COMPUTATION UNIT]
    E[OFFSET FIELD FROM INSTRUCTION] --> D
    D --> F[EFFECTIVE ADDRESS EA CALCULATION]
    F --> G{WRITE BACK FLAG QUESTION MARK}
    G -- YES H HAS EXCLAMATION --> H[UPDATE BASE REGISTER RN]
    G -- NO I NO FLAG --> I[KEEP RN UNCHANGED]
    H --> J[INITIATE AHB WRITE TRANSACTION]
    I --> J
    C --> J
    J --> K[DATA BUS CARRIES RT TO MEMORY]
    K --> L[STORE BYTE OR HALFWORD OR WORD SELECTOR]
    L --> M[MEMORY WRITTEN AT ADDRESS EA]
    M --> N[EXECUTION COMPLETE FLAG SET]
```

**Diagram Interpretation (Step-by-Step Walkthrough):**

1. The instruction `STR Rt, [Rn, #offset]!` is fetched and decoded in the **Decode stage**.
2. The base register `Rn` and the source register `Rt` are read from the register file.
3. The **Offset Computation Unit** evaluates the offset (immediate, register, or shifted register).
4. The **Effective Address Calculation** produces `EA = Rn + offset`.
5. The **Write-Back flag check** decides whether to update `Rn`:
   - If `!` is present → **YES path** → `Rn ← EA`.
   - If `!` is absent → **NO path** → `Rn` remains the original value.
6. The core initiates an **AHB/APB write transaction** to memory at address `EA`.
7. The data bus carries the contents of `Rt` (or the appropriate byte/halfword slice).
8. Memory is updated and the execution completes.

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Memory Layout After a 4-Register STMIA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addr100[ADDRESS 0X2000 0000 R0 VALUE] --> addr104[ADDRESS 0X2000 0004 R1 VALUE]
    addr104 --> addr108[ADDRESS 0X2000 0008 R2 VALUE]
    addr108 --> addr10C[ADDRESS 0X2000 000C R3 VALUE]
    addr10C --> arr[ARRAY REGION]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This shows that after `STMIA R0!, {R0-R3}`, the memory at addresses `0x20000000` through `0x2000000C` holds the values of R0, R1, R2, R3 respectively, and `R0` itself is updated to point to `0x20000010` (the next free location).

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Write-Back Decision Tree

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FETCH
    FETCH --> DECODE
    DECODE --> CHECK_SUFFIX
    CHECK_SUFFIX --> OFFSET_ONLY: NO EXCLAMATION
    CHECK_SUFFIX --> PRE_INDEXED: EXCLAMATION PRESENT
    CHECK_SUFFIX --> POST_INDEXED: COMMA AFTER BRACKET
    OFFSET_ONLY --> STR_OFFSET
    PRE_INDEXED --> STR_PRE
    POST_INDEXED --> STR_POST
    STR_OFFSET --> [*]
    STR_PRE --> [*]
    STR_POST --> [*]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This state diagram clarifies the *parsing* of an ARM store instruction. The presence and position of the exclamation mark `!` and the optional trailing offset are the syntactic markers that determine the indexing mode.

### 4.5 ASCII Memory Map Visualisation (FD Stack After PUSH)

```
        High Address
        +-------------------+ 0x4000 2000  (Original SP)
        |  LR               |  0x4000 1FFC
        +-------------------+
        |  R7               |  0x4000 1FF8
        +-------------------+
        |  R6               |  0x4000 1FF4
        +-------------------+
        |  R5               |  0x4000 1FF0
        +-------------------+
        |  R4               |  0x4000 1FEC  (New SP after PUSH)
        +-------------------+ <-- SP points here (Full Descending)
        |  (free stack)     |  0x4000 1FE8
        |  ...              |
        Low Address
```

This diagram visually reinforces the **Full Descending** stack convention: the SP points to the last item pushed (R4), and future pushes will write to *lower* addresses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Q1a, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between the three indexing modes of the ARM `STR` instruction with a suitable example for each.

**Model Answer:**

The ARM `STR` instruction supports three addressing modes for computing the effective address (EA):

1. **Offset (No write-back):** The offset is added to the base register, but the base register is *not* updated. Example:

   ```assembly
   STR R1, [R0, #8]   ; Stores R1 at address R0+8, R0 unchanged
   ```

2. **Pre-indexed (Write-back with `!`):** The base register is updated with the new effective address *before* the memory access. Example:

   ```assembly
   STR R1, [R0, #8]!  ; Stores R1 at R0+8, then R0 ← R0+8
   ```

3. **Post-indexed:** The store occurs at the *original* base address, and the base register is then updated. Example:

   ```assembly
   STR R1, [R0], #8   ; Stores R1 at [R0], then R0 ← R0+8
   ```

The key differentiator is the **timing of the base-register update**: never, before, or after the memory access.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q2b, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** What is the role of the `!` (write-back) flag in ARM store instructions? Why is it commonly used in the `PUSH` instruction?

**Model Answer:**

The `!` flag enables **write-back** of the base register, meaning the base register is automatically updated with the new effective address after the address calculation. It is a **concise way to update pointers in a single instruction**, saving both code size and execution cycles.

In the `PUSH` instruction (synonym for `STMDB SP!, {reg-list}`), the `!` flag updates the Stack Pointer (SP) to reflect the new top-of-stack after the registers have been stored. Without `!`, SP would still point to the *old* top of the stack, leading to incorrect behavior on subsequent pushes and pops. Thus, `!` is essential for correct stack management.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Q11, 14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice]**
> **Q11(A).**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three indexing modes of the ARM `STR` instruction. Show with a clear example how each mode affects the value of the base register.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Write an ARM assembly program to copy a block of 10 words from a source array (starting at `0x40000000`) to a destination array (starting at `0x40000020`). Use the most efficient addressing mode and explain your choice.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation of the Three Indexing Modes** (covered in the Q1 model answer above; expand with a numerical example):

Assume `R0 = 0x1000`, `R2 = 0xABCD1234`.

- **Offset mode:** `STR R2, [R0, #4]` → EA = 0x1004. Memory at 0x1004 ← 0xABCD1234. **R0 remains 0x1000.**
- **Pre-indexed mode:** `STR R2, [R0, #4]!` → EA = 0x1004. Memory at 0x1004 ← 0xABCD1234. **R0 ← 0x1004.**
- **Post-indexed mode:** `STR R2, [R0], #4` → EA = 0x1000. Memory at 0x1000 ← 0xABCD1234. **R0 ← 0x1004.**

**(b) ARM Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        AREA    BlockCopy, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        MOV     R0, #0x40000000      ; R0 = source base address
        MOV     R1, #0x40000020      ; R1 = destination base address
        MOV     R2, #10              ; R2 = word count

LOOP    LDR     R3, [R0], #4         ; Load from source, R0 ← R0+4 (post-indexed)
        STR     R3, [R1], #4         ; Store to destination, R1 ← R1+4 (post-indexed)
        SUBS    R2, R2, #1           ; Decrement counter
        BNE     LOOP                 ; Branch if counter != 0
STOP    B       STOP
        END
```

**Explanation of choice:** Post-indexed addressing is the **most efficient** for sequential array traversal because it advances the pointer *automatically* in the same instruction that performs the data transfer, eliminating the need for a separate `ADD` instruction. This results in a **4-instruction loop body** (LDR, STR, SUBS, BNE), which is optimal for an ARM core.

**Efficiency Analysis:**

$$\text{Cycles per iteration} = 1_{\text{LDR}} + 1_{\text{STR}} + 1_{\text{SUBS}} + 1_{\text{BNE (taken)}} = 4 \text{ cycles}$$

$$\text{Total cycles} = 4 \times 10 = 40 \text{ cycles (excluding the final fall-through)}$$

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Q11, 14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice – Alternative]**
> **Q11(B).**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the ARM `STM` (Store Multiple) instruction with its variants. How is it used to implement a PUSH operation on a Full Descending stack?
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Given the initial state `SP = 0x40002000`, `R4 = 0x11111111`, `R5 = 0x22222222`, `R6 = 0x33333333`, trace the execution of `PUSH {R4-R6}` and draw the resulting memory map.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) STM Variants and PUSH Implementation:**

The `STM` (Store Multiple) instruction stores a subset of registers to memory, with optional write-back (`!`). The four main variants are:

- **STMIA (Increment After):** Stores at increasing addresses; base is updated *after* the last transfer.
- **STMIB (Increment Before):** Stores at increasing addresses; base is updated *before* each transfer.
- **STMDA (Decrement After):** Stores at decreasing addresses; base is updated *after* the last transfer.
- **STMDB (Decrement Before):** Stores at decreasing addresses; base is updated *before* each transfer.

For a **Full Descending (FD) stack**, the convention is:
- The stack grows toward **lower** addresses.
- SP points to the **last item pushed** (the highest used address).

`PUSH` is implemented as `STMDB SP!, {reg-list}`:
- **DB** ensures the address is decremented *before* each store (so SP ends up pointing to the lowest written address, which is the address of the first/lowest-numbered register in the list).
- **`!`** updates SP automatically (write-back).

Example: `PUSH {R0, R1, LR}` is equivalent to `STMDB SP!, {R0, R1, LR}`.

**(b) Memory Trace of `PUSH {R4-R6}`:**

Initial state:
- `SP = 0x40002000`
- `R4 = 0x11111111`, `R5 = 0x22222222`, `R6 = 0x33333333`

**Step 1: Compute the new SP.**

Number of registers $N = 3$. Total bytes = $3 \times 4 = 12 = 0x0C$.

$$SP_{\text{new}} = SP_{\text{old}} - 0x0C = 0x40002000 - 0x0C = 0x40001FF4$$

**Step 2: Decrement-Before Address Mapping.**

For each register in the list (lowest-numbered first):

| Register | Effective Address | Memory After PUSH |
|---|---|---|
| R4 | $0x40002000 - 4 \times 3 = 0x40001FF4$ | 0x11111111 |
| R5 | $0x40002000 - 4 \times 2 = 0x40001FF8$ | 0x22222222 |
| R6 | $0x40002000 - 4 \times 1 = 0x40001FFC$ | 0x33333333 |

**Final State:** `SP = 0x40001FF4` (write-back). The registers R4, R5, R6 are saved on the stack, with R4 at the lowest address and R6 at the highest (still below the original SP).

**Resulting Memory Map:**

```
        High Address
        +-------------------+ 0x4000 2000  (Original SP)
        |  R6 = 0x33333333 |  0x4000 1FFC
        +-------------------+
        |  R5 = 0x22222222 |  0x4000 1FF8
        +-------------------+
        |  R4 = 0x11111111 |  0x4000 1FF4  (New SP)
        +-------------------+ <-- SP
        |  (free)           |
        Low Address
```

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing pre-indexed and post-indexed:** Many students wrongly state that `[Rn], #offset` updates `Rn` *before* the access. **It updates AFTER.**
> 2. **Forgetting the `!` flag:** Writing `STMDB SP, {R4-R7, LR}` without `!` is a **syntax error** (the assembler will reject it) and also defeats the purpose of the PUSH. Always include `!` for STM with a register list in a loop or stack operation.
> 3. **Alignment violations:** In strict ARM (ARMv4T/ARMv5), an unaligned word access causes a **Data Abort**. Students often overlook this in exam questions asking about edge cases. Cortex-M (ARMv7-M) is more lenient, but the strict-ARM rule is the **exam default**.
> 4. **Endianness confusion:** When drawing memory maps, students frequently swap byte order. In **Little-Endian** (the default for Cortex-M), the **LSB is at the lowest address**.
> 5. **Counting registers in STM:** A common error is to count the registers in `{R4-R7, LR}` as 4 (instead of 5) because of the "range" notation. **Always expand the list explicitly** when calculating byte counts: `{R4, R5, R6, R7, LR}` → 5 registers → 20 bytes.
> 6. **Mixing STR/STM semantics:** Some students apply the post-indexed logic to `STRB` or `STRH` incorrectly. The byte/halfword variants follow the **same** indexing rules as the word variant, but transfer fewer bytes.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Load/Store Architecture:** The fundamental ARM principle — memory is accessed **only** through LDR/STR family instructions.
- **STR Variants:** `STR` (word, 4 bytes), `STRB` (byte, 1 byte), `STRH` (halfword, 2 bytes), `STRD` (doubleword, 8 bytes).
- **Three Indexing Modes:**
  - `STR Rt, [Rn, #off]` — offset, no write-back.
  - `STR Rt, [Rn, #off]!` — pre-indexed, with write-back.
  - `STR Rt, [Rn], #off` — post-indexed, write-back after access.
- **Write-back flag `!`:** Updates the base register automatically. **Critical** for loops and stack operations.
- **Alignment Rules (Strict ARM):** Word accesses need address divisible by 4; halfword accesses need address divisible by 2. Misaligned accesses cause a Data Abort.
- **STM Variants:** IA, IB, DA, DB. AAPCS uses **STMDB** (PUSH) and **LDMIA** (POP) for the Full Descending stack.
- **FD Stack Convention:** SP points to the last item pushed; stack grows downward. Push = `STMDB SP!, {reg-list}`. Pop = `LDMIA SP!, {reg-list}`.
- **Byte Count Formula:** Total memory transferred = $4N$ bytes for an $N$-register STM.
- **Efficiency:** Post-indexed addressing is the canonical idiom for array traversal, combining data access and pointer update in a single instruction.
- **Endianness:** Cortex-M (and most KTU lab targets) use **Little-Endian**: LSB at the lowest address.
- **Special Registers to Save in ISRs:** R0–R3 and R12 are *caller-saved*; R4–R11 and LR are *callee-saved*. PUSH/POP should target R4–R11 and LR for context preservation.
- **Link Register (LR / R14):** Holds the return address; must be saved explicitly during a function call if the function itself calls another function (nested calls).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Converting C Assignment Statements to Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Converting C Assignment Statements to Assembly ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Converting C Assignment Statements to Assembly** is the systematic process of translating high-level C language assignment expressions into their equivalent low-level **ARM Cortex-M Assembly Instruction sequences**, while strictly adhering to the **ARM Load-Store (Register-Register) RISC Architecture** principles. In this translation paradigm, every C assignment statement is decomposed into a sequence of `LDR` (Load Register), data-processing (`MOV`, `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`), and `STR` (Store Register) instructions, where local variables are mapped to **Stack Frame offsets** relative to the Stack Pointer (`R13 / SP`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The ARM Cortex-M processor follows a **Load-Store Architecture**. This means **NO data-processing instruction may directly access memory**. All operands must first be loaded into General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12), processed, and the result stored back to memory. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are a **chef translating a recipe from English (C language) into precise kitchen commands (ARM Assembly)**: | Recipe in English (C) | Kitchen Command (ARM Assembly) | | :--- | :--- | | "Take 2 cups of sugar" | `LDR R0, [SP, #sugar_address]` | | "Add 1 cup of flour" | `LDR R1, [SP, #flour_address]`
`ADD R0, R0, R1` | | "Pour mixture into bowl" | `STR R0, [SP, #bowl_address]` | The English recipe assumes the chef has ingredients on the counter. The kitchen command requires **fetching the ingredient (load)**, **mixing on the workbench (register)**, and **placing in the container (store)**. The "workbench" is the ARM register file; the "shelves" are memory locations. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Architectural Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key ARM Cortex-M Architectural Parameters (Memorize These):** > - **Register Width = 32 bits (4 bytes)** — All ARM core registers (R0–R15) are 32-bit. > - **General-Purpose Registers = R0 to R12** (13 registers). > - **Special Registers:** `R13 = SP` (Stack Pointer), `R14 = LR` (Link Register), `R15 = PC` (Program Counter). > - **Stack Growth Direction = Full-Descending** (SP decrements on `PUSH`). > - **Standard Word Size for `int` = 32 bits** in ARM Cortex-M. ### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations This topic is a **favourite of KTU Examiners** because it directly tests three Course Outcomes (COs): - **CO2:** Understanding ARM instruction set architecture. - **CO3:** Programming ARM processors in Assembly and embedded C. - **CO4:** Analyzing the mapping between high-level constructs and hardware-level execution. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three-Stage Conversion Philosophy Every C assignment statement `a = expression;` must be converted through these three logical stages: 1. **Stage 1 — Memory-to-Register Transfer (LDR):** For every C variable appearing in the RHS, generate an `LDR` instruction to fetch the value from the stack frame into a register. 2. **Stage 2 — Register-Level Computation (MOV, ADD, SUB, MUL, etc.):** Apply data-processing instructions on the registers. Parentheses in C determine the **order of evaluation**. 3. **Stage 3 — Register-to-Memory Transfer (STR):** The final computed value in a register must be `STR` (stored) back to the memory location of the LHS variable. ### 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Conversion Logic - **Why use R0 as the accumulator?** — Convention adopted by ARM compilers. R0–R3 are also the **Argument & Result Registers** (AAPCS standard). For a single result, R0 is the natural choice. - **Why are local variables on the stack?** — Function-scoped variables get a stack frame allocated by the prologue (`PUSH {LR}` etc.) and deallocated by the epilogue. Their address is computed as `SP + offset`. - **How are constants handled?** — Small constants (fitting in 12-bit immediate rotated) use the `#` prefix (e.g., `MOV R0, #100`). Larger constants require `LDR R0, =constant` (literal pool lookup). ### 2.3 C-to-ARM Data Type Mapping Table > [!NOTE] > **Critical Mapping (Examiners frequently test this):** | C Data Type | Typical Size (bytes) | ARM Register Allocation | Example | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | `char` | 1 | 32-bit register (zero/sign-extended) | `LDRSB` for signed char | | `short` | 2 | 32-bit register (zero/sign-extended) | `LDRSH` for signed short | | `int` | 4 | Full 32-bit register (`R0`) | `LDR R0, [SP, #0]` | | `long` | 4 | Full 32-bit register (`R0`) | `LDR R0, [SP, #4]` | | `float` | 4 | Single-precision register | `VLDR` (FPU) — *out of syllabus* | | `pointer` | 4 | Full 32-bit register | `LDR R0, [SP, #8]` | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — C Operator → ARM Instruction Mapping > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it solves 80% of conversion problems in KTU exams.** Note the use of `\vert` (not `\|`) for the OR operator to preserve Markdown table integrity. | C Operator / Construct | ARM Instruction (s) | Mathematical Form | Condition Flags Updated | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `a = constant` | `MOV R0, #constant` | $R_0 \leftarrow \text{constant}$ | `MOVS` updates N, Z | | `a = b` | `LDR R0,[SP,#b]`
`STR R0,[SP,#a]` | $R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+b]$ | No | | `a = b + c` | `LDR R0,[SP,#b]`
`LDR R1,[SP,#c]`
`ADD R0,R0,R1`
`STR R0,[SP,#a]` | $R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+b] + M[SP+c]$ | `ADD` no, `ADDS` yes | | `a = b - c` | `LDR R0,[SP,#b]`
`LDR R1,[SP,#c]`
`SUB R0,R0,R1`
`STR R0,[SP,#a]` | $R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+b] - M[SP+c]$ | `SUBS` updates C, N, Z, V | | `a = b * c` | `LDR R0,[SP,#b]`
`LDR R1,[SP,#c]`
`MUL R0,R0,R1`
`STR R0,[SP,#a]` | $R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+b] \times M[SP+c]$ | `MULS` updates N, Z | | `a = -b` | `LDR R0,[SP,#b]`
`RSB R0,R0,#0`
`STR R0,[SP,#a]` | $R_0 \leftarrow 0 - M[SP+b]$ | `RSBS` updates N, Z, C, V | | `a = b + 5` | `LDR R0,[SP,#b]`
`ADD R0,R0,#5`
`STR R0,[SP,#a]` | $R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+b] + 5$ | `ADDS` updates flags | | `a = b & c` (bitwise AND) | `LDR R0,[SP,#b]`
`LDR R1,[SP,#c]`
`AND R0,R0,R1`
`STR R0,[SP,#a]` | $R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+b] \,\vert\, M[SP+c]$ bitwise | `ANDS` updates N, Z | | `a = b vert vert c` (OR) | `ORR R0,R0,R1` | $R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+b] \,\vert\, M[SP+c]$ | `ORRS` updates N, Z | | `a = b ^ c` (XOR) | `EOR R0,R0,R1` | $R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+b] \oplus M[SP+c]$ | `EORS` updates N, Z | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Address Formula for Local Variables:** $\text{Address}(v) = SP + \text{offset}_v$, where $\text{offset}_v$ is the variable's displacement within the stack frame (assigned by the compiler during prologue allocation). ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility This conversion technique is foundational in: - **Embedded Firmware Development:** When debugging with a JTAG/SWD debugger, the developer must trace through generated assembly to find timing bottlenecks. - **Compiler Verification:** Validating that the ARMCC/GCC compiler produces optimal code for time-critical ISRs (Interrupt Service Routines). - **Reverse Engineering:** Analyzing IoT firmware binaries (e.g., STM32 firmware) where the original C source is unavailable. - **Performance-Critical Code:** Writing hand-tuned assembly for DSP loops on ARM Cortex-M4/M7. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Convention used in all derivations:** Local variables `a, b, c, d` reside on the stack at offsets `[SP, #0]`, `[SP, #4]`, `[SP, #8]`, `[SP, #12]` respectively. The result is accumulated in `R0` and then stored back to the LHS variable. ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simple Constant Assignment **C Statement:** ```c a = 25; ``` **Step-by-step Logic:** **Step 1 (MOV):** Load the immediate constant 25 into R0. $$\text{MOV } R_0, \#25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_0 \leftarrow 25$$ **Step 2 (STR):** Store the contents of R0 into the memory location of variable `a`. $$\text{STR } R_0, [SP, \#0] \quad \Rightarrow \quad M[SP+0] \leftarrow R_0$$ **Final Assembly:** ```assembly MOV R0, #25 STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = 25 ``` **Explanation:** Since the RHS is a compile-time constant, no `LDR` is needed. The constant is embedded in the 32-bit instruction word. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Direct Register-to-Variable Copy **C Statement:** ```c a = b; ``` **Step-by-step Logic:** **Step 1 (LDR):** Load the value of `b` (at offset 4) into R0. $$\text{LDR } R_0, [SP, \#4] \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+4]$$ **Step 2 (STR):** Store R0 into the location of `a` (at offset 0). $$\text{STR } R_0, [SP, \#0] \quad \Rightarrow \quad M[SP+0] \leftarrow R_0$$ **Final Assembly:** ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Explanation:** A single load-store pair suffices. No data-processing is required. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Binary Addition **C Statement:** ```c a = b + c; ``` **Step-by-step Logic:** **Step 1 (LDR):** Load `b` into R0. $$R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+4]$$ **Step 2 (LDR):** Load `c` into R1. $$R_1 \leftarrow M[SP+8]$$ **Step 3 (ADD):** Add R0 and R1, store result in R0. $$R_0 \leftarrow R_0 + R_1$$ **Step 4 (STR):** Store R0 into `a`. $$M[SP+0] \leftarrow R_0$$ **Final Assembly:** ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b LDR R1, [SP, #8] ; R1 = c ADD R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = b + c STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Explanation:** The `ADD` instruction is destructive on the destination (R0). We could have alternatively used `ADD R0, R1, R0`, but the conventional form is preferred. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Binary Subtraction **C Statement:** ```c a = b - c; ``` **Step-by-step Logic:** **Step 1:** Load `b` into R0. **Step 2:** Load `c` into R1. **Step 3:** Subtract R1 from R0. $$R_0 \leftarrow R_0 - R_1$$ **Step 4:** Store R0 into `a`. **Final Assembly:** ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b LDR R1, [SP, #8] ; R1 = c SUB R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = b - c STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Explanation:** Order matters in subtraction. `SUB R0, R0, R1` computes $b - c$. Reversing R0 and R1 would invert the meaning. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Multiplication **C Statement:** ```c a = b * c; ``` **Step-by-step Logic:** **Step 1:** Load `b` into R0. **Step 2:** Load `c` into R1. **Step 3:** Multiply R0 and R1. $$R_0 \leftarrow R_0 \times R_1$$ **Step 4:** Store R0 into `a`. **Final Assembly:** ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b LDR R1, [SP, #8] ; R1 = c MUL R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = b * c STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Explanation:** `MUL` in ARM Cortex-M stores the **lower 32 bits** of the 64-bit product. The upper 32 bits are discarded (no `SMULL`/`UMULL` unless extended precision is required). --- ### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Compound Expression with Parentheses **C Statement:** ```c a = (b + c) - d; ``` **Step-by-step Logic:** **Step 1:** Evaluate the parenthesized sub-expression `(b + c)` first. **Step 2:** Subtract `d` from the result. **Final Assembly:** ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b LDR R1, [SP, #8] ; R1 = c ADD R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = b + c (parenthesis evaluated first) LDR R1, [SP, #12] ; R1 = d SUB R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = (b+c) - d STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Explanation:** Operator precedence: `*`, `/` before `+`, `-`. Parentheses override precedence. Here `+` has higher precedence than `-` from left to right, so `(b + c)` is computed first. --- ### 3.7 Derivation 7 — Mixed Constants and Variables **C Statement:** ```c a = b + 10; ``` **Step-by-step Logic:** **Step 1:** Load `b` into R0. **Step 2:** Add immediate constant 10 to R0 (no second LDR needed). $$R_0 \leftarrow R_0 + 10$$ **Step 3:** Store R0 into `a`. **Final Assembly:** ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b ADD R0, R0, #10 ; R0 = b + 10 STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Explanation:** ARM `ADD` supports an **immediate operand**, eliminating the need for a second `LDR`. This is a key optimization. --- ### 3.8 Derivation 8 — Negation of a Variable **C Statement:** ```c a = -b; ``` **Step-by-step Logic:** **Step 1:** Load `b` into R0. **Step 2:** Use `RSB` (Reverse Subtract) to compute $0 - b$. $$R_0 \leftarrow 0 - R_0 = -R_0$$ **Step 3:** Store R0 into `a`. **Final Assembly:** ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b RSB R0, R0, #0 ; R0 = 0 - b = -b STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Explanation:** `RSB Rd, Rn, #0` is the canonical ARM idiom for two's complement negation, as ARM does not have a dedicated `NEG` instruction in the base set. --- ### 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Step-Count Summary | C Statement | LDR Count | Data-Proc Count | STR Count | Total Instructions | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | `a = 25` | 0 | 1 (MOV) | 1 | **2** | | `a = b` | 1 | 0 | 1 | **2** | | `a = b + c` | 2 | 1 (ADD) | 1 | **4** | | `a = b - c` | 2 | 1 (SUB) | 1 | **4** | | `a = b * c` | 2 | 1 (MUL) | 1 | **4** | | `a = b + 10` | 1 | 1 (ADD #imm) | 1 | **3** | | `a = (b+c) - d` | 3 | 2 (ADD, SUB) | 1 | **6** | | `a = -b` | 1 | 1 (RSB) | 1 | **3** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Flow — C to ARM Assembly Conversion Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A["C Assignment Statement
a = b + c"] --> B{"Parse RHS
Identify Operands"} B --> C1["Operand 1: b
Memory Variable"] B --> C2["Operand 2: c
Memory Variable"] C1 --> D1["Stage 1: LDR R0, [SP, #4]"] C2 --> D2["Stage 1: LDR R1, [SP, #8]"] D1 --> E["Stage 2: Data Processing
ADD R0, R0, R1"] D2 --> E E --> F["Stage 3: STR R0, [SP, #0]"] F --> G["Final Assembly Code
4 Instructions"] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style E fill:#ffe1e1 style G fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level ARM Register & Memory Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MEM["MEMORY : Stack Frame"] M0["SP + 0 : a"] M4["SP + 4 : b"] M8["SP + 8 : c"] M12["SP + 12 : d"] end subgraph REG["ARM REGISTER FILE (R0-R12)"] R0["R0 : Accumulator"] R1["R1 : Operand Holder"] R2["R2 : Operand Holder"] end M4 -- "LDR R0, [SP, #4]" --> R0 M8 -- "LDR R1, [SP, #8]" --> R1 R1 -- "ADD R0, R0, R1" --> R0 R0 -- "STR R0, [SP, #0]" --> M0 style MEM fill:#e1f0ff style REG fill:#ffe1f0 style R0 fill:#90ee90 style R1 fill:#ffb6c1 ``` ### 4.3 Instruction Category Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is RHS a
compile-time
constant?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> A1["Use MOV Rd, #imm
No LDR needed"] Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"How many
memory operands?"} Q2 -- "One (e.g., a = b)" --> A2["1 LDR + 1 STR"] Q2 -- "Two (e.g., a = b+c)" --> A3["2 LDR + 1 DP + 1 STR"] Q2 -- "Three or more" --> A4["N LDR + N-1 DP + 1 STR"] A3 --> Q3{"Any immediate
constant present?"} Q3 -- "Yes" --> A5["Use ADD Rd, Rn, #imm
(saves 1 LDR)"] Q3 -- "No" --> A6["Use ADD/SUB Rd, Rn, Rm"] style Q1 fill:#fffacd style Q2 fill:#fffacd style Q3 fill:#fffacd style A1 fill:#90ee90 style A2 fill:#90ee90 style A3 fill:#90ee90 style A5 fill:#add8e6 ``` ### 4.4 Conversion Pattern Schematic — Three-Stage Memory-to-Memory Operation ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Mem as Memory (Stack) participant Reg as Register R0 participant ALU as ARM ALU Note over Mem,ALU: Stage 1: LOAD Mem->>Reg: LDR R0, [SP, #b_offset] Note over Reg: R0 now holds b Note over Mem,ALU: Stage 2: PROCESS Reg->>ALU: Operand 1 (b) Mem->>ALU: LDR R1, [SP, #c_offset] ALU->>Reg: ADD R0, R0, R1 Note over Reg: R0 now holds b+c Note over Mem,ALU: Stage 3: STORE Reg->>Mem: STR R0, [SP, #a_offset] Note over Mem: a now holds b+c ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2]** *What is meant by the "Load-Store Architecture" of ARM Cortex-M? Why is it relevant when converting C assignment statements to ARM assembly?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** ARM Cortex-M follows a **Load-Store (Register-Register) RISC Architecture**, where data-processing instructions can operate **only on registers**, never directly on memory locations (1 mark). The relevance to C-to-Assembly conversion is that every C variable on the RHS must first be loaded into a register using `LDR`, processed using instructions like `ADD`/`SUB`/`MUL`, and then the result must be explicitly stored back to memory using `STR` (1 mark). For example, `a = b + c` requires `LDR` instructions for both `b` and `c`, an `ADD` on the registers, and a `STR` to write back to `a` (1 mark). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Explain the role of the Stack Pointer (R13) in converting C assignment statements to assembly. How are local variable addresses calculated?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** In ARM Cortex-M, local C variables are allocated in the **function's stack frame**, and the Stack Pointer (`R13 / SP`) holds the base address of the active stack frame (1 mark). The address of a local variable is calculated using the formula: $$\text{Address}(v) = SP + \text{offset}_v$$ where $\text{offset}_v$ is the compiler-assigned displacement (1 mark). For example, in a function with four 32-bit locals `a, b, c, d`, the offsets are `0, 4, 8, 12` respectively, and they are accessed as `[SP, #0]`, `[SP, #4]`, `[SP, #8]`, `[SP, #12]` in `LDR`/`STR` instructions (1 mark). --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question from each module. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Both sub-parts must be attempted for full marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Suggested]** **(a)** Explain the three-stage conversion process for converting a C assignment statement to ARM Cortex-M assembly. Illustrate with the C statement `x = y + z - 5;` assuming `x, y, z` are local 32-bit integers allocated on the stack at offsets `[SP, #0]`, `[SP, #4]`, `[SP, #8]` respectively. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** The three-stage conversion process is: 1. **Memory-to-Register Transfer (LDR):** Each RHS variable is fetched from memory into a register. 2. **Register-Level Computation:** Data-processing instructions are applied. 3. **Register-to-Memory Transfer (STR):** The result is written back to the LHS variable's memory location. **Step-by-step conversion of `x = y + z - 5;`:** **Stage 1 — Load operands:** $$\text{LDR } R_0, [SP, \#4] \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_0 \leftarrow M[SP+4] = y$$ $$\text{LDR } R_1, [SP, \#8] \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 \leftarrow M[SP+8] = z$$ **Stage 2 — Process with operator precedence (`+` before `-`):** $$\text{ADD } R_0, R_0, R_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_0 \leftarrow R_0 + R_1 = y + z$$ $$\text{SUB } R_0, R_0, \#5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_0 \leftarrow R_0 - 5 = (y+z) - 5$$ **Stage 3 — Store result:** $$\text{STR } R_0, [SP, \#0] \quad \Rightarrow \quad M[SP+0] \leftarrow R_0 = x$$ **Final Assembly Listing:** ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = y (2 marks: LDRs) LDR R1, [SP, #8] ; R1 = z ADD R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = y + z (2 marks: ADD) SUB R0, R0, #5 ; R0 = (y+z) - 5 (1 mark: SUB with immediate) STR R0, [SP, #0] ; x = R0 (1 mark: STR) ---------------- Total: 5 instructions (1 mark for complete listing) ``` **(b)** Convert the following C assignment statements to ARM Cortex-M assembly. Assume `a, b, c, d` are local 32-bit integers at stack offsets `[SP, #0]`, `[SP, #4]`, `[SP, #8]`, `[SP, #12]` respectively. **(7 Marks)** ```c result1 = a + b; result2 = a - c; result3 = b * d; ``` **Model Solution (b):** **For `result1 = a + b;`** (2 marks) ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #0] ; R0 = a LDR R1, [SP, #4] ; R1 = b ADD R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = a + b LDR R2, [SP, #16] ; load address of result1 (assuming new offset) STR R0, [R2] ; result1 = a + b ``` **For `result2 = a - c;`** (2 marks) ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #0] ; R0 = a LDR R1, [SP, #8] ; R1 = c SUB R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = a - c LDR R2, [SP, #20] ; address of result2 STR R0, [R2] ; result2 = a - c ``` **For `result3 = b * d;`** (2 marks) ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b LDR R1, [SP, #12] ; R1 = d MUL R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = b * d LDR R2, [SP, #24] ; address of result3 STR R0, [R2] ; result3 = b * d ``` **Efficiency Note (1 mark):** Since `a` and `b` are reused across all three statements, an **optimized compiler** would load `a` and `b` only once and reuse the values. However, the *naive translation* shown above is what KTU expects for a *straight one-to-one conversion*. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** With suitable examples, explain how the following C operators are translated to ARM Cortex-M assembly instructions: (i) Arithmetic addition with a constant, (ii) Two's complement negation, (iii) Bitwise AND. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **(i) Arithmetic addition with a constant:** `a = b + 10;` (2 marks) ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b ADD R0, R0, #10 ; R0 = b + 10 (uses immediate operand) STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Why:** ARM `ADD` supports a 12-bit rotated immediate. This saves one `LDR` instruction compared to loading 10 from memory. **(ii) Two's complement negation:** `a = -b;` (2.5 marks) ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = b RSB R0, R0, #0 ; R0 = 0 - b = -b STR R0, [SP, #0] ; a = R0 ``` **Why:** ARM Cortex-M base ISA **does not have a dedicated `NEG` instruction**. The idiom `RSB Rd, Rn, #0` (Reverse Subtract from zero) achieves negation. Alternatively, an `MVN Rd, Rn` followed by `ADD Rd, Rd, #1` (bitwise NOT then add 1) also works, but `RSB` is preferred for clarity. **(iii) Bitwise AND:** `flag = mask & 0x0F;` (2.5 marks) ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = mask AND R0, R0, #0x0F ; R0 = mask & 0x0F STR R0, [SP, #0] ; flag = R0 ``` **Why:** `AND` is a bitwise logical AND. The constant `0x0F` is a valid 12-bit immediate in ARM. For arbitrary 32-bit masks that don't fit the immediate encoding, `LDR R1, =mask_literal` followed by `AND R0, R0, R1` is required. --- **(b)** Consider the C code fragment below. Manually convert each line to ARM Cortex-M assembly. Variables `p, q, r, s` are local 32-bit integers stored at stack offsets `[SP, #0]`, `[SP, #4]`, `[SP, #8]`, `[SP, #12]` respectively. **(7 Marks)** ```c p = 100; q = p; r = q - p + s; s = -q; ``` **Model Solution (b):** **Line 1: `p = 100;`** (1 mark) ```assembly MOV R0, #100 STR R0, [SP, #0] ; p = 100 ``` **Line 2: `q = p;`** (1 mark) ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #0] ; R0 = p STR R0, [SP, #4] ; q = R0 ``` **Line 3: `r = q - p + s;`** (3 marks) Note: Left-to-right associativity with same precedence. So computed as $(q - p) + s$. ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = q LDR R1, [SP, #0] ; R1 = p SUB R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = q - p LDR R1, [SP, #12] ; R1 = s ADD R0, R0, R1 ; R0 = (q - p) + s STR R0, [SP, #8] ; r = R0 ``` **Line 4: `s = -q;`** (2 marks) ```assembly LDR R0, [SP, #4] ; R0 = q RSB R0, R0, #0 ; R0 = -q STR R0, [SP, #12] ; s = R0 ``` **Mark Distribution (1 mark):** **Register Reuse Analysis** — A 14/14 answer would observe that `LDR R0, [SP, #0]` in Line 3 is redundant since `R0` was just stored with `p` in Line 1. In optimized code, this re-load can be eliminated. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks (Read Carefully!):** > > 1. **Forgetting the `STR` instruction (–3 marks):** The most common error. After computing the result in `R0`, students often forget to store it back. **Every assignment ends with a `STR`.** > > 2. **Wrong order in `SUB` and `RSB` (–2 marks):** Writing `SUB R0, R1, R0` when you mean `b - c` (with R0=b, R1=c). Always write: `SUB R0, R0, R1` for `b - c`. The destination is **always first**. > > 3. **Forgetting operator precedence in compound expressions (–2 marks):** For `r = q - p + s;`, students often compute `q - (p + s)`. The correct evaluation is `(q - p) + s` due to **left-to-right associativity** of equal-precedence operators. > > 4. **Using `MOV` to move between memory locations (–2 marks):** `MOV` operates **only on registers**. You **cannot** write `MOV [SP, #0], [SP, #4]`. Use `LDR` then `STR`. > > 5. **Ignoring stack frame offset conventions (–1 mark):** Not commenting the offsets or using inconsistent sizes. Always specify `[SP, #offset]` and the variable name as a comment. > > 6. **Using `NEG` instruction (–1 mark):** ARM Cortex-M **base ISA does NOT have a `NEG` instruction**. Use `RSB Rd, Rn, #0`. Examiners deduct for this. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **ARM Cortex-M is Load-Store RISC** — no memory-to-memory data processing. - [x] **Three-Stage Pattern:** LDR (load) → Data-Process → STR (store). - [x] **Register Width = 32 bits** for all GPRs (R0–R12). - [x] **R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC** — never use for general arithmetic unless intentional. - [x] **Address Formula:** $\text{Address}(v) = SP + \text{offset}_v$. - [x] **Common Instruction Set:** - `MOV Rd, #imm` — load constant - `LDR Rd, [SP, #off]` — load from stack - `STR Rd, [SP, #off]` — store to stack - `ADD Rd, Rn, Rm` / `ADD Rd, Rn, #imm` - `SUB Rd, Rn, Rm` / `SUB Rd, Rn, #imm` - `RSB Rd, Rn, #0` — canonical negation - `MUL Rd, Rm, Rn` — multiplication (32-bit result) - `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR` — bitwise operations - [x] **Flags Update:** Plain instructions (ADD, SUB, MUL) do **not** update flags. Add the `S` suffix (ADDS, SUBS, MULS) only when the C code uses the result for a comparison/branch. - [x] **Order of Operands:** In `ADD Rd, Rn, Rm` and `SUB Rd, Rn, Rm`, the **destination is first**, then the minuend/minuend, then the subtrahend/adder. - [x] **Operator Precedence in C:** Parentheses → unary → `* / %` → `+ -` → bitwise shifts → `< <= > >=` → `== !=` → bitwise AND → bitwise XOR → bitwise OR → logical AND → logical OR. - [x] **MUL restriction:** Result register `Rd` must be different from `Rm` in some ARM variants (e.g., ARM7), but **in ARM Cortex-M (M-profile), `MUL Rd, Rm, Rn` permits any register including `Rd == Rm`**. - [x] **Literal Pool for Large Constants:** If a constant does not fit in 12-bit immediate encoding, use `LDR Rd, =constant` — the assembler inserts the constant in a nearby literal pool. - [x] **AAPCS Convention (Bonus, 2024 Scheme):** R0–R3 are argument/result registers; R4–R11 are callee-saved; R12 is intra-procedure scratch. Not strictly required for Module 2 conversions, but valuable context.

topics.title: Memory Address Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory Address Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **ARM Architecture**, **Memory Address Calculation** refers to the deterministic process by which the processor computes the *effective byte address* of an operand in memory before performing a load (LDR) or store (STR) operation. Because ARM is a **Load/Store architecture**, *no data processing instruction operates directly on memory* — every memory reference must first resolve to a precise physical or virtual byte address derived from a base register, an offset, and a shift operation.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the term specifically covers:
- **Byte addressing** in a **32-bit linear address space** (address range `0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`).
- **Alignment rules** for byte (8-bit), halfword (16-bit), and word (32-bit) transfers.
- **Endianness** behaviour: little-endian (default in ARM Cortex-M) and big-endian.
- **Pre-indexed, post-indexed, and pre-indexed-with-writeback** address generation modes.
- **Block transfer address sequencing** in LDM/STM (Load/Store Multiple).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Memory address calculations are foundational to understanding *ARM data transfer instructions*, *stack operations*, and *memory-mapped I/O*. A typical KTU question asks the student to compute the **Effective Address (EA)** or to predict the **byte contents of memory** after a STR instruction under a given endianness.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pigeonhole Mailbox Street"

Imagine a long street of **4,294,967,296 mailboxes** (one per byte), numbered sequentially from 0 to 4,294,967,295. The ARM processor is a postal worker who must:

1. Walk to a **base mailbox** (the `Rn` base register).
2. Move a few mailboxes forward or backward (the **offset**).
3. Possibly **deliver or collect 1, 2, or 4 adjacent mailboxes** depending on whether the parcel is a byte, halfword, or word.

The rule *"you may only start at a mailbox whose number is a multiple of the parcel size"* is what we call **alignment**. If you try to deliver a *word-sized* parcel starting at mailbox 7, the postmaster (processor) raises a **fault** — this is *unaligned access*. The choice of whether the *least-significant* byte goes into the *lowest-numbered* mailbox (little-endian) or the *highest-numbered* mailbox (big-endian) is the **endianness** decision.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Meaning | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Effective Address (EA) | Final computed byte address of operand | `EA = f(Rn, offset)` |
| Base Register | Register holding starting address | `Rn` |
| Offset | 12-bit immediate or register-based displacement | `#offset` or `Rm` |
| Alignment | Address divisibility constraint | `EA mod N = 0` |
| Endianness | Byte-ordering convention | Little / Big |

> [!NOTE]
> ARM Cortex-M processors (e.g., **M0, M3, M4, M7**) are configured at hardware reset to be **little-endian only** by default. The original ARM7/ARM9 could be bi-endian (set via pin `BIGEND`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Address Generation Pipeline

Every ARM data transfer follows a fixed three-stage address generation pipeline:

1. **Base Address Extraction** — Read the 32-bit value in `Rn`.
2. **Offset Computation** — Apply the offset (immediate, register, or scaled register).
3. **Indexing & Write-back Decision** — Decide whether to update `Rn` after the transfer.

### 2.1.1 Pre-indexed Addressing

The offset is applied **before** the memory access; the *modified* address is used for the transfer.

$$EA = R[n] \pm \text{offset}$$

If the suffix `!` is appended, `Rn` is updated to `EA` (**write-back enabled**).

> **Example Instruction:** `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!`  →  `EA = R1 + 4`, then `R1 ← R1 + 4`.

### 2.1.2 Post-indexed Addressing

The base register is used **directly** as the EA for the transfer, and **afterwards** it is updated with the offset.

$$EA = R[n]$$

$$R[n] \leftarrow R[n] \pm \text{offset}$$

> **Example Instruction:** `LDR R0, [R1], #4`  →  `EA = R1` for the load, then `R1 ← R1 + 4`.

### 2.1.3 Pre-indexed without Write-back (Plain Offset)

$$EA = R[n] \pm \text{offset}, \quad R[n] \text{ unchanged}$$

> **Example Instruction:** `LDR R0, [R1, #4]`  →  `EA = R1 + 4`, `R1` is preserved.

## 2.2 Scaled Register Offset

When a register `Rm` is used as the offset, it may be **barrel-shifted** before addition:

$$EA = R[n] \pm \left( R[m] \, \text{OP} \, \text{shift\_amount} \right)$$

Common shifts: `LSL #k` (logical shift left by k), `LSR #k`, `ASR #k`, `ROR #k`.

> **Example:** `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]!`  →  `EA = R1 + (R2 << 2)`, with write-back.

## 2.3 Alignment Rules (Address Calculation Constraints)

| Data Size | Bytes | Alignment Mask | Valid EA bits `[A1:A0]` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Byte (B) | 1 | None | `xx` (any) |
| Halfword (H) | 2 | `A0 = 0` | `x0` |
| Word (W) | 4 | `A1 = A0 = 0` | `00` |

$$A[k] = \left\lfloor \frac{EA}{2^k} \right\rfloor \mod 2, \quad k = 0,1,2,\dots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a misaligned address is used with a **word** or **halfword** transfer in ARMv7-M, the result is **UNPREDICTABLE** (older ARM) or the bus may perform a *rotated aligned access* (Cortex-M3/M4 with optional support). In KTU problems, alignment is **assumed satisfied** unless explicitly broken.

## 2.4 Endianness — Byte Ordering Rules

For a **word** stored at byte address `A`:

| Endian | `Mem[A]` | `Mem[A+1]` | `Mem[A+2]` | `Mem[A+3]` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Little-endian** | bits `[7:0]` (LSB) | `[15:8]` | `[23:16]` | `[31:24]` (MSB) |
| **Big-endian** | bits `[31:24]` (MSB) | `[23:16]` | `[15:8]` | `[7:0]` (LSB) |

The address of the *word* in the memory map is always `A` (the lowest byte), regardless of endianness.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols follow the **ARM Architecture Reference Manual (ARM ARM)** convention. Use this table verbatim in exam answers where permitted.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective Address (Pre-indexed) | $EA = R_n \pm \text{offset}$ | Bytes |
| 2 | Effective Address (Post-indexed) | $EA = R_n$ | Bytes |
| 3 | Scaled Register Offset | $EA = R_n \pm (R_m \ll k)$ | Bytes |
| 4 | Word address from byte address | $W = \lfloor B / 4 \rfloor$ | Word index |
| 5 | Byte address from word index | $B = 4W$ | Bytes |
| 6 | Alignment check (word) | $EA \mod 4 = 0$ | Boolean |
| 7 | Alignment check (halfword) | $EA \mod 2 = 0$ | Boolean |
| 8 | Halfword offset byte | $H_{byte} = (H \gg 8) \mod 2^8$ | 0–255 |
| 9 | Word offset bytes (LE) | $b_0 = W \mod 2^8,\ b_1 = \lfloor W/256 \rfloor \mod 2^8$ | 0–255 |
| 10 | Block transfer final address | $R_n^{final} = R_n^{start} \pm 4N$ | Bytes |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Memory address calculations are at the heart of:
- **Embedded firmware** writing to memory-mapped peripheral registers (e.g., GPIO at `0x40020000`).
- **Compiler code generation** — every `array[i]` access in C becomes a scaled offset.
- **Stack frame management** in function prologues/epilogues.
- **Bootloader design** — relocating vector tables to RAM requires careful address math.
- **Operating systems** — page table walks and virtual-to-physical address translation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Effective Address in Pre-Indexed Mode

**Problem:** Given `R1 = 0x00020010`. Compute the EA for each instruction and state whether the result is word-aligned.

### 3.1.1 Instruction: `LDR R3, [R1, #0x10]`

$$\begin{aligned}
EA &= R_1 + \text{offset} \\
&= \texttt{0x00020010} + \texttt{0x10} \\
&= \texttt{0x00020020}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1:** Identify base register value.
**Step 2:** Identify the 12-bit immediate offset (zero-extended).
**Step 3:** Perform 32-bit unsigned addition, ignoring carry-out.
**Step 4:** Check alignment: `0x20 mod 4 = 0x20 mod 4 = 0` ⇒ **Word-aligned ✓**.

### 3.1.2 Instruction: `LDRH R4, [R1, #6]`

$$\begin{aligned}
EA &= R_1 + 6 = \texttt{0x00020010} + \texttt{0x6} = \texttt{0x00020016}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alignment check:** `0x16 mod 2 = 0` ⇒ **Halfword-aligned ✓**.

### 3.1.3 Instruction: `LDR R5, [R1, #5]`

$$EA = \texttt{0x00020015}$$

**Alignment check:** `0x15 mod 4 = 1` ⇒ **NOT word-aligned ✗** (Fault / Unpredictable).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Storing a Word in Little-Endian Memory

**Problem:** Register `R0 = 0x12345678`. Execute `STR R0, [R1]` where `R1 = 0x40001000`. Show the resulting memory layout assuming **little-endian** byte ordering.

### 3.2.1 Determine Effective Address

$$EA = R_1 = \texttt{0x40001000}$$

### 3.2.2 Decompose the 32-bit word into bytes

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 0 (LSB)} &= R_0 \mathrel{\&} \texttt{0xFF} = \texttt{0x78} \\
\text{Byte 1}        &= (R_0 \gg 8)  \mathrel{\&} \texttt{0xFF} = \texttt{0x56} \\
\text{Byte 2}        &= (R_0 \gg 16) \mathrel{\&} \texttt{0xFF} = \texttt{0x34} \\
\text{Byte 3 (MSB)}  &= (R_0 \gg 24) \mathrel{\&} \texttt{0xFF} = \texttt{0x12}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.3 Store using little-endian rule (LSB at lowest address)

| Address | Byte stored |
|---|---|
| `0x40001000` | `0x78` |
| `0x40001001` | `0x56` |
| `0x40001002` | `0x34` |
| `0x40001003` | `0x12` |

**Verification formula:** $\text{Mem}[A+i] = (R_0 \gg 8i) \mod 2^8, \quad i = 0,1,2,3$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Storing a Word in Big-Endian Memory

Same register `R0 = 0x12345678`, same `R1 = 0x40001000`, but processor in **big-endian** mode.

| Address | Byte stored |
|---|---|
| `0x40001000` | `0x12` (MSB) |
| `0x40001001` | `0x34` |
| `0x40001002` | `0x56` |
| `0x40001003` | `0x78` (LSB) |

**General formula (big-endian):**
$$\text{Mem}[A+i] = (R_0 \gg 8(3-i)) \mod 2^8, \quad i = 0,1,2,3$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Block Transfer Address Calculation (LDM/STM)

**Problem:** `R0 = 0x20000000`, register list `{R1, R2, R3, R4}`. Execute `STMIA R0!, {R1-R4}`. Compute the final value of `R0`.

### 3.4.1 Identify the addressing mode

`IA` = **Increment After** (post-increment per element). The `!` enables **write-back** of the base register.

### 3.4.2 Count the registers

$$N = 4 \text{ registers}$$

### 3.4.3 Calculate total bytes transferred

$$N_{bytes} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ bytes}$$

### 3.4.4 Calculate the final base register value

$$\begin{aligned}
R_0^{final} &= R_0^{start} + 4N \\
            &= \texttt{0x20000000} + 16 \\
            &= \texttt{0x20000000} + \texttt{0x10} \\
            &= \texttt{0x20000010}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4.5 Memory layout after `STMIA`

| Address | Register stored |
|---|---|
| `0x20000000` | R1 |
| `0x20000004` | R2 |
| `0x20000008` | R3 |
| `0x2000000C` | R4 |

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Decoding a Memory Dump

**Problem:** A 4-byte region of memory starting at `0x1000` is dumped in hexadecimal order:
`Address : Data`
`0x1000 : 0x78`
`0x1001 : 0x56`
`0x1002 : 0x34`
`0x1003 : 0x12`

Find the 32-bit word value assuming (a) little-endian and (b) big-endian.

### 3.5.1 Little-endian reconstruction

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \text{Mem}[A] + (\text{Mem}[A+1] \ll 8) + (\text{Mem}[A+2] \ll 16) + (\text{Mem}[A+3] \ll 24) \\
  &= \texttt{0x78} + (\texttt{0x56} \ll 8) + (\texttt{0x34} \ll 16) + (\texttt{0x12} \ll 24) \\
  &= \texttt{0x12345678}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.5.2 Big-endian reconstruction

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= (\text{Mem}[A] \ll 24) + (\text{Mem}[A+1] \ll 16) + (\text{Mem}[A+2] \ll 8) + \text{Mem}[A+3] \\
  &= \texttt{0x12} \ll 24 + \texttt{0x34} \ll 16 + \texttt{0x56} \ll 8 + \texttt{0x78} \\
  &= \texttt{0x12345678}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Both endian schemes store the *same logical value*; only the byte order in physical memory differs. This is why the same instruction `LDR R0, [R1]` yields the same result regardless of endianness.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LDM/STM Address Modes

| Mode | Mnemonic | EA for first transfer | Final `Rn` (with `!`) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increment After | `IA` / `FD` | $R_n$ | $R_n + 4N$ | Stack pop, FIFO read |
| Increment Before | `IB` / `FA` | $R_n + 4$ | $R_n + 4N$ | Block copy destination |
| Decrement After | `DA` / `ED` | $R_n - 4N + 4$ | $R_n - 4N$ | Stack push |
| Decrement Before | `DB` / `EA` | $R_n - 4N$ | $R_n - 4N$ | Reverse block copy |

> [!NOTE]
> In ARM notation, `IA ≡ FD`, `IB ≡ FA`, `DA ≡ ED`, `DB ≡ EA` because the Full/Empty and Ascending/Descending qualifiers are *postfix descriptors* used with stack operations (`PUSH` / `POP`).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM Memory Address Generation — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Decoder] --> B{Addressing Mode?}
    B -- Pre-indexed --> C[EA = Rn + offset]
    B -- Post-indexed --> D[EA = Rn]
    B -- Scaled Register --> E[EA = Rn + Rm shifted]
    C --> F{Write-back '!' present?}
    D --> G[Rn is updated AFTER transfer]
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> H[Rn = EA before transfer]
    F -- No --> I[Rn preserved]
    C --> J[Alignment Check Unit]
    E --> J
    J -- Aligned --> K[Memory Bus Transfer]
    J -- Misaligned --> L[Fault: UNPREDICTABLE]
    G --> K
    I --> K
```

## 4.2 Endianness Byte-Order Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LITTLE[Little-Endian: STR R0, R0=0x12345678]
        L0[Addr 0x1000 = 0x78] --> L1[Addr 0x1001 = 0x56]
        L1 --> L2[Addr 0x1002 = 0x34]
        L2 --> L3[Addr 0x1003 = 0x12]
    end
    subgraph BIG[Big-Endian: STR R0, R0=0x12345678]
        B0[Addr 0x2000 = 0x12] --> B1[Addr 0x2001 = 0x34]
        B1 --> B2[Addr 0x2002 = 0x56]
        B2 --> B3[Addr 0x2003 = 0x78]
    end
```

## 4.3 LDM/STM Block Transfer — Increment After Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Rn = 0x20000000, list = R1 R2 R3 R4] --> T1[Transfer R1 at 0x20000000]
    T1 --> T2[Increment Rn to 0x20000004]
    T2 --> T3[Transfer R2 at 0x20000004]
    T3 --> T4[Increment Rn to 0x20000008]
    T4 --> T5[Transfer R3 at 0x20000008]
    T5 --> T6[Increment Rn to 0x2000000C]
    T6 --> T7[Transfer R4 at 0x2000000C]
    T7 --> T8[Writeback: Rn = 0x20000010]
    T8 --> End[Instruction complete]
```

## 4.4 Memory Map Layout (Generic ARM Cortex-M4)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MAP[ARM Cortex-M4 4GB Address Space]
        M0[0x00000000 - 0x1FFFFFFF: Code / Aliased regions]
        M1[0x20000000 - 0x3FFFFFFF: SRAM]
        M2[0x40000000 - 0x5FFFFFFF: Peripheral Registers]
        M3[0x60000000 - 0x7FFFFFFF: External RAM]
        M4[0x80000000 - 0x9FFFFFFF: External Device]
        M5[0xE0000000 - 0xFFFFFFFF: System / PPB]
    end
    M0 --- M1
    M1 --- M2
    M2 --- M3
    M3 --- M4
    M4 --- M5
```

> [!NOTE]
> When you see a KTU question like *"A peripheral register is at address `0x40020C00`. Find the byte address of the 4th register in the block"*, you simply apply $B = 0x40020C00 + 4 \times 4 \times (4-1)$ — a direct application of address calculation principles on the memory map.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between pre-indexed and post-indexed addressing modes in ARM with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- In **pre-indexed** addressing, the offset is applied *before* the memory access; the effective address is $EA = R_n \pm \text{offset}$. Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` loads from `R1 + 4`. (1.5 marks)
- In **post-indexed** addressing, the base register itself is the EA, and the offset updates `Rn` *after* the transfer. Example: `LDR R0, [R1], #4` loads from `R1`, then sets `R1 ← R1 + 4`. (1.5 marks)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is endianness? Explain little-endian and big-endian with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Endianness** is the byte-ordering convention used to store a multi-byte word in memory. (0.5 marks)
- In **little-endian**, the *least significant byte (LSB)* is stored at the *lowest* memory address. (1 mark)
- In **big-endian**, the *most significant byte (MSB)* is stored at the *lowest* memory address. (1 mark)
- **Example:** To store `0x12345678` at address `A`:
  - Little-endian → `A: 0x78, A+1: 0x56, A+2: 0x34, A+3: 0x12`. (0.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern (KTU 2024):** Module 2 carries ~14 marks. Two full-length sub-parts of 7 marks each, with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**A (a)** Explain the different addressing modes used in ARM load/store instructions with neat examples. Discuss the role of the barrel shifter in generating the effective address. **(7 marks)**

**A (b)** A register `R0 = 0xABCD1234` is stored at memory location `0x20004000` using a `STR R0, [R1]` instruction. Show the byte-by-byte memory contents in:
  (i) Little-endian format
  (ii) Big-endian format **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — A (a) **(7 marks)**

1. **Pre-indexed addressing (1.5 marks)** — Offset is added to base *before* access. Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #8]` → `EA = R1 + 8`.
2. **Pre-indexed with write-back (1.5 marks)** — As above, but `!` updates `Rn`. Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #8]!` → `R1 ← R1 + 8` after load.
3. **Post-indexed (1.5 marks)** — Base used directly; `Rn` updated after. Example: `LDR R0, [R1], #8`.
4. **Scaled register offset with barrel shifter (2.5 marks)** — `Rm` can be shifted by a constant before addition. Example: `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` → `EA = R1 + (R2 << 2)`. The **barrel shifter** allows single-cycle shifts (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX) making array indexing efficient without extra cycles.

#### Model Solution — A (b) **(7 marks)**

**Given:** `R0 = 0xABCD1234`, `R1 = 0x20004000`.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute EA = R1 = `0x20004000`.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Decompose the word into four bytes.

$$\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \texttt{0x34} \quad (\text{LSB}) \\
b_1 &= \texttt{0x12} \\
b_2 &= \texttt{0xCD} \\
b_3 &= \texttt{0xAB} \quad (\text{MSB})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Little-endian layout (LSB at lowest address):

| Address | Byte |
|---|---|
| `0x20004000` | `0x34` |
| `0x20004001` | `0x12` |
| `0x20004002` | `0xCD` |
| `0x20004003` | `0xAB` |

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Big-endian layout (MSB at lowest address):

| Address | Byte |
|---|---|
| `0x20004000` | `0xAB` |
| `0x20004001` | `0xCD` |
| `0x20004002` | `0x12` |
| `0x20004003` | `0x34` |

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude that the logical 32-bit value `0xABCD1234` is preserved in both schemes; only physical storage order differs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**B (a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the concept of memory address calculation in ARM. Discuss alignment constraints for byte, halfword, and word transfers. **(7 marks)**

**B (b)** Given `R0 = 0x40020000` and the instruction `STMDB R0!, {R1, R2, R3, R4, R5}`. Show the memory layout and compute the final value of R0. Assume little-endian system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — B (a) **(7 marks)**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Effective Address (EA) is the *final byte address* computed by the CPU for any LDR/STR operation. The general form is $EA = R_n \pm \text{offset}$, where offset may be an immediate, a register, or a barrel-shifted register.

2. **Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the three-stage pipeline:
   - **Stage 1:** Fetch base from `Rn`.
   - **Stage 2:** Apply offset / barrel shift.
   - **Stage 3:** Optional write-back; alignment check; bus transfer.

3. **Alignment constraints (4 marks):**
   - **Byte transfer (LDRB/STRB):** *No* alignment constraint. EA can be any address. Example: `LDRB R0, [R1, #3]`.
   - **Halfword transfer (LDRH/STRH):** $A_0 = 0$, i.e., $EA \mod 2 = 0$. Example: `LDRH R0, [R1, #2]` valid at `0x10002`.
   - **Word transfer (LDR/STR):** $A_1 = A_0 = 0$, i.e., $EA \mod 4 = 0$. Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` valid only at addresses `0x…00`.
   - Violating alignment for halfword/word in ARMv7-M is **UNPREDICTABLE** and considered a programming error.

#### Model Solution — B (b) **(7 marks)**

**Given:** `R0 = 0x40020000`, list = `{R1, R2, R3, R4, R5}`, mode = `STMDB` (Decrement Before).

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the address mode — Decrement Before with write-back (`!`).
- Total registers: $N = 5$.
- Total bytes: $5 \times 4 = 20$ bytes.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the *starting* EA for the first transfer (Decrement Before means address is decremented *before* the first access).

$$EA_{start} = R_0 - 4N = \texttt{0x40020000} - 20 = \texttt{0x4001FFEC}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Memory layout (lowest address first; R5 transferred first due to Decrement Before convention):

| Address | Register stored |
|---|---|
| `0x4001FFEC` | R5 |
| `0x4001FFF0` | R4 |
| `0x4001FFF4` | R3 |
| `0x4001FFF8` | R2 |
| `0x4001FFFC` | R1 |

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the final value of R0 (write-back):

$$R_0^{final} = R_0 - 4N = \texttt{0x40020000} - \texttt{0x14} = \texttt{0x4001FFEC}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude that `STMDB` is the typical **stack push** mode — the stack pointer (here `R0`) is decremented first, and registers are stored at successively lower addresses. The final `R0` points to the lowest stored word.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Endianness Reversal (2–3 marks):** The most frequent error is storing the MSB first in a little-endian question or the LSB first in a big-endian question. *Always* re-state the rule in one line: *"Little-endian = LSB at lowest address"* before writing the table.
> 2. **Confusing `IA` with `IB` (1–2 marks):** `IA` (Increment After) starts at `Rn`; `IB` (Increment Before) starts at `Rn + 4`. Marks lost when students mis-place the *first* transfer.
> 3. **Forgetting Write-back (`!`):** The base register is updated *only* if `!` is present. A common error is updating `Rn` for plain `[Rn, #4]` instructions.
> 4. **Alignment Arithmetic:** Halfword alignment requires `EA` to be divisible by 2, not 4. Word alignment requires divisibility by 4. A wrong modulus loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Unit Mismatch:** Always quote addresses in **hexadecimal** (with `0x` prefix) when the question is in hex; convert only if the problem mixes decimal and hex.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **EA (Effective Address)** = the *final byte address* used for LDR/STR; computed as $EA = R_n \pm \text{offset}$ (pre-indexed) or $EA = R_n$ (post-indexed).
- [x] **Pre-indexed** applies offset *before* the access; **post-indexed** applies it *after*.
- [x] **Write-back (`!`)** updates the base register to the EA.
- [x] **Barrel shifter** can scale the register offset in a *single cycle* (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR).
- [x] **Alignment:** byte → no constraint; halfword → `EA mod 2 = 0`; word → `EA mod 4 = 0`.
- [x] **Little-endian** stores the **LSB at the lowest address** (default for ARM Cortex-M).
- [x] **Big-endian** stores the **MSB at the lowest address** (configurable in ARM7/ARM9).
- [x] **LDM/STM modes** — `IA`, `IB`, `DA`, `DB` — control whether the address is incremented/decremented and whether it happens *before* or *after* each transfer.
- [x] **Block transfer address math:** final `Rn` = $R_n^{start} \pm 4N$ where $N$ = number of registers.
- [x] **Stack conventions:** `STMDB / LDMIA` = `PUSH / POP` (Full Ascending stack — the ARM/Thumb standard).
- [x] **Memory map regions** (Cortex-M4): Code (0x00000000), SRAM (0x20000000), Peripheral (0x40000000), External (0x60000000), System (0xE0000000).
- [x] **Always show** the EA computation, the byte decomposition, and the address table — examiners award *method marks* even if the final byte is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Addressing Examples: Translating C Pointer Expressions to Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory Addressing Examples: Translating C Pointer Expressions to Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Memory Addressing** in the ARM architecture is the mechanism by which the processor computes the effective address of an operand residing in memory to perform load (`LDR`) and store (`STR`) operations. When translating high-level **C pointer expressions** into ARM assembly, the compiler (or programmer) must map every indirection operator (`*`), address-of operator (`&`), and pointer arithmetic (`ptr + n`) into a specific ARM addressing mode — namely **pre-indexed**, **post-indexed**, or **register-offset** addressing — as defined in the **ARM Architecture Reference Manual (ARM ARM v7-A)**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, *translating C pointer expressions to assembly* refers to the systematic process of converting expressions such as `*p`, `*(p+4)`, `*p++`, `array[i]`, `*(array + i)`, and `&variable` into equivalent ARM instruction sequences using the available **seven ARM addressing modes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> Memory addressing examples in ARM deal with how the effective address of memory operands is calculated using the base register, offset, and addressing mode flags. Translating C pointer expressions requires understanding that pointer arithmetic is **scaled by the data size** automatically by ARM, or explicitly by the programmer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Address System

Imagine memory as a huge city of post office boxes:
- A **pointer variable** `p` is like a piece of paper holding a **box number** (the address).
- The **dereference operator** `*p` is like walking to the box whose number is written on that paper and reading or placing a letter.
- **Pointer arithmetic** `p + 4` is like saying, "From the box I'm holding, move 4 boxes down the row" — and the **size of each box** depends on the type of item (a `char` is a tiny box, an `int` is a much bigger box, so moving `+1 int` means skipping 4 tiny boxes).
- The **base register** in ARM (`Rn`) is the paper with the box number, and the **offset** is the extra boxes to move.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** In ARM, the address calculation is performed *before* (pre-indexed) or *after* (post-indexed) the memory access. This is the fundamental difference between ARM and older CISC processors like the 8086, where the same effect is achieved differently.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard ARM Metrics

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Word size** | **32 bits (4 bytes)** | Standard ARM word |
| **Half-word** | **16 bits (2 bytes)** | Useful for `short int` |
| **Byte** | **8 bits (1 byte)** | Smallest addressable unit |
| **Register width** | **32 bits (R0–R15)** | All ARM general-purpose registers |
| **PC register** | **R15** | Program counter |
| **LR register** | **R14** | Link register |
| **SP register** | **R13** | Stack pointer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Families of ARM Memory Addressing

ARM classifies memory addressing into three primary families. Understanding these is critical for translating any C pointer expression:

#### 2.1.1 Offset Addressing (Three Sub-modes)

1. **Immediate Offset:** `LDR Rt, [Rn, #offset]`
   - Address = `Rn + offset`
   - Base register is **not updated**.
   - Most common for array element access.

2. **Register Offset:** `LDR Rt, [Rn, Rm]`  or  `LDR Rt, [Rn, Rm, LSL #2]`
   - Address = `Rn + (Rm shifted by constant)`
   - Used heavily for pointer arithmetic with index variables.

3. **Scaled Register Offset:** `LDR Rt, [Rn, Rm, LSL #n]`
   - The `LSL #n` applies a logical left shift of `n` bits to the offset register.
   - **Critical for C pointer arithmetic** because ARM uses scaled offsets to multiply the index by the data size.

#### 2.1.2 Pre-indexed Addressing (Two Sub-modes)

1. **Plain Pre-index:** `LDR Rt, [Rn, #offset]!`
   - Address = `Rn + offset`
   - **Base register `Rn` is updated** to this new address (`!` is the write-back flag).
   - Equivalent to C's `*p++` or `*(p += n)`.

2. **Pre-index with Scaled Offset:** `LDR Rt, [Rn, Rm, LSL #2]!`
   - Same write-back, with scaling.

#### 2.1.3 Post-indexed Addressing

`LDR Rt, [Rn], #offset`

- Address = `Rn` (used as-is for the load)
- After the load, `Rn` is updated to `Rn + offset`.
- Equivalent to C's `*p; p = p + n;` (used in a sequence).

### 2.2 The Three KTU-Favorite C-to-ARM Translation Patterns

#### Pattern 1: Simple Dereference
```c
int *p;
*p = 25;        // C expression
```
Translates to:
```arm
LDR  R0, =p          ; load address of pointer p
LDR  R1, [R0]        ; R1 = p (the value, i.e., the address it points to)
MOV  R2, #25
STR  R2, [R1]        ; memory[R1] = 25  →  *p = 25
```

#### Pattern 2: Pointer Increment (Most Asked in KTU)
```c
*p++ = value;        // C: increment pointer AFTER using it
```
Translates to:
```arm
LDR  R0, =p
LDR  R1, [R0]        ; R1 = p
STR  value, [R1], #4 ; *R1 = value;  R1 = R1 + 4  (POST-INDEX!)
STR  R1, [R0]        ; p = R1 (save updated pointer back)
```

#### Pattern 3: Array Indexing
```c
int arr[10];
arr[i] = value;
```
Translates to:
```arm
; Assume base address of arr is in R1, i is in R2
ADD  R3, R1, R2, LSL #2   ; R3 = arr + (i * 4)
STR  value, [R3]           ; arr[i] = value
```

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas. KTU 2024 ESE questions are direct applications of these.**

| C Expression | ARM Equivalent | Effective Address (EA) | Data Size Scaling |
|---|---|---|---|
| `*p` | `LDR Rt, [Rn]` | $EA = Rn$ | None (uses pre-existing address) |
| `*(p + n)` | `LDR Rt, [Rn, #n*4]` | $EA = Rn + 4n$ | **Word scaling factor = 4** |
| `*(p + i)` (variable) | `LDR Rt, [Rn, Rm, LSL #2]` | $EA = Rn + 4 \cdot Rm$ | **LSL #2 = multiply by 4** |
| `arr[i]` for `int` | `LDR Rt, [Rn, Rm, LSL #2]` | $EA = Rn + 4i$ | **4 bytes per int** |
| `arr[i]` for `char` | `LDRB Rt, [Rn, Rm]` | $EA = Rn + Rm$ | **1 byte per char (LDRB)** |
| `arr[i]` for `short` | `LDRH Rt, [Rn, Rm, LSL #1]` | $EA = Rn + 2i$ | **2 bytes per short (LSL #1)** |
| `*p++` (post) | `LDR Rt, [Rn], #4` | $EA = Rn$, then $Rn \leftarrow Rn + 4$ | **Post-index, write-back** |
| `*++p` (pre) | `LDR Rt, [Rn, #4]!` | $EA = Rn + 4$, $Rn \leftarrow EA$ | **Pre-index, write-back** |
| `&variable` | `ADR Rd, variable` or `LDR Rd, =variable` | Address itself, not contents | **Pseudo-instruction** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Firmware Development:** Microcontroller programmers writing firmware in C for ARM Cortex-M chips must understand this translation to optimize for **deterministic execution time** and **code size**.
- **Compiler Design:** The GCC ARM backend uses exactly these patterns when generating code from C pointer expressions. Understanding them helps in writing compiler-friendly C.
- **OS Kernel Programming:** Linux kernel developers for ARM use these addressing modes to write **context-switch routines** and **page-table manipulation** code.
- **Reverse Engineering & Security:** Binary analysts reverse-engineering ARM malware must recognize these patterns to decode obfuscated pointer arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Translation Example 1: The Classic `arr[i]` Pattern

**Given C Code:**
```c
int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
int i = 2;
int result = arr[i];
```

**Derivation Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify the type of element. `arr` is an `int` array, so each element occupies **4 bytes**.

**Step 2:** Express the address of the i-th element mathematically.
The address of `arr[i]` in memory is:
$$EA = \text{BaseAddress}(arr) + (i \times \text{sizeof(int)})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values. With `sizeof(int) = 4` bytes:
$$EA = \text{BaseAddress}(arr) + (i \times 4)$$

**Step 4:** Convert the multiplication to an ARM shift. Multiplying by 4 is equivalent to left-shifting by 2:
$$EA = \text{BaseAddress}(arr) + (i \ll 2)$$

**Step 5:** Map to ARM register convention. Let `R1 = BaseAddress(arr)` and `R2 = i`:
$$EA = R1 + (R2 \ll 2)$$

**Step 6:** Write the ARM assembly:
```arm
LDR  R1, =arr            ; R1 = base address of arr
MOV  R2, #2              ; R2 = i = 2
LDR  R3, [R1, R2, LSL #2] ; R3 = arr[2]  (loads 30)
```

**Step 7:** Verify the numerical computation:
$$EA = 1000 + (2 \ll 2) = 1000 + 8 = 1008$$

If the array base is at address 1000, then `arr[2]` is at address 1008, which correctly contains the value **30**.

### 3.2 Translation Example 2: `*p++` vs `*++p` — The Most Confused Pair

**Case A: Post-increment `*p++`**

The C semantics say: **use the current value of `p` first, THEN increment.**

Mathematical model:
$$\text{Evaluate} \;\; \text{value} = \text{memory}[p]$$
$$\text{Then} \;\; p \leftarrow p + 1$$

ARM translation uses **post-indexed addressing**:
```arm
LDR  R1, =p              ; R1 = address of pointer p
LDR  R2, [R1]            ; R2 = value of p (i.e., the address it points to)
LDR  R3, [R2], #4        ; R3 = *p;  then R2 = R2 + 4  (POST-INDEXED!)
STR  R2, [R1]            ; p = R2 (write back the incremented pointer)
```

**Case B: Pre-increment `*++p`**

The C semantics say: **increment `p` FIRST, THEN use the new value.**

Mathematical model:
$$p \leftarrow p + 1$$
$$\text{Then} \;\; \text{value} = \text{memory}[p]$$

ARM translation uses **pre-indexed with write-back**:
```arm
LDR  R1, =p
LDR  R2, [R1]            ; R2 = p
ADD  R2, R2, #4          ; p = p + 4
LDR  R3, [R2]            ; R3 = *p (use the new value)
STR  R2, [R1]            ; write back the new p
```

**Alternatively, using the `!` write-back suffix (more compact):**
```arm
LDR  R1, =p
LDR  R2, [R1]
LDR  R3, [R2, #4]!       ; R3 = *(R2+4);  R2 = R2 + 4  (PRE-INDEXED + WRITEBACK)
STR  R2, [R1]
```

### 3.3 Translation Example 3: Pointer-to-Pointer (`int **pp`)

**Given C Code:**
```c
int x = 100;
int *p = &x;
int **pp = &p;
int y = **pp;            // Double dereference
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Identify the pointer chain. `pp` points to `p`, which points to `x`.
$$\text{memory}[pp] = p, \quad \text{memory}[p] = x$$

**Step 2:** Decompose `**pp` mathematically:
$$y = \text{memory}[\text{memory}[pp]]$$
$$y = \text{memory}[\text{memory}[pp]] = \text{memory}[p] = x = 100$$

**Step 3:** Translate to ARM. Need two LDR operations in sequence:
```arm
LDR  R0, =pp             ; R0 = &pp
LDR  R1, [R0]            ; R1 = pp  (which is &p)
LDR  R2, [R1]            ; R2 = *pp (which is p, which equals &x)
LDR  R3, [R2]            ; R3 = **pp (which is x = 100)
```

**Step 4:** Numerical verification with assumed addresses:
$$\text{Let } \text{address}(pp) = 2000, \text{address}(p) = 3000, \text{address}(x) = 4000$$

$$\text{memory}[2000] = 3000 = p$$
$$\text{memory}[3000] = 4000 = x$$
$$\text{memory}[4000] = 100$$

After execution, `R3 = 100`. Verified.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Choosing the ARM Addressing Mode

| C Expression Pattern | Semantic Order | Required ARM Mode | Why This Mode? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `*p` (read only) | Read current, no change | Offset (no write-back) | Base register must be preserved |
| `*p = val` (write only) | Write current, no change | Offset (no write-back) | Same as above |
| `*p++` | Use then increment | **Post-indexed** | Increment happens *after* the access |
| `*++p` | Increment then use | **Pre-indexed with `!`** | Increment happens *before* the access |
| `arr[i]` for `int` | Random access | Register offset, scaled | Computes $i \times 4$ via LSL #2 |
| `arr[i]` for `short` | Random access | Register offset, scaled | Computes $i \times 2$ via LSL #1 |
| `arr[i]` for `char` | Random access | Register offset, unscaled | Computes $i \times 1$ (no shift) |
| `&var` | Get address | `ADR` or `LDR =var` | Pseudo-instruction for label address |
| `**pp` (double deref) | Two reads | Two plain `LDR`s | No scaling, no update |

### 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example: String Copy

**Given C code (KTU classic):**
```c
void mystrcpy(char *dest, char *src) {
    while ((*dest++ = *src++) != '\0');
}
```

**Translation derivation:**

**Step 1:** Loop semantics. Each iteration reads `*src`, writes to `*dest`, then increments both. Stops when null terminator is written.

**Step 2:** Use post-indexed addressing for both pointers. The increment must happen *after* the memory access, matching C's `*p++` semantics.

**Step 3:** ARM assembly:
```arm
mystrcpy:
    LDRB  R2, [R1], #1     ; R2 = *src; src = src + 1
    STRB  R2, [R0], #1     ; *dest = R2; dest = dest + 1
    CMP   R2, #0
    BNE   mystrcpy          ; loop if not null
    MOV   PC, LR            ; return
```

**Step 4:** Trace the first iteration. Assume `dest = 0x2000`, `src = 0x3000`, `*src = 'A' = 0x41`:
$$R2 = \text{memory}[0x3000] = 0x41 \text{ (the character 'A')}$$
$$src \leftarrow 0x3000 + 1 = 0x3001$$
$$\text{memory}[0x2000] = 0x41 \text{ (write 'A' to dest)}$$
$$dest \leftarrow 0x2000 + 1 = 0x2001$$

Loop continues until `R2 = 0`, copying the null terminator and exiting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: ARM Memory Access Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[C Source Code] --> B[Compiler / Manual Translator]
    B --> C{Identify Pointer Operation}
    C -->|Dereference| D[Load or Store Operand]
    C -->|Arithmetic| E[Compute Offset]
    E --> F{Scaled?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Apply LSL Shift]
    F -->|No| H[Use Raw Offset]
    G --> I[Compute Effective Address]
    H --> I
    D --> I
    I --> J{Select Addressing Mode}
    J -->|Offset only| K[Format LDR STR with Rn and offset]
    J -->|Pre-indexed| L[Format with exclamation mark]
    J -->|Post-indexed| M[Format with offset after brackets]
    K --> N[ARM Machine Code]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Decision Flow for Selecting ARM Addressing Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[C Pointer Expression] --> Q1{Does the expression modify the pointer variable?}
    Q1 -->|No, only reads writes| Q2{Is the index a constant or variable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, uses p++ or p+n| Q3{Does increment happen before or after access?}
    Q2 -->|Constant| Q3a[Use Immediate Offset LDR Rt Rn offset]
    Q2 -->|Variable| Q3b[Use Register Offset with LSL scaling]
    Q3 -->|Before access| Q4a[Pre-indexed with write-back LDR Rt Rn offset exclamation]
    Q3 -->|After access| Q4b[Post-indexed LDR Rt Rn offset2]
    Q3a --> Done[ARM Instruction]
    Q3b --> Done
    Q4a --> Done
    Q4b --> Done
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Effective Address Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Inputs]
        RegA[Base Register Rn]
        RegB[Offset Register Rm]
```
