        Const[Immediate Constant 12-bit]
    end
    subgraph Shifter[Barrel Shifter]
        ShiftOp{Operation}
        LSL[LSL Logical Shift Left]
        LSR[LSR Logical Shift Right]
        ASR[ASR Arithmetic Shift Right]
        ROR[ROR Rotate Right]
    end
    subgraph Adder[Address Adder]
        AddOp[Effective Address Rn plus Shifted Offset]
    end
    RegA --> AddOp
    Const --> ShiftOp
    RegB --> ShiftOp
    ShiftOp --> LSL
    ShiftOp --> LSR
    ShiftOp --> ASR
    ShiftOp --> ROR
    LSL --> AddOp
    LSR --> AddOp
    ASR --> AddOp
    ROR --> AddOp
    AddOp --> Out[Final Memory Address]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Mapping of C Operator Categories to ARM Instructions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Class[C Pointer Expression Class] --> Arith[Arithmetic Operator]
    Class --> Relat[Relational Operator]
    Class --> Logic[Logical Operator]
    Class --> Bitw[Bitwise Operator]
    Class --> Deref[Dereference Operator]
    Arith --> Add[ADD SUB RSB]
    Relat --> Cmp[CMP and conditional branches]
    Logic --> AndOr[AND ORR EOR with conditional]
    Bitw --> BitOps[AND ORR EOR BIC]
    Deref --> LdrStr[LDR STR LDRB STRB LDRH STRH]
    Deref --> PrePost[Pre-indexed Post-indexed Offset]
    PrePost --> Final[ARM Memory Instruction]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
Explain the difference between pre-indexed and post-indexed addressing modes in ARM with a suitable example.

> **Model Answer:**
> In **pre-indexed addressing**, the offset is added to the base register *before* the memory access, and optionally the base register is updated (write-back indicated by `!`). For example: `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` computes $EA = R1 + 4$, performs the load, and updates $R1 \leftarrow R1 + 4$.
>
> In **post-indexed addressing**, the base register is used directly as the address for the memory access, and *after* the access the base is updated. For example: `LDR R0, [R1], #4` performs the load from $EA = R1$, then updates $R1 \leftarrow R1 + 4$.
>
> The key difference is the **timing of the update** — pre-indexed updates *before* the access, post-indexed updates *after*. The C expression `*++p` maps to pre-indexed, while `*p++` maps to post-indexed.

**Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
Translate the following C expression into ARM assembly. Assume `int *p` is in `R0`, `int n` is in `R1`, and the result should be loaded into `R2`.
```c
int val = *(p + n);
```

> **Model Answer:**
> Since `p` is an `int` pointer, pointer arithmetic scales by **4 bytes** (the size of an int). The expression `p + n` means $p + 4n$, which is computed using `LSL #2`.
>
> ARM translation:
> ```arm
> ADD  R2, R0, R1, LSL #2   ; R2 = p + (n * 4)
> LDR  R2, [R2]             ; R2 = *(p + n)
> ```
>
> The effective address is:
> $$EA = R0 + (R1 \ll 2) = R0 + 4 \cdot R1$$
>
> Three marks are awarded for: correct scaling, correct LDR usage, and correct register allocation.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain with examples the **three families of ARM data-processing addressing modes** — offset addressing, pre-indexed addressing, and post-indexed addressing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Translate the following C code snippet into equivalent ARM assembly. Assume the array `buf` is an `int` array, `i` is an `int`, and the base address of `buf` is in `R4`. Write the complete sequence of instructions. **(7 marks)**

```c
int buf[100];
int sum = 0;
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    sum = sum + buf[i];
}
```

> **Model Answer for Part (a):**
>
> The ARM architecture provides three families of addressing modes for memory access, each with distinct semantics. The classification is based on **when the base register is updated** relative to the memory access.
>
> **Family 1: Offset Addressing (No Write-Back)**
> The effective address is computed by adding an offset to the base register, but the base register is **not modified**. Three sub-variants exist: immediate offset, register offset, and scaled register offset.
> - Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #8]` loads from $EA = R1 + 8$ without altering R1.
> - Use case: Random access to an array element without disturbing the base pointer.
>
> **Family 2: Pre-indexed Addressing (With Write-Back)**
> The effective address is computed *first*, the memory access uses this new address, and *then* the base register is updated to the new value. The `!` suffix indicates write-back.
> - Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` loads from $EA = R1 + 4$ and updates $R1 \leftarrow R1 + 4$.
> - Use case: Auto-incrementing pointers in loops, equivalent to C's `*++p`.
>
> **Family 3: Post-indexed Addressing (Write-Back After Access)**
> The original base register value is used *as-is* for the memory access, and *after* the access, the base register is updated by adding the offset.
> - Example: `LDR R0, [R1], #4` loads from $EA = R1$ (original), then updates $R1 \leftarrow R1 + 4$.
> - Use case: Stream processing where the current value is used before moving on, equivalent to C's `*p++`.
>
> **Comparison Table:**
>
> | Family | EA Computation | Base Update | C Equivalent |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Offset | $EA = Rn + \text{off}$ | None | `*p` |
> | Pre-index | $EA = Rn + \text{off}$ then access | Before access | `*++p` |
> | Post-index | $EA = Rn$ then access | After access | `*p++` |
>
> **(7 marks: 2 for definition of each family, 1 for comparison table)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Answer for Part (b):**
>
> The C code accumulates the sum of all 100 elements of `buf`. The key translation steps:
>
> 1. Initialize `sum` to 0 and `i` to 0.
> 2. Loop: load `buf[i]`, add to `sum`, increment `i`, repeat until `i = 100`.
>
> ARM assembly (assuming `R4` = base of `buf`, using `R5` for `sum`, `R6` for `i`):
>
> ```arm
>       MOV   R5, #0              ; sum = 0
>       MOV   R6, #0              ; i = 0
> LOOP  CMP   R6, #100            ; compare i with 100
>       BGE   DONE                ; exit if i >= 100
>       LDR   R7, [R4, R6, LSL #2] ; R7 = buf[i]  (scaled offset)
>       ADD   R5, R5, R7          ; sum = sum + buf[i]
>       ADD   R6, R6, #1          ; i = i + 1
>       B     LOOP                ; repeat
> DONE  ; sum is in R5
> ```
>
> **Explanation of key instruction:** `LDR R7, [R4, R6, LSL #2]` computes $EA = R4 + (R6 \ll 2) = R4 + 4 \cdot R6$, which is precisely the address of `buf[i]`. The LSL #2 implements the multiplication by 4 required for int-sized array elements.
>
> **(7 marks: 1 for loop setup, 2 for correct LDR with scaling, 2 for accumulation, 2 for correct loop control)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** What is pointer arithmetic? Explain how ARM handles pointer arithmetic for `int`, `short`, and `char` pointer types. Provide the ARM addressing mode used in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following C code. Translate it into ARM assembly and explain the addressing mode used. **(7 marks)**

```c
char *src = (char *)0x4000;
char *dst = (char *)0x5000;
int n = 10;
while (n > 0) {
    *dst++ = *src++;
    n--;
}
```

> **Model Answer for Part (a):**
>
> **Pointer arithmetic** is the set of operations that a programmer can perform on a pointer variable, primarily addition and subtraction of integers. In C, when an integer `n` is added to a pointer `p` of type `T*`, the address advances by $n \times \text{sizeof}(T)$ bytes, not by $n$ bytes. This is because the compiler (or programmer) must account for the size of the pointed-to type.
>
> **ARM's handling of pointer arithmetic:**
>
> ARM does not have dedicated pointer-arithmetic instructions. Instead, the programmer or compiler synthesizes the scaled offset using the **barrel shifter** in the addressing mode.
>
> For an `int *` (size 4 bytes), adding `n` means adding $4n$ to the address. This is achieved with `LSL #2`:
> ```arm
> LDR  R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]     ; R0 = *(p + n*4)
> ```
>
> For a `short *` (size 2 bytes), adding `n` means adding $2n$ to the address. This is achieved with `LSL #1`:
> ```arm
> LDRH R0, [R1, R2, LSL #1]     ; R0 = *(p + n*2), half-word load
> ```
>
> For a `char *` (size 1 byte), adding `n` means adding $n$ to the address. No scaling is needed:
> ```arm
> LDRB R0, [R1, R2]             ; R0 = *(p + n), byte load
> ```
>
> **Summary table:**
>
> | C Type | Scaling Factor | ARM Instruction | Shift |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | `int *` | 4 | `LDR` | `LSL #2` |
> | `short *` | 2 | `LDRH` | `LSL #1` |
> | `char *` | 1 | `LDRB` | None |
>
> **(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for the three cases, 2 for the summary)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Answer for Part (b):**
>
> The C code copies `n` bytes from `src` to `dst` using post-increment semantics (`*dst++ = *src++`). For `char` pointers, no scaling is needed since `sizeof(char) = 1`.
>
> ARM assembly:
> ```arm
>       MOV   R0, #0x4000       ; R0 = src (base address)
>       MOV   R1, #0x5000       ; R1 = dst (base address)
>       MOV   R2, #10           ; R2 = n
> LOOP  CMP   R2, #0            ; compare n with 0
>       BEQ   DONE              ; exit if n == 0
>       LDRB  R3, [R0], #1      ; R3 = *src;  src = src + 1  (POST-INDEXED)
>       STRB  R3, [R1], #1      ; *dst = R3;  dst = dst + 1  (POST-INDEXED)
>       SUB   R2, R2, #1        ; n--
>       B     LOOP              ; repeat
> DONE  ; copying complete
> ```
>
> **Explanation of addressing mode used:**
> The `*src++` and `*dst++` patterns in C use the value *first*, then increment. This maps directly to ARM's **post-indexed addressing** mode. The format `LDRB R3, [R0], #1` means:
> 1. Effective address for the load = R0 (the current value).
> 2. Load byte from that address into R3.
> 3. Update R0 ← R0 + 1.
>
> Since the elements are `char` (1 byte), the offset in post-indexed mode is `#1`. If this were an `int` array, the offset would be `#4` to reflect the 4-byte size.
>
> **(7 marks: 2 for LDRB/STRB usage, 2 for correct post-index syntax, 2 for loop structure, 1 for explanation)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 1–3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling factor.** Writing `LDR R0, [R1, R2]` instead of `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` for an `int` array. This compiles but reads the wrong memory location.
>
> 2. **Confusing pre-index with post-index.** Writing `LDR R0, [R1], #4` when the C code says `*++p` instead of `*p++`. Always check whether the increment happens before or after the dereference.
>
> 3. **Using LDR instead of LDRB/LDRH.** For `char` and `short` arrays, using the 4-byte `LDR` instruction will read adjacent bytes as well, giving garbage values.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the write-back `!` on pre-indexed mode.** Writing `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` instead of `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` for a pre-increment operation. The pointer won't actually be updated.
>
> 5. **Wrong register allocation in the formula.** The base register is `Rn`, not `Rm`. The offset register is `Rm` (or an immediate). Mixing them up is a common error.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to load the pointer's address first.** In C, `p` is a variable that holds an address. To dereference it, you must first LDR the pointer's storage location to get the actual address, then use that to access the target.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This for the Day Before the Exam):**
>
> - **The effective address formula** is: $EA = \text{Base} + (\text{Offset} \ll \text{Shift})$.
> - **Scaling rule:** `char` = no shift, `short` = LSL #1, `int`/`float` = LSL #2, `long long`/`double` = LSL #3.
> - **Three families of ARM addressing:** Offset (no update), Pre-indexed (`!`, update before access), Post-indexed (update after access).
> - **`*p++` always uses post-indexed** addressing: `LDR Rt, [Rn], #scale`.
> - **`*++p` always uses pre-indexed with `!`:** `LDR Rt, [Rn, #scale]!`.
> - **`*p` (no change) uses plain offset:** `LDR Rt, [Rn]` or `LDR Rt, [Rn, #offset]`.
> - **For arrays:** `arr[i]` for an `int` array becomes `LDR Rt, [Rn, Rm, LSL #2]` because the address must skip $4i$ bytes.
> - **LDR vs LDRB vs LDRH:** Match the load width to the C type — `LDR` for `int`/`float` (4 bytes), `LDRH` for `short` (2 bytes), `LDRB` for `char` (1 byte).
> - **Pointer-to-pointer requires multiple LDRs:** `**pp` needs two sequential `LDR` instructions, one for each level of indirection.
> - **The barrel shifter is the secret weapon:** All scaling in ARM addressing modes is done free of charge by the barrel shifter, costing zero additional clock cycles.
> - **The `!` symbol is the write-back flag:** Always remember that `!` means "update the base register to the new address."
> - **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Expect at least one 7-mark question requiring a C-to-ARM translation, and one 3-mark question on pre vs post addressing differences.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Translating C Subscript Expressions to Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Translating C Subscript Expressions to Assembly # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Translating C Subscript Expressions to Assembly** is the systematic process of converting high-level array index expressions (e.g., `arr[i]`, `mat[i][j]`) used in the C programming language into their equivalent low-level **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) Assembly Language** instructions. The compiler must resolve the index expression, compute the effective memory address by applying the formula `Base_Address + (Index × Element_Size)`, and then emit an appropriate `LDR` (Load Register) or `STR` (Store Register) instruction using one of ARM's flexible indexed addressing modes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 2):** Students must be able to convert C statements containing subscripted variables (1-D and 2-D arrays) into equivalent ARM assembly using immediate, register, and scaled register offsets. This is a **high-frequency** ESE (End Semester Evaluation) question. ## 1.2 Intuition Through Real-World Analogy Imagine a **long apartment building (memory)** where each apartment is numbered sequentially starting from zero. | Real-World Object | C / Assembly Equivalent | |---|---| | Building itself | Entire memory / array | | Ground floor entrance address | Base address of array | | Each apartment (same size) | One element (int = 4 bytes) | | Elevator panel number `i` | Subscript index `i` | | Apartment `(i × 4)` from entrance | Effective byte offset | When you say **"go to apartment #i"** in C (`arr[i]`), the compiler's job is to calculate *exactly* which physical elevator stop corresponds to that logical number. Since each apartment (an `int`) is 4 doors wide, **apartment #5 is not 5 doors from the entrance — it is 5 × 4 = 20 doors away.** This multiplication by element size is the heart of subscript translation. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In C, the array name `arr` is essentially a **constant pointer** to the first element (`&arr[0]`). The subscript operator `[i]` is defined to be equivalent to `*(arr + i)`. The compiler exploits this identity during code generation. ## 1.3 ARM Data Type Sizes (Mandatory Reference) | C Data Type | Size (Bytes) | ARM Load/Store Mnemonic | |---|---|---| | `char` | **1** | `LDRB` / `STRB` | | `short` | **2** | `LDRH` / `STRH` | | `int` | **4** | `LDR` / `STR` | | `long` | **4** | `LDR` / `STR` | | `float` | **4** | `FLD` (FPU) / `VLDR` | | `double` | **8** | `FLDD` / `VLDR` | > [!TIP] > The suffix `B` (Byte), `H` (Halfword), and the absence of suffix (Word = 4 bytes) tells the ARM processor **how many bytes** to transfer. Choosing the wrong suffix is a common KTU exam blunder. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Subscript Translation Works The translation process follows a strict, deterministic pipeline that a compiler (or a KTU exam student) must execute: 1. **Identify the Array Base Address:** This is the symbolic label or pointer value pointing to `arr[0]`. 2. **Identify the Index Expression:** Determine whether the index is a **constant literal**, a **single variable**, or a **complex expression** (e.g., `2*i + 1`). 3. **Identify the Element Data Type:** Look up the size in bytes (Section 1.3 table). 4. **Compute the Byte Offset:** Apply the canonical formula: $$\text{Byte\_Offset} = \text{Index\_Expression} \times \text{Element\_Size\_in\_Bytes}$$ 5. **Choose an ARM Addressing Mode:** Pick the most efficient mode: - **Immediate offset** — if the index is a compile-time constant → `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]` - **Register offset** — if the index is a variable → `LDR Rd, [Rn, Rm]` - **Scaled register offset** — when you need to multiply on the fly → `LDR Rd, [Rn, Rm, LSL #n]` 6. **Emit the LDR/STR instruction** with the correct width suffix (`B`, `H`, or none). > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Step 4 is unavoidable because C pointers are **byte-addressable**. The CPU must know the *exact byte* on the data bus. Step 5 matters because ARM was designed for efficiency — using a scaled register offset `LSL #2` lets the barrel shifter multiply the index by 4 in **a single clock cycle**, avoiding a separate `MUL` instruction. ## 2.2 ARM Indexed Addressing Modes — The Translator's Toolkit ARM provides **seven** addressing modes for `LDR`/`STR`. For KTU Module 2, the following three are mandatory: | Addressing Mode | Syntax | When Used (C → ASM) | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Immediate Offset** | `[Rn, #±offset]` | Constant index `arr[5]` | `LDR R0, [R1, #20]` | | **Register Offset** | `[Rn, ±Rm]` | Variable index where size = 1 | `LDRB R0, [R1, R2]` | | **Scaled Register Offset** | `[Rn, ±Rm, LSL #n]` | Variable index, size = 2ⁿ | `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Scaled Register Trick:** Because `arr[i]` for an `int` array needs `4 × i`, ARM's **barrel shifter** can perform `i << 2` for free as part of the address calculation. The shift count `n` is restricted to the values where `2ⁿ` equals the element size: `n = 0` (size 1), `n = 1` (size 2), `n = 2` (size 4), `n = 3` (size 8). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Address of element | $\&a[i] = \text{Base} + i \times S$ | bytes | $S$ = element size | | 2 | For `int arr[N]` | $\&a[i] = \text{Base} + 4i$ | bytes | Use `LSL #2` | | 3 | For `char arr[N]` | $\&a[i] = \text{Base} + i$ | bytes | Use `LDRB/STRB` | | 4 | For `short arr[N]` | $\&a[i] = \text{Base} + 2i$ | bytes | Use `LSL #1` | | 5 | 2-D Row-Major | $\&a[i][j] = \text{Base} + (i \times N + j) \times S$ | bytes | $N$ = number of columns | | 6 | 2-D Column-Major | $\&a[i][j] = \text{Base} + (j \times M + i) \times S$ | bytes | $M$ = number of rows | | 7 | Address alignment | Address must be multiple of $S$ | bytes | Misaligned access → fault | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** C arrays are stored in **row-major order** by default (leftmost index varies slowest). Students frequently confuse row-major with column-major and lose 2-3 marks per error in 14-mark questions. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility In embedded systems development (the primary domain of this KTU course), the ability to mentally translate `arr[i]` to assembly is critical for: - **Performance optimization:** Recognizing when the compiler has failed to use a scaled offset (a wasted `MUL`). - **Debugging disassembly:** Understanding compiler-generated `.s` files in `gcc -S` output. - **Real-time systems:** Predicting worst-case execution time (WCET) requires knowing instruction count, which depends on whether a `MUL` was emitted. - **DSP and image processing:** Pixel arrays (`uint8_t image[height][width]`) demand manual address calculation in tight inner loops. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Formula is `Base + i × S` **Starting Point (C Language Definition):** The C standard (ISO/IEC 9899) defines the subscript operator formally as: $$ a[i] \equiv *(a + i) $$ This means `a[i]` is "the value stored at the memory address obtained by adding `i` to pointer `a`." **Step 1 — Pointer Arithmetic Rule:** When an integer `i` is added to a pointer `a` of type `T`, the compiler does **not** perform a raw byte addition. It scales the integer by the size of `T$: $$ a + i \;=\; a_{\text{byte}} + i \times \text{sizeof}(T) $$ **Step 2 — Combine with Dereference:** Substituting back: $$ \&a[i] \;=\; a_{\text{byte}} + i \times \text{sizeof}(T) $$ **Step 3 — Concretize for `int`:** For `int arr[]`, `sizeof(int) = 4` on a 32-bit ARM target: $$ \&a[i] \;=\; \text{Base} + 4i $$ **Step 4 — Convert to Power-of-Two for ARM:** Recognize that $4 = 2^2$, so multiplication by 4 is a **left shift by 2**: $$ 4i \;=\; i \ll 2 $$ **Step 5 — Final ARM Instruction Form:** Given `R1` holds `Base` and `R2` holds `i`: $$ \text{LDR } R0,\; [R1,\; R2,\; \text{LSL } \#2] $$ This single instruction performs `Base + 4i` in one cycle thanks to the barrel shifter. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A 14-mark question will often ask "Translate `sum = arr[i] + arr[j];` to ARM assembly." The key is recognizing that the **shift count `2` is a direct consequence of the data type size**. Forgetting the `LSL #2` means computing the wrong memory address — a complete failure in execution, not just style. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Matrix: Translating Common C Subscript Patterns Below is an exhaustive worked matrix mapping C expressions to ARM assembly. The convention is: `R1` = base address of `arr`, `R2` = index `i`, `R3` = index `j`, `R4` = result, `R5` = general scratch. ### 3.2.1 One-Dimensional Arrays of Type `int` (size = 4) | C Statement | Effective Address Formula | ARM Assembly Code | |---|---|---| | `x = arr[0];` | Base + 0 | `LDR R4, [R1]` | | `x = arr[5];` | Base + 20 | `LDR R4, [R1, #20]` | | `x = arr[i];` | Base + 4i | `LDR R4, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` | | `arr[i] = 7;` | Base + 4i | `MOV R5, #7`
`STR R5, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` | | `x = arr[2*i+1];` | Base + 8i + 4 | `ADD R2, R2, R2, LSL #2` *(R2 = 8i + 4)*
`LDR R4, [R1, R2]` | ### 3.2.2 One-Dimensional Arrays of Type `char` (size = 1) | C Statement | Effective Address Formula | ARM Assembly Code | |---|---|---| | `c = str[0];` | Base + 0 | `LDRB R4, [R1]` | | `c = str[i];` | Base + i | `LDRB R4, [R1, R2]` | | `str[i] = 'A';` | Base + i | `MOV R5, #0x41`
`STRB R5, [R1, R2]` | > [!NOTE] > Notice that for `char`, **no shift is required** because size = 1, which is $2^0$. The assembly is shorter and faster — a real engineering reason to prefer `char` over `int` for storage-sensitive applications. ### 3.2.3 Two-Dimensional Arrays (Row-Major) For an `int mat[3][4]` (3 rows, 4 columns, `S = 4`): | C Statement | Effective Address Formula | ARM Assembly Code | |---|---|---| | `x = mat[i][j];` | Base + (4·i + j)·4 | See expansion below | **Detailed expansion of `x = mat[i][j]` for `int mat[3][4]`:** 1. Compute row offset: `4 × i` (4 columns per row). 2. Add column index: `4 × i + j`. 3. Multiply by element size: `(4i + j) × 4 = 16i + 4j`. 4. Add base. Final assembly (with `R2 = i`, `R3 = j`, `R1 = base`): ```assembly ADD R2, R2, R2, LSL #2 ; R2 = 4*i (i << 2 + i) ADD R2, R2, R3 ; R2 = 4*i + j LDR R4, [R1, R2, LSL #2] ; R4 = mat[i][j] ``` ### 3.2.4 Pointer-Based Subscript (Equivalent Translation) Since `a[i] ≡ *(a + i)`, the following C snippet: ```c int *p = arr; x = p[i]; ``` generates **identical** ARM code to `x = arr[i]`. The pointer is just a movable base. ## 3.3 Complete Exhaustive Example: Translating a Function **C Source Code:** ```c int scale(int arr[], int n) { int i, sum = 0; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { sum += arr[i]; } return sum; } ``` **Step-by-Step ARM Assembly Translation:** ```assembly scale: MOV R0, #0 ; sum = 0 (return value in R0) MOV R1, #0 ; i = 0 loop: CMP R1, R2 ; if (i >= n) BGE done ; exit loop LDR R3, [R0, R1, LSL #2] ; R3 = arr[i] [Base + 4i] ADD R0, R0, R3 ; sum += arr[i] ADD R1, R1, #1 ; i++ B loop ; repeat done: BX LR ; return sum ``` > [!WARNING] > **Subtle bug above (intentional for instruction):** Using `R0` for both the return value AND as a scratch accumulator means `arr[i]` loads the wrong base once `sum` grows. A correct translation must reserve **a separate base register**. This is the kind of trap examiners set in 14-mark questions. **Corrected Version:** ```assembly scale: PUSH {R4, LR} ; save callee-saved registers MOV R4, #0 ; R4 = sum MOV R1, #0 ; R1 = i loop: CMP R1, R2 ; if (i >= n) BGE done ; exit loop LDR R3, [R0, R1, LSL #2] ; R3 = arr[i] (R0 = base, preserved) ADD R4, R4, R3 ; sum += arr[i] ADD R1, R1, #1 ; i++ B loop done: MOV R0, R4 ; return sum POP {R4, LR} BX LR ``` # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Translation Pipeline Flow (Mermaid) The following diagram captures the **complete decision tree** a KTU student must mentally execute while translating a C subscript expression to ARM assembly. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: C Subscript Expression e.g. arr i] --> B{Is index a compile time constant} B -- Yes --> C[Compute literal byte offset = constant x element size] C --> D{Does offset fit in 12 bit immediate range 0 to 4095} D -- Yes --> E[Emit LDR STR with immediate offset format] D -- No --> F[Split into multiple ADD instructions then LDR STR] B -- No --> G{Is element size a power of 2} G -- Yes --> H[Determine shift count n where 2 to power n = element size] H --> I[Emit LDR STR with scaled register offset Rn Rm LSL n] G -- No --> J[Emit explicit MUL instruction for index x size] J --> K[Use register offset mode without shift] E --> L{Is data type char short or int} I --> L K --> L L -- char --> M[Append B suffix: LDRB STRB] L -- short --> N[Append H suffix: LDRH STRH] L -- int or long --> O[No suffix: LDR STR] M --> P[Final ARM instruction ready] N --> P O --> P F --> P ``` ## 4.2 Memory Layout Schematic: `int arr[5]` at Base Address 0x1000 ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["0x1000
arr 0
Index 0"]:::box --> A1["0x1004
arr 1
Index 1"]:::box --> A2["0x1008
arr 2
Index 2"]:::box --> A3["0x100C
arr 3
Index 3"]:::box --> A4["0x1010
arr 4
Index 4"]:::box A0 -.->|gap 4 bytes| A1 A1 -.->|gap 4 bytes| A2 A2 -.->|gap 4 bytes| A3 A3 -.->|gap 4 bytes| A4 classDef box fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Caption:** Each box represents 4 bytes. The address difference between consecutive elements is exactly **4** (the size of `int`). The address of `arr[i]` is `0x1000 + 4i`. ## 4.3 2-D Array Memory Layout: `int mat[2][3]` ```mermaid flowchart LR R0C0["0x2000 mat 0 0"]:::r0 --> R0C1["0x2004 mat 0 1"]:::r0 --> R0C2["0x2008 mat 0 2"]:::r0 --> R1C0["0x200C mat 1 0"]:::r1 --> R1C1["0x2010 mat 1 1"]:::r1 --> R1C2["0x2014 mat 1 2"]:::r1 R0C0 -.->|row 0 ends| R1C0 classDef r0 fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#FF6600,stroke-width:2px classDef r1 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px ``` **Caption:** Row-major storage means `mat[0][0], mat[0][1], mat[0][2], mat[1][0], mat[1][1], mat[1][2]` are stored sequentially. The stride between rows is `N_cols × 4 = 12` bytes. The address of `mat[i][j]` is `Base + (i × 3 + j) × 4`. ## 4.4 Functional Architecture: How ARM Hardware Executes `LDR R4, [R1, R2, LSL #2]` ```mermaid flowchart LR R1Reg[R1: Base Address e.g. 0x1000]:::reg --> Adder R2Reg[R2: Index e.g. 3]:::reg --> Barrel[Barrel Shifter
Logical Shift Left by 2]:::shifter Barrel --> Adder Adder[Hardware Adder
Computes 0x1000 + 12]:::adder --> Bus[Memory Data Bus]:::bus Bus --> R4Reg[R4: Loaded with word at 0x100C]:::reg classDef reg fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#3334FF,stroke-width:2px classDef shifter fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px classDef adder fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px classDef bus fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px ``` **Caption:** This is **why** the scaled offset mode is so powerful: the multiplication by 4 happens *inside* the address-generation hardware (the barrel shifter) for free, while a regular processor would need a separate `MUL` instruction taking 2-3 extra cycles. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Translate the following C statement to ARM assembly, assuming `R0` contains the base address of the integer array `marks`, and `R1` contains the index `i`: ```c total = marks[i]; ``` **Model Answer:** Given: `marks` is `int` array → size = 4 bytes, shift = 2. Effective address = `Base + 4 × i`. ```assembly LDR R2, [R0, R1, LSL #2] ; R2 = marks[i] MOV R3, R2 ; R3 = total (assuming R3 = total) ``` **Explanation:** The `LSL #2` performs `4 × i` via the barrel shifter. `LDR` with no suffix loads a 4-byte word because `int` is 4 bytes. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing `LDRB` instead of `LDR` is a 1-mark deduction. Using `LSL #0` (forgetting the shift) reads a wrong address and earns **0 marks** for the data-transfer portion. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]** State the effective address computed by the ARM instruction `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #3]` if `R1 = 0x2000` and `R2 = 5`. Mention the data type for which this addressing pattern is typically used. **Model Answer:** The barrel shifter computes `R2 << 3` = `5 × 8` = `40` (decimal) = `0x28`. Effective address = `R1 + 40` = `0x2000 + 0x28` = **`0x2028`**. **Data type:** `double` (8 bytes) or `long long` (8 bytes) array — both have size = $2^3$ bytes. **Explanation:** The shift count `3` is chosen because $2^3 = 8$ = size of `double`. This allows a `VLDR.64` or `LDRD` to fetch 8 bytes starting at `0x2028`. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Forgetting that `LSL #3` is a *left* shift (multiplication), not a *right* shift. Also, confusing this with a `double` (8 bytes) versus `float` (4 bytes) — using shift `3` for a `float` is a logic error. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Translation with Sub-parts** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** Consider the following C function operating on an `int` array: ```c int compute(int A[], int n) { int i, result = 0; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { result += A[i] * 2; } return result; } ``` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Translate this function into equivalent ARM assembly language, showing proper register usage. Use `R0` for the base address of `A`, `R1` for `i`, `R2` for `n`, and any other registers you need. **Model Solution:** ```assembly compute: PUSH {R4, R5, LR} ; save callee-saved regs MOV R4, #0 ; R4 = result = 0 MOV R1, #0 ; R1 = i = 0 loop: CMP R1, R2 ; if (i >= n) BGE done ; exit loop LDR R3, [R0, R1, LSL #2] ; R3 = A[i] (int → shift 2) ADD R3, R3, R3 ; R3 = A[i] * 2 ADD R4, R4, R3 ; result += A[i]*2 ADD R1, R1, #1 ; i++ B loop done: MOV R0, R4 ; return result in R0 POP {R4, R5, LR} BX LR ``` **Step-by-step explanation (as expected in valuation):** 1. **Line 1 — Prologue:** `PUSH {R4, R5, LR}` saves registers R4, R5, and the link register to stack. R4 and R5 are callee-saved per AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard). 2. **Line 2 — Initialization:** `MOV R4, #0` sets `result = 0`. R4 is used (not R0) because R0 holds the base address and must not be clobbered. 3. **Line 3 — Loop counter:** `MOV R1, #0` initializes `i = 0`. 4. **Line 4 — Loop guard:** `CMP R1, R2` compares `i` with `n`. 5. **Line 5 — Exit condition:** `BGE done` branches if `i >= n` (signed greater-or-equal). 6. **Line 6 — Array load (KEY TRANSLATION):** `LDR R3, [R0, R1, LSL #2]` translates `A[i]`. The `LSL #2` accounts for the 4-byte `int` size, computing the byte offset `4 × i`. R0 is the base. 7. **Line 7 — Multiplication by 2:** `ADD R3, R3, R3` computes `A[i] * 2` (a fast shift-add idiom, faster than `MOV R3, R3, LSL #1` or `MUL`). 8. **Line 8 — Accumulation:** `ADD R4, R4, R3` updates `result`. 9. **Line 9 — Increment:** `ADD R1, R1, #1` does `i++`. 10. **Line 10 — Branch back:** `B loop` continues the loop. 11. **Line 11 — Epilogue:** `MOV R0, R4` puts the return value in R0. 12. **Line 12 — Restore & return:** `POP` and `BX LR` complete the function. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** If the array were declared as `char A[]` instead of `int A[]`, show the **single line of code** that would change in the loop body and explain why. **Model Solution:** The changed line is the array load: ```assembly LDRB R3, [R0, R1] ; R3 = A[i] for char array ``` **Explanation:** 1. The data width suffix changes from **`LDR` (4-byte word) to `LDRB` (1-byte unsigned load)** because a `char` occupies only 1 byte in memory. Using `LDR` would erroneously load 3 extra bytes belonging to neighbouring elements, corrupting the value. 2. The **shift is removed** (`LSL #2` becomes nothing) because for a `char`, the element size is $1 = 2^0$, so the multiplication factor is 1 and no shifting is needed. 3. If `A[i]` were a signed `char` that may be negative, the instruction would be `LDRSB` (Load Register Signed Byte) to sign-extend the byte into the full 32-bit register. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Forgetting the `LSL #2` shift means the address is wrong. Forgetting the `PUSH`/`POP` of R4-R5 means the calling program's registers get corrupted (1-2 mark deduction). Using `BGE` instead of `BLT` reverses the loop condition. > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Writing `LDR R3, [R0, R1, LSL #2]` again (copy-paste mistake) — a 0 mark answer for this part because the answer is wrong. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice (2-D Array Focus)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]** Consider a 2-dimensional integer array declared as `int matrix[4][5]`. It is stored in row-major order starting at memory address `0x4000`. #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Derive the general formula for the byte address of the element `matrix[i][j]`. Show your derivation in detail. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the storage convention:** Row-major order means all elements of row 0 are stored contiguously, followed by all elements of row 1, etc. **Step 2 — Compute the number of elements before row i:** Each row contains `N_cols = 5` elements. So the number of elements in rows `0, 1, ..., i-1` is: $$ \text{Elements\_Before\_Row\_i} = i \times N_{\text{cols}} = i \times 5 $$ **Step 3 — Add column offset within row i:** The number of elements before column `j` within row `i` is simply `j`. So the total element index (zero-based) of `matrix[i][j]` in the linear storage is: $$ \text{LinearIndex} = i \times 5 + j $$ **Step 4 — Multiply by element size (sizeof(int) = 4):** The byte offset from the base is: $$ \text{ByteOffset} = (i \times 5 + j) \times 4 $$ **Step 5 — Expand the multiplication:** $$ \text{ByteOffset} = 20i + 4j $$ **Step 6 — Add the base address:** $$ \&\text{matrix}[i][j] = \text{Base} + 20i + 4j $$ Substituting `Base = 0x4000`: $$ \&\text{matrix}[i][j] = 0x4000 + 20i + 4j $$ **Final Formula:** $$ \boxed{\&\text{matrix}[i][j] = 0x4000 + 20i + 4j} $$ #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Assuming `R0 = 0x4000`, `R1 = i`, `R2 = j`, write the ARM assembly to load `matrix[i][j]` into `R3`. **Model Solution:** We need to compute `0x4000 + 20i + 4j` and then perform a 4-byte load. **Strategy:** Decompose `20i` as `16i + 4i`, then add `4j`. ```assembly ADD R1, R1, R1, LSL #2 ; R1 = 5*i (i + 4i = 5i) ADD R1, R1, R1, LSL #2 ; R1 = 20*i (5i << 2 = 20i) ADD R1, R1, R2, LSL #2 ; R1 = 20*i + 4*j LDR R3, [R0, R1] ; R3 = matrix[i][j] ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** 1. **Line 1 — `R1 = 5i`:** The idiom `R1 + R1 LSL 2` computes `i + 4i = 5i` using only an ADD, no MUL. This exploits the fact that $5 = 1 + 4 = 2^0 + 2^2$. 2. **Line 2 — `R1 = 20i`:** Multiplying `5i` by 4 gives `20i`. The shift `LSL #2` is again free via the barrel shifter. 3. **Line 3 — `R1 = 20i + 4j`:** Computes the full byte offset and stores it back in R1. `R2 LSL #2` gives `4j` for free. 4. **Line 4 — Load:** `LDR R3, [R0, R1]` reads the 4-byte word at the computed address. **Why not use a single scaled offset?** We *could* write it as `LDR R3, [R0, R1, LSL #2]` if `R1` already held `(5i + j)`, but the issue is that `5i` is **not** a power of 2, so the barrel shifter cannot produce it in one cycle. Therefore, a multi-instruction sequence is mandatory. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Students often write `(i + j) × 4` — completely wrong. The two indices do not simply add; row-major storage has a stride equal to the *number of columns* between rows. > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Using `MUL` instructions when an ADD-shift idiom exists is suboptimal but not wrong — minor (1 mark) deduction. Forgetting that `LSL #2` is needed on the final load is a critical error. --- ## KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic (Module 2):** > 1. **Forgetting the shift count** — writing `LDR R3, [R0, R1]` for an `int` array. The data will be loaded from the wrong address and the program will crash. **Penalty: 50% of marks for the load instruction.** > 2. **Wrong suffix** — `LDRB` for `int` loads 1 byte, corrupting the value. **Penalty: full marks for the load instruction.** > 3. **Confusing row-major vs column-major** in 2-D arrays. **Penalty: 2-3 marks.** > 4. **Not preserving base register** — overwriting the array's base address in a loop. **Penalty: 1-2 marks for "register management".** > 5. **Writing `LDR Rd, =label`** (assembler pseudo-instruction) instead of an indexed addressing mode. The KTU examiner wants to see **indexed addressing syntax** — pseudo-instructions don't demonstrate understanding. **Penalty: 1-2 marks.** --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Core Formula:** Address of `a[i]` = `Base + i × Element_Size` (in bytes). - ⭐ **Element Size Determines Shift:** `char` → shift 0, `short` → shift 1, `int/long/float` → shift 2, `double` → shift 3. - ⭐ **Mnemonic Suffix Rule:** `B` = Byte (1), `H` = Halfword (2), nothing = Word (4). The suffix **must match** the C data type. - ⭐ **Address Equation:** The compiler emits `LDR/STR` with the addressing mode that is most efficient for the index type: constant → immediate offset, variable → register/scaled offset. - ⭐ **Barrel Shifter Advantage:** `LSL #n` is **free** (zero extra cycles), so always prefer scaled register offset over explicit `MUL`. - ⭐ **2-D Row-Major Formula:** `&a[i][j] = Base + (i × N_cols + j) × S`. - ⭐ **2-D Column-Major Formula:** `&a[i][j] = Base + (j × N_rows + i) × S` (rare in C, used in Fortran). - ⭐ **Pointer-Subscript Equivalence:** `a[i]` and `*(a + i)` produce identical assembly. - ⭐ **Register Preservation:** Never overwrite the base address in a loop; use callee-saved registers (R4-R11) for accumulators. - ⭐ **AAPCS Compliance:** R0-R3 are caller-saved (scratch); R4-R11 are callee-saved (preserve with `PUSH`/`POP`). - ⭐ **Alignment Requirement:** ARM requires word addresses to be multiples of 4. `int arr[i]` is naturally aligned, but packed structs may not be. - ⭐ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Forgetting that `LDR` loads **4 bytes** regardless of what you "want" — the suffix controls width, not the data type declaration.

topics.title: Translating Structure References to Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Translating Structure References to Assembly ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition **Translating Structure References to Assembly** is the process by which a high-level language compiler converts symbolic structure member access (e.g., `student.marks` in C) into low-level ARM load/store instructions that physically compute the target memory address by adding a **byte offset** to a **base address**. In ARM architecture, this is performed using the base-register-plus-offset addressing mode: `[Rn, #offset]`, where `Rn` holds the base address of the structure and `#offset` is the pre-computed displacement (in bytes) of the required member from the start of the structure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > A structure (`struct`) in C is mapped onto memory as a **contiguous sequence of bytes**. The compiler allocates a single base address for the entire structure, and every member is reached by adding a *constant offset* to this base. Understanding this mapping is essential for writing efficient ARM assembly, hand-optimized code, and for the **Part B (14-mark) questions** which frequently test offset calculation. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **post office with a huge wall of PO Boxes**: - The **entire wall** = the structure (a contiguous block of memory). - The **building address** (e.g., "Civil Lines PO, Box Wall") = the **base address** stored in a register like `R0`. - Each **PO Box number** = the **byte offset** of a member from the start. - To collect your parcel, the postman walks to the building (base), then walks `N` boxes down the wall (offset). This is *exactly* what ARM does with `[R0, #8]` — start at `R0`, step 8 bytes forward. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in ARM:** Unlike x86 (which supports complex SIB/byte-offset addressing in one instruction), ARM's clean `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]` and `STR Rd, [Rn, #offset]` make the base-plus-offset model very explicit. **Offset values are pre-calculated by the compiler at compile time** and are embedded as immediate constants in the instruction. ### Key Terms (KTU Glossary) - **Structure** — A user-defined aggregate data type grouping heterogeneous members under one name. - **Base Address** — The starting memory address of a structure instance, held in a register. - **Byte Offset** — The number of bytes from the base address to a specific member. - **Structure Pointer** — A register/variable holding the address of a structure, used with `->` in C. - **Alignment** — A member is placed at an address divisible by its own size (ARM default for word = 4 bytes). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Memory Layout Principle A C structure is stored in memory with members placed **sequentially in the order declared**, padded only as needed to satisfy the alignment requirement of the next member. The compiler emits a **symbol table entry** for the structure containing the **offset of each member** from offset `0`. ### 2.2 Operational Steps in Translation The compiler executes the following logic for every structure reference: 1. **Symbol Resolution:** Locate the structure variable (or follow a pointer) to obtain its **base address**. 2. **Offset Lookup:** Look up the member's **byte offset** from the structure's compile-time symbol table. 3. **Address Calculation:** Compute `Effective_Address = Base + Offset`. 4. **Emit Instruction:** Generate `LDR` (for read) or `STR` (for write) with the appropriate data width (byte `B`, halfword `H`, or word, default). 5. **Code Generation:** For pointer dereferences (`ptr->member`), the base is loaded from the pointer register first, and the same offset logic follows. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------------|----------------| | 1 | Effective Address of a member | $E_{addr} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}$ | Offset is in **bytes** | | 2 | Address of `i`-th element in array of structs | $E_{addr} = \text{Base} + i \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{struct})$ | `sizeof` returned in bytes | | 3 | Address of nested member | $E_{addr} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}_{outer} + \text{Offset}_{inner}$ | Cumulative offsets | | 4 | Alignment of a member of size $S$ | $\text{Offset} \equiv 0 \pmod{S}$ | Word $= 4$ B, Halfword $= 2$ B | | 5 | Total structure size | $\text{sizeof} = \text{Offset}_{last} + \text{sizeof}_{last}$ (rounded up to alignment) | May include padding | | 6 | Pointer dereference | $E_{addr} = \text{Load}(\text{PtrReg}) + \text{Offset}$ | First dereference, then offset | | 7 | Structure passed by value (ARM AAPCS) | Copied onto stack, accessed via `SP` + offset | Up to 4 words in `R0`-`R3` | | 8 | Structure passed by reference | Pointer in `R0`/`R1`, accessed via `[Rn, #off]` | Most efficient in ARM | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The **offset is NOT the cumulative sum of preceding member sizes alone** — it includes any **padding bytes** inserted by the compiler to satisfy alignment. Always re-derive offsets from the *start* of the struct, not from the previous member. ### 2.4 Why 'How' and 'Why' this Translation Model? - **Why base + offset?** ARM's `LDR/STR` instruction format natively supports a 12-bit immediate offset, making member access a **single-cycle operation** with no extra arithmetic instruction. - **Why a fixed offset table?** The compiler pre-computes offsets at compile time, so the running program never recalculates them. This trades a small fixed amount of code space for **fast, predictable runtime performance** — a hallmark of RISC design. - **Real-world engineering use:** Embedded firmware, device-driver structures (e.g., `struct UART_Config`), and RTOS task control blocks all rely on this deterministic offset mechanism for portability across ARM cores (Cortex-M0/M3/M4/A53/A57). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple Structure Offset Calculation **C Declaration (given in question):** ```c struct Student { int id; // 4 bytes, aligned to 4 char grade; // 1 byte, aligned to 1 float marks; // 4 bytes, aligned to 4 short rank; // 2 bytes, aligned to 2 }; ``` **Step 1 — Determine each member's offset from the base address `0x0000`:** - `id` : placed at offset $\mathbf{0}$, occupies bytes $0{-}3$. - `grade` : placed at offset $\mathbf{4}$, occupies byte $4$. - Padding : the compiler inserts **3 padding bytes** at offsets $5, 6, 7$ so that the next 4-byte float `marks` is aligned to a 4-byte boundary. - `marks` : placed at offset $\mathbf{8}$, occupies bytes $8{-}11$. - `rank` : placed at offset $\mathbf{12}$, occupies bytes $12{-}13$. - Trailing padding: 2 bytes at $14, 15$ to make `sizeof(struct Student) = 16` (multiple of 4). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Offset}_{id} &= 0 \\ \text{Offset}_{grade} &= 4 \\ \text{Offset}_{marks} &= 8 \\ \text{Offset}_{rank} &= 12 \\ \text{sizeof}(struct\ Student) &= 16 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Logic row 1:** `id` is the first member, so it always begins at offset `0` from the base. > **Logic row 2:** `grade` follows `id`; the next free byte is `4`, and no alignment is required for a `char` (size 1), so offset is `4`. > **Logic row 3:** `marks` is a `float` (4 bytes) and **must** start at a multiple of 4. The next multiple of 4 after byte 5 is byte 8, hence offset `8`. > **Logic row 4:** `rank` is a `short` (2 bytes) and must start at an even offset. Byte 12 is even, so offset `12`. > **Logic row 5:** The structure's total size is rounded up to the largest alignment (4 bytes), giving `16`. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Translating C Code to ARM Assembly **C Source:** ```c struct Student s; // s at base address 0x20000000 s.id = 1042; s.marks = 88.5f; total = s.marks + s.rank; // assume 'total' is a float ``` **Step-by-step ARM translation (assuming base address of `s` is already in `R4`):** **Step A — Store the integer `id` (offset 0, 4 bytes):** ```assembly LDR R0, =1042 ; load immediate value 1042 STR R0, [R4, #0] ; store into s.id (base + 0) ``` > **Logic row:** We use a `STR` with word-width (default) and offset `#0` to write the 4-byte integer exactly at the base address. **Step B — Store the float `marks` (offset 8, 4 bytes):** ```assembly ; In ARM, floats are stored in single-precision FPU registers (S0..S31) VLDR S0, =88.5 ; load constant 88.5 into FPU register VSTR S0, [R4, #8] ; store into s.marks (base + 8) ``` > **Logic row:** Because `marks` is a `float`, the Floating Point Unit (VFP) instructions `VLDR`/`VSTR` are used. The offset `#8` matches the byte position derived in Worked Example 1. **Step C — Load and add `marks` + `rank`:** ```assembly VLDR S1, [R4, #8] ; S1 = s.marks LDRSH R2, [R4, #12] ; R2 = s.rank (signed halfword) VMOV S2, R2 ; move R2 into FPU register S2 VADD.F32 S3, S1, S2 ; S3 = s.marks + (float)s.rank ``` > **Logic row:** `s.marks` is at base+8, read with `VLDR`. `s.rank` is a 16-bit `short`, so we use `LDRSH` (Load Register Signed Halfword) with offset `#12`. The integer is moved into the FPU register and added using `VADD.F32`. ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Array of Structures (Indexed Access) **C Source:** ```c struct Point { int x; int y; }; // sizeof = 8 bytes struct Point arr[5]; // base in R5 int i = 3; int val = arr[i].y; ``` **Derivation of effective address:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sizeof}(struct\ Point) &= 4 + 4 = 8 \text{ bytes} \\ E_{addr}\ \text{of}\ arr[i].y &= \text{Base}_{arr} + (i \cdot 8) + \text{Offset}_{y} \\ &= \text{Base}_{arr} + (3 \cdot 8) + 4 \\ &= \text{Base}_{arr} + 28 \end{aligned} $$ **ARM translation (with `R5` = base, `R6` = `i`):** ```assembly MOV R6, #3 ; i = 3 LDR R0, [R5, R6, LSL #3] ; R0 = arr[i].x (base + i*8 + 0) LDR R1, [R5, R6, LSL #3] ; (R1 holds intermediate) ADD R1, R1, #4 ; R1 now points to arr[i].y LDR R2, [R1] ; R2 = arr[i].y ``` > **Logic row 1:** ARM's *register-offset* addressing `[R5, R6, LSL #3]` elegantly computes `Base + i*8` in **one instruction** (shift left by 3 = multiply by 8). > **Logic row 2:** The y-offset (4) is then added explicitly, and the value loaded. This is a common compiler output pattern. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Member Access Patterns | C Expression | Pre-requisite | ARM Pattern | Offset (B) | Instruction | |--------------|---------------|-------------|-----------|-------------| | `s.id` (int) | `R0` = &s | Read word at base | 0 | `LDR R1, [R0, #0]` | | `s.grade` (char) | `R0` = &s | Read single byte | 4 | `LDRB R1, [R0, #4]` | | `s.marks` (float) | `R0` = &s | Read via VFP | 8 | `VLDR S0, [R0, #8]` | | `s.rank` (short) | `R0` = &s | Read 2 bytes | 12 | `LDRSH R1, [R0, #12]` | | `p->marks` | `R0` = p (pointer) | Same as `(*p).marks` | 8 | `VLDR S0, [R0, #8]` | | `arr[i].y` | `R0`=arr, `R1`=i | Base + i*size + offset | 4 (post) | `LDR R2, [R0, R1, LSL #3]` then `+4` | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Memory Layout of `struct Student` ```mermaid graph LR A["BASE 0x00
int id 4B"] --> B["+0x04
char grade 1B"] B --> C["+0x05
PAD 3B"] C --> D["+0x08
float marks 4B"] D --> E["+0x0C
short rank 2B"] E --> F["+0x0E
PAD 2B"] F --> G["+0x10
END
sizeof=16"] ``` ### 4.2 Translation Flow: C Source to ARM Assembly ```mermaid graph TD src["C Source:
s.marks = 88.5f"] --> lex["Lexical Analysis
Identify 's' as struct
Identify 'marks' as member"] lex --> symtab["Symbol Table Lookup
base(s) = R4
offset(marks) = 8"] symtab --> addrcalc["Address Calculation
EA = R4 + 8"] addrcalc --> emit["Emit ARM Instruction
VSTR S0, [R4, #8]"] emit --> bin["Object Code
0xED84 0A08"] ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram of Structure Access Mechanism ```mermaid graph TD A["ARM Register R0
holds base address
of structure"] --> B["ALU
Base + Offset"] O["Immediate Field
#offset 12-bit"] --> B B --> C["Effective Address
sent to memory bus"] C --> D["Memory Unit
reads/writes 1, 2, or 4 bytes"] D --> E["Data Register
R1 or FPU S0"] ``` ### 4.4 Array-of-Structures Indexing Flow ```mermaid graph LR BASE["R5 = arr base"] --> MUL["i 3 shift left 3 = 24"] MUL --> ADD1["+ offset of x = 0"] ADD1 --> XMEM["arr 3 x location"] ADD2["+ offset of y = 4"] --> YMEM["arr 3 y location"] MUL --> ADD2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** *Explain how a C structure is mapped to memory. Why is byte alignment necessary when translating structure references to ARM assembly?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A C structure is stored as a **contiguous block of memory** with members laid out in the order of declaration, each placed at a fixed byte offset from the structure's base address. (1 mark) - The compiler embeds these offsets as immediate constants in `LDR`/`STR` instructions of the form `[Rn, #offset]`. (1 mark) - Byte alignment is necessary because ARM data-transfer instructions require the **address of a word to be a multiple of 4**, a halfword to be a multiple of 2, and a byte to have no restriction. Misaligned access causes a *Data Abort* exception in ARM. The compiler therefore inserts **padding bytes** to ensure each member's offset is a multiple of its own size. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, S1 Sample]** *With a suitable example, show how the expression `p->salary` is translated to ARM assembly.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The arrow operator `->` is syntactically equivalent to `(*p).salary`. (1 mark) - The compiler treats `p` (held in say `R0`) as the base address of the structure. (1 mark) - Suppose `salary` is an `int` at offset `12` inside the structure, the compiler emits: `LDR R1, [R0, #12]`. The base register `R0` is the pointer value, and the offset is added inside the brackets. (1 mark) --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (commonly losing 2-4 marks):** > 1. **Forgetting padding** — students compute offsets as a running sum of member sizes, missing the alignment bytes the compiler inserts. > 2. **Using the wrong data-width instruction** — emitting `LDR` (word) for a `char` field will fetch 3 extra bytes, causing a read of adjacent members. > 3. **Treating `->` differently from `.`** — in assembly, both produce *the same instruction*; the arrow only signals that the LHS is a pointer. > 4. **Forgetting the array-of-struct stride** — accessing `arr[i].member` with offset `i` instead of `i * sizeof(struct)`. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Consider the following C structure declaration in an ARM Cortex-M program: ```c struct Sensor { char type; // 1 byte short id; // 2 bytes int value; // 4 bytes double timestamp;// 8 bytes } sensor; ``` Assume the structure `sensor` is allocated at memory location `0x20002000`. **(i)** Draw the memory layout of the structure showing all members and any padding bytes, and compute the total `sizeof(struct Sensor)`. (7 marks) **(ii)** Write the ARM assembly code sequence to: - Store the value `0x55` into `sensor.type`, - Store the value `1234` into `sensor.id`, - Load `sensor.value` into register `R3`. (7 marks) --- ##### Model Solution **(a)(i) Memory Layout (7 marks):** - `type` — first member, placed at offset $\mathbf{0}$, occupies byte 0. - `id` — a `short` (2 bytes) needs offset to be a multiple of 2. Next multiple of 2 after 0 is 0; but since `type` occupies 1 byte, the next free byte is 1, and the next multiple of 2 is 2. So `id` is placed at offset $\mathbf{2}$, occupying bytes 2-3. - `value` — an `int` (4 bytes) needs offset to be a multiple of 4. Next multiple of 4 after byte 4 is 4, so `value` is at offset $\mathbf{4}$, occupying bytes 4-7. - `timestamp` — a `double` (8 bytes) needs offset to be a multiple of 8. Next multiple of 8 after byte 8 is 8, so `timestamp` is at offset $\mathbf{8}$, occupying bytes 8-15. - No trailing padding is required because the total size (16) is already a multiple of the largest alignment (8). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Offset}_{type} &= 0 \\ \text{Offset}_{id} &= 2 \\ \text{Offset}_{value} &= 4 \\ \text{Offset}_{timestamp} &= 8 \\ \text{sizeof}(struct\ Sensor) &= 16 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Memory map at base 0x20002000:** | Offset (B) | Address | Member | Size | |-----------:|---------------|--------------|------| | 0 | 0x20002000 | `type` | 1 B | | 1 | 0x20002001 | *padding* | 1 B | | 2 | 0x20002002 | `id` | 2 B | | 4 | 0x20002004 | `value` | 4 B | | 8 | 0x20002008 | `timestamp` | 8 B | | **Total** | **0x20002010**| **End** | **16 B** | **(a)(ii) ARM Assembly Code (7 marks):** ```assembly LDR R4, =0x20002000 ; R4 = base address of sensor ; Store 0x55 into sensor.type (offset 0, 1 byte) MOV R0, #0x55 STRB R0, [R4, #0] ; byte write at base + 0 ; Store 1234 into sensor.id (offset 2, 2 bytes) MOV R0, #1234 STRH R0, [R4, #2] ; halfword write at base + 2 ; Load sensor.value (offset 4, 4 bytes) into R3 LDR R3, [R4, #4] ; word read at base + 4 ``` **Mark distribution rationale (KTU pattern):** - 2 marks: Correct base address setup. - 2 marks: Correct use of `STRB` (byte store) for `char`. - 1 mark: Correct use of `STRH` (halfword store) for `short`. - 2 marks: Correct `LDR` with right offset for `int`. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** **(a)** Given the C declarations: ```c struct Employee { int empId; // 4 bytes char name[8]; // 8 bytes (array of chars) float salary; // 4 bytes int deptCode; // 4 bytes }; struct Employee e[4]; // array of 4 employees, base in R5 int idx = 2; // index, in R6 ``` **(i)** Compute the byte offset of each member, the total size of one `struct Employee`, and the effective address of `e[idx].salary`. (7 marks) **(ii)** Write the ARM assembly code to read `e[idx].salary` into FPU register `S5`, then add 5000.0 to it and store the result back into `e[idx].salary`. (7 marks) --- ##### Model Solution **(b)(i) Offset Table and Effective Address (7 marks):** - `empId` at offset 0 (4 bytes). - `name[8]` at offset 4 (8 bytes, byte-aligned, no padding). - `salary` at offset 12 (4 bytes, aligned to 4 — next multiple of 4 after byte 12 is 12, so no padding). - `deptCode` at offset 16 (4 bytes). - Total size = 20 bytes. Rounded up to next multiple of 4 = 20 (already a multiple). So `sizeof = 20`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Offset}_{empId} &= 0 \\ \text{Offset}_{name} &= 4 \\ \text{Offset}_{salary} &= 12 \\ \text{Offset}_{deptCode} &= 16 \\ \text{sizeof} &= 20 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Effective address of `e[idx].salary`:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{addr} &= \text{Base}_{e} + (\text{idx} \times 20) + 12 \\ &= \text{Base}_{e} + (2 \times 20) + 12 \\ &= \text{Base}_{e} + 52 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification (5 mark components):** - 2 marks: correct offsets (including 0, 4, 12, 16). - 2 marks: correct `sizeof` (20 bytes). - 3 marks: correct effective-address formula and numerical evaluation. **(b)(ii) ARM Assembly Code (7 marks):** ```assembly ; R5 = base address of array e ; R6 = idx = 2 ; Compute (idx * sizeof) = 2 * 20 = 40 MOV R7, R6, LSL #4 ; R7 = idx * 16 = 32 (no, this is wrong) ; Correction: use MUL or a sequence MOV R0, #20 MUL R7, R6, R0 ; R7 = idx * 20 = 40 ADD R7, R7, #12 ; R7 = 40 + 12 = 52 (offset within struct) ; Read e[idx].salary (offset 52 from base) ADD R8, R5, R7 ; R8 = effective address VLDR S5, [R8] ; S5 = e[idx].salary ; Load constant 5000.0 VLDR S6, =5000.0 VADD.F32 S5, S5, S6 ; S5 = S5 + 5000.0 ; Store back VSTR S5, [R8] ; e[idx].salary = new value ``` **Mark distribution rationale:** - 2 marks: Correct stride computation `idx * 20`. - 2 marks: Correct addition of struct-internal offset (12). - 1 mark: Correct `VLDR` from computed address. - 1 mark: Correct `VADD.F32` with 5000.0. - 1 mark: Correct `VSTR` back to memory. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake in Question B:** Students often write `R7 = idx, LSL #4` thinking it is "multiply by 16", but they actually need **multiply by 20**. ARM has no `LSL` by a non-power-of-two, so either use `MUL` or shift and add: `R7 = (idx LSL #4) + (idx LSL #2)` = `idx*16 + idx*4 = idx*20`. **Loss of 2 marks** if the stride is wrong. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] A structure occupies a **contiguous block of memory** starting at its base address. - [ ] Every member has a **fixed byte offset** from offset 0, pre-computed by the compiler. - [ ] ARM translates member access into **`LDR/STR [Rn, #offset]`** (or VFP variants for `float`/`double`). - [ ] The **arrow operator `->` produces the same instructions as the dot operator `.`** — both rely on a base register plus an offset. - [ ] **Byte alignment is mandatory** in ARM: a word must be at an address divisible by 4, a halfword by 2. - [ ] **Padding bytes** are inserted between members (and at the end) to satisfy alignment; total `sizeof` is rounded up to the largest member's alignment. - [ ] For an **array of structures**, the element stride is `sizeof(struct)`, and ARM's `[Rn, Rm, LSL #k]` elegantly handles the multiplication. - [ ] Always use the **correct data-width instruction**: `LDRB`/`STRB` for `char`, `LDRH`/`STRH`/`LDRSH` for `short`, `LDR`/`STR` for `int`/`long`, `VLDR`/`VSTR` for `float`/`double`. - [ ] The **arrow operator** requires an **extra `LDR` first** to dereference the pointer, *only* if the base is in memory; if the base is already in a register, no extra load is needed. - [ ] Most frequently tested fact in KTU exams: **"Offset of a member is the sum of sizes of all preceding members + any inserted padding."** - [ ] The instruction `LDR R0, [R1, #8]` reads 4 bytes from address `R1 + 8` into `R0`; it is **one fetch** in the instruction cycle, not a multi-step ALU operation.

topics.title: Stack Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stack Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Instructions** in the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** architecture are a specialized subset of the *Block Data Transfer Instructions* that use the **Stack Pointer (SP / R13)** as the base register to either store (PUSH) or load (POP) a set of general-purpose registers onto/from a region of memory called the **Stack**.

A **Stack** is a *Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)* data structure implemented in hardware memory. In ARM, it is a contiguous region of memory whose top-of-stack address is always held in **R13 (SP)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Stack of Trays" in a Canteen:**
> Imagine a spring-loaded stack of trays in a college canteen.
> - You can only **add (PUSH)** a new tray on the **top** of the existing pile.
> - You can only **remove (POP)** a tray from the **top** of the pile.
> - Your hand (the Stack Pointer) always points to the **topmost tray** (the highest filled position).
> - You cannot pull out a tray from the **middle** or **bottom** of the stack directly.
>
> In ARM, the **Stack Pointer (R13)** is your "hand", and **PUSH/POP** are the only allowed operations. The stack grows **downward in memory** (toward lower addresses) in the default ARM convention.

### Key Registers Associated with Stacks

| Register | Name | Role in Stack Operation |
|----------|------|--------------------------|
| **R13** | **SP** (Stack Pointer) | Holds the address of the current top-of-stack |
| **R14** | **LR** (Link Register) | Stores the return address when a function is called (used with `BL`) |
| **R15** | **PC** (Program Counter) | The instruction being fetched/executed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Default Stack Convention:** **Full Descending (FD)**
> - **Full** → The SP points to the last item pushed (contains valid data).
> - **Descending** → The stack grows toward **lower memory addresses**.

The four theoretical stack models are:
- **FA** — Full Ascending
- **FD** — Full Descending ← *ARM default*
- **EA** — Empty Ascending
- **ED** — Empty Descending

### Stack Instruction Mnemonics

| Instruction | Direction | Operation |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| **`PUSH {<reg_list>}`** | Register → Memory | Decrement SP, then store registers |
| **`POP {<reg_list>}`** | Memory → Register | Load registers, then increment SP |

Explicit examples: `PUSH {R0, R1-R4, LR}` and `POP {R0, R1-R4, PC}`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Stack Pointer (SP) — R13

The **Stack Pointer (R13)** in ARM is a **banked register** in privileged modes, meaning each operating mode can have its own physical SP, but the same R13 name is used. In **User mode**, R13 is unbanked. The SP is always **word-aligned** (4-byte aligned) because all stack operations move **32-bit words**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why R13 must be word-aligned:** Each PUSH or POP operates on entire 32-bit registers, and ARM memory is byte-addressable. Pushing/Popping in units other than 4 bytes would misalign the pointer and cause a fault.

### 2.2 PUSH Instruction — Step-by-Step Logic

The instruction format is:
```
PUSH {<reg_list>}
```

**Operational steps executed by the ARM core:**
1. The **stack pointer (SP)** is **decremented by 4** for *each* register in the register list.
2. Each register in the list is **stored at the new SP value**.
3. The **lowest-numbered register** is stored at the **lowest memory address** (the final SP after decrement).
4. The **highest-numbered register** is stored at the **highest memory address** (SP + 4×(n-1)).
5. SP is updated to point to the lowest address used.

**Memory address formula for PUSH (Full Descending, n registers):**
$$
\text{Final SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4n
$$

**Address of register $R_i$ in the push block:**
$$
\text{Addr}(R_i) = \text{SP}_{\text{new}} + 4 \times (i - \text{min\_reg})
$$

### 2.3 POP Instruction — Step-by-Step Logic

The instruction format is:
```
POP {<reg_list>}
```

**Operational steps executed by the ARM core:**
1. The **lowest-numbered register** is **loaded from the address pointed to by SP**.
2. Each subsequent register in the list is loaded from the next higher address.
3. The **SP is incremented by 4** for *each* register loaded.
4. SP ends up pointing to the first free location *above* the popped data.

**Final SP after POP of n registers:**
$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 4n
$$

### 2.4 Special Uses of LR and PC in Stack Instructions

- `PUSH {... , LR}` — saves the link register to preserve the return address across function calls.
- `POP  {... , PC}` — restores the program counter, effectively **returning from a subroutine** by loading the saved return address into PC. This is equivalent to `MOV PC, LR` but occurs *atomically* with the POP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Pattern:** Function Prologue and Epilogue
> ```assembly
> SUB    SP, SP, #16        ; Allocate 16 bytes on stack
> STMFD  SP!, {R4-R7, LR}   ; PUSH (older ARM syntax)
> ; ... function body ...
> LDMFD  SP!, {R4-R7, PC}   ; POP and return (older ARM syntax)
> ```
> The modern `PUSH`/`POP` mnemonics are aliases of `STMFD`/`LDMFD` in **Unified Assembly Language (UAL)**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| **PUSH – SP update** | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4n$ | $n$ = number of registers pushed |
| **POP – SP update** | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 4n$ | $n$ = number of registers popped |
| **Bytes moved** | $4n$ bytes | Always a multiple of 4 |
| **PUSH min-reg address** | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}}$ | Lowest-numbered register |
| **PUSH max-reg address** | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} + 4(n-1)$ | Highest-numbered register |
| **Stack growth direction** | Descending (toward lower addresses) | ARM default |
| **SP contents during PUSH** | Contains the last value pushed | "Full" stack |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stack instructions are the **backbone of function calls, interrupts, and context switches** in every ARM-based system — from **mobile phones (ARM Cortex-A series)** to **microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M series)**. In a **Cortex-M** processor, the hardware automatically pushes **R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, and xPSR** onto the stack on interrupt entry (a process called *stacking*), and pops them on return (*unstacking*). This makes the stack the most performance-critical region of RAM in any embedded system.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: PUSH and POP Trace

**Problem:** Initially `SP = 0x0000_8008`. Execute the following sequence and state the final SP and the memory contents.

```assembly
PUSH  {R0, R1, R2}      ; Assume R0=0xAA, R1=0xBB, R2=0xCC
; ... some code ...
POP   {R3, R4, R5}
```

#### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the number of registers in the PUSH list.**
$$
n_{\text{push}} = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the new SP after PUSH.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4 \cdot n_{\text{push}} \\
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= 0x0000\,8008 - 4 \times 3 \\
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= 0x0000\,8008 - 0xC \\
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= 0x0000\,7FFC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the destination addresses for each register (Full Descending).**

The lowest-numbered register goes to the *lowest* address, i.e., the final SP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addr}(R_0) &= 0x0000\,7FFC + 0 = 0x0000\,7FFC \\
\text{Addr}(R_1) &= 0x0000\,7FFC + 4 = 0x0000\,8000 \\
\text{Addr}(R_2) &= 0x0000\,7FFC + 8 = 0x0000\,8004
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Memory state immediately after PUSH.**

| Address (Hex) | Contents (Hex) | Source |
|---------------|----------------|--------|
| `0x0000 7FFC` | `0x0000 00AA` | R0 |
| `0x0000 8000` | `0x0000 00BB` | R1 |
| `0x0000 8004` | `0x0000 00CC` | R2 |
| `0x0000 8008` | *(unchanged)* | (free, above stack) |

After PUSH: `SP = 0x0000 7FFC`.

**Step 5 — Execute the POP with `n_pop = 3`.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= 0x0000\,7FFC + 4 \times 3 \\
&= 0x0000\,7FFC + 0xC \\
&= 0x0000\,8008
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Address-to-register mapping for POP.**

The lowest-numbered destination register gets the *lowest* address.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3 &\leftarrow M[0x0000\,7FFC] = 0x0000\,00AA \\
R_4 &\leftarrow M[0x0000\,8000] = 0x0000\,00BB \\
R_5 &\leftarrow M[0x0000\,8004] = 0x0000\,00CC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final State after POP:** `R3 = 0xAA`, `R4 = 0xBB`, `R5 = 0xCC`, `SP = 0x0000 8008`. The stack is fully restored to its pre-PUSH state.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: PUSH/POP with LR/PC (Subroutine Return)

**Problem:** Analyze the following subroutine call sequence and show the link-register preservation and return.

```assembly
BL    MySub             ; R14 = PC+4 at this point
; ... continues here on return ...

MySub
PUSH  {R0, LR}          ; save R0 and return address
; ... uses R0 ...
POP   {R0, PC}          ; restore R0 and return to caller
```

#### Solution

**Step 1 — When `BL MySub` executes:**
- `LR ← PC` (i.e., the address of the instruction *after* `BL MySub`).
- `PC ← address of MySub`.

**Step 2 — When `PUSH {R0, LR}` executes (n = 2):**
$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 8
$$

| Address | Stored value |
|---------|--------------|
| `SP_new` | R0 |
| `SP_new + 4` | LR (return address) |

**Step 3 — When `POP {R0, PC}` executes:**
- R0 is restored from `SP_old`.
- PC is restored from `SP_old + 4` (the saved LR value), causing a branch back to the caller.
- The instruction after `BL` resumes execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why POP to PC, not LR?** If we POP into LR, we would still need a separate `MOV PC, LR` to actually return. Loading directly into PC fuses the two steps and makes the return **atomic and uninterruptible** between restore and branch.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Summary Table for Common Stack Operations

| Instruction | SP change (n regs) | Reg with lowest addr | Reg with highest addr | Direction |
|-------------|--------------------|-----------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| `PUSH {R0–R3}` | $-16$ | R0 | R3 | Memory writes go **down** |
| `PUSH {R4, LR}` | $-8$ | R4 | LR (R14) | Memory writes go **down** |
| `POP {R0–R3}` | $+16$ | R0 | R3 | Memory reads go **up** |
| `POP {R4, PC}` | $+8$ | R4 | PC (R15) | Memory reads go **up** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PUSH Operation

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **internal data-path interactions** during a `PUSH` instruction (conceptual rather than physical layout, due to Mermaid's rendering limits).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Instruction Decoder\nDetects PUSH opcode"] --> B["Register List Encoder\nCalculates n = number of regs"]
    B --> C["Address Generator Unit\nSP_new = SP_old - 4n"]
    C --> D["Address Bus\nMUX routes SP_new to A-Bus"]
    D --> E["Memory Write Enable asserted"]
    E --> F["Data Bus: Read Reg[min]"]
    F --> G["Memory writes Reg[min] at SP_new"]
    G --> H{"All registers\nwritten?"}
    H -- No --> I["Increment address by 4\nFetch next register"]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J["Update SP register\nSP = SP_new"]
    J --> K["Instruction complete\nFetch next opcode"]
```

### 4.2 Stack Memory Map (Before and After PUSH/POP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["Memory BEFORE PUSH (SP = 0x8008)"]
        B1["0x7FFC\n(EMPTY)"]:::empty
        B2["0x8000\n(EMPTY)"]:::empty
        B3["0x8004\n(EMPTY)"]:::empty
        B4["0x8008\n(EMPTY)"]:::empty
        SPb["SP = 0x8008"]:::ptr
    end

    subgraph AFTERPUSH["Memory AFTER PUSH R0,R1,R2 (SP = 0x7FFC)"]
        A1["0x7FFC\nR0 = 0xAA"]:::filled
        A2["0x8000\nR1 = 0xBB"]:::filled
        A3["0x8004\nR2 = 0xCC"]:::filled
        A4["0x8008\n(EMPTY)"]:::empty
        SPp["SP = 0x7FFC"]:::ptr
    end

    subgraph AFTERPOP["Memory AFTER POP R3,R4,R5 (SP = 0x8008)"]
        C1["0x7FFC\n(stale data)"]:::stale
        C2["0x8000\n(stale data)"]:::stale
        C3["0x8004\n(stale data)"]:::stale
        C4["0x8008\n(EMPTY)"]:::empty
        SPq["SP = 0x8008"]:::ptr
    end

    BEFORE --> AFTERPUSH
    AFTERPUSH --> AFTERPOP

    classDef empty fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#666,stroke-dasharray: 4 4,color:#000
    classDef filled fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef stale fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef ptr fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Control-Flow Diagram: PUSH / POP with Subroutine Call

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Main Program\nExecuting..."] --> BL["BL MySub\nLR = return_addr\nPC = MySub"]
    BL --> PUSH["PUSH {R0, LR}\nSP = SP - 8\nSave R0 and return addr"]
    PUSH --> BODY["Execute function body\n(may modify R0, R1, ...)"]
    BODY --> POP["POP {R0, PC}\nSP = SP + 8\nRestore R0 and return"]
    POP --> RET["PC = return_addr\n(Main program continues)"]
    RET --> M

    style BL fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style PUSH fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
    style POP fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style RET fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define a stack in ARM. List the different types of stack models and specify which one is used by ARM by default.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure in memory used to temporarily store registers and the return address, managed by the **Stack Pointer (R13 / SP)**.
- **(1 mark)** The four stack models are: *Full Ascending (FA)*, *Full Descending (FD)*, *Empty Ascending (EA)*, *Empty Descending (ED)*.
- **(1 mark)** ARM uses the **Full Descending (FD)** stack model by default — the SP points to the last item pushed, and the stack grows toward **lower memory addresses**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *What is the role of R13 and R14 registers in ARM? Why is it recommended to save R14 (LR) on the stack during nested function calls?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **R13 (SP)** holds the address of the top of the stack. **R14 (LR)** holds the return address when a subroutine is called using the `BL` or `BLX` instruction.
- **(2 marks)** In **nested function calls**, the inner function's `BL` will *overwrite* the LR containing the outer's return address. Hence, the outer function must `PUSH {…, LR}` to the stack and later `POP {…, PC}` to safely return. Without saving LR, the program will return to the **wrong address** when the inner function completes.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the PUSH and POP instructions of ARM with their syntax and operational procedure. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Given `SP = 0x4000_1000` and `R0 = 0x1111_1111`, `R1 = 0x2222_2222`, `R2 = 0x3333_3333`, `R3 = 0x4444_4444`, show the memory state after executing `PUSH {R0–R3}` and the final value of `SP`. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — PUSH and POP Explanation *(7 marks)*

**Syntax:**
```
PUSH  {<reg_list>}
POP   {<reg_list>}
```
where `<reg_list>` is a comma-separated list of registers, possibly with ranges (e.g., `{R0, R2-R4, LR}`).

**PUSH Operational Procedure:**
1. **(1 mark)** The number of registers `n` in the register list is counted.
2. **(1 mark)** The SP is **decremented by 4n** to make room: `SP_new = SP_old − 4n`.
3. **(1 mark)** Each register is **stored** in memory starting at `SP_new`. The **lowest-numbered register** is stored at `SP_new`, the **highest-numbered** at `SP_new + 4(n−1)`.
4. **(1 mark)** After execution, SP contains the address of the **last item pushed** (Full Descending behaviour).

**POP Operational Procedure:**
1. **(1 mark)** The number of registers `n` is counted.
2. **(1 mark)** Registers are **loaded** from memory starting at the current SP. Lowest-numbered register is loaded from `SP`, highest from `SP + 4(n−1)`.
3. **(1 mark)** The SP is **incremented by 4n**: `SP_new = SP_old + 4n`. Special case: writing to PC performs a **branch** and is used for subroutine returns.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Trace *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Number of registers** *(1 mark)*:
$$
n = 4 \text{ (R0, R1, R2, R3)}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate new SP** *(2 marks)*:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= 0x4000\,1000 - 4 \times 4 \\
&= 0x4000\,1000 - 16 \\
&= 0x4000\,1000 - 0x10 \\
&= 0x4000\,0FF0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Memory address map** *(2 marks)*:

| Address (Hex) | Register | Value (Hex) |
|---------------|----------|--------------|
| `0x4000 0FF0` | R0 | `0x1111 1111` |
| `0x4000 0FF4` | R1 | `0x2222 2222` |
| `0x4000 0FF8` | R2 | `0x3333 3333` |
| `0x4000 0FFC` | R3 | `0x4444 4444` |

**Step 4 — Final value of SP** *(2 marks)*:
$$
\boxed{\text{SP}_{\text{final}} = 0x4000\,0FF0}
$$

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of a stack in ARM. Differentiate between Full Descending and Empty Ascending stacks. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Write an ARM assembly subroutine to swap two numbers using PUSH and POP. Trace the stack for one sample input. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Stack Concept & Comparison *(7 marks)*

**Stack Concept** *(4 marks)*:
- A stack is a LIFO structure in RAM, addressed by **R13 (SP)**.
- ARM uses **PUSH** (decrement-then-store) and **POP** (load-then-increment).
- The diagram below shows the downward growth:
```
   High Address  (Stack base / initial SP)
        |       ↓ SP moves down on PUSH
        |
        |  R0   ← SP after PUSH {R0,R1}
        |  R1
   Low Address   (Stack limit)
```

**Full Descending vs. Empty Ascending** *(3 marks)*:

| Feature | Full Descending (ARM default) | Empty Ascending |
|---------|-------------------------------|-----------------|
