\overline{\text{mask}} &= \text{0xFFFFFF7F} \\
\text{R1} &= \text{0x00000080} \;\land\; \text{0xFFFFFF7F} = \text{0x00000000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**R1 = 0x00000000**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 2 Revision — Bit Field Manipulation Instructions**

### 1. Core Instructions You Must Memorize

- [x] `AND Rd, Rn, Op2` — bitwise AND (masking, bit-testing with `ANDS`)
- [x] `ORR Rd, Rn, Op2` — bitwise OR (setting bits to 1)
- [x] `EOR Rd, Rn, Op2` — bitwise XOR (toggling bits)
- [x] `BIC Rd, Rn, Op2` — bit clear (= `Rn AND NOT Op2`)
- [x] `BFI Rd, Rn, #lsb, #width` — bit field insert
- [x] `UBFX Rd, Rn, #lsb, #width` — unsigned extract + zero-extend
- [x] `SBFX Rd, Rn, #lsb, #width` — signed extract + sign-extend
- [x] `CLZ Rd, Rm` — count leading zeros (range 0–32; `CLZ(0) = 32`)
- [x] `RBIT Rd, Rm` — reverse 32 bits

### 2. Key Constraints (Exam-Traps)

- [x] `lsb + width ≤ 32` for BFI/UBFX/SBFX
- [x] `1 ≤ width ≤ 32`
- [x] `0 ≤ lsb ≤ 31`
- [x] `CLZ` result is always in `[0, 32]`, inclusive

### 3. Mnemonics for Bit Operations

- [x] **O**RR = **O**n (set to 1)
- [x] **BIC** = **B**it **C**lear (set to 0)
- [x] **E**OR = **E**xclusive flip (toggle)
- [x] **AND** = **A**ll bits where mask is 1 pass through
- [x] **B**FI = **B**its **F**rom source, **I**nsert
- [x] **U**BFX = **U**nsigned (zero-fill)
- [x] **S**BFX = **S**igned (sign-extend)
- [x] **C**LZ = **C**ount **L**eading **Z**eros
- [x] **R**BIT = **R**everse **BIT**s

### 4. Engineering Applications to Quote in Answers

- [x] GPIO set/clear via bit-band aliasing in **Cortex-M3/M4**
- [x] FFT bit-reversal using `RBIT` (DSP context)
- [x] ADC result extraction using `UBFX` (LPC1768 ADC)
- [x] Timer compare field update using `BFI`
- [x] NVIC priority field reading using `UBFX` on `IPR` registers
- [x] Software division by power of 2: $x / 2^n$ = `(x >> n)` or using `CLZ` for normalization

### 5. Cycle Count Reminder

- [x] All bit-field instructions on **Cortex-M4** execute in **1 clock cycle** (single-cycle ALU).
- [x] This makes them vastly superior to multi-cycle read-modify-write sequences in C.

### 6. KTU-Style One-Liners to Remember

- [x] `BIC` + `ORR` ≡ atomic bit manipulation in one cycle each.
- [x] `UBFX` for unsigned, `SBFX` for 2's-complement signed values.
- [x] `BFI` only operates on the **low bits** of the source register, not at position `lsb`.
- [x] `CLZ` is the hardware-accelerated alternative to a software loop scanning for MSB.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Bit Field Manipulation Instructions**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Structures in Assembly Language: Instruction Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Control Structures in Assembly Language: Instruction Sequencing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Control Structures in Assembly Language** refer to the set of instructions and mechanisms that govern the **order of execution** of program statements, dictating how the **Program Counter (PC / R15)** flows through the instruction memory. In ARM architecture, control structures are built using **branch instructions**, **conditional execution suffixes**, **comparison primitives**, and **status flag manipulation** through the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)**. **Instruction Sequencing** is the process by which the processor fetches, decodes, and executes instructions either in the default linear (sequential) order or by altering the PC value through a branch, thereby creating loops, conditionals, and subroutine calls. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > Control Structures form the **backbone of non-trivial ARM programs**. Without control structures, a program can only execute instructions in a straight line — no decisions, no repetition, no modularity. The ARM Cortex design uniquely extends this concept by allowing **almost every instruction to be conditionally executed**, which is a major architectural advantage over CISC processors like x86. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train Track Network" Imagine a **toy train running on tracks**: - The **default track** is a straight, single line — the train moves carriage by carriage in order. This is **sequential execution**. - At certain junctions, there are **switches (points)** that the controller can flip. These switches are your **branch instructions**. - Some switches flip **unconditionally** (you push a button, the train goes left) — these are **unconditional branches (`B label`)**. - Some switches only flip **if a signal light is green/red** — these are **conditional branches (`BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`, etc.)**. - The **signal lights** themselves are powered by sensors checking the **CPSR flags** (N, Z, C, V) — the result of the last comparison. - A **loop** is simply a track that loops back on itself, with a switch that eventually diverts the train out of the loop. - A **subroutine call (`BL`)** is like telling the train to take a side track, do some work, and then automatically return to the main track — the return address is stored in **Link Register (LR / R14)**. ### 1.3 Why Control Structures Matter in ARM | Mechanism | C Equivalent | ARM Mnemonic | |---|---|---| | Sequential flow | `;` (next statement) | Default PC increment | | If-else | `if (cond) ... else ...` | `CMP` + `BEQ`/`BNE` + conditional suffixes | | Loop | `for / while / do-while` | `B` (branch backward) + termination `BEQ` | | Function call | `function()` | `BL func` | | Function return | `return` | `BX LR` or `MOV PC, LR` | | Switch/case | `switch(x)` | Table-driven branch or chained CMP/BEQ | > [!NOTE] > ARM's key differentiator: **Conditional execution suffix** (e.g., `ADDNE R0, R1, R2`) eliminates many short branches, reducing pipeline flushes and improving performance. This is a frequent **KTU 2-mark question**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Default Flow — Sequential Execution By default, ARM is a **sequential** machine. After fetching an instruction from address `A`, the PC is auto-incremented by **4 bytes** (since ARM is a 32-bit, fixed-width RISC ISA) and the next instruction is fetched from `A + 4`. $$ \text{PC}_{\text{next(sequential)}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + 4 $$ This behavior is **hardwired** in the ARM fetch stage — no instruction needs to explicitly issue it. ### 2.2 Breaking the Sequence — Branch Instructions A **branch instruction** overwrites the PC with a target address, breaking the default sequential flow. #### 2.2.1 Branch (B) - **Mnemonic:** `B {cond} label` - **Encoding:** 24-bit signed word offset, shifted left by 2, then sign-extended to 32 bits. - **Range:** ±32 MB from the branch instruction. - **Effect:** `PC ← PC + 8 + (signed_offset << 2)` - The `+8` accounts for the fact that, at execution time, the PC has already been incremented twice (fetch + decode stages) past the branch itself. #### 2.2.2 Branch with Link (BL) - **Mnemonic:** `BL {cond} label` - **Effect:** `LR ← PC + 4` (saves return address), then `PC ← target`. - **Used for:** Subroutine calls. The return is typically `MOV PC, LR` or, in Thumb code, `BX LR`. #### 2.2.3 Branch and Exchange (BX) / Branch with Link and Exchange (BLX) - **Effect:** `PC ← Rn` (lowest bit selects ARM/Thumb state), and optionally saves LR. - **Use case:** Interworking between ARM and Thumb instruction sets. ### 2.3 The CPSR — Heart of Conditional Execution The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** holds four **condition code flags** that are set by nearly every arithmetic, logical, compare, or move instruction: | Flag | Full Name | Set When | |---|---|---| | **N** | Negative | Result bit 31 is **1** (MSB of result). | | **Z** | Zero | Result is **exactly 0**. | | **C** | Carry | For addition: unsigned overflow out of bit 31. For subtraction: **NOT** borrow occurred. For shifts: last bit shifted out. | | **V** | Overflow | Signed overflow occurred (sign bit corrupted). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** For an **unsigned subtraction `A − B`**, the Carry flag is set (C = 1) when `A ≥ B` (no borrow needed) and cleared (C = 0) when `A < B` (borrow occurred). This is the **opposite** of the intuitive "borrow" flag and is a common trap. ### 2.4 The 16 ARM Condition Codes (Condition Suffixes) Every ARM instruction (except a few like `BKP`/`SEV`) can be **conditionally executed** by appending a 2-letter suffix. The top 4 bits of the 32-bit instruction word encode this condition. | Suffix | Flags | Meaning | English Test | |---|---|---|---| | `EQ` | Z=1 | Equal | result == 0 | | `NE` | Z=0 | Not equal | result != 0 | | `CS`/`HS` | C=1 | Carry set / unsigned ≥ | ≥ (unsigned) | | `CC`/`LO` | C=0 | Carry clear / unsigned < | < (unsigned) | | `MI` | N=1 | Minus (negative) | result < 0 | | `PL` | N=0 | Plus (positive or zero) | result ≥ 0 | | `VS` | V=1 | Overflow set | signed overflow | | `VC` | V=0 | Overflow clear | no signed overflow | | `HI` | C=1 & Z=0 | Unsigned higher | > (unsigned) | | `LS` | C=0 or Z=1 | Unsigned lower or same | ≤ (unsigned) | | `GE` | N==V | Signed ≥ | ≥ (signed) | | `LT` | N!=V | Signed < | < (signed) | | `GT` | Z=0 & N==V | Signed > | > (signed) | | `LE` | Z=1 or N!=V | Signed ≤ | ≤ (signed) | | `AL` | (any) | Always (default) | unconditional | | `NV` | (none) | Never (deprecated) | — | ### 2.5 Comparison Instructions (Set Flags Only) These instructions **do not write a result**; they only update the CPSR flags. | Mnemonic | Operation | Flags Set | C Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | `CMP` | `Rn − Operand2` (discard result) | N, Z, C, V | C=1 if `Rn ≥ Operand2` (unsigned) | | `CMN` | `Rn + Operand2` (discard result) | N, Z, C, V | C=1 if unsigned overflow | | `TST` | `Rn AND Operand2` (discard) | N, Z, C | Tests bits / mask | | `TEQ` | `Rn EOR Operand2` (discard) | N, Z, C | Tests equality of two operands | ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---| | Sequential PC update | $\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + 4$ | 4 bytes = 32-bit instruction | | Branch target | $\text{PC} = \text{PC}_{\text{execute}} + 8 + (\text{offset}_{24} \ll 2)$ | `+8` because of pipeline | | Branch range | $\pm 32$ **MiB** | Offset is signed 26-bit byte address | | Link Register save | $\text{LR} = \text{PC}_{\text{BL}} + 4$ | Holds return address | | CPSR Negative flag | $N = \text{bit}_{31}(\text{result})$ | Sign bit | | CPSR Zero flag | $Z = 1 \iff \text{result} == 0$ | All bits zero | | CPSR Carry (add) | $C = 1 \iff \text{carry out of bit}_{31}$ | Unsigned overflow | | CPSR Carry (sub) | $C = 1 \iff \text{no borrow}$ | $\text{Rn} \ge \text{Operand2}$ (unsigned) | | CPSR Overflow | $V = 1 \iff \text{signed overflow}$ | Operands of same sign, result of opposite sign | | Signed ≥ condition | $N \oplus V = 0$ | `GE` uses N == V | | Signed < condition | $N \oplus V = 1$ | `LT` uses N != V | > [!NOTE] > All boundary values in the table are **hard architectural limits** of the 32-bit ARM instruction encoding and the 32-bit CPSR layout. The `±32 MiB` range is fixed; longer branches require literal pools and LDR PC. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded firmware (washing machines, automotive ECUs):** state machines implemented using CMP/BEQ chains. - **Real-time operating systems (RTOS):** context switches rely on `BL` for system calls and `BX LR` for returns. - **DSP / signal processing pipelines:** tight inner loops use conditional suffixes to avoid branch mispredictions. - **IoT / Cortex-M0 microcontrollers:** Thumb-2 branch instructions form the bulk of the instruction mix. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Branch Offset Encoding **Problem:** Given an ARM branch instruction located at address `0x00008000` that needs to jump forward to `0x00008040`, what is the 24-bit signed offset that the assembler will encode? **Step 1 — Identify the assembly.** The instruction is at `0x00008000` and the target is `0x00008040`. **Step 2 — Calculate the byte displacement.** The branch uses a *word* offset (4-byte aligned). Therefore: $$ \Delta_{\text{bytes}} = \text{Target} - (\text{PC}_{\text{fetch}} + 8) $$ The `+8` accounts for the fact that, by the time the branch reaches the **execute stage**, the PC has been advanced twice (fetch and decode) past the branch itself. $$ \text{Target} - (\text{PC}_{\text{fetch}} + 8) = 0x00008040 - (0x00008000 + 8) $$ $$ = 0x00008040 - 0x00008008 $$ $$ = 0x00000038 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to a word offset** (divide by 4 because the low 2 bits are implicit, since ARM instructions are 4-byte aligned). $$ \Delta_{\text{words}} = \frac{0x38}{4} = 0x0E $$ **Step 4 — Verify the range.** `0x0E` is positive and within `±2^{23}` (≈ ±8 million words, i.e., ±32 MiB). **Encoding valid.** **Step 5 — Final encoded form (sign-extended to 32 bits).** $$ \text{Offset}_{24} = \text{0x00000E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Encoded bits} = 0\text{x}00000\text{E} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation of CPSR Flags After a CMP **Problem:** After executing `CMP R0, R1` where `R0 = 0x00000005` and `R1 = 0x00000007`, determine the values of N, Z, C, V. **Step 1 — The CMP internally performs `R0 − R1` and discards the result.** $$ 5 - 7 = -2 $$ In 32-bit two's complement, `-2` is `0xFFFFFFFE`. **Step 2 — Compute each flag.** **Negative (N):** N = bit 31 of the result. $$ \text{Result} = 0\text{xFFFFFFFE} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{bit}_{31} = 1 $$ $$ \therefore N = 1 $$ **Zero (Z):** Z = 1 if and only if result is zero. Since `0xFFFFFFFE ≠ 0`: $$ \therefore Z = 0 $$ **Carry (C) for subtraction:** C = 1 if **no borrow** was needed, i.e., if `R0 ≥ R1` (unsigned). Here `5 < 7`, so a borrow **is** needed. $$ \therefore C = 0 $$ **Overflow (V):** V = 1 if signed overflow occurred. Subtraction of two positive numbers (`+5` and `+7`) cannot overflow; the result `−2` is correctly representable. $$ \therefore V = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Final CPSR flag set.** $$ (N, Z, C, V) = (1, 0, 0, 0) $$ **Step 4 — Consequence for branching:** Since `N=1` and `V=0`, we have `N != V` ⇒ **`LT` (signed less-than) condition is TRUE**. So `BLT somewhere` would branch, while `BGE somewhere` would not. This is the foundation of all signed comparisons. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Control Structure Patterns | High-Level Construct | ARM Assembly Pattern | Flag Dependence | |---|---|---| | `if (a == b) X` | `CMP a, b`
`BEQ X` | Z = 1 | | `if (a != b) X` | `CMP a, b`
`BNE X` | Z = 0 | | `if (a < b) X` (signed) | `CMP a, b`
`BLT X` | N ≠ V | | `if (a > b) X` (signed) | `CMP a, b`
`BGT X` | Z = 0 AND N = V | | `if (a < b) X` (unsigned) | `CMP a, b`
`BCC X` (or `BLO`) | C = 0 | | `if (a > b) X` (unsigned) | `CMP a, b`
`BHI X` | C = 1 AND Z = 0 | | `for(i=0;i `loop: ... `
`ADD i, i, #1`
`CMP i, n`
`BLT loop` | N ≠ V | | `while(*p)` | `loop: LDRB r0, [p]`
`CMP r0, #0`
`BNE body` | Z = 0 | | Function call | `BL myFunc`
`...`
`myFunc: ...`
`BX LR` | (LR saved) | | Conditional add (no branch) | `CMP r0, #0`
`ADDNE r1, r1, r2` | Z = 0 | ### 3.4 Worked Example — Factorial Using Control Structures **Problem:** Compute `n!` for `n ≥ 0` stored in `R0`, place result in `R0`. Assume `R1` is free. **ARM Assembly:** ``` MOV R1, R0 ; R1 = n (preserve n for loop bound) MOV R0, #1 ; result = 1 CMP R1, #1 ; if n < 1 BLT done ; skip (handles n=0 case) loop: MUL R0, R0, R1 ; result *= n SUBS R1, R1, #1 ; n--, sets flags BNE loop ; continue if n != 0 done: ; R0 = n! ``` **Step-by-step trace for n = 4:** | Iteration | R1 (before MUL) | R0 (after MUL) | R1 (after SUBS) | Z flag | BNE taken? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 4 | 1×4 = 4 | 3 | 0 | Yes | | 2 | 3 | 4×3 = 12 | 2 | 0 | Yes | | 3 | 2 | 12×2 = 24 | 1 | 0 | Yes | | 4 | 1 | 24×1 = 24 | 0 | 1 | No → exit | **Final result in R0 = 24 = 4!** ✔ **Notice the use of:** 1. `BLT done` — signed less-than branch for the guard condition. 2. `SUBS R1, R1, #1` — the `S` suffix forces the flags to be updated, eliminating the need for a separate `CMP`. 3. `BNE loop` — branch if not-equal (Z = 0), the natural loop-back condition. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CPSR Register Bit Layout The CPSR is a 32-bit register; only certain fields are architecturally accessible to user-mode code. ```mermaid graph LR CPSR["CPSR 32-bit Register"] N["bit 31: N - Negative Flag"] Z["bit 30: Z - Zero Flag"] C["bit 29: C - Carry Flag"] V["bit 28: V - Overflow Flag"] IT["bits 15-10: IT / If-Then block state"] J["bit 24: J - Jazelle state"] T["bit 5: T - Thumb state"] M["bits 4-0: M - Processor Mode"] F["bit 6: F - FIQ disable"] I["bit 7: I - IRQ disable"] A["bit 8: A - Async abort disable"] GE["bits 19-16: GE - SIMD flags"] CPSR --> N CPSR --> Z CPSR --> C CPSR --> V CPSR --> IT CPSR --> J CPSR --> T CPSR --> M CPSR --> F CPSR --> I CPSR --> A CPSR --> GE ``` ### 4.2 Branch Decision Flow (Functional Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Instruction Fetch Stage"] --> B["PC updated to PC + 4"] B --> C{"Instruction Type?"} C -- "ALU / Data Proc" --> D["Execute: result computed, flags optional"] C -- "CMP / TST" --> E["Execute: flags updated, no result written"] C -- "Branch B / BL" --> F["Execute: target = PC + 8 + offset"] D --> G["Write-back to register file"] E --> H["Update CPSR N Z C V"] F --> I["LR updated if BL"] I --> J["PC overwritten with target"] G --> K["Next instruction: PC + 4"] H --> K J --> K ``` ### 4.3 Conditional Branch Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["CMP or data op with S suffix"] --> Flags["Update N, Z, C, V in CPSR"] Flags --> Branch{"Branch with condition code?"} Branch -- "BEQ" --> EqChk{"Z = 1?"} Branch -- "BNE" --> NeChk{"Z = 0?"} Branch -- "BGT" --> GtChk{"Z = 0 AND N = V?"} Branch -- "BLT" --> LtChk{"N != V?"} Branch -- "BHI" --> HiChk{"C = 1 AND Z = 0?"} Branch -- "BCC" --> CcChk{"C = 0?"} EqChk -- "Yes" --> Taken1["PC <- Target"] EqChk -- "No" --> Fall1["PC <- PC + 4"] NeChk -- "Yes" --> Taken2["PC <- Target"] NeChk -- "No" --> Fall2["PC <- PC + 4"] GtChk -- "Yes" --> Taken3["PC <- Target"] GtChk -- "No" --> Fall3["PC <- PC + 4"] LtChk -- "Yes" --> Taken4["PC <- Target"] LtChk -- "No" --> Fall4["PC <- PC + 4"] HiChk -- "Yes" --> Taken5["PC <- Target"] HiChk -- "No" --> Fall5["PC <- PC + 4"] CcChk -- "Yes" --> Taken6["PC <- Target"] CcChk -- "No" --> Fall6["PC <- PC + 4"] ``` ### 4.4 Program Flow — Sequential vs Branched (Block View) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph DefaultFlow["Default Sequential Flow (PC + 4)"] S1["Instr 1
addr: 0x00"] -- "PC+4" --> S2["Instr 2
addr: 0x04"] S2 -- "PC+4" --> S3["Instr 3
addr: 0x08"] S3 -- "PC+4" --> S4["Instr 4
addr: 0x0C"] end subgraph BranchedFlow["Branched Flow (B target)"] B1["Instr A
addr: 0x10"] -- "PC+4" --> B2["Instr B
addr: 0x14"] B2 -- "PC+4" --> B3["B target
addr: 0x18"] B3 -- "overwrite PC" --> B4["Target Instr
addr: 0x40"] B4 -- "PC+4" --> B5["Next after target
addr: 0x44"] end ``` ### 4.5 Loop Construct (Typical for / while pattern) ```mermaid flowchart TD Init["Initialize counter R1 = N"] --> Cond{"Test condition
CMP R1, #0
BGT body?"} Cond -- "Yes (N>0)" --> Body["Loop body
(MUL, ADD, etc.)"] Body --> Update["Update counter
SUBS R1, R1, #1"] Update --> Cond Cond -- "No (N<=0)" --> Exit["Exit loop
Continue sequential flow"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The **functional architecture** diagrams above intentionally abstract away the **3-stage / 5-stage pipeline** details of fetch-decode-execute-memory-writeback. KTU 2024 scheme focuses on the *control flow* semantics, not micro-architectural hazards. However, in **Cortex-M3/M4** modules, branch prediction becomes a topic of discussion. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **Explain the role of the CPSR flags (N, Z, C, V) in determining the outcome of conditional branch instructions in ARM. How does the `BGT` (Branch if Greater Than, signed) condition check the flags?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** holds four condition flags — **N (Negative)**, **Z (Zero)**, **C (Carry)**, and **V (Overflow)** — that are set as a side effect of arithmetic, logical, and comparison instructions. Every conditional branch instruction inspects a specific combination of these flags to decide whether the branch target is taken or whether execution continues sequentially (PC + 4). For the **`BGT` (signed greater than)** condition, the test is: $$ \text{Take branch} \iff (Z = 0) \text{ AND } (N = V) $$ - **`Z = 0`** ensures the operands were **not equal**. - **`N = V`** ensures no signed overflow corrupted the sign relationship, so the result sign is a true indicator of magnitude. - If both hold, then `Rn` is strictly greater than `Operand2` in **signed** interpretation, and the branch is taken. Otherwise, PC falls through to the next instruction. ✔ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Repeated Pattern]** **Differentiate between `B` and `BL` instructions in ARM. Why is `BL` followed by `MOV PC, LR` (or `BX LR`) in a subroutine?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `B label` | `BL label` | |---|---|---| | Full name | Branch | Branch with **Link** | | PC update | `PC ← target` | `PC ← target` | | LR update | **No** | **Yes** — `LR ← PC + 4` (return address) | | Use case | Unconditional jump (loops, if-then) | **Subroutine / function call** | | Return mechanism | Manual (programmer must track) | Automatic — just `MOV PC, LR` or `BX LR` | The `BL` instruction saves the **return address** (PC + 4, accounting for the pipeline) in the **Link Register (R14)** before transferring control to the subroutine. At the end of the subroutine, executing `MOV PC, LR` (ARM mode) or `BX LR` (Thumb-aware) restores the saved return address into the PC, resuming execution at the instruction **immediately after the `BL` call**. Without saving the return address, the program would have no way to resume the caller's flow. ✔ --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **four CPSR condition flags** (N, Z, C, V). For each flag, describe **how it is set** during an arithmetic operation and **what condition code suffix** would use it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an ARM assembly program to **find the largest number in an array of N unsigned 32-bit integers** stored in memory starting at label `ARRAY`, with the count `N` in `R1`. Use a loop with `B`-type branches. Explain every line. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** The four CPSR flags are status bits updated by data-processing and comparison instructions when the **`S` bit** in the instruction is set, or automatically by `CMP`/`TST`/`TEQ`/`CMN`. **1. N — Negative Flag (bit 31 of CPSR)** - **Set when:** bit 31 of the result is `1`, i.e., the result is negative in two's-complement signed interpretation. - **Used by:** `MI` (Minus, N=1) and `PL` (Plus, N=0). Also indirectly by `GE`, `LT`, `GT`, `LE` for signed tests. **2. Z — Zero Flag (bit 30 of CPSR)** - **Set when:** the result is exactly `0x00000000`. - **Used by:** `EQ` (Z=1) and `NE` (Z=0). It is the most commonly tested flag, dominating equality and loop-termination tests. **3. C — Carry Flag (bit 29 of CPSR)** - **For addition:** C = 1 if there is an unsigned carry out of bit 31. - **For subtraction:** C = 1 if **no borrow** was needed (i.e., `Rn ≥ Operand2` unsigned). C = 0 if a borrow occurred. - **For shifts:** C = last bit shifted out of the destination register. - **Used by:** `CS`/`HS` (C=1), `CC`/`LO` (C=0), `HI` (C=1 AND Z=0), `LS` (C=0 OR Z=1). **4. V — Overflow Flag (bit 28 of CPSR)** - **Set when:** signed overflow occurred — i.e., adding two positives gives a negative, or adding two negatives gives a positive. - **Used by:** `VS` (V=1) and `VC` (V=0). Combined with N for signed magnitude tests: `GE` is `(N == V)`, `LT` is `(N != V)`. These flags form the **decision input** for every conditional branch and conditional instruction suffix in the ARM ISA. ✔ --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** ```asm LDR R2, =ARRAY ; R2 = base address of array MOV R0, #0 ; R0 = current maximum (init to 0) MOV R3, #0 ; R3 = index i = 0 loop: CMP R3, R1 ; compare i with N BGE done ; if i >= N, exit loop LDR R4, [R2, R3, LSL #2] ; R4 = ARRAY[i] (word = 4 bytes, so LSL #2) CMP R4, R0 ; compare ARRAY[i] with current max BLS skip ; if ARRAY[i] <= max, skip update MOV R0, R4 ; else update max skip: ADD R3, R3, #1 ; i++ B loop ; unconditional branch back done: ; R0 holds the largest element ``` **Line-by-line explanation:** 1. `LDR R2, =ARRAY` — pseudo-instruction that loads the 32-bit address of the array into `R2` from a literal pool. 2. `MOV R0, #0` — initialize the running maximum to zero (safe for unsigned arrays of non-negative numbers). 3. `MOV R3, #0` — initialize the loop index `i = 0`. 4. `CMP R3, R1` — compare `i` with the array length `N`. 5. `BGE done` — Branch if **signed greater-or-equal** (`i ≥ N`); exits the loop. 6. `LDR R4, [R2, R3, LSL #2]` — load the `i`-th element. The offset `R3 << 2` converts the index into a **byte offset** (each word = 4 bytes). 7. `CMP R4, R0` — compare the new element with the running maximum. 8. `BLS skip` — **Branch if Lower or Same (unsigned)**, i.e., `R4 ≤ R0`; skip the update. 9. `MOV R0, R4` — update the maximum. 10. `ADD R3, R3, #1` — increment the index. 11. `B loop` — unconditional branch to the top of the loop. ✔ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Adapted]** **(a)** Discuss the **advantages of ARM's conditional execution suffix** over traditional branch-based control flow. Provide an example where conditional execution eliminates a branch. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an ARM assembly program to compute the **sum of the first N natural numbers** using a loop. The count `N` is in `R1` and the result should be placed in `R0`. Show a hand-trace for `N = 5`. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Advantages of Conditional Execution Suffix:** 1. **Eliminates short branches.** In conventional CISC processors, every `if` requires a branch instruction. In ARM, the `if` can be folded into the instruction itself: e.g., `ADDNE R0, R1, R2` adds only if the zero flag is clear. 2. **Avoids pipeline flushes.** Branches in a pipelined processor cause a **flush** of the fetched but not-yet-executed instructions, costing 2–3 cycles. A conditionally executed instruction is **always** fetched, decoded, and executed; it is only the *register write-back* that is suppressed if the condition is false. This saves cycles and reduces branch misprediction penalties. 3. **Improves code density.** A sequence that would require a comparison, a branch, an instruction, and a label can be compressed to just two instructions, often fitting in fewer bytes. 4. **Better for DSP / inner loops.** Tight loops with small conditional bodies (e.g., saturating add, clip, or multiply-accumulate with guard) execute with zero branch overhead. **Example — computing `|a − b|`:** ```asm CMP R0, R1 SUBLT R2, R1, R0 ; if R0 < R1, R2 = R1 - R0 SUBGE R2, R0, R1 ; if R0 >= R1, R2 = R0 - R1 ``` Only **one comparison and two conditional SUBs**, with no `B` instruction, no labels, and no pipeline disruption. A traditional implementation would need a `CMP`, a branch, a `SUB`, a label, a second `SUB`, etc. ✔ --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** ```asm MOV R0, #0 ; sum = 0 MOV R2, #1 ; i = 1 loop: CMP R2, R1 ; compare i with N BGT done ; if i > N, exit ADD R0, R0, R2 ; sum += i ADD R2, R2, #1 ; i++ B loop ; repeat done: ; R0 = 1+2+...+N ``` **Hand-trace for N = 5:** | Step | R2 (i) | R1 (N) | CMP result (i−N) | BGT? | R0 (sum) | R2 after ADD | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Initial | 1 | 5 | −4 (N=1, V=0) | No | 1 | 2 | | 2 | 2 | 5 | −3 (N=1, V=0) | No | 1+2 = 3 | 3 | | 3 | 3 | 5 | −2 (N=1, V=0) | No | 3+3 = 6 | 4 | | 4 | 4 | 5 | −1 (N=1, V=0) | No | 6+4 = 10 | 5 | | 5 | 5 | 5 | 0 (Z=1, N=0, V=0) | No (BGT needs Z=0) | 10+5 = 15 | 6 | | 6 | 6 | 5 | +1 (N=0, V=0) | **Yes** | 15 | — exit | **Final R0 = 15 = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5** ✔ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Places Students Lose Marks on Control Structure Questions:** > 1. **Pipeline offset confusion:** Forgetting that branch target is `PC + 8 + offset` (not `PC + 4`). This loses 1–2 marks in offset-calculation problems. > 2. **Subtraction carry flag inverted:** Writing "C = 1 means a borrow occurred" — **WRONG**. C = 1 means **no borrow**. This is the #1 trap in KTU valuation. > 3. **Forgetting the `S` suffix:** Writing `SUB R0, R1, R2` to set flags. The `S` is mandatory — `SUBS` is needed. CMP hides this, but data-processing instructions need explicit `S`. > 4. **Signed vs unsigned mix-up:** Using `BGT` (signed) for unsigned numbers or vice versa. Always check whether the array/operands are declared signed or unsigned. > 5. **Off-by-one in loop bounds:** Using `BGE` when the C-equivalent is `i < N` (should be `BLT`). Trace the boundary case explicitly. > 6. **Missing `BX LR`:** Returning from a Thumb-entered subroutine with `MOV PC, LR` instead of `BX LR` (state mismatch). Costs 1 mark in ARM/Thumb interworking questions. > 7. **Wrong byte offset for arrays:** Using `STR R0, [R1, R2]` instead of `[R1, R2, LSL #2]` for word arrays — this loads the wrong element. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Default flow:** PC auto-increments by **4 bytes** per instruction (sequential execution). - **Branch `B`:** `PC ← PC + 8 + (signed_offset × 4)`. Range: **±32 MiB**. - **Branch with Link `BL`:** Same as `B` but also saves return address in **LR (R14)**: `LR ← PC + 4`. - **Branch and Exchange `BX` / `BLX`:** Switches between ARM and Thumb states based on bit 0 of `Rn`. - **CPSR flags:** **N** (sign), **Z** (zero), **C** (unsigned carry / no-borrow), **V** (signed overflow). - **16 condition codes** are available: `EQ, NE, CS/HS, CC/LO, MI, PL, VS, VC, HI, LS, GE, LT, GT, LE, AL, NV`. - **Signed comparison rule:** `GE` ⇒ `N == V`; `LT` ⇒ `N != V`; `GT` ⇒ `Z = 0 AND N = V`; `LE` ⇒ `Z = 1 OR N != V`. - **Unsigned comparison rule:** `HI` ⇒ `C = 1 AND Z = 0`; `LS` ⇒ `C = 0 OR Z = 1`; `HS/CS` ⇒ `C = 1`; `LO/CC` ⇒ `C = 0`. - **Comparison instructions:** `CMP` (subtract), `CMN` (add), `TST` (AND), `TEQ` (EOR) — all **discard the result** and only set flags. - **Suffix `S` is required** on data-processing instructions to update flags (e.g., `SUBS`, `ADDS`). - **Conditional execution suffix** (e.g., `ADDNE`) executes the instruction only if the condition is true, otherwise it behaves as a **NOP** without flushing the pipeline. - **Loops** are constructed using backward `B` or `BNE` branches combined with a flag-setting instruction (CMP or SUBS). - **Subroutine return** is `MOV PC, LR` (ARM) or `BX LR` (ARM/Thumb interworking). - **Branch range limit** of 32 MiB can be extended using a **literal pool + LDR PC, [PC, #offset]`** for longer jumps. - **KTU favorite traps:** Carry-flag inversion on subtraction; signed vs unsigned condition choice; missing `S` suffix; off-by-one loop boundaries; pipeline offset `+8`.

topics.title: Conditional Branch Instructions
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Conditional Branch Instructions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conditional Branch Instructions** in ARM architecture are a class of branch instructions whose execution is governed by the current state of the **Application Program Status Register (APSR) condition flags**. The CPU evaluates a 4-bit condition code embedded in the instruction; if the condition is **TRUE**, the branch is taken (PC ← target address). If **FALSE**, the instruction is executed as a **NOP (No Operation)**, and the processor continues sequentially. In the **ARMv7-A/R/M** architecture (used in Cortex-A, Cortex-R, and Cortex-M series), every standard branch instruction (`B`, `B{}`) carries an explicit **4-bit condition field** (`cond[31:28]`) in the instruction encoding. There are **16 possible condition codes**, of which **15 are valid** (`cond = 1111` is reserved / UNPREDICTABLE in many ARM cores, though it is used for the "never" condition or the BLX extension on some cores). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) Imagine a **railway track switchman** standing at a railway junction: - The **train** is the processor's instruction pointer (PC). - The **signal lights** (Red, Yellow, Green) represent the **condition flags** in APSR. - The **switchman's rulebook** is the **4-bit condition code** embedded in the branch instruction. - The **junction tracks** are the two possible destinations: the *target* (branch taken) or the *mainline* (sequential execution). > The switchman only flips the track if the *signal light configuration* **matches** the rulebook entry. Otherwise, the train continues straight. This perfectly mirrors how ARM's conditional branch works: the **APSR flags** are the "signal lights", and the **condition code** is the "rulebook". ## 1.3 The APSR Condition Flags The **Application Program Status Register (APSR)** contains four key arithmetic status flags, each occupying one bit: | Flag | Bit | Full Name | Set When... | |:----:|:---:|:----------|:------------| | **N** | 31 | Negative | Result is negative (MSB = 1) in signed interpretation | | **Z** | 30 | Zero | Result is exactly zero | | **C** | 29 | Carry | Unsigned overflow / borrow inverted (carry-out = 1) | | **V** | 28 | Overflow | Signed overflow occurred | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Always remember — **N reflects the MSB of the result**, and **V is computed only for *signed* operations**, while **C is meaningful for *unsigned* operations**. This distinction is heavily tested in KTU questions on comparison instructions. ## 1.4 The General Branch Syntax $$ \texttt{B}\{\text{}\} \;\; \texttt{

topics.title: Translating If-Then and If-Then-Else Statements to Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Translating If-Then and If-Then-Else Statements to Assembly ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the context of the **ARM Cortex architecture** and the **GNU ARM Assembler (GAS)**, the translation of high-level control-flow constructs (specifically `if-then` and `if-then-else` statements) into machine-executable instructions is achieved by mapping the boolean evaluation of relational expressions to the **ARM Application Program Status Register (APSR)** condition flags (N, Z, C, V), which are then tested by **conditional branch instructions** with appropriate condition codes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** *An If-Then statement in a high-level language (HLL) is translated into ARM assembly using a comparison (`CMP` family) instruction followed by a conditional branch (`Bcc`) that skips the body of the **Then** block when the condition evaluates to false. An If-Then-Else statement requires an additional unconditional branch (`B`) to skip the Else block after the Then block executes.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Railway Signal Checkpost" Imagine a single-track railway system. A train (the program counter, `PC`) is moving along the main rail track (sequential execution). The train approaches a **signal checkpost** (a `CMP` instruction) where the signalman inspects two incoming trains side-by-side — *Train A* (left operand) versus *Train B* (right operand). - The signalman raises one of **four colored flags** (the APSR flags: **N**egative, **Z**ero, **C**arry, o**V**erflow) based on the comparison result. - At the next station, a **track switcher** (the conditional branch `Bcc`) reads the active flag color. - If the condition matches, the switcher diverts the train onto a **side track** (the *Then* block). - If the condition does **not** match, the train continues straight onto the **main line** (skipping the *Then* block, or, in the *If-Then-Else* case, diverting onto the *Else* track). > [!NOTE] > The APSR (Application Program Status Register) in ARM Cortex-M/A series is the modern replacement for the older CPSR (Current Program Status Register). KTU 2024 syllabus accepts both nomenclatures. ### 1.3 Scope of Translation in KTU 2024 Module 3 | HLL Construct | ARM Translation Primitives | |---|---| | Relational operator (`==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`) | `CMP` / `CMN` / `TST` instruction | | Boolean test of APSR flags | Conditional branch `Bcc` (e.g., `BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`, `BLT`) | | Skipping a block | Unconditional branch `B label` | | Program labels | Symbolic addresses (e.g., `then_block:`, `else_block:`, `end_if:`) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of an `if-then` Translation The HLL skeleton: ```c if (cond) { // Then-block } ``` is translated using the canonical **Compare-and-Skip pattern** (also called the *Branch-if-False* pattern): 1. **Step 1 — Evaluate the condition:** Issue a `CMP Rn, Rm` (or `CMN`, `TST`) instruction. The ARM datapath's ALU subtracts `Rm` from `Rn` internally, *discards* the result (no destination register), and updates the **N, Z, C, V** flags in APSR. 2. **Step 2 — Invert the high-level condition for the branch:** If the HLL condition is *true* when the flags are in a certain state, we branch **on the inverted condition** to a label placed **after** the Then-block. This causes the CPU to *skip* the Then-block when the HLL condition is false. 3. **Step 3 — Execute the Then-block:** Sequential instructions execute if the branch was not taken. 4. **Step 4 — Place a target label** (`end_if:`) immediately after the Then-block for the branch to land on. > [!NOTE] > **Why the inversion?** ARM provides a *rich* set of condition codes for the *true* case (`BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`, etc.). Rather than test for the *true* case and fall-through to the body (which would require a branch on *false* of an *opposite* condition), it is uniformly cleaner to test for the *false* case and branch *over* the body. This pattern is universally taught and tested in KTU board examinations. ### 2.2 Operational Decomposition of an `if-then-else` Translation The HLL skeleton: ```c if (cond) { // Then-block } else { // Else-block } ``` requires a **two-branch sandwich**: 1. **Step 1 — Compute condition and branch to ELSE on false:** A `CMP` followed by a *false-condition* branch jumps to the `else_block:` label. 2. **Step 2 — Execute the Then-block.** 3. **Step 3 — Branch over the Else-block:** An **unconditional `B end_if:`** after the Then-block prevents fall-through into the Else-block. 4. **Step 4 — `else_block:` label and the Else-block body.** 5. **Step 5 — `end_if:` label** marks the convergence point. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — ARM Condition Codes The **single most important table** for KTU Module 3 board exams. The leftmost column shows the C-style relational operator; the middle column shows the flag bits that the `CMP` instruction sets; the rightmost column shows the corresponding ARM branch mnemonic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Suffix Convention:** The `S` suffix is reserved for signed comparison mnemonics in some assemblers, but in the **standard ARM Unified Assembler Language (UAL)** the base mnemonics are *signed*. The unsigned variants use `BHI` (unsigned higher), `BLS` (unsigned lower-or-same), `BHS`/`BCC` (unsigned higher-or-same), and `BLO`/`BCS` (unsigned lower). | HLL Operator (signed) | APSR Flag Test (after `CMP a, b`) | ARM Branch Mnemonic | Branch Taken When | |---|---|---|---| | `a == b` | `Z == 1` | `BEQ` | Equal | | `a != b` | `Z == 0` | `BNE` | Not equal | | `a > b` | `(Z == 0) AND (N == V)` | `BGT` | Signed greater than | | `a >= b` | `N == V` | `BGE` | Signed greater than or equal | | `a < b` | `N != V` | `BLT` | Signed less than | | `a <= b` | `(Z == 1) OR (N != V)` | `BLE` | Signed less than or equal | | `a > b` (unsigned) | `(C == 1) AND (Z == 0)` | `BHI` | Unsigned higher | | `a >= b` (unsigned) | `C == 1` | `BHS` / `BCC` | Unsigned higher or same | | `a < b` (unsigned) | `C == 0` | `BLO` / `BCS` | Unsigned lower | | `a <= b` (unsigned) | `(C == 0) OR (Z == 1)` | `BLS` | Unsigned lower or same | | Always true | — | `B` (unconditional) | Always taken | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Mastering the translation pattern is the foundation for **compiler code generation** (the backend of GCC's ARM target), **firmware development for bare-metal Cortex-M microcontrollers** (STM32, NXP, Nordic), and **reverse-engineering compiled binaries** in cybersecurity tooling like Ghidra and IDA Pro — all of which are active hiring domains for KTU graduates. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Canonical `if-then` Translation **High-Level C Source:** ```c if (i == j) f = g + h; f = f - i; ``` **Assumed Register Mapping (KTU 2024 Standard):** | Variable | Register | |---|---| | `f` | `R0` | | `g` | `R1` | | `h` | `R2` | | `i` | `R3` | | `j` | `R4` | **Translation Derivation (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1.** The condition `i == j` translates to `CMP R3, R4` because the ARM `CMP` instruction internally computes `R3 − R4` and sets the **Z flag** to **1** if and only if `R3` equals `R4`. **Step 2.** The HLL body executes *only* when the condition is true. To skip the body on false, we branch on the *opposite* condition, which is `i != j`, i.e., `BNE`. The branch target is the label immediately after the body, named `END_IF1`. **Step 3.** The body `f = g + h` translates to `ADD R0, R1, R2`. **Step 4.** Place the label `END_IF1:` so the `BNE` lands here. **Step 5.** The statement `f = f - i` is unconditional and translates to `SUB R0, R0, R3`. **Final ARM Assembly (UAL syntax):** ```asm CMP R3, R4 ; Compute i - j, set flags BNE END_IF1 ; If i != j, skip the Then-block ADD R0, R1, R2 ; Then-block: f = g + h END_IF1: SUB R0, R0, R3 ; f = f - i (always executes) ``` ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Canonical `if-then-else` Translation **High-Level C Source:** ```c if (i <= j) f = g + h; else f = g - h; f = f - i; ``` **Register Mapping:** Same as Section 3.1. **Translation Derivation:** **Step 1.** The condition is `i <= j`. The *true* form is `BLE` (signed less-than-or-equal). To branch to the **Else-block on false**, we use the *opposite* condition, which is `BGT` (signed greater-than). **Step 2.** Issue `CMP R3, R4`. **Step 3.** `BGT ELSE_BLOCK` — if `i > j`, jump to the Else-block. **Step 4.** Then-block: `ADD R0, R1, R2` for `f = g + h`. **Step 5.** After the Then-block, place an unconditional `B END_IF2` to skip over the Else-block. **Step 6.** `ELSE_BLOCK:` label followed by `SUB R0, R1, R2` for `f = g - h`. **Step 7.** `END_IF2:` label marks the convergence point. **Step 8.** The final `f = f - i` statement translates to `SUB R0, R0, R3`. **Final ARM Assembly:** ```asm CMP R3, R4 ; Compute i - j BGT ELSE_BLOCK ; If i > j (false of <=), branch to Else ADD R0, R1, R2 ; Then-block: f = g + h B END_IF2 ; Skip over the Else-block ELSE_BLOCK: SUB R0, R1, R2 ; Else-block: f = g - h END_IF2: SUB R0, R0, R3 ; f = f - i (always executes) ``` ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Compound Boolean Conditions For HLL constructs such as `if (a == b && c != d)`, the compiler (and the KTU-exam student) **must decompose the condition** into sequential `CMP`-then-`Bcc` pairs, using **short-circuit evaluation**. | HLL Condition | Translation Strategy | |---|---| | `A && B` | If `A` is false, branch to skip. If `A` is true, evaluate `B`; if `B` is false, branch to skip. | | `A \|\| B` | If `A` is true, branch to body. If `A` is false, evaluate `B`; if `B` is true, branch to body; else fall through to skip. | **Worked Example:** `if (i == j && f == g)` with same register map as above. ```asm CMP R3, R4 ; i == j ? BNE END_IF3 ; Short-circuit: false -> skip CMP R0, R1 ; f == g ? BNE END_IF3 ; Short-circuit: false -> skip ADD R0, R0, R2 ; Then-block (executed only if both true) END_IF3: ``` ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Control-Flow Graph of the `if-then` Translation Pattern The following Mermaid flowchart captures the *Compare-and-Skip* pattern universally required by KTU 2024 Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Program Entry]) --> nodeA["Execute CMP Rn, Rm
Sets APSR Flags N Z C V"] nodeA --> nodeB{"Bcc to END_IF
on inverted condition"} nodeB -- Branch taken
condition false --> nodeD["END_IF label
Fall through"] nodeB -- Fall through
condition true --> nodeC["Execute Then-block
ADD / SUB / MOV etc"] nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeStop([Continue Sequential Execution]) ``` ### 4.2 Control-Flow Graph of the `if-then-else` Translation Pattern ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Program Entry]) --> nodeA["CMP Rn, Rm
APSR flag update"] nodeA --> nodeB{"Bcc to ELSE
on false condition"} nodeB -- Branch taken
false --> nodeE["ELSE_BLOCK label"] nodeB -- Fall through
true --> nodeC["Then-block executes
e.g. ADD R0, R1, R2"] nodeC --> nodeD["Unconditional B END_IF
skip over Else-block"] nodeD --> nodeF["END_IF label
Convergence point"] nodeE --> nodeG["Else-block executes
e.g. SUB R0, R1, R2"] nodeG --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeStop([Continue Sequential Execution]) ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ARM Branch Resolution Datapath The following diagram illustrates the **hardware datapath** interaction that *enforces* the if-then translation pattern. It maps the HLL semantics to ARM hardware units. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FETCH_DECODE["Fetch and Decode Stage"] IR["Instruction Register
Holds Bcc opcode"] end subgraph EXECUTE["Execute Stage"] ALU["32-bit ALU
Subtraction for CMP"] APSR["APSR Register
Flags N Z C V"] CONDCK["Condition Code Checker
Tests flags vs opcode suffix"] end subgraph WRITEBACK["Writeback and PC Update"] PCMUX["PC Mux
Selects branch target or PC + 4"] end IR --> CONDCK ALU --> APSR APSR --> CONDCK CONDCK -->|"Condition met"| PCMUX CONDCK -->|"Condition not met"| PCMUX PCMUX --> FETCH_DECODE ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Mapping HLL to ARM Translation Primitives ```mermaid flowchart LR HLL["HLL Source Code
if then else"] --> PARSE["Lexical and Syntactic Parse
Identify relational operator"] PARSE --> SELCOND["Select ARM Condition Code
Bcc from KTU table"] SELCOND --> CMPGEN["Generate CMP instruction
with correct operand registers"] CMPGEN --> BRGEN["Generate Bcc with
inverted false condition"] BRGEN --> LBLGEN["Emit target label
END_IF or ELSE_BLOCK"] LBLGEN --> ASMOUT["ARM Assembly Listing
UAL syntax"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1a]** > **Q1.** *What is the role of the APSR (Application Program Status Register) in the execution of an `if-then` statement translated to ARM assembly?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The ARM processor uses the APSR flags — **N** (Negative), **Z** (Zero), **C** (Carry), and **V** (oVerflow) — to record the outcome of a `CMP` instruction. In an `if-then` translation, the `CMP` sets these flags based on the relational expression, and the subsequent conditional branch `Bcc` examines the flags. If the *inverted* (false) condition is met, the branch is taken, skipping the Then-block. Thus the APSR acts as the **bridge between data evaluation (CMP) and control-flow redirection (Bcc)**. **(3 marks)** --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q1b]** > **Q2.** *Differentiate between `BLT` and `BLO` branch instructions in ARM assembly. When is each used during the translation of an `if` statement?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** `BLT` (Branch Less Than) is used for **signed** comparison and tests the condition `N != V` after a `CMP`. It is used when the HLL condition involves signed integers, e.g., `if (i < j)` where `i` and `j` are declared `int`. `BLO` (Branch LOwer, equivalent to `BCS` in UAL) is used for **unsigned** comparison and tests `C == 0`. It is used when the operands are unsigned types, e.g., `if (a < b)` where `a` and `b` are declared `unsigned int`. Choosing the wrong suffix produces incorrect branch behavior for boundary values. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q6a, Module 3, 14 Marks]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Translate the following C code into ARM assembly using UAL syntax. Assume variables are mapped as: `a → R0`, `b → R1`, `c → R2`, `d → R3`, `e → R4`, `f → R5`. Show all labels and explain the inversion logic for the branch condition. **(7 marks)** ```c if (a < b) f = a + c; else f = a - c; f = f + e; ``` **(b)** Write the ARM assembly for the compound conditional `if (a == b && d != e)` using the same register map, and explain short-circuit evaluation. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Identify the condition:** `a < b` is a signed less-than. The *true* ARM mnemonic is `BLT`. The *false* (inverted) mnemonic is `BGE`. **Step 2 — Generate CMP:** `CMP R0, R1` computes `a − b`. **Step 3 — Branch to Else on false:** `BGE ELSE` — if `a >= b` (false of `<`), branch to Else. **Step 4 — Then-block:** `f = a + c` → `ADD R5, R0, R2`. **Step 5 — Unconditional skip:** `B END_IFA` to jump over the Else-block. **Step 6 — Else-block:** `f = a - c` → `SUB R5, R0, R2`. **Step 7 — Convergence:** `END_IFA:` label. **Step 8 — Final statement:** `f = f + e` → `ADD R5, R5, R4`. **Final Assembly:** ```asm CMP R0, R1 ; a - b BGE ELSE ; if a >= b (false of <), go to Else ADD R5, R0, R2 ; f = a + c B END_IFA ; skip over Else ELSE: SUB R5, R0, R2 ; f = a - c END_IFA: ADD R5, R5, R4 ; f = f + e ``` **Inversion Logic Explanation (KTU expects 1-2 lines):** The HLL condition is true when `a < b`. The assembly uses `BGE` because the *fall-through* path must execute the Then-block when the HLL condition is *true*, i.e., when `a < b`, i.e., when the *opposite* condition (`a >= b`) is **false**. Hence we branch on `BGE` to skip the Then-block only when `a >= b`. **(7 marks)** --- **Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compound condition decomposition:** The HLL uses logical AND (`&&`). ARM does not have a single instruction for this; we must *sequence* the tests using short-circuit evaluation. **Step 2 — First test:** `CMP R0, R1` for `a == b`. If false, the entire `&&` is false, so branch to skip using `BNE END_IFB`. **Step 3 — Second test (only if first was true):** `CMP R3, R4` for `d != e`. If true (i.e., `Z == 0`), proceed to the body. If false, fall through to skip. Use `BEQ END_IFB` to branch on false. **Step 4 — Body:** For this question, a representative body is added (`f = f + 1` → `ADD R5, R5, #1`). **Final Assembly:** ```asm CMP R0, R1 ; a == b ? BNE END_IFB ; short-circuit: if false, skip body CMP R3, R4 ; d != e ? BEQ END_IFB ; if d == e, skip body ADD R5, R5, #1 ; body executes only if both true END_IFB: ``` **Short-Circuit Explanation:** If the first sub-condition `a == b` evaluates to false, there is no need to evaluate `d != e` — the overall result is already false. The `BNE` immediately branches out, *short-circuiting* the second test. Only when the first test passes does the second `CMP` execute. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks)** **(a)** Translate the following C code to ARM assembly. Variable map: `x → R0`, `y → R1`, `z → R2`, `w → R3`. **(7 marks)** ```c if (x >= y) w = x - y; else w = x + y; w = w * 2; ``` **(b)** Explain with an example how the unsigned comparison mnemonics `BHI`, `BLS`, `BHS`, and `BLO` differ from the signed mnemonics `BGT`, `BLE`, `BGE`, and `BLT` in the context of translating `if` statements. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):** **Condition:** `x >= y` is signed `BGE`; inverted is `BLT`. The multiplication by 2 is realized via `LSL` (logical shift left by 1 bit) for efficiency. ```asm CMP R0, R1 ; x - y BLT ELSE2 ; if x < y (false of >=), go to Else SUB R3, R0, R1 ; w = x - y B END_IFB2 ELSE2: ADD R3, R0, R1 ; w = x + y END_IFB2: LSL R3, R3, #1 ; w = w * 2 (shift left by 1) ``` **(7 marks)** **Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):** Signed mnemonics (`BGT`, `BGE`, `BLT`, `BLE`) interpret the APSR flags using the formula `(N == V)`, which is correct only when the two's-complement bit patterns represent signed integers. Unsigned mnemonics (`BHI`, `BHS`, `BLO`, `BLS`) use the **C**arry flag, which is the legitimate indicator of magnitude for unsigned values. For example, comparing `0xFFFFFFFF` (`-1` signed, `4294967295` unsigned) with `0x00000001`: - As signed: `-1 < 1` is **true**, so `BLT` would branch. - As unsigned: `4294967295 > 1` is **true**, so `BHI` would branch. Using `BLT` on unsigned operands, or `BLO` on signed operands, produces **wrong** branch decisions. KTU examiners frequently test this boundary case. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing the "true" vs "false" branch direction (–2 marks):** Students often use the *true* condition (`BEQ` for `==`) to branch to the body, but the canonical KTU-accepted pattern is to branch on the *inverted* condition *over* the body. Both are functionally correct, but KTU's model answer uses the inverted pattern, and deviating risks partial credit loss. > 2. **Forgetting the unconditional `B END_IF` between Then and Else blocks (–3 marks):** This is the most common error in `if-then-else` translation and causes the Then-block to *fall through* into the Else-block, corrupting execution. > 3. **Using `BLT` for unsigned types (–2 marks):** This is a semantic error, not a syntactic one, and the KTU rubric explicitly penalizes it. > 4. **Missing the `END_IF` label placement (–1 mark):** Forgetting to define the convergence label causes the assembler to throw an "undefined symbol" error. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - The **Compare-and-Skip pattern** is the *universal* translation scheme: `CMP` → inverted-condition `Bcc` → Then-block body → `END_IF` label. - The **`if-then-else` pattern** adds one extra instruction: an **unconditional `B END_IF`** placed at the end of the Then-block to jump over the Else-block. - **APSR flags N, Z, C, V** are set by `CMP`/`CMN`/`TST` and *implicitly* tested by every `Bcc` instruction; the flags are *not* modified by most data-processing instructions unless the `S` suffix is appended. - **Signed** comparisons use `BGT`, `BGE`, `BLT`, `BLE` (test the `N` and `V` flags). **Unsigned** comparisons use `BHI`, `BHS`, `BLO`, `BLS` (test the `C` and `Z` flags). - **Compound conditions** (`&&`, `||`) are translated by **sequencing** multiple `CMP`/`Bcc` pairs with **short-circuit evaluation** — there is no single ARM instruction for logical AND/OR. - Labels (`END_IF:`, `ELSE_BLOCK:`) are **symbolic addresses** resolved at link time; they require a trailing colon in UAL syntax. - The `CMP` instruction performs `Rn − Rm` internally; the destination register is **not** written. To test for `Rn + Rm == 0`, use `CMN Rn, Rm`. To test for bit-mask conditions, use `TST Rn, Rm`. - The `B` (unconditional branch) and `Bcc` (conditional branch) instructions have a range of **±32 MB** in ARM state and **±4 GB** in Thumb-2 state for forward and backward jumps — sufficient for all single-function control flow. - For KTU 14-mark questions, **always** state the inversion logic in one sentence (1 mark), generate the assembly (5 marks), and add explanatory comments (1 mark).

topics.title: Compound Conditionals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Compound Conditionals # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) architecture, **Compound Conditionals** refer to the mechanism of combining multiple primitive condition flags (N, Z, C, V) — set in the Current Program Status Register (`CPSR`) by the ALU after every data-processing operation — to form complex decision-making logic. These compound conditions are encoded in the 4-bit `cond` field of nearly every ARM instruction word, allowing every instruction to be **conditionally executed**. The two primary mechanisms for compound conditionals in ARM are: 1. **Extended Condition Codes** (e.g., `HI`, `LS`, `GE`, `LT`, `GT`, `LE`) — these are pre-encoded logic combinations of the raw flags N, Z, C, and V. 2. **The IT (If–Then) Block** — a Thumb-2 instruction that allows up to **4 subsequent instructions** to be conditionally executed using the *same* condition, with optional `ELSE` branches, effectively creating a compact, branch-free `if-then-else` compound conditional structure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > Students must master the **16 ARM condition codes**, the flag-setting behavior of data-processing instructions (the `S` suffix), and the syntax/semantics of the **IT block** for writing efficient compound conditional logic in ARM assembly. ## 1.2 The CPSR Condition Flags — The "Four-Way Sensor" Before any compound conditional can be evaluated, the four flags in the CPSR must be updated. Think of them as four sensors that the ALU automatically trips every time it performs an arithmetic or logical operation: | Flag | Full Name | What it "senses" | |------|-----------|------------------| | **N** | Negative | Result's MSB (bit 31). `N=1` means the signed result is negative. | | **Z** | Zero | Result is exactly zero. `Z=1` means the operands were equal (after subtraction). | | **C** | Carry | For addition: unsigned overflow. For subtraction: **NO borrow** (i.e., minuend ≥ subtrahend, unsigned). | | **V** | oVerflow | Signed overflow: the sign bit was corrupted by an operation that exceeded the representable signed range `[−2³¹, 2³¹−1]`. | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Automatic Traffic Signal System Imagine you are driving an autonomous car approaching a crossroads. Your car's four sensors (N, Z, C, V) are like four dashboard warning lights: - **N light** = "the result of your last calculation was negative" (e.g., speed − distance). - **Z light** = "the result was exactly zero" (e.g., two distances were equal). - **C light** = "you did NOT run out of fuel/space unsigned-wise." - **V light** = "you did NOT overshoot the signed range." A **compound conditional** is the rulebook your car follows: *"If `C=1` **AND** `Z=0` (HI), then accelerate; else if `C=0` **OR** `Z=1` (LS), then brake."* The 16 ARM condition codes are exactly 16 such pre-canned rule combinations — they are decoded in hardware in a single cycle, making compound branching on ARM dramatically faster than the equivalent `if-else` ladder in CISC architectures. > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters in engineering:** Compound conditionals eliminate almost all short forward branches, which would otherwise flush the 3-stage (or deeper) pipeline. This is why ARM code is famously dense and power-efficient — perfect for embedded systems (mobile phones, IoT, automotive ECUs) that KTU graduates frequently design. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an ARM Instruction's `cond` Field Every 32-bit ARM instruction word has its **top 4 bits [31:28]** reserved for the condition code. If the condition evaluates to **FALSE**, the instruction is decoded as a **NOP** (no operation, single-cycle bubble). If **TRUE**, it executes normally. This is called **conditional execution** and is a unique superpower of the ARM ISA. $$ \text{Instruction Word} = [\underbrace{\text{cond}}_{4 \text{ bits}} \mid \text{opcode} \mid \text{operands}] $$ The Boolean predicate for the execution of an instruction stamped with condition `cc` is: $$ \text{Execute} = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } f_{\text{cc}}(N, Z, C, V) = 1 \\ \text{FALSE (NOP)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ## 2.2 The 16 ARM Condition Codes — Complete Truth Table The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU exams on this topic. Memorize the **Predicate**, **Flag test**, and **Meaning** columns. > [!NOTE] > In all expressions, the comparison is treated as **`Rn − Operand2`** (the result of a `CMP` instruction). Thus `Z=1` literally means "Rn equals Operand2". | Code | Mnemonic | Predicate (Boolean on flags) | Flag test | Meaning | Category | |:----:|:--------:|:----------------------------:|:---------:|:--------|:--------:| | `0000` | **EQ** | $\overline{Z}$ complement → $Z$ | `Z = 1` | Equal | Equality | | `0001` | **NE** | $\overline{Z}$ | `Z = 0` | Not equal | Equality | | `0010` | **CS / HS** | $C$ | `C = 1` | Carry set / Unsigned ≥ | Unsigned | | `0011` | **CC / LO** | $\overline{C}$ | `C = 0` | Carry clear / Unsigned < | Unsigned | | `0100` | **MI** | $N$ | `N = 1` | Minus (negative) | Sign | | `0101` | **PL** | $\overline{N}$ | `N = 0` | Plus (positive or zero) | Sign | | `0110` | **VS** | $V$ | `V = 1` | Signed overflow | Overflow | | `0111` | **VC** | $\overline{V}$ | `V = 0` | No signed overflow | Overflow | | **1000** | **HI** | $C \cdot \overline{Z}$ | `C=1 AND Z=0` | **Unsigned higher** | **Compound (Unsigned)** | | **1001** | **LS** | $\overline{C} + Z$ | `C=0 OR Z=1` | **Unsigned lower or same** | **Compound (Unsigned)** | | **1010** | **GE** | $\overline{N \oplus V}$ | `N = V` | **Signed ≥** | **Compound (Signed)** | | **1011** | **LT** | $N \oplus V$ | `N ≠ V` | **Signed <** | **Compound (Signed)** | | **1100** | **GT** | $\overline{Z} \cdot \overline{N \oplus V}$ | `Z=0 AND N=V` | **Signed >** | **Compound (Signed)** | | **1101** | **LE** | $Z + (N \oplus V)$ | `Z=1 OR N≠V` | **Signed ≤** | **Compound (Signed)** | | `1110` | **AL** | $1$ (always true) | Ignored | Always (default) | Unconditional | | `1111` | (NV) | — (special) | — | Never (deprecated, used as extension in v5+) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > The 8 codes from `1000` to `1101` (HI, LS, GE, LT, GT, LE, plus their implicit "always") are the **true compound conditionals** — they require **two or more flags** to be tested simultaneously. KTU frequently asks students to derive the Boolean expression for any of these. ## 2.3 The IT (If–Then) Block — Thumb-2 Compound Conditional Engine Introduced in **ARMv6T2**, the IT block is a *meta-instruction* (the assembler inserts it; hardware does not "execute" it in the conventional sense). Its purpose is to allow conditional execution of **1 to 4 subsequent instructions**, providing an `if-then-else` structure **without any branch**. ### 2.3.1 Syntax ```asm IT{x{y{z}}} firstcond ``` Where: - `firstcond` = the condition for the **first** instruction in the block (one of the 16 codes). - `x`, `y`, `z` = each is either **`T`** (Then, same condition as firstcond) or **`E`** (Else, the **logical inverse** of firstcond). - The block contains `1 + (\text{number of }x,y,z \text{ letters})` instructions, **maximum 4**. ### 2.3.2 Hardware Decoding Rule For an instruction at position $i$ in the IT block, its effective condition is: $$ \text{effcond}_i = \begin{cases} \text{firstcond} & \text{if letter}_i = T \\ \overline{\text{firstcond}} & \text{if letter}_i = E \end{cases} $$ ### 2.3.3 Canonical KTU Examples | IT Block | Equivalent C-style Logic | |:---------|:------------------------| | `ITTE EQ` | `if (EQ) instr1 Then instr2; else instr3;` | | `ITTET NE` | `if (NE) instr1, instr2, instr3; else instr4;` | | `IT AL` | `instr1` (the block has just one instruction, with no special behavior) | > [!WARNING] > **Strict Rules the Assembler Enforces:** > 1. The instructions inside the IT block **must not** use the `S` suffix to update the condition flags (this would corrupt the predicate for subsequent instructions in the block). > 2. The block **must not** contain a branch, writeback, or another IT. > 3. The total instruction count must equal $1 + |\{x,y,z\}|$. ## 2.4 The CMP/TST/CMN/TEQ Flag-Setting Quartet These are the four **comparison instructions** (no destination, only side-effect on flags) that ARM programmers use to populate the CPSR before evaluating a compound conditional: | Mnemonic | Operation Performed | Effect on flags | |:--------:|:-------------------:|:---------------:| | `CMP Rn, Op2` | $Rn - Op2$ | Sets N, Z, C, V | | `CMN Rn, Op2` | $Rn + Op2$ | Sets N, Z, C, V (good for "> −128" tests) | | `TST Rn, Op2` | $Rn \,\&\, Op2$ | Sets N, Z (C = NOT-shifted-out, V unaffected) | | `TEQ Rn, Op2` | $Rn \oplus Op2$ | Sets N, Z (C, V cleared) | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive ECU (ABS braking):** `if (wheel_speed > threshold) { trigger ABS; }` translates to `CMP` + `BGT` or `IT GT`. - **IoT battery monitor:** `if (voltage >= 3.3V && temperature < 60°C)` — the `&&` is exactly the AND inside `GT`/`HI` predicates, so the C compiler emits a single `BGT` instead of two branches. - **Digital filter DSP loops:** Branch-free IT blocks keep the pipeline full in tight inner loops, doubling throughput on Cortex-M4 cores. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the `GE` and `LT` Compound Predicates These are the most commonly-asked derivations in KTU exams. The question is: *"After `CMP R0, R1`, derive the Boolean expression for the `cond` field of `BGE` (Branch if Greater-than-or-Equal, signed)."* ### 3.1.1 Setup A signed comparison `R0 ≥ R1` is performed via the subtraction $R0 - R1$. There are three cases to consider, and the V flag's job is to disambiguate the two cases where the unsigned logic (C flag) would give the *wrong* signed answer. ### 3.1.2 The Three Cases Let $A = R0$ and $B = R1$, both interpreted as **signed 2's complement**. 1. **No signed overflow occurs** ($V = 0$). Then the sign of the result is correct, and `N = 1` means the result is negative, i.e., $A - B < 0$, i.e., $A < B$. 2. **Signed positive overflow occurs** ($V = 1$ and the operands had opposite signs resulting in a positive result that wrapped). In this case `N = 0` even though the true mathematical result was negative, so we have the rule: when $V = 1$, the **N flag is inverted** in its meaning. 3. **Signed negative overflow occurs** ($V = 1$ and the operands had opposite signs resulting in a negative result that wrapped). The N flag here is also inverted in meaning. ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Truth Table | Case | $A$ | $B$ | $A - B$ (true math) | N | V | $A \stackrel{?}{\ge} B$ | |:----:|:---:|:---:|:-------------------:|:-:|:-:|:------------------:| | 1 | +5 | +2 | +3 | 0 | 0 | TRUE | | 2 | +5 | +2 | (same as 1) | 0 | 0 | TRUE | | 3 | −5 | +2 | −7 | 1 | 0 | FALSE | | 4 | +5 | −2 | +7 (overflows to −) | 0 | 1 | TRUE | | 5 | −5 | +2 | −7 | 1 | 0 | FALSE | | 6 | −5 | −2 | −3 | 1 | 0 | FALSE | | 7 | +2·10⁹ | −2·10⁹ | +4·10⁹ (overflows) | 0 | 1 | TRUE | In **Case 4**: the true answer is "5 ≥ −2" → TRUE, and N=0, V=1. The simple `N=0` test would say "5 < −2" which is FALSE. So when V=1, we must **invert N**. In **Case 1**: N=0, V=0, and the true answer is TRUE. So when V=0, the test `N=0` is correct. Therefore, the predicate for "signed ≥" is: $$ \text{GE} \equiv (N = V) \equiv \overline{N \oplus V} $$ $$ \therefore \text{LT} \equiv \overline{\text{GE}} \equiv (N \neq V) \equiv (N \oplus V) $$ ### 3.1.4 Final Boolean Algebraic Verification $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GE} &= \overline{N \oplus V} \\ &= \overline{N}\cdot\overline{V} + N \cdot V \quad &\text{(by De Morgan's law on XOR)} \\ \text{LT} &= N \oplus V \\ &= N \cdot \overline{V} + \overline{N} \cdot V \end{aligned} $$ This matches the entries in Section 2.2. ## 3.2 Derivation of the `GT` and `LE` Compound Predicates (Signed Greater-Than / Less-or-Equal) A signed greater-than is *strict*: we want $A > B$, not $A \geq B$. So we require GE *and* the result was not zero. 1. From the prior derivation, the GE predicate is $\overline{N \oplus V}$. 2. The result is non-zero iff `Z = 0`, i.e., $\overline{Z}$. 3. Both must hold, so we take the **logical AND**: $$ \boxed{\text{GT} \equiv \overline{Z} \cdot \overline{N \oplus V}} $$ The dual is the OR of the negations (LE means "not GT"): $$ \boxed{\text{LE} \equiv Z + (N \oplus V)} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of `HI` and `LS` (Unsigned Compound Conditionals) For **unsigned** arithmetic, only the C and Z flags matter (N is just the MSB of the unsigned bit pattern and is meaningless for ordering; V is irrelevant). The rule is: - **HI** (Higher) = strictly greater unsigned, so C=1 (no borrow, i.e., A ≥ B) **and** the result is non-zero (Z=0): $$ \boxed{\text{HI} \equiv C \cdot \overline{Z}} $$ - **LS** (Lower or Same) = the logical complement of HI: $$ \boxed{\text{LS} \equiv \overline{C} + Z} $$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Evaluating `BGT` **Problem.** Given the state of the CPSR after an arithmetic operation: $N = 1$, $Z = 0$, $C = 1$, $V = 1$. Will a `BGT label` (Branch if Greater Than, signed) instruction execute or be skipped? ### 3.4.1 Step 1 — Identify the Predicate From Section 3.2: $\text{GT} \equiv \overline{Z} \cdot \overline{N \oplus V}$. ### 3.4.2 Step 2 — Evaluate $\overline{Z}$ $$ \overline{Z} = \overline{0} = 1 $$ ### 3.4.3 Step 3 — Evaluate $N \oplus V$ $$ N \oplus V = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 $$ ### 3.4.4 Step 4 — Evaluate $\overline{N \oplus V}$ $$ \overline{N \oplus V} = \overline{0} = 1 $$ ### 3.4.5 Step 5 — Final AND $$ \text{GT} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{TRUE} $$ **Conclusion:** The `BGT` instruction **will execute** and the branch to `label` will be taken. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — IT Block Construction Exercise **Problem.** Translate the following C code into an ARM Thumb-2 assembly fragment using a single IT block, **no branches**: ```c if (R0 == R1) { R2 = R2 + 1; R3 = R3 - 1; } else { R4 = R4 + 1; } ``` ### 3.5.1 Step 1 — Identify the Predicate Equality test → `EQ` (Z=1). The `if` is the `Then` part, the `else` is the `Else` part. ### 3.5.2 Step 2 — Count the Statements - Then part: 2 instructions (`ADD R2, R2, #1` and `SUB R3, R3, #1`). - Else part: 1 instruction (`ADD R4, R4, #1`). - Total: 3 → we need the `IT` to declare 2 extra letters, so we use the `IT{x{y}}` form. ### 3.5.3 Step 3 — Construct the IT Block The first instruction is in the Then arm (T), the second is in the Then arm (T), the third is in the Else arm (E). So: ```asm ITTE EQ ADDEQ R2, R2, #1 ; executes if EQ (Z=1) SUBEQ R3, R3, #1 ; executes if EQ (Z=1) ADDNE R4, R4, #1 ; executes if NE (Z=0) ← note: NE is the inverse of EQ ``` ### 3.5.4 Step 4 — Verify the Hardware Decoding Rule - `ADDEQ` is at position 0, letter `T`, so effcond = `EQ` ✓ - `SUBEQ` is at position 1, letter `T`, so effcond = `EQ` ✓ - `ADDNE` is at position 2, letter `E`, so effcond = $\overline{\text{EQ}}$ = `NE` ✓ ### 3.5.5 Step 5 — Branch-Free Bonus This entire construct executes in **4 cycles** (IT itself takes 1 cycle, then 3 single-cycle instructions), with **zero pipeline flushes**. The equivalent CISC branch-based code would be ~7 cycles with 2 potential mispredictions. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Compound Conditional Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Arithmetic or Logical Instruction Executes] --> B[ALU Computes Result] B --> C[Flags Updated in CPSR] C --> D{N Z C V Values} D --> E[Encode 4-bit cond field in next instruction] E --> F{Decode cond field} F --> G[Compound Predicate Hardware Evaluates] G --> H{Predicate = 1?} H -->|YES| I[Instruction Executes Fully] H -->|NO| J[Instruction Decoded as NOP] I --> K[Write Back to Register File] J --> L[Pipeline Bubble 1 Cycle] K --> M[Continue to Next Instruction] L --> M M --> N{IT Block Active?} N -->|YES| O[Apply per-instruction T or E rule] N -->|NO| P[Next instruction normally conditional] O --> P ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — IT Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ITB[IT Block in Thumb2] IT[IT or ITT or ITTT or ITTTT
First Condition EQ] --> I1[Instruction 1
Condition T thencond] IT --> I2[Instruction 2
Condition T or E] IT --> I3[Instruction 3
Condition T or E] IT --> I4[Instruction 4
Condition T or E] end I1 --> FLAGCHECK1{Condition met?} I2 --> FLAGCHECK2{Condition met?} I3 --> FLAGCHECK3{Condition met?} I4 --> FLAGCHECK4{Condition met?} FLAGCHECK1 -->|YES| EX1[Execute Instruction 1] FLAGCHECK1 -->|NO| NOP1[NOP] FLAGCHECK2 -->|YES| EX2[Execute Instruction 2] FLAGCHECK2 -->|NO| NOP2[NOP] FLAGCHECK3 -->|YES| EX3[Execute Instruction 3] FLAGCHECK3 -->|NO| NOP3[NOP] FLAGCHECK4 -->|YES| EX4[Execute Instruction 4] FLAGCHECK4 -->|NO| NOP4[NOP] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Compound Predicate Synthesis (the "Why" of `GE`) ```mermaid flowchart TD CMP[CMP R0 R1 performs R0 minus R1] --> SIGN{True Sign of Math Result} SIGN -->|positive| P1[N equals 0 V equals 0 normal] SIGN -->|negative| P2[N equals 1 V equals 0 normal] SIGN -->|positive overflow| P3[N equals 0 V equals 1 N misleading] SIGN -->|negative overflow| P4[N equals 1 V equals 1 N misleading] P1 --> TEST{N equals V test} P2 --> TEST P3 --> TEST P4 --> TEST TEST -->|TRUE| GEQ[Result means R0 greater or equal R1] TEST -->|FALSE| LT[Result means R0 less than R1] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — ARM Instruction Word Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR W[32 bit ARM Instruction Word] --> W31[Bits 31 to 28] W31 --> COND[cond field 4 bits selects one of 16 condition codes] W --> W27[Bits 27 to 25] W27 --> OPC[Opcode or type identifier] W --> W24[Bits 24 to 21] W24 --> OPR[Operand addressing mode] W --> W20[Bits 20 to 0] W20 --> REST[Rn Rd Operand2 shifter details] COND -.->|decoded| HW[Hardware Predicate Unit] HW -->|TRUE| EXEC[Instruction executes in ALU] HW -->|FALSE| SKIP[Instruction treated as NOP] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q:** What is an **IT block** in ARM Thumb-2 architecture? Write the syntax and explain the meaning of the letters `T` and `E`. **Model Answer:** The **IT (If–Then) block** is a Thumb-2 instruction introduced in ARMv6T2 that allows **1 to 4 subsequent instructions** to be conditionally executed without using branches. Syntax: ```asm IT{x{y{z}}} firstcond ``` - **`T`** stands for **"Then"**. The instruction with the `T` letter executes under the **same condition as the first instruction** (`firstcond`). - **`E`** stands for **"Else"**. The instruction with the `E` letter executes under the **logical inverse** of `firstcond`. Example: `ITTE EQ` means *"If Equal, do instructions 1 and 2; Else do instruction 3."* ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q:** List the **16 ARM condition codes** with their predicates in terms of the flags N, Z, C, and V. Highlight which of them are **compound conditionals**. **Model Answer:** | Mnemonic | Predicate | Compound? | |:--------:|:---------:|:---------:| | EQ | $Z$ | No | | NE | $\overline{Z}$ | No | | CS/HS | $C$ | No | | CC/LO | $\overline{C}$ | No | | MI | $N$ | No | | PL | $\overline{N}$ | No | | VS | $V$ | No | | VC | $\overline{V}$ | No | | **HI** | $C \cdot \overline{Z}$ | **Yes (Unsigned)** | | **LS** | $\overline{C} + Z$ | **Yes (Unsigned)** | | **GE** | $\overline{N \oplus V}$ | **Yes (Signed)** | | **LT** | $N \oplus V$ | **Yes (Signed)** | | **GT** | $\overline{Z} \cdot \overline{N \oplus V}$ | **Yes (Signed)** | | **LE** | $Z + (N \oplus V)$ | **Yes (Signed)** | | AL | $1$ (always) | No | | NV | Never (deprecated) | No | The **8 conditional codes** that depend on more than one flag (i.e., the **last 4 pairs above with their sign or unsigned dual**) are the **compound conditionals**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **CPSR condition flags** N, Z, C, and V in detail. For each flag, give one example ARM instruction and the resulting flag value. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the **Boolean expression** for the `GE` (Greater-than-or-Equal, signed) condition code from first principles using the $R0 - R1$ subtraction model. Show all cases in a truth table. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to A(a) The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** contains four condition flags that the ALU updates automatically after every data-processing instruction (when the `S` suffix is used, or implicitly by `CMP`/`TST`/`CMN`/`TEQ`). | Flag | Meaning | Example Instruction | After execution | |:----:|:-------:|:-------------------:|:---------------:| | **N (Negative)** | Set if the **MSB (bit 31)** of the result is 1, i.e., the signed result is negative. | `MOV R0, #-5` | `N = 1` (because −5 in 2's complement is `0xFFFFFFFB`) | | **Z (Zero)** | Set if the result is **exactly zero**. | `CMP R0, R0` | `Z = 1` (R0 − R0 = 0) | | **C (Carry)** | For addition: **1 if unsigned overflow occurred** (i.e., result > 2³²−1). For subtraction: **1 if NO borrow** (i.e., unsigned minuend ≥ subtrahend). | `SUBS R0, R5, R3` with R5=10, R3=5 | `C = 1` (no borrow) | | **V (oVerflow)** | Set if a **signed overflow** occurred: the sign of the result is incorrect because the true mathematical result is outside `[−2³¹, 2³¹−1]`. | `ADDS R0, R1, R2` with R1=+2·10⁹, R2=+2·10⁹ | `V = 1` (true sum 4·10⁹ doesn't fit in signed 32-bit) | These flags serve as the inputs to all 16 ARM condition codes. Without the `S` suffix (e.g., plain `ADD` instead of `ADDS`), the flags are **not** updated. --- #### Model Solution to A(b) We want the condition under which the signed subtraction $R0 - R1$ indicates $R0 \geq R1$. **Step 1:** List all four sign cases for the operands and the true mathematical result, with the corresponding ARM hardware flag values N and V. | Case | $R0$ (signed) | $R1$ (signed) | True $R0-R1$ (signed) | Hardware N | Hardware V | |:----:|:-------------:|:-------------:|:---------------------:|:----------:|:----------:| | 1 | +5 | +2 | +3 | 0 | 0 | | 2 | −5 | +2 | −7 | 1 | 0 | | 3 | +5 | −2 | +7 → overflows to −2³¹+6 (N=0) | 0 | 1 | | 4 | −5 | −2 | −3 | 1 | 0 | | 5 | +2·10⁹ | −2·10⁹ | +4·10⁹ → overflows | 0 | 1 | | 6 | −2·10⁹ | +2·10⁹ | −4·10⁹ → overflows | 1 | 1 | **Step 2:** For each case, determine whether $R0 \geq R1$ is TRUE. | Case | $R0 \geq R1$? | N | V | N = V? | |:----:|:-------------:|:-:|:-:|:------:| | 1 | TRUE | 0 | 0 | TRUE | | 2 | FALSE | 1 | 0 | FALSE | | 3 | TRUE | 0 | 1 | TRUE | | 4 | FALSE | 1 | 0 | FALSE | | 5 | TRUE | 0 | 1 | TRUE | | 6 | FALSE | 1 | 1 | TRUE | (still FALSE mathematically, but N=V!)
**WAIT — recheck:** −2·10⁹ < +2·10⁹, so the predicate should be FALSE, but N=V gives TRUE. ⚠ This is the corner case. | **Step 3 (CRITICAL RE-CHECK):** The N flag is the **truncated** result's MSB. For Case 6, the true result −4·10⁹ is representable in 32-bit signed? 2³¹ ≈ +2.147·10⁹, so −4·10⁹ < −2.147·10⁹, **yes it overflows**. The truncated 32-bit result is + (some large positive). Thus N=0, V=1, but the true result is negative, so $R0 \not\geq R1$.
