After re-evaluating the truth table carefully: | Case | True $R0-R1$ signed | Hardware N | Hardware V | $R0 \geq R1$ TRUE? | $N = V$ TRUE? | Match? | |:----:|:-------------------:|:----------:|:----------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:------:| | 1 | +3 | 0 | 0 | TRUE | TRUE | ✓ | | 2 | −7 | 1 | 0 | FALSE | FALSE | ✓ | | 3 | +7 (overflow→−) | 0 | 1 | TRUE | TRUE | ✓ | | 4 | −3 | 1 | 0 | FALSE | FALSE | ✓ | | 5 | +4·10⁹ (overflow→−) | 1 | 1 | TRUE | TRUE | ✓ | | 6 | −4·10⁹ (overflow→+) | 0 | 1 | FALSE | FALSE | ✓ | > [!NOTE] > **Correction:** In Case 5, +4·10⁹ wraps around to a *negative* 32-bit value, so N=1, not 0. And in Case 6, −4·10⁹ wraps to a *positive* 32-bit value, so N=0, not 1. The corrected table now confirms the identity perfectly. **Step 4: Final Boolean Expression.** Across all 6 cases, "$R0 \geq R1$" is true **if and only if** $N = V$. Therefore: $$ \boxed{\text{GE} \equiv (N = V) \equiv \overline{N \oplus V} \equiv \overline{N} \cdot \overline{V} + N \cdot V} $$ The hardware implements this as a **single XNOR gate** on the N and V flag bits, evaluated in 1 cycle. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With neat syntax, explain the **IT (If-Then) block** of the ARM Thumb-2 architecture. State **three rules** that the assembler enforces on instructions inside an IT block. (7 marks) **(b)** Write an ARM assembly program to find the **largest of three numbers** stored in `R0`, `R1`, `R2`. Use compound conditional branches (`BGE`, `BGT`, etc.) and the `IT` block where appropriate. Show the result in `R3`. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to B(a) **Syntax of the IT block:** ```asm IT{x{y{z}}} firstcond ``` - `firstcond` is one of the 16 ARM condition codes (e.g., `EQ`, `NE`, `GT`, `LE`). - Each of `x`, `y`, `z` is either **`T`** (Then) or **`E`** (Else), and determines the condition of the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th instruction in the block, respectively. - The total number of instructions in the block = $1 + (\text{count of }x, y, z \text{ letters})$; maximum 4. **Three rules enforced by the assembler:** 1. **No flag updates inside the block:** Instructions within an IT block must **not** have the `S` suffix. This prevents a single instruction from corrupting the CPSR flags that subsequent instructions in the block are testing. 2. **No control-flow inside the block:** Instructions within an IT block must **not** be branches, label definitions, or other IT blocks. The IT block must be a straight-line sequence of data-processing or load/store instructions. 3. **The block must be physically contiguous:** All instructions in the IT block must be **immediately consecutive** in memory (no labels, no other instructions between them) and must all be **inside an IT-executable region** (i.e., not crossing a literal pool or a function boundary). --- #### Model Solution to B(b) ```asm ; Assume: R0, R1, R2 contain the three numbers ; Result: R3 will contain the largest MOV R3, R0 ; R3 = R0 (tentative largest) ; If R3 < R1, update R3 to R1 CMP R3, R1 ITT LT ; if (R3 < R1) MOVLT R3, R1 ; R3 = R1 ; (we need an else? not necessary here — but suppose we wanted to log it) NOP ; placeholder to show the IT block ends cleanly ; If R3 < R2, update R3 to R2 CMP R3, R2 ITT LT ; if (R3 < R2) MOVLT R3, R2 ; R3 = R2 NOP ; R3 now holds the largest of the three ; End of program ``` **Alternative branch-based version (also acceptable in KTU):** ```asm MOV R3, R0 CMP R0, R1 BGE SKIP1 MOV R3, R1 SKIP1: CMP R3, R2 BGE SKIP2 MOV R3, R2 SKIP2: ; R3 = max(R0, R1, R2) ``` The **IT-block version is preferred** because it eliminates the two branches and the two `SKIP` labels, producing denser, faster code. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU examiners look for (each costs 1–2 marks):** > 1. **Confusing signed and unsigned compound conditionals.** `BHI` and `BGT` are *not* interchangeable. `BHI` uses the C and Z flags (unsigned); `BGT` uses the N, Z, and V flags (signed). Mixing them is the #1 error. > 2. **Forgetting the `S` suffix on the flag-setting instruction.** `CMP R0, R1` *does* set flags, but `ADD R0, R0, #1` does **not**. Students often write `ADD R0, R0, #1` followed by `BGT` and are surprised it never branches. Use `ADDS` or use `CMP`. > 3. **Using `B` instead of `BGE`/`BGT` etc.** A bare `B label` is **unconditional** and ignores all flags. Always specify the condition. > 4. **Writing `BGT` after a logical (`AND`, `ORR`) instruction.** The C and V flags are *cleared* by logical instructions, so `BGT` and `BHI` after `AND` will give wrong results. Use `CMP`/`TST` instead. > 5. **Forgetting the inverse condition in the `E` arm of an IT block.** The assembler will not warn if you write `ITTE EQ` followed by `ADDEQ / SUBEQ / ADDEQ` — the third instruction is silently a NOP. Always double-check: the `E` instructions must have the **opposite** condition mnemonic (`NE` in this case). > 6. **Putting more than 4 instructions in an IT block.** The assembler will error: *"IT block too long"*. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **ARM has 16 condition codes**, encoded in the top 4 bits of every instruction word. - [x] The **4 CPSR condition flags** are **N, Z, C, V**, set by data-processing instructions with the `S` suffix or by `CMP`/`TST`/`CMN`/`TEQ`. - [x] **`Z=1`** ⇒ equality (after `CMP`). - [x] **`C=1`** ⇒ unsigned "no borrow" (after `CMP`/subtraction) **or** unsigned overflow (after `ADD`). - [x] **`N`** reflects the MSB of the result (sign bit). - [x] **`V=1`** indicates signed overflow; the N flag is then **unreliable** for ordering. - [x] The **8 compound conditionals** are the ones that depend on **two or more flags**: `HI, LS, GE, LT, GT, LE` (and their implicit symmetric versions). - [x] **`HI`** = $C \cdot \overline{Z}$ — unsigned strictly greater. - [x] **`LS`** = $\overline{C} + Z$ — unsigned less-or-equal. - [x] **`GE`** = $\overline{N \oplus V}$ — signed ≥. - [x] **`LT`** = $N \oplus V$ — signed <. - [x] **`GT`** = $\overline{Z} \cdot \overline{N \oplus V}$ — signed >. - [x] **`LE`** = $Z + (N \oplus V)$ — signed ≤. - [x] The **IT block** (Thumb-2, ARMv6T2+) enables branch-free compound conditional execution of **1 to 4** instructions using `T`/`E` letters. - [x] The **`E` letter** = the **logical inverse** of the first condition; the **`T` letter** = the same as the first condition. - [x] **Rules inside an IT block:** no `S` suffix, no branches, no labels, no other ITs, max 4 instructions. - [x] **Every ARM instruction can be made conditional** by appending a condition mnemonic (e.g., `MOVEQ R0, #1`, `ADDLT R2, R2, #1`). - [x] If the condition is FALSE, the instruction is **a single-cycle NOP** — pipeline-friendly. - [x] Compound conditionals in ARM drastically reduce branch mispredictions, making code faster and lower-power — a major reason ARM dominates **embedded** and **mobile** markets.

topics.title: Implementing Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing Loops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **PEECT525 – ARM Architecture, Programming and Interfacing** syllabus, a **Loop** in ARM assembly is a *programmed control-flow construct* that causes the processor to repeatedly execute a sequence of instructions until a **specified termination condition**—encoded via the **Application Program Status Register (APSR)** condition flags (N, Z, C, V)—is satisfied. For the **Cortex-M** family (Thumb-2 ISA), loops are implemented using a combination of:

- **Branch instructions** (`B <label>`, `B<cond> <label>`, `BX <Rm>`, `BL <label>`)
- **Data-processing instructions that update APSR flags** (`CMP`, `SUBS`, `ADDS`, `TST`, `MOVS`)
- **Label-based local addressing** (assembler-resolved PC-relative offsets, range $\pm$ **2048 bytes** in Thumb mode for conditional `B<cond>`, and $\pm$ **16 MB** for unconditional `B`)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3):** *“Writing ARM assembly programs – arithmetic, logical, branch, loop and subroutine operations.”* Loop implementation is treated as a *core sub-topic* of the broader *branching and program-flow* unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Classroom Roll Call" Loop

Imagine a teacher taking attendance in a class of **30 students** (the **loop counter**).

1. The teacher starts with the count `R1 = 30` (the **loop limit**).
2. For each student, the teacher says "Present?" – this is the **loop body** (the actual work).
3. The teacher then **decrements** the counter (`SUBS R1, R1, #1`).
4. The teacher asks, "Is the count **zero**?" – this check is performed *implicitly* by the **Z flag** of the APSR after the `SUBS`.
5. If `Z = 0` (count not zero), the teacher goes back to the next student (`BNE loop_body`).
6. If `Z = 1` (count is zero), attendance is over – the program **exits** the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ARM Uses a "Test-Then-Branch" Model:** Unlike high-level C, where `while(x--)` decrements *and* tests in one micro-op, ARM assembly **splits the operation into two distinct instructions**: a flag-setting arithmetic op (`SUBS`) followed by a conditional branch (`BEQ`/`BNE`). This separation is a deliberate **RISC design philosophy** that allows the pipeline to fetch the next instruction *speculatively* while the branch is being resolved.

## 1.3 Loop Taxonomy in ARM Assembly

| Loop Type | High-Level C Equivalent | ARM Assembly Pattern | Termination Test Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Counted (For) Loop** | `for(i=0; i<N; i++)` | `MOVS` + `SUBS` + `BNE` | Bottom (post-test) |
| **Pre-test (While) Loop** | `while(cond)` | `<op>` + `B<cond> end` | Top (pre-test) |
| **Post-test (Do-While) Loop** | `do { ... } while(cond);` | `loop: ...; <op>; BNE loop` | Bottom (post-test) |
| **Infinite Loop** | `while(1)` | `loop: ...; B loop` | Never |
| **Delay Loop** | `for(i=0; i<50000; i++);` | Nested `SUBS` + `BNE` | Inner bottom |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A *very common* KTU question differentiates between a *post-test* loop and a *pre-test* loop. Remember: a **pre-test loop** has a *guard branch at the top* (`B<cond> exit` placed *before* the loop body), while a **post-test loop** has a *conditional branch at the bottom* (`B<cond> loop` placed *after* the body).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The APSR Condition Flags – The Heart of Every Loop

Every conditional branch instruction (`B<cond>`) in ARM checks the bits of the **APSR** (Application Program Status Register). After any data-processing instruction with the **`S` suffix** (e.g., `SUBS`, `ADDS`, `CMP`, `TST`), the four flags are updated:

| Flag | Bit | Set When (After `SUBS Rd, Rn, Operand2` ≡ `Rd = Rn − Operand2`) | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | 31 | Result is **negative** (MSB = 1) | Signed overflow indicator |
| **Z** | 30 | Result is **zero** (Rd = 0) | Equality / counter-exhausted |
| **C** | 29 | **No unsigned borrow** occurred (Rn $\geq$ Operand2) | Unsigned $\geq$ test |
| **V** | 28 | **Signed overflow** occurred | Signed-overflow trap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mnemonic to Memorize:** *"N is Negative, Z is Zero, C is Carry (no borrow), V is oVerflow."* In KTU exams, examiners frequently give a snippet like `SUBS R0, R1, R2` and ask *"Which flag is set if R1 = R2?"* — the answer is always **Z = 1**.

## 2.2 ARM Branch Instruction Encoding (Thumb-2)

For the **Cortex-M** (Thumb-2), conditional branch instructions are encoded as a **16-bit opcode** with the following simplified format:

$$\text{B}\langle cond \rangle \text{ label} \;\equiv\; \underbrace{1101}_{\text{opcode}} \;\underbrace{cccc}_{\text{cond\,code}} \;\underbrace{\text{imm8}}_{\text{signed offset}}$$

The branch offset is computed by the assembler as:

$$\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + 4 + (\text{imm8} \times 2)$$

This yields the canonical range of $\pm$ **2048 bytes** (i.e., $\pm$ **1024 instructions** in Thumb) for conditional `B<cond>`. Unconditional `B` (16-bit) is limited to $\pm$ **2048 bytes**; for larger jumps, the assembler auto-promotes it to a **32-bit** `B.W` (wide) instruction with a $\pm$ **16 MB** range.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thumb `B<cond>` range | $\text{imm8} \in [-128, +127]$ (words) | $\pm$ **2048 bytes** | Small inner loops |
| Thumb `B.W` (unconditional) range | $\text{imm11} \in$ extended | $\pm$ **16 MB** | Large program jumps |
| Loop cycles (counted) | $T_{loop} = N \times (T_{body} + T_{branch})$ | Clock cycles | Real-time scheduling |
| Delay loop total cycles | $T_{delay} = N_1 \times (N_2 \times T_{inner} + T_{dec})$ | Clock cycles | Software debouncing |
| Z flag setting (CMP/SUBS) | $Z = 1 \iff R_n = \text{Operand2}$ | Boolean | Equality / loop-exit |
| C flag (unsigned) | $C = 1 \iff R_n \geq_u \text{Operand2}$ | Boolean | Unsigned $\geq$ check |
| V flag (signed) | $V = 1 \iff \text{signed overflow}$ | Boolean | Signed-overflow trap |
| Two's complement negation | $-x \equiv \overline{x} + 1$ | Bitwise | Decrement by `SUBS` |
| Hex-to-decimal quick | $0\text{x}FF = 255$, $0\text{x}100 = 256$ | — | Counter math |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Engineers use *delay loops* in embedded firmware to generate precise software-based timing — for example, generating a **1 µs high-pulse** on a GPIO pin, debouncing a mechanical switch for **10 ms**, or producing a custom baud-rate divisor. In a Cortex-M0 running at **48 MHz**, one `SUBS + BNE` pair consumes **3 clock cycles**, so an outer counter of 16000 gives exactly **1 ms** of delay.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Loop-Construction Pattern (Exhaustive)

The canonical ARM loop is built in **four mandatory steps**. Below is the *exhaustive* derivation of each step, including register state tracing.

### Step 1 — Initialize the Counter

$$\text{MOVS} \; R_1, \; \#N$$

- `MOVS` (move and set flags) is used instead of `MOV` so that the **Z flag is initialized correctly** if `N = 0` (edge case: empty loop).
- `R_1` is a *high register* choice permitted in Thumb-2; `R0–R7` are *low registers* and are preferred for the **ARM Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** for argument passing.

### Step 2 — Execute Loop Body

The body can be any sequence of ARM instructions — `LDR`, `STR`, `ADD`, `AND`, `ORR`, etc. For this derivation, we model a generic *accumulator*:

$$\text{ADD} \; R_0, R_0, R_2$$

This adds the value of `R_2` into accumulator `R_0`. *No flag update* because we omitted the `S` suffix.

### Step 3 — Decrement and Test

$$\text{SUBS} \; R_1, R_1, \#1$$

The **`S` suffix is critical.** It causes the ALU to:
1. Compute $R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - 1$.
2. Update **N, Z, C, V** flags based on the result.

The relationship between $R_1$ and `Z` is:

$$Z = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } R_1 = 0 \text{ after decrement (i.e., previous } R_1 = 1) \\ 0, & \text{if } R_1 \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 — Conditional Branch

$$\text{BNE} \; \text{loop\_label}$$

The `BNE` (Branch if Not Equal) instruction is a macro for `B 0001` (cond code = `0001`). It evaluates the in-APSR **Z flag**:

$$\text{Branch taken} \iff Z = 0$$

The complete loop, then, is:

```asm
        MOVS    R1, #N          ; Step 1: initialize
loop    ADD     R0, R0, R2      ; Step 2: body
        SUBS    R1, R1, #1      ; Step 3: decrement + flag update
        BNE     loop            ; Step 4: branch if Z=0 (counter not zero)
```

## 3.2 Numerical Trace — The "Sum of First 5 Integers" Loop

Let us trace the loop that computes $S = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15$, with `R2 = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5` loaded in successive iterations.

```asm
        MOV     R0, #0          ; S = 0
        MOV     R1, #5          ; counter N = 5
        MOV     R2, #1          ; first addend
loop    ADD     R0, R0, R2      ; S += R2
        ADD     R2, R2, #1      ; R2++
        SUBS    R1, R1, #1      ; N--, update flags
        BNE     loop            ; if N != 0, repeat
```

**Trace Table:**

| Iteration | $R_0$ (S) | $R_2$ (addend) | $R_1$ (counter) before `SUBS` | $R_1$ after `SUBS` | Z flag | Branch taken? |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0 + 1 = **1** | 2 | 5 | 4 | 0 | **Yes** |
| 2 | 1 + 2 = **3** | 3 | 4 | 3 | 0 | **Yes** |
| 3 | 3 + 3 = **6** | 4 | 3 | 2 | 0 | **Yes** |
| 4 | 6 + 4 = **10** | 5 | 2 | 1 | 0 | **Yes** |
| 5 | 10 + 5 = **15** | 6 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **No (exit)** |

Final result: $R_0 = 15$ ✓

## 3.3 Derivation — Delay Loop (Nested Counter Structure)

A delay loop of duration $T_d$ is constructed by **nesting** two counters. If the inner loop takes $T_{inner}$ cycles per iteration and is executed $M$ times for each of $N$ outer iterations:

$$T_d = N \times \left( M \times T_{inner} + T_{overhead} \right)$$

**Step-by-step assembly derivation:**

```asm
        MOVS    R1, #N          ; outer counter
outer   MOVS    R2, #M          ; inner counter
inner   SUBS    R2, R2, #1      ; inner: R2--, set flags
        BNE     inner           ; repeat inner if R2 != 0
        SUBS    R1, R1, #1      ; outer: R1--, set flags
        BNE     outer           ; repeat outer if R1 != 0
```

**Cycle-counting derivation** (Cortex-M0 @ 48 MHz, 1 cycle = 1/48 µs):

$$T_{inner\_iter} = 1_{\text{SUBS}} + 1_{\text{BNE taken}} + 1_{\text{BNE not-taken}} = 3 \text{ cycles}$$

Wait — for the *last* iteration of the inner loop, the `BNE` is **not** taken (1 cycle). For the *first $M-1$* iterations, the `BNE` is **taken** (2 cycles each, plus a 1-cycle pipeline refill). Simplified average:

$$T_{inner} \approx 3M \text{ cycles}$$

$$T_{d} \approx 3 \times N \times M \text{ cycles}$$

**Example numerical derivation:** To generate a **1 ms delay** at 48 MHz:

$$N \times M = \frac{T_d \times f_{clk}}{3} = \frac{0.001 \times 48 \times 10^6}{3} = 16000$$

Choosing $N = 4, M = 4000$ yields $T_d = 3 \times 4 \times 4000 = 48000$ cycles $= 1.0$ ms. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control-Flow Graph — Generic Counted Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: MOVS R1, N] --> B{R1 == 0}
    B -- No, Z=0 --> C[Execute Loop Body]
    C --> D[SUBS R1, R1, 1]
    D --> E{Z flag set?}
    E -- No, Z=0 --> C
    E -- Yes, Z=1 --> F[Exit: Continue Main]
    B -- Yes, Z=1 --> F
    F --> G[End]
```

## 4.2 Nested Delay-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Init: MOVS R1, N outer] --> A1[outer label]
    A1 --> B0[Init: MOVS R2, M inner]
    B0 --> B1[inner label]
    B1 --> C[SUBS R2, R2, 1]
    C --> D{R2 == 0}
    D -- No --> B1
    D -- Yes --> E[SUBS R1, R1, 1]
    E --> F{R1 == 0}
    F -- No --> A1
    F -- Yes --> G[Exit Delay]
```

## 4.3 APSR Flag-Decision Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP[SUBS / CMP / ADDS instruction] --> ALU[ALU Result]
    ALU --> NFLAG[N flag: MSB of result]
    ALU --> ZFLAG[Z flag: result is zero]
    ALU --> CFLAG[C flag: unsigned borrow]
    ALU --> VFLAG[V flag: signed overflow]
    NFLAG --> BEQ[BEQ: branch if Z=1]
    ZFLAG --> BEQ
    ZFLAG --> BNE[BNE: branch if Z=0]
    CFLAG --> BCS[BCS: branch if C=1 unsigned greater equal]
    VFLAG --> BVS[BVS: branch if V=1 signed overflow]
    BEQ --> OUT[Modify PC]
    BNE --> OUT
    BCS --> OUT
    BVS --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Loop-Type Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PTOP[Pre-test While Loop]
        P1[Top Test] --> P2[Body]
        P2 --> P3{Branch back to P1 if true}
    end
    subgraph POST[Post-test Do-While Loop]
        Q1[Body] --> Q2[Bottom Test]
        Q2 --> Q3{Branch back to Q1 if true}
        Q3 -- exit false --> Q4[Continue]
    end
    subgraph COUNT[Counted For Loop]
        R1[Initialize counter] --> R2[Body]
        R2 --> R3[Update + test]
        R3 -- not done --> R2
        R3 -- done --> R4[Exit]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of the APSR flags in implementing an ARM assembly loop. Which flag is most commonly used for loop termination and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Application Program Status Register (APSR)** contains four condition flags — **N, Z, C, V** — that are automatically updated by any data-processing instruction with the `S` suffix (e.g., `SUBS`, `CMP`, `ADDS`).

- The **N (Negative) flag** indicates a negative result (MSB = 1).
- The **Z (Zero) flag** is set to 1 when the result of an operation is zero.
- The **C (Carry) flag** indicates no unsigned borrow in a subtraction.
- The **V (Overflow) flag** indicates a signed overflow.

The **Z flag is most commonly used for loop termination** because counted loops terminate when the counter reaches **zero**. A `SUBS R1, R1, #1` followed by `BNE` is the canonical pattern: when `R1` becomes 0, the `Z` flag is set, and `BNE` (Branch if Not Equal, i.e., Z = 0) is *not* taken, exiting the loop.

*(Marks: 1 – APSR description; 1 – flag roles; 1 – Z flag justification)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a pre-test loop and a post-test loop in ARM assembly, giving one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pre-test Loop (While) | Post-test Loop (Do-While) |
|---|---|---|
| **Test location** | At the **top**, before the body | At the **bottom**, after the body |
| **Minimum executions** | **Zero** (body may never run) | **One** (body always runs at least once) |
| **ARM pattern** | `B<cond> exit` placed *before* body | `B<cond> loop` placed *after* body |

**Example – Pre-test** (loop runs only if R0 > 0):

```asm
        CMP     R0, #0
        BLE     skip
loop    ...body...
        SUBS    R0, R0, #1
        BNE     loop
skip
```

**Example – Post-test** (body runs at least once):

```asm
loop    ...body...
        SUBS    R0, R0, #1
        BNE     loop
```

*(Marks: 1 – distinction; 1 – example pre-test; 1 – example post-test)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Write an ARM Cortex-M assembly program to compute the **sum of the first N natural numbers** stored in `R0` and `R1`, using a post-test counted loop. The value of **N is given in `R1`**, and the final sum must be stored back in `R0`. Include proper comments and the loop label. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step execution trace** of your program for **N = 4**, showing the contents of `R0` and `R1` after every instruction, and identify which APSR flag governs the branch decision. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

```asm
        AREA    SumNatural, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        MOV     R0, #0          ; R0 = accumulator S = 0
        MOV     R2, #1          ; R2 = current natural number k = 1
loop    ADD     R0, R0, R2      ; S = S + k
        ADD     R2, R2, #1      ; k = k + 1
        SUBS    R1, R1, #1      ; N--, update APSR
        BNE     loop            ; if Z=0 (N != 0), branch back
stop    B       stop            ; infinite loop to halt
        END
```

*Mark split: 2 marks for initialization, 2 marks for body, 2 marks for decrement/branch, 1 mark for proper labels & syntax.*

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Trace for N = 4** (initial: R0=0, R1=4, R2=1):

| Step | Instruction | R0 | R1 | R2 | Z flag | Branch? |
|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|:---:|
| 1 | `ADD R0,R0,R2` | 0+1 = **1** | 4 | 1 | 0 (not set by ADD) | — |
| 2 | `ADD R2,R2,#1` | 1 | 4 | **2** | 0 | — |
| 3 | `SUBS R1,R1,#1` | 1 | **3** | 2 | 0 | **Yes (BNE)** |
| 4 | `ADD R0,R0,R2` | 1+2 = **3** | 3 | 2 | 0 | — |
| 5 | `ADD R2,R2,#1` | 3 | 3 | **3** | 0 | — |
| 6 | `SUBS R1,R1,#1` | 3 | **2** | 3 | 0 | **Yes (BNE)** |
| 7 | `ADD R0,R0,R2` | 3+3 = **6** | 2 | 3 | 0 | — |
| 8 | `ADD R2,R2,#1` | 6 | 2 | **4** | 0 | — |
| 9 | `SUBS R1,R1,#1` | 6 | **1** | 4 | 0 | **Yes (BNE)** |
| 10 | `ADD R0,R0,R2` | 6+4 = **10** | 1 | 4 | 0 | — |
| 11 | `ADD R2,R2,#1` | 10 | 1 | **5** | 0 | — |
| 12 | `SUBS R1,R1,#1` | 10 | **0** | 5 | **1** | **No (exit)** |

**Final result:** $R_0 = 10 = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4$ ✓

**The branch is governed by the Z (Zero) flag** of the APSR. The `BNE` instruction is a conditional branch that is taken only when $Z = 0$ (i.e., the counter has not yet reached zero).

*Mark split: 1 mark for initial state, 4 marks for full trace, 1 mark for correct final answer, 1 mark for flag identification.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Design an ARM assembly program using a **nested delay loop** to generate an approximate **2 ms software delay** on a Cortex-M0 processor running at **24 MHz**. Clearly state the inner and outer counter values and justify your calculation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Modify the delay-loop program to also **toggle a GPIO pin (PA5)** at the end of every outer-loop iteration. Write the modified assembly code and explain how loop-based delays are used in real-time embedded systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Cycle budget derivation:**

$$N \times M = \frac{T_d \times f_{clk}}{3 \text{ cycles}} = \frac{0.002 \times 24 \times 10^6}{3} = 16000$$

Choosing $N = 200, M = 80$ gives $200 \times 80 = 16000$ ✓

**Assembly program:**

```asm
        LDR     R3, =RCC_BASE    ; (conceptual: enable GPIO clock)
        ...
        MOVS    R0, #200         ; outer counter N = 200
outer   MOVS    R1, #80          ; inner counter M = 80
inner   SUBS    R1, R1, #1       ; R1--, set flags
        BNE     inner            ; inner loop
        SUBS    R0, R0, #1       ; R0--, set flags
        BNE     outer            ; outer loop
```

*Mark split: 2 marks for cycle-budget derivation, 2 marks for correct N & M, 2 marks for proper assembly code, 1 mark for justification.*

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Modified assembly with GPIO toggle** (conceptual, using a memory-mapped register at address `0x40020014` for GPIOA->BSRR):

```asm
        LDR     R2, =GPIOA_BSRR  ; R2 = 0x40020014
        MOVS    R0, #200
outer   MOVS    R1, #80
inner   SUBS    R1, R1, #1
        BNE     inner
        ; ---- Toggle PA5 ----
        LDR     R3, [R2]         ; read current state (conceptual)
        EOR     R3, R3, #(1<<5)  ; toggle bit 5
        STR     R3, [R2]         ; write back
        SUBS    R0, R0, #1
        BNE     outer
```

**Real-time embedded use-cases** (explanation):

1. **Switch debouncing:** A mechanical switch "bounces" for ~5–10 ms. A delay loop reads the pin, waits 10 ms via a loop, and re-reads to confirm a stable state.
2. **Custom baud-rate generation:** Software UARTs use delay loops to manually time each bit period.
3. **LED blinking without timers:** In low-cost Cortex-M0 designs without a SysTick, a delay loop produces a visible blink frequency.
4. **ADC settling time:** Waiting a few µs after channel-select before sampling.
5. **LCD/display enable pulses:** Generating precise $\geq$ **100 ns** enable strobes.

*Mark split: 2 marks for GPIO modification, 2 marks for complete code, 2 marks for real-time use-cases, 1 mark for proper comments/structure.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `S` suffix on `SUBS`/`CMP`** — without it, flags are *not* updated, and the branch never fires correctly. **−2 marks** if missed.
> 2. **Using `MOV` instead of `MOVS` for counter initialization** when the next instruction is a compare — this fails to pre-set Z. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing `BEQ` and `BNE`** — always dry-trace once: if loop must exit when counter = 0, the decrement gives Z=1, so the branch must be `BNE` (taken when Z=0).
> 4. **Off-by-one in delay calculations** — students forget to include the *non-taken* branch cycle in the last iteration. Always use `3 cycles` per inner iteration, not 2.
> 5. **No `stop: B stop` halt label** at the end of a Keil/uVision program — this leads to a "hard fault" or unintended execution past `END`. **−1 mark** in lab exams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **loop** in ARM assembly is built from **flag-setting arithmetic** (`SUBS`/`CMP`/`ADDS`) + a **conditional branch** (`B<cond>`).
- ✅ The **Z (Zero) flag** is the most frequently used flag for loop termination.
- ✅ The canonical **counted-loop template** is: `MOVS counter` → `[body]` → `SUBS counter, #1` → `BNE label`.
- ✅ **Pre-test loops** (while) place the branch at the top; body may execute **zero** times.
- ✅ **Post-test loops** (do-while) place the branch at the bottom; body executes **at least once**.
- ✅ Thumb-2 `B<cond>` range is **$\pm$2048 bytes**; for larger jumps the assembler auto-emits a 32-bit `B.W`.
- ✅ **Delay loops** are nested counter loops used for software timing; cycle budget = $3 \times N \times M$ on Cortex-M0.
- ✅ Always include the `S` suffix on instructions that must update APSR flags.
- ✅ The **APSR flags** are: **N** (Negative), **Z** (Zero), **C** (Carry/no-borrow), **V** (oVerflow).
- ✅ Mnemonic: *"BEQ when equal, BNE when not-equal, BGT for signed greater-than, BCS/BHS for unsigned $\geq$."*
- ✅ AAPCS reserves `R0–R3` for arguments/return values; **don't** store loop counters in `R0` if calling subroutines.
- ✅ Always end Keil/uVision programs with a `stop: B stop` infinite loop to prevent code fall-through.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests: flag identification, loop trace tables, delay-loop cycle math, and post-test vs. pre-test code identification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speeding Up Array Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speeding Up Array Access

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Speeding Up Array Access in ARM Architecture

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Array access speeding-up** in ARM refers to a set of architectural techniques — flexible *indexed addressing modes*, *auto-indexing (writeback)*, *barrel-shifter based offset generation*, and *block transfer instructions (LDM/STM)* — that minimize instruction count, reduce clock cycles per element, and exploit pipelining to accelerate sequential memory traversal of arrays, records, and stack frames.

In ARM7/ARM9 classical RISC terminology (Sloss, Symes & Wright), these mechanisms are collectively the **indexing strategies** for memory access instructions of the form:

$$\text{LDR} \mid \text{STR} \quad Rd, \quad [\text{addressing mode expression}]$$

where the bracketed expression governs how the Effective Address (EA) is computed and whether the base register is updated.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The unit explicitly focuses on *indexed addressing modes* and *block data transfer* as the two pillars of fast array traversal. Mastering the pre/post-indexed variants and the `!` writeback flag is mandatory for Part A & Part B questions.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Postman and the Mailbox
Imagine a postman delivering letters to houses on a long street (the **array** in memory). 

- **Naive approach (plain indexed)**: The postman is given a *separate piece of paper* each time with the next house number written on it. He reads it, walks there, and only then looks at the next paper. Two operations per house.
- **Auto-indexed (writeback) approach**: The postman has a *counter on his wrist* that automatically ticks to the next house after each delivery. He delivers AND updates in *one motion*. One combined operation.
- **Block transfer (LDM/STM) approach**: A courier van pulls up, and a helper loads/unloads **16 letters at once** into 16 mailboxes in a single trip.

That is precisely the *philosophy* of ARM's address-mode 3 (post-indexed with writeback) and the LDM/STM multiple-register transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Performance Levers in ARM Array Access**
> 1. **Addressing mode** — controls EA computation cost.
> 2. **Writeback (`!`)** — eliminates the separate `ADD` instruction that would otherwise update the pointer.
> 3. **Block transfer (LDM/STM)** — amortizes instruction fetch/decode overhead over many elements.

---

## 2. ARM Addressing Mode Family — Quick Reference

ARM provides **nine** addressing modes, but for array access only Modes 2, 3, 4, and 9 are practically used (Mode 9 for PC-relative is the LDR literal).

| Mode | Syntax | Effective Address | Writeback? | Typical Array Use |
|------|--------|-------------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | `[Rn]` | EA = Rn | No | Pointer dereference |
| 2 | `[Rn, #±offset]` | EA = Rn ± offset | No | Random access |
| 3 | `[Rn, #±offset]!` | EA = Rn ± offset | **Yes (Rn = EA)** | Sequential forward/reverse |
| 4 | `[Rn], #±offset` | EA = Rn, then Rn = EA | **Yes (post-access)** | Loop-friendly traversal |
| 5 | `[Rn, Rm]` | EA = Rn + Rm | No | Index by register |
| 6 | `[Rn, Rm, shift]` | EA = Rn + shifted Rm | No | Strided array access |
| 7 | `[Rn, Rm, shift]!` | EA = Rn + shifted Rm | **Yes** | Strided with auto-advance |
| 8 | `[Rn], Rm{, shift}` | EA = Rn, then Rn = EA | **Yes** | Strided loop traversal |
| 9 | `[Rn, #±offset]` (PC) | EA = PC ± offset | No | Literal pool |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Three Fast-Access Mechanisms

### Mechanism 1 — Pre-Indexed with Writeback (Mode 3)
$$\text{LDR} \;\; Rd, \;\; [Rn, \#\text{offset}]\;!$$

**Step-by-step micro-operation:**
1. **Fetch** the instruction.
2. **Compute** EA = Rn + offset *in the EX stage* using the barrel shifter (single cycle, no separate ADD).
3. **Issue** memory read at EA.
4. **Write-back** EA into Rn (`!` flag set).
5. **Write** loaded data into Rd (interlock if Rd = Rn).

**Why it speeds up array access:** The pointer increment is **fused** with the load — the fetch of `Rn` for the *next* iteration is already updated by the time the loop branches back. No pipeline bubble introduced by a separate `ADD Rn, Rn, #4`.

### Mechanism 2 — Post-Indexed Addressing (Mode 4)
$$\text{LDR} \;\; Rd, \;\; [Rn], \;\;\#\text{offset}$$

**Step-by-step micro-operation:**
1. **Compute** EA = Rn (no shift needed for `[Rn]`).
2. **Issue** memory read at EA.
3. **Update** Rn ← Rn + offset (writeback in EX stage).
4. **Write** data to Rd.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-indexed (Mode 4) is the canonical "array-walk" idiom in ARM assembly.** It is the single most-tested concept in KTU Module 3 Part A questions because it produces the cleanest, branch-predictor-friendly loop.

### Mechanism 3 — Block Transfer: LDM / STM
LDM/STM transfers **1 to 16 registers** to/from consecutive memory locations in **one instruction**, dramatically reducing fetch-decode bandwidth pressure.

$$\text{LDMIA} \;\; R0, \;\; \{R1-R4, R6, R8\}$$

Direction suffixes:
- **IA** — Increment After (lowest address first, post-increment base)
- **IB** — Increment Before
- **DA** — Decrement After
- **DB** — Decrement Before

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Effective Address (pre-indexed) | $EA = R_n \pm \text{offset}$ | bytes | 12-bit unsigned offset (0–4095) |
| 2 | Effective Address (post-indexed) | $EA = R_n \; ; \; R_n \leftarrow R_n \pm \text{offset}$ | bytes | Two-stage, single instruction |
| 3 | Register offset with shift | $EA = R_n + (R_m \;\text{shift}\;\#k)$ | bytes | shift ∈ {LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX} |
| 4 | Stride access loop | $R_n \leftarrow R_n + (S \cdot 4)$ where S = stride | bytes | Used for 2-D / sparse arrays |
| 5 | Block transfer address progression (LDMIA) | $EA_k = R_{base} + 4k$, $k=0,1,\ldots,n-1$ | bytes | 4 bytes per register |
| 6 | Instruction count for N-element copy (naive) | $I_{naive} = 2N$ | instr | one LDR + one ADD per element |
| 7 | Instruction count with auto-index (Mode 4) | $I_{auto} = N$ | instr | 1 fused instruction per element |
| 8 | Block transfer for N elements | $I_{blk} = \lceil N / r \rceil$ where r = regs used | instr | $r \in [1,16]$ |
| 9 | Speedup factor (naive vs auto-index) | $S = I_{naive} / I_{auto} = 2$ | – | Halves dynamic instr count |
| 10 | Total cycles (incl. fetch/decode) | $C = I \cdot CPI_{avg} + N_{mem} \cdot (h \cdot P_{miss})$ | cycles | $h$ = miss rate, $P_{miss}$ = penalty |
| 11 | Time to traverse N elements | $T = N \cdot t_{mem} + I \cdot t_{core}$ | seconds | $t_{mem}$ depends on cache hit |
| 12 | Array base alignment requirement | base $\equiv 0 \pmod{4}$ | – | Word alignment; 8 for double-word |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DSP / FIR Filters:** Real-time convolution loops over sample arrays rely on post-indexed addressing with register-offset shifts for circular buffer pointer wrap-around (`STMFD`/`LDMFD` for register save/restore on IRQ entry).
- **Embedded Protocol Stacks:** TCP/IP packet parsers use block transfers to copy headers in one shot.
- **Memory-to-Memory DMA in Firmware:** Non-DMA microcontrollers use LDM/STM as the "poor man's DMA" for LCD framebuffer blits.
- **OS Context Switch:** The standard ARM procedure-call standard AAPCS uses `STMFD`/`LDMFD` to push/pop the register file on function entry and exception entry.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern multi-issue Cortex-A processors, LDM/STM is decomposed into micro-ops (µops) and the raw speedup is less dramatic, but on **ARM7TDMI** and **Cortex-M** cores (the bread-and-butter of KTU labs) it remains a primary optimization lever.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speedup of Auto-Indexing Over Manual Pointer Update

Consider a C loop compiled naively vs. the ARM-optimized version.

**C source:**
```c
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) sum += A[i];
```

**Naive ARM translation (no writeback):**
```asm
    MOV   R0, #A_BASE      ; R0 = pointer (auto-incremented below)
    MOV   R1, #N           ; R1 = loop counter
    MOV   R2, #0           ; R2 = sum accumulator
LOOP LDR   R3, [R0]        ; EA = R0
    ADD   R2, R2, R3       ; accumulate
    ADD   R0, R0, #4       ; <-- EXTRA INSTRUCTION to bump pointer
    SUBS  R1, R1, #1       ; decrement counter
    BNE   LOOP             ; branch back
```

**Instruction count per iteration:** $I_{naive} = 4$ core instructions (plus the BNE's partial cost).

**Auto-indexed ARM translation (post-indexed, Mode 4):**
```asm
    MOV   R0, #A_BASE
    MOV   R1, #N
    MOV   R2, #0
LOOP LDR   R3, [R0], #4    ; FUSED: read then increment R0
    ADD   R2, R2, R3
    SUBS  R1, R1, #1
    BNE   LOOP
```

**Instruction count per iteration:** $I_{auto} = 3$ core instructions.

The speedup ratio is therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{instr}} &= \frac{I_{naive}}{I_{auto}} \\[6pt]
                  &= \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

If the loop counter update and branch are kept identical in both versions, the *meaningful* per-iteration savings come from the elimination of the `ADD R0, R0, #4`, i.e. a **1-instruction / 25% reduction** in the data-handling instructions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Block Transfer Amortization

Suppose we copy a 256-byte (64-word) array from `src` to `dst`. The ARM7TDMI core has a fetch/decode bandwidth of 1 instruction per cycle, but memory can serve 1 word per cycle when pipelined.

**Naive word-by-word approach:**
$$
I_{naive} = 2N = 2(64) = 128 \text{ instructions}
$$

**LDM/STM with 8 registers per transfer:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Transfers needed} &= \frac{N}{r} = \frac{64}{8} = 8 \\
I_{blk} &= 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ instructions (one LDM, one STM per group)} \\[6pt]
S_{blk} &= \frac{128}{16} = 8\times
\end{aligned}
$$

**With 16 registers per transfer (full bank, the maximum):**
$$
I_{blk16} = 2 \times \frac{64}{16} = 8 \text{ instructions} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_{blk16} = 16\times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `S` (speedup) here is **instruction-fetch bandwidth recovery**, not raw execution time. On a single-issue core with a stall on data dependency, the execution-time speedup is approximately $S \cdot \alpha$ where $\alpha$ is the average cycles saved per fused operation (typically 0.3–0.5 in pipelined cores).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Strided Array Access Using Register-Offset Shifts

For a 2-D array `B[M][N]` accessed row-by-row, the inner-loop address progression is:
$$
EA_{i+1} = EA_i + 4 \quad (\text{word stride})
$$

This is handled by `LDR Rd, [Rn, #4]!` (pre-indexed with writeback). For a *non-unit* stride $S$ (e.g. accessing every 3rd element of a 1-D array), use register-offset:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-indexed:} \quad & EA = R_n + (R_m \;\text{LSL}\;\#k), \quad R_n \leftarrow EA \\
\text{Post-indexed:} \quad & EA = R_n, \quad R_n \leftarrow R_n + (R_m \;\text{LSL}\;\#k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example — Sum every 3rd element of an array of 32-bit words:**

```asm
    LDR   R0, =A_BASE      ; R0 = pointer
    MOV   R1, #N/3         ; loop count
    MOV   R2, #0           ; sum
    MOV   R3, #12          ; R3 = stride * 4 = 3*4
LOOP LDR   R4, [R0], R3    ; Post-indexed: read then R0 = R0 + 12
    ADD   R2, R2, R4
    SUBS  R1, R1, #1
    BNE   LOOP
```

The barrel shifter computes `R3` shifted left by 0 (i.e. R3 itself, value 12) in **one cycle**, for free. A non-ARM ISA would need a separate `MUL` or `LSL` instruction.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Mechanism | Instructions Saved vs. Naive | Extra Hardware Used | Cache Friendliness | Pipeline Hazard Risk |
|-----------|------------------------------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Pre-index (Mode 2) | 0 | None | High (predictable) | None |
| Pre-index + WB (Mode 3) | 1 / element | Writeback path | High | Low (Rd = Rn interlock) |
| **Post-index (Mode 4)** | **1 / element** | Writeback path | **High** | **Low** (cleanest) |
| Register offset (Modes 5,6) | 1 / element + 1 shift | Barrel shifter | High | Medium if Rm is loop-carried |
| LDM/STM block | $N-1$ per block | Multiple-bus memory | High (spatial locality) | Register-file read/write port contention |
| LDM/STM `!` writeback | +1 / block | Writeback path | High | Same as single-transfer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Comparative Flow of Three Array-Traversal Idioms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Base address in Rn, N in Rcount, sum=0] --> B{Pick Strategy}
    B -- Naive --> C[NaiveLoop: LDR Rd, [Rn]]
    C --> D[ADD sum, sum, Rd]
    D --> E[ADD Rn, Rn, #4]
    E --> F[SUBS Rcount, Rcount, #1]
    F --> G{Rcount==0?}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> Z[Exit: sum in sum-reg]

    B -- PostIndexed --> H[PostLoop: LDR Rd, [Rn], #4]
    H --> I[ADD sum, sum, Rd]
    I --> J[SUBS Rcount, Rcount, #1]
    J --> K{Rcount==0?}
    K -- No --> H
    K -- Yes --> Z

    B -- BlockLDM --> L[BlockLoop: LDMIA Rn!, {R1-R4}]
    L --> M[Process 4 words in regs]
    M --> N[SUBS Rcount, Rcount, #4]
    N --> O{Rcount==0?}
    O -- No --> L
    O -- Yes --> Z

    classDef opt fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#3c763d,color:#1b4d1b
    classDef sub fill:#fcf8e3,stroke:#8a6d3b,color:#5a4a1b
    classDef exit fill:#f2dede,stroke:#a94442,color:#5a1a1a
    class H,I,J,K opt
    class L,M,N,O sub
    class Z exit
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ARM LDMIA Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Instruction LDMIA Rn, list] --> B0[Fetch/Decode]
    B0 --> C0[Base reg Rn -> Address Gen 0]
    C0 --> D0[EA0 = Rn + 0]
    B0 --> C1[Address Gen 1]
    C1 --> D1[EA1 = Rn + 4]
    B0 --> C2[Address Gen 2]
    C2 --> D2[EA2 = Rn + 8]
    B0 --> C3[Address Gen 3]
    C3 --> D3[EA3 = Rn + 12]
    D0 --> E0[Mem read EA0]
    D1 --> E1[Mem read EA1]
    D2 --> E2[Mem read EA2]
    D3 --> E3[Mem read EA3]
    E0 --> F0[Reg0 in list <- data]
    E1 --> F1[Reg1 in list <- data]
    E2 --> F2[Reg2 in list <- data]
    E3 --> F3[Reg3 in list <- data]
    B0 --> G0[Writeback: Rn <- Rn + 16]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Addressing Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Need to access array element?] --> Q2{Pointer changes during access?}
    Q2 -- No --> A1[Use Mode 2: LDR Rd, [Rn, #off]]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Stride is a constant?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Update pointer BEFORE or AFTER read?}
    Q4 -- After --> A2[Mode 4: LDR Rd, [Rn], #off]
    Q4 -- Before --> A3[Mode 3: LDR Rd, [Rn, #off]!]
    Q3 -- No, variable stride --> A4[Mode 5/6/8: register offset]
    Q1 --> Q5{Moving many contiguous words?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> A5[LDMIA / STMIA with WB]
    Q5 -- No --> A1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
Differentiate between pre-indexed and post-indexed addressing modes in ARM. How does each affect the base register?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pre-Indexed `[Rn, #off]!` | Post-Indexed `[Rn], #off` |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Order of operations | Update pointer **first**, then access memory | Access memory **first**, then update pointer |
| Effective address | $EA = R_n \pm \text{off}$ | $EA = R_n$ |
| Base register after | Holds the new offset address | Holds the new offset address |
| Use case | Pre-computed stride in a tight loop | Idiomatic "load-and-walk" pattern |

Both update the base register because of the `!` writeback flag (or implicit writeback in Mode 4). **(1 mark)** Pre-indexed computes the new address, accesses it, and stores the new address in `Rn` — useful when the next iteration must use the *offset* address **(1 mark)**. Post-indexed accesses `Rn` *as-is*, then adds the offset to `Rn` for the next iteration — cleaner for forward sequential walks **(1 mark)**.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Sessional II]**
What is the role of the barrel shifter in ARM array addressing? Give an example instruction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The barrel shifter is a **dedicated hardware unit** that performs an arbitrary shift or rotate on a 32-bit operand in **one clock cycle**, *for free* — i.e. without consuming an extra instruction. **(1 mark)** In array addressing it allows the offset operand `Rm` to be shifted on-the-fly, enabling **strided access patterns** (e.g. stepping through a 2-D array row) without a separate multiply or shift instruction. **(1 mark)**

**Example:**
```asm
LDR   R4, [R0, R1, LSL #2]   ; EA = R0 + 4*R1
```
This loads the array element at index `R1` (with each element being 4 bytes wide) — the `LSL #2` is performed by the barrel shifter alongside the address generation, in a single cycle. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Auto-Indexing and Loop Optimization

#### (a) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** — 7 Marks
Write an ARM assembly program to compute the sum of a 32-element word array `A` using **post-indexed addressing**. Show the optimized code and explain how auto-indexing reduces the instruction count compared to a naive loop.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

```asm
        AREA    SumArray, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        LDR     R0, =A_BASE      ; R0 = pointer to array start  (1 mark)
        MOV     R1, #32          ; R1 = element count
        MOV     R2, #0           ; R2 = running sum (accumulator)
LOOP    LDR     R3, [R0], #4     ; Post-indexed: read, then R0 += 4   (2 marks)
        ADD     R2, R2, R3       ; sum += element
        SUBS    R1, R1, #1       ; decrement count, set flags
        BNE     LOOP             ; branch if not done               (1 mark)
        ; ---- sum is now in R2 ----
STOP    B       STOP
        END
```

**Explanation of speedup (4 marks):**
- A naive loop requires **two** memory-related instructions per iteration: `LDR Rd, [Rn]` followed by `ADD Rn, Rn, #4` to advance the pointer. The auto-indexed `LDR Rd, [Rn], #4` **fuses** both into one instruction, saving one fetch, one decode, and one execute per iteration. **(2 marks)**
- Over 32 iterations, the optimized loop saves **32 instructions** out of the original 128 (4 instr × 32) — a **25% reduction** in the dynamic instruction count. **(1 mark)**
- Additionally, the pointer update happens in the **EX stage of the same instruction** that performs the load, so there is no pipeline bubble between the load and the next iteration's address generation. **(1 mark)**

#### (b) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — 7 Marks
Explain the use of **pre-indexed addressing with writeback (`!`)** for traversing an array in *reverse order*. Provide the instruction sequence for the first three elements of a 4-element array starting at address `0x40000000`.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

In reverse traversal, the pointer must *decrement* by 4 bytes (one word) per step. Pre-indexed with writeback is ideal because the offset is applied *before* the access, and `!` ensures the new (lower) address is stored back in `Rn` ready for the next loop iteration. **(1 mark)**

Instruction pattern (offset = `#-4`, written as `-4` or as a negative literal):

```asm
        LDR   R0, =0x40000010    ; R0 = &A[3] (top of 4-word array)  (1 mark)
        MOV   R1, #3             ; 3 more elements to fetch
        MOV   R2, #0             ; accumulator
LOOP    LDR   R3, [R0, #-4]!    ; R0 = R0-4 first, then load        (3 marks)
        ADD   R2, R2, R3
        SUBS  R1, R1, #1
        BNE   LOOP
```

**Walk-through of the first three iterations (2 marks):**
| Iteration | R0 (before) | EA = R0-4 | R0 (after, via `!`) | Loaded value |
|-----------|------------|-----------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1 | 0x40000010 | 0x4000000C | 0x4000000C | A[2] |
| 2 | 0x4000000C | 0x40000008 | 0x40000008 | A[1] |
| 3 | 0x40000008 | 0x40000004 | 0x40000004 | A[0] |

The `!` writeback flag is **mandatory** here — without it, R0 would never decrement, and the loop would load A[3] three times.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write `[R0, #-4]` *without* the `!`, expecting the assembler to add the writeback automatically. It will **not**. The `!` is the only signal to the assembler to update `Rn`. A common loss is **2 marks** for missing `!`. Equally, students often forget that pre-indexing **replaces** the base register with the offset address, so the *initial* pointer must be set to *one past* the first element to be fetched.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Block Transfer (LDM/STM) and Register-Offset Stride

#### (a) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** — 7 Marks
Compare **LDM/STM block transfers** with single-register LDR/STR for copying a 64-word array. Show assembly code for both approaches and compute the **instruction-count speedup** when 8 registers are used per block.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Approach 1 — Naive word-by-word copy (1 mark):**
```asm
        LDR   R4, =SRC
        LDR   R5, =DST
        MOV   R6, #64
LOOP1   LDR   R0, [R4], #4
        STR   R0, [R5], #4
        SUBS  R6, R6, #1
        BNE   LOOP1
```
Instruction count: $2 \times 64 = 128$ body instructions (plus 2 setup + 1 SUBS + 1 BNE = 130 total but per-element body = 2). **(1 mark)**

**Approach 2 — Block transfer with 8 registers (2 marks):**
```asm
        LDR   R4, =SRC
        LDR   R5, =DST
        MOV   R6, #8            ; 8 blocks of 8 words
LOOP2   LDMIA R4!, {R0-R7}      ; 8-word block read
        STMIA R5!, {R0-R7}      ; 8-word block write
        SUBS  R6, R6, #1
        BNE   LOOP2
```
Instruction count: $2 \times 8 = 16$ body instructions. **(1 mark)**

**Speedup calculation (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{naive} &= 2N = 128 \\
I_{block} &= 2 \times \frac{N}{r} = 2 \times \frac{64}{8} = 16 \\[4pt]
S &= \frac{128}{16} = 8\times
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this is fast in practice (extra credit, often asked as follow-up):**
The LDM instruction issues **r memory reads in parallel** through the ARM core's dual-bus (or triple-bus) memory interface. The fetch bandwidth saved (1 instruction = 1 word per cycle freed) is then available for the **prefetch queue** to keep the pipeline full during long copies. **(0 marks if not asked, 1 mark as bonus)**

#### (b) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — 7 Marks
Using a **register-offset addressing mode**, write an ARM assembly fragment to read every 4th element of a word array `A` of size 16 into registers `R4`–`R7`. The base pointer is in `R0`. Show the address calculation for each access.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Stride = $4 \text{ elements} \times 4 \text{ bytes/element} = 16 \text{ bytes}$. This is implemented by shifting `Rm` (a register holding the index) left by `#2` to multiply by 4, *or* by using a pre-loaded `Rm = 16` and shifting by 0. **(1 mark)**

**Two valid implementations:**

**Option 1 — Using `Rm` with shift in the offset field (3 marks):**
```asm
        MOV   R1, #0            ; index = 0
        MOV   R2, #4            ; loop count
        LDR   R3, =16           ; R3 = stride in bytes
LOOP    LDR   R4, [R0, R1, LSL #2]  ; EA = R0 + 4*R1
        ; ... process R4 ...
        ADD   R1, R1, #4        ; index += 4 (next 4th element)
        SUBS  R2, R2, #1
        BNE   LOOP
```
**Address calculations (1 mark):**
| Iter | R1 | EA = R0 + 4·R1 | Element read |
|------|----|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | 0  | R0 + 0        | A[0] |
| 2 | 4  | R0 + 16       | A[4] |
| 3 | 8  | R0 + 32       | A[8] |
| 4 | 12 | R0 + 48       | A[12] |

**Option 2 — Pre-loaded stride register with writeback (3 marks):**
```asm
        LDR   R3, =16
        MOV   R2, #4
LOOP    LDR   R4, [R0], R3      ; Post-indexed, R0 += 16 after each read
        SUBS  R2, R2, #1
        BNE   LOOP
```

The `LSL #2` in Option 1 is performed **for free** by the barrel shifter; Option 2 avoids even that by storing the *byte-stride* directly in `R3`. **(1 mark for the comparison)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Part B):**
> 1. **Forgetting the `!` writeback** in pre-indexed mode — costs up to **2 marks** because the loop becomes infinite or incorrect.
> 2. **Confusing IA (Increment After) with IB (Increment Before)** for stack operations — IA is the *only* correct choice for the standard ARM AAPCS full-descending stack; IB will corrupt the stack frame.
> 3. **Mis-aligning the base address** for word access — LDR/STR word requires a word-aligned address (`base mod 4 == 0`); using a misaligned address triggers a *data abort* exception on ARM7TDMI.
> 4. **Counting the wrong number of elements** in a block-transfer loop — the loop counter must decrement by the *block size*, not by 1.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Speeding Up Array Access"**

- **Three fast mechanisms**: (1) Pre-indexed with `!` writeback, (2) Post-indexed, (3) LDM/STM block transfer. **(★★★★★ exam weightage)**
- **Post-indexed syntax**: `LDR Rd, [Rn], #offset` — *read first, update Rn second*. The canonical array-walk idiom.
- **Pre-indexed syntax**: `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]!` — *update Rn first, then read*. The `!` is what makes it auto-incrementing.
- **Writeback flag `!` is independent of indexing direction** — its presence is the *only* thing that causes the base register to be updated.
- **Block transfer direction suffixes**: `IA` (Increment After), `IB`, `DA`, `DB`. For AAPCS stacks, use `FD` (Full Descending) → `STMFD` / `LDMFD`.
- **Barrel shifter** is a *zero-cycle-cost* hardware unit; use `LDR Rd, [Rn, Rm, LSL #k]` for variable-stride / 2-D array access.
- **Instruction count speedup** for auto-indexing vs. naive loop: from 4 to 3 instructions/element ≈ **1.33×**; for LDM/STM with 8 registers vs. naive: **8×**; with 16 registers: **16×**.
- **Alignment rule**: word LDR/STR requires 4-byte alignment; double-word LDR/STR requires 8-byte alignment. Misalignment → Data Abort on classic ARM.
- **Post-indexed for forward walk, pre-indexed-with-`!` for reverse walk** (or for pre-computed strides).
- **LDM/STM register list** can be any subset of R0–R15, with a minimum of 1 and maximum of 16 registers, encoded in a 16-bit bitmap.
- **Cycle accounting**: auto-indexing saves *one fetch + one decode + one execute* per element but does **not** save the actual memory access time (cache/memory hierarchy dominates for large arrays).
- **KTU-typical Part-A trap**: "What is the effective address in `LDR R0, [R1, R2, LSL #2]!`?" — answer: $EA = R_1 + 4R_2$, and $R_1$ is updated to $EA$.
- **KTU-typical Part-B trap**: writing `LDR R0, [R1], #4` and asking whether R0 is updated — **no, R1 is updated**, not R0. R0 receives the *loaded data*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Functions: Function Call and Return
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Implementing Functions: Function Call and Return ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **ARM Architecture**, a *function* (also termed a *procedure* or *subroutine*) is a self-contained, named block of instructions that performs a specific, well-defined task. **Function Call and Return** refers to the hardware-and-software supported mechanism by which the **Program Counter (PC / R15)** is redirected to the entry point of a target function (the *call*), a return address is preserved so execution can resume correctly (the *link*), and control is subsequently transferred back to the instruction following the call site (the *return*). This entire contract is governed by the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)**, which prescribes how registers, the stack, and parameter/return-value passing are managed across function boundaries. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — AAPCS Compliance** > The AAPCS is *not* optional in KTU exam questions. Any function you write or analyze in the exam MUST be AAPCS-compliant, or marks will be deducted. AAPCS fixes: > - R0–R3: **Caller-saved / Scratch** registers (used to pass the first four 32-bit arguments and the return value in R0) > - R4–R11: **Callee-saved** registers (must be preserved by the called function) > - R12: **Intra-procedure-call scratch register (IP)** > - R13: **Stack Pointer (SP)** > - R14: **Link Register (LR)** — holds the return address > - R15: **Program Counter (PC)** ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are cooking dinner (**the main program / `main`**) and your mother asks you to bring salt from the kitchen shelf (**a function call to `getSalt`**). What happens? 1. **The Call (`BL getSalt`)** — You *stop* what you are doing and *go to* the kitchen. The instruction you are currently executing gets frozen in time. 2. **The Link Register (`LR = return address`)** — Before you walk away, you *write down on your hand* the exact step of the recipe you were on. This is your **return address**. 3. **Stack Frame (`PUSH {R4-R11, LR}`)** — You also put down your *recipe cards* (local variables and any values you were using) on the counter. They are now safe on the **stack**. 4. **The Work (`getSalt` body)** — You grab the salt, and if `getSalt` itself needs the pepper (`nested call`), you again write down its own return address on *its* hand. This is *recursion* and *nesting*. 5. **The Return (`BX LR` or `POP {..., PC}`)** — You walk back to the stove, read your hand, and *continue exactly where you left off*. The counter (stack) is cleared. > [!NOTE] > **Why a Link Register instead of saving to memory directly?** > The LR is a dedicated, always-on **CPU register (R14)**. Storing the return address in a register is **one CPU cycle** instead of a multi-cycle memory write, making function calls extremely fast — a defining efficiency feature of RISC architectures like ARM. ### Key Hardware/Software Entities (in **bold**) - **Link Register (LR / R14)** — Hardware register holding the return address. - **Stack Pointer (SP / R13)** — Points to the top of the active stack frame in memory. - **Program Counter (PC / R15)** — The address of the next instruction to execute; reading PC returns `current + 8` in ARM state due to pipeline. - **Branch with Link (`BL`)** — The ARM instruction that performs the call. - **Branch and Exchange (`BX`)** — Returns and optionally switches instruction set state (ARM ↔ Thumb). - **Stack Frame** — The region of memory a function allocates on the stack for its locals and saved registers. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Phases of a Function Call #### Phase 1 — The CALL (Caller Side) The caller (e.g., `main`) prepares arguments and jumps to the function. **Step 1: Place arguments in R0–R3** (first four 32-bit values; rest go on stack per AAPCS). **Step 2: Execute `BL func`** — This is a single ARM instruction that does two things atomically: $$ \text{LR} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 4 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{Target Address} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Pipeline Note (Board Favorite!)** — In ARM state, the PC reads as `current + 8` (because of the 2-stage fetch/decode pipeline). Hence `BL` stores `PC + 4` (the address of the *next* instruction after the branch delay slot's effective address), which is the return address the callee must use. **Step 3: Continue execution at the callee entry label.** #### Phase 2 — The PROLOGUE (Callee Side) The called function sets up its own safe working environment. **Step 1: `PUSH {R4-R11, LR}`** — Saves all callee-saved registers AND the return address on the stack. This is the **stack frame creation**. **Step 2: `SUB SP, SP, #N`** — Allocates space for local variables (N bytes). **Step 3: Optionally set up `FP` (R7 in ARM ABI, R11 in older code)** as a frame pointer for debug-friendly stack walking. #### Phase 3 — The EPILOGUE & RETURN (Callee Side) **Step 1: Place return value in R0** (and R1 for 64-bit values). **Step 2: `ADD SP, SP, #N`** — De-allocates local variable space. **Step 3: `POP {R4-R11, PC}`** — The magic instruction. `PC` (not `LR`) is in the register list, so the popped value of LR is loaded *directly into PC*, executing the return in a single cycle. This is the canonical ARM idiom for returning. > [!IMPORTANT] > **`POP {..., PC}` vs `BX LR`** — Both work, but `POP {..., PC}` is preferred because: > - It restores the frame **AND** returns **in one instruction**. > - It is safe across nested calls (the original LR was already saved on the stack in the prologue). > - `BX LR` is used when the function does NOT need to save LR (i.e., it makes no further calls — a *leaf function*). ### 2.2 AAPCS Register Usage Contract (High-Yield Table) | Register | AAPCS Role | Preserved By | Purpose in Function Call/Return | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | **R0** | a1 / Result | Caller | **First argument** OR **32-bit return value** | | **R1** | a2 | Caller | Second argument | | **R2** | a3 | Caller | Third argument | | **R3** | a4 | Caller | Fourth argument | | **R4–R8** | v1–v5 | **Callee** | Variable registers that *must* be saved on stack by callee | | **R9** | v6 / SB | **Callee** | Platform register (often Static Base) | | **R10** | v7 / SL | **Callee** | Stack Limit / data limit | | **R11** | v8 / FP | **Callee** | Frame Pointer (in some ABI variants) | | **R12** | IP | Caller | Intra-Procedure scratch (used by linkers/veneers) | | **R13** | **SP** | **Callee** | Stack Pointer — must be restored exactly | | **R14** | **LR** | Caller (logical) | Link Register — return address of *current* call | | **R15** | **PC** | — | Program Counter | > [!NOTE] > **Convention Pitfall (Common KTU Mistake):** "Preserved by Callee" means if `func` modifies R4, it MUST save the *original* value first. If it does not modify R4, no save is required. The caller assumes R4 is untouched *after the call returns*. ### 2.3 Leaf vs Non-Leaf Functions A **leaf function** does not call any other function. It does **not** need to save LR on the stack — it can simply return via `BX LR`. A **non-leaf function** calls at least one other function. Since `BL` overwrites LR, the function MUST save its incoming LR onto the stack in the prologue (typically via `PUSH {..., LR}`) and restore it via `POP {..., PC}` in the epilogue. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler code generation** (GCC's `-mapcs`/`-mabi=aapcs`): Every C/C++ function call is lowered to exactly this sequence. - **RTOS context switching**: The context-save code in an interrupt service routine (ISR) uses the same `PUSH/POP` mechanism to save LR_svc or LR_irq. - **Reverse engineering / binary analysis**: Recognizing `PUSH {R4-R11, LR}` followed by `POP {R4-R11, PC}` is the signature of a standard ARM function in IDA/Ghidra. - **Secure boot / firmware**: Understanding LR manipulation is critical to detecting **Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)** attacks. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Return Address Computation in `BL` We derive the precise return address stored in LR for an ARM `BL` instruction at memory address `A`. **Given:** - ARM7/ARM9/ARM Cortex-A classic state pipeline: Fetch → Decode → Execute. - At any moment, when the CPU executes an instruction at address `A`, the PC has *already* been advanced to fetch the next instruction. - The ARM architecture spec states: **In ARM state, reading R15 (PC) returns the address of the current instruction + 8 bytes.** **Step-by-Step Logic:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let the BL instruction be at address } & A. \\ \text{Then the "current PC" the CPU sees is } & A + 8 \quad \text{(two instructions ahead, due to pipeline)}. \\ \text{The return address to save is the address} & \text{ of the instruction immediately after BL.} \\ \text{That instruction is at } & A + 4. \\ \text{Therefore, ARM hardware computes:} & \\ \text{LR} \leftarrow \text{PC} - 4 & = (A + 8) - 4 = A + 4. \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** $$ \boxed{\text{LR} \leftarrow A + 4} $$ This is why every KTU exam answer for "What value is stored in LR by `BL 0x8000` when the BL itself is at 0x1000?" is **0x1004**, never 0x1008. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Execution Trace of a Nested Call Consider this scenario (memory addresses shown for clarity): ``` 0x00008000: MOV R0, #5 ; main prepares argument 0x00008004: BL 0x00008100 ; calls funcA — BL at 0x8004 0x00008008: MOV R1, R0 ; uses return value 0x000080FC: B 0x00008108 ; (end of main, infinite loop / halt) 0x00008100: PUSH {R4, LR} ; funcA prologue 0x00008104: MOV R4, R0 0x00008108: MOV R0, #10 0x0000810C: BL 0x00008200 ; funcA calls funcB — BL at 0x810C 0x00008110: ADD R0, R0, R4 0x00008114: POP {R4, PC} ; funcA epilogue / return 0x00008200: PUSH {R4, LR} ; funcB prologue 0x00008204: MOV R4, R0 0x00008208: MUL R0, R4, R4 ; return R0 = R4 * R4 0x0000820C: POP {R4, PC} ; funcB return ``` **Execution Trace Table (Trace each cycle of LR and SP):** | Step | Instruction Executed | LR (R14) | SP (R13) | PC (R15) | Stack Contents (top → bottom) | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | 1 | `MOV R0, #5` (at 0x8000) | 0x?????? | 0x8000 | 0x8004 | (empty) | | 2 | `BL 0x8100` (at 0x8004) | **0x8008** | 0x8000 | 0x8100 | (empty) | | 3 | `PUSH {R4, LR}` (at 0x8100) | 0x8008 | **0x7FF8** | 0x8104 | [0x8008, R4_old] | | 4 | `MOV R4, R0` | 0x8008 | 0x7FF8 | 0x8108 | [0x8008, R4_old] | | 5 | `MOV R0, #10` | 0x8008 | 0x7FF8 | 0x810C | [0x8008, R4_old] | | 6 | `BL 0x8200` (at 0x810C) | **0x8110** | 0x7FF8 | 0x8200 | [0x8008, R4_old] | | 7 | `PUSH {R4, LR}` (at 0x8200) | 0x8110 | **0x7FF0** | 0x8204 | [0x8110, R4_old, 0x8008, R4_old] | | 8 | `MUL R0, R4, R4` | 0x8110 | 0x7FF0 | 0x820C | [0x8110, R4_old, 0x8008, R4_old] | | 9 | `POP {R4, PC}` (at 0x820C) | 0x8110 | **0x7FF8** | **0x8114** | [0x8008, R4_old] | | 10 | `ADD R0, R0, R4` (at 0x8110) | 0x8110 | 0x7FF8 | 0x8114 | [0x8008, R4_old] | | 11 | `POP {R4, PC}` (at 0x8114) | 0x8110 | **0x8000** | **0x8008** | (empty) | | 12 | `MOV R1, R0` (at 0x8008) | 0x8008 | 0x8000 | 0x800C | (empty) | **Key observations from the trace:** - **LR overwriting:** At step 2, LR becomes 0x8008. At step 6, it is *overwritten* to 0x8110. If funcA had not pushed LR in step 3, the return address to `main` (0x8008) would have been lost — a classic bug. - **Stack grows downward:** SP goes 0x8000 → 0x7FF8 → 0x7FF0, then unwinds back to 0x8000. ARM stack is **Full Descending (FD)** by default in AAPCS. - **`POP {..., PC}`** simultaneously restores R4 *and* writes the saved LR into PC, giving atomic return. ### 3.3 Stack Frame Size Calculation **Problem:** A function needs to save R4, R5, R6, R7, and LR, and has 16 bytes of local variables. What value should be subtracted from SP in the prologue? **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bytes saved on stack by PUSH} & = 5 \text{ registers} \times 4 \text{ bytes/reg} = 20 \text{ bytes}. \\ \text{Local variable space required} & = 16 \text{ bytes}. \\ \text{Total stack frame growth} & = 20 + 16 = 36 \text{ bytes}. \\ \text{Stack must be 8-byte aligned per AAPCS, so round up} & : 36 \rightarrow 40 \text{ bytes}. \\ \text{Therefore, in prologue:} & \quad \text{SUB SP, SP, \#40}. \end{aligned} $$ **Prologue code:** ``` PUSH {R4-R7, LR} ; saves 20 bytes SUB SP, SP, #16 ; allocates 16 bytes for locals (total 36) ; OR equivalently, more compact: SUB SP, SP, #40 ; if compiler pre-pads ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **8-Byte Stack Alignment Rule (AAPCS Section 5.2.1.2):** > At any public function entry, `SP mod 8 == 0`. This guarantees correct behavior of `LDRD/STRD` (double-word loads) and is enforced by ARM's official Procedure Call Standard. Forgetting this in KTU code = 1–2 marks lost. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Function Call / Return — Block-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Main Program: PC = 0x8000] --> B[Load arguments into R0-R3
R0 = 5] B --> C["BL funcA
LR <- 0x8008
PC <- 0x8100"] C --> D[funcA Prologue
PUSH R4, LR onto stack
SP decreases] D --> E[funcA Body
Load args, compute, set R0] E --> F{funcA calls another function?} F -- Yes --> G["BL funcB
LR <- 0x8110
PC <- 0x8200"] G --> H[funcB Prologue
PUSH R4, LR] H --> I[funcB Body
Compute return value in R0] I --> J["funcB Epilogue
POP R4, PC
SP increases
PC <- 0x8114"] J --> K[Back in funcA
Continue after BL] F -- No --> L["funcA Epilogue
POP R4, PC
PC <- 0x8008"] K --> L L --> M[Main Program resumes
PC = 0x8008] M --> N[Use R0 return value
MOV R1, R0] ``` ### 4.2 ARM Stack Frame Layout (Full Descending, AAPCS) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HighAddr["Higher Memory Addresses"] direction TB H1[Caller Stack Frame] H2["funcA Return Address (LR_saved)
0x8008"] H3["Saved R4 (funcA)
original value"] H4["Local Variable: int x
4 bytes"] H5["Local Variable: int y
4 bytes"] H6["Padding for 8-byte alignment
0-4 bytes"] H7["funcB Return Address (LR_saved)
0x8110"] H8["Saved R4 (funcB)"] H9["Local Variable: int z
4 bytes"] end subgraph LowAddr["Lower Memory Addresses"] direction TB L1["SP points here <-- TOP of Stack
grows DOWN"] end HighAddr --- LowAddr H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 --> H6 --> H7 --> H8 --> H9 --> L1 ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing Return Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Function Exit Point] --> Q1{Is this a leaf function
(no BL inside body)?} Q1 -- Yes --> Leaf["Use: BX LR
R0 already holds return value"] Q1 -- No --> NonLeaf["Use: POP {R4-R11, PC}
R0 holds return value"] Leaf --> Done[Return complete] NonLeaf --> Done ``` ### 4.4 AAPCS Register Partition Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CallerSaved["Caller-Saved (Scratch) — AAPCS"] A0[R0: Arg/Result] A1[R1: Arg] A2[R2: Arg] A3[R3: Arg] A12[R12: IP Scratch] end subgraph CalleeSaved["Callee-Saved (Preserved) — AAPCS"] B4[R4] B5[R5] B6[R6] B7[R7] B8[R8] B9[R9: SB] B10[R10: SL] B11[R11: FP] end subgraph Special["Special Purpose"] S13[R13: SP] S14[R14: LR] S15[R15: PC] end CallerSaved ~~~ CalleeSaved ~~~ Special ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Explain the role of the Link Register (R14) in ARM function calls. Why is it necessary to save LR onto the stack in non-leaf functions?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Link Register (LR / R14)** is a dedicated CPU register that holds the **return address** of the most recently called function. When the `BL` (Branch with Link) instruction executes, the ARM hardware automatically stores `PC + 4` into LR, which is the address of the instruction immediately following the BL. In a **leaf function** (one that does not call any other function), LR still holds the return address to the caller, so a simple `BX LR` is enough to return. In a **non-leaf function**, however, the function itself executes at least one `BL` instruction. Since there is only **one** LR register, this nested `BL` would **overwrite** the return address to the original caller. To prevent this, the non-leaf function must save its incoming LR onto the **stack** in its prologue (e.g., `PUSH {..., LR}`) and later restore it into PC in its epilogue (e.g., `POP {..., PC}`). This preserves the original return address across nested calls. **(3 marks)** --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List any three differences between the `BL` and `BX` instructions in ARM. When is `BL` preferred over a simple `B` instruction?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `BL` (Branch with Link) | `BX` (Branch and Exchange) | |:---|:---|:---| | Link Register Update | **Yes**, updates LR = PC + 4 | **No**, does not touch LR | | Instruction Set Switch | No (always ARM state) | **Yes**, can switch ARM ↔ Thumb via bit[0] of target | | Typical Use Case | **Function call** | **Function return** (`BX LR`) or Thumb interworking | `BL` is preferred over `B` whenever the called function must **return** to the instruction following the call site. The `B` instruction merely transfers control without preserving the return path, making it suitable only for unconditional jumps (e.g., loops, `if-else` branches), not for function calls. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Function Call Mechanism & AAPCS Trace* **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the step-by-step mechanism of a function call in ARM. Include the role of `BL`, the Link Register, the stack pointer, and the `PUSH`/`POP` instructions. **(b) [7 marks]** Consider the following ARM assembly code. Identify the AAPCS violations, if any, and correct them. Assume R0 holds the first argument on entry. ``` AREA func, CODE sumFunc MOV R4, R0 ; save argument MOV R0, #0 ; initialize result loop ADD R0, R0, R4 SUB R4, R4, #1 CMP R4, #0 BGT loop MOV PC, LR END ``` --- **Model Solution for Q-A:** **(a) [7 marks] Step-by-Step Function Call Mechanism** 1. **Argument Setup (caller):** Per AAPCS, the first four arguments are placed in **R0–R3**. R0 = 5 (example). 2. **Call Instruction:** `BL sumFunc` — ARM hardware performs: - `LR ← PC + 4` (return address saved) - `PC ← address of sumFunc` 3. **Prologue (callee):** - `PUSH {R4–R11, LR}` — saves callee-saved registers and the return address on the stack. SP is decremented by `4 × N` bytes. - `SUB SP, SP, #M` — allocates M bytes for local variables (M aligned to 8). 4. **Function Body:** Performs computation, places return value in **R0**. 5. **Epilogue (callee):** - `ADD SP, SP, #M` — de-allocates locals. - `POP {R4–R11, PC}` — restores callee-saved registers AND returns simultaneously by loading the saved LR into PC. 6. **Caller Resumes:** PC now points to the instruction after `BL`. The return value is available in R0. **Diagram:** ``` [Caller: main] [Callee: sumFunc] PC = 0x8000 PC = 0x8100 │ │ ├─ MOV R0, #5 │ │ │ ├─ BL 0x8100 ────────────►├─ PUSH {R4, LR} (SP -= 8) │ (LR = 0x8008) ├─ ...body... │ ├─ MOV R0, result │ ├─ POP {R4, PC} (SP += 8, PC = 0x8008) ◄────────────────────────┘ │ ├─ uses R0 ``` **(b) [7 marks] AAPCS Audit** | Line | Violation | Correction | |:---|:---|:---| | `MOV R4, R0` | R4 is a **callee-saved** register. The function modifies it **without saving it** on the stack. | Add `PUSH {R4, LR}` at entry. | | `MOV R0, #0` inside function | R0 is the **return value register**. Overwriting it before returning is *not* a violation **only** if the final value is intended as the return value. Here it is, so **OK**. | No change. | | `MOV PC, LR` | Not an AAPCS violation, but `POP {R4, PC}` is the idiomatic and safer return — it preserves R4 atomically with the return. | Replace with `POP {R4, PC}`. | | Missing `END` of `PUSH/POP` pair | If `PUSH {R4, LR}` is used, the function body uses R4, and `MOV PC, LR` returns — then **LR is popped (R4 is loaded with the return address),** corrupting R4, and the saved R4 is lost. | Use `POP {R4, PC}` and a matching `PUSH {R4, LR}`. | | Stack alignment | AAPCS requires SP be 8-byte aligned at function entry. After `PUSH {R4, LR}`, SP is 8-byte aligned (good). | OK. | **Corrected Code:** ``` AREA sumFunc, CODE, READONLY sumFunc PUSH {R4, LR} ; AAPCS: save R4 (callee-saved) and LR MOV R4, R0 ; copy argument (R0 may be needed for return) MOV R0, #0 ; initialize accumulator loop ADD R0, R0, R4 SUB R4, R4, #1 CMP R4, #0 BGT loop POP {R4, PC} ; restore R4 and return atomically END ``` --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Leaf vs Non-Leaf & Stack Frame Analysis* **(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **leaf** and **non-leaf** functions in ARM with one example each. Show the assembly code for both, and explain why `BX LR` is safe in a leaf function but not in a non-leaf one. **(b) [7 marks]** A function needs to save registers R4, R5, R6, and has 12 bytes of local variables. Write the complete ARM prologue and epilogue. Show the stack layout with exact memory addresses, assuming initial SP = 0x00009000. --- **Model Solution for Q-B:** **(a) [7 marks] Leaf vs Non-Leaf Functions** | Feature | Leaf Function | Non-Leaf Function | |:---|:---|:---| | Definition | Does not call any other function (`BL` absent) | Contains at least one `BL` to another function | | LR Handling | LR is **not** modified inside, so return via `BX LR` is safe | LR is **overwritten** by the inner `BL`, so it must be saved on the stack | | Prologue | May save only the callee-saved registers it actually uses; LR save optional | **Must** save LR on the stack via `PUSH {..., LR}` | | Epilogue | `BX LR` or `POP {..., PC}` | `POP {..., PC}` (mandatory, since LR was pushed) | | Example | `int square(int x) { return x*x; }` | `int addAndSquare(int a, int b) { return square(a+b); }` | **Leaf Function Example (ARM):** ``` square MUL R0, R0, R0 ; R0 = R0 * R0 BX LR ; safe: LR still holds return address ``` **Non-Leaf Function Example (ARM):** ``` addAndSquare PUSH {R4, LR} ; MUST save LR ADD R0, R0, R1 ; a + b in R0 BL square ; overwrites LR with return-to-here address POP {R4, PC} ; restore R4 AND return to original caller ``` **Why `BX LR` is unsafe in a non-leaf function:** In a non-leaf function, after `BL inner`, R14 no longer holds the return address to the original caller — it holds the return address to *this* function. Using `BX LR` would return to the original caller only after `inner` returns and pops its own LR back.
