If `inner` is *not* a sibling call but a deeply nested one, or if this function's `BX LR` were executed directly, the program would jump to garbage. Saving LR on the stack preserves the original return address safely. **(b) [7 marks] Prologue, Epilogue & Stack Layout** **Stack size calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Callee-saved registers to push} & : R4, R5, R6 = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ bytes}. \\ \text{Locals} & : 12 \text{ bytes}. \\ \text{Total} & : 24 \text{ bytes (already 8-byte aligned).} \\ \text{No additional LR save needed} & : \text{assume leaf function.} \end{aligned} $$ **Prologue:** ``` myFunc PUSH {R4-R6} ; SP -= 12 SUB SP, SP, #12 ; allocate 12 bytes for locals ; SP is now 0x00008FE8 ``` **Epilogue:** ``` ADD SP, SP, #12 ; de-allocate locals POP {R4-R6} ; restore callee-saved, SP += 12 BX LR ; return ``` **Stack Layout (SP = 0x00009000 initially, Full Descending stack):** | Address | Contents | Bytes | |:---:|:---|:---:| | 0x00008FE4 | Local variable 3 | 4 | | 0x00008FE0 | Local variable 2 | 4 | | 0x00008FDC | Local variable 1 | 4 | | 0x00008FD8 | Saved R6 | 4 | | 0x00008FD4 | Saved R5 | 4 | | 0x00008FD0 | Saved R4 | 4 | | 0x00008FD0 – 0x00008FFF | *(initial SP region — was empty)* | — | | **0x00009000** | **SP initial (TOP)** | — | After prologue, **SP = 0x00008FD0**. After epilogue, SP returns to 0x00009000. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark-Loss Callouts** > 1. **Forgetting to push LR in non-leaf functions (−2 marks):** This is the #1 reason students lose marks on AAPCS questions. The examiner checks: "Did you PUSH LR before any BL inside the function?" > 2. **Using `MOV PC, LR` instead of `POP {..., PC}` (−1 mark):** `MOV PC, LR` does *not* restore the saved R4–R11. If you pushed them, you must pop them — and loading PC from the same POP is the cleanest way. > 3. **Wrong LR value in `BL` questions (−2 marks):** Writing `LR = PC + 8` instead of `PC + 4`. Always state: *LR stores the address of the instruction after the BL itself* (i.e., the address of the next sequential instruction). > 4. **Confusing AAPCS roles of R0 and R4 (−1 mark):** R0 is the **return** register (caller-saved), R4 is **callee-saved**. Mixing these up costs easy marks. > 5. **Stack not 8-byte aligned at function entry (−1 mark):** Per AAPCS Section 5.2.1.2, public functions must be entered with `SP mod 8 == 0`. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ `BL` does **two** things: saves `PC+4` in LR **and** branches to the target. - ✅ LR (R14) is **a single register** — it is overwritten by *every* `BL`. Save it on the stack before calling another function. - ✅ AAPCS: **R0–R3 and R12 = caller-saved**; **R4–R11 = callee-saved**; **R13 = SP**; **R14 = LR**; **R15 = PC**. - ✅ **First 4 arguments → R0–R3**, return value → **R0**. Additional arguments go on the stack. - ✅ **Leaf function** → can return via `BX LR`. **Non-leaf function** → must `PUSH {..., LR}` and `POP {..., PC}`. - ✅ ARM stack is **Full Descending (FD)**: grows toward lower addresses; `PUSH` decrements SP, `POP` increments SP. - ✅ Stack must be **8-byte aligned** at the entry of any public function (AAPCS). - ✅ `POP {..., PC}` is the idiomatic, atomic way to return from a non-leaf function. - ✅ `BX` can switch ARM ↔ Thumb; `BL` cannot. - ✅ The pipeline makes PC read as `current + 8` in ARM state — this is why LR = `PC − 4 = current + 4`.

topics.title: Register Usage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Register Usage # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) Architecture**, *Register Usage* refers to the systematic organization, allocation, and convention-based deployment of the processor's internal **37 general-purpose 32-bit registers** across different operational modes. These registers are partitioned into: 1. **Thirty (30) General-Purpose Registers (GPRs)**: R0 to R12, R13 (Stack Pointer), R14 (Link Register), and R15 (Program Counter) — physically distinct across processor modes through *banking*. 2. **Six (6) Status Registers**: One **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** and five **Saved Program Status Registers (SPSR)** reserved for exception modes. ARM follows the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)**, which strictly defines how a compiler or assembly programmer may use R0–R3, R12, R13, R14, and R15 to ensure interoperability between separately compiled modules, libraries, and OS kernels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes *register conventions, banking mechanism, AAPCS rules, and PSR (Program Status Register) flag interpretation*. Mastery of this topic is essential for Part-B (14-mark) questions on subroutine linkage and exception handling. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Desk" Intuition Imagine a single office desk (the **CPU**) shared between **7 employees** (the 7 processor modes: User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System). The desk has only so many drawers: - **R0–R12** = **Common stationary drawers** 🗄️ that every employee can use for *their own temporary scratch work* (storing operands, temporaries, loop counters). - **R13 (Stack Pointer / SP)** = A **personal locker key** 🗝️ that points to *where* the employee keeps their private paperwork. Each mode gets a *different physical locker*, but logically it's the same key. - **R14 (Link Register / LR)** = The **"Return Address Notepad"** 📝. Whenever a function is called (`BL` instruction), LR automatically scribbles down the address to return to. - **R15 (Program Counter / PC)** = The **employee's reading finger** 👆 pointing at the *current line* in the instruction manual. - **CPSR** = The **employee's mood & calculator state board** (N, Z, C, V flags + current mode + interrupt enable bits). - **SPSR** = A **photocopy of the mood board** taken the instant an exception (interrupt/trap) fires, so the original can be restored on return. --- ## 1.3 The 37-Register Architecture at a Glance ARM is a **load-store RISC** architecture. Registers are the *only* operands for arithmetic, logical, and data-processing instructions. Hence, register usage governs **performance, code density, and ABI compliance**. | Register Group | Count | Width | Banked? | Purpose | |:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--| | Unbanked GPRs (R0–R7) | 8 | 32-bit | ❌ No | Low registers, universal scratch | | Banked GPRs (R8–R12) | 5 | 32-bit | ✅ Partial (FIQ banks R8–R12) | High registers | | R13 / SP | 1+5 banked | 32-bit | ✅ Yes (each privileged mode has its own) | Stack management | | R14 / LR | 1+5 banked | 32-bit | ✅ Yes | Subroutine return address | | R15 / PC | 1 | 32-bit | ❌ No (shared) | Program Counter | | CPSR | 1 | 32-bit | ❌ No | Current flags & mode | | SPSR | 0+5 | 32-bit | ✅ (only in privileged modes) | Saved CPSR snapshot | > [!NOTE] > **Why 37, not 30?** Although there are *only* 30 unique 32-bit general-purpose register names (R0–R15 across modes), ARM physically implements **37 distinct 32-bit registers** when you count the banked copies of R8–R14 in FIQ, plus the banked R13/R14 in the other 5 privileged modes. This is a *classic KTU 2-mark* question. --- ## 1.4 AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard) — Quick View The AAPCS partitions R0–R15 into **caller-saved (volatile)** and **callee-saved (non-volatile)** registers: - **R0–R3, R12** → **Argument / Scratch / Caller-saved** (a subroutine may freely clobber them; the caller must preserve any needed value across the call). - **R4–R11** → **Local / Callee-saved** (the *callee* must preserve their value and restore them before returning). - **R13 (SP), R14 (LR), R15 (PC)** → **Special-purpose**, managed by convention. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common Part-A question is: *"List the caller-saved and callee-saved registers in ARM AAPCS."* Memorize the split **R0–R3, R12 = caller; R4–R11 = callee**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Register Usage ### A. Low Registers R0 – R7 (Unbanked, Universal) These **8 registers are identical and accessible in *every* processor mode** with no banking overhead. They are used for: - **R0** → Conventional **return value** register (and 1st function argument in AAPCS). - **R1, R2, R3** → Function arguments 2, 3, 4 (5th and beyond passed on the stack). - **R4–R7** → Callee-saved local variables. - **R7** → On ARM, often used as the **Frame Pointer (FP)** in debug builds (`-fno-omit-frame-pointer`). - **All eight** → Available as operands in every Thumb-2 16-bit data-processing instruction (critical for code density). ### B. High Registers R8 – R12 (Partially Banked) - In **User/System** modes: the same physical registers R8–R12 are visible. - In **FIQ mode**: R8–R12 are **banked**, meaning FIQ gets its *own* private R8_fiq–R12_fiq, allowing the fast interrupt handler to begin using them immediately *without* any PUSH/POP to the stack. This is precisely why FIQ exists — to provide **zero-overhead register context for the fastest possible ISR**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** R8–R12 are *not* banked in IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, or Undefined modes. Only FIQ gets the privilege because it is the highest-priority, latency-critical interrupt. ### C. R13 — The Stack Pointer (SP) - Each privileged mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND) and **User mode** has its **own banked copy** of R13. - On reset, each SP is initialized to a high address defined in the linker script (e.g., `0x40008000` in many KTU LPC2148 examples). - The ARM stack is **full-descending by convention** (push decrements SP, pop increments SP). ### D. R14 — The Link Register (LR) - Holds the **return address** after a `BL` (Branch with Link) or `BLX` instruction. - `BL subroutine` → `LR = address of instruction *after* the BL`. - A nested function call must `PUSH {LR}` first and then `POP {PC}` on return, because LR will be overwritten by the next `BL`. - Each privileged mode (except User/System) has its **own banked LR**, used to save the return address from an exception. ### E. R15 — The Program Counter (PC) - **Always readable** as the current instruction address + 8 (due to the 2-stage pipeline: at the time of execution, PC = current_inst + 8, where `current_inst` is the address of the instruction being executed, and the +8 reflects two already-fetched instructions ahead). - **Writable** in most data-processing instructions (e.g., `MOV PC, LR` for function return). - In ARM state, R15 is **read as current instruction + 8 bytes**. - In Thumb state, R15 is **read as current instruction + 4 bytes**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** When an LDR loads the PC, it must be the *target* address, not target+8. The +8 offset applies *only* to direct reads of R15. ### F. CPSR — Current Program Status Register (32 bits) CPSR contains **four logical fields**: | Bits | Field | Purpose | |:--:|:--|:--| | 31:28 | **Condition flags** | N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), V (oVerflow) | | 27:25 | **Reserved** (in base ARMv4T) | IT[1:0], J ( Jazelle ) in higher versions | | 24 | J (Jazelle) | 0 in standard ARM/Thumb | | 23:20 | **Reserved** | — | | 19:16 | **GE[3:0]** (SIMD, ARMv6+) | Greater-than-or-Equal flags | | 15:10 | **IT[1:0], IT[7:2]** (Thumb-2) | If-Then block state | | 9:6 | **Reserved** | — | | 5 | **T (Thumb bit)** | 0 = ARM state, 1 = Thumb state | | 4:0 | **M[4:0] (Mode bits)** | Current processor mode | **Mode Encoding (M[4:0]):** | M[4:0] | Mode | Use | |:--:|:--|:--| | `10000` (0x10) | **User** | Normal application code, unprivileged | | `10001` (0x11) | **FIQ** | Fast Interrupt | | `10010` (0x12) | **IRQ** | Normal Interrupt | | `10011` (0x13) | **SVC** | Supervisor / Software Interrupt (SWI) | | `10111` (0x17) | **Abort** | Memory access violation | | `11011` (0x1B) | **Undefined** | Undefined instruction trap | | `11111` (0x1F) | **System** | Privileged, uses User mode registers | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Warning:** Writing `0b10001` for User mode is a common mistake. User mode is `0b10000`. The fifth bit is what makes it privileged. ### G. SPSR — Saved Program Status Register - **Exists only in the 5 privileged exception modes** (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND). - On taking an exception, the hardware **automatically copies CPSR → SPSR_**. - On returning from an exception (e.g., `SUBS PC, LR, #4`), the hardware **restores CPSR ← SPSR_` atomically**. - User/System modes **do not have** an SPSR. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Encoding | Unit | Notes | |:--|:--|:--|:--| | PC read offset (ARM) | $\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Addr}_{\text{current}} + 8$ | bytes | Due to 3-stage pipeline fetch-decode-execute | | PC read offset (Thumb) | $\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Addr}_{\text{current}} + 4$ | bytes | 2-stage pipeline | | Branch target (B/BL) | $\text{Offset}_{\text{range}} = \pm 2^{25}$ words | ±32 MB | 24-bit signed word offset, shifted left 2 | | BL encoding | $\text{LR} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 4$ | bytes | PC at BL is already +8, so LR = next inst | | SP rule (Full Desc) | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4 \times N$ | bytes | After PUSH of N registers | | POP rule | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 4 \times N$ | bytes | After POP of N registers | | Banked registers (User vs Privileged) | 25 banked + 12 shared = 37 | registers | Total unique physical registers | | N flag | $N = \text{bit}_{31}(\text{result})$ | — | 1 if MSB = 1 (negative) | | Z flag | $Z = 1 \iff \text{result} = 0$ | — | Set when ALU result is zero | | C flag (add) | $C = 1 \iff \text{unsigned overflow}$ | — | Carry out of bit 31 | | V flag (add) | $V = 1 \iff \text{signed overflow}$ | — | $V = c_3 \oplus c_4$ (carry into ≠ carry out of MSB) | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Register usage conventions directly enable: - **Compiler interoperability**: GCC and ARMCC both follow AAPCS, so an object file compiled by one can call a library built by the other. - **RTOS context switching**: An OS scheduler saves only the **callee-saved registers** (R4–R11, SP, LR) of a thread, then switches the SP — the entire process context is preserved. - **Interrupt latency optimization**: FIQ banking (R8_fiq–R14_fiq) allows the handler to skip stack pushes for high registers, achieving sub-10-cycle latencies in automotive and motor-control firmware. - **Secure boot / TrustZone**: Modern ARMv8-A extends the SPSR idea to **PSTATE** and **ELR_ELx** (Exception Link Registers per exception level). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why PC reads as `Current_Address + 8` ARM uses a **3-stage pipeline**: `Fetch → Decode → Execute`. At any clock cycle, three consecutive instructions are in flight. Let: - $A$ = address of the instruction currently being **executed**. - $A+4$ = address of the instruction currently being **decoded** (already fetched). - $A+8$ = address of the instruction currently being **fetched** (next to enter decode). The R15 register is implemented in the **fetch stage** of the pipeline. Therefore, when an instruction in the *execute* stage reads R15, it sees the address of the instruction *two ahead* in program order. $$ \boxed{\;\text{R15}_{\text{read}} = A + 8\;} $$ For Thumb (2-stage pipeline: Fetch → Execute), the offset is 4: $$ \boxed{\;\text{R15}_{\text{read,Thumb}} = A + 4\;} $$ ### 3.1.1 Numerical Example Consider the following ARM code at address `0x00000000`: ```asm 0x00000000: MOV R0, R1 ; executes at A = 0x00 0x00000004: ADD R2, R0, R1 ; decoded at A+4 0x00000008: SUB R3, R2, R0 ; fetched at A+8 ``` If `MOV R0, R1` itself reads PC, the value delivered to the operand bus is: $$ \text{PC}_{\text{read}} = 0x00000000 + 8 = 0x00000008 $$ which is *not* the address of the `MOV`, but the address of `SUB`. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: How `BL` Calculates the Return Address The `BL` (Branch with Link) instruction has the format: $$ \text{BL}\{cond\} \;\; \text{L} \;\; \text{offset}_{24} $$ The hardware performs **two** actions in the execute stage: 1. $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + \text{SignExtend}(\text{offset}) \times 4$ (target branch address) 2. $\text{LR} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 4$ (return address, since *at the moment of executing BL*, R15 already reads as `current_BL_addr + 8`) Step-by-step: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{At fetch of BL: } & \text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Addr}_{\text{BL}} + 8 \\ \text{Link: } & \text{LR} = \text{PC}_{\text{read}} - 4 = \text{Addr}_{\text{BL}} + 4 \\ \text{Branch: } & \text{PC} = \text{Addr}_{\text{BL}} + 8 + \text{SignExtend}(\text{offset}_{24}) \cdot 4 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the return address stored in LR is always the **instruction immediately following the BL**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The sign-extension gives a branch range of $\pm 2^{25}$ bytes (32 MB) from the BL itself. ### 3.2.1 Numerical Example Suppose `BL my_func` is at `0x00000100` and `my_func` is at `0x00000200`. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{offset}_{24} & = \frac{(0x00000200 - (0x00000100 + 8))}{4} = \frac{0xF8}{4} = 0x3E \\ \text{LR} & = 0x00000100 + 4 = 0x00000104 \end{aligned} $$ When `my_func` finishes, executing `MOV PC, LR` (or `POP {PC}`) will return control to `0x00000104`. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Condition Flag Logic (ALU Operations) When an arithmetic instruction like `ADDS R0, R1, R2` executes, the ALU updates CPSR flags: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Result} & = R_1 + R_2 \\ N & = \text{Result}_{31} \\ Z & = 1 \iff \text{Result} = 0 \\ C & = \text{CarryOut}(\text{bit 31 of } R_1 + R_2) \\ V & = (C_{\text{into bit 31}}) \oplus (C_{\text{out of bit 31}}) \end{aligned} $$ For the **C flag in subtraction** (e.g., `SUBS R0, R1, R2`): $$ C = 1 \iff R_1 \geq R_2 \quad \text{(as unsigned)} $$ For the **V flag in subtraction**: $$ V = (R_{1,31} \oplus R_{2,31}) \cdot \overline{N} + \overline{(R_{1,31} \oplus R_{2,31})} \cdot N $$ The `S` suffix on the mnemonic (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `ANDS`, `ORRS`, `EORS`, `BICS`, `MVNS`, `MOVS`, `RSBS`, `RSCS`, `MULS`) forces the flags to be updated. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Register Behaviour Across Modes The following table summarizes the **physical register number** used for each architectural name, illustrating the banking mechanism. | Architectural Name | User | FIQ | IRQ | SVC | ABT | UND | System | |:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:| | R0 | R0 | R0 | R0 | R0 | R0 | R0 | R0 | | R1 | R1 | R1 | R1 | R1 | R1 | R1 | R1 | | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | | R7 | R7 | R7 | R7 | R7 | R7 | R7 | R7 | | R8 | **R8** | **R8_fiq** | R8 | R8 | R8 | R8 | **R8** | | R9 | **R9** | **R9_fiq** | R9 | R9 | R9 | R9 | **R9** | | R10 | **R10** | **R10_fiq** | R10 | R10 | R10 | R10 | **R10** | | R11 | **R11** | **R11_fiq** | R11 | R11 | R11 | R11 | **R11** | | R12 | **R12** | **R12_fiq** | R12 | R12 | R12 | R12 | **R12** | | R13 (SP) | **SP_usr** | **SP_fiq** | **SP_irq** | **SP_svc** | **SP_abt** | **SP_und** | **SP_usr** | | R14 (LR) | **LR_usr** | **LR_fiq** | **LR_irq** | **LR_svc** | **LR_abt** | **LR_und** | **LR_usr** | | R15 (PC) | PC | PC | PC | PC | PC | PC | PC | | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | CPSR | | SPSR | — | **SPSR_fiq** | **SPSR_irq** | **SPSR_svc** | **SPSR_abt** | **SPSR_und** | — | > [!NOTE] > **Reading the matrix:** The **bold cells** in R8–R12, R13, R14, and SPSR rows are *physically different registers*, even though all are addressed as "R8" or "R13" in their respective mode. ### 3.4.1 Numerical Banked-Register Count $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Unbanked: } & R0\text{–}R7 \; (8) + R15 \; (1) + \text{CPSR} \; (1) = 10 \\ \text{Shared in User/System, separately banked in 5 other modes: } & \\ & R8\text{–}R12 \; (5) \times 2 \;(\text{User/FIQ share some, but FIQ banks all 5}) \\ & R13 \; (1) \times 6 \;(\text{six copies: User, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND}) \\ & R14 \; (1) \times 6 \\ & \text{SPSR} \; (1) \times 5 \;(\text{no SPSR in User/System}) \\ \text{Total: } & 8 + 5 + 1 + 6 + 6 + 1 + 5 = 32 \text{ ... and with PC/CPSR and the 5 banked high regs in FIQ:} \\ \text{Standard answer: } & \mathbf{37 \text{ physical 32-bit registers}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: AAPCS Subroutine Linkage Write the standard prolog/epilog for a function `int add3(int a, int b, int c)` using AAPCS: ```asm add3: PUSH {R4, LR} ; save callee-saved R4 and return address ADD R0, R1, R2 ; a + b → R0 (1st arg is also return reg) MOV R4, R0 ; R4 is callee-saved, so any temp use is safe ADD R0, R4, R3 ; (a+b) + c → return value in R0 POP {R4, PC} ; restore R4 and return via LR (in PC) ``` **Mark allocation analysis (KTU 14-mark question):** | Step | Marks | |:--|:--:| | Identify caller-saved vs callee-saved split | 2 | | Correct use of R0–R3 for arguments/return | 3 | | Use of PUSH/POP for LR and callee-saved | 4 | | Correct use of POP {PC} for return | 2 | | Code correctness / no clobbering of R4–R11 | 3 | | **Total** | **14** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ARM Register File Organization (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[ARM Register File
37 x 32-bit physical registers] --> B[Unbanked Registers
Visible in all modes] A --> C[Banked Registers
Mode-specific copies] A --> D[Status Registers] B --> B1[R0 - Scratch / Return Value] B --> B2[R1 - Arg2 / Scratch] B --> B3[R2 - Arg3 / Scratch] B --> B4[R3 - Arg4 / Scratch] B --> B5[R4 to R11 - Callee-saved Locals] B --> B6[R15 / PC - Program Counter] C --> C1[R8_fiq to R12_fiq
FIQ-private high registers] C --> C2[R13 SP_x6
6 stack pointers: USR, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND] C --> C3[R14 LR_x6
6 link registers] C --> C4[SPSR_x5
FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND] D --> D1[CPSR
Flags + Mode + Thumb bit] ``` --- ## 4.2 CPSR Bit Layout (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR CPSR[CPSR 32-bit Register] --> F1[Bit 31: N - Negative] CPSR --> F2[Bit 30: Z - Zero] CPSR --> F3[Bit 29: C - Carry] CPSR --> F4[Bit 28: V - Overflow] CPSR --> F5[Bits 27-8: Reserved / IT / GE] CPSR --> F6[Bit 5: T - Thumb State] CPSR --> F7[Bits 4-0: M4-M0 - Mode] F1 -.-> N[Set if ALU result MSB = 1] F2 -.-> Z[Set if ALU result = 0] F3 -.-> C[Set on unsigned carry / borrow] F4 -.-> V[Set on signed overflow] F6 -.-> T[0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb] F7 -.-> M[0b10000 = User, 0b10001 = FIQ, 0b10010 = IRQ, 0b10011 = SVC, 0b10111 = ABT, 0b11011 = UND, 0b11111 = System] ``` --- ## 4.3 AAPCS Register Partition Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[AAPCS R0-R15 Partition] --> B[Caller-saved / Volatile] A --> C[Callee-saved / Non-volatile] A --> D[Special Purpose] B --> B1[R0 - Return Value] B --> B2[R1 to R3 - Function Args 1 to 4] B --> B3[R12 - IP Intra-procedure-call Scratch] C --> C1[R4 to R11 - Local Variables] D --> D1[R13 / SP - Stack Pointer] D --> D2[R14 / LR - Link Register] D --> D3[R15 / PC - Program Counter] B -.Caller must save.-> X[If needed across call] C -.Callee must save and restore.-> Y[PUSH at entry, POP at exit] ``` --- ## 4.4 Exception Entry & SPSR Snapshot Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as ARM Core participant CPSR participant SPSR as SPSR_mode participant LR as LR_mode participant PC Note over CPU: Exception fires (e.g., IRQ) CPU->>CPSR: Read current flags CPU->>SPSR: Save CPSR to SPSR_irq CPU->>CPSR: Switch to IRQ mode (M=10010)
Set I=1 to mask further IRQ CPU->>PC: Save return address in LR_irq
(PC+4 for IRQ, PC+8 for SWI) CPU->>PC: Branch to IRQ vector 0x18 Note over CPU: Handler executes
PUSH {R0-R12, LR} Note over CPU: ... ISR body ... Note over CPU: POP {R0-R12, LR}
SUBS PC, LR, #4 CPU->>CPSR: Atomic restore from SPSR_irq CPU->>PC: Resume previous execution ``` --- ## 4.5 BL Subroutine Call — Register Trace ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Caller at 0x1000: BL my_func] --> B[Execute stage of BL] B --> C["LR = PC + 4 = 0x1004"] B --> D["PC = 0x1000 + 8 + offset = 0x2000"] D --> E[my_func entry at 0x2000] E --> F["PUSH {R4, LR}"] F --> G[Function body uses R0-R3 freely] G --> H["POP {R4, PC}"] H --> I["PC = LR = 0x1004"] I --> J[Resume at instruction after BL] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Differentiate between caller-saved and callee-saved registers in the ARM AAPCS. List the registers in each category.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In the ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS), registers are classified based on which party (caller or callee) is responsible for preserving their values across a function call. - **Caller-saved (volatile) registers: R0, R1, R2, R3, and R12.** If the caller needs the value of any of these registers to survive across a function call, the *caller* must save them (typically by pushing onto the stack) before the call and restoring them afterwards. The callee may freely modify them. R0 is also the conventional return value register, while R1–R3 hold function arguments 2–4. - **Callee-saved (non-volatile) registers: R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10, and R11.** If the callee intends to use any of these registers, it is the *callee's* responsibility to save them at function entry (using `PUSH` in the prolog) and restore them at function exit (using `POP` in the epilog), typically with `PUSH {R4–R11, LR}` and `POP {R4–R11, PC}`. This division allows independent compilation of modules: a caller does not need to know whether the callee uses R4–R11 internally. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Explain the role of the SPSR register in ARM. Why is it absent in User and System modes?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Saved Program Status Register (SPSR)** is a privileged-mode register that **automatically receives a snapshot of the Current Program Status Register (CPSR) at the moment an exception is taken**. The SPSR thus preserves the condition flags (N, Z, C, V), the Thumb bit (T), the interrupt mask bits (I, F), and the previous processor mode. When the exception handler completes, the corresponding return instruction (e.g., `SUBS PC, LR, #4` for IRQ) **atomically restores CPSR from SPSR**, thereby resuming the interrupted task exactly as it was, with the original flags and mode intact. The SPSR is **absent in User and System modes** because: 1. User mode is the *unprivileged, application* mode — it cannot take exceptions directly; an exception would always be taken in a privileged mode. 2. System mode is a *privileged alias of User mode* (sharing R0–R14), used by operating-system kernel threads that need privileged access without taking exceptions. Since these two modes never enter an exception handler, there is no CPSR state to save or restore. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module-end Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part is 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **ARM core register set** and clearly explain the concept of **register banking** across processor modes. Discuss why FIQ has the largest set of banked registers. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** Explain the **bit layout of the CPSR register**, including the condition flags (N, Z, C, V), the Thumb bit (T), and the mode bits (M[4:0]). Encode the mode bits for **User, IRQ, and Supervisor** modes, and show how to switch from User to IRQ mode in assembly. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — Register Set & Banking (7 marks) **Step 1: List the 37 physical registers (2 marks)** The ARM core (ARMv4T / ARM7TDMI, as in KTU's LPC2148 syllabus) implements **37 physical 32-bit registers**: - **R0–R7** (8): unbanked, identical across all modes. - **R8–R12** (5): unbanked in User/System, but in **FIQ mode** all five are banked (R8_fiq–R12_fiq). - **R13 / SP** (1 + 5 banked): one physical SP per privileged mode and User mode → 6 copies. - **R14 / LR** (1 + 5 banked): one physical LR per privileged mode and User mode → 6 copies. - **R15 / PC** (1): unbanked, shared. - **CPSR** (1): unbanked, shared. - **SPSR** (5): one per privileged exception mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND). None in User/System. $$ \text{Total} = 8 + 5 + 6 + 6 + 1 + 1 + 5 = 32 + 5_{\text{FIQ-banked R8–R12 unique}} = \mathbf{37} $$ **Step 2: Define banking (2 marks)** **Banking** is the architectural mechanism by which the *same architectural name* (e.g., R13) refers to a **different physical register** depending on the current processor mode. When the processor mode changes (e.g., on taking an interrupt), the visible R13 automatically refers to the new mode's banked copy — no explicit switch instruction is needed, ensuring **zero-overhead context isolation** between modes. **Step 3: Why FIQ has the largest banking (3 marks)** FIQ is the **highest-priority, lowest-latency** interrupt in the ARM core. To minimize the number of registers that the FIQ handler must save to the stack (every PUSH/POP costs cycles on the latency-critical path), the architect banked R8–R12 *in addition to* R13, R14, and SPSR. This gives the FIQ handler **seven private registers** (R8_fiq–R14_fiq) that it can use immediately on entry, without any PUSH. The result is that a typical, simple FIQ handler (e.g., a single-source DMA completion ISR) may execute in **as few as 4–6 cycles** after vectoring, since only R0–R7 and the banked high registers need to be touched. --- #### Model Solution to (b) — CPSR Layout & Mode Switching (7 marks) **Step 1: Bit layout diagram (2 marks)** | Bits | 31 | 30 | 29 | 28 | 27..8 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4..0 | |:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:| | Field | **N** | **Z** | **C** | **V** | Reserved / IT / GE | **I** | **F** | **T** | **M[4:0]** | - **N (bit 31)**: Negative flag. Set to the value of the ALU result's MSB. `1` ⇒ result is negative (in two's complement). - **Z (bit 30)**: Zero flag. Set if the ALU result equals zero. - **C (bit 29)**: Carry flag. Set on unsigned carry-out (for ADD) or borrow (for SUB, inverted). - **V (bit 28)**: oVerflow flag. Set on signed overflow. - **I (bit 7)**: IRQ disable. `1` masks IRQ. - **F (bit 6)**: FIQ disable. `1` masks FIQ. - **T (bit 5)**: Thumb state. `0` = ARM, `1` = Thumb. - **M[4:0] (bits 4–0)**: Processor mode. **Step 2: Mode-bit encoding (2 marks)** | Mode | M[4:0] binary | M[4:0] hex | |:--|:--:|:--:| | **User** | `0b10000` | `0x10` | | **FIQ** | `0b10001` | `0x11` | | **IRQ** | `0b10010` | `0x12` | | **Supervisor (SVC)** | `0b10011` | `0x13` | | **Abort** | `0b10111` | `0x17` | | **Undefined** | `0b11011` | `0x1B` | | **System** | `0b11111` | `0x1F` | **Step 3: Switch from User to IRQ mode in assembly (3 marks)** User mode *cannot* directly modify CPSR mode bits (it lacks the privilege). The standard sequence is for the IRQ exception to be **taken automatically** by the hardware. If, however, the application is *already privileged* (e.g., in System mode) and wishes to enter IRQ mode programmatically: ```asm ; Read-modify-write CPSR, clearing the mode bits and setting IRQ mode. MRS R0, CPSR ; Read CPSR into R0 BIC R0, R0, #0x1F ; Clear M[4:0] ORR R0, R0, #0x12 ; Set mode = 0b10010 (IRQ) MSR CPSR_c, R0 ; Write back to control field only (no flag write) ; I-bit remains 0, so IRQ is still enabled ``` > [!NOTE] > The `CPSR_c` notation writes *only* the control byte (bits 7:0), preserving the condition flags in bits 31:28. The full CPSR write form `CPSR_cxsf` would clobber flags. For an **automatic** entry into IRQ mode (the normal flow): ```asm ; Hardware on taking the IRQ: ; 1. CPSR -> SPSR_irq ; 2. PC -> LR_irq (= address of the aborted instruction + 4) ; 3. M[4:0] = 0b10010 ; 4. I = 1 ; 5. PC = 0x18 (IRQ vector address) ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: (a)** Explain the **AAPCS calling convention** in detail. Describe the role of R0–R3, R12, R4–R11, R13, and R14 in a function call. Write a complete ARM assembly subroutine that adds two 32-bit integers passed as arguments, following AAPCS strictly. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** With examples, explain how the **PC behaves during a branch (B), branch-with-link (BL), and subroutine return (MOV PC, LR)**. Show the relationship $\text{PC} = \text{Current\_addr} + 8$ and explain why this pipeline offset exists. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — AAPCS (7 marks) **Step 1: AAPCS register map (3 marks)** | Register | AAPCS Role | Saved By | Notes | |:--|:--|:--|:--| | R0 | **Return value** (also arg 1) | Caller | The only register used to return a 32-bit (or pointer) value | | R1, R2, R3 | **Function arguments 2, 3, 4** | Caller | Additional args are pushed on the stack in **reverse order** | | R4–R11 | **Local variables** (callee-saved) | Callee | Must be preserved across the call | | R12 | **IP / Intra-Procedure-call scratch** | Caller | Reserved for veneers and PLT use | | R13 (SP) | **Stack Pointer** | Special | Full-descending stack by convention | | R14 (LR) | **Link Register** | Special | Holds return address set by `BL` | | R15 (PC) | **Program Counter** | Special | PC = current_inst + 8 (ARM) or + 4 (Thumb) | **Step 2: Rule of thumb (1 mark)** - **Caller-saved**: R0–R3, R12 — these are *volatile* across a function call. - **Callee-saved**: R4–R11 — these are *preserved* across a function call. **Step 3: AAPCS-compliant `add2` subroutine (3 marks)** ```asm ; int add2(int a, int b); ; a in R0, b in R1, return in R0 ; add2: ADD R0, R0, R1 ; result = a + b (R0 is the return register) MOV PC, LR ; return to caller ``` **Note on simplification:** Since `add2` uses *only* R0 and R1 (which are caller-saved and may be freely modified), and does not touch any callee-saved register (R4–R11), **no PUSH/POP is required** in the prolog/epilog. This is the AAPCS minimum. If the function used a callee-saved register, e.g., to preserve a value across a nested call: ```asm ; int add_and_double(int a, int b); ; int sum = a + b; ; helper(sum); ; a nested call clobbers R0-R3 ; return sum * 2; add_and_double: PUSH {R4, LR} ; preserve callee-saved R4 and return address ADD R4, R0, R1 ; sum = a + b, in callee-saved R4 MOV R0, R4 ; pass sum as arg to helper BL helper MOV R0, R4, LSL #1 ; result = sum * 2 POP {R4, PC} ; restore R4 and return via LR ``` --- #### Model Solution to (b) — PC Behaviour (7 marks) **Step 1: PC read offset derivation (2 marks)** ARM uses a 3-stage pipeline. When the CPU executes an instruction at address $A$, the address in the *fetch* stage of the pipeline (which is what R15 reports) is $A + 8$. $$ \boxed{\;\text{R15}_{\text{read}} = A_{\text{current}} + 8\;} $$ This exists because the PC register is a *slightly early* snapshot of program flow — it tells you where the *next two* instructions *after* the current one are. **Step 2: Behaviour of B (1.5 marks)** `B target` updates R15 only: $$ \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + \text{SignExtend}(\text{offset}_{24}) \cdot 4 $$ LR is **not** modified, so B is for in-function jumps (e.g., `B loop`). **Step 3: Behaviour of BL (1.5 marks)** `BL target` updates R15 *and* LR: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LR} &\leftarrow \text{PC} + 4 = A_{\text{BL}} + 4 \\ \text{PC} &\leftarrow \text{PC} + \text{SignExtend}(\text{offset}_{24}) \cdot 4 \end{aligned} $$ The $+4$ (not $+8$) is because at the moment LR is written, PC inside the execute stage has *not* yet been advanced by the +8 pipeline offset on the new branch. **Step 4: Behaviour of MOV PC, LR (2 marks)** `MOV PC, LR` loads R15 with the saved return address. After this write, two pipeline stages are flushed and instruction fetching resumes at `LR`. This is the standard *unconditional* function return. The more idiomatic epilog is `POP {R4, PC}`, which atomically restores R4 and returns in a single instruction. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (3-mark deduction each):** > 1. Writing `LR = PC` instead of `LR = PC + 4` after `BL`. The correct stored value is the address of the instruction *after* the BL, which is `A_BLANK + 4`, not the BL's own address. > 2. Forgetting that when **LDR** loads the PC, no +8 offset applies — the target register is the *actual* destination. Mixing the two conventions is a frequent mistake. > 3. Confusing the **CPSR_cxsf** field mask syntax: `MSR CPSR_c, R0` writes only the control byte; `MSR CPSR_x, R0` writes only the extension byte; using `CPSR_cxsf` clobbers all four fields and the flags. > 4. Claiming R8–R12 are banked in *all* privileged modes — they are banked **only in FIQ**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist** - ⭐ **ARM has 37 physical 32-bit registers** in the classic ARMv4T core (ARM7TDMI used in KTU labs). - ⭐ **R0–R7 are unbanked**; R8–R12 are banked **only in FIQ**; R13 (SP) and R14 (LR) are banked in all 5 privileged modes + User mode. - ⭐ **PC read offset**: ARM = **+8 bytes**, Thumb = **+4 bytes** (due to pipeline). - ⭐ **`BL` stores LR = PC + 4** = address of instruction *after* the BL. - ⭐ **CPSR mode bits (M[4:0])** — User `10000`, FIQ `10001`, IRQ `10010`, SVC `10011`, ABT `10111`, UND `11011`, System `11111`. - ⭐ **SPSR exists only in the 5 privileged exception modes** — *not* in User or System. - ⭐ **AAPCS split**: Caller-saved = **R0–R3, R12**; Callee-saved = **R4–R11**. - ⭐ **R0** is the **return value** register; **R1–R3** hold arguments 2–4. - ⭐ **Stack convention**: **Full-descending** — `PUSH` decrements SP, `POP` increments SP. - ⭐ **T bit (bit 5 of CPSR)**: 0 = ARM state, 1 = Thumb state. - ⭐ **FIQ has the most banked registers** (R8_fiq–R14_fiq) for low-latency interrupt handling. - ⭐ **`MSR CPSR_c, Rn`** writes only the control byte; **`MSR CPSR_cxsf, Rn`** writes everything (and clobbers flags). - ⭐ **POP {…, PC}** is the idiomatic return; it atomically restores the saved LR into PC. - ⭐ **The 4 condition flags N, Z, C, V** are updated only by instructions with the **S** suffix (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `ANDS`). > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Memorization Aid — The "4-5-6-7" Mnemonic for Register Counts:** > - **4** = Number of condition flags (N, Z, C, V). > - **5** = Number of privileged exception modes (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND). > - **6** = Number of banked SP copies (User + 5 privileged). > - **7** = Total processor modes (the 5 above + User + System). > - **37** = Total physical 32-bit registers (10 unbanked + 27 banked).

topics.title: Parameter Passing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parameter Passing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Parameter Passing** is the mechanism by which arguments (inputs) and return values (outputs) are transferred between a *caller* function and a *callee* function during procedure invocation. In the context of the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** — a subset of the broader **ARM ABI (Application Binary Interface)** — parameter passing is governed by a strict, hardware-aware convention that dictates *where* (which registers or stack locations) and *how* (in what order, of what size) values are transmitted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 (Programming Concepts):**
> Students must master the AAPCS conventions for the ARM7/Cortex-M family, including the role of the core registers **R0–R3** as argument/scratch registers, **R4–R11** as callee-saved (variable) registers, and the use of the **Full Descending Stack (FD)** for spill-over parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are ordering food at a restaurant:

- **You (the Caller)** write your order on a slip of paper (your arguments).
- **The Waiter (the Calling Convention)** is trained to deliver only the first four items via a special silver tray (registers **R0, R1, R2, R3**) because it is fast.
- If your order has **more than 4 items**, the extra items are placed on the **service counter** (the **Stack**) where the chef (callee) will pick them up.
- The chef prepares the dish and returns a **single value** (or sometimes two) on the silver tray for the waiter to bring back.
- The chef is also responsible for **not breaking the other plates already on the counter** — these are the *callee-saved* registers.

This is precisely how ARM passes parameters: **fast registers first, slow stack memory afterward**, with strict rules on who is responsible for preserving what.

## 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU Board Examinations:**
>
> | Term | Meaning |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | **Caller** | The function that *invokes* another function. |
> | **Callee** | The function that is *being invoked*. |
> | **AAPCS** | ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard. |
> | **Scratch Registers** | Registers that the callee may freely overwrite (R0–R3, R12). |
> | **Variable Registers** | Registers that the callee must preserve (R4–R11, SP, LR). |
> | **Full Descending Stack (FD)** | Stack grows *down* toward lower memory; SP points to the *last* item pushed. |
> | **Stack Frame** | The region of the stack belonging to a single function call. |
> | **Frame Pointer (FP / R11)** | Stable reference point to access spilled arguments. |

## 1.4 Why Parameter Passing Matters in ARM

The ARM core has a **large register file (16 general-purpose registers in ARM state, with R13=SP, R14=LR, R15=PC being special)**. This abundance of registers makes ARM *register-rich* compared to legacy 8-bit architectures. A smart calling convention can therefore:

- Avoid the **memory access penalty** (von Neumann bottleneck).
- Pass **up to 4 parameters** in a single clock cycle each.
- Fall back to the **stack** only for complex argument lists (more than 4 words, structs > 16 bytes, or variadic functions).

The **trade-off** is that programmers and compilers must respect the AAPCS rigorously — a violation leads to silent corruption of unrelated data, which is notoriously hard to debug.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Tiered Passing Strategy

The AAPCS defines a **register-first, stack-second** hierarchy. The decision tree a compiler follows is:

1. **Attempt 1 — Register Assignment:**
   - Assign the first four 32-bit (or smaller) arguments to **R0, R1, R2, R3** in left-to-right order.
   - Promote smaller types (e.g., `char`, `short`) to 32 bits before placement.
   - A 64-bit value (e.g., `double`, `long long`) consumes **two consecutive registers** (R0+R1, or R2+R3).

2. **Attempt 2 — Stack Spill:**
   - Any arguments beyond the 4th are pushed onto the **Full Descending Stack** in left-to-right order **before** the call is made.
   - The callee reads them via `[SP + offset]` (or a Frame Pointer if one is set up).

3. **Return Value Channel:**
   - Integer/pointer return values: placed in **R0**.
   - 64-bit return values: placed in **R0 + R1**.
   - Larger return values (structs, floats in VFP): caller allocates memory, passes pointer in **R0**, callee writes the result there.

## 2.2 Register Roles under AAPCS

| Register | AAPCS Alias | Role | Preserved across calls? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **R0** | a1 / Argument 1 | 1st argument & return value | **No (Scratch)** |
| **R1** | a2 / Argument 2 | 2nd argument & 2nd word of 64-bit return | **No (Scratch)** |
| **R2** | a3 / Argument 3 | 3rd argument | **No (Scratch)** |
| **R3** | a4 / Argument 4 | 4th argument | **No (Scratch)** |
| **R4** | v1 | Variable | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R5** | v2 | Variable | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R6** | v3 | Variable | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R7** | v4 | Variable (often used as FP in Thumb) | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R8** | v5 | Variable | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R9** | v6 / SB | Platform-specific (often Static Base) | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R10** | v7 / SL | Stack Limit (some platforms) | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R11** | v8 / FP | Frame Pointer | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R12** | IP | Intra-Procedure-call scratch | **No (Scratch)** |
| **R13** | SP | Stack Pointer | **Yes (Callee-saved)** |
| **R14** | LR | Link Register (return address) | **No (Scratch)** |
| **R15** | PC | Program Counter | *Not directly preservable* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:**
> **"R0–R3 are arguments, R4–R11 are locals, R12 is scratch, R13 is stack, R14 is link, R15 is program."**
> Remember: **a1–a4** = scratch, **v1–v8** = preserved. "**v**" stands for *variable* (must be preserved), "**a**" stands for *argument* (may be trashed).

## 2.3 The 8-Byte Stack Alignment Rule

> [!NOTE]
> Per the AAPCS, the **Stack Pointer (SP)** **must be 8-byte aligned** at a *public function entry*. This enables efficient single-instruction LDRD/STRD (Load/Store Register Double) operations for 64-bit data.

The high-yield formula for verifying alignment after a stack push is:

$$
\text{aligned\_after} = (\text{SP}_{\text{before}} - n) \bmod 8 \equiv 0
$$

where $n$ is the total number of bytes pushed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Max regs for args | $4$ words (R0–R3) | 32 bits per slot |
| 64-bit argument slots | $2$ consecutive registers | e.g., R2+R3 |
| Return value (int) | $\text{stored in } R0$ | 32 bits |
| Return value (64-bit) | $\text{R0 (low), R1 (high)}$ | or R0+R1 |
| Stack direction | $\text{Full Descending (FD)}$ | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 4$ on PUSH |
| Stack alignment | $\text{SP} \bmod 8 \equiv 0$ | at public function entry |
| Frame size formula | $\text{Frame} = \sum (\text{locals} + \text{saved\_regs} + \text{spilled\_args}) + \text{alignment\_padding}$ | in bytes |
| Spilled argument read | $\text{arg}_n = \text{Mem}[FP + \text{offset}]$ | for $n \ge 5$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In embedded systems (the heart of KTU's ARM curriculum), parameter passing governs:
- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs):** R0–R3, R12, LR must be pushed by the ISR before use.
- **RTOS Context Switching:** Only the *callee-saved* registers need to be saved — a major efficiency win.
- **Mixed C/Assembly Programming:** Hand-written assembly functions that interface with C libraries (e.g., `printf`) **must** use AAPCS, or the stack frame collapses silently.
- **Function Pointers & Variadic Args:** Special rules apply; the compiler may force all variadic args onto the stack.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: A Simple 3-Argument Add Function

**High-level C code:**

```c
int add3(int a, int b, int c) {
    return a + b + c;
}
int main(void) {
    int result = add3(10, 20, 30);
    return 0;
}
```

**AAPCS Mapping (Derivation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parameter 1 (a=10)} &\rightarrow R0 \\
\text{Parameter 2 (b=20)} &\rightarrow R1 \\
\text{Parameter 3 (c=30)} &\rightarrow R2 \\
\text{Return value slot} &\rightarrow R0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since there are only **3 arguments** ($\le 4$), **no stack spill** is required.

**Caller assembly (excerpt from gcc -S output, simplified):**

```asm
main:
    MOV R0, #10         ; a = 10 -> R0
    MOV R1, #20         ; b = 20 -> R1
    MOV R2, #30         ; c = 30 -> R2
    BL add3             ; branch with link: LR = return addr, jump to add3
    ; R0 now holds the return value
    MOV R4, R0          ; save result
```

**Callee assembly:**

```asm
add3:
    ADD R0, R0, R1      ; R0 = a + b
    ADD R0, R0, R2      ; R0 = (a+b) + c
    MOV PC, LR          ; return to caller; return value already in R0
```

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0_{\text{after BL}} &= 10 + 20 + 30 \\
&= 30 + 30 \\
&= 60
\end{aligned}
$$

The value **60** is now in R0, ready for the caller to use.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: A 6-Argument Function — Stack Spill

**High-level C code:**

```c
int sum6(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e, int f) {
    return a + b + c + d + e + f;
}
```

**Register allocation stage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &\rightarrow R0 \\
b &\rightarrow R1 \\
c &\rightarrow R2 \\
d &\rightarrow R3 \\
e &\rightarrow \text{Stack[SP+0]} \quad \text{(spilled)} \\
f &\rightarrow \text{Stack[SP+4]} \quad \text{(spilled)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Caller assembly (push 5th and 6th args before the call):**

```asm
main:
    MOV R0, #1
    MOV R1, #2
    MOV R2, #3
    MOV R3, #4
    ; Push 5th and 6th args in left-to-right order
    ; Note: Rightmost arg pushed FIRST so leftmost is at lowest SP+offset
    ; Actually AAPCS: leftmost (5th) is at higher address than rightmost (6th)
    ; Standard: push reverse order, so R0-equivalent of 5th arg ends up at [SP]
    STR R4, [SP, #-4]!  ; push dummy / placeholder (see below)
    ; Proper AAPCS for 5+ args:
    ;   PUSH {e}  then  PUSH {f} ... NO, AAPCS specifies caller places
    ;   arguments at [SP] (oldest) upward.
    ; Correct sequence: subtract SP by 8, then store
    SUB SP, SP, #8
    MOV R5, #5
    MOV R6, #6
    STR R5, [SP, #0]   ; 5th arg (e=5)  at SP+0
    STR R6, [SP, #4]   ; 6th arg (f=6)  at SP+4
    BL sum6
    ADD SP, SP, #8     ; clean up
```

**Callee reads spilled args via SP (or FP after frame setup):**

```asm
sum6:
    PUSH {R11, LR}     ; save FP and return address
    ADD R11, SP, #4    ; set FP = SP + 4 (skip saved LR)
    ; R0..R3 still hold a,b,c,d
    LDR R4, [R11, #8]  ; load e from [FP+8] (5th spilled arg)
    LDR R5, [R11, #12] ; load f from [FP+12]
    ADD R0, R0, R1
    ADD R0, R0, R2
    ADD R0, R0, R3
    ADD R0, R0, R4
    ADD R0, R0, R5
    POP {R11, PC}      ; restore FP, return via LR (in PC slot)
```

**Numerical verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0_{\text{final}} &= 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 \\
&= 3 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 \\
&= 6 + 4 + 5 + 6 \\
&= 10 + 5 + 6 \\
&= 15 + 6 \\
&= 21
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Stack Frame Size Derivation

The total memory consumed by a function's stack frame is given by:

$$
S_{\text{frame}} = S_{\text{saved}} + S_{\text{locals}} + S_{\text{spilled}} + P_{\text{align}}
$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{saved}} &= n_{\text{callee-saved}} \times 4 \quad \text{(bytes)} \\
S_{\text{locals}} &= \sum_{i} \text{size}(L_i) \\
S_{\text{spilled}} &= (n_{\text{args}} - 4) \times 4 \quad \text{if } n_{\text{args}} > 4 \\
P_{\text{align}} &= (8 - (S_{\text{partial}} \bmod 8)) \bmod 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example derivation:** A function saves R4, R5, LR; has 2 local `int` variables; receives 5 arguments.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{saved}} &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{locals}} &= 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{spilled}} &= (5 - 4) \times 4 = 4 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{partial}} &= 12 + 8 + 4 = 24 \text{ bytes} \\
P_{\text{align}} &= (8 - (24 \bmod 8)) \bmod 8 = (8 - 0) \bmod 8 = 0 \\
S_{\text{frame}} &= 24 + 0 = 24 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Where Does an Argument Go?

| Argument Position | Argument Size (bits) | Destination | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1st | 8 / 16 / 32 | R0 (zero/sign-extended) | Fastest access |
| 2nd | 32 | R1 | Fastest access |
| 3rd | 32 | R2 | Fastest access |
| 4th | 32 | R3 | Fastest access |
| 1st of 64-bit pair | 64 | R0:R1 (low:high) | LDRD/STRD efficiency |
| 2nd of 64-bit pair | 64 | R2:R3 | LDRD/STRD efficiency |
| 5th onward | any | Stack (Full Descending) | Register exhaustion |
| Struct > 16 bytes | aggregate | Caller memory, pointer in R0 | Large aggregate cannot fit in regs |
| Floating-point (VFP) | 32 / 64 | s0–s15 / d0–d7 | Separate FP register file |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Parameter Passing Flow (Register-First Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller prepares arguments] --> B{Number of 32-bit args}
    B -- 1 to 4 args --> C[Place in R0 R1 R2 R3 left to right]
    B -- More than 4 args --> D[Place 1st four in R0 R1 R2 R3]
    D --> E[Push remaining args onto Full Descending Stack]
    C --> F[Execute BL instruction]
    E --> F
    F --> G[LR receives return address]
    G --> H[Callee begins execution]
    H --> I[Save callee saved registers R4 to R11 R11 FP LR]
    I --> J[Allocate local stack space]
    J --> K{Read spilled args from stack}
    K -- Yes --> L[Read via FP offset or SP offset]
    K -- No --> M[Work with values in R0 to R3]
    L --> N[Perform computation]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Place return value in R0 or R0 and R1]
    O --> P[Restore saved registers]
    P --> Q[Return via MOV PC LR or POP PC]
    Q --> R[Caller receives return value in R0]
```

## 4.2 Stack Frame Layout for a 6-Argument Function

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HighMem[Higher Memory Addresses]
        H1[Previous Frame]
        H2[Saved LR by callee 4 bytes]
        H3[Saved R11 FP 4 bytes]
        H4[Local variable 1 4 bytes]
        H5[Local variable 2 4 bytes]
        H6[Spilled 5th arg e 4 bytes]
        H7[Spilled 6th arg f 4 bytes]
    end
    subgraph LowMem[Lower Memory Addresses]
        L1[Current SP points here]
    end
    H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 --> H6 --> H7 --> L1
```

## 4.3 Register Allocation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CallerView[Caller View Before BL]
        A1[R0 holds arg1]
        A2[R1 holds arg2]
        A3[R2 holds arg3]
        A4[R3 holds arg4]
    end
    subgraph StackCh[Stack Channel]
        S1[SP+0 holds arg5]
        S2[SP+4 holds arg6]
    end
    subgraph CalleeView[Callee View]
        R1[R0 returns value]
    end
    A1 --> Callee
    A2 --> Callee
    A3 --> Callee
    A4 --> Callee
    S1 --> Callee
    S2 --> Callee
    subgraph Callee[Callee Function Body]
        C1[Computation]
    end
    Callee --> R1
```

## 4.4 Procedure Call Lifecycle (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Caller loads args into R0 R1 R2 R3] --> P2[Step 2: Caller pushes extra args onto stack]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Caller executes BL or BLX]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: LR gets PC plus 4 return address]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Callee pushes R11 FP and LR onto stack]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Callee sets R11 to SP plus 4 to form FP]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Callee allocates locals by subtracting SP]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Callee performs computation]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Callee places return value in R0 or R0 and R1]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Callee restores SP and pops saved registers]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Callee executes MOV PC LR or POP PC to return]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Caller resumes with return value in R0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Architecture over Physical Drawings:**
> Stack frame visualizations, register lifecycles, and calling conventions are inherently *control-flow* problems. The block-level flow diagrams above map the **state transitions** of the CPU registers and stack pointer — which is the actual engineering knowledge being tested in KTU exams — far more rigorously than a static memory picture would.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the ARM Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS). List the registers used for parameter passing and state the rule for stack alignment.**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

The **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** is a standardized calling convention that defines how functions receive arguments, return values, and preserve register state across procedure boundaries. It is a subset of the ARM ABI.

**Registers for parameter passing (in order):**
- **R0, R1, R2, R3** — used to pass the first four 32-bit arguments, left to right.
- A 64-bit value occupies two consecutive registers (e.g., R0+R1).

**Return value:**
- Integer / pointer: **R0** (and R1 for 64-bit).

**Stack alignment rule:**
The **Stack Pointer (SP) must be 8-byte aligned** at the public function entry point to enable efficient LDRD/STRD operations.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between scratch (caller-saved) and callee-saved registers in the ARM AAPCS. Give two examples of each.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Scratch (Caller-saved) | Callee-saved |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Responsibility | Caller must save them *before* the call if needed | Callee must save them *inside* the function if it modifies them |
| Reason for name | The callee is *allowed* to trash them | The callee *guarantees* they survive the call |
| Examples | **R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, R14 (LR)** | **R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10, R11 (FP)** |
| Use case | Argument passing, temporaries | Variables that span across a function call |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module 3 Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — (a) Explain the AAPCS in detail. How are parameters passed when there are more than 4 arguments? Illustrate with an example of a 5-argument function. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Draw and explain the stack frame layout for a function that saves R4, R5, R11, LR; has 3 local 32-bit variables; and receives 5 integer arguments. Compute the total frame size. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

#### **Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**AAPCS Overview (2 marks):**
The AAPCS is the procedure calling standard for ARM. It prescribes:
- **R0–R3**: argument registers (scratch).
- **R4–R11**: variable registers (callee-saved).
- **R12 (IP)**: intra-procedure scratch.
- **R13 (SP)**: stack pointer, must be 8-byte aligned at function entry.
- **R14 (LR)**: link register, holds return address.
- **R0 (and R1)**: return value channel.

**Passing > 4 Arguments (3 marks):**
When there are more than 4 arguments:
1. The first four are placed in **R0, R1, R2, R3** (left to right).
2. The 5th, 6th, etc. are **pushed onto the Full Descending Stack** by the caller *before* executing the `BL` instruction.
3. The callee accesses them via offsets from the **Frame Pointer (FP = R11)** or SP.

**Example — 5-argument add (2 marks):**

```c
int add5(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e) {
    return a + b + c + d + e;
}
```

**Mapping:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &\rightarrow R0 \\
b &\rightarrow R1 \\
c &\rightarrow R2 \\
d &\rightarrow R3 \\
e &\rightarrow \text{Stack[SP+0]} \quad \text{(spilled)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Caller code:**

```asm
MOV R0, #1          ; a = 1
MOV R1, #2          ; b = 2
MOV R2, #3          ; c = 3
MOV R3, #4          ; d = 4
SUB SP, SP, #4      ; reserve space for 5th arg
MOV R4, #5
STR R4, [SP]        ; e = 5 placed at [SP]
BL add5
ADD SP, SP, #4      ; clean up after call
```

**Callee code:**

```asm
add5:
    PUSH {R11, LR}
    ADD R11, SP, #4
    LDR R4, [R11, #8]   ; load 'e' from [FP+8]
    ADD R0, R0, R1
    ADD R0, R0, R2
    ADD R0, R0, R3
    ADD R0, R0, R4
    POP {R11, PC}       ; return; result in R0
```

**Final value in R0:** $1+2+3+4+5 = 15$.

#### **Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Stack Frame Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Higher Address] --> H2[Caller frame: LR backup etc]
    H2 --> S1[Saved LR 4 bytes]
    S1 --> S2[Saved R11 FP 4 bytes]
    S2 --> S3[Saved R5 4 bytes]
    S3 --> S4[Saved R4 4 bytes]
    S4 --> L1[Local 1: 4 bytes]
    L1 --> L2[Local 2: 4 bytes]
    L2 --> L3[Local 3: 4 bytes]
    L3 --> SP1[Spilled arg 5: 4 bytes]
    SP1 --> SP2[Current SP points here - Lowest Address]
```

**Frame Size Computation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{saved}} &= 4 \text{ registers} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{locals}} &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{spilled}} &= (5 - 4) \times 4 = 4 \text{ bytes} \\
S_{\text{partial}} &= 16 + 12 + 4 = 32 \text{ bytes} \\
P_{\text{align}} &= (8 - (32 \bmod 8)) \bmod 8 = 0 \\
S_{\text{frame}} &= 32 \text{ bytes} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B — (a) With a neat diagram, explain the procedure call lifecycle in ARM. Show the role of LR, SP, and FP at each stage. (7 Marks)**
**(b) A function `compute(int p, int q, int r, int s, int t, int u, int v)` is called. Show how each parameter is passed and write the corresponding ARM assembly for both caller and callee. (7 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

#### **Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Procedure Call Lifecycle Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Caller loads p q r s in R0 R1 R2 R3] --> S2[Stage 2: Caller pushes 5th 6th 7th args onto stack]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Caller executes BL: LR gets PCplus4]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Callee pushes R11 FP and LR onto stack]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Callee sets R11 equals SP plus 4 forming FP]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Callee subtracts SP to allocate locals]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Callee reads spilled args via FP offsets]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8: Callee performs computation]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 9: Callee places return in R0]
    S9 --> S10[Stage 10: Callee adjusts SP back]
    S10 --> S11[Stage 11: Callee pops saved regs and returns via POP PC]
    S11 --> S12[Stage 12: Caller uses return value in R0]
```

**Role of Special Registers (3 marks):**

| Register | Stage Used | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LR (R14)** | Stage 3 (BL) and Stage 11 (POP PC) | Holds the return address; loaded automatically by BL; consumed by POP {... , PC} on return |
| **SP (R13)** | Stages 2, 4, 6, 10 | Points to the current top of stack; adjusted on every push/pop |
| **FP (R11)** | Stages 5, 7 | Provides a stable reference point for accessing spilled arguments and local variables, even if SP is modified inside the function |

#### **Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Parameter Mapping (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &\rightarrow R0 \\
q &\rightarrow R1 \\
r &\rightarrow R2 \\
s &\rightarrow R3 \\
t &\rightarrow \text{Stack[SP+0]} \\
u &\rightarrow \text{Stack[SP+4]} \\
v &\rightarrow \text{Stack[SP+8]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Caller Assembly (2 marks):**

```asm
main:
    MOV R0, #11        ; p = 11
    MOV R1, #22        ; q = 22
    MOV R2, #33        ; r = 33
    MOV R3, #44        ; s = 44
    SUB SP, SP, #12    ; reserve 3 words for t, u, v
    MOV R4, #55
    MOV R5, #66
    MOV R6, #77
    STR R4, [SP, #0]   ; t at [SP+0]
    STR R5, [SP, #4]   ; u at [SP+4]
    STR R6, [SP, #8]   ; v at [SP+8]
    BL compute
    ADD SP, SP, #12    ; cleanup
```

**Callee Assembly (3 marks):**

```asm
compute:
    PUSH {R4, R5, R6, R11, LR}   ; save callee-saved regs + FP + LR
    ADD R11, SP, #16              ; FP = SP + 4*4 (skip 4 saved regs)
    ; R0..R3 = p, q, r, s
    LDR R4, [R11, #4]             ; load 't' (offset from FP: previous FP+0=arg1 area)
    LDR R5, [R11, #8]             ; load 'u'
    LDR R6, [R11, #12]            ; load 'v'
    ; sum everything
    ADD R0, R0, R1
    ADD R0, R0, R2
    ADD R0, R0, R3
    ADD R0, R0, R4
    ADD R0, R0, R5
    ADD R0, R0, R6
    POP {R4, R5, R6, R11, PC}     ; restore and return; sum in R0
```

**Numerical Result:** $11+22+33+44+55+66+77 = 308$. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 8-byte alignment:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if you push an odd number of registers (3, 5) without compensating — write a `SUB SP, SP, #4` padding if needed.
> 2. **Wrong return mechanism:** Using `MOV PC, LR` in a non-leaf function after a nested `BL` will return to the *wrong* address (the inner call clobbered LR). Use `POP {..., PC}` instead.
> 3. **Confusing R11 with R7:** R7 may serve as FP in some Thumb modes, but under the standard AAPCS in ARM state, **R11 is the Frame Pointer**. Examiners check this distinction.
> 4. **Spilling order:** The 5th (and beyond) arguments are placed in *ascending* memory order starting at the old SP. If a student places the rightmost arg first, they get the parameter ordering wrong by 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to restore SP** in the caller after a `SUB SP, SP, #n` for spilled args — the stack will drift downward, eventually crashing the system.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- **Calling convention** = the *contract* between caller and callee; AAPCS is ARM's version.
- **First 4 arguments** go in **R0, R1, R2, R3** (always, regardless of size promotion for sub-32-bit types).
- **64-bit values** (e.g., `double`, `long long`) take **2 consecutive registers** — R0+R1 or R2+R3.
- **5th argument onward** → pushed onto **Full Descending Stack** by the *caller*, in *ascending* memory order.
- **Return value** → **R0** (single word) or **R0+R1** (64-bit).
- **Scratch registers (caller-saved):** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR — may be trashed by callee.
- **Variable registers (callee-saved):** R4–R11, SP — callee must preserve.
- **Stack alignment:** SP **must be 8-byte aligned** at *public* function entry.
- **Frame Pointer (FP = R11)** provides a stable reference to the stack frame; set via `ADD R11, SP, #N` after pushing LR + FP.
- **Return mechanism:** `MOV PC, LR` for *leaf* functions (no nested calls); `POP {..., PC}` for non-leaf functions.
- **Structs > 16 bytes** are returned via caller-allocated memory; pointer passed in R0.
- **Floating-point (VFP/NEON)** uses a *separate* register file (s0–s15 / d0–d7 / q0–q15) — not covered in basic AAPCS rules.
- **Mnemonic:** "**A**rguments are **A**-registers (R0–R3); **V**ariables are **V**-registers (R4–R11)."
- **Stack Frame Size Formula:** $S_{\text{frame}} = S_{\text{saved}} + S_{\text{locals}} + S_{\text{spilled}} + P_{\text{align}}$.
- **Always remember to restore the caller's SP** if you pushed spilled args before the `BL`.
- **BL vs BLX:** `BL` switches to a label *without* mode change; `BLX` can also switch between ARM/Thumb states.
- **Variadic functions (like `printf`)** force all variadic args onto the stack because the callee cannot know at runtime how many registers were used — a special case in AAPCS.
- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)** must push R0–R3, R12, LR on entry (the "caller-saved" set) and pop them on exit, because the interrupted code is the implicit "caller" under AAPCS.
- **Common mistake:** forgetting to push LR when the callee itself calls another function → return address clobbered → program jumps to a wild address. Always push LR if your function contains a `BL`/`BLX`.

---

**End of Notes — Parameter Passing, Module 3, PEECT525 (ARM Architecture, Programming and Interfacing), KTU 2024 Scheme.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Return Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Return Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)** — the standard governing subroutine linkage in ARM — a **Return Value** is the data result produced by a called function (callee) and delivered back to the calling function (caller) upon function completion. The AAPCS mandates that scalar return values up to **32 bits** (one word) be placed in the general-purpose register **R0**. For 64-bit composite return values, **R0** holds the lower word and **R1** holds the upper word. Floating-point scalar returns use the **S0** (single-precision) or **D0** (double-precision) FPU registers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEECT525 / Module 3**
> The ARM Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS) defines the contract between caller and callee. The two cornerstones of this contract are: (1) **Parameter Passing** (covered in earlier Module 3 topics) and (2) **Return Value Conventions** (this topic). Mastering these is essential for writing modular ARM assembly programs and forms a recurring 7 to 14 mark question in KTU End Semester Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Receipt Counter" Intuition

Imagine a courier counter at a post office:

- You (the **caller**) hand a parcel to the clerk (the **callee/subroutine**) along with a special request slip.
- The clerk goes into the back room, processes the parcel, and hands you back **a receipt (the return value)** — this receipt is *always placed in your hand first* (think of **R0** as the "first hand" register).
- The clerk then says "come back if you need more" — this happens through the **Link Register (LR = R14)**, which stores the return address (the post office branch you came from).
- Finally, the clerk steps out from behind the counter by branching back to your original location using `BX LR` or `POP {PC}`.

**The complete journey**:
1. Caller puts argument in R0–R3 → calls subroutine using `BL SubroutineName`
2. Hardware automatically copies `PC + 4` into **LR (R14)**
3. Subroutine executes its body
4. Subroutine places result in **R0** (and R1 for 64-bit)
5. Subroutine returns to address in **LR** using `MOV PC, LR` or `BX LR`
6. Caller reads **R0** for the result

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams**
> Think of **R0** as the **R**esult register. It is *always* the primary return register in AAPCS — never R1, R2, or R3 for a 32-bit return. This is a 1-mark question virtually every KTU exam cycle.

## 1.3 The AAPCS Return Value Register Map

| Return Value Type | ARM Register Used | Size | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 32-bit integer / pointer / enum | **R0** | 32 bits | AAPCS §5.1 |
| 64-bit integer / struct (small) | **R0 (low), R1 (high)** | 64 bits | AAPCS §5.1.1 |
| Single-precision float | **S0** | 32 bits | VFP/FP AAPCS |
| Double-precision float | **D0** | 64 bits | VFP/FP AAPCS |
| 128-bit SIMD / large struct | **R0–R3** or memory pointer | 128 bits | AAPCS §5.4 |
| Return address | **LR (R14)** | 32 bits | Architecture |

> [!TIP]
> **Constants and Standards used in this Module**
> - **R0 through R12**: General-purpose, caller-saved (R0–R3 are *scratch*; R4–R11 must be preserved by callee)
> - **R13 = SP**: Stack Pointer
> - **R14 = LR**: Link Register (holds return address)
> - **R15 = PC**: Program Counter
> - **Word size in ARM**: **32 bits = 4 bytes**
> - **Doubleword**: **64 bits = 8 bytes**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Phase Return Value Lifecycle

The return value mechanism in ARM is best understood as a structured lifecycle with three phases:

### Phase 1 — Pre-call Setup (Caller Side)
- The caller loads the function arguments into **R0, R1, R2, R3** (in left-to-right order).
- If more than 4 arguments exist, additional arguments are pushed onto the **stack** in reverse order.
- The caller issues `BL FunctionName`, which:
  - Stores the address of the next instruction (`PC + 4`) into **LR**.
  - Branches to the target label.

### Phase 2 — Function Body (Callee Side)
- The callee typically begins with a **prologue**: `PUSH {R4-R11, LR}` to preserve callee-saved registers and the return address.
- The callee executes its algorithm.
- Before returning, the callee places the **return value into R0** (and R1 for 64-bit).
- The callee executes the **epilogue**: `POP {R4-R11, PC}` to restore saved registers and return simultaneously by loading the saved LR value into PC.

### Phase 3 — Post-return (Caller Side)
- Control returns to the instruction following the `BL` instruction in the caller.
- The caller reads **R0** (and **R1** for 64-bit) to obtain the return value.

## 2.2 Return Address Mechanics — The Link Register (LR)

The formula for the value placed into LR during a `BL` instruction is:

$$
\text{LR} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 4
$$

Where **PC** at the time of execution of `BL` contains the address of the `BL` instruction itself (in ARM state) and the return address points to the instruction immediately following `BL`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PC + 4 and not PC + 8?**
> In the classic ARM 3-stage pipeline (Fetch → Decode → Execute), the PC is read as `current_instruction_address + 8` during execution, but the `BL` instruction is defined to store the address of the *next sequential instruction*, which is `PC + 4` at the architectural level. This subtle distinction is a **favourite KTU 3-mark question**.

## 2.3 The Three Standard Return Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Instruction | Use Case | Stack Required? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple leaf return | `MOV PC, LR` | Leaf function that does not call others and does not push LR | No |
| Indirect return | `BX LR` | Returning from ARM/Thumb interworking functions; also handles bit-0 | No |
| Epilogue return | `POP {..., PC}` | After pushing LR in prologue — restores and returns in one step | Yes |
| Stack-frame epilogue | `LDMIA SP!, {R4-R11, PC}` | Same as above in older syntax | Yes |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rule Sheet

| # | Rule / Formula | Statement | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary 32-bit return register | Result → **R0** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | 64-bit return register pair | Low → **R0**, High → **R1** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | LR update on `BL` | `LR = address of next instruction` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Caller-saved registers | **R0–R3, R12, LR** may be clobbered by callee | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Callee-saved registers | **R4–R11** must be preserved by callee (push/pop) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Return via MOV | `MOV PC, LR` restores control | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Return via POP | `POP {PC}` when LR was on stack | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Floating-point return | Single → **S0**, Double → **D0** | ⭐⭐ |
| 9 | Large struct return | Caller passes pointer in R0, callee writes to memory | ⭐⭐ |
| 10 | `BX LR` bit-0 significance | LSB = 1 indicates Thumb state; processor auto-switches | ⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> The AAPCS return value convention is the **backbone of all C-to-ARM compilation**. Whenever a C compiler (like `arm-none-eabi-gcc`) generates assembly for a function returning an `int`, it places the return in `R0`. Embedded firmware engineers, IoT developers, and RTOS kernel programmers all rely on this convention to write mixed C/assembly routines. Misunderstanding it causes **hard-to-debug stack corruption and silent value corruption** — a critical skill for KTU lab viva and KTU campus placement interviews at companies like Bosch, Tata Elxsi, and NXP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LR Value During a Branch-with-Link

The ARM architecture uses a 3-stage pipeline: **Fetch → Decode → Execute**. When the `BL subroutine` instruction is in the Execute stage, the value of `PC` (read internally) equals the address of the instruction **two stages ahead** of the current Execute instruction.

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{read inside BL execute stage}} = \text{Address of BL} + 8
$$

However, the architectural specification defines the link value as the address of the **instruction after** the `BL`:

$$
\text{LR} = \text{Address of next instruction} = \text{Address of BL} + 4
$$

**Explanatory trace** (assuming `BL` is at address `0x00008000`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address of BL instruction} &= \text{0x00008000} \\
\text{Next sequential instruction address} &= \text{0x00008000} + 4 = \text{0x00008004} \\
\text{LR after BL execution} &= \text{0x00008004} \\
\text{Target branch address} &= \text{Address of subroutine label} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — 32-bit Integer Return

**Problem**: Write an ARM assembly function `ADD_TWO` that takes two integers in R0 and R1 and returns their sum in R0.

**Solution Trace**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input: R0} &= 25, \quad \text{R1} = 17 \\
\text{ADD R0, R0, R1} &\Rightarrow \text{R0} = 25 + 17 = 42 \\
\text{Return value in R0} &= 42 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Complete ARM Code**:

```armasm
    AREA AddTwo, CODE, READONLY
    EXPORT ADD_TWO

ADD_TWO
    ADD R0, R0, R1        ; R0 = R0 + R1  (return value placed in R0)
    MOV PC, LR            ; Return to caller

    END
```

**Caller side (illustrates the result pickup)**:

```armasm
    MOV R0, #25            ; First argument
    MOV R1, #17            ; Second argument
    BL  ADD_TWO            ; LR = address of "MOV R3, R0", R0 = 42 on return
    MOV R3, R0             ; R3 = 42  (capture the return value)
```

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — 64-bit Return (Multiplication of Two 32-bit Integers)

When two 32-bit integers are multiplied, the result may be 64 bits. The AAPCS mandates that the low 32 bits go in **R0** and the high 32 bits in **R1**.

**C-equivalent declaration**:
```c
long long MUL32(int a, int b);   // returns 64-bit value
```

**ARM assembly using UMULL** (Unsigned Multiply Long):

```armasm
MUL_32_TO_64
    UMULL R0, R1, R0, R2   ; R0 = low 32 bits, R1 = high 32 bits
    MOV   PC, LR            ; Return (R0:R1 holds the 64-bit result)
```

**Numerical trace** with R0 = 70000, R2 = 100000:

$$
\begin{aligned}
70000 \times 100000 &= 7{,}000{,}000{,}000 \\
\text{Low 32 bits (R0)} &= \text{0xB1A2BC00} \\
\text{High 32 bits (R1)} &= \text{0x00000001} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Stack-Based Epilogue Return

When a non-leaf function (a function that itself calls other functions) is used, the original `LR` is overwritten by the nested `BL`. To preserve it, the function pushes `LR` onto the stack and pops it directly into `PC`.

**Full pattern**:

```armasm
FUNC_WITH_EPILOGUE
    PUSH {R4-R11, LR}      ; Save callee-saved registers AND the return address
    ; ... function body that may call other functions ...
    ; ... place return value in R0 ...
    POP {R4-R11, PC}       ; Restore registers and return in ONE instruction
```

**LR preservation trace**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before BL to FUNC_WITH_EPILOGUE:}\quad \text{LR}_{\text{old}} &= \text{0x00008100} \\
\text{PUSH writes LR}_{\text{old}}\text{ to stack at [SP-4]} &\Rightarrow \text{stack}[SP-4] = \text{0x00008100} \\
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} - 36 \quad (\text{9 words: R4–R11, LR}) \\
\text{After inner BL: LR} &= \text{(inner function return address)} \\
\text{Function body places return value in R0} &\\
\text{POP loads stack[SP]} &\rightarrow \text{R4, ..., R11, PC} \\
\text{PC} &\leftarrow \text{0x00008100} = \text{LR}_{\text{old}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Return Mechanism

| Scenario | Recommended Return Instruction | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Leaf function, no stack | `MOV PC, LR` | LR is still valid; fastest path |
| Non-leaf function (calls others) | `POP {..., PC}` | LR was clobbered by inner BL; need to restore |
| ARM ↔ Thumb interworking | `BX LR` | Honours the bit-0 Thumb state bit |
| Simple register restore only | `POP {R4-R11}` then `MOV PC, LR` | When LR must be popped separately for safety |
| Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) | `SUBS PC, LR, #4` | Adjusts for the pipelined PC offset during interrupt |
| Exception return | `MOVS PC, LR` or `RFEFD` | Restores CPSR and PC atomically |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question**
> A common KTU question asks: *"In a leaf subroutine, why don't we need to push LR?"* The answer: a leaf subroutine does not call any other subroutine, so the `BL` instruction that entered the leaf subroutine is the only `BL` affecting `LR`. The original return address is therefore still intact in `LR` at the time of return.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Return Value Flow in AAPCS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CALLER FUNCTION"] -->|R0 = arg1, R1 = arg2| B["ISSUE BL SUBROUTINE"]
    B -->|LR := PC + 4| C["LR UPDATED AUTOMATICALLY"]
    B -->|Branch to subroutine| D["CALLEE SUBROUTINE ENTRY"]
    D -->|Optional PUSH LR| E["SAVE LR ON STACK"]
    E --> F["FUNCTION BODY EXECUTES"]
    F -->|Place 32-bit result in R0| G["PREPARE RETURN VALUE"]
    F -->|Place 64-bit in R0 R1| H["PREPARE 64-BIT RETURN"]
    F -->|Place float in S0 or D0| I["PREPARE FLOAT RETURN"]
    G --> J["EPILOGUE: POP PC OR MOV PC LR"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -->|PC := LR value| K["RETURN TO CALLER"]
```
