```mermaid
    K -->|R0 holds result| L["CALLER READS R0"]
    L --> M["USE RETURN VALUE"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Caller–Callee Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant LR as Link Register R14
    participant Callee
    participant Stack

    Caller->>Caller: Load R0=arg1, R1=arg2
    Caller->>LR: BL SUBROUTINE (LR <- PC+4)
    Caller->>Callee: Branch to SUBROUTINE
    Callee->>Stack: PUSH {R4-R11, LR}
    Callee->>Callee: Execute function body
    Callee->>Callee: Place result in R0
    Callee->>Stack: POP {R4-R11, PC}
    Callee-->>Caller: Return (PC <- saved LR)
    Caller->>Caller: Read R0 as return value
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Return Register Allocation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS ["ARGUMENTS FROM CALLER"]
        A0["R0 - arg1"] --> CALLEE_BLOCK
        A1["R1 - arg2"] --> CALLEE_BLOCK
        A2["R2 - arg3"] --> CALLEE_BLOCK
        A3["R3 - arg4"] --> CALLEE_BLOCK
    end

    subgraph CALLEE_BLOCK ["CALLEE SUBROUTINE EXECUTES"]
        BODY["Function body uses R0-R12 freely"] --> RES_PREP["Prepare return value"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS ["RETURN VALUES TO CALLER"]
        RES_PREP -->|32-bit result| OUT0["R0 - primary return"]
        RES_PREP -->|64-bit high word| OUT1["R1 - secondary return"]
        RES_PREP -->|float result| OUTS0["S0 - float return"]
        RES_PREP -->|double result| OUTD0["D0 - double return"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Memory Map of Stack During Function Call (Schematic)

```
HIGH ADDRESS
+-------------------+
|       ...         |
+-------------------+
|  Return address   |  <-- Pushed LR  (saved by callee)
|     (LR value)    |
+-------------------+
|   Saved R11       |
+-------------------+
|   Saved R10       |
+-------------------+
|   Saved R9        |
+-------------------+
|   Saved R8        |
+-------------------+
|   Saved R7        |
+-------------------+
|   Saved R6        |
+-------------------+
|   Saved R5        |
+-------------------+
|   Saved R4        |
+-------------------+
|  (stack args 5+)  |  <-- If >4 args, pushed by caller before BL
LOW ADDRESS  ---->   SP points here after PUSH
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows a typical **non-leaf function** stack frame. The order of saving is "highest register first" so that the stack pointer remains doubleword-aligned at the bottom. KTU exams often ask students to draw this frame — practice it for the 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: According to the ARM Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS), which register is used to return a 32-bit integer value from a subroutine? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
According to the AAPCS, a 32-bit integer return value is placed in the **general-purpose register R0**. This is justified by the AAPCS specification, which states that R0 serves as the primary return register for word-sized results. R1 is used only in conjunction with R0 for 64-bit return values, where R0 holds the lower-order 32 bits and R1 holds the upper-order 32 bits. *(1 mark for naming R0, 1 mark for stating the AAPCS basis, 1 mark for mentioning the R0+R1 64-bit extension)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Explain the role of the Link Register (LR, R14) in a subroutine call. What value is stored in LR when a `BL` instruction is executed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The Link Register (R14) holds the **return address** of a subroutine call, allowing the processor to resume execution at the correct point in the caller after the subroutine completes. When a `BL subroutine` instruction is executed, the address of the **instruction immediately following the BL** is automatically written into LR. In an ARM 3-stage pipeline, this corresponds to `PC + 4` at the architectural level. The function then typically returns by executing `MOV PC, LR` or by popping a previously-saved LR value into PC. *(1 mark for LR = return address, 1 mark for BL stores next-instruction address, 1 mark for usage in return mechanism)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the ARM Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS) rules for parameter passing and return values. Write an ARM assembly program to add two 32-bit numbers using a subroutine that returns the sum in R0.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the AAPCS rules for parameter passing and return values with a block diagram.**

**Model Answer**:

The ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS) is a specification that governs the binary interface between separately compiled or assembled modules. It defines how subroutines receive parameters and how they return results.

**Parameter Passing Rules**:
1. The first four 32-bit word arguments are passed in registers **R0, R1, R2, R3** in left-to-right order.
2. Additional arguments (5th onwards) are passed on the stack, pushed in right-to-left order.
3. 64-bit arguments are aligned to an even-numbered register pair (e.g., R0:R1 or R2:R3).
4. Floating-point arguments are passed in FPU registers S0–S15 (single-precision) or D0–D7 (double-precision).

**Return Value Rules**:
1. A 32-bit scalar return value is placed in **R0**.
2. A 64-bit scalar return value is placed in **R0 (low)** and **R1 (high)**.
3. Single-precision float return → **S0**; double-precision float return → **D0**.
4. Structures larger than 4 bytes are returned by the caller allocating memory and passing a pointer in R0.
5. The return address is held in **LR (R14)**; the callee uses `MOV PC, LR` or `POP {PC}` to return.

**Block Diagram** (recap of Section 4.3 above) shows argument inputs in R0–R3, the callee body, and return outputs in R0/R1/S0/D0. *(Full 7 marks: 2 marks for parameter rules, 2 marks for return rules, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for register responsibilities)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write the ARM assembly program for the subroutine that adds two numbers and returns the sum in R0. Show the complete caller-callee interaction.**

**Model Answer (ARM Assembly Code)**:

```armasm
        AREA AdditionProgram, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT __main

__main
        MOV     R0, #45            ; First argument = 45
        MOV     R1, #75            ; Second argument = 75
        BL      ADD_SUB            ; Call subroutine; LR := return address
        LDR     R2, =RESULT        ; Load address of RESULT
        STR     R0, [R2]           ; Store return value (R0) into memory
STOP    B       STOP                ; Infinite loop to halt

ADD_SUB
        ADD     R0, R0, R1         ; R0 = R0 + R1  (the sum = 120)
        MOV     PC, LR             ; Return to caller; R0 holds the result

        AREA DataSection, DATA, READWRITE
RESULT  DCD     0                   ; Reserve 4 bytes for the return value

        END
```

**Explanation**:
- `MOV R0, #45` and `MOV R1, #75` place the arguments in R0 and R1 per AAPCS.
- `BL ADD_SUB` branches to the subroutine and saves the return address in LR.
- Inside the subroutine, `ADD R0, R0, R1` produces the sum in R0.
- `MOV PC, LR` returns control to the instruction after the BL.
- The caller stores R0 (the return value = 120) into RESULT.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for caller code with correct AAPCS, 2 marks for subroutine code, 2 marks for return value handling, 1 mark for proper comments and area declarations)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the role of the Link Register (LR) and the Stack Pointer (SP) in subroutine linkage. Write an ARM assembly program that uses a non-leaf subroutine to multiply two numbers and return a 64-bit result, demonstrating proper LR preservation using the stack.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain LR and SP roles in subroutine linkage.**

**Model Answer**:

**Link Register (LR = R14)**:
- The Link Register is a dedicated general-purpose register that holds the **return address** when a subroutine is called.
- It is automatically loaded by the `BL` instruction with the address of the next instruction (`PC + 4` at architectural level).
- For a **leaf subroutine** (one that calls no other subroutines), LR remains valid throughout and the function can return via `MOV PC, LR`.
- For a **non-leaf subroutine** (one that calls other subroutines), the inner `BL` overwrites LR, so the original return address must be preserved on the stack at function entry.

**Stack Pointer (SP = R13)**:
- SP points to the **top of the stack** in memory and is updated automatically by `PUSH` and `POP` instructions.
- In AAPCS, the stack is **Full Descending (FD)** — it grows downward in memory, and SP always points to the last occupied location.
- SP is used to save:
  1. **Callee-saved registers** (R4–R11) so the caller sees them unchanged.
  2. **LR** for non-leaf functions.
  3. **Local variables** of the function.
  4. **Arguments beyond 4** passed by the caller.
- The stack must remain **8-byte (doubleword) aligned** at public function entry (AAPCS requirement) — this is critical for compatibility with VFP/NEON operations and for certain ARM linker requirements.

**Cooperation**:
- On entry, the function pushes LR (and any callee-saved registers) using `PUSH {R4-R11, LR}`, decrementing SP.
- On exit, the function pops the saved values using `POP {R4-R11, PC}`, which simultaneously restores the saved registers and writes the saved LR into PC, achieving a return in a single instruction.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for LR explanation, 2 marks for SP explanation, 2 marks for FD/alignment rules, 1 mark for PUSH/POP epilogue explanation)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an ARM assembly program for a non-leaf subroutine that multiplies two 32-bit numbers and returns a 64-bit result.**

**Model Answer**:

```armasm
        AREA MultiplyProgram, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT __main

__main
        MOV     R0, #50000           ; First argument
        MOV     R1, #40000           ; Second argument
        BL      MUL_NONLEAF          ; R0,R1 will hold 64-bit result
        LDR     R2, =RESULT_LOW
        LDR     R3, =RESULT_HIGH
        STR     R0, [R2]             ; Store low 32 bits
        STR     R1, [R3]             ; Store high 32 bits
STOP    B       STOP

; ---- Non-leaf subroutine: calls an inner helper ----
MUL_NONLEAF
        PUSH    {R4-R11, LR}         ; Save callee-saved regs + LR
        ; Now demonstrate non-leaf nature by calling an inner helper
        MOV     R4, R0               ; Save R0 in callee-saved R4
        MOV     R5, R1               ; Save R1 in callee-saved R5
        BL      INNER_MUL            ; This BL would have clobbered LR
        ; R0, R1 now hold the 64-bit result from INNER_MUL
        POP     {R4-R11, PC}         ; Restore and return in one step

INNER_MUL
        ; Inputs: R0, R1 are already in place
        UMULL   R0, R1, R4, R5       ; R0 = low, R1 = high of R4*R5
        MOV     PC, LR               ; Return to MUL_NONLEAF (LR was preserved on stack by MUL_NONLEAF's PUSH)

        AREA DataSection, DATA, READWRITE
RESULT_LOW  DCD  0
RESULT_HIGH DCD  0

        END
```

**Explanation of LR Preservation in Non-Leaf Subroutine**:
- When `BL MUL_NONLEAF` is executed from `__main`, LR = `address of "LDR R2, =RESULT_LOW"`.
- Inside `MUL_NONLEAF`, the `PUSH {R4-R11, LR}` saves this return address on the stack.
- The inner `BL INNER_MUL` overwrites LR with the address of `POP` in `MUL_NONLEAF`. This is safe because `MUL_NONLEAF`'s original return address is safely on the stack.
- `INNER_MUL` returns via `MOV PC, LR` to `MUL_NONLEAF`.
- `MUL_NONLEAF` then executes `POP {R4-R11, PC}`, which simultaneously restores R4–R11 and writes the original saved return address into PC, returning to `__main`.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for non-leaf identification, 2 marks for PUSH/POP usage, 2 marks for 64-bit result placement, 1 mark for comments and correctness)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Returning 32-bit value in R1 instead of R0** — a 0.5 to 1 mark deduction. AAPCS strictly mandates **R0** for primary 32-bit return.
> 2. **Forgetting to preserve LR in non-leaf functions** — leads to the famous "returning to garbage address" HardFault. Costs 2–3 marks in the 14-mark question.
> 3. **Confusing `MOV PC, LR` with `BX LR`** — students write `BX LR` for a non-interworking return. While functionally correct, it triggers an unnecessary mode switch check. Examiners deduct for lack of conceptual clarity.
> 4. **Pushing LR but popping into R14** instead of PC — this is a functional error; the function never returns. Major deduction.
> 5. **Stack misalignment** — pushing an odd number of registers breaks AAPCS 8-byte alignment. Examiners check this in long-answer diagrams.
> 6. **Wrong register pair for 64-bit return** — students place the high word in R0 and low word in R1. AAPCS specifies **R0 = LOW, R1 = HIGH**. Deduct 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **R0 is the universal 32-bit return register** under AAPCS — memorize this fact.
- **R0 + R1 together return 64-bit values**, with R0 = low word, R1 = high word.
- **LR (R14) is automatically loaded with `PC + 4` (architectural)** when a `BL` instruction executes.
- **Three return mechanisms**: `MOV PC, LR` (leaf), `POP {PC}` (after saving LR), `BX LR` (Thumb interworking).
- **Callee-saved registers (R4–R11)** must be preserved using `PUSH` and `POP` if used; failing to do so corrupts the caller's state.
- **Caller-saved registers (R0–R3, R12)** may be freely modified by the callee without preservation.
- **Non-leaf subroutines MUST preserve LR on stack** using `PUSH {..., LR}` and restore via `POP {..., PC}`.
- **Stack is Full Descending (FD)** in ARM; SP points to the last used address and grows downward.
- **Stack must be 8-byte aligned** at every public function entry (AAPCS rule).
- **ISR return uses `SUBS PC, LR, #4`** to compensate for the ARM pipeline's PC offset.
- **Floating-point return**: S0 for `float`, D0 for `double`, with VFP variant of AAPCS.
- **The return value is always read by the caller from R0** *after* the BL instruction completes — never from a memory location (unless the struct is large and a pointer was passed).
- **AAPCS = ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard** — the official name; do not call it "ARM Calling Convention" in KTU exams.
- **Thumb mode `BL` sets LR to `PC | 1`** (bit 0 = 1 indicates Thumb state) — this is the bit-0 significance used by `BX LR`.
- **Link Register is also used by exception handlers** — FIQ/IRQ vector automatically loads LR with a special "return-from-exception" address requiring `SUBS PC, LR, #4` to return correctly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temporary Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temporary Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temporary Variables (in ARM Architecture Context):** Temporary variables are short-lived storage locations—either CPU registers or stack-allocated memory cells—used by a program to hold intermediate computational results whose lifetime is bounded by a single expression, statement, or function scope. In ARM assembly programming, temporaries are predominantly mapped onto the **scratch registers R0–R3** and **R12 (IP)**, as defined by the **ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS)**.

In the AAPCS, registers are classified into two functional groups based on **callee-save** and **caller-save** semantics:

- **Caller-save (Scratch / Temporary) Registers:** R0–R3 and R12. The calling function is responsible for preserving these if their values must survive across a function call.
- **Callee-save Registers:** R4–R11. The called function must preserve these if it uses them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The terms *temporary variable*, *scratch register*, and *caller-save register* are used interchangeably in KTU board examinations. Students should treat them as synonyms unless explicitly qualified.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are solving a math problem on a whiteboard:

- **Long-term variables** (callee-save registers R4–R11) are like the *final answer* written in your notebook—once committed, you wouldn't expect someone else to erase it without telling you.
- **Temporary variables** (caller-save registers R0–R3) are like *rough scratch work* on a corner of the whiteboard—you use them to compute intermediate values, and you fully expect them to be wiped clean once another function (another "person") takes over the board.

In a nested function call, if you scribbled a temporary value and then called a colleague to do a sub-task, you cannot complain that your scratch work was overwritten—*you* should have copied it to your notebook (stack) before making the call.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - In **32-bit ARM (AArch32)** state, each register is **32 bits (4 bytes)** wide.
> - In **AArch64 (ARMv8)**, the general-purpose registers **W0–W30** are 32-bit windows into the 64-bit **X0–X30** registers; the same temporary semantics apply, with **X0–X18** considered caller-save.
> - The ARM Procedure Call Standard mandates a **register usage count of 16 general-purpose registers** in AArch32 (R0–R15) and **31 registers** in AArch64 (X0–X30).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Registers under AAPCS

The ARM Procedure Call Standard partitions the 16 general-purpose registers of AArch32 into four distinct roles. This classification is **the single most testable concept** in the Temporary Variables module.

| Register(s) | Alias / Role | Save Convention | Used For Temporary Values? |
|---|---|---|---|
| R0 | a1 / Arg / Return | Caller-save | **Yes** — primary temporary |
| R1 | a2 / Arg | Caller-save | **Yes** — primary temporary |
| R2 | a3 / Arg | Caller-save | **Yes** — primary temporary |
| R3 | a4 / Arg | Caller-save | **Yes** — primary temporary |
| R4–R8 | v1–v5 | Callee-save | No (long-term locals) |
| R9 | v6 / SB / TR | Platform-dependent | No |
| R10 | v7 / SL | Callee-save | No |
| R11 | v8 / FP | Callee-save (frame pointer) | No |
| R12 | IP / Intra-Procedure-call scratch | Caller-save | **Yes** — linker veneer temporary |
| R13 | SP (Stack Pointer) | Special | No |
| R14 | LR (Link Register) | Special | No |
| R15 | PC (Program Counter) | Special | No |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Temporary Register Allocation

ARM's design philosophy minimizes register pressure through the following logic chain:

1. **Function arguments** (up to four) are passed in R0–R3 → these registers are inherently *busy* with inputs.
2. The **return value** must be placed in R0 by the callee.
3. Therefore, R0–R3 are *guaranteed to be volatile* across any function call—the compiler exploits this by using them as temporaries.
4. **R12 (IP)** is reserved by the ARM-Thumb Procedure Call Standard (ATPCS) as a scratch register for **inter-procedure veneers** (e.g., when the linker must insert a trampoline to reach a far-away function). R12 is thus "trashable" between any two function calls.
5. Registers R4–R11 are too valuable (callee-save implies they survive calls) to waste on single-statement temporaries.

## 2.3 Lifetime Rules of a Temporary Variable

A temporary variable in ARM C/assembly has a precisely bounded **lifetime**:

- **Expression temporaries:** Created and destroyed within a single C expression. Example: `int t = a + b * c;` → `t` is a temporary that may be allocated to R0.
- **Statement temporaries:** Live only between two semicolons.
- **Call-site temporaries:** Hold argument values just before a `BL` instruction. Example: `add r0, r1, r2; bl sum_func` → R0 is a temporary holding the first argument.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Understanding temporaries is essential for:
> - **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs):** ISR authors must save only the temporaries they actually use, minimizing interrupt latency.
> - **Bare-metal embedded programming:** Manual register allocation (e.g., in startup code) requires explicit knowledge of which registers are safe to use as temporaries.
> - **Compiler optimization:** Recognizing temporaries helps in interpreting disassembly and debugging optimized code.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for this topic. Memorize the formulas and conventions.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Boundary | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of caller-save (temporary) registers in AArch32 | $N_{temp} = 4 + 1 = 5$ | R0, R1, R2, R3, R12 | **High** |
| Number of caller-save registers in AArch64 | $N_{temp,64} = 19$ | X0–X18 | Medium |
| Maximum function arguments via registers | $A_{max} = 4$ | R0–R3 in AArch32 | **High** |
| Stack alignment (AAPCS) | $SP \equiv 0 \pmod{8}$ | 8-byte boundary at public interface | High |
| Return value location | $R0$ (32-bit), $R0:R1$ (64-bit) | AArch32 convention | **High** |
| Temporary storage for a 32-bit local | $N_{bytes} = 4$ | Word-sized stack slot | Medium |
| Temporary storage for a 64-bit local | $N_{bytes} = 8$ | Double-word stack slot (AArch64) | Medium |
| Stack growth direction | $SP \leftarrow SP - N_{bytes}$ | Descending full-stack (FD) | Medium |
| Spilled temporaries count | $T_{spill} = \max(0,\; \text{used} - 5)$ | When $5$ regs are insufficient | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** Whenever a KTU question asks "How many temporary registers are available in ARM?", the answer for AArch32 is **5** (R0–R3, R12) — not 4. Forgetting R12 is the most common one-mark loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stack Frame Size for Spilled Temporaries

Suppose a function has $L$ local long-term variables (must be callee-saved) and $T$ temporaries that the compiler decides to spill to memory. The total stack frame size $F$ is calculated as follows.

**Step 1: Identify the components of the stack frame.**

A typical ARM stack frame (descending, full-descending) contains:

- **Saved Frame Pointer** (4 bytes) — only if frame pointer `FP` is used.
- **Saved Link Register** (4 bytes) — `LR` is pushed if the function makes calls.
- **Callee-saved registers** (4 bytes each) — for the long-term variables in R4–R11.
- **Spilled temporaries** (4 bytes each) — for temporaries that did not fit in R0–R3, R12.
- **Local arrays / structures** (variable size).
- **Outgoing argument space** — for arguments to functions it calls, beyond the four register-passed ones.

**Step 2: Write the size equation.**

$$
F = \underbrace{4 \cdot S_{FP}}_{\text{saved FP}} + \underbrace{4 \cdot S_{LR}}_{\text{saved LR}} + \underbrace{4 \cdot N_{cs}}_{\text{callee-saved regs}} + \underbrace{4 \cdot N_{spill}}_{\text{spilled temporaries}} + \underbrace{S_{locals}}_{\text{arrays/structs}} + \underbrace{4 \cdot N_{out,extra}}_{\text{stack-passed args}}
$$

where:

- $S_{FP} \in \{0, 1\}$ — whether the frame pointer is saved.
- $S_{LR} \in \{0, 1\}$ — whether the link register is saved.
- $N_{cs}$ — number of callee-save registers (R4–R11) actually used as locals.
- $N_{spill}$ — number of temporaries spilled beyond the 5 available.
- $S_{locals}$ — total bytes of local arrays/structures.
- $N_{out,extra}$ — number of arguments beyond 4 that must be passed on the stack by the *current* function to a *callee*.

**Step 3: Apply AAPCS alignment.**

The final $F$ must be rounded up so that $SP$ remains **8-byte aligned** at any public function entry:

$$
F_{final} = 8 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{F}{8} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4: Worked Example.**

A function uses 3 callee-saved registers as locals, spills 2 temporaries, calls another function with 6 arguments (so 2 are stack-passed), and has a 16-byte local array. Compute $F_{final}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= (4 \cdot 1) + (4 \cdot 1) + (4 \cdot 3) + (4 \cdot 2) + 16 + (4 \cdot 2) \\
&= 4 + 4 + 12 + 8 + 16 + 8 \\
&= 52 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply 8-byte alignment:

$$
F_{final} = 8 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{52}{8} \right\rceil = 8 \cdot 7 = 56 \text{ bytes}
$$

Therefore, the function prologue is:

$$
SP \leftarrow SP - 56
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping C Temporaries to ARM Registers

The table below traces how a typical C function with temporaries is compiled to ARM assembly. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU ESE.

| C Source Line | Compiled ARM Assembly | Temporary Register Used | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| `int a = 5;` | `MOV R4, #5` | None (R4 = long-term) | Callee-save local |
| `int b = 10;` | `MOV R5, #10` | None (R5 = long-term) | Callee-save local |
| `int t = a + b;` | `ADD R0, R4, R5` | **R0** | Scratch reg, single use |
| `foo(t, a);` | `MOV R1, R4` then `BL foo` | **R0** (already), **R1** | Args passed in R0–R3 |
| `int c = a * 2;` | `LSL R0, R4, #1` | **R0** | Reused as temporary |
| `return c;` | `MOV R0, R0` (or no-op) | **R0** | Return value in R0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the above matrix, observe that **R0 is the "universal temporary"**—it is used to compute `t`, then holds the first argument to `foo`, then is reused for `c`, and finally holds the return value. This is precisely why R0 is *caller-save*; its value is destroyed by every subroutine call, and the compiler must reload it if needed.

## 3.3 Derivation: When Must a Temporary Be Spilled?

A temporary is **spilled** (moved from register to stack) if and only if the register pressure exceeds the available scratch pool.

**Condition for no spill:**

$$
N_{live\_temporaries} \leq N_{temp} = 5
$$

**Condition for $k$ spills:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{spill} &= N_{live\_temporaries} - N_{temp} \\
&= N_{live\_temporaries} - 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** A function has 8 simultaneously-live temporaries.

$$
N_{spill} = 8 - 5 = 3
$$

These 3 temporaries occupy $3 \times 4 = 12$ bytes of stack space, and the function's prologue must reserve this:

$$
SP \leftarrow SP - 12
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Temporary Register Allocation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Function] --> B{Live Temporaries <= 5 ?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Allocate to R0 R1 R2 R3 R12]
    B -- No --> D[Allocate first 5 to R0 R1 R2 R3 R12]
    D --> E[Spill remaining to stack]
    E --> F[Reserve stack slots 4 bytes each]
    C --> G[Function Body Executes]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Function Call BL instruction ?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Save R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 to stack]
    H -- No --> J[Continue execution]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Function returns]
    K --> L[Restore callee saved R4 R11]
    L --> M[Restore SP and return via LR]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: AAPCS Register Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Regs[ARM AArch32 General Purpose Registers] --> Caller[Caller Save Scratch Temporaries]
    Regs --> Callee[Callee Save Long Term Locals]
    Regs --> Special[Special Purpose Registers]
    Caller --> R0A[R0 a1 Return]
    Caller --> R1A[R1 a2 Argument]
    Caller --> R2A[R2 a3 Argument]
    Caller --> R3A[R3 a4 Argument]
    Caller --> R12A[R12 IP Scratch]
    Callee --> R4A[R4 v1]
    Callee --> R5A[R5 v2]
    Callee --> R6A[R6 v3]
    Callee --> R7A[R4 v4]
    Callee --> R8A[R8 v5]
    Callee --> R9A[R9 v6 SB or TR]
    Callee --> R10A[R10 v7 SL]
    Callee --> R11A[R11 v8 FP]
    Special --> R13A[R13 SP Stack Pointer]
    Special --> R14A[R14 LR Link Register]
    Special --> R15A[R15 PC Program Counter]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Temporary Storage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[ARM CPU Core]
        REGS[Register File R0 to R15]
    end
    subgraph TEMP[Temporary Storage Pool]
        SCRATCH[R0 R1 R2 R3 R12 Scratch Registers]
        STACK[Stack Memory Temporary Slots]
    end
    subgraph LONG[Long Term Storage]
        CALLEE[R4 to R11 Callee Saved]
        MEM[Data Memory Global Static]
    end
    REGS --> SCRATCH
    REGS --> CALLEE
    SCRATCH --> STACK
    STACK --> MEM
    CALLEE --> MEM
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Lifetime of a Temporary Variable

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Defined
    Defined --> Allocated: Compiler assigns to R0 to R3 or R12
    Allocated --> Live: Value computed
    Live --> Consumed: Used in expression or function call
    Live --> Spilled: Register pressure exceeds 5
    Spilled --> Consumed
    Consumed --> Dead: End of statement or expression
    Dead --> [*]
    Dead --> Reused: Register reassigned to next temporary
    Reused --> Allocated
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "temporary variable" in the context of ARM programming. List the registers used as temporaries in AArch32 state.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *temporary variable* is a short-lived storage location used to hold an intermediate computational result whose lifetime is bounded by a single expression, statement, or function call. In ARM AArch32 state, the following **five registers** are designated as temporaries (caller-save / scratch registers) per the AAPCS:

- **R0, R1, R2, R3** — used for passing function arguments and returning values; inherently volatile across function calls.
- **R12 (IP)** — reserved as the Intra-Procedure-call scratch register, used by linkers and veneers.

The remaining general-purpose registers (R4–R11) are callee-saved and are used for **long-term** local variables, not temporaries. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between caller-save and callee-save registers in ARM. Why is R0–R3 classified as caller-save?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Caller-Save (R0–R3, R12) | Callee-Save (R4–R11) |
|---|---|---|
| Preservation duty | Calling function must save if needed | Called function must save if it uses |
| Typical role | Arguments, return values, temporaries | Long-term local variables |
| Lifespan across `BL` | **Not guaranteed** | Guaranteed preserved |

R0–R3 are classified as **caller-save** because the AAPCS uses them to pass the **first four function arguments** and to return the **function result in R0**. Since the called function is permitted to freely overwrite them to set up its own arguments and return value, the responsibility of preservation falls on the caller. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the AAPCS classification of ARM registers. List the temporary (caller-save) registers and justify why each is designated as such. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following C function:
```c
int compute(int a, int b, int c, int d, int e) {
    int t1 = a + b;
    int t2 = c * d;
    int t3 = t1 - t2;
    return t3 + e;
}
```
Write the equivalent ARM assembly code, clearly identifying which registers are used as temporaries and which are used as long-term locals. If the function `compute` calls another function `helper` after computing `t3`, show the necessary code to preserve temporaries. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) AAPCS Register Classification (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram. The classification is:

- **Caller-Save (Temporaries):** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12 (5 registers).
  - *Justification:* R0–R3 are mandated by AAPCS for argument passing (a1–a4) and R0 for return value. R12 is reserved as the Intra-Procedure-call scratch register (IP), explicitly set aside by the ABI for the linker to use as a temporary when inserting procedure veneers.
- **Callee-Save (Locals):** R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10, R11 (8 registers).
  - *Justification:* These are used for variables whose lifetime extends across function calls. The called function must push them to the stack and pop them before returning, ensuring their values are preserved.
- **Special Purpose:** R13 (SP), R14 (LR), R15 (PC) — dedicated roles.

**(b) ARM Assembly Translation (7 marks):**

```asm
compute:
    ; Prologue: save callee-saved registers we will use as locals
    PUSH    {R4, R5, LR}          ; reserve stack space

    ; Map C variables to ARM registers
    ; a -> R0, b -> R1, c -> R2, d -> R3, e -> [stack] (5th arg passed on stack)
    ; t1, t2, t3 -> use temporaries R0, R1, R2 (only 5 available)

    MOV     R4, R0                ; R4 = a (long-term local)
    MOV     R5, R1                ; R5 = b (long-term local)
    ; R2 already holds c, R3 already holds d, e is on stack

    ; t1 = a + b  -> use temporary R0
    ADD     R0, R4, R5            ; *** R0 is a TEMPORARY ***

    ; t2 = c * d  -> use temporary R1
    MUL     R1, R2, R3            ; *** R1 is a TEMPORARY ***

    ; t3 = t1 - t2 -> use temporary R2
    SUB     R2, R0, R1            ; *** R2 is a TEMPORARY ***

    ; If we now call helper(t3), we must preserve t3 because R0-R3 are caller-save
    ; helper is declared as: int helper(int x);
    MOV     R0, R2                ; pass t3 to helper (R0 = argument)
    BL      helper                ; *** R0, R1, R2, R3, R12 may be clobbered ***

    ; After call, t3 in R2 is GONE — must reload if still needed
    ; (in this program, we proceed to return; no further use of t3)

    ; return t3 + e
    ; reload e from stack
    LDR     R3, [SP, #8]          ; R3 = e (5th argument lives at SP+8 after PUSH of 3 regs)
    ADD     R0, R2, R3            ; BUG! R2 is no longer t3 — it was clobbered by helper
    ; Correct version: recompute t3
    SUB     R0, R0, R1            ; this is sloppy; the clean version is:
    ; ... (see corrected code below)

    ; Epilogue
    POP     {R4, R5, PC}          ; return via restored LR saved in LR slot
```

**Corrected and clean version (the version expected in a 14-mark answer):**

```asm
compute:
    PUSH    {R4, R5, LR}          ; save callee-saved + LR
    LDR     R4, [SP, #12]         ; R4 = e (5th arg, after 3 pushes = SP+12)

    ADD     R0, R0, R1            ; R0 = a + b = t1   (TEMPORARY in R0)
    MUL     R1, R2, R3            ; R1 = c * d = t2   (TEMPORARY in R1)
    SUB     R12, R0, R1           ; R12 = t3          (TEMPORARY in R12/IP)

    ; Before calling helper(t3), save t3 to a callee-save register
    MOV     R5, R12               ; R5 = t3 (preserve across call)
    MOV     R0, R12               ; R0 = t3 (argument to helper)
    BL      helper                ; R0-R3, R12 may be clobbered

    ; t3 is safely in R5; reload it
    ADD     R0, R5, R4            ; R0 = t3 + e
    POP     {R4, R5, PC}          ; return; PC <- saved LR
```

**Key observation the examiner looks for:** The student must recognize that **R0–R3 and R12 are temporaries that do NOT survive a `BL` instruction**. To preserve `t3` across the call to `helper`, the value must be moved to a **callee-saved register (R5)** before the `BL`, or pushed to the stack. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** What is a temporary variable spill? Derive the formula for the number of temporaries that must be spilled to the stack when the live temporaries exceed the available scratch registers. Calculate the stack frame size for a function that uses 3 long-term locals, spills 4 temporaries, and calls a function taking 7 arguments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an ARM assembly function that computes the **Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)** of two numbers using the Euclidean algorithm. Identify each temporary in your code and explain the role of the stack in preserving the link register. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Spill Derivation and Stack Frame Calculation (7 marks):**

A *temporary variable spill* occurs when the number of simultaneously live temporaries exceeds the number of available scratch registers ($N_{temp} = 5$ in AArch32). The excess temporaries are stored on the stack and reloaded when needed.

**Derivation:**

$$
N_{spill} = \max\!\left(0,\; N_{live} - N_{temp}\right) = \max(0,\; N_{live} - 5)
$$

**Stack frame size formula (from Section 3.1):**

$$
F = 4 \cdot S_{LR} + 4 \cdot N_{cs} + 4 \cdot N_{spill} + 4 \cdot N_{out,extra}
$$

where $N_{out,extra}$ is the number of arguments beyond 4 that are passed on the stack to the callee.

**Numerical calculation:**

- Long-term locals: $N_{cs} = 3$
- Spilled temporaries: $N_{spill} = 4$
- The function calls a callee with 7 arguments → $N_{out,extra} = 7 - 4 = 3$
- Assume LR is saved ($S_{LR} = 1$), no frame pointer.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 4 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 3 + 4 \cdot 4 + 4 \cdot 3 \\
&= 4 + 12 + 16 + 12 \\
&= 44 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply AAPCS 8-byte alignment:

$$
F_{final} = 8 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{44}{8} \right\rceil = 8 \cdot 6 = 48 \text{ bytes}
$$

The function prologue is therefore: `SUB SP, SP, #48`. (7 marks)

**(b) GCD in ARM Assembly (7 marks):**

```asm
        AREA    GCD_Code, CODE, READONLY
        ENTRY
        EXPORT  gcd

gcd:
        PUSH    {LR}               ; *** Save LR to stack (4 bytes) ***

        CMP     R1, #0             ; if b == 0, return a
        BEQ     gcd_done           ; *** R0, R1 used as TEMPORARIES throughout ***

loop:
        ; Compute a mod b
        ; a is in R0, b is in R1
        ; We need a temporary for the remainder
        UDIV    R2, R0, R1         ; R2 = a / b   (TEMPORARY in R2)
        MUL     R3, R2, R1         ; R3 = (a/b)*b (TEMPORARY in R3)
        SUB     R12, R0, R3        ; R12 = a mod b (TEMPORARY in R12)

        MOV     R0, R1             ; a = b
        MOV     R1, R12            ; b = a mod b
        CMP     R1, #0
        BNE     loop

gcd_done:
        ; R0 already holds the GCD (return value)
        POP     {PC}               ; *** Restore PC from saved LR — return ***

        END
```

**Explanation of temporaries and stack role (3 marks):**

- **R0** holds the first argument `a` and the return value — a *temporarily-used* register.
- **R1** holds the second argument `b` and is also a temporary.
- **R2, R3, R12** are pure scratch temporaries used to compute `a mod b`. They are caller-save, and since `gcd` makes no further function calls, they need not be preserved.
- **The stack** stores the **Link Register (LR)** at function entry via `PUSH {LR}` and restores it to **PC** via `POP {PC}`. This two-step preservation is essential because ARM's `BL` instruction overwrites LR with the return address; without pushing LR to the stack, a nested call inside `gcd` would clobber the return address. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting R12 (IP):** Many students answer "4 temporary registers" — the correct count is **5** (R0, R1, R2, R3, R12). This is the **single most common one-mark loss** on this topic.
> 2. **Confusing caller-save with callee-save:** A common mnemonic is: *"If the callee is allowed to scribble, it's scratch."* Memorize which side bears the saving responsibility.
> 3. **Ignoring stack alignment:** AAPCS mandates **8-byte alignment** at all public function entries. Forgetting the $8 \cdot \lceil F/8 \rceil$ rounding loses 1–2 marks in Part B calculations.
> 4. **Failing to preserve temporaries across `BL`:** When a function calls another, R0–R3 and R12 are *not* guaranteed to retain their values. Students often write `BL helper` and then continue using R2, expecting it to hold `t3` — this is wrong unless R2 was first moved to a callee-saved register.
> 5. **Mixing up LR and PC in the epilogue:** The correct epilogue is `POP {R4-R11, PC}` (not `POP {R4-R11, LR}` followed by `BX LR`). Using `POP {..., PC}` atomically restores PC and effects the return in one instruction.
> 6. **Counting SP, LR, PC as "general-purpose":** These are **special-purpose** registers and are never classified as temporaries in AAPCS.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Temporary variables** in ARM are short-lived storage mapped to **scratch / caller-save** registers.
- In **AArch32**, the temporary register pool is **R0, R1, R2, R3, and R12** — total **5 registers**.
- In **AArch64**, the pool expands to **X0–X18** — total **19 registers**.
- The **AAPCS** mandates that R0–R3 are used for the first four arguments and R0 holds the return value.
- **R12 (IP)** is explicitly reserved for inter-procedure-call scratch (linker veneers); it is *always* caller-save.
- A temporary is **spilled to the stack** if the live count exceeds **5** (AArch32) or **19** (AArch64).
- Stack frames for temporaries grow **downward** in ARM's full-descending model: $SP \leftarrow SP - 4 N_{spill}$.
- AAPCS requires **8-byte stack alignment** at every public function entry.
- The formula $F_{final} = 8 \cdot \lceil F/8 \rceil$ must be applied to every computed frame size.
- Before issuing a `BL` instruction, save any temporaries you wish to keep into **callee-save registers (R4–R11)** or push them onto the stack.
- The Link Register (LR) must be saved to the stack in the prologue of any non-leaf function, and restored via `POP {..., PC}` in the epilogue.
- Special registers **SP (R13), LR (R14), PC (R15)** are **never** temporaries.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preserving Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preserving Registers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Register Preservation** in ARM architecture refers to the strict set of rules defined by the **AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard)** that govern which general-purpose registers must be saved and restored by a subroutine (callee) and which registers may be freely modified by the callee without saving, based on the calling convention.

The ARM processor has **16 general-purpose registers** (R0–R15), and these are partitioned into two classes for subroutines:

- **Caller-Saved Registers (Scratch Registers):** R0–R3, R12, and the Link Register (LR / R14) when explicitly needed. The *caller* is responsible for saving these registers before invoking a subroutine if their values are required after the function returns.
- **Callee-Saved Registers (Preserved Registers):** R4–R11 (variable registers) and the Stack Pointer (SP / R13). The *callee* is responsible for pushing these onto the stack on entry and popping them back on exit, ensuring they retain their original values upon return.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT525 – Module 3):**
> The concept of "Preserving Registers" is a core topic under **ARM Subroutines / Functions**. Students must memorize the AAPCS register classification and be able to write subroutines that correctly use `PUSH` and `POP` instructions to preserve the working environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **AAPCS Compliance:**
> The ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard (AAPCS) is the *de facto* ABI (Application Binary Interface) standard for ARM. Any deviation (e.g., a callee clobbering R4) results in undefined behavior in the calling program and is a guaranteed **marks deduction** in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Desk" Intuition

Imagine you are working at a shared office desk with **16 drawers (registers R0–R15)**. You are about to leave your desk to attend a meeting in another room (calling a subroutine).

- **Drawers R0–R3 and R12 (Caller-Saved):** These contain *temporary, throwaway* papers — meeting agendas, scribbled notes. You don't care if someone rummages through them. If you need them later, **you must take a photocopy (push to stack) before leaving**.

- **Drawers R4–R11 (Callee-Saved):** These contain your **important project files**. Before leaving, you instruct the colleague taking over: *"Please lock these drawers and put the keys back exactly as you found them."* The colleague (callee) **must preserve** them.

- **Drawer R13 (Stack Pointer):** Your desk address book. **Nobody is allowed to move your desk.**

- **Drawer R14 (Link Register):** The "return address" — the note saying *"Return to Room 102 after the meeting."*

This convention allows **modular, re-entrant, and safe** subroutine design without one function accidentally destroying the working state of another.

## 1.3 Physical Context

The **standard metrics** associated with register preservation in the ARM AAPCS are:

- **Register File Width:** **32 bits** (in ARM state) or **64 bits** (in AArch64).
- **Number of General Purpose Registers:** **13 user-mode** (R0–R12) + **3 special** (SP=R13, LR=R14, PC=R15).
- **Stack Growth Direction:** **Full Descending** (FD) — the stack grows *downward* in memory from a high address toward a low address.
- **Standard Push/Pop Encoding:** `STMFD` / `LDMFD` (equivalent to `PUSH` / `POP` in ARM/Thumb-2 unified assembly).
- **Stack Alignment Requirement:** **8-byte aligned** (for ARM state); **16-byte aligned** (for AArch64 / ARMv8).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AAPCS Register Partitioning

The AAPCS divides the 16 registers into distinct roles. Understanding this partitioning is the **core requirement** for writing modular ARM assembly.

| Register | Alias | Role | Preservation Class | Purpose |
|----------|-------|------|-------------------|---------|
| R0 | A1 | Argument / Return | Caller-Saved | First function argument; return value (up to 32 bits) |
| R1 | A2 | Argument | Caller-Saved | Second function argument |
| R2 | A3 | Argument | Caller-Saved | Third function argument |
| R3 | A4 / V1 | Argument | Caller-Saved | Fourth function argument |
| R4 | V2 | Variable | **Callee-Saved** | Holds local variables across calls |
| R5 | V3 | Variable | **Callee-Saved** | Holds local variables across calls |
| R6 | V4 | Variable | **Callee-Saved** | Holds local variables across calls |
| R7 | V5 | Variable | **Callee-Saved** | Often used as FP (frame pointer) in optimized builds |
| R8 | V6 | Variable | **Callee-Saved** | Holds local variables across calls |
| R9 | V7 / SB / TR | Variable | **Callee-Saved** | Platform-specific; often static base |
| R10 | V8 / SL | Variable | **Callee-Saved** | Stack limit / variable |
| R11 | V9 / FP | Variable | **Callee-Saved** | Frame pointer (in AAPCS-conformant code) |
| R12 | IP / Scratch | Intra-procedure | Caller-Saved | Used as scratch by veneers and PIC code |
| R13 | SP | Stack Pointer | **Callee-Saved** | Points to the current top of the stack |
| R14 | LR | Link Register | Caller-Saved (special) | Holds the return address from `BL` |
| R15 | PC | Program Counter | N/A | Holds the address of the current instruction + 8 |

## 2.2 The "Why" — Rationale Behind the Convention

The AAPCS partitioning is not arbitrary. It is the result of a **trade-off between code size, call overhead, and register pressure**:

1. **Minimize Stack Usage:** The most frequently used registers (R0–R3 for arguments/return values) are designated as scratch so the compiler can avoid pushing/popping them on every call. In deeply nested call trees, this saves **significant** stack memory and execution cycles.

2. **Maximize Throughput:** If every register were callee-saved, every function call would require a mass save/restore, slowing down the pipeline. By giving the compiler "free scratch registers" (R0–R3, R12), leaf functions can operate without touching the stack at all.

3. **Enable Optimization:** The compiler's register allocator knows that R4–R11 are *safe* across calls, so it can park long-lived values in them without worrying about clobbering.

## 2.3 The Mechanics of Preservation — Step-by-Step Logic

When a subroutine `foo` is called by `bar`, the following sequence occurs (logically):

**Step 1 — Caller (`bar`) prepares the arguments:**  
The caller places up to 4 arguments in **R0, R1, R2, R3** (caller-saved, since their original values are no longer needed by `bar` after the call).

**Step 2 — Caller invokes the subroutine:**  
The `BL` (Branch with Link) instruction is executed. This:
- Stores the return address in **LR (R14)**.
- Branches to the subroutine's entry label.

**Step 3 — Callee (`foo`) preserves the working environment:**  
At the very start of the subroutine, the callee executes a `PUSH` instruction (e.g., `PUSH {R4-R11, LR}`) to save the callee-saved registers *and* the return address (LR is special — the callee may itself call other functions, clobbering LR).

**Step 4 — Callee performs its work:**  
The subroutine body may freely modify R0–R3, R12, and LR. It may also modify R4–R11, but their pre-modification values are safely on the stack.

**Step 5 — Callee prepares the return value:**  
The result (if any) is placed in **R0** (and **R1** for 64-bit results).

**Step 6 — Callee restores the working environment:**  
A `POP` instruction (e.g., `POP {R4-R11, PC}`) is executed. Notice that `PC` is included in the `POP` list — this simultaneously restores the saved LR value into the **Program Counter**, causing an immediate return to the caller. This is the canonical ARM idiom for function epilogues.

**Step 7 — Caller continues execution:**  
The caller resumes with R0 holding the return value, and R4–R11 unchanged from before the call.

## 2.4 The PUSH and POP Instructions — Detailed Semantics

### 2.4.1 PUSH

- **Encoding:** `PUSH{cond} <registers>` or `STMFD SP!, <registers>`.
- **Behavior:** Stores the registers on the stack in **descending order** (highest-numbered register at the lowest address), pre-decrements SP by 4 times the number of registers, and writes to memory.
- **Shorthand:** `PUSH {R4-R11}` is equivalent to `STMFD SP!, {R4-R11}`.
- **Stack Adjustment:** $\Delta SP = -4 \times N$ where $N$ is the number of registers in the list.

### 2.4.2 POP

- **Encoding:** `POP{cond} <registers>` or `LDMFD SP!, <registers>`.
- **Behavior:** Loads registers from the stack in **ascending order**, post-increments SP.
- **Special Form:** `POP {..., PC}` is the standard return idiom; it loads the value into PC, causing an immediate branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Endianness Note for KTU:**
> ARM cores can be configured as either little-endian or big-endian. In **little-endian** mode (the most common, and the default in KTU lab kits), the least significant byte is stored at the lowest address. This does not affect the logical operation of PUSH/POP, but is critical when manually inspecting stack memory.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---------|----------------|--------------|
| Stack pointer adjustment on PUSH | $\Delta SP = -4 \cdot N$ | Bytes (negative = downward) |
| Stack pointer adjustment on POP | $\Delta SP = +4 \cdot N$ | Bytes (positive = upward) |
| Number of callee-saved regs to push (minimum) | $N_{c-save} = 8$ | R4 through R11 |
| Total stack frame (callee-save only) | $S_{frame} = 32$ | **32 bytes** (8 regs × 4 bytes) |
| Total stack frame (callee-save + LR) | $S_{frame} = 36$ | Bytes (one extra for LR) |
| Stack alignment (ARM state) | $SP \equiv 0 \pmod 8$ | **8-byte aligned** |
| Stack alignment (AArch64) | $SP \equiv 0 \pmod{16}$ | **16-byte aligned** |
| Return address offset from BL | $LR = PC_{after\_BL} + 0$ | LR holds address of instruction *after* `BL` |
| Number of function arguments via regs | $N_{args} \leq 4$ | Excess args passed on the stack |
| Size of return value in R0 | **32 bits** | 64-bit returns use R0:R1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Math Quick-Reference:**
> $S_{frame} = 4 \times (N_{GPR\_saved} + N_{special\_saved})$ where $N_{GPR\_saved} \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 8\}$ for R4–R11 and $N_{special\_saved} \in \{0, 1\}$ for LR.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Register preservation is the **cornerstone of all modular software engineering on ARM**:

- **Operating System Kernels:** Linux on ARM relies on AAPCS for context switches. The kernel saves the callee-saved registers of one process and restores those of the next.
- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs):** ISRs must preserve the entire working state of the interrupted code. They typically push R0–R12, LR, and the CPSR/SPSR (using `STMFD` / `LDMFD` with `^`).
- **Compiler Code Generation:** The GCC and ARMCC back-ends use the AAPCS rules to emit correct prologs and epilogs automatically.
- **Embedded Firmware:** In bare-metal ARM Cortex-M development (e.g., STM32, NXP), subroutines called from ISRs must preserve R4–R11 explicitly, and the `__attribute__((interrupt))` directive in GCC handles this automatically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — A Compliant Subroutine

Consider the following ARM assembly subroutine that adds two 32-bit integers and returns the sum. We will derive the full AAPCS-compliant version from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Write an ARM subroutine `ADD_TWO` that:
- Accepts two arguments (in R0 and R1).
- Returns their sum in R0.
- Is fully AAPCS-compliant (preserves R4–R11 and LR).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the registers that need to be preserved.**

The subroutine uses only R0, R1, and R2 (intermediate). R0 and R1 are caller-saved and are *inputs* — we do not need to preserve them. R2 is also caller-saved. The only registers we must preserve are:
- **None of R4–R11** (we do not use them, but for *robustness* and *future-proofing*, we may still preserve them).
- **LR (R14)** — because the subroutine itself may be called and might call other subroutines.

**Step 2 — Compute the stack frame size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{frame} &= 4 \times N_{regs} \\
&= 4 \times (N_{R4{-}R11} + N_{LR}) \\
&= 4 \times (0 + 1) \\
&= 4 \text{ bytes (if we only push LR)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In this minimal example, we push only LR. However, in real-world KTU questions, you will typically be asked to preserve the entire callee-saved set:

$$
S_{frame}^{full} = 4 \times (8 + 1) = 36 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the AAPCS-compliant prolog.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ADD\_TWO} \; &\rightarrow \; \text{PUSH } \{R4-R11, LR\} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Subtract 36 from SP} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Store R4–R11 and LR to memory at [SP, SP+4, …, SP+32]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the computation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MOV R2, R0} &\rightarrow \text{; Copy R0 to R2 (R2 is scratch)} \\
\text{ADD R0, R1, R2} &\rightarrow \text{; R0 = R1 + R2 = R1 + R0} \\
&\rightarrow \text{; Result in R0 per AAPCS return convention}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the AAPCS-compliant epilog.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{POP } \{R4-R11, PC\} &\rightarrow \text{Restore R4–R11 from stack} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Load saved LR into PC (return to caller)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Add 36 back to SP (implicit in POP)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Code Listing

```asm
    AREA    MyCode, CODE, READONLY
    EXPORT  ADD_TWO
    ENTRY

ADD_TWO
    PUSH    {R4-R11, LR}      ; Prolog: preserve callee-saved regs + LR
    MOV     R2, R0            ; R2 = first argument (R0)
    ADD     R0, R1, R2        ; R0 = R1 + R2 (result)
    POP     {R4-R11, PC}      ; Epilog: restore and return

    END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall — The Missing `PC` in the POP:**
> A common mistake is writing `POP {R4-R11}` and then a separate `BX LR` or `MOV PC, LR`. While this *works*, it is **less efficient** (two instructions instead of one) and breaks atomicity. The single-instruction form `POP {..., PC}` is the **canonical** ARM idiom and earns full marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping AAPCS Rules to Common Subroutine Patterns

| Subroutine Scenario | Registers to PUSH | Registers to POP | Stack Frame Size (bytes) |
|---------------------|--------------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Leaf function**, no internal calls, no local R4–R11 | `{LR}` | `{PC}` | **4** |
| **Leaf function** with local R4–R7 usage | `{R4-R7, LR}` | `{R4-R7, PC}` | **20** |
| **Non-leaf function** with R4–R11 usage | `{R4-R11, LR}` | `{R4-R11, PC}` | **36** |
| **Function with local arrays** (extra stack space) | `{R4-R11, LR}` plus `SUB SP, SP, #N` | `ADD SP, SP, #N` plus `{R4-R11, PC}` | **36 + N** |
| **Interrupt Service Routine** (Cortex-M) | `{R0-R3, R12, LR, PC}` (using `STMFD` with `^`) | same list | **36** (or more with `spsr`) |

### 3.2.1 Stack Frame Memory Layout (Visual Derivation)

After executing `PUSH {R4-R11, LR}`, the memory layout (growing downward, high address on top) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address} = & \; SP + 32 \rightarrow \text{[R11 saved]} \\
& \; SP + 28 \rightarrow \text{[R10 saved]} \\
& \; SP + 24 \rightarrow \text{[R9 saved]} \\
& \; SP + 20 \rightarrow \text{[R8 saved]} \\
& \; SP + 16 \rightarrow \text{[R7 saved]} \\
& \; SP + 12 \rightarrow \text{[R6 saved]} \\
& \; SP + 8 \rightarrow \text{[R5 saved]} \\
& \; SP + 4 \rightarrow \text{[R4 saved]} \\
& \; SP + 0 \rightarrow \text{[LR saved]} \quad \leftarrow \text{SP points here}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pop operation restores them in **reverse order**, ending with `PC` getting the value of the saved LR, causing a branch.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Function with Stack-Local Variables

**Problem:** Write an ARM subroutine `SUM_ARRAY` that:
- Receives a base address in R0 and a length in R1.
- Returns the sum of an array of 32-bit integers in R0.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Determine the registers needed.**  
We need a loop counter, an accumulator, and the array base. Let:
- R0: argument (base address) — *caller-saved*, will be the return value at the end.
- R1: argument (length) — *caller-saved*.
- R2: loop counter (we will use R2; if we use R4, we must preserve it).
- R3: accumulator (running sum).
- R4: array base (preserved copy of R0).

Since we use R4, we must preserve it. Since this is a non-leaf function (well, leaf here, but we push LR for safety), we push R4 and LR.

**Step 2 — Compute stack frame size:**

$$
S_{frame} = 4 \times (1 + 1) = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Final code listing:**

```asm
SUM_ARRAY
    PUSH    {R4, LR}          ; Prolog: 8 bytes
    MOV     R4, R0            ; R4 = base address (preserved)
    MOV     R3, #0            ; R3 = sum = 0
    MOV     R2, #0            ; R2 = i = 0

LOOP
    CMP     R2, R1            ; if i == length, exit
    BXEQ    LR                ; *** This would be wrong! LR is on stack. ***
    LDR     R0, [R4, R2, LSL #2] ; R0 = array[i]
    ADD     R3, R3, R0        ; sum += array[i]
    ADD     R2, R2, #1        ; i++
    B       LOOP              ; continue

DONE
    MOV     R0, R3            ; R0 = sum (return value)
    POP     {R4, PC}          ; Epilog: restore R4, return via PC
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Corrected Logic:** The `BXEQ LR` line above is **incorrect** because LR is *not* a callee-saved register and is currently on the stack (we pushed it). The correct way to exit the loop early is to branch to a label that does `POP {R4, PC}`. This is a **classic KTU trap**.

**Corrected version:**

```asm
SUM_ARRAY
    PUSH    {R4, LR}          ; Prolog
    MOV     R4, R0            ; R4 = base
    MOV     R3, #0            ; sum = 0
    MOV     R2, #0            ; i = 0
    B       TEST              ; jump to test first

LOOP
    LDR     R0, [R4, R2, LSL #2]  ; R0 = array[i]
    ADD     R3, R3, R0             ; sum += array[i]
    ADD     R2, R2, #1             ; i++

TEST
    CMP     R2, R1                  ; i < length ?
    BLT     LOOP                    ; if so, continue

    MOV     R0, R3                  ; R0 = sum (return value)
    POP     {R4, PC}                ; Epilog: restore and return
```

## 3.4 Summary of State Transitions

The state of the CPU registers across a function call `BL SUM_ARRAY` (called from a context where R4 holds a long-lived value):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before call:} \quad & R4 = 0xDEADBEEF, \; LR_{caller} = PC_{return} \\
\text{After PUSH:} \quad & SP \leftarrow SP - 8, \; [SP] = LR_{caller}, \; [SP+4] = 0xDEADBEEF \\
\text{During execution:} \quad & R4 \text{ may be modified by callee} \\
\text{After POP:} \quad & R4 = 0xDEADBEEF, \; PC = LR_{caller}, \; SP \leftarrow SP + 8
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Function Call Lifecycle with Register Preservation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete lifecycle of a function call, emphasizing the role of register preservation at each step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller Code: bar] --> B[Step 1: Place arguments in R0-R3]
    B --> C[Step 2: Execute BL SUM_ARRAY]
    C --> D[LR = address of return instruction]
    D --> E[PC jumps to SUM_ARRAY]
    E --> F[Step 3: SUM_ARRAY Prolog]
    F --> G[PUSH {R4-R11, LR} to stack]
    G --> H[SP decremented by 36 bytes]
    H --> I[Step 4: SUM_ARRAY body executes]
    I --> J[Step 5: Place return value in R0]
    J --> K[Step 6: SUM_ARRAY Epilog]
    K --> L[POP {R4-R11, PC}]
    L --> M[SP incremented by 36 bytes]
    M --> N[R4-R11 restored to original values]
    N --> O[PC = saved LR - returns to caller]
    O --> P[Caller bar continues with R0 = return value]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Register File Partitioning (AAPCS View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ARM_Register_File["ARM Register File (16 x 32-bit)"]
        R0["R0 - A1\nCaller-Saved\nArg/Return"]
        R1["R1 - A2\nCaller-Saved\nArg"]
        R2["R2 - A3\nCaller-Saved\nArg"]
        R3["R3 - A4\nCaller-Saved\nArg"]
        R4["R4 - V2\nCallee-Saved"]
        R5["R5 - V3\nCallee-Saved"]
        R6["R6 - V4\nCallee-Saved"]
        R7["R7 - V5\nCallee-Saved"]
        R8["R8 - V6\nCallee-Saved"]
        R9["R9 - V7\nCallee-Saved"]
        R10["R10 - V8\nCallee-Saved"]
        R11["R11 - V9\nCallee-Saved"]
        R12["R12 - IP\nCaller-Saved\nScratch"]
        R13["R13 - SP\nCallee-Saved\nStack Pointer"]
        R14["R14 - LR\nCaller-Saved\nLink Register"]
        R15["R15 - PC\nN/A\nProgram Counter"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stack Frame Layout After PUSH {R4-R11, LR}

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HighAddress["High Memory Address"]
        D1["SP+32: R11 saved value"]
        D2["SP+28: R10 saved value"]
        D3["SP+24: R9 saved value"]
        D4["SP+20: R8 saved value"]
        D5["SP+16: R7 saved value"]
        D6["SP+12: R6 saved value"]
        D7["SP+8: R5 saved value"]
        D8["SP+4: R4 saved value"]
        D9["SP+0: LR saved value (return address)"]
    end
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 --> D6 --> D7 --> D8 --> D9
    D9 -.-> SP["SP points here (Top of Stack)"]
    SP -.-> Low["Lower Memory Address"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Decision Flow: When to Preserve Registers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Writing ARM Subroutine] --> Q1{Does the subroutine<br/>use R4-R11?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Push1[Add R4-R11 to PUSH list]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does the subroutine<br/>call BL/BLX?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Push2[Add LR to PUSH list]
    Q2 -->|No| Push3[Optionally still push LR<br/>for robustness]
    Push1 --> Q2
    Push2 --> Check[Mirror in POP list<br/>with PC instead of LR]
    Push3 --> Check
    Check --> Done[Subroutine is AAPCS-compliant]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Prolog and Epilog Idioms (Side-by-Side)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Prolog["PROLOG (Entry)"]
        P1["PUSH {R4-R11, LR}"]
        P2["; Save callee-saved regs<br/>; and return address"]
        P3["; SP -= 36 bytes"]
    end
    subgraph Body["FUNCTION BODY"]
        B1["; Use R0-R3, R12 freely"]
        B2["; Modify R4-R11 safely<br/>; (originals on stack)"]
        B3["; Place result in R0"]
    end
    subgraph Epilog["EPILOG (Exit)"]
        E1["POP {R4-R11, PC}"]
        E2["; Restore callee-saved regs"]
        E3["; PC = saved LR (return)<br/>; SP += 36 bytes"]
    end
    Prolog --> Body --> Epilog
```
