The unimplemented upper bits are **Read-As-Zero (RAZ) / Write-Ignored (WI)**, often called **shadow bits**.

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Relationship Between Implemented Bits and Priority Levels

For an NVIC implementing **n** priority bits:

$$\text{Number of distinct priority levels} = 2^{n}$$

$$\text{Priority value range} = [0,\ 2^{n} - 1]$$

$$\text{Lowest numeric value (highest urgency)} = 0x00$$

$$\text{Highest numeric value (lowest urgency)} = 2^{n} - 1$$

**Examples (commonly asked in KTU):**

| Implemented Bits (n) | Levels = 2ⁿ | Priority Range | Typical Microcontroller |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 3 | 8 | 0–7 | LPC1768 (basic) |
| 4 | 16 | 0–15 | STM32F0 series |
| 5 | 32 | 0–31 | Some NXP Kinetis |
| 8 | 256 | 0–255 | STM32F4, TM4C123 |

### 2.1.2 Priority Grouping — Preemption vs. Sub-Priority

The implemented priority bits are divided into two logical fields using the **PRIGROUP** field of the **AIRCR** (Application Interrupt and Reset Control Register):

- **Preemption Priority** (higher-order bits): Determines whether one exception can *interrupt* another. Two exceptions with **different preemption priorities** will result in **nesting** (preemption).
- **Sub-Priority** (lower-order bits): When two exceptions have the **same preemption priority**, the one with the **lower sub-priority value** (i.e., more urgent) is serviced **first** — but **no nesting** occurs. The second-handler waits for the first to complete.

The mathematical split is governed by:

$$\text{PRIGROUP} \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$$

$$\text{Preemption bits} = \text{PRIGROUP}$$

$$\text{Sub-priority bits} = n - \text{PRIGROUP}$$

### 2.2 Exception Entry Sequence — The Hardware Pipeline

When an exception is accepted by the NVIC, the Cortex-M core performs a **deterministic sequence**:

1. **Push (Stack) Eight Registers** onto the active stack pointer:
   - `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0` (in that order on the stack)
   - This is called the **stacking sequence** and takes **12 clock cycles** on Cortex-M3/M4.
2. **Read the Vector Address** from the Vector Table (for the accepted exception).
3. **Fetch the ISR address** and load it into the PC.
4. **Update the LR** to a special value called `EXC_RETURN` (encoding which stack pointer to use on return).

The total **interrupt entry latency** is therefore:

$$T_{\text{entry}} = T_{\text{stack}} + T_{\text{fetch}} = 12\ \text{cycles} + 2\ \text{cycles} = 14\ \text{cycles}$$

For zero-wait-state memory, this yields:

$$T_{\text{entry, ns}} = 14 \times T_{\text{clk}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This deterministic 12-cycle stacking is the foundation of **real-time guarantees** in Cortex-M systems. Automotive ECU designers (AUTOSAR), industrial PLC controllers, and medical device firmware rely on this fixed latency for **WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time)** analysis.

## 2.3 Exception Priority Cheat Sheet (KTU Exam Gold)

| Feature | Formula / Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Priority levels for *n* bits | $L = 2^{n}$ | Total unique urgency tiers |
| Reset priority | $-3$ (fixed) | Non-maskable, highest |
| NMI priority | $-2$ (fixed) | Non-maskable |
| HardFault priority | $-1$ (fixed) | Fault escalation priority |
| Configurable priority range | $0$ to $2^{n}-1$ | Lower = more urgent |
| Stacking cycles (Cortex-M3/M4) | $12$ | Hardware push latency |
| Vector fetch cycles | $2$ | Memory read of handler address |
| Total entry latency | $14$ cycles | $T_{\text{stack}} + T_{\text{fetch}}$ |
| Exit latency (unstacking) | $12$ cycles | Hardware pop latency |
| EXC_RETURN value | `0xFFFFFFFx` (specific patterns) | Encodes stack & mode |
| Max exceptions (theoretical) | $256$ | Per ARMv7-M spec |
| External IRQs (max) | $240$ | IRQ0 to IRQ239 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse:** A *smaller* numeric priority value means *higher* urgency. Many students mistakenly write "priority 7 is the highest" — that is **incorrect**. Priority 0 is the most urgent among configurable exceptions.

### 2.4 NVIC Optimization Mechanisms (Frequently Tested)

The NVIC implements three key optimizations that reduce latency in nested exception scenarios:

1. **Tail-Chaining**: When an exception completes and another is *pending*, the CPU skips the 12-cycle unstack and immediately refetches the new vector → **saves 6 cycles** versus full unstacking + restacking.
2. **Late-Arrival**: If a higher-priority exception arrives while the CPU is still stacking for a lower-priority one, the CPU **switches the vector fetch** to the higher-priority handler without restacking → **saves up to 12 cycles**.
3. **Pop-Preemption**: If a higher-priority exception arrives during the unstacking (exit) of a previous handler, the unstack is **aborted**, the new vector is fetched, and stacking is done for the new exception.

$$T_{\text{tail-chain}} = 6\ \text{cycles}$$

$$T_{\text{late-arrival, saved}} = 12\ \text{cycles max}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU (ABS, Airbag controllers):** Nested exceptions with deterministic latency ensure that an airbag sensor firing is serviced *before* a less critical wheel-speed interrupt completes.
- **Motor Control (BLDC/PMSM drives):** Timer-based PWM interrupts can be preempted by overcurrent protection interrupts for **hardware safety**.
- **RTOS (FreeRTOS, RTX) on Cortex-M:** The `PendSV` exception is set to the **lowest** priority so that context switches happen *only* when no other ISR is active — this is the **heartbeat of cooperative scheduling in FreeRTOS**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Priority Grouping and Nesting Decision

**Given:** An ARMv7-M NVIC with **n = 4 priority bits** and a **PRIGROUP = 2**.

**Step 1 — Compute preemption field width:**

$$\text{Preemption bits} = \text{PRIGROUP} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Compute sub-priority field width:**

$$\text{Sub-priority bits} = n - \text{PRIGROUP} = 4 - 2 = 2$$

**Step 3 — Express a generic IPR byte:**

A 4-bit implemented priority is stored in the **upper 4 bits** of the 8-bit IPR field (lower 4 bits are shadow/RAZ). The byte value is therefore:

$$\text{IPR}_{\text{byte}} = P_{\text{preempt}} \ll (n - \text{PRIGROUP}) \mid P_{\text{sub}}$$

where $P_{\text{preempt}}$ is the preemption field and $P_{\text{sub}}$ is the sub-priority field.

**Step 4 — Convert the IPR byte to a numeric priority for arbitration:**

The NVIC compares priority values numerically. The effective priority seen by the arbiter is:

$$P_{\text{eff}} = P_{\text{preempt}} \times 2^{n - \text{PRIGROUP}} + P_{\text{sub}}$$

For our example:

$$P_{\text{eff}} = P_{\text{preempt}} \times 2^{2} + P_{\text{sub}} = 4 P_{\text{preempt}} + P_{\text{sub}}$$

**Step 5 — Nesting condition:**

Two exceptions, A and B, will result in **B preempting A** if and only if:

$$P_{\text{preempt, B}} < P_{\text{preempt, A}}$$

If $P_{\text{preempt, A}} = P_{\text{preempt, B}}$ but $P_{\text{sub, B}} < P_{\text{sub, A}}$, then B is serviced **after A** completes (no nesting — just **prioritization**).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Interrupt Latency Computation

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A Cortex-M4 processor runs at **72 MHz**. An external interrupt arrives while the CPU is in Thread mode. Compute the time from interrupt assertion to first instruction of the ISR, assuming zero-wait-state Flash.

**Step 1 — Identify fixed entry cycles:**

$$N_{\text{entry}} = N_{\text{stack}} + N_{\text{fetch}} = 12 + 2 = 14\ \text{cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Compute clock period:**

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} \approx 13.89\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total entry latency:**

$$T_{\text{entry}} = N_{\text{entry}} \times T_{\text{clk}} = 14 \times 13.89\ \text{ns} \approx 194.4\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 4 — Express as a quick rule of thumb:**

$$T_{\text{entry, ns}} \approx \frac{14 \times 10^{3}}{f_{\text{clk, MHz}}}$$

**Verification for a 168 MHz STM32F4:**

$$T_{\text{entry}} \approx \frac{14\,000}{168} \approx 83.3\ \text{ns}$$

This matches ST Microelectronics datasheet values for STM32F407 zero-wait-state Flash.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Exam Scenarios

| Scenario | Preemption Priority | Sub-Priority | Outcome |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Two IRQs arrive simultaneously, different preemption | `0` vs `2` | `0` vs `0` | IRQ with priority 0 **preempts** IRQ 2 if 0 arrives during 2's execution |
| Same preemption, different sub-priority | `1` vs `1` | `0` vs `1` | Both **do not nest**; sub 0 runs first, sub 1 waits |
| NMI arrives during any ISR | N/A (fixed −2) | N/A | **Always preempts** — NMI is non-maskable |
| HardFault escalates from BusFault | `−1` (HardFault) | N/A | HardFault **preempts** the original fault handler |
| Reset asserted | `−3` (fixed) | N/A | **Resets the entire system** — all state lost |

## 3.4 Full Step-by-Step: Stacking on Nested Exception

Suppose **ISR_A** (priority 2) is running, and **ISR_B** (priority 1) arrives.

**Step 1 — Latency before stacking begins (NVIC arbitration):**

The NVIC detects the pending bit of ISR_B, compares priorities, and recognizes that B's preemption priority (1) is **less than** A's (2).

**Step 2 — Stacking begins for ISR_B:**

The CPU pushes the **current context** (which is itself already stacked from Thread mode) onto the MSP/PSP. This is a **second, deeper stack frame**:

$$\text{Stack}_{\text{pointing}} \leftarrow \text{Stack}_{\text{pointing}} - 32\ \text{bytes}$$

The 8 registers pushed are: `xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0`.

**Step 3 — LR is set to `EXC_RETURN_B`:**

The Link Register is loaded with a value such as `0xFFFFFFFD`, encoding that on exit, the CPU should unstack back to ISR_A's frame.

**Step 4 — Vector fetch for ISR_B:**

$$T_{\text{fetch}} = 2\ \text{cycles}$$

**Step 5 — ISR_B executes.**

**Step 6 — On B's completion, the EXC_RETURN triggers unstacking:**

$$T_{\text{unstack}} = 12\ \text{cycles}$$

The CPU restores `R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR` from the stack frame, **resuming ISR_A exactly** at the point it was preempted (PC was saved in step 2).

**Total time for one nesting transition:**

$$T_{\text{nest}} = T_{\text{stack}} + T_{\text{fetch}} + T_{\text{ISR_B}} + T_{\text{unstack}}$$

$$T_{\text{nest}} = 12 + 2 + T_{\text{ISR_B}} + 12 = T_{\text{ISR_B}} + 26\ \text{cycles}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exception Priority Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exception Signal Asserted] --> B{NVIC Pending Bit Set?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Signal Ignored / Rejected]
    B -- Yes --> C[Arbiter Reads IPR Value]
    C --> D{Compare with Current Active Priority}
    D -- Lower numeric value --> E[Accept Exception Begin Stacking]
    D -- Higher or equal --> F[Remain Pending Until Active ISR Finishes]
    E --> G[Push 8 Registers to Stack 12 cycles]
    G --> H[Fetch Vector from Vector Table 2 cycles]
    H --> I[Load PC with ISR Address LR set to EXC RETURN]
    I --> J[Execute ISR]
    J --> K{Another Higher Priority IRQ Pending?}
    K -- Yes --> E2[Preempt Nest Again Go to E]
    K -- No --> L[Trigger EXC RETURN on ISR Completion]
    L --> M[Unstack 8 Registers 12 cycles]
    M --> N[Resume Previous Context]
```

## 4.2 Nested Exception Stack Frame Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph STACK_MEMORY[Stack Memory Higher Address at Bottom]
        D0[Thread Mode Initial Frame]:::frame
        D1[Frame A Saved by Stacking during ISR A]:::frame
        D2[Frame B Saved by Stacking during ISR B Nested]:::frame
        D3[Current SP Points Here]:::sp
    end

    classDef frame fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91
    classDef sp fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:3px,color:#7B1F1A
```

**Reading the diagram (bottom = lowest address, top = highest):**

| Layer | Contents | When Pushed |
|---|---|---|
| `Frame B` (innermost) | xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0 of ISR-A's state | When ISR-B preempts ISR-A |
| `Frame A` (middle) | xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0 of Thread state | When ISR-A started |
| `Thread Frame` (outermost) | Original thread context (if main was running) | Startup |

## 4.3 Priority Grouping Field Mapping (PRIGROUP = 2, n = 4 bits)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[Bit 7]:::bit --> A1[Bit 6]:::bit --> A2[Bit 5]:::bit --> A3[Bit 4]:::bit --> A4[Bit 3 RAZ]:::raz --> A5[Bit 2 RAZ]:::raz --> A6[Bit 1 RAZ]:::raz --> A7[Bit 0 RAZ]:::raz

    subgraph PREEMPT[Preemption Field 2 bits]
        P0[Bit 7]:::pre --> P1[Bit 6]:::pre
    end

    subgraph SUB[Sub Priority Field 2 bits]
        S0[Bit 5]:::sub --> S1[Bit 4]:::sub
    end

    classDef bit fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#5F6368,stroke-width:1px,color:#202124
    classDef raz fill:#F1F3F4,stroke:#9AA0A6,stroke-width:1px,color:#5F6368
    classDef pre fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7B1F1A
    classDef sub fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D5226
```

**Interpretation:** Bits 7–6 = preemption priority. Bits 5–4 = sub-priority. Bits 3–0 = unimplemented (RAZ/WI).

## 4.4 NVIC Optimization: Tail-Chaining Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as Cortex M Core
    participant STK as Stack Memory
    participant VEC as Vector Table
    participant ISR1 as ISR 1 Handler
    participant ISR2 as ISR 2 Handler

    CPU->>STK: Push 8 registers 12 cycles
    CPU->>VEC: Read ISR1 vector 2 cycles
    VEC-->>CPU: ISR1 address
    CPU->>ISR1: Execute Handler
    ISR1-->>CPU: Return
    Note over CPU,STK: Tail chain skip unstack
    CPU->>VEC: Read ISR2 vector 6 cycles
    VEC-->>CPU: ISR2 address
    CPU->>ISR2: Execute Handler
    CPU->>STK: Pop 8 registers on exit 12 cycles
```

**Time saved by tail-chaining:**

$$T_{\text{saved}} = T_{\text{unstack}} - T_{\text{tail-chain}} = 12 - 6 = 6\ \text{cycles}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define exception priority in the ARM Cortex-M architecture. Explain why a smaller numerical priority value indicates a higher urgency.**

**Model Answer:**

In ARM Cortex-M, exception priority is a programmable numeric value stored in the **Interrupt Priority Registers (IPR0–IPR\[N\])** of the NVIC. The NVIC uses this value to arbitrate when multiple exceptions occur simultaneously.

A **smaller numerical value corresponds to a higher logical priority** because the priority comparison in the NVIC arbiter is performed as a *less-than* operation. Exception X preempts exception Y if and only if `Priority(X) < Priority(Y)`. Thus, priority 0 is the most urgent among the *configurable* levels, while the maximum value (e.g., 0xFF for 8 bits) is the *least* urgent.

For example, in an NVIC with 4 priority bits, IRQ with priority 1 will preempt an IRQ with priority 3 currently being serviced, but not vice versa. This is the standard ARM convention and applies to all v7-M implementations.

*(Examiner expects: mention of IPR, NVIC, less-than comparison, and an example.)* **[3 marks]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three NVIC optimization techniques used in ARM Cortex-M to reduce interrupt latency. State the cycle savings for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three NVIC optimization techniques are:

1. **Tail-Chaining** — When an ISR completes and another exception is already pending, the CPU skips the 12-cycle unstack and directly fetches the new vector. **Cycle savings: 6 cycles** (no full unstack required, only a 6-cycle vector fetch).

2. **Late-Arrival** — If a higher-priority exception arrives while the CPU is still performing the 12-cycle stacking for a lower-priority one, the CPU redirects the vector fetch to the higher-priority handler without redoing the stack push. **Cycle savings: up to 12 cycles**.

3. **Pop-Preemption** — If a higher-priority exception arrives during the 12-cycle unstacking of a previous handler, the unstack is abandoned and stacking for the new exception begins immediately. **Cycle savings: up to 12 cycles** per preemption event.

*(Examiner expects: technique name + cycle count for each.)* **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. ❌ Writing "priority 7 is the highest" — **DEDUCT 1 MARK**. Lower numeric = higher urgency.
> 2. ❌ Confusing preemption priority with sub-priority during nesting analysis — **DEDUCT 2 MARKS**.
> 3. ❌ Forgetting to specify the **active stack pointer (MSP vs PSP)** when describing stacking.
> 4. ❌ Omitting the `EXC_RETURN` mechanism when explaining exception exit — examiner specifically tests this.
> 5. ❌ Writing "the CPU is interrupted" without mentioning **NVIC arbitration** — generic answers lose the architecture-specific marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **NVIC priority grouping** mechanism in ARM Cortex-M. How does the `PRIGROUP` field in the AIRCR register affect the division of priority bits into preemption and sub-priority fields? Use a numerical example with 4 implemented priority bits and PRIGROUP = 2. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **exception entry and exit sequence** in ARM Cortex-M3/M4 with the help of a block diagram. State the number of clock cycles for stacking and unstacking. Show how the `EXC_RETURN` value is generated and what it encodes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Concept of Priority Grouping:** [2 marks]
The implemented priority bits in the NVIC IPR registers are divided by the **`PRIGROUP` field** of the **Application Interrupt and Reset Control Register (AIRCR)** into two fields:
- **Preemption priority** (upper bits): Decides if an exception can *preempt* (nest into) a running one.
- **Sub-priority** (lower bits): Decides the *service order* when two exceptions share the same preemption level — **no nesting** in this case.

**Step 2 — Formulae for Split:** [2 marks]

$$\text{Preemption bits} = \text{PRIGROUP value}$$

$$\text{Sub-priority bits} = n - \text{PRIGROUP value}$$

where $n$ is the number of implemented priority bits.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (n = 4, PRIGROUP = 2):** [2 marks]

- Preemption bits = 2 → 4 preemption levels (0, 1, 2, 3)
- Sub-priority bits = 2 → 4 sub-priority levels (0, 1, 2, 3)
- IPR byte for an IRQ with preemption = 1, sub = 2:

$$\text{IPR} = (1 \ll 2) + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 \quad \text{(i.e., 0b0110\_xxxx)}$$

The lower 4 bits are shadow (RAZ/WI). Two IRQs with the same preemption value but different sub-priority values will *not* nest — the lower sub-priority runs first, and the second waits.

**Step 4 — Diagram:** [1 mark]
A block diagram showing the IPR byte split:

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| P1 | P0 | S1 | S0 | x | x | x | x |

where `P = Preemption`, `S = Sub-priority`, `x = shadow/RAZ`.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Exception Entry Sequence:** [3 marks]

When the NVIC accepts an exception (because its priority is higher than the currently active one), the CPU performs:

1. **Stacking** (12 cycles on Cortex-M3/M4): Eight registers are pushed onto the active stack pointer (MSP for handlers, PSP for threads):
   `xPSR → PC → LR → R12 → R3 → R2 → R1 → R0`
2. **Vector Fetch** (2 cycles): The vector address is read from the Vector Table at offset = (exception number × 4).
3. **LR Update**: The Link Register is loaded with the special `EXC_RETURN` value.
4. **PC Update**: The PC is loaded with the handler address from the vector.
5. **Execution of the handler** begins.

**Step 2 — Exit Sequence:** [2 marks]

When the handler executes a `BX LR` or `POP {PC}` where LR = `EXC_RETURN`:

1. **Unstacking** (12 cycles): The 8 registers are popped in reverse order.
2. **PC restored** → execution resumes at the preempted location.

**Step 3 — `EXC_RETURN` Encoding:** [2 marks]

The `EXC_RETURN` value is a 32-bit value of the form `0xFFFF_FFFx` where the lower bits encode:

| Bit | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Bit 2 (`0x8` mask) | `0` = return to Handler mode, `1` = return to Thread mode |
| Bit 3 | Stack pointer used: `0` = MSP, `1` = PSP |
| Bit 4 | `0` = standard, `1` = FP lazy stacking active (Cortex-M4F) |

Example: `EXC_RETURN = 0xFFFF_FFFD` → return to Thread mode using PSP (typical for RTOS context switch).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **nested exceptions** in ARM Cortex-M. With the help of a stack diagram, describe what happens when a higher-priority exception preempts a currently executing lower-priority ISR. How many registers are saved on each nesting level and how many clock cycles does it consume? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **interrupt entry latency** for a Cortex-M4 processor running at **168 MHz** with zero-wait-state Flash? Show the calculation. Compare this with the case of a **wait-state of 1** added to the Flash. State two software-level techniques to further reduce interrupt response time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Definition of Nesting:** [1 mark]
Nesting is the ability of the NVIC and Cortex-M core to suspend the currently running ISR (lower priority) and immediately begin servicing a newly arrived higher-priority exception, with full hardware context preservation.

**Step 2 — Mechanism:** [2 marks]
- The NVIC continuously monitors pending bits. When a new exception arrives:
  1. Compare its preemption priority with the currently active exception.
  2. If the new exception has a **lower numeric value** (higher priority), it **preempts**.
  3. The CPU performs a **second stacking** — 8 more registers are pushed onto the stack.
  4. A new `EXC_RETURN` is loaded into LR for the inner ISR.
  5. The inner ISR runs. On exit, the CPU **unwinds** to the outer ISR.

**Step 3 — Stack Diagram (textual representation):** [2 marks]

```
High Address
+-------------------+
|  (Reserved)       |
+-------------------+ ← Original MSP (Thread mode)
|  xPSR_outer       |
|  PC_outer         |
|  LR_outer         |
|  R12_outer        |
|  R3_outer         |
|  R2_outer         |
|  R1_outer         |
|  R0_outer         |   ← Frame A (8 regs × 4 bytes = 32 bytes)
+-------------------+ ← SP after first stacking
|  xPSR_inner       |
|  PC_inner         |
|  LR_inner=EXC_RET |
|  R12_inner        |
|  R3_inner         |
|  R2_inner         |
|  R1_inner         |
|  R0_inner         |   ← Frame B (another 32 bytes)
+-------------------+ ← SP after second stacking (low address)
```

**Step 4 — Cycle Count:** [2 marks]
- 8 registers saved per nesting level (4 bytes each = 32 bytes per frame).
- Each stacking operation = **12 clock cycles** on Cortex-M3/M4.
- Each unstacking = **12 clock cycles**.
- So a single preemption adds 12 + 2 (vector fetch) + 12 = 26 cycles of overhead (excluding the inner ISR's execution time).

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Zero-Wait-State Latency:** [3 marks]

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{168\ \text{MHz}} = 5.952\ \text{ns}$$

$$T_{\text{entry}} = 14\ \text{cycles} \times 5.952\ \text{ns} = 83.3\ \text{ns}$$

(12 cycles stacking + 2 cycles vector fetch = 14 cycles total)

**Step 2 — With 1 Wait State:** [2 marks]

If the Flash has 1 wait state, vector fetch becomes 2 + 1 = 3 cycles (or more, depending on implementation; assume 1 added wait):

$$T_{\text{entry, 1WS}} = 12 + 3 = 15\ \text{cycles} \times 5.952\ \text{ns} \approx 89.3\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 3 — Software Techniques to Reduce Latency:** [2 marks]

1. **Place the Vector Table in RAM** (set `VTOR` to a RAM address) — RAM access is single-cycle, eliminating Flash wait-state penalty.
2. **Use the `__attribute__((interrupt))` or ARM Compiler's `__irq` keyword** to allow the compiler to generate the optimal ISR prologue/epilogue (no extra register saves).
3. **Keep ISRs short** — defer non-critical work to a PendSV or main-loop handler (RTOS-friendly).
4. **Tail-chain optimization by clearing pending bits promptly** to allow back-to-back ISRs to chain.

*(Any two of the above, well-explained, carry full marks.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read before every KTU exam:**

- **Priority** in Cortex-M: **lower numeric value = higher urgency** (priority 0 is most urgent among configurable levels).
- **Reset (-3), NMI (-2), HardFault (-1)** have **fixed, non-maskable** priorities — cannot be reprogrammed.
- NVIC implements **at least 3 bits** of priority (8 levels); typical implementations use **4–8 bits**.
- **Stacking = 12 cycles, unstacking = 12 cycles, vector fetch = 2 cycles** on Cortex-M3/M4.
- **Nesting occurs only when preemption priorities differ.** Same preemption + different sub-priority = **no nesting** (just ordering).
- **`EXC_RETURN`** is loaded into LR on exception entry; it encodes the return mode and stack pointer.
- The CPU pushes **8 registers** (`xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0`) — always in this order.
- The **Vector Table** is located at the address pointed to by `VTOR` (default `0x00000000`).
- NVIC optimizations: **Tail-chain (6 cycles)**, **Late-arrival (save 12)**, **Pop-preemption (save 12)**.
- **`PRIGROUP`** divides implemented priority bits into preemption (upper) and sub-priority (lower) fields.
- `PRIGROUP = x` ⇒ **x preemption bits** and **n − x sub-priority bits**.
- For **n priority bits**: levels = $2^{n}$; range = $[0, 2^{n}-1]$.
- Unimplemented (shadow) bits in IPR are **RAZ/WI** (Read-As-Zero, Write-Ignored).
- **RTOS tip (FreeRTOS):** `PendSV` is set to the **lowest** priority to enable deferred context switching.
- For deterministic real-time analysis: **interrupt entry latency = 14 cycles** (with zero wait states).
- In Cortex-M, **interrupts cannot interrupt themselves at the same priority level** — this prevents accidental re-entry.
- The **BASEPRI** register masks all exceptions with a *numerically greater or equal* priority value — useful for *critical sections* in RTOS kernels.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronization** in the context of ARM-based embedded systems (Cortex-M3 / LPC1768) is the coordinated control of **concurrent access** to shared resources by multiple execution contexts (tasks, ISRs, main thread) to ensure **data consistency**, **deterministic behavior**, and **atomicity** of critical operations.

In ARMv7-M architecture, synchronization is achieved through three complementary mechanisms:
1. **Hardware Atomic Primitives** — `LDREX` / `STREX` (Load-Exclusive / Store-Exclusive) instructions.
2. **Software Primitives** — Semaphores, Mutexes, Event Flags, Critical Sections.
3. **Memory Ordering** — Barrier instructions (`DMB`, `DSB`, `ISB`) and `PRIMASK` register for interrupt masking.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Single-Key Bathroom Analogy 🚻**
>
> Imagine a hostel with **one bathroom** (shared resource) and **three roommates** (tasks/ISRs). Without rules, all three will try to enter simultaneously — chaos, embarrassment, and damaged locks. The hostel warden hangs a **single physical key** on a hook outside the door. Whoever holds the key is the *exclusive owner*. When they finish, they place the key back.
>
> - The **key** = a semaphore token / mutex lock.
> - The **hook** = a memory-mapped hardware flag or exclusive monitor register.
> - **Lifting the key** = `LDREX` (read & mark).
> - **Returning the key after use** = `STREX` (conditional write).
> - If someone else took the key between lift-and-return, you *cannot* return it (`STREX` fails, status = 1).

This is **exactly** how the ARM Cortex-M3 exclusive access monitor works at the silicon level.

## 1.3 Why Synchronization is Mandatory in KTU Syllabus

| Symptom of Missing Sync | Real-World Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Race condition** | Sensor reads inconsistent data |
| **Torn read/write** | Half-updated 32-bit variable on 8-bit bus |
| **Priority inversion** | Mars Pathfinder mission reset (1997) |
| **Deadlock** | Vehicle ECU hangs mid-drive |
| **Lost wakeup** | RTOS task never runs again |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 explicitly demands knowledge of *race conditions, critical sections, semaphores, mutexes, and exclusive accesses on ARM Cortex-M3 (LPC1768)*. Direct questions of **7 or 14 marks** are routinely asked in End Semester Evaluations (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of ARM Synchronization

### Pillar 1 — Race Conditions & Critical Sections

A **race condition** exists when the output of a system depends on the unpredictable *ordering* of concurrent operations. The portion of code that accesses the shared resource is called the **Critical Section (CS)**.

**Conditions for a race condition (all must hold):**
- **Shared mutable state** exists in memory.
- **More than one execution context** can access it.
- **Preemption** is possible (interrupts, RTOS task switch).

### Pillar 2 — Atomicity via LDREX / STREX

The Cortex-M3 implements **local exclusive monitoring** at the processor core. Two new instructions replace ordinary `LDR`/`STR` for atomic sequences:

| Instruction | Mnemonic | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| Load-Exclusive | `LDREX Rt, [Rn]` | Reads memory, sets local exclusive monitor for address `[Rn]` |
| Store-Exclusive | `STREX Rd, Rt, [Rn]` | Stores only if exclusive monitor is still set; returns **0 = success**, **1 = failure** |

The exclusive monitor is **automatically cleared** by:
- Any successful `STREX`
- Any exception entry (interrupt firing)
- A `CLREX` instruction
- Any other exclusive access to a *different* address

### Pillar 3 — Memory Barriers (ARMv7-M)

| Barrier | Full Name | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `DMB` | Data Memory Barrier | Ensures all prior memory accesses complete before subsequent ones |
| `DSB` | Data Synchronization Barrier | Same as DMB + stalls the pipeline until buffer drains |
| `ISB` | Instruction Synchronization Barrier | Flushes pipeline; subsequent instructions re-fetched |

> [!NOTE]
> **`PRIMASK` Register (Interrupt Mask):** Setting bit 0 of `PRIMASK` blocks all maskable interrupts — a *coarse* but highly effective synchronization tool for very short critical sections in bare-metal LPC1768 firmware.

## 2.2 Software Synchronization Primitives

### 2.2.1 Semaphores

A semaphore is a **non-negative integer counter** with two atomic operations (Dijkstra, 1965):

$$\text{wait}(S): \quad \text{while } S \le 0 \text{ do block}; \quad S \leftarrow S - 1$$

$$\text{signal}(S): \quad S \leftarrow S + 1; \quad \text{wakeup one waiter}$$

- **Binary Semaphore:** $S \in \{0, 1\}$ — used for signaling / mutual exclusion.
- **Counting Semaphore:** $S \in [0, N]$ — used to manage $N$ identical resources (e.g., buffer slots).
- **Mutex (MUT-ual EX-clusion):** A special *binary semaphore* with **ownership**, **priority inheritance**, and **recursive locking** capability.

### 2.2.2 Critical Section Protocol (Pseudocode Pattern)

```
ENTER_CRITICAL():
    save PRIMASK into local variable
    disable interrupts globally (CPSID i  OR  __disable_irq())

EXIT_CRITICAL():
    restore PRIMASK from local variable
```

### 2.2.3 Priority Inversion & Inheritance

**Priority Inversion** occurs when a *low-priority* task holds a mutex that a *high-priority* task needs, while a *medium-priority* task preempts the low-priority task indefinitely.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** The low-priority task *temporarily inherits* the high-priority task's priority while holding the mutex, preventing medium-priority preemption.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes `$\vert$` inside tables. Use `\mid` or `\vert` outside.**

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Semaphore invariant | $S \ge 0$ always | $S_{\max} = N$ (counting) or $1$ (binary) |
| 2 | Mutual exclusion condition | $\sum \text{holders} \le 1$ for any mutex | Holder count $\in \{0, 1\}$ |
| 3 | Deadlock (Coffman) | Circular wait + Hold & Wait + No preemption + Mutual exclusion | All 4 must hold |
| 4 | STREX status | $R_d = 0 \Rightarrow$ success, $R_d = 1 \Rightarrow$ retry | Exclusive state cleared on success |
| 5 | PRIMASK | $PRIMASK = 1 \Rightarrow$ mask all IRQ | NMI + HardFault unaffected |
| 6 | BASEPRI threshold | $BASEPRI > n \Rightarrow$ mask all IRQ with priority $\le n$ | $n \in [0, 255]$ |
| 7 | DMB ordering | $\text{memory ops before} \prec \text{memory ops after}$ | No effect on instruction fetch |
| 8 | Priority Inheritance | $P_{\text{inherited}} = \max(P_{\text{holder}}, P_{\text{waiter}})$ | Reversed on `signal()` |
| 9 | ISR-safe lock | $\text{lock\_time} \ll T_{\text{latency, max}}$ | Empirical guideline |
| 10 | Round-trip atomicity | $\text{STREX} \to \text{LDREX} \to \text{STREX}$ chain | Local monitor $\le 1$ address |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR):** Synchronization protects CAN bus buffers shared between communication tasks and application tasks.
- **Medical devices (pacemakers):** Priority inheritance prevents missed therapy delivery.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Mutexes coordinate robot arm motion with safety interlocks.
- **IoT edge nodes (LPC1768-based):** Semaphores synchronize DMA completion with CPU data processing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Atomic Increment Using LDREX/STREX

**Goal:** Implement `++shared_counter` atomically without disabling interrupts (lock-free).

**Step 1 — Initial State**

Let $A$ be the shared memory address, value $V_0$. The local exclusive monitor state is $E \in \{0, 1\}$.

$$E = 0 \quad (\text{not held})$$

**Step 2 — Load-Exclusive (`LDREX`)**

The CPU reads $V_0$ from $A$ into register $R_t$ and sets the local exclusive monitor for address $A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_t &\leftarrow \text{MEM}[A] = V_0 \\
E &\leftarrow 1 \quad \text{(locked to address } A\text{)} \\
\text{ExclTag} &\leftarrow A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute New Value**

$$
R_t^{\text{new}} \leftarrow R_t + 1 = V_0 + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Store-Exclusive (`STREX Rd, Rt, [A]`) Attempt**

The hardware performs an **atomic check-and-conditional-store**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{if } E = 1 \ \land\ \text{ExclTag} = A \ \land\ \text{no other LDREX to } A \text{ occurred:} \\
&\text{MEM}[A] \leftarrow R_t^{\text{new}} \\
&E \leftarrow 0 \\
&R_d \leftarrow 0 \quad \text{(success)} \\
\text{else:} \\
&\text{MEM}[A] \text{ unchanged} \\
&E \leftarrow 0 \\
&R_d \leftarrow 1 \quad \text{(failure — retry required)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Retry Loop (Spinlock Pattern)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{retry:} & \\
&\text{LDREX } R_t, [A] \\
&R_t \leftarrow R_t + 1 \\
&\text{STREX } R_d, R_t, [A] \\
&\text{if } R_d = 1 \text{ goto retry} \\
&\text{else return success}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Probability of success on first attempt:**
> $$P_{\text{success}} = 1 - P_{\text{contention}} \approx 1 - \frac{N \cdot T_{\text{CS}}}{T_{\text{quantum}}}$$
> where $N$ = number of contending tasks, $T_{\text{CS}}$ = critical section time, $T_{\text{quantum}}$ = RTOS tick period.

## 3.2 Derivation: Race Condition Probability (Two Tasks)

Two tasks $T_1$ and $T_2$ write to shared variable $X$ with probability $p$ in any time slot $\Delta t$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{race in slot } \Delta t) &= P(T_1 \text{ writes}) \cdot P(T_2 \text{ writes}) \\
&= p \cdot p = p^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Over $N$ independent slots in a time window $W$:

$$
P(\text{at least one race in } W) = 1 - (1 - p^2)^{N}
$$

As $N \to \infty$ (continuous contention) and $p$ fixed:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} P = 1 - e^{-N p^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **synchronization is not optional** in real-time embedded systems — the probability of a race event approaches **certainty** over long runtimes.

## 3.3 Derivation: Priority Inversion Worst-Case Blocking Time

Let $T_H$ = high-priority task, $T_M$ = medium-priority task, $T_L$ = low-priority task (mutex holder). Let $C_i$ = computation time of task $i$.

**Unbounded (no inheritance) priority inversion blocking time:**

$$
B_{T_H} = C_{T_L} + \sum_{k} C_{T_M, k}
$$

where the sum is over *all* medium-priority executions during the inversion window — **unbounded** if $T_M$ is periodic.

**With Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

$$
B_{T_H, \text{PIP}} = C_{T_L,\text{critical section}}
$$

The blocking time becomes **bounded by the critical section length** — a fundamental guarantee for hard real-time systems (used in AUTOSAR OS, VxWorks, FreeRTOS-mutex-with-inheritance).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Synchronization Primitive Selection

| Requirement | Recommended Primitive | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Protect 1 shared variable, no RTOS | `__disable_irq()` + `__enable_irq()` | Lowest overhead, deterministic |
| Producer-Consumer buffer | Counting Semaphore | Natural resource counting |
| Protect shared peripheral register | Mutex (with priority inheritance) | Avoids priority inversion |
| ISR ↔ Task signaling | Binary Semaphore | ISR `osSemaphoreRelease()`, task `osSemaphoreAcquire()` |
| Lock-free single-writer / multi-reader | `LDREX` / `STREX` spinlock | No OS dependency |
| Compiler-reordering prevention | `__DMB()`, `__DSB()`, `__ISB()` | Compiler intrinsic barriers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ARM Cortex-M3 Exclusive Access Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CPU Core Pipeline] -->|LDREX opcode| B[Load Store Unit LSU]
    A -->|STREX opcode| B
    B --> C{Local Exclusive Monitor}
    C -->|Set on LDREX| D[Address Tag Register]
    C -->|Check on STREX| E{Address Match?}
    E -->|Yes and no CLREX or exception| F[Memory Write Allowed]
    E -->|No mismatch or cleared| G[Write Blocked Status = 1]
    F --> H[Return Status Rd = 0 Success]
    G --> I[Return Status Rd = 1 Failure]
    H --> J[Clear Exclusive Monitor]
    I --> J
    J --> C
```

## 4.2 State Machine — LDREX/STREX Atomic Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle: Idle E equals 0
    Loaded: Loaded E equals 1 tag equals A
    Success: STREX success Rd equals 0
    Failure: STREX failure Rd equals 1
    Retry: Spinning for retry

    Idle --> Loaded: LDREX Rt, A
    Loaded --> Success: STREX pass no contention
    Loaded --> Failure: STREX fail contention or exception
    Loaded --> Idle: Exception entry clears monitor
    Success --> Idle: Monitor cleared
    Failure --> Retry: Application loops back
    Retry --> Loaded: LDREX Rt, A
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Critical Section Entry/Exit with PRIMASK

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Task Entry]) --> P1[Read PRIMASK into local var]
    P1 --> P2[CPSID i  Disable all IRQ]
    P2 --> CS{Critical Section Body}
    CS --> P3[Write local var back to PRIMASK]
    P3 --> P4[CPSIE i  Re-enable IRQ if previously set]
    P4 --> End([Task Continuation])
    CS -.->|Interrupt fires| Panic[Unwanted Interruption prevented]
    style Panic fill:#ffd6d6
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Producer/Consumer with Two Counting Semaphores

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Producer Task] -->|osSemaphoreRelease full| SF[Semaphore Full counter N]
    P2[Consumer Task] -->|osSemaphoreRelease empty| SE[Semaphore Empty counter N]
    SF -->|osSemaphoreAcquire| P2
    SE -->|osSemaphoreAcquire| P1
    P1 --> WB[Write to shared buffer]
    WB --> SF
    P2 --> RB[Read from shared buffer]
    RB --> SE
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Priority Inversion vs. Priority Inheritance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Without Priority Inheritance"
        A1[High Priority Task T_H needs Mutex] -->|Blocked| A2[Low Priority Task T_L holds Mutex]
        A3[Medium Priority Task T_M preempts T_L] -->|Runs indefinitely| A2
        A2 -.->|Unbounded blocking| A1
    end
    subgraph "With Priority Inheritance"
        B1[High Priority Task T_H needs Mutex] -->|Blocked| B2[Low Priority Task T_L holds Mutex gets boosted to P_H]
        B3[Medium Priority Task T_M cannot preempt] -->|Suspended| B2
        B2 -->|Releases mutex| B4[T_L reverts to P_L]
        B4 --> B1
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define race condition. With a suitable example, explain how it can be prevented in an ARM Cortex-M3 system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Race condition** is a situation where the behavior of a system depends on the relative timing or interleaving of concurrent execution contexts (tasks/ISRs) accessing shared data, leading to non-deterministic results.

**Example:** A global `uint32_t sensor_value` updated by `ADC_IRQHandler` and read by the main loop. If read occurs during write, a *torn* value may be observed.

**Prevention on ARM Cortex-M3:**
1. Mark shared data as `volatile`.
2. Use `__disable_irq()` / `__enable_irq()` to enter/exit a critical section.
3. Use atomic `LDREX` / `STREX` sequences for lock-free access.
4. Use RTOS mutex (with priority inheritance) for task-level protection.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — example, 1 mark — prevention methods)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between a binary semaphore and a mutex. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Mutex |
|---|---|---|
| Ownership | No owner concept | Has owner (task that took it) |
| Priority Inheritance | Not provided | Yes (in full RTOS like Keil RTX) |
| Recursive Locking | Not allowed | Allowed |
| ISR Release | Can be released from ISR | Cannot be released from ISR |
| Use Case | Signaling between task ↔ ISR | Mutual exclusion of shared resource |

**Preference Rule:** Use a **binary semaphore** for one-way *signaling* (e.g., ISR-to-task notification). Use a **mutex** when protecting a *shared resource* against concurrent task access to prevent priority inversion.

*(2 marks — table/differences, 1 mark — preference rule)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the working of the **LDREX and STREX** instructions in ARM Cortex-M3. Draw the state diagram of an exclusive access sequence and explain the role of the **local exclusive monitor**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement a **lock-free atomic counter increment** using LDREX/STREX. Discuss what happens when contention occurs and how the retry loop ensures correctness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) LDREX / STREX Explanation (7 marks):**

- `LDREX Rt, [Rn]` — Loads the word at address `Rn` into register `Rt` and **marks** the local exclusive monitor for that address. *(1 mark)*
- `STREX Rd, Rt, [Rn]` — Attempts to store `Rt` into `[Rn]`. The store is committed **only if** the exclusive monitor is still held; otherwise, the memory is untouched. *(1 mark)*
- `Rd` receives the status: `0 = success`, `1 = failure`. *(1 mark)*
- The **local exclusive monitor** is a small hardware register inside the Cortex-M3 core. It holds either *0* (idle) or *1* (exclusive reservation) plus the address tag. *(2 marks)*
- It is **cleared** on: (i) successful STREX, (ii) any exception entry, (iii) `CLREX` instruction, (iv) any LDREX to a *different* address. *(1 mark)*
- **State diagram** is already shown in Section 4.2 above. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Lock-Free Atomic Counter Implementation (7 marks):**

**ARM Assembly Implementation (LPC1768 / Cortex-M3):**

```assembly
        EXPORT  atomic_increment
atomic_increment
        LDR     r0, =counter_addr     ; r0 = &counter
retry
        LDREX   r1, [r0]              ; r1 = *counter, mark exclusive
        ADD     r1, r1, #1            ; r1 = r1 + 1
        STREX   r2, r1, [r0]          ; try store, r2 = status
        CMP     r2, #1                ; check failure
        BEQ     retry                 ; retry if failure
        DMB                           ; ensure visibility
        BX      LR                    ; return
counter_addr DCD counter               ; global counter
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. `LDREX` reads the current value and reserves the address. *(1 mark)*
2. The new value `+1` is computed locally in `r1`. *(1 mark)*
3. `STREX` attempts atomic write. If the monitor was cleared (by another LDREX, exception, or CLREX), the store is **aborted** and `r2 = 1`. *(2 marks)*
4. The `BEQ retry` loop spins until success — this guarantees that *no two tasks ever write a stale value*. *(1 mark)*
5. `DMB` is issued after success to ensure the write is observable to other cores / DMA engines. *(1 mark)*
6. **Contention scenario:** If a higher-priority ISR modifies `counter` between our `LDREX` and `STREX`, the exclusive monitor is cleared → `STREX` fails → we safely retry with the *fresh* value. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What is **priority inversion**? Explain the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** with a real-world example. How does it solve the unbounded blocking problem? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **disabling interrupts** vs. **using a mutex** for protecting shared data in an RTOS-based LPC1768 application. Provide code snippets for both approaches. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Priority Inversion & PIP (7 marks):**

- **Definition:** Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly where a *high-priority* task is blocked waiting for a resource held by a *low-priority* task, while a *medium-priority* task preempts the low-priority task — indirectly blocking the high-priority task for an *unbounded* time. *(2 marks)*
- **Classic Real-World Example:** *Mars Pathfinder mission (1997).* The meteorological task (low priority) held a shared bus semaphore, was preempted by the communication task (medium priority), and the bus management task (high priority) was starved — causing repeated system resets. NASA uplinked a patch enabling **priority inheritance** to fix it. *(2 marks)*
- **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When a high-priority task blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority task, the OS *temporarily raises* the low-priority task's priority to that of the highest-priority waiter. *(1 mark)*
- **Effect on Unbounded Blocking:** Medium-priority tasks can no longer preempt the (now-boosted) low-priority task, so the critical section completes in *bounded* time. The blocking bound becomes $B_{T_H} = C_{T_L,\text{CS}}$ instead of $C_{T_L} + \sum C_{T_M}$. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Interrupt Disabling vs. Mutex (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Interrupt Disabling | Mutex |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Entire CPU (or BASEPRI subset) | Per-resource |
| Affects other tasks? | Yes (all tasks paused) | No (only mutex waiters paused) |
| Affects ISRs? | Yes (if `CPSID i`) | No (mutex not ISR-safe) |
| Priority Inversion | Not applicable | Possible (use PIP mutex) |
| Determinism | Highly deterministic | Depends on RTOS tick |
| Use Case | Very short CS, bare-metal | Task-level shared resource, RTOS |

**Code Snippet 1 — Interrupt Disabling (Bare-Metal LPC1768):**

```c
volatile uint32_t sensor_value;

void read_sensor_safe(uint32_t *out) {
    uint32_t primask = __get_PRIMASK();  // save
    __disable_irq();                     // enter CS
    *out = sensor_value;                 // critical section
    __set_PRIMASK(primask);              // exit CS
}
```

**Code Snippet 2 — RTOS Mutex (Keil RTX on LPC1768):**

```c
#include "cmsis_os2.h"

osMutexId_t sensor_mutex;
volatile uint32_t sensor_value;

void thread_consumer(void *arg) {
    while (1) {
        osMutexAcquire(sensor_mutex, osWaitForever);  // lock
        uint32_t local = sensor_value;                // CS body
        process(local);
        osMutexRelease(sensor_mutex);                 // unlock
        osDelay(10);
    }
}

void ADC_IRQHandler(void) {
    // ISR updates the raw register; the *task* reads it via mutex
    osMutexAcquire(sensor_mutex, 0);  // try-lock
    if (status == osOK) {
        sensor_value = LPC_ADC->DR;
        osMutexRelease(sensor_mutex);
    }
}
```

**Justification:** Use **interrupt disabling** for very short CS (<10 cycles) and single-byte/word access in bare-metal. Use a **PIP-aware mutex** for longer CS or when multiple tasks contend for the same resource. *(1 mark)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Synchronization questions:**
>
> 1. ❌ **Confusing `LDREX` with `LDR`.** `LDREX` is a *load with side-effect* (sets the exclusive monitor). Plain `LDR` does not.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting the retry loop after `STREX` failure.** A single non-looped attempt is *not* atomic.
> 3. ❌ **Releasing a mutex from an ISR.** Mutexes are *not* ISR-safe; release a binary semaphore instead.
> 4. ❌ **Nesting `__disable_irq()` without saving `PRIMASK`.** Causes asymmetric interrupt enabling and leaves IRQs permanently disabled after nested returns.
> 5. ❌ **Using a binary semaphore where a mutex is required.** Loses priority inheritance → priority inversion risk.
> 6. ❌ **Skipping `DMB` after `STREX`.** Subsequent reads on other bus masters may still see stale data.
> 7. ❌ **Forgetting to declare shared variables as `volatile`.** Compiler optimization can eliminate "redundant" reads/writes, breaking synchronization.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Synchronization in ARM Cortex-M3 (LPC1768)**

- ✅ **Race condition** = unpredictable outcome due to interleaved concurrent access.
- ✅ **Critical section** = code region accessing shared state; must be executed atomically.
- ✅ **Three pillars of ARM sync:** Hardware (LDREX/STREX), Software (Semaphores/Mutexes), Ordering (DMB/DSB/ISB).
- ✅ **`LDREX`** = load + set exclusive monitor; **`STREX`** = conditional store returning 0 (success) or 1 (failure).
- ✅ **Exclusive monitor is cleared** by: successful STREX, exception entry, CLREX, or LDREX to a different address.
- ✅ **Lock-free pattern:** `LDREX → modify → STREX → branch-if-fail retry`.
- ✅ **Binary semaphore** = signaling; **Counting semaphore** = resource pool; **Mutex** = ownership + priority inheritance.
- ✅ **`PRIMASK = 1`** masks all maskable interrupts; `BASEPRI` allows *selective* priority-based masking.
- ✅ **Priority inversion** = unbounded blocking of high-priority task; **PIP** = temporary priority boost solves it.
- ✅ **Always** declare shared variables as `volatile`; **always** match `__disable_irq()` with `__set_PRIMASK(saved)`.
- ✅ **Barrier instructions:** `DMB` (data ordering), `DSB` (data + pipeline stall), `ISB` (instruction pipeline flush).
- ✅ **Mutex cannot be released from an ISR** — use a binary semaphore for ISR-to-task signaling.
- ✅ Mars Pathfinder (1997) is the **canonical real-world PIP success story** — quote it in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ In **KEIL RTX CMSIS-OS v2** API: `osMutexNew`, `osMutexAcquire`, `osMutexRelease`, `osSemaphoreNew`, etc.
- ✅ LPC1768 base address: `0x40000000` (APB0) / `0x40080000` (APB1) / `0x50000000` (AHB) — required when discussing peripheral sharing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Rate and Latency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transfer Rate and Latency # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of ARM-based embedded systems, computer architecture, and I/O interfacing, **Transfer Rate** and **Latency** are the two fundamental metrics that characterize the performance of *any* data-movement subsystem — whether it is a CPU-to-memory bus, a peripheral interface (UART, SPI, I2C), a DMA channel, an Ethernet MAC, or an SSD storage controller. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transfer Rate (Bandwidth)** is the rate at which data is moved between two points, measured in **bytes per second (B/s)**, **kilobytes per second (KB/s)**, or **megabytes per second (MB/s)**. It quantifies *how much* data can be moved per unit *time* after the pipeline is full. > > **Latency** is the *time delay* between the initiation of a data-transfer request and the moment the first bit (or first byte) of useful data becomes available at the destination. It is measured in **microseconds (μs)**, **milliseconds (ms)**, or **clock cycles**. Mathematically, for transferring a block of **N bytes** over a channel of bandwidth **B** and latency **L**: $$T_{\text{total}} = L + \frac{N}{B}$$ where: - $T_{\text{total}}$ is the **total transfer time**, - $L$ is the **latency** (one-time setup overhead), - $N$ is the **number of bytes** transferred, - $B$ is the **sustained transfer rate** (bandwidth). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Pipeline Imagine you are filling a swimming pool using a long garden hose connected to a tap. - **Latency** is the time you stand there *waiting* after turning the tap before the first drop of water appears at the hose nozzle. This is a **fixed overhead** — it does not matter if the pool needs 1 litre or 1,000 litres; you always wait this delay once. - **Transfer Rate** is how *fast* water flows *out* of the hose *after* the water starts coming — measured in litres per minute. A wider hose gives a higher rate. - The **total time** to fill the pool is `latency + (pool_size ÷ flow_rate)`. For a small glass of water, the latency dominates. For an Olympic-sized pool, the transfer rate dominates. This is *exactly* how memory reads, disk I/O, and network packets behave in an ARM SoC. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly classifies these two metrics under the *"Performance Equation"* of a memory/data-transfer subsystem. You will see them appear in problems involving *multi-stage pipelines* (e.g., CPU → Cache → Main RAM → SSD → Network). ## 1.3 Where These Two Metrics Live Inside an ARM SoC | Subsystem in ARM Cortex-M/A System | Typical Latency | Typical Transfer Rate | |---|---|---| | Cortex-M4 Tightly-Coupled Memory (TCM) | 1–2 core cycles (~10 ns @ 100 MHz) | Up to **400 MB/s** | | SRAM on AHB-Lite bus | 10–20 ns | ~100 MB/s | | External SDRAM (32-bit, 100 MHz) | 50–100 ns | **400 MB/s** | | QSPI Flash (XIP mode) | 30–80 ns | ~50 MB/s | | SDMMC/eMMC card | 100–500 μs | 50–200 MB/s | | Ethernet MAC (100 Mbit/s) | network RTT dependent | 12 MB/s | | SPI peripheral @ 50 MHz | 1–2 μs | ~6 MB/s | The two metrics are *independent*. A device can be high-bandwidth but high-latency (e.g., DDR SDRAM) or low-latency but low-bandwidth (e.g., on-chip SRAM). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Performance Equation (Deconstructing the Total Transfer Time) When the CPU core issues a read/write transaction, the data does **not** arrive instantly. The total time taken to complete a transfer of **N bytes** is composed of two clearly distinguishable phases: **Phase 1 — The Latency Phase (Fixed Overhead)** - Time spent issuing the command. - Time for the request to travel through bus arbitration logic. - Time for the device to recognize, decode, and begin the operation. - Time for the first byte to physically appear on the data bus. **Phase 2 — The Transfer Phase (Size-Dependent)** - Time spent streaming the remaining `N - 1` bytes. - This phase scales **linearly** with the block size and **inversely** with the bandwidth. For a single contiguous transfer: $$T_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{L}_{\text{latency}} + \underbrace{\frac{N}{B}}_{\text{transfer time}}$$ - When **N is very small** (a few bytes — e.g., reading a single peripheral register), the **latency term dominates**, and a faster bus barely helps. - When **N is very large** (a kilobyte or more — e.g., a DMA burst or a block memory copy), the **transfer term dominates**, and bandwidth is everything. > [!TIP] > This is *exactly why* ARM Cortex-M cores use a *TCM (Tightly-Coupled Memory)* for interrupt vectors and small critical data — its latency is single-digit nanoseconds, even though its bandwidth is similar to a slower SRAM. ## 2.2 Multi-Stage Pipeline — The Resistor Analogy In a real ARM SoC, data rarely travels directly from CPU register to a peripheral. It crosses a *chain* of stages: $$\text{CPU} \rightarrow \text{L1 Cache} \rightarrow \text{Bus Matrix (AXI/AHB)} \rightarrow \text{Memory Controller} \rightarrow \text{DRAM} \rightarrow \text{SSD Controller} \rightarrow \text{SSD NAND}$$ For such **series-connected stages**: - **Total Latency is additive** (like resistors in *parallel*): $$L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + \dots + L_n$$ - **Effective Bandwidth is bounded by the slowest stage** (like resistors in *series*): $$\frac{1}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1}{B_1} + \frac{1}{B_2} + \dots + \frac{1}{B_n}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The slowest stage is called the **bottleneck**. No matter how fast the other stages are, the overall bandwidth cannot exceed the bandwidth of the bottleneck. ## 2.3 Effective Transfer Rate (Observed Bandwidth) Because of the latency overhead, the **observed (effective)** transfer rate for a small block is *always less* than the advertised peak bandwidth: $$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{N}{L + \frac{N}{B}}$$ As `N → ∞` (very large transfers), $B_{\text{eff}} \rightarrow B$ (peak). As `N → 0` (tiny transfers), $B_{\text{eff}} \rightarrow 0$ (latency kills throughput). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{\text{total}} = L + \dfrac{N}{B}$ | Total time for a single transfer | seconds (s) | | 2 | $B_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{N}{L + N/B}$ | Observed/Effective transfer rate | bytes/s | | 3 | $L_{\text{total}} = \sum L_i$ | Series-connected latencies add | s, μs, or cycles | | 4 | $\dfrac{1}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{B_i}$ | Series-connected bandwidths (harmonic sum) | s/byte | | 5 | $N_{\text{half}} = L \times B$ | Block size at which latency = transfer time (crossover point) | bytes | | 6 | $T_{\text{pipeline}} = L + (k - 1)\tau$ | Pipeline latency formula (k stages, τ stage time) | s | | 7 | $\text{Throughput}_{\text{pipeline}} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | Steady-state pipeline throughput | items/s | > [!NOTE] > **Formula 5 is a KTU favourite.** Set $L = N/B \Rightarrow N = L \times B$. This is the *crossover block size* at which latency and transfer time contribute equally. Below this size, reduce latency. Above it, increase bandwidth. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility These two metrics are the foundation of every *performance budget* in ARM-based product design: - **Real-time embedded systems** (motor control, ADAS): Latency is critical → use TCM, lock cache lines. - **Bulk data movers** (video streaming, SSD controllers): Bandwidth is critical → use wide buses, DMA, burst mode. - **IoT / Battery devices**: Power ∝ (transitions × capacitance). High bandwidth with frequent short transfers wastes energy — so designers minimize both `L` and `B` for low-duty workloads. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Total Transfer Time Equation We start from first principles. **Step 1 — Define the components of a transfer.** Any data transfer consists of a *fixed setup phase* (latency) followed by a *variable streaming phase* (transfer time). Let: - $T_{\text{setup}} = L$ (the latency, a constant). - $R$ = the rate of byte arrival once streaming starts = $B$ (bytes per second). - $N$ = the total number of bytes to be transferred. **Step 2 — Express the streaming time.** The streaming phase duration is simply the total bytes divided by the rate: $$T_{\text{stream}} = \frac{N}{B}$$ **Step 3 — Add the two phases (they occur sequentially, not in parallel).** Since the setup must complete *before* streaming can begin: $$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{setup}} + T_{\text{stream}}$$ Substituting: $$T_{\text{total}} = L + \frac{N}{B}$$ **Step 4 — Sanity check (boundary cases).** - If $L = 0$ (ideal zero-latency channel): $T_{\text{total}} = N / B$. Correct. - If $B \to \infty$ (instantaneous channel): $T_{\text{total}} = L$. Correct. - If $N = 1$ byte: $T_{\text{total}} = L + 1/B$. Correct (latency + one byte of streaming). ## 3.2 Derivation of the Crossover Block Size We seek the value of $N$ at which the latency contribution equals the transfer-time contribution. **Step 1 — Equate the two terms.** $$L = \frac{N}{B}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $N$.** $$N = L \times B$$ This is the *crossover block size* $N_{\text{half}}$. For transfers smaller than this, latency dominates. For transfers larger than this, bandwidth dominates. **Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.** If $L = 200\,\text{ns} = 200 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}$ and $B = 100\,\text{MB/s} = 100 \times 10^{6}\,\text{B/s}$: $$N_{\text{half}} = 200 \times 10^{-9} \times 100 \times 10^{6} = 20 \text{ bytes}$$ So for any block >20 bytes, improving bandwidth matters more than reducing latency. ## 3.3 Derivation of Effective Bandwidth in a Multi-Stage Pipeline **Step 1 — Setup.** Consider two stages in series. Each stage $i$ has latency $L_i$ and bandwidth $B_i$. A packet of size $N$ must pass through *both* stages. **Step 2 — Time spent in stage 1.** $$T_1 = L_1 + \frac{N}{B_1}$$ **Step 3 — Time spent in stage 2.** $$T_2 = L_2 + \frac{N}{B_2}$$ **Step 4 — Total time (sequential).** $$T_{\text{total}} = L_1 + L_2 + N\left(\frac{1}{B_1} + \frac{1}{B_2}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Effective bandwidth definition.** $$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N}{T_{\text{total}}}$$ **Step 6 — Factor out $N$ and rearrange.** $$\frac{1}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{N} = \frac{L_1 + L_2}{N} + \frac{1}{B_1} + \frac{1}{B_2}$$ **Step 7 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$ (steady state, large transfer).** The first term vanishes, leaving: $$\frac{1}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1}{B_1} + \frac{1}{B_2}$$ Generalizing to $n$ stages: $$\frac{1}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{B_i}$$ ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** An ARM Cortex-A53 cluster reads a 16 KB image from an SD card. The path is: - SD card interface: $L_1 = 200\,\mu\text{s}$, $B_1 = 20\,\text{MB/s}$ - AHB bus: $L_2 = 50\,\text{ns}$, $B_2 = 100\,\text{MB/s}$ - L1 cache load: $L_3 = 20\,\text{ns}$, $B_3 = 400\,\text{MB/s}$ **Find:** (a) total time, (b) effective bandwidth, (c) which stage is the bottleneck. **Solution (a) — Total time:** $$N = 16 \text{ KB} = 16{,}384 \text{ bytes}$$ Stage times: - $T_1 = 200 \times 10^{-6} + \dfrac{16{,}384}{20 \times 10^{6}} = 200 \times 10^{-6} + 819.2 \times 10^{-6} = 1019.2\,\mu\text{s}$ - $T_2 = 50 \times 10^{-9} + \dfrac{16{,}384}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 50 \times 10^{-9} + 163.84 \times 10^{-6} \approx 163.89\,\mu\text{s}$ - $T_3 = 20 \times 10^{-9} + \dfrac{16{,}384}{400 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \times 10^{-9} + 40.96 \times 10^{-6} \approx 40.98\,\mu\text{s}$ $$T_{\text{total}} = 1019.2 + 163.89 + 40.98 = 1224.07\,\mu\text{s} \approx 1.224\,\text{ms}$$ **Solution (b) — Effective bandwidth:** $$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{16{,}384}{1224.07 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 13.39\,\text{MB/s}$$ **Solution (c) — Bottleneck identification:** The SD card stage contributes $1019.2\,\mu\text{s}$, more than **83%** of the total time. It is the bottleneck. Speeding up the AHB or L1 cache would have negligible effect. > [!TIP] > **KTU-style exam tip:** Always compute each stage's contribution *as a percentage*. The stage with the highest percentage is the bottleneck. This is the fastest way to score full marks on a 7-mark sub-question. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multi-Stage Transfer Pipeline The following Mermaid block-level architecture flow illustrates how a data block propagates from the ARM CPU core through several latency/bandwidth-limited stages to the external storage device. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CPU CORE
L=2 cycles, B=2000 MB/s"] B["L1 CACHE
L=3 cycles, B=800 MB/s"] C["L2 CACHE
L=15 cycles, B=400 MB/s"] D["AXI BUS MATRIX
L=20 ns, B=200 MB/s"] E["MEMORY CONTROLLER
L=50 ns, B=133 MB/s"] F["DRAM MODULE
L=80 ns, B=20 MB/s"] G["SSD NAND FLASH
L=200 us, B=5 MB/s"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G H["TOTAL LATENCY
L_total = sum of L_i"] I["EFFECTIVE BANDWIDTH
1/B_eff = sum of 1/B_i"] H -.governs.- A I -.governs.- A ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** The rectangles represent transfer stages; each has its own latency (delay) and bandwidth (rate). Latencies add up along the path. The harmonic sum of bandwidths gives the *effective* bandwidth. The slowest stage is the **bottleneck**. ## 4.2 Concept Diagram — Latency vs Transfer Time vs Block Size This diagram shows the *crossover point* at which the latency term and the transfer-time term contribute equally to the total time. ```mermaid flowchart TD J["BLOCK SIZE N = small
e.g. 1 register read = 4 bytes"] K["LATENCY L
dominates total time"] L["TOTAL TIME approx EQUAL TO L
B has very little effect"] M["BLOCK SIZE N = LARGE
e.g. 4 KB DMA burst"] N["TRANSFER TIME N/B
dominates total time"] O["TOTAL TIME approx EQUAL TO N/B
L is negligible"] P["CROSSOVER POINT
N_half = L x B"] J --> K --> L M --> N --> O L --> P O --> P ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineer's Takeaway:** A 2× reduction in latency helps *small* transfers dramatically but barely affects bulk transfers. Conversely, a 2× increase in bandwidth helps *bulk* transfers dramatically but is wasted on small transfers. ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Optimizing a Transfer Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TD Q["START: Identify workload
block size N"] R{"Is N less than N_half ?"} S["OPTIMIZE LATENCY
Use TCM, zero-wait-state SRAM,
cache lock, fast interrupts"] T["OPTIMIZE BANDWIDTH
Use wider bus, DMA, burst mode,
multi-channel memory controller"] U["N_half = L x B
Calculate crossover"] Q --> U U --> R R -- YES --> S R -- NO --> T ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip (7-mark question):** If a question gives you an N and asks *"which parameter should you optimize?"*, the answer is decided by comparing N with $L \times B$. Do **not** skip this calculation — it is the marker for the "Apply" level in Bloom's taxonomy. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] Define **Transfer Rate** and **Latency** in the context of an ARM-based memory system. Give one example for each metric. **Model Answer:** > **Transfer Rate (Bandwidth):** It is the rate at which data bytes are moved between two points in the system, measured in bytes per second (B/s). Example: A 32-bit AHB bus running at 100 MHz has a peak transfer rate of `4 × 100 × 10^6 = 400 MB/s`. > > **Latency:** It is the time delay between the issuance of a transfer request and the arrival of the first byte of data at the destination, measured in seconds or clock cycles. Example: Reading from external SDRAM typically has a CAS latency of 2–3 clock cycles, i.e., 20–30 ns at 100 MHz. *(3 marks: 1 for definition of transfer rate, 1 for definition of latency, 1 for example.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] Write the performance equation for transferring a block of N bytes over a channel of bandwidth B and latency L. Explain the significance of the crossover point $N_{\text{half}} = L \times B$. **Model Answer:** > $$T_{\text{total}} = L + \dfrac{N}{B}$$ > > where $L$ is the latency, $B$ is the bandwidth, and $N$ is the block size. > > **Significance of $N_{\text{half}}$:** It is the block size at which the latency term and the transfer-time term contribute equally to the total time. For $N < N_{\text{half}}$, latency dominates and reducing $L$ gives the biggest performance gain. For $N > N_{\text{half}}$, bandwidth dominates and increasing $B$ gives the biggest gain. *(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for explaining the two terms, 1 for crossover point significance.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** (a) Derive the total time equation $T_{\text{total}} = L + \dfrac{N}{B}$ for a single-stage memory transfer. Define each variable and explain the role of latency and bandwidth for small vs large block sizes. **\[7 marks\]** (b) An ARM Cortex-M4 processor reads data from an external QSPI flash device in two scenarios: - Scenario 1: $L = 100\,\text{ns}$, $B = 50\,\text{MB/s}$, $N = 32\,\text{bytes}$ - Scenario 2: $L = 100\,\text{ns}$, $B = 50\,\text{MB/s}$, $N = 4\,\text{KB}$ Calculate the total time and effective bandwidth for each scenario. What conclusion do you draw about the relative importance of latency vs bandwidth? **\[7 marks\]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** The total transfer time consists of two sequential phases: - A *fixed setup phase* of duration $L$ (latency). - A *variable streaming phase* of duration $N / B$ (transfer time), where $B$ is the rate and $N$ is the block size. Since these phases occur sequentially and cannot overlap, the total is their sum: $$T_{\text{total}} = L + \frac{N}{B}$$ Variables: - $L$ = latency (seconds) - $N$ = number of bytes transferred - $B$ = sustained bandwidth (bytes/second) For **small N** (e.g., register reads), the $L$ term dominates — total time ≈ $L$. For **large N** (e.g., bulk DMA), the $N/B$ term dominates — total time ≈ $N/B$. This is the central design principle for choosing TCM vs DRAM in ARM SoCs. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Scenario 1: N = 32 bytes** $$T_{\text{total}} = 100 \times 10^{-9} + \frac{32}{50 \times 10^6} = 100\,\text{ns} + 640\,\text{ns} = 740\,\text{ns}$$ $$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{32}{740 \times 10^{-9}} = 43.24\,\text{MB/s}$$ **Scenario 2: N = 4 KB = 4096 bytes** $$T_{\text{total}} = 100 \times 10^{-9} + \frac{4096}{50 \times 10^6} = 100\,\text{ns} + 81{,}920\,\text{ns} = 82{,}020\,\text{ns} = 82.02\,\mu\text{s}$$ $$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{4096}{82{,}020 \times 10^{-9}} = 49.94\,\text{MB/s}$$ **Conclusion:** In Scenario 1, effective bandwidth (43.24 MB/s) is far below the peak (50 MB/s) because latency is 13.5% of the total. In Scenario 2, effective bandwidth (49.94 MB/s) is very close to peak because latency is only 0.12% of the total. For small transfers, reducing latency matters most; for large transfers, increasing bandwidth matters most. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** > Students often forget to **convert units consistently** before adding. If latency is in nanoseconds and bandwidth is in MB/s, convert one of them. Here, $N/B$ gives seconds directly when $B$ is in **bytes/s** — note that `50 MB/s = 50 × 10^6 B/s`, *not* `50 × 2^20 B/s`. Mixing SI (×10^6) and binary (×2^20) units is a 1-mark deduction. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Alternative]** (a) Explain with a derivation why the effective bandwidth of `n` series-connected transfer stages is given by $\dfrac{1}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{B_i}$. Why is the slowest stage called the bottleneck? **\[7 marks\]** (b) A three-stage data path in an ARM-based SSD controller is given below: | Stage | Latency | Bandwidth | |---|---|---| | 1: CPU-to-PCIe bus | 200 ns | 2 GB/s | | 2: NVMe controller | 500 ns | 1 GB/s | | 3: NAND flash die | 50 μs | 200 MB/s | If a 1 MB file is transferred from the NAND to the CPU registers, calculate: (i) Total latency (ii) Time spent in each stage (iii) Effective bandwidth (iv) Percentage contribution of each stage, and identify the bottleneck. **\[7 marks\]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of series bandwidth formula:** Consider two stages with latencies $L_1, L_2$ and bandwidths $B_1, B_2$. A block of $N$ bytes passes through both sequentially: $$T_1 = L_1 + \frac{N}{B_1}, \quad T_2 = L_2 + \frac{N}{B_2}$$ $$T_{\text{total}} = L_1 + L_2 + N\left(\frac{1}{B_1} + \frac{1}{B_2}\right)$$ Dividing by $N$ and taking $N \to \infty$ (steady state): $$\frac{1}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1}{B_1} + \frac{1}{B_2}$$ Generalizing: $$\frac{1}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{B_i}$$ **Why the slowest stage is the bottleneck:** Since the harmonic sum is dominated by the *smallest* $B_i$ (i.e., the *reciprocal* $1/B_i$ is largest for the slowest stage), the bottleneck stage contributes the largest $1/B_i$ to the sum, and hence limits the overall throughput. Speeding up the other stages provides diminishing returns. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $N = 1 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes (or $10^6$ bytes; we will use $10^6$ for SI consistency). **Step (i) — Total Latency:** $$L_{\text{total}} = 200\,\text{ns} + 500\,\text{ns} + 50{,}000\,\text{ns} = 50{,}700\,\text{ns} = 50.7\,\mu\text{s}$$ **Step (ii) — Time per stage:** - Stage 1: $T_1 = 200 \times 10^{-9} + \dfrac{1 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.2\,\mu\text{s} + 0.5\,\mu\text{s} = 0.7\,\mu\text{s}$ - Stage 2: $T_2 = 500 \times 10^{-9} + \dfrac{1 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^9} = 0.5\,\mu\text{s} + 1.0\,\mu\text{s} = 1.5\,\mu\text{s}$ - Stage 3: $T_3 = 50 \times 10^{-6} + \dfrac{1 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^6} = 50\,\mu\text{s} + 5\,\mu\text{s} = 55\,\mu\text{s}$ **Total time:** $T_{\text{total}} = 0.7 + 1.5 + 55 = 57.2\,\mu\text{s}$ **Step (iii) — Effective bandwidth:** $$B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}}{57.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 17.48 \times 10^9 \text{ B/s} = 17.48\,\text{GB/s}$$ **Step (iv) — Percentage contribution:** - Stage 1: $0.7 / 57.2 \times 100 = 1.22\%$ - Stage 2: $1.5 / 57.2 \times 100 = 2.62\%$ - Stage 3: $55 / 57.2 \times 100 = 96.15\%$ **Bottleneck identification:** Stage 3 (NAND flash die) contributes **96.15%** of the total time. It is the bottleneck. Optimizing the PCIe bus or NVMe controller would yield negligible improvement; the only meaningful optimization is replacing or parallelizing the NAND flash dies. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** > A very common error is to *sum the bandwidths* instead of summing the *reciprocals*. Students write $B_{\text{eff}} = B_1 + B_2 + B_3 = 3.2\,\text{GB/s}$ — this is **wrong** for series stages. Series bandwidth uses **harmonic sum** of reciprocals. Parallel bandwidth uses algebraic sum. Always check whether the stages are *series* (data passes through each) or *parallel* (data is split across them). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Two metrics:** Transfer rate (bandwidth) is *how fast* data flows; latency is *how long until first data arrives*. They are independent and must be optimized separately. - **Total time:** $T_{\text{total}} = L + N/B$ — latency and transfer-time are **additive**, not multiplicative. - **Crossover block size:** $N_{\text{half}} = L \times B$ — below this size, optimize latency; above it, optimize bandwidth. - **Series-connected stages:** Latencies add directly; reciprocals of bandwidths add (harmonic sum). This is the **bottleneck principle**. - **Bottleneck:** The stage with the **highest percentage contribution** to total time. Optimizing any other stage has a vanishingly small effect (Amdahl-like argument). - **Effective bandwidth** is always $\le$ peak bandwidth; the gap widens for small block sizes due to latency overhead. - **Pipeline latency** $T_{\text{pipeline}} = L + (k-1)\tau$ applies when the *same hardware* is reused (instruction pipeline), and is *not* the same as the multi-stage transfer latency formula. - **Engineering practice in ARM SoCs:** Use TCM for low-latency, small-data access; use DRAM with wide AXI bus + DMA for high-bandwidth bulk transfer; place bottleneck-aware logic only after profiling. - **SI vs Binary units:** KTU exam answers must specify whether `MB = 10^6` or `MB = 2^20`. The standard convention in transfer-rate calculations is **decimal (×10^6)**, but flash memory sizes are usually **binary (×2^20)**. Mismatch is a frequent 1-mark deduction. - **Marking scheme compliance (14-mark Q):** Typically 2 marks for definitions/diagram, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for substitution & calculation. Always show the formula before substitution. - **KTU Bloom's level:** Definition-type → *Remember* (L1); Crossover-point identification → *Apply* (L3); Multi-stage bottleneck analysis → *Analyse* (L4). Always phrase answers in the *verb* of the CO descriptor.

topics.title: Buffers and Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buffers and Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buffer (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A **buffer** is a region of physical memory storage used to temporarily hold data while it is being moved from one place to another. In the context of ARM-based embedded systems, buffers are critical data structures that decouple the **producer** (data source) and **consumer** (data sink), allowing them to operate at different speeds without data loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Queue (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A **queue** is a linear data structure that follows the **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** discipline. In ARM programming, queues are used to manage ordered sequences of data items (bytes, words, or structures) using two primary pointers — a **head pointer** (for removal/dequeue) and a **tail pointer** (for insertion/enqueue).

### Classification of Buffers and Queues in ARM Systems

| Type | Description | Typical ARM Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Linear Buffer** | Sequential memory region; once full, cannot accept more data without overwrite. | Simple data logging in SRAM |
| **Circular Buffer (Ring Buffer)** | Wraps around using modular arithmetic; head/tail pointers wrap to base address. | UART RX/TX buffers, ADC sampling |
| **Double Buffer** | Two alternating buffers for ping-pong operation. | DMA transfers, display refresh |
| **Priority Queue** | Items removed based on priority, not arrival order. | RTOS task scheduling |
| **FIFO Queue** | Strict first-come-first-served discipline. | Peripheral data handling |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Restaurant Tray Counter (Circular Buffer):**
> Imagine a **circular conveyor belt** at a sushi restaurant. Plates (data) are placed on the belt by chefs (producer) at one end and removed by waiters (consumer) at the other. The belt has a fixed number of slots (buffer size). When the belt is full, the chef must wait. When the belt is empty, the waiter must wait. The belt keeps moving in a circle — when the last slot is reached, the index **wraps back to slot 0**. This is precisely how a **circular buffer** operates in ARM memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Token Counter at a Bank (FIFO Queue):**
> Customers take a token from a machine when they arrive. The clerk serves the customer holding the **oldest token first**.
