> The **bell** is the hardware **IRQ**, and your reaction (stop, serve, return) is the **ISR execution + context restoration**. The CPU is the chef, the peripheral (UART, ADC, GPIO) is the grill cook, and the bell wire is the interrupt controller (NVIC).

This analogy captures the essence of interrupt driven I/O: the CPU remains free to execute the main program (background tasks) and is only disturbed when a peripheral genuinely demands attention.

---

## 1.3 Why Interrupt Driven I/O? — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight: The syllabus specifically contrasts **Polled I/O** with **Interrupt Driven I/O**. Students must justify *why* interrupts are preferred in real-time embedded systems.

The principal engineering motivations are:

1. **CPU Utilization Efficiency**: Polled I/O ties up 100% of the CPU cycles for waiting. Interrupt driven I/O frees the CPU to execute meaningful code, often achieving **CPU utilization > 95%**.
2. **Deterministic Response**: Modern ARM Cortex-M3/M4 NVIC provides **tail-chain latency of just 12 clock cycles**, critical for hard real-time systems.
3. **Power Efficiency**: The CPU can be put into sleep/WFI (Wait For Interrupt) modes, waking only on IRQ, drastically reducing dynamic power consumption.
4. **Multi-tasking Emulation**: Allows pseudo-concurrent handling of multiple peripherals (UART, Timer, ADC) without a full RTOS.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (ARM Cortex-M3/M4)

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are **typical baseline values** for the ARM Cortex-M3/M4 processor used in industry-standard LPC1768 / STM32 boards (KTU lab references).

| Metric | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Number of Interrupt Inputs | Up to **240** external + 16 internal = **256 total** |
| Priority Levels | **8 to 256** programmable levels (device dependent) |
| Tail-Chain Latency | **12 clock cycles** |
| Interrupt Entry Latency | **12 cycles** |
| Interrupt Exit Latency | **12 cycles** |
| NVIC Register Base | **0xE000E000** (ARM standard memory map) |
| Vector Table Offset Register | **0xE000ED08** (VTOR) |

---

## 1.5 Polled I/O vs Interrupt Driven I/O — A Direct Comparison

| Feature | Polled I/O | Interrupt Driven I/O |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CPU Involvement | Continuous checking | Only on demand |
| Complexity | Very simple | Requires ISR design |
| Response Time | Depends on polling period | Deterministic (NVIC controlled) |
| CPU Utilization | Low (CPU wastes cycles) | High (CPU does useful work) |
| Power Consumption | High (always active) | Low (sleep modes possible) |
| Multi-Device Support | Inefficient | Excellent (priority-based) |
| Suitability for Real-Time | Poor | Excellent |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Cross-Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**: This topic directly maps to the syllabus outcome **CO4** — *"Design and program interrupt-driven I/O systems using ARM Cortex microcontrollers."* Make sure you can differentiate the two I/O methods in two-sentence answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of an Interrupt Driven I/O Transaction

The complete lifecycle of an interrupt driven I/O operation can be broken into **six distinct phases**. Each phase has a deterministic hardware or software responsibility.

### Phase 1: Peripheral Event Occurrence

The I/O device (e.g., UART receiver) finishes its internal operation. For example, a byte has been received and placed into the receive data register. At this instant, the peripheral's internal **status flag** is set (e.g., `UART->S1.RDRF = 1` on Kinetis, or `USART->SR.RXNE = 1` on STM32).

### Phase 2: Interrupt Request Generation

The peripheral, when properly configured, **asserts its IRQ line** to the NVIC. This is a one-bit hardware signal routed to the corresponding NVIC channel. The peripheral must have its **interrupt enable bit** set in software during initialization (e.g., `USART->CR1.RXNEIE = 1`).

### Phase 3: NVIC Arbitration and Acceptance

The NVIC receives the IRQ, compares its **priority level** against the currently executing priority (if any), and decides whether to:
- **Accept immediately** (higher or equal priority pending)
- **Pre-empt the current handler** (if pre-emption is enabled and priority is higher)
- **Queue it for later** (lower priority, pre-emption disabled)

The processor then completes the current instruction (or accepts a pre-emption point).

### Phase 4: Automatic State Preservation (Hardware Stacking)

The ARM Cortex-M processor **automatically pushes** the following registers onto the current stack (Process Stack Pointer – PSP, or Main Stack Pointer – MSP):
- `R0, R1, R2, R3, R12`
- `LR (R14)`
- `PC (R15)`
- `xPSR`

This is the **hardware stacking** mechanism — no software `PUSH` instructions are required, ensuring speed and atomicity.

### Phase 5: Vector Table Lookup and ISR Execution

The NVIC reads the address of the corresponding ISR from the **Vector Table** located at the address pointed to by the **VTOR (Vector Table Offset Register)**. This address is loaded into the PC, and execution begins at the first instruction of the ISR.

### Phase 6: ISR Body and Return

The ISR:
1. Reads/writes the peripheral data register (clearing the IRQ).
2. Performs any necessary data processing.
3. May post a semaphore or set a flag for the main program.
4. Executes `BX LR` or returns via the special `EXC_RETURN` value that the hardware pushed onto the stack.

The hardware then **un-stacks** the saved registers and resumes the main program at the exact point of interruption.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Interrupt Priority

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Board Exam Favourite: Be prepared to explain **priority-based pre-emption** with an example.

The NVIC implements **two bits of priority** by default in the simplest ARM Cortex-M3 configuration, but most modern devices (e.g., LPC1768) implement **5 bits of priority** (32 levels). The lowest numerical priority value is the **highest** logical priority (priority 0 is the highest, non-maskable).

**Example**: If two interrupts (UART0 and TIMER0) fire simultaneously, and UART0 is assigned priority 2 while TIMER0 is assigned priority 1, the NVIC will execute the TIMER0 ISR first, then UART0 ISR (tail-chained).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **mathematical foundation** of interrupt latency, response time, and throughput — all are recurring KTU exam questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Interrupt Latency** ($t_{lat}$) | $t_{lat} = t_{inst} + t_{stack} + t_{vector}$ | seconds | Time from IRQ assertion to first ISR instruction |
| 2 | **Interrupt Response Time** ($t_{resp}$) | $t_{resp} = t_{lat} + t_{ISR}$ | seconds | Time from IRQ assertion to ISR completion |
| 3 | **CPU Utilization (Polled)** | $U_{polled} = \dfrac{t_{active}}{t_{active} + t_{wait}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of time CPU is busy waiting |
| 4 | **CPU Utilization (Interrupt)** | $U_{int} \approx 1 - \dfrac{t_{ISR} \cdot f_{IRQ}}{f_{CPU}}$ | dimensionless | Approximation for low interrupt rates |
| 5 | **Effective Throughput** | $\theta = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{t_{total}}$ | bytes/sec | Bytes transferred per unit time |
| 6 | **Maximum Sustainable IRQ Rate** | $f_{IRQ,max} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{C_{ISR} \cdot N_{cycles,ISR}}$ | Hz | IRQ rate where CPU is 100% saturated |
| 7 | **Stack Memory per Interrupt** | $S_{frame} = 8 \times 4\,\text{bytes} = 32\,\text{bytes}$ | bytes | Hardware-stacked registers (Cortex-M) |
| 8 | **Total Stack Memory (Nested)** | $S_{total} = S_{frame} \times N_{nested,max}$ | bytes | Worst-case nested IRQ depth |

Where:
- $t_{inst}$ = time to complete the currently executing instruction
- $t_{stack}$ = time to push 8 registers (8 cycles on Cortex-M3)
- $t_{vector}$ = time to fetch the ISR address from the vector table (4 cycles)
- $C_{ISR}$ = average cycles consumed per ISR
- $N_{cycles,ISR}$ = number of instructions in the ISR
- $N_{nested,max}$ = maximum nesting depth allowed

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Interrupt driven I/O is the **backbone of modern embedded systems**:

1. **Automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units)**: Use CAN bus interrupt driven I/O to handle 1000+ messages per second from various sensors without missing a single engine cycle.
2. **IoT Edge Devices (ESP32, STM32)**: GPIO interrupts wake the CPU from deep sleep only on user button press, extending battery life from days to **months**.
3. **Medical Devices (Pacemakers, Insulin Pumps)**: Hard real-time interrupt priorities ensure critical life-safety signals are serviced within microseconds.
4. **Industrial Robotics**: Encoder interrupts on motor shafts provide precise position feedback at 100 kHz, enabling sub-millimeter accuracy.
5. **Consumer Electronics (Smartphones)**: Touch screen interrupt driven I/O allows the application processor to sleep until the user touches the screen, saving substantial power.

---

## 2.5 Latency vs Response Time — The Distinction KTU Loves to Test

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often confuse **latency** with **response time**. They are NOT the same.
>
> - **Latency** = Time until the ISR *begins* executing.
> - **Response Time** = Time until the ISR *finishes* executing (latency + ISR execution duration).

Example: A UART ISR with latency 200 ns and a body taking 1.5 µs has a **response time of 1.7 µs**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Sustainable Interrupt Rate

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is frequently asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions to test analytical depth.

**Problem Statement**: A Kinetis ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller operates at a clock frequency $f_{CPU} = 100\,\text{MHz}$. An interrupt service routine (ISR) consumes an average of $C_{ISR} = 250\,\text{clock cycles}$ per invocation. Determine the **maximum sustainable interrupt request (IRQ) rate** such that the CPU is not overloaded (assuming 100% CPU saturation is the boundary).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the fundamental constraint.**

The CPU can only execute $f_{CPU}$ clock cycles per second. If the ISR consumes $C_{ISR}$ cycles per interrupt, and interrupts arrive at a rate $f_{IRQ}$, then the total cycles consumed per second is:

$$
C_{total} = f_{IRQ} \cdot C_{ISR}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the saturation condition.**

For the CPU to remain at the edge of saturation (not overloaded):

$$
C_{total} = f_{CPU}
$$

$$
f_{IRQ} \cdot C_{ISR} = f_{CPU}
$$

**Step 3: Solve algebraically for $f_{IRQ}$.**

$$
f_{IRQ} = \frac{f_{CPU}}{C_{ISR}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
f_{IRQ} = \frac{100 \times 10^6\,\text{Hz}}{250\,\text{cycles/IRQ}}
$$

$$
f_{IRQ} = \frac{100{,}000{,}000}{250}\,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
f_{IRQ} = 400{,}000\,\text{Hz} = 400\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**

The maximum sustainable IRQ rate is **400,000 interrupts per second**. If interrupts arrive faster than this, the CPU will saturate, and latency will grow unbounded.

**Step 6: Compute the safe operating limit (80% rule).**

To leave headroom for the main loop, embedded engineers typically design for 80% of this limit:

$$
f_{IRQ,safe} = 0.8 \times 400{,}000 = 320{,}000\,\text{Hz} = 320\,\text{kHz}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{f_{IRQ,max} = 400\,\text{kHz}, \quad f_{IRQ,safe} = 320\,\text{kHz}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Interrupt Latency in Clock Cycles

**Problem Statement**: A peripheral asserts an IRQ in the middle of a `LDR` instruction that requires $N_{inst} = 3\,\text{bus cycles}$ to complete. The Cortex-M3 hardware stacking consumes 8 cycles, and the vector fetch consumes 4 cycles. Compute the total interrupt latency in nanoseconds for a $72\,\text{MHz}$ clock.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the latency components.**

From the formula sheet:

$$
t_{lat} = t_{inst} + t_{stack} + t_{vector}
$$

**Step 2: Convert each component to clock cycles.**

- $t_{inst} = 3\,\text{cycles}$ (worst-case completion of the current instruction)
- $t_{stack} = 8\,\text{cycles}$ (push of 8 registers)
- $t_{vector} = 4\,\text{cycles}$ (vector table fetch)

**Step 3: Sum the cycle count.**

$$
N_{lat} = 3 + 8 + 4 = 15\,\text{cycles}
$$

**Step 4: Convert cycles to seconds using $T = 1/f_{CPU}$.**

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^6}\,\text{s} = 13.89\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the latency in nanoseconds.**

$$
t_{lat} = N_{lat} \cdot T_{cycle} = 15 \times 13.89\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
t_{lat} = 208.33\,\text{ns}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{t_{lat} \approx 208.33\,\text{ns}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Edge-Triggered vs Level-Triggered Interrupts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Exam Tip: A common 7-mark question asks for the comparison of edge-triggered and level-triggered interrupts. Master this table.

| Parameter | Edge-Triggered | Level-Triggered |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Activation** | On 0→1 (rising) or 1→0 (falling) transition | While line is held at active level |
| **Persistence** | Single event per transition | Continuous if line stays active |
| **Use Case** | Button press, one-shot events | UART RX FIFO, slow peripherals |
| **Spurious IRQs** | Possible (bounce/glitch) | Possible (line held low) |
| **Re-triggerability** | Cannot re-trigger until release | Re-triggers if not serviced |
| **ARM Cortex-M Default** | Configurable per-pin | Configurable per-pin |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: CPU Utilization in Interrupt Mode

**Problem**: A sensor produces an IRQ at $f_{IRQ} = 1\,\text{kHz}$. The ISR executes 500 instructions on a Cortex-M4 running at $f_{CPU} = 50\,\text{MHz}$. Each instruction takes 1 cycle on average. Calculate the CPU utilization.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute ISR execution time.**

$$
t_{ISR} = \frac{N_{cycles,ISR}}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{500}{50 \times 10^6} = 10\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the time between IRQs.**

$$
T_{IRQ} = \frac{1}{f_{IRQ}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\,\text{ms} = 1000\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the duty cycle (utilization).**

$$
U_{int} = \frac{t_{ISR}}{T_{IRQ}} = \frac{10\,\mu\text{s}}{1000\,\mu\text{s}} = 0.01
$$

**Step 4: Express as a percentage.**

$$
U_{int} = 0.01 \times 100\% = 1\%
$$

### Interpretation

The CPU spends only **1% of its time** servicing the interrupt, leaving **99% free** for the main program. Compare this with a polled system that would spend 100% of the time in the polling loop.

---

## 3.5 Verification and Sanity Checks

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Sanity Checklist**:
>
> 1. **Latency must be less than the deadline**: If the deadline is $1\,\text{ms}$, latency of $208\,\text{ns}$ is excellent.
> 2. **Sum of all ISRs' duty cycles must be < 100%**: $\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i < 1$.
> 3. **Stack overflow check**: Maximum nested depth × 32 bytes must fit in the allocated stack region.
> 4. **IRQ rate must be below $f_{IRQ,max}$**: Otherwise, the system is mathematically guaranteed to fail.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Interrupt Driven I/O Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Program Loop] --> B{CPU Idle}
    B --> C[Execute Background Tasks]
    C --> D{IRQ Asserted?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[NVIC Priority Check]
    E --> F{Higher Priority?}
    F -- No --> G[Queue IRQ]
    G --> C
    F -- Yes --> H[Save Context - 8 Registers]
    H --> I[Fetch ISR Address from Vector Table]
    I --> J[Execute ISR]
    J --> K[Clear IRQ Flag in Peripheral]
    K --> L[Restore Context]
    L --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart shows the **complete interrupt driven I/O cycle** in the ARM Cortex-M architecture. Notice the central role of the **NVIC** in deciding whether to service the interrupt immediately or defer it.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — NVIC Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NVIC[Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller]
        REG[Interrupt Set-Enable Register - ISER] --> MASK[Active Mask]
        MASK --> ARB[Priority Arbiter]
        ARB --> VEC[Vector Table Decoder]
        VEC --> LAT[Latency Counter]
    end
    PER1[UART Peripheral] -- IRQ0 --> REG
    PER2[TIMER Peripheral] -- IRQ1 --> REG
    PER3[GPIO Peripheral] -- IRQ2 --> REG
    PER4[ADC Peripheral] -- IRQ3 --> REG
    LAT --> CPU[CPU Core]
    CPU --> STK[Stack Memory]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The NVIC sits between the **peripherals** and the **CPU core**. It is the *traffic controller* for all interrupt requests, ensuring deterministic priority-based delivery.

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Interrupt Service Routine Execution Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant NVIC as NVIC
    participant UART as UART Peripheral
    participant MEM as Vector Table
    CPU->>CPU: Execute Main Loop
    Note over CPU,UART: Main program running normally
    UART->>UART: Byte Received
    UART->>NVIC: Assert IRQ (RXNE Flag Set)
    NVIC->>CPU: Interrupt Pending Signal
    CPU->>CPU: Complete Current Instruction
    CPU->>NVIC: Acknowledge IRQ
    NVIC->>MEM: Read ISR Vector Address
    MEM-->>NVIC: Return ISR Address
    NVIC->>CPU: Load PC with ISR Address
    CPU->>CPU: Hardware Stack 8 Registers
    CPU->>UART: Read Data Register (Clear Flag)
    CPU->>CPU: Process Received Byte
    CPU->>CPU: Hardware Un-stack 8 Registers
    CPU->>CPU: Resume Main Loop
```

> [!TIP]
> This sequence diagram is **golden for KTU viva voce** — examiners often ask students to walk through the exact sequence of events when an interrupt fires.

---

## 4.4 State Machine — Interrupt Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Pending : Peripheral Asserts IRQ
    Pending --> Active : NVIC Accepts (Priority Check)
    Active --> Active : Nested Higher Priority IRQ
    Active --> Servicing : Execute ISR Body
    Servicing --> Pending : IRQ Not Cleared (Re-trigger)
    Servicing --> Idle : Clear Flag and EXC_RETURN
    Idle --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The state machine illustrates the **four primary states** of an interrupt: *Idle → Pending → Active → Servicing → Idle*. Understanding transitions is critical for explaining ISR pre-emption in viva questions.

---

## 4.5 Memory Map — Vector Table Layout (ARM Cortex-M3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM[Vector Table in Flash - Base 0x00000000]
        A0[Offset 0x00 - Initial MSP Value]
        A1[Offset 0x04 - Reset Handler Address]
        A2[Offset 0x08 - NMI Handler Address]
        A3[Offset 0x0C - HardFault Handler Address]
        A4[Offset 0x10 - Reserved]
        A5[Offset 0x14 - Reserved]
        A6[Offset 0x18 - Reserved]
        A7[Offset 0x1C - Reserved]
        A8[Offset 0x20 - SVCall Handler Address]
        A9[Offset 0x24 - Debug Monitor]
        A10[Offset 0x28 - Reserved]
        A11[Offset 0x2C - PendSV Handler]
        A12[Offset 0x30 - SysTick Handler]
        A13[Offset 0x34 to 0x100 - External IRQs IRQ0...IRQ239]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vector table is the **cornerstone** of vectored interrupts. On reset, the first entry (offset 0x00) is loaded into the Main Stack Pointer (MSP), and the second entry (offset 0x04) is loaded into the Program Counter (PC) to start execution at the Reset Handler.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Interrupt Driven I/O. Mention any two advantages over polled I/O.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interrupt Driven I/O** is a data transfer technique in which the I/O peripheral **signals the processor** via a hardware interrupt request line (IRQ) when it requires servicing, eliminating the need for the CPU to continuously check the device status.
>
> **Two advantages over polled I/O**:
> 1. **Higher CPU Utilization**: The CPU is free to execute other tasks while waiting, achieving efficiency > 95% compared to nearly 0% in heavy polling.
> 2. **Lower Power Consumption**: The CPU can be placed in sleep mode and woken only on IRQ, drastically reducing dynamic power.
> 3. **Faster Response Time**: NVIC provides deterministic, hardware-controlled latency (typically 12 clock cycles on Cortex-M3) rather than depending on a software polling period.
> 4. **Better Multi-Device Handling**: Multiple peripherals can be serviced concurrently through priority-based pre-emption.
>
> *(Any two advantages — 1 mark each; definition — 1 mark)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)? List two characteristics of a well-designed ISR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** — also called an **Interrupt Handler** — is a special software function that the CPU executes in response to a hardware or software interrupt request. It services the requesting peripheral, performs minimal data handling, and then returns control to the interrupted main program.

**Two characteristics of a well-designed ISR**:
1. **Short Execution Time**: ISRs should be as brief as possible (typically < 10 µs) to avoid blocking other interrupts. Heavy processing should be deferred to the main loop using flags or queues.
2. **Re-entrant and Atomic**: ISRs should not call non-re-entrant functions, and shared data with the main program should be protected using `volatile` variables, atomic access, or critical sections.
3. **No Blocking Calls**: ISRs must never use blocking delays (`while` loops, semaphore waits) as they would freeze the entire system.
4. **Clears the Interrupt Flag**: The ISR must clear the peripheral's interrupt flag, or the IRQ will re-trigger immediately upon return.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4]`

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operation of Interrupt Driven I/O in an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller. Discuss the role of the NVIC and the Vector Table in interrupt handling.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Block Diagram and Architecture**

**Model Answer:**

> The Interrupt Driven I/O architecture in an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller consists of three main blocks:
>
> 1. **The I/O Peripheral** (e.g., UART, ADC, Timer): Asserts an IRQ when it requires attention.
> 2. **The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)**: Receives all IRQs, performs priority arbitration, and notifies the CPU.
> 3. **The CPU Core**: Saves its context, fetches the ISR address from the Vector Table, and executes the ISR.
>
> **Block Diagram** (already shown in SECTION 4.2): The NVIC sits between peripherals and CPU, controlling all interrupt flows.
>
> **Vector Table Role**: The Vector Table is a data structure in flash memory that stores the starting address of every ISR. When an interrupt is accepted, the NVIC reads the corresponding 32-bit vector and loads it into the Program Counter (PC). On ARM Cortex-M, the Vector Table starts at address **0x00000000** (configurable via VTOR register at 0xE000ED08).
>
> The first 16 entries (0x00 to 0x40) are reserved for system exceptions (Reset, NMI, HardFault, SVCall, SysTick, etc.), and the remaining 240 entries are for external IRQs.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Step-by-Step Operation**

**Model Answer:**

The complete operation of an interrupt driven I/O cycle in ARM Cortex-M is:

**Step 1 — Peripheral Initialization**:
The peripheral's interrupt enable bit is set in software (e.g., `USART->CR1 |= RXNEIE;`). The NVIC's corresponding channel is enabled via the Interrupt Set-Enable Register (ISER), and a priority is assigned via the Interrupt Priority Register (IPR).

**Step 2 — Event Occurrence**:
When the peripheral's event occurs (e.g., byte received), it sets an internal status flag and asserts its IRQ output line to the NVIC.

**Step 3 — NVIC Arbitration**:
The NVIC compares the new IRQ's priority with the currently active priority. If the new IRQ has equal or higher priority, it pre-empts (or tail-chains) the current handler.

**Step 4 — Automatic Hardware Stacking**:
The processor completes the current instruction, then **automatically** pushes 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) onto the stack pointed to by MSP. This takes 8 clock cycles.

**Step 5 — Vector Fetch**:
The NVIC reads the 32-bit ISR address from the Vector Table entry corresponding to the IRQ number. This takes 4 clock cycles.

**Step 6 — ISR Execution**:
The PC is loaded with the ISR address. The ISR:
- Reads/clears the peripheral's data register
- Processes the data minimally
- Posts a flag or queues the data for main loop processing
- Returns via `BX LR` (using the special `EXC_RETURN` value)

**Step 7 — Hardware Unstacking and Resume**:
The processor automatically pops the 8 saved registers and resumes the main program at the exact point of interruption.

> **Timing Summary** (at 72 MHz, 13.89 ns/cycle):
> - Hardware stacking: 8 × 13.89 = 111 ns
> - Vector fetch: 4 × 13.89 = 56 ns
> - **Total minimum entry latency: 12 × 13.89 ≈ 167 ns**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]`

**Q: Compare Polled I/O and Interrupt Driven I/O. Compute the maximum sustainable interrupt rate and CPU utilization for a sensor system with the following specifications: Sensor IRQ rate = 2 kHz, ISR instruction count = 800, ARM Cortex-M4 clock = 100 MHz, average cycles per instruction = 1.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Comparison Table**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Polled I/O | Interrupt Driven I/O |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CPU Activity | Continuous polling loop | Event-driven |
| Response Time | Depends on polling period | Deterministic (12–15 cycles) |
| Power Consumption | High | Low (sleep support) |
| Multi-device handling | Poor | Excellent (NVIC priority) |
| Code complexity | Simple | Moderate (ISR design) |
| Latency | Variable | Bounded |
| Suitability for real-time | Poor | Excellent |

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Computation**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute ISR execution time.**

$$
t_{ISR} = \frac{N_{cycles,ISR}}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{800 \times 1}{100 \times 10^6} = 8\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute maximum sustainable IRQ rate.**

$$
f_{IRQ,max} = \frac{f_{CPU}}{C_{ISR}} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{800} = 125{,}000\,\text{Hz} = 125\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the time between IRQs at 2 kHz.**

$$
T_{IRQ} = \frac{1}{f_{IRQ}} = \frac{1}{2000} = 500\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Compute CPU utilization.**

$$
U_{int} = \frac{t_{ISR}}{T_{IRQ}} = \frac{8\,\mu\text{s}}{500\,\mu\text{s}} = 0.016 = 1.6\%
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

- The system is **extremely underutilized** (1.6%), meaning the CPU has 98.4% free time for the main loop.
- The sensor's 2 kHz rate is **62.5 times lower** than the maximum sustainable rate (125 kHz), so there is massive headroom.
- If additional sensors were added, the system could handle up to $\frac{125}{2} = 62$ sensors of similar load before saturating.

### Final Results

$$
\boxed{f_{IRQ,max} = 125\,\text{kHz}, \quad U_{int} = 1.6\%, \quad \text{Free CPU} = 98.4\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> Students frequently lose marks on the following mistakes:
>
> 1. **Confusing latency with response time**: Latency is *start* of ISR, response time is *completion*. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to clear the interrupt flag in the ISR**: The IRQ will re-trigger immediately, causing an infinite loop. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Using blocking code in the ISR**: `delay_ms()` or `while(flag)` inside an ISR hangs the entire system. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting to enable the NVIC channel**: Just enabling the peripheral IRQ bit is not enough; the NVIC's ISER must also be set. (Lose 2 marks)
> 5. **Incorrect priority semantics**: ARM priority 0 is *highest* logical priority, not lowest. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Missing the VTOR register explanation**: Examiners love to ask how the Vector Table is relocated in bootloader scenarios. (Lose 1 mark)
> 7. **Unit conversion errors in numerical problems**: Always convert MHz to Hz before plugging into formulas. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Interrupt Driven I/O** ✅
>
> - [ ] Interrupt driven I/O is **event-driven**; the CPU is free until an IRQ is asserted.
> - [ ] The **ISR** is the software handler that services the IRQ; it must be short, atomic, and clear the interrupt flag.
> - [ ] **NVIC** (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller) is the ARM Cortex-M hardware block that manages up to 256 interrupts with priority-based pre-emption.
> - [ ] The **Vector Table** stores the addresses of all ISRs; it is located at the address in **VTOR** (default 0x00000000 on Cortex-M).
> - [ ] On interrupt entry, ARM Cortex-M **automatically stacks** 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) — no software PUSH is needed.
> - [ ] **Minimum interrupt latency** on Cortex-M3 is **12 clock cycles** (tail-chain); 15 cycles for a non-tail-chained interrupt.
> - [ ] **Latency** = time from IRQ assertion to ISR *start*; **Response Time** = time from IRQ assertion to ISR *end*.
> - [ ] **Maximum sustainable IRQ rate**: $f_{IRQ,max} = f_{CPU} / C_{ISR}$.
> - [ ] **CPU utilization in interrupt mode**: $U = t_{ISR} / T_{IRQ}$.
> - [ ] ISRs must **never** use blocking calls; defer heavy processing to the main loop using flags or queues.
> - [ ] Edge-triggered vs Level-triggered interrupts have different use cases (one-shot vs continuous).
> - [ ] The vector table's first entry (offset 0x00) holds the **initial MSP value**, the second (0x04) holds the **Reset Handler** address.
> - [ ] NVIC's Interrupt Set-Enable Register (ISER) is at offset 0x100; Interrupt Clear-Enable (ICER) at 0x180; IPR starts at 0x400.
> - [ ] The **SysTick** interrupt (vector 15) is commonly used for RTOS tick generation.
> - [ ] The **PendSV** interrupt (vector 14) is used for context switching in RTOS designs.
> - [ ] Power-saving modes (Sleep, Deep Sleep) integrate seamlessly with interrupt driven I/O via the **WFI** (Wait For Interrupt) instruction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** DMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a high-speed data transfer mechanism in ARM Cortex-M based microcontrollers (such as the LPC1768) that allows peripheral devices and memory blocks to exchange data **autonomously**, without continuous involvement of the Central Processing Unit (CPU). The DMA controller, a dedicated hardware block on the Advanced High-performance Bus (AHB) matrix, takes control of the bus from the CPU, performs the read/write operations, and returns the bus back to the CPU — thereby freeing the processor for computational tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO4):** *Describe the architecture, programming model, and data transfer mechanisms of peripheral controllers including DMA, and develop interrupt-driven and DMA-based interfacing programs for real-time embedded systems.*

The DMA controller in LPC1768 is called the **General Purpose DMA (GPDMA)** controller and is capable of:
- **8 independent DMA channels**
- Transfer widths of **8, 16, and 32 bits**
- Up to **65535 transfers per single operation**
- Linked-list (scatter-gather) descriptor chaining
- Programmable **burst size of 1, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, or 256 beats**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Analogy

Imagine a busy hospital where a doctor (CPU) is treating patients. Normally, every time lab reports arrive, the doctor must stop, receive them, and file them before resuming treatment — this is **polled I/O** or even **interrupt-driven I/O** (the doctor is interrupted briefly but still does the work).

Now imagine installing a pneumatic tube system (DMA controller) that **automatically sucks the lab reports from the lab** and **files them directly into the patient's chart** in the records room. The doctor never even knows the reports came in — he continues treating patients without interruption.

- The **lab** = Source peripheral (e.g., ADC, UART RX register)
- The **patient's chart** = Destination memory (RAM buffer)
- The **pneumatic tube system** = DMA controller (GPDMA)
- The **doctor** = ARM Cortex-M3 CPU

This is exactly what DMA achieves in embedded systems: **offloading bulk data movement** so the CPU can focus on computation, control logic, and decision making.

## 1.3 Why DMA is Essential in ARM Systems

| Without DMA | With DMA |
|--------------|----------|
| CPU reads byte by byte from peripheral | DMA controller reads/writes autonomously |
| CPU is busy 100% during transfer | CPU is free for other tasks |
| Slow for large buffers (e.g., 1 KB) | Fast — bus speed is the only limit |
| Higher power consumption | Lower power (CPU can even sleep) |
| Limited real-time capability | Deterministic, predictable transfers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** DMA is a **bus master** on the AHB matrix, just like the CPU. Arbitration logic decides who gets the bus when both want it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the LPC1768 GPDMA Controller

The General Purpose DMA controller sits on the AHB bus matrix. Each channel consists of:

- **Source address register** — pointer to read location
- **Destination address register** — pointer to write location
- **Control register** — transfer size, width, burst, increment behavior
- **Configuration register** — enables, interrupts, transfer type
- **Linked list pointer** — for chained (scatter-gather) operations

### 2.1.1 Signal Flow of a DMA Transfer

1. The CPU configures the channel registers (source, destination, size, control).
2. A **trigger** (peripheral request or software trigger) activates the channel.
3. The DMA controller requests the AHB bus from the arbiter.
4. The CPU is **temporarily stalled** (or the DMA gets a higher priority slot).
5. DMA performs **read from source** → **write to destination** cycles.
6. When all transfers complete, an **interrupt flag** is set (Terminal Count).
7. The bus is released back to the CPU.

## 2.2 DMA Transfer Modes

| Mode | Description | Typical Use Case |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Memory-to-Memory** | Both endpoints are RAM locations | Block copy, frame buffer shift |
| **Memory-to-Peripheral** | Source = RAM, Destination = peripheral data register | UART TX, DAC output |
| **Peripheral-to-Memory** | Source = peripheral data register, Destination = RAM | UART RX, ADC capture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Peripheral-to-Memory** transfers, the source address is typically **fixed** (the peripheral's data register does not change its address), while the destination increments.

## 2.3 Burst vs Single Transfer

- **Single transfer**: One AHB beat per bus request. The DMA controller and CPU can interleave more fairly.
- **Burst transfer**: Multiple consecutive AHB beats in one bus ownership. **Higher throughput** but CPU may be stalled for longer.

**Burst size** options in LPC1768: **1, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256** beats per burst.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside markdown table cells to avoid table-breaking issues.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{N \times C_{\text{beat}}}{f_{\text{AHB}}}$ | Total transfer time for N bytes | seconds |
| 2 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N \times 8}{T_{\text{trans}}}$ | Effective data rate | bits per second (bps) |
| 3 | $N_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{burst}} \times B$ | Total beats = beats per burst × number of bursts | beats |
| 4 | $T_{\text{CPU\_free}} = T_{\text{trans}} - T_{\text{setup}}$ | Time CPU is freed by using DMA | seconds |
| 5 | $\text{Bus utilization} = \dfrac{T_{\text{trans}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Fraction of bus time DMA occupies | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Channel priority}$ | 0 (lowest) to 7 (highest) for LPC1768 | ordinal |
| 7 | $f_{\text{AHB}} = f_{\text{CPU}}$ | In LPC1768, AHB runs at CPU clock | MHz |
| 8 | $T_{\text{beat}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{AHB}}}$ | Time for one AHB transfer | seconds |

Where:
- $N$ = number of data units (bytes, halfwords, or words)
- $C_{\text{beat}}$ = AHB cycles per beat (typically **1** for AHB; **2** for APB peripherals via bridge)
- $f_{\text{AHB}}$ = AHB bus frequency in Hz
- $B$ = burst size in beats
- $N_{\text{burst}}$ = number of bursts

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DMA is the backbone of high-performance embedded systems:
- **Audio processing**: Streaming PCM audio from SD card to DAC (e.g., 44.1 kHz, 16-bit stereo = 1.4 Mbps continuous)
- **Display driving**: Refreshing LCD frame buffers at 60 Hz without CPU overhead
- **Communication stacks**: UART, SPI, I2S bulk transfers in modems and IoT devices
- **Sensor fusion**: Reading multi-channel ADC at high sample rates (e.g., 1 MSPS)
- **Motor control**: Updating PWM duty cycles from control loops in real time

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DMA Transfer Time for a UART Block Transfer

**Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**
An LPC1768 operates at $f_{\text{CPU}} = f_{\text{AHB}} = 100\,\text{MHz}$. A UART RX DMA transfer moves a block of **1024 bytes** from the UART RX register to RAM using burst size $B = 4$ bytes and 8-bit transfer width. The UART peripheral is on the APB bus, which requires **2 AHB cycles per byte** to bridge.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 1024 \text{ bytes} \\
B &= 4 \text{ bytes per burst} \\
C_{\text{beat}} &= 2 \text{ AHB cycles per byte (APB bridge)} \\
f_{\text{AHB}} &= 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because the UART sits on the APB peripheral bus, every single byte read actually consumes two AHB clock cycles — one for the AHB-to-APB bridge handshake, and one for the actual APB data phase. Hence $C_{\text{beat}} = 2$.

**Step 2 — Compute total AHB cycles consumed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{cycles}} &= N \times C_{\text{beat}} \\
&= 1024 \times 2 \\
&= 2048 \text{ AHB cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each of the 1024 bytes requires 2 cycles, so the total bus time is 2048 cycles.

**Step 3 — Convert cycles to seconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{trans}} &= \frac{N_{\text{cycles}}}{f_{\text{AHB}}} \\
&= \frac{2048}{100 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 20.48 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
&= 20.48 \,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Frequency is cycles per second, so dividing cycles by frequency yields seconds.

**Step 4 — Compute the effective throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{N \times 8}{T_{\text{trans}}} \\
&= \frac{1024 \times 8}{20.48 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{8192}{20.48 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 400 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} \\
&= 400 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** 1024 bytes × 8 bits/byte = 8192 bits total. Dividing by time in seconds gives bits per second.

**Step 5 — CPU time saved compared to polling.**

In polled mode, the CPU would execute a load-store loop. Assuming each loop iteration takes **4 CPU cycles** (load, compare, branch, store), the CPU would consume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{CPU\_cycles}} &= 1024 \times 4 = 4096 \text{ cycles} \\
T_{\text{CPU\_polled}} &= \frac{4096}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 40.96 \,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

CPU time freed by using DMA:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{CPU\_free}} &= T_{\text{CPU\_polled}} - T_{\text{CPU\_setup}} \\
&\approx 40.96 - 2 \,\mu\text{s (config overhead)} \\
&= 38.96 \,\mu\text{s} \text{ freed per 1024-byte block}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Even after subtracting the small one-time DMA setup, the CPU gets ~95% of the transfer duration back for other work.

## 3.2 Derivation: Burst Transfer Time for Memory-to-Memory Copy

**Problem:** Copy 256 words (32-bit) from Flash to RAM using DMA with burst size $B = 8$ and $f_{\text{AHB}} = 72\,\text{MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Compute number of bursts.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{bursts}} &= \frac{N}{B} = \frac{256}{8} = 32 \text{ bursts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total beats (1 beat = 1 word = 32 bits).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{beats}} &= N_{\text{bursts}} \times B = 32 \times 8 = 256 \text{ beats}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute transfer time (1 AHB cycle per beat for memory-to-memory).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{trans}} &= \frac{N_{\text{beats}} \times 1}{f_{\text{AHB}}} = \frac{256}{72 \times 10^{6}} = 3.56 \,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with single-beat transfer (no bursting).**

If bursting were disabled, the DMA controller would release the bus after every beat, allowing the CPU to interleave. The total time remains similar, but **CPU latency improves** because the CPU is not starved for 8 consecutive cycles.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DMA vs Interrupt vs Polled I/O

| Criterion | Polled I/O | Interrupt I/O | DMA |
|-----------|------------|---------------|-----|
| CPU overhead per byte | High | Moderate (entry/exit + 1 transfer) | Negligible (one-time setup) |
| Transfer speed | Slow (limited by code loop) | Medium | Fast (hardware bus speed) |
| CPU availability during transfer | 0% | 30–60% | ~100% (CPU runs other tasks) |
| Determinism | Low (jitter from loop) | Medium | High (predictable bursts) |
| Code complexity | Low | Medium | High (descriptor setup) |
| Power efficiency | Poor | Medium | Excellent (CPU can sleep) |
| Suitable for | Single byte / debugging | Occasional small transfers | Bulk / continuous streams |

## 3.4 Linked-List (Scatter-Gather) Descriptor Chaining

For complex transfers (e.g., gathering ADC samples from 3 different channels into 3 separate buffers), the LPC1768 GPDMA supports **linked-list descriptors**. Each descriptor contains source, destination, control, configuration, and a pointer to the **next descriptor**.

When a transfer completes, the DMA automatically loads the next descriptor from memory, enabling **zero-CPU-overhead** multi-buffer or circular acquisition schemes.

> **Conversion Logic:** The DMA reads the next descriptor using the **LLI — Linked List Item** pointer. The final descriptor's LLI is set to **0** to terminate the chain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — GPDMA in the AHB Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuNode["ARM CORTEX M3 CPU"]
    arbiterNode["AHB BUS ARBITER"]
    gpdmaNode["GPDMA CONTROLLER 8 CHANNELS"]
    flashNode["FLASH MEMORY"]
    ramNode["SRAM 32 KB"]
    uartNode["UART0 UART1"]
    adcNode["ADC"]
    sspNode["SSP0 SSP1"]
    timerNode["TIMER MATCH REGISTERS"]

    cpuNode --> arbiterNode
    gpdmaNode --> arbiterNode
    arbiterNode --> flashNode
    arbiterNode --> ramNode
    arbiterNode --> uartNode
    arbiterNode --> adcNode
    arbiterNode --> sspNode
    arbiterNode --> timerNode

    uartNode -.DMA REQUEST.-> gpdmaNode
    adcNode -.DMA REQUEST.-> gpdmaNode
    sspNode -.DMA REQUEST.-> gpdmaNode
    timerNode -.DMA REQUEST.-> gpdmaNode
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The CPU and the GPDMA are both **bus masters** arbitrated by the AHB matrix. Peripherals (UART, ADC, SSP, Timer) act as **bus slaves** and raise DMA requests to the GPDMA when their data registers need servicing.

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — DMA Transfer Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["START CPU Configures DMA Channel"]
    cfgA["Set Source Address"]
    cfgB["Set Destination Address"]
    cfgC["Set Transfer Size and Width"]
    cfgD["Set Burst Size and Priority"]
    enA["Enable Channel Bit"]
    waitA["Wait for DMA Request Trigger"]
    busreq["DMA Requests AHB Bus"]
    grant["AHB Arbiter Grants Bus"]
    readop["DMA Reads from Source"]
    writeop["DMA Writes to Destination"]
    decA["Decrement Transfer Counter"]
    chkA{"Counter Equal Zero"}
    intA["Raise Terminal Count Interrupt"]
    relbus["Release AHB Bus"]
    endA["END Transfer Complete"]

    startA --> cfgA --> cfgB --> cfgC --> cfgD --> enA --> waitA --> busreq --> grant --> readop --> writeop --> decA --> chkA
    chkA -- NO --> busreq
    chkA -- YES --> intA --> relbus --> endA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flow shows the **closed-loop transfer loop** until the byte counter reaches zero. This is the canonical state machine for any DMA channel operation.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — CPU vs DMA Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph polled["WITHOUT DMA POLLED OR INTERRUPT PATH"]
        p1["PERIPHERAL"] -->|each byte| p2["CPU READS"]
        p2 -->|each byte| p3["CPU WRITES TO RAM"]
        p3 --> p4["CPU RESUMES TASK"]
    end

    subgraph dma["WITH DMA AUTONOMOUS PATH"]
        d1["PERIPHERAL"] -->|DMA request| d2["GPDMA CONTROLLER"]
        d2 -->|hardware burst| d3["RAM BUFFER"]
        d4["CPU FREE FOR OTHER TASKS"] -.independent.-> d2
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The visual emphasizes that with DMA, the CPU's role shifts from **byte mover** to **supervisor of independent hardware**.

## 4.4 Memory-Mapped Register Map of a DMA Channel

| Offset (from channel base) | Register | Purpose |
|----------------------------|----------|---------|
| 0x00 | DMACCxSrcAddr | Source address |
| 0x04 | DMACCxDestAddr | Destination address |
| 0x08 | DMACCxLLI | Linked list item pointer |
| 0x0C | DMACCxControl | Transfer size, width, burst, increment |
| 0x10 | DMACCxConfig | Enable, mask, trigger, HALT, lock |

> **Note:** In LPC1768, the GPDMA registers are memory-mapped in the AHB peripheral region. Channel-specific registers are at offsets `0x100 + (ch × 0x20)` to `0x100 + (ch × 0x20) + 0x14`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define DMA. List any two advantages of using DMA-based data transfer in ARM-based microcontrollers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a mechanism by which data is transferred between peripheral registers and memory (or between two memory locations) **without CPU intervention**, using a dedicated hardware controller called the **GPDMA** in LPC1768.

**Two advantages:**
1. **CPU offloading** — the CPU is freed to execute application code in parallel with data movement, drastically improving system throughput.
2. **Higher and more deterministic transfer rates** — DMA uses the AHB bus at hardware speed, which is faster than software loops, and the timing is predictable (important for real-time systems).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between burst-mode and single-mode DMA transfers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single Transfer | Burst Transfer |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Data per bus grant | 1 beat | Multiple beats (4, 8, 16, …) |
| CPU latency | Lower — bus is released often | Higher — CPU may be stalled |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher |
| Use case | Low-latency small transfers | Bulk streaming (audio, video) |

*(Award 1 mark for definition of each mode + 1 mark for differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **General Purpose DMA (GPDMA)** controller in LPC1768 with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each channel register. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LPC1768 (CPU clock = 100 MHz) needs to transfer 2048 bytes from the ADC data register to a RAM buffer using DMA in burst mode with burst size = 8 bytes. The ADC is on APB bus requiring 2 AHB cycles per beat. Calculate the total transfer time and effective throughput. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The GPDMA controller in LPC1768 is a bus master on the AHB matrix and supports **8 independent DMA channels**. Each channel consists of the following registers:

1. **DMACCxSrcAddr (Source Address Register)** — Holds the address from which data is read.
2. **DMACCxDestAddr (Destination Address Register)** — Holds the address to which data is written.
3. **DMACCxLLI (Linked List Item Register)** — Pointer to the next descriptor for scatter-gather chaining.
4. **DMACCxControl (Control Register)** — Configures transfer size (up to 65535), transfer width (8/16/32 bits), source/destination increment, and **burst size** (1 to 256).
5. **DMACCxConfig (Configuration Register)** — Enables the channel, masks interrupts, sets channel priority (0–7), and enables peripheral requests.

**Working:**
- The CPU configures the five registers once.
- A peripheral DMA request activates the channel.
- The GPDMA arbitrates for the AHB bus, performs read-write cycles, decrements the counter, and raises a **Terminal Count interrupt** when done.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $N = 2048$ bytes, $B = 8$ bytes/burst, $C_{\text{beat}} = 2$ AHB cycles, $f_{\text{AHB}} = 100$ MHz.

**Step 1 — Total AHB cycles:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{cycles}} &= N \times C_{\text{beat}} = 2048 \times 2 = 4096 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total transfer time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{trans}} &= \frac{N_{\text{cycles}}}{f_{\text{AHB}}} = \frac{4096}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 40.96 \,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{2048 \times 8}{40.96 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{16384}{40.96 \times 10^{-6}} = 400 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} = 400 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that **APB peripherals** need **2 AHB cycles per beat** due to the AHB-APB bridge. Using $C_{\text{beat}} = 1$ will give a wrong (doubled) throughput. Always check whether the peripheral is on AHB (1 cycle) or APB (2 cycles).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **step-by-step sequence of operations** performed by the DMA controller during a memory-to-peripheral transfer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare DMA-based transfer with **interrupt-driven** and **polled** I/O techniques on the basis of CPU overhead, transfer speed, code complexity, and real-time determinism. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Sequence of operations for Memory-to-Peripheral DMA transfer:**

1. **CPU configures channel** — Loads source RAM address into `DMACCxSrcAddr`, peripheral data register address into `DMACCxDestAddr`, transfer count into `DMACCxControl`, and enables the channel in `DMACCxConfig`.

2. **Trigger arrives** — The peripheral raises a DMA request line (e.g., UART TX ready) which is routed to the GPDMA.

3. **Bus arbitration** — The GPDMA requests ownership of the AHB bus from the matrix arbiter. If the CPU is using the bus, DMA is stalled until the CPU releases it.

4. **Read from source** — DMA performs a single AHB read from the source RAM location.

5. **Write to destination** — DMA performs a single AHB/APB write to the peripheral data register. The data is now physically shifted out by the peripheral.

6. **Address update and counter decrement** — Source address increments (since it is RAM), destination address remains fixed (peripheral register address is constant), and the transfer counter decrements by one.

7. **Loop or terminate** — If counter ≠ 0, the DMA repeats steps 3–6. If counter = 0, it raises a **Terminal Count (TC) interrupt** to the NVIC.

8. **Bus release and CPU notification** — The AHB bus is released; the CPU can either continue other tasks or service the TC interrupt to reconfigure or stop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Criterion | Polled I/O | Interrupt I/O | DMA |
|-----------|------------|---------------|-----|
| **CPU overhead per byte** | Very high (busy-wait loop) | Moderate (entry/exit + handler) | Negligible (one-time setup) |
| **Transfer speed** | Low (limited by CPU clock + loop overhead) | Medium | High (hardware bus speed) |
| **Code complexity** | Very low (simple while loop) | Medium (ISR coding) | High (channel + descriptor setup) |
| **Real-time determinism** | Poor (jitter from code) | Moderate (interrupt latency varies) | Excellent (predictable bus cycles) |
| **CPU availability** | 0% during transfer | ~30–60% | ~100% |
| **Power efficiency** | Poor (CPU always active) | Medium | Excellent (CPU can sleep) |

**Conclusion:** For **bulk or continuous** data streams (audio, ADC, display, communication buffers), DMA is the only viable solution. For **occasional single-byte** events (e.g., a push-button), polled or interrupt I/O is simpler and more economical.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Do not write a **one-sided** answer in comparison questions. You must present a **balanced table** with at least 4 criteria. Marks are awarded **per valid comparison row**, not per paragraph. A vague "DMA is better" statement without justification scores zero.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DMA = Direct Memory Access**, a bus master that moves data autonomously between memory and peripherals.
- LPC1768 has a **GPDMA** with **8 channels**, supporting 8/16/32-bit widths and bursts up to **256 beats**.
- **Bus master vs slave**: CPU and GPDMA are both masters; peripherals and memory are slaves. AHB arbiter decides who gets the bus.
- **Three transfer modes**: Memory-to-Memory, Memory-to-Peripheral, Peripheral-to-Memory.
- **APB peripherals cost 2 AHB cycles per beat**; AHB peripherals cost 1. This is the most common numerical error.
- **Key formulas to memorize**:
  - $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{N \times C_{\text{beat}}}{f_{\text{AHB}}}$
  - $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N \times 8}{T_{\text{trans}}}$
  - $N_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{burst}} \times B$
- **Burst mode** = high throughput, but higher CPU stall latency. **Single mode** = lower throughput, better CPU responsiveness.
- **Linked-list descriptors (LLI)** allow scatter-gather and circular buffer DMA without CPU intervention.
- **Terminal Count (TC) interrupt** signals completion of a DMA transfer; the channel must be re-enabled or pointed to a new descriptor for further transfers.
- **Channel priority (0–7)** determines arbitration when multiple channels request the bus simultaneously.
- **Real-time applications**: Audio streaming, ADC sampling, display refresh, motor control PWM updates, high-speed communication.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme exam tip**: Always show **units** in derivations and **clearly state assumptions** (e.g., "APB bridge = 2 cycles").
- **Common CO mapping**: This topic is mapped to **CO4** of PEECT525 — *Design and develop ARM-based interfacing solutions using interrupts and DMA for real-time embedded applications.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Initialization: Memory Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** System Initialization: Memory Layout ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **System Initialization** in the context of ARM-based embedded systems refers to the predefined, deterministic sequence of operations executed immediately after a processor reset (or power-on) that prepares the hardware and software environment for the main application. The **Memory Layout** is the central artifact of this initialization process: it is the *linker-defined map* describing how a program's various logical sections — `.text` (code), `.data` (initialized variables), `.bss` (zero-initialized variables), `.rodata`, **heap**, and **stack** — are physically or virtually placed across the processor's unified or Harvard memory architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "System initialization is the process of configuring the processor's stack pointers, copying initialized data from non-volatile memory (ROM/Flash) to volatile memory (RAM), zeroing the BSS section, and initializing the C runtime — all before `main()` is called." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House-Warming Ritual" Imagine you just bought a brand-new house (the **ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller**) but it is completely empty, dark, and unfurnished. Before you can "live" in it (i.e., run your `main()` program), a sequence of preparations must occur: | House Analogy | Embedded Equivalent | |---|---| | 🔑 Unlocking the front door | **Reset vector fetch** — CPU reads the initial Stack Pointer and Reset_Handler address from the vector table at `0x00000000`. | | 🪑 Bringing in furniture from the moving truck (stored in garage) | **Copying `.data` from Flash → RAM** (initialized global variables). | | 🧹 Cleaning empty rooms | **Zeroing the `.bss` section** in RAM. | | 🛏️ Setting up the bedroom for use | **Stack pointer initialization** to the top of RAM. | | 🏗️ Deciding which room gets what purpose (kitchen, bedroom) | **Linker scatter file / memory map** defining code/region placement. | | 🏃 Entering the house to start daily life | **Jumping to `main()`** after the startup code finishes. | > [!NOTE] > **Why is this critical for KTU exams?** Examiners frequently test whether students understand *who* performs the data copy, *where* the vector table resides, and *why* BSS must be zeroed — these are the three pillars of the System Initialization memory layout question. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - The Cortex-M vector table occupies the **first 256 bytes (0x100) of the address space** (system memory remap possible). - Typical RAM size for LPC2148 / STM32 boards: **32 KB to 256 KB**. - Flash (ROM) typical size: **128 KB to 1 MB**. - Stack grows **downward** (ARM AAPCS convention) from `__StackTop` toward lower addresses. - Heap grows **upward** from the end of BSS toward the stack bottom. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The ARM Memory Map (Cortex-M Unified) ARM Cortex-M processors use a **4 GB linear address space** (`0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`). The map is intentionally split into well-defined regions to allow deterministic access and vendor customization. | Address Range | Region | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | `0x00000000 – 0x1FFFFFFF` | **Code (Flash)** | Vector table, `.text`, `.rodata` | | `0x20000000 – 0x3FFFFFFF` | **SRAM** | `.data`, `.bss`, `heap`, `stack` | | `0x40000000 – 0x5FFFFFFF` | **Peripheral** | GPIO, UART, Timer registers | | `0x60000000 – 0x7FFFFFFF` | **External RAM** | SDRAM banks | | `0x80000000 – 0x9FFFFFFF` | **External Device** | External memory-mapped I/O | | `0xE0000000 – 0xE00FFFFF` | **System / PPB** | NVIC, SysTick, SCB | | `0xF0000000 – 0xFFFFFFFF` | **Vendor System** | ROM table, debug | ### 2.2 The Five Logical Memory Sections 1. **`.text` (Code Section)** — Read-only, executable, lives in Flash. Contains the machine instructions. 2. **`.rodata`** — Read-only data: string literals, `const` globals, lookup tables. Also in Flash. 3. **`.data`** — *Initialized* global/static variables. **Source lives in Flash, runtime copy lives in RAM.** This is the famous *copy-down* requirement. 4. **`.bss` (Block Started by Symbol)** — *Uninitialized* or *zero-initialized* globals/statics. Only the **size** is stored; the section is zeroed at startup. 5. **Stack + Heap** — Dynamic regions in RAM. Stack grows down, heap grows up. ### 2.3 The Startup Sequence — Step by Step The reset handler executes the following deterministic sequence (memorize the order!): 1. **Copy the initial Stack Pointer (MSP)** from vector table offset `0x00`. 2. **Fetch the Reset_Handler address** from vector table offset `0x04` and branch to it. 3. **Set up the stack** for each mode (in Cortex-M, just main stack pointer is needed; ARM7/ARM9 need banked SPs). 4. **Copy `.data` from LMA (Load Memory Address — Flash) to VMA (Virtual Memory Address — RAM)** using `__etext`, `__data_start__`, `__data_end__` symbols. 5. **Zero-fill the `.bss` section** using `__bss_start__`, `__bss_end__`. 6. **Run C++ static constructors** (`__libc_init_array`) if applicable. 7. **Call `main()`**. 8. **Enter infinite loop** when `main()` returns (since embedded systems have no OS to return to). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbol / Equation | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Vector table size (Cortex-M) | $V_{\text{size}} = 256 \text{ bytes} = 2^8$ | First 0x100 of memory map | | RAM required for `.data` | $\text{RAM}_{\text{data}} = \sum \text{sizeof(initialized globals)}$ | bytes | | RAM required for `.bss` | $\text{RAM}_{\text{bss}} = \sum \text{sizeof(zero-init globals)}$ | bytes | | Total static RAM | $\text{RAM}_{\text{static}} = \text{RAM}_{\text{data}} + \text{RAM}_{\text{bss}}$ | bytes | | Stack pointer initial value | $\text{SP}_{\text{init}} = \text{Address of } \texttt{\_\_StackTop}}$ | points to *one past* the last element | | Stack growth direction | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4$ | Full descending stack (FD) — ARM standard | | Heap growth direction | $\text{Heap}_{\text{new}} = \text{Heap}_{\text{old}} + \text{alloc}$ | grows upward | | Collision condition | $\text{Stack}_{\text{ptr}} \leq \text{Heap}_{\text{ptr}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{OVERFLOW}$ | catastrophic failure | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility:** In real-time safety-critical systems (automotive ECU, medical devices, avionics), a deterministic startup is *non-negotiable*. The IEC 61508 SIL-3 standard mandates that every variable must reach a known, declared state before application logic begins. The BSS zeroing and DATA copy steps guarantee this. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stack Memory Consumption per Function Call The ARM AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard) mandates that a function may freely clobber registers `r0–r3` (caller-saved / scratch) and `r12`, and must preserve `r4–r11` (callee-saved). The **minimum stack frame** a function may push contains: - Return address (LR backup): 4 bytes - Frame pointer (FP / r11 backup): 4 bytes - Callee-saved registers r4–r10: 7 × 4 = 28 bytes - Local variables: $L$ bytes - Function arguments on stack: $A$ bytes $$ S_{\text{frame}} = 4 + 4 + 7 \cdot 4 + L + A $$ $$ S_{\text{frame}} = 36 + L + A \;\;\text{bytes} $$ For $N$ nested function calls, the worst-case stack depth is: $$ S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_{\text{frame},i} = N \cdot (36 + \overline{L} + \overline{A}) $$ where $\overline{L}$ and $\overline{A}$ denote the average local-variable and argument sizes per function. ### 3.2 Derivation: Verifying a Successful Data Copy Given linker symbols: - `__etext` = LMA start of `.data` in Flash - `__data_start__` = VMA start of `.data` in RAM - `__data_end__` = VMA end of `.data` in RAM The data size is: $$ D_{\text{size}} = \text{\_\_data\_end\_\_} - \text{\_\_data\_start\_\_} $$ The startup code in C typically does: ```c uint32_t *src = &__etext; // source in Flash uint32_t *dst = &__data_start__; // destination in RAM uint32_t *end = &__data_end__; while (dst < end) { *dst++ = *src++; // copy 4 bytes per iteration } ``` **Numerical evaluation for KTU:** Suppose `__data_start__ = 0x20000000`, `__data_end__ = 0x20000100`, `__etext = 0x00008000`. $$ D_{\text{size}} = 0x20000100 - 0x20000000 = 0x100 = 256 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ \text{Iterations} = \frac{256}{4} = 64 \text{ loop iterations} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Memory Region Behavior | Region | Read | Write | Volatile? | Contains | |---|---|---|---|---| | `.text` (Flash) | ✅ | ❌ (Cortex-M) | No | Machine instructions, ISRs | | `.rodata` (Flash) | ✅ | ❌ | No | `const` arrays, strings | | `.data` (RAM) | ✅ | ✅ | Yes | Initialized globals | | `.bss` (RAM) | ✅ | ✅ | Yes | Zero-initialized globals | | Stack (RAM) | ✅ | ✅ (push) | Yes | Local vars, return addresses | | Heap (RAM) | ✅ | ✅ (malloc) | Yes | Dynamically allocated memory | | Peripherals (APB/AHB) | ✅ | ✅ | Yes | MMIO registers | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 examiner tip:** If asked "Why is `.data` copied from Flash to RAM?", the model answer is: *"RAM provides faster read/write access than Flash and supports modification at runtime, while Flash retains the data during power-off and serves as the non-volatile source of truth."* --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Memory Layout Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FLASH["0x00000000 - FLASH Region (Non-Volatile)"] VECT["0x00000000: Vector Table (256 B)
SP_init, Reset_Handler, IRQs"] TEXT["0x00000100: .text section
Machine code, ISRs"] RODATA["After .text: .rodata
const data, string literals"] ETEXT["__etext = LMA start of .data copy source"] end subgraph RAM["0x20000000 - SRAM Region (Volatile)"] DATASTART["0x20000000: __data_start__
.data section (initialized globals)"] DATAMID["Initialized global copies
e.g., int sensor_cal = 42"] DATAEND["0x20000080: __data_end__"] BSSSTART["0x20000080: __bss_start__
.bss section (zero-init)"] BSSEND["0x20000200: __bss_end__"] HEAPAREA["Heap region
grows UPWARD on malloc"] STACKAREA["Stack region
grows DOWNWARD on push"] STACKTOP["0x20007FFC: __StackTop (initial SP)"] end subgraph PERI["0x40000000 - Peripheral Region"] GPIO["GPIO Registers"] UART["UART Registers"] TIMER["Timer Registers"] end VECT --> TEXT TEXT --> RODATA RODATA --> ETEXT ETEXT -. "Startup copies
D_size bytes" .-> DATASTART DATASTART --> DATAMID --> DATAEND DATAEND --> BSSSTART --> BSSEND BSSEND --> HEAPAREA HEAPAREA -. "Collision!" .-> STACKAREA STACKAREA --> STACKTOP ``` ### 4.2 Startup Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Power-On / Reset"] --> B["CPU reads Vector[0] → initial SP"] B --> C["CPU reads Vector[1] → Reset_Handler address"] C --> D["Branch to Reset_Handler"] D --> E["Disable Interrupts (PRIMASK)"] E --> F["Copy .data: Flash LMA → RAM VMA
loop dst++ = src++"] F --> G["Zero-fill .bss section"] G --> H["Call C++ constructors (libc_init_array)"] H --> I["Enable Interrupts (optional)"] I --> J["Call main()"] J --> K{"main() returns?"} K -- "No" --> J K -- "Yes" --> L["Enter infinite loop or reboot"] ``` ### 4.3 Scatter Loading Concept (Linker View) ```mermaid flowchart LR OBJ1["main.o"] --> LINK["ARM Linker"] OBJ2["startup.o"] --> LINK OBJ3["uart.o"] --> LINK LIB["C Runtime Library (libc.a)"] --> LINK LINK --> SCATTER["Scatter File .sct"] SCATTER --> EXEC["Linked Executable (.elf / .axf)"] EXEC --> FLASHIMG["Flash Image: .text + .rodata + .data LMA"] EXEC --> RAMIMG["RAM Image: .data VMA + .bss (zero) + heap/stack"] ``` ### 4.4 Vector Table Schematic (Cortex-M Style) ```mermaid flowchart LR OFFSET0["Offset 0x00
Initial SP"] --> OFFSET1["Offset 0x04
Reset_Handler"] OFFSET1 --> OFFSET2["Offset 0x08
NMI_Handler"] OFFSET2 --> OFFSET3["Offset 0x0C
HardFault_Handler"] OFFSET3 --> OFFSET4["Offset 0x10 - 0x28
Reserved / MemManage / BusFault / UsageFault / Reserved x4"] OFFSET4 --> OFFSET5["Offset 0x2C
SVCall"] OFFSET5 --> OFFSET6["Offset 0x30 - 0x38
Debug Monitor / Reserved"] OFFSET6 --> OFFSET7["Offset 0x3C
PendSV_Handler"] OFFSET7 --> OFFSET8["Offset 0x40
SysTick_Handler"] OFFSET8 --> OFFSET9["Offset 0x44+
External IRQs (Vendor-specific)"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3 Mark Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is the significance of the vector table in ARM Cortex-M system initialization? List any four entries it contains.* **Model Answer:** The vector table is a predefined array of 32-bit words located at the start of memory (typically `0x00000000` in Cortex-M). The CPU fetches the initial Stack Pointer from offset `0x00` and the Reset_Handler address from offset `0x04` immediately after reset. The table contains: 1. Initial Main Stack Pointer 2. Reset_Handler 3. NMI_Handler 4. HardFault_Handler (Other entries: MemManage, BusFault, UsageFault, SVCall, PendSV, SysTick, and external IRQs.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between `.data` and `.bss` sections in ARM memory layout.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `.data` | `.bss` | |---|---|---| | Initialization | Has initial values stored in Flash | Zero-initialized at runtime | | Flash footprint | Yes (occupies LMA space) | No (only size stored) | | RAM footprint | Yes (VMA in RAM) | Yes (VMA in RAM) | | Startup action | Copied from Flash to RAM | Zero-filled by startup code | --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the memory layout of a typical ARM Cortex-M embedded system showing Flash and RAM regions, the vector table, `.text`, `.rodata`, `.data`, `.bss`, heap, and stack. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the complete startup sequence executed by the Reset_Handler before `main()` is called. Why is each step necessary? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** The memory layout is depicted as follows (adapted for LPC2148 / STM32 class devices): | Address (approx) | Region | Content | |---|---|---| | `0x00000000` | Flash | Vector Table (256 B) | | `0x00000100` | Flash | `.text` (code, ISRs) | | `0x0000XXXX` | Flash | `.rodata` (constants) | | `0x0000YYYY` | Flash (LMA) | Source of `.data` | | `0x20000000` | RAM (VMA) | `.data` (initialized vars) | | `0x200000ZZ` | RAM | `.bss` (zero-init globals) | | `0x20000WWW` | RAM | Heap (grows up) | | `0x20007FFC` | RAM | `__StackTop` (initial SP) | **Model Solution (b):** The Reset_Handler performs: 1. **Set stack pointer** (already loaded by hardware from vector[0]). 2. **Disable interrupts** to ensure atomicity. 3. **Copy `.data` from Flash to RAM** using `__etext`, `__data_start__`, `__data_end__` because RAM is faster and writable, while Flash holds the non-volatile source. 4. **Zero `.bss`** so all uninitialized globals begin at a deterministic zero value (mandatory for safety-critical systems). 5. **Invoke `__libc_init_array()`** to run C++ static constructors. 6. **Call `main()`** — control transfers to user application. 7. **Trap return** from `main()` in an infinite loop (embedded systems cannot exit to an OS). Each step is necessary to ensure deterministic, repeatable, and safe startup behavior. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** What is a scatter file in ARM? Explain its role in defining the memory layout. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A system has `__data_start__ = 0x20000000`, `__data_end__ = 0x20000200`, and `__etext = 0x00010000`. Calculate the number of bytes and loop iterations required to copy `.data` to RAM. If `__bss_start__ = 0x20000200` and `__bss_end__ = 0x20000500`, how many bytes of BSS will be zero-filled? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** A scatter file (`.sct` in Keil, `.lds` in GCC) is a linker configuration file that describes the **memory map** and **placement of execution regions** within a target. It defines: - Physical memory regions (`FLASH 0x00000000 0x00040000`, `RAM 0x20000000 0x00010000`). - Execution regions (`ER_RO`, `RW_IRAM1`, `STACK`) and their load/view addresses. - How input sections (`.o` files) are mapped into the final image. It is essential for multi-bank memories, bootloader-over-the-air updates, and mixed Flash/RAM designs. **Model Solution (b):** Data copy size: $$ D_{\text{size}} = 0x20000200 - 0x20000000 = 0x200 = 512 \text{ bytes} $$ Number of 32-bit loop iterations: $$ N_{\text{iter}} = \frac{512}{4} = 128 \text{ iterations} $$ BSS zero-fill size: $$ B_{\text{size}} = 0x20000500 - 0x20000200 = 0x300 = 768 \text{ bytes} $$ **Final answer:** 512 bytes of `.data` copied in 128 iterations; 768 bytes of `.bss` zero-filled. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes:** > 1. Confusing **LMA (Load Memory Address)** with **VMA (Virtual Memory Address)**. Always state that `.data` has TWO addresses. > 2. Stating "stack grows upward" — it grows **downward** in ARM (Full Descending). > 3. Forgetting to mention that the **vector table size is 256 bytes** on Cortex-M, not variable. > 4. Skipping the **"trap return from main"** step — it is a mandatory part of the startup sequence. > 5. Drawing the heap **above** the stack in diagrams — heap is at the low end, stack at the high end of free RAM. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Reset vector** is at `0x00000000`; CPU reads **SP** then **Reset_Handler** address. - ✅ Memory map is **4 GB linear** in Cortex-M, divided into Code, SRAM, Peripheral, External, System regions. - ✅ Five logical sections: **`.text`, `.rodata`, `.data`, `.bss`, stack/heap**. - ✅ **`.data`** requires a startup copy from Flash (LMA) to RAM (VMA). - ✅ **`.bss`** is zero-initialized at startup — Flash stores *only its size*. - ✅ **Stack grows down**; **heap grows up**; **collision = catastrophic failure**. - ✅ Startup sequence order: **Copy `.data` → Zero `.bss` → Init C++ → `main()` → Trap return**. - ✅ **Vector table** occupies first 256 bytes; first entry is initial SP, second is Reset_Handler. - ✅ **Scatter file** is the linker's blueprint for memory placement — mandatory in professional ARM builds. - ✅ Cortex-M has **hardware-controlled vector fetch**; legacy ARM7/ARM9 required software vector relocation. ---

topics.title: CPU and Vector Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** CPU and Vector Table # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The ARM Cortex-M CPU Core The **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** of the ARM Cortex-M family is based on the **ARMv7-M (Cortex-M3/M4)** or **ARMv6-M (Cortex-M0)** architecture. It is a **32-bit RISC** processor that uses a **load-store architecture** with a fixed instruction length of either 16-bit (Thumb) or 32-bit (Thumb-2). The CPU fetches instructions from the vector table on reset, decodes them, and executes them using its internal register bank. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Cortex-M CPU is a **Thumb-2 instruction set** processor that supports **bit-band**, **unaligned access**, and a **hardware-based exception model** built around a **fixed vector table** located at the start of memory (typically address `0x00000000`). ### 1.2 The Vector Table — Formal Definition The **Vector Table** is a predefined block of memory located at the beginning of the system memory map. It is an array of **32-bit words** (4 bytes each), where each entry holds the **starting address (handler function pointer)** of a specific exception or interrupt service routine (ISR). When an exception occurs, the CPU automatically fetches the corresponding handler address from the vector table and loads it into the **Program Counter (PC)**, causing a jump to the handler. > [!NOTE] > The term "vector" in *vector table* refers to a **jump address** (a pointer), not a mathematical vector. The first entry is the **Initial Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** value, not a function pointer. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **large office building** with a security desk at the main entrance. | Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Works | |---|---|---| | **CPU Registers** | The security guard's *clipboard* — holds the current visitor's info being processed | Fast scratchpad, accessed instantly | | **Program Counter (PC)** | The arrow on the building's *floor directory* — points to which office/room to visit next | Tracks execution flow | | **Stack Pointer (SP)** | A *stack of visitor badges* on the desk — last one placed is first one taken (LIFO) | Manages function calls and context | | **Link Register (LR)** | A *post-it note* with the room number you came from — so you can return after visiting | Holds return address | | **Vector Table** | The building's *emergency response directory* — each entry tells the guard exactly which team to call for each type of incident | Predefined lookup → fast response | | **Exception** | A *fire alarm* going off — interrupts normal visiting flow | Forces CPU to handle a special event | | **Handler / ISR** | The specialized *fire-fighting team* dispatched to handle that incident | Code that services the exception | So when a "fire alarm" (exception) rings, the guard doesn't wander the building searching for the right team. He just glances at the **emergency directory (vector table)**, finds the address of the fire team for that specific alarm, and dispatches them immediately. This is exactly how the ARM Cortex-M handles exceptions in **hardware, with zero software overhead**. > [!TIP] > KTU students should remember: **The vector table is read by HARDWARE, not by software.** You don't write code to "look up" the vector table — the CPU does it for you automatically in response to an exception. --- ## 1.4 ARM Cortex-M Register File The CPU has the following core registers (from the ARMv7-M Architecture Reference Manual): - **R0 – R12**: Thirteen **General-Purpose Registers (GPRs)** used for data operations. - **R13 / SP**: The **Stack Pointer** (banked: MSP = Main SP, PSP = Process SP). - **R14 / LR**: The **Link Register** — holds the return address after a function call (BL/BLX) or exception entry. - **R15 / PC**: The **Program Counter** — points to the current instruction being executed. - **xPSR**: The **Program Status Register** — combined view of APSR, IPSR, and EPSR. - **PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI**: **Exception mask registers** used to disable interrupts. - **CONTROL**: Special **control register** that selects the active stack pointer (MSP/PSP) and privilege level. > [!IMPORTANT] > In Cortex-M3/M4, there are **no CPSR/SPSR** like in classic ARM (ARM7/ARM9). The state is encoded in the unified **xPSR** register. This is a frequent KTU exam point of confusion. --- ## 1.5 Operating Modes (Cortex-M3/M4) | Mode | Description | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Thread Mode** | Normal program execution | Application code (can use MSP or PSP) | | **Handler Mode** | Exception/Interrupt service routines | ISR code (always uses MSP) | | **Privileged** | Full access to all memory and system control | Default after reset; used in ISRs | | **Unprivileged** | Restricted access — no access to system control space | Used for RTOS tasks to enforce sandboxing | > [!NOTE] > The **CONTROL** register's bit **[0]** selects between **MSP (0)** and **PSP (1)**. Bit **[1]** controls the privilege level in Thread mode. In Handler mode, the CPU is **always privileged** and **always uses MSP**, regardless of CONTROL. --- ## 1.6 The Vector Table at a Glance (Cortex-M3/M4) The vector table is typically defined in the startup file (e.g., `startup_stm32f407xx.s`) using the `.isr_vector` section.
