The standard table contains: | Offset | Exception Number | Vector Name | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `0x000` | — | **Initial MSP Value** | Top of stack on reset | | `0x004` | 1 | **Reset_Handler** | Entry point after power-on | | `0x008` | 2 | **NMI_Handler** | Non-Maskable Interrupt | | `0x00C` | 3 | **HardFault_Handler** | All unhandled faults | | `0x010` | 4 | **MemManage_Handler** | MPU fault | | `0x014` | 5 | **BusFault_Handler** | Bus access error | | `0x018` | 6 | **UsageFault_Handler** | Undefined instruction, divide by zero, etc. | | `0x01C` | 7–10 | Reserved | — | | `0x028` | 11 | **SVCall_Handler** | Supervisor call (used by RTOS) | | `0x02C` | 12 | **DebugMonitor_Handler** | Debug monitor | | `0x030` | 13 | Reserved | — | | `0x034` | 14 | **PendSV_Handler** | Pendable service (RTOS context switch) | | `0x038` | 15 | **SysTick_Handler** | System tick timer | | `0x03C+` | 16+ | **IRQ0, IRQ1, …** | External interrupts from peripherals | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why a Vector Table? The Hardware-Driven Exception Model In classic ARM (ARM7TDMI), when an IRQ occurs, the CPU: 1. Finishes the current instruction. 2. Switches to IRQ mode. 3. Saves PC + CPSR onto IRQ stack. 4. Forces PC = `0x00000018` (IRQ vector address). 5. Executes whatever is at that address. In **Cortex-M**, the process is **vastly improved**: 1. The exception number is used to **index directly** into the vector table (`address = base + 4 × exception_number`). 2. The **handler address** is loaded straight into **PC** — no fixed address, fully relocatable. 3. The hardware automatically **stacks** R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR onto the active stack (8 registers = 32 bytes). This is called **Tail-Chaining** when no unstacking is needed between back-to-back exceptions, giving **zero-cycle entry overhead**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The vector table can be **relocated** to any address in the code or SRAM region by writing the new base address into the **SCB->VTOR** register (Vector Table Offset Register). This is essential for **bootloaders** that place a secondary vector table in SRAM. --- ## 2.2 CPU Operational Stages on Reset The Cortex-M CPU follows a strict, hardware-defined boot sequence. Every KTU exam question on this topic revolves around these stages: ### Stage 1: Hardware Reset - Power-On Reset (POR) or system reset is asserted. - CPU samples boot pins (e.g., `BOOT0`, `BOOT1` on STM32) to determine the boot memory region. ### Stage 2: Initial SP Loading - The CPU reads the **first 32-bit word** of the vector table (offset `0x000`) and loads it into **MSP** (Main Stack Pointer). - This is the **only** memory access before the first instruction is fetched. ### Stage 3: Vector Fetch - The CPU reads the **second 32-bit word** (offset `0x004`) — this is the **Reset_Handler address**. - This value is loaded into the **PC**. ### Stage 4: Reset_Handler Execution - The CPU begins executing instructions at the reset handler address. - Typical reset handler code (in assembly, from the startup file) does: 1. Copy `.data` section from Flash → SRAM. 2. Zero-initialize the `.bss` section in SRAM. 3. Invoke the C runtime's `__libc_init_array()` for static constructors. 4. Call `main()`. - If `main()` ever returns, the reset handler typically jumps to an infinite loop or triggers a software reset. > [!NOTE] > The **reset vector** is therefore the *very first executable instruction* the CPU runs. The initial MSP value is *not an instruction* — it is a data value that the hardware consumes. --- ## 2.3 Exception Stacking — The "Hardware Push" When an exception is accepted, the CPU automatically performs this push sequence onto the active stack (MSP or PSP): $$\text{Stack} \leftarrow \{xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0\}$$ Each is a 32-bit word, so **8 words × 4 bytes = 32 bytes** are pushed. The **stacking order** (highest addressed word pushed first to lowest): | Stack Frame Offset | Register Pushed | |---|---| | `+0x1C` | `xPSR` | | `+0x18` | `PC` (return address) | | `+0x14` | `LR` | | `+0x10` | `R12` | | `+0x0C` | `R3` | | `+0x08` | `R2` | | `+0x04` | `R1` | | `+0x00` | `R0` | > [!WARNING] > KTU Pitfall: Students often confuse the **stack frame layout** direction. The xPSR is at the **highest address** (was pushed first), R0 is at the **lowest address** (pushed last). On exception return, R0 is restored first, xPSR last. --- ## 2.4 Exception Return — The "Hardware Pop" On exception return, the CPU uses a special mechanism called **EXC_RETURN**. When LR is loaded with a special value like `0xFFFFFFF1` (Return to Handler mode, MSP), the special value acts as a command — any instruction that writes to PC (like `BX LR` or `POP {PC,...}`) causes the CPU to **unstack** the 8 registers and resume execution. | EXC_RETURN Value | Mode to Return To | Stack to Use | |---|---|---| | `0xFFFFFFF1` | Handler | MSP | | `0xFFFFFFF9` | Thread | MSP | | `0xFFFFFFF D` | Thread | PSP | > [!TIP] > The high bits `0xF` followed by `0xF` in the EXC_RETURN are the magic prefix that the CPU recognizes. KTU students should remember that **EXC_RETURN is loaded into LR on exception entry** — your ISR function does not need to set it up; the hardware does it. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Vector Table Entry Size** | $S_{\text{vec}} = 4 \text{ bytes}$ | Each vector is a 32-bit address | | **Vector Address Calculation** | $V_{\text{addr}} = V_{\text{base}} + 4 \times N_{\text{exc}}$ | $N_{\text{exc}}$ = exception number (0-based) | | **Stack Frame Size on Exception** | $F_{\text{stack}} = 8 \text{ registers} \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}$ | Full hardware push, fixed for Cortex-M3/M4 | | **Total Vector Table Size (Cortex-M3/M4)** | $N_{\text{vec}} = \text{system exceptions} + \text{number of IRQs}$ | E.g., STM32F407: 16 + 82 = 98 → 392 bytes | | **MSP Reload on Reset** | $\text{MSP} \leftarrow M[0x00000000]$ | First word of vector table | | **PC Reload on Reset** | $\text{PC} \leftarrow M[0x00000004]$ | Second word = Reset_Handler | | **EXC_RETURN Format** | $\text{EXC\_RETURN} = 0xF\text{xxxxxxx}$ | Always in upper half-word | | **Vector Table Relocation** | $\text{SCB} \rightarrow \text{VTOR} = V_{\text{new\_base}}$ | Must be aligned to $\geq 2^{\lceil \log_2(\text{size}) \rceil}$ | | **VTOR Alignment Requirement** | $\text{align} = 2^{\lceil \log_2(N_{\text{vec}} \times 4) \rceil}$ | E.g., 98 vectors → align to 512 bytes | | **CPU Bit Width** | $W_{\text{CPU}} = 32 \text{ bits}$ | All registers, ALU, bus paths | | **NMI Priority** | $P_{\text{NMI}} = -2$ (fixed) | Cannot be masked, locked priority | | **HardFault Priority** | $P_{\text{HF}} = -1$ (fixed) | Catches all faults that escalate | > [!IMPORTANT] > The vertical bar `|` symbol was deliberately replaced with `\vert` / `−` in tables to prevent markdown table corruption. Use these formulas verbatim in your derivations. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bare-Metal Firmware**: Every embedded systems developer writes or modifies a `startup_*.c` file that contains the vector table. Without understanding this, you cannot write a bootloader. - **RTOS Design**: The **PendSV** and **SysTick** vectors are critical for preemptive context switching in FreeRTOS, CMSIS-RTOS, and similar. - **Crash Diagnostics**: The **HardFault_Handler** is where engineers dump the stacked PC, LR, and CFSR registers to identify the source of firmware crashes. - **Firmware Updates (OTA)**: A bootloader relocates the application's vector table to a different Flash region using `SCB->VTOR`. - **Safety-Critical Systems**: The **fixed, hardware-driven** nature of the Cortex-M exception model is what makes it preferable over classic ARM for automotive (AUTOSAR) and industrial (IEC 61508) systems. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Vector Address from Exception Number **Problem:** Given a Cortex-M3 MCU with the vector table located at base address `0x08000000` (internal Flash), calculate the address of the **SVCall_Handler** entry. **Step 1 — Identify the exception number of SVCall.** From the ARMv7-M architecture specification, SVCall is exception number **11**. **Step 2 — Apply the vector address formula.** $$ V_{\text{addr}} = V_{\text{base}} + 4 \times N_{\text{exc}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the values.** $$ V_{\text{addr}} = 0x08000000 + 4 \times 11 $$ $$ V_{\text{addr}} = 0x08000000 + 44 $$ **Step 4 — Convert 44 to hexadecimal.** $$ 44_{10} = 32_{16} = 0x2C $$ **Step 5 — Final address.** $$ V_{\text{addr}} = 0x0800002C $$ **Conclusion:** The CPU will jump to whatever 32-bit value is stored at Flash address `0x0800002C` whenever an SVC instruction is executed. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Total Vector Table Size and VTOR Alignment **Problem:** A hypothetical Cortex-M4 MCU has **96 total vectors** (16 system exceptions + 80 IRQs). Calculate the total vector table size in bytes and the required alignment for `SCB->VTOR`. **Step 1 — Compute total table size.** $$ S_{\text{table}} = N_{\text{vec}} \times 4 = 96 \times 4 = 384 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2 — Find the smallest power of 2 ≥ 384.** $$ 2^8 = 256 \quad (\text{too small}) $$ $$ 2^9 = 512 \quad (\text{acceptable}) $$ **Step 3 — VTOR alignment requirement.** $$ A_{\text{VTOR}} = 2^{\lceil \log_2(S_{\text{table}}) \rceil} = 2^9 = 512 \text{ bytes} $$ This means the vector table base address must be a multiple of 512, i.e., the lowest 9 bits of VTOR must be **zero**. **Step 4 — Verify with a sample address.** Valid base: `0x08000000` ✓ (multiple of 512) Valid base: `0x08010000` ✓ (multiple of 512) Invalid base: `0x08000100` ✗ (not a multiple of 512) --- ## 3.3 Derivation: SP Value After Stacking on Exception **Problem:** The MSP is initially `0x20008000` (top of SRAM). An IRQ is accepted. Calculate the new MSP after the hardware pushes the exception stack frame. **Step 1 — Stack direction in Cortex-M.** Cortex-M uses a **full-descending stack** by default. The stack grows **downward** in memory (toward lower addresses). Push operations **decrement SP first**, then store. **Step 2 — Each push decrements SP by 4 (one 32-bit word).** **Step 3 — Total decrement for a full exception push (8 words).** $$ \Delta_{\text{SP}} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 4 — Compute new MSP.** $$ \text{MSP}_{\text{new}} = \text{MSP}_{\text{old}} - 32 $$ $$ \text{MSP}_{\text{new}} = 0x20008000 - 0x20 $$ $$ \text{MSP}_{\text{new}} = 0x20007\text{FE0} $$ **Step 5 — Verify with the stack layout.** The lowest word at `0x20007FE0` holds `R0`, and the highest word at `0x20007FFC` holds `xPSR`. This matches the stacking order described in Section 2.3. > [!NOTE] > **Full-descending** means: SP starts high, points to the *last item pushed*, and each push *decrements* SP. This is the default for ARM AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Exception Behavior vs. Priority | Exception | Number | Priority (Fixed / Configurable) | Vector Address Offset | Stack Frame | |---|---|---|---|---| | Initial MSP | 0 | N/A | `0x00` | N/A | | Reset | 1 | -3 (highest) | `0x04` | Not stacked (PC loaded directly) | | NMI | 2 | -2 | `0x08` | 32 bytes | | HardFault | 3 | -1 | `0x0C` | 32 bytes | | MemManage | 4 | Configurable | `0x10` | 32 bytes | | BusFault | 5 | Configurable | `0x14` | 32 bytes | | UsageFault | 6 | Configurable | `0x18` | 32 bytes | | SVCall | 11 | Configurable | `0x2C` | 32 bytes | | PendSV | 14 | Configurable | `0x38` | 32 bytes | | SysTick | 15 | Configurable | `0x3C` | 32 bytes | | External IRQ0 | 16 | Configurable | `0x40` | 32 bytes | **Reading the matrix:** - **Lower priority number = higher urgency** (priority 0 is the "normal" baseline; negative numbers are higher). - All exceptions use the **same 32-byte frame** size — uniform hardware design simplifies ISR entry code. - Only **Reset** skips the stacking — it directly loads PC and starts running. --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Tail-Chain Latency Savings **Problem:** Two IRQs arrive back-to-back. The first ISR takes 50 cycles, the second takes 30 cycles. Compare total latency with and without tail-chaining. **Without Tail-Chaining (classic ARM style):** $$ T_{\text{noTC}} = T_{\text{unstack1}} + T_{\text{ISR1}} + T_{\text{stack2}} + T_{\text{ISR2}} + T_{\text{unstack2}} $$ $$ T_{\text{noTC}} = 12 + 50 + 12 + 30 + 12 = 116 \text{ cycles} $$ (Assuming 12-cycle unstack/stack overhead — typical for Cortex-M3/M4.) **With Tail-Chaining (Cortex-M3/M4 native):** $$ T_{\text{TC}} = T_{\text{unstack1}} + T_{\text{ISR1}} + T_{\text{TC\_gap}} + T_{\text{ISR2}} + T_{\text{unstack2}} $$ $$ T_{\text{TC}} = 12 + 50 + 6 + 30 + 12 = 110 \text{ cycles} $$ The 6-cycle "TC gap" replaces the 12 + 12 = 24 cycles of unstack + stack, saving 12 cycles per exception transition. This is a major engineering win for high-interrupt-rate systems like motor control and USB. > [!TIP] > KTU examiners love asking "**Why is Cortex-M better than ARM7 for interrupt-heavy applications?**" — Tail-chaining is the textbook answer. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cortex-M CPU Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal architecture of the ARM Cortex-M3/M4 CPU and its core register file. ```mermaid flowchart TB coreBus["AHB-Lite System Bus (32-bit)"] fetch["Instruction Fetch Unit
Reads from I-Code Bus"] decode["Decode Stage
Thumb / Thumb-2"] exec["Execute Stage
ALU + Multiply + Bit-Band"] intCtrl["Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller NVIC
Vector Table Pointer VTOR"] regFile["Register File (32-bit)"] r0["R0"] r1["R1"] r2["R2"] r3["R3"] r4["R4"] r5["R5"] r6["R6"] r7["R7"] r8["R8"] r9["R9"] r10["R10"] r11["R11"] r12["R12"] sp["R13 SP
MSP and PSP"] lr["R14 LR
Link Register"] pc["R15 PC
Program Counter"] xpsr["xPSR
APSR IPSR EPSR"] ctrlRegs["Special Registers
PRIMASK FAULTMASK BASEPRI CONTROL"] stackMem["SRAM Stack Memory
Full Descending"] coreBus --> fetch fetch --> decode decode --> exec exec --> regFile regFile --> r0 regFile --> r1 regFile --> r2 regFile --> r3 regFile --> r4 regFile --> r5 regFile --> r6 regFile --> r7 regFile --> r8 regFile --> r9 regFile --> r10 regFile --> r11 regFile --> r12 regFile --> sp regFile --> lr regFile --> pc regFile --> xpsr regFile --> ctrlRegs intCtrl -. "provides handler address" .-> pc sp -. "auto push/pop on exception" .-> stackMem intCtrl -. "reads vector" .-> coreBus ``` > [!NOTE] > The **NVIC** is integral to the Cortex-M CPU — it is not a separate peripheral. It works in lockstep with the CPU's PC loading to deliver hardware-level exception dispatch. --- ## 4.2 Vector Table Layout Diagram (Cortex-M3/M4) The following Mermaid diagram shows the standard vector table structure in Flash memory, including byte offsets and exception numbers. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph vecTab["Vector Table Base Address e.g. 0x08000000"] dir0["Offset 0x00
Initial MSP Value
(e.g. 0x20020000)"] dir1["Offset 0x04
Reset_Handler
Exception 1"] dir2["Offset 0x08
NMI_Handler
Exception 2"] dir3["Offset 0x0C
HardFault_Handler
Exception 3"] dir4["Offset 0x10 to 0x1C
MemManage BusFault UsageFault
Exceptions 4 5 6"] dir5["Offset 0x20 to 0x28
Reserved
Exceptions 7 to 10"] dir6["Offset 0x2C
SVCall_Handler
Exception 11"] dir7["Offset 0x30
DebugMonitor
Exception 12"] dir8["Offset 0x34
Reserved
Exception 13"] dir9["Offset 0x38
PendSV_Handler
Exception 14"] dir10["Offset 0x3C
SysTick_Handler
Exception 15"] dir11["Offset 0x40 onwards
IRQ0 IRQ1 ... IRQ81
Exceptions 16 to 97"] end bootROM["CPU Reset"] -->|read MSP| dir0 bootROM -->|load PC| dir1 dir1 -->|jump to| resetCode["Reset_Handler Code
Init clocks copy .data zero .bss"] resetCode -->|call| mainEntry["main function"] intSrc["Peripheral Interrupt e.g. USART1"] -->|exception 37| dir11 dir11 -->|jump to| isrCode["USART1_IRQHandler"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **All vector entries are 4 bytes wide (32 bits).** The first entry is **data** (MSP value), not a code address. The remaining entries are **code addresses** (function pointers). This is one of the most common KTU conceptual errors. --- ## 4.3 Reset Sequence Flowchart The following Mermaid sequence diagram captures the exact hardware-driven reset flow of a Cortex-M3/M4 CPU. ```mermaid flowchart TD a["Power On or NRST Pin Low"] b["CPU samples boot pins BOOT0 BOOT1"] c["Select boot region: Flash System Memory or SRAM"] d["Read 32-bit word at 0x0000 from selected region"] e["Load this value into MSP"] f["Read 32-bit word at 0x0004"] g["Load this value into PC"] h["CPU begins executing Reset_Handler"] i["Assembly startup runs: Copy .data to SRAM"] j["Zero .bss section in SRAM"] k["Call C runtime __libc_init_array"] l["Call main function"] m["Application code runs in Thread mode"] a --> b --> c --> d --> e --> f --> g --> h --> i --> j --> k --> l --> m m -->|exception IRQ| n["CPU enters Handler mode"] n --> o["Stack 8 registers onto active SP 32 bytes"] o --> p["Read handler address from VTOR + 4N"] p --> q["Jump to handler ISR"] q -->|EXC_RETURN| m ``` > [!TIP] > For KTU's "explain the reset sequence" question (typically 7 marks), walk through this flow step-by-step. Mention: boot pin sampling, MSP load, PC load, then software startup steps. --- ## 4.4 Exception Stacking — Block-Level Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["Active Stack SP points to top of stack"] s2["SP decrements by 4"] s3["R0 stored at new SP"] s4["SP decrements by 4"] s5["R1 stored at new SP"] s6["SP decrements by 4"] s7["R2 stored at new SP"] s8["SP decrements by 4"] s9["R3 stored at new SP"] s10["SP decrements by 4"] s11["R12 stored at new SP"] s12["SP decrements by 4"] s13["LR stored at new SP"] s14["SP decrements by 4"] s15["Return PC stored at new SP"] s16["SP decrements by 4"] s17["xPSR stored at new SP"] s18["CPU loads handler address into PC"] s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10 --> s11 --> s12 --> s13 --> s14 --> s15 --> s16 --> s17 --> s18 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **8 words are pushed in a fixed order** to keep the stack frame format predictable. This allows debuggers to always find R0 at `SP+0x00`, xPSR at `SP+0x1C`, etc., regardless of which exception fired. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** > **Q1. What is a vector table in ARM Cortex-M? List the first four entries.** **Model Answer:** A **vector table** is a block of memory at the start of the system memory map that contains the starting addresses of exception handlers. The CPU automatically reads it on reset and during exception handling. The first four entries are: | Offset | Entry | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `0x00` | **Initial MSP value** | Top of stack loaded into MSP | | `0x04` | **Reset_Handler** | First code executed after reset | | `0x08` | **NMI_Handler** | Non-Maskable Interrupt handler | | `0x0C` | **HardFault_Handler** | Catches all unhandled faults | > **[3 marks]** --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Q2. Differentiate between MSP and PSP in ARM Cortex-M3.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | MSP (Main Stack Pointer) | PSP (Process Stack Pointer) | |---|---|---| | **Used in** | Handler mode and Thread mode (default) | Thread mode only (optional) | | **Selected by** | `CONTROL[0] = 0` | `CONTROL[0] = 1` | | **After Reset** | Always active | Not used by default | | **Typical use** | Kernel, ISRs, exception stack frames | Application task stacks in RTOS | | **Privilege** | Default privileged stack | Can be used in unprivileged Thread mode | > **[3 marks]** --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]** ### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose ONE > **Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain the **CPU register organization** of the ARM Cortex-M3 processor. Highlight the role of SP, LR, and PC. **Model Solution:** The ARM Cortex-M3 CPU has a 32-bit register file consisting of the following: **(1) General-Purpose Registers R0 – R12 (13 registers):** - R0 – R7: Low registers, accessible by all 16-bit Thumb instructions. - R8 – R12: High registers, accessible only by 32-bit Thumb-2 instructions. - Used for data manipulation, address calculation, and function parameter passing (R0 – R3 hold the first four arguments per AAPCS). **(2) R13 — Stack Pointer (SP):** - A banked register: **MSP** (Main SP) and **PSP** (Process SP). - Only one is visible to software at any time, selected by `CONTROL[0]`. - **MSP** is used on reset, in Handler mode, and by the kernel. - **PSP** is used by application tasks in Thread mode (especially in RTOS environments). - Stack direction is **full-descending** by default. **(3) R14 — Link Register (LR):** - Holds the **return address** after a subroutine call (`BL` or `BLX`). - On exception entry, the CPU loads LR with a special value called **EXC_RETURN** (e.g., `0xFFFFFFF9`). - This value is a magic constant that tells the CPU to perform an exception return on the next write to PC. **(4) R15 — Program Counter (PC):** - Always points to the **next instruction to be fetched** (i.e., current address + 4 for 32-bit instructions, +2 for 16-bit instructions). - On reset, PC is loaded with the address of the **Reset_Handler** read from the vector table offset `0x04`. - Writing to PC causes a branch. **(5) xPSR — Program Status Register:** - A composite of **APSR** (flags: N, Z, C, V), **IPSR** (current exception number), and **EPSR** (Thumb state bit). - Read in three views: `APSR`, `IPSR`, `EPSR`; or combined as `xPSR`. **(6) Special Mask Registers:** - **PRIMASK**: Disables all exceptions except NMI and HardFault when set to 1. - **FAULTMASK**: Disables all exceptions except NMI when set to 1. - **BASEPRI**: Masks exceptions with priority **greater than or equal to** a threshold. **Diagram (Textual Representation):** ``` +---------------------------------+ | R0 | R1 | R2 | R3 | Low Registers | R4 | R5 | R6 | R7 | (Thumb-1 + Thumb-2) +---------------------------------+ | R8 | R9 | R10 | R11 R12 | High Registers +---------------------------------+ (Thumb-2 only) | R13 = SP (MSP or PSP) | | R14 = LR | | R15 = PC | +---------------------------------+ | xPSR (APSR + IPSR + EPSR) | +---------------------------------+ | PRIMASK | FAULTMASK | BASEPRI | +---------------------------------+ | CONTROL (SP selection, priv) | +---------------------------------+ ``` **Role Summary:** | Register | Role | |---|---| | **SP** | Stack management for function calls, exception stacking | | **LR** | Stores return address / EXC_RETURN value | | **PC** | Tracks current execution point, set on reset from vector table | > **[7 marks]** --- > **Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Vector Table** of ARM Cortex-M3. How is the address of an exception handler calculated? Illustrate with an example for IRQ5. **Model Solution:** **Definition:** The vector table is a **predefined array of 32-bit words** located at the base of the system memory map (typically `0x00000000` for Flash-based systems, but relocatable via `SCB->VTOR`). **Structure of the Vector Table (Cortex-M3):** | Offset | Exception # | Name | |---|---|---| | `0x00` | — | Initial MSP value | | `0x04` | 1 | Reset | | `0x08` | 2 | NMI | | `0x0C` | 3 | HardFault | | `0x10` | 4 | MemManage | | `0x14` | 5 | BusFault | | `0x18` | 6 | UsageFault | | `0x1C – 0x28` | 7 – 10 | Reserved | | `0x2C` | 11 | SVCall | | `0x30` | 12 | Debug Monitor | | `0x34` | 13 | Reserved | | `0x38` | 14 | PendSV | | `0x3C` | 15 | SysTick | | `0x40 +` | 16 + | External IRQs | **Address Calculation Formula:** $$ V_{\text{addr}} = V_{\text{base}} + 4 \times N_{\text{exc}} $$ where $V_{\text{base}}$ is the value in `SCB->VTOR` and $N_{\text{exc}}$ is the exception number. **Example for IRQ5:** External IRQs start at exception number 16. So IRQ5 is: $$ N_{\text{exc}} = 16 + 5 = 21 $$ With $V_{\text{base}} = 0x08000000$: $$ V_{\text{addr}} = 0x08000000 + 4 \times 21 = 0x08000000 + 84 $$ $$ 84_{10} = 0x54 $$ $$ V_{\text{addr}} = 0x08000054 $$ When IRQ5 fires, the CPU: 1. Stacks R0 – R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR (32 bytes). 2. Reads the 32-bit value at `0x08000054` from Flash. 3. Loads it into PC and begins executing the handler. > **[7 marks]** --- ### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **reset sequence** of an ARM Cortex-M3 processor in detail. What is the role of the initial stack pointer value in the vector table? **Model Solution:** The reset sequence is a **hardware-driven, deterministic process** that the CPU performs on power-on or external reset. **Step 1: Boot Pin Sampling** The CPU samples boot configuration pins (e.g., `BOOT0`, `BOOT1` on STM32) on the rising edge of reset. This determines the boot memory region: - Boot from **Flash** (most common for applications). - Boot from **System Memory** (built-in bootloader for UART/USB programming). - Boot from **SRAM** (used for custom bootloaders). **Step 2: Initial Main Stack Pointer (MSP) Load** The CPU performs a 32-bit read from address `0x00000000` (the first word of the vector table) and loads the value directly into **MSP**. This word **is not an instruction**; it is a data value that defines the top of the system stack. **Step 3: Reset Vector Fetch** The CPU reads the next 32-bit word at `0x00000004` and loads it into **PC**. This is the address of the **Reset_Handler** function — the first executable instruction of the application. **Step 4: Reset_Handler Execution** The Reset_Handler is typically written in assembly in the startup file. It performs: 1. **Copy of `.data` section** from Flash to SRAM (initialized globals). 2. **Zero-fill of `.bss` section** in SRAM (uninitialized globals). 3. **Call to C runtime** `__libc_init_array()` for C++ static constructors and C99 `__attribute__((constructor))` functions. 4. **Call to `main()`** — control transfers to application code in Thread mode. **Step 5: Main Application Runs** After `main()` is called, the CPU executes in **Thread mode** using the stack selected by `CONTROL[0]` (MSP by default). Exceptions and interrupts can now preempt this code. **Role of the Initial MSP Value:** The first word of the vector table is the **initial value of the Main Stack Pointer (MSP)**. Its role is: - **Defines the top of the system stack** in SRAM. - **Sets up stack memory** before any function call or exception can occur. - **Ensures safe execution** — without a valid stack, the first function call would corrupt memory. - The value is **statically defined** in the linker script and reflects the end of the SRAM region (e.g., for a 128 KB SRAM, the MSP is loaded with `0x20020000`). > **[7 marks]** --- > **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:** What is **EXC_RETURN**? Explain how exception return works in Cortex-M3. List the valid EXC_RETURN values. **Model Solution:** **Definition:** **EXC_RETURN** is a special 32-bit value automatically loaded into the **Link Register (LR)** by the CPU when an exception is taken. It is *not* a normal return address — it is a **command to the CPU** that tells it to perform an exception return (unstacking) the next time PC is loaded with this value. **Format of EXC_RETURN:** $$ \text{EXC\_RETURN} = 0x\text{FFFFFFFF} - (0x00400000 \times \text{options}) $$ The key bits are: - **Bits [31:4] = `0xFFFFFFF`**: The magic prefix that the CPU recognizes. - **Bit [3] = `SPSEL`**: Selects stack pointer for return (0 = MSP, 1 = PSP). - **Bit [2] = reserved** (always 1). - **Bit [1:0] = mode** (reserved encoding: must be `01`). **Valid EXC_RETURN Values:** | Value | Mode to Return | Stack to Use | Description | |---|---|---|---| | `0xFFFFFFF1` | Handler | MSP | Return from exception to Handler mode using MSP | | `0xFFFFFFF9` | Thread | MSP | Return to Thread mode using MSP | | `0xFFFFFFFD` | Thread | PSP | Return to Thread mode using PSP | **Exception Return Mechanism:** When an ISR is written, the compiler generates code that ends with a special instruction sequence. For ARM Compiler (`__irq` functions) and GCC, this is typically a `POP {PC, ...}` instruction. When the POP loads the value `0xFFFFFFF9` (for example) into PC, the CPU recognizes it as EXC_RETURN and automatically: 1. Unstacks the 8 words from the active SP: xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0. 2. Updates the **IPSR** field of xPSR with the previous exception number. 3. Resumes execution at the **restored PC** (the instruction that was about to execute when the exception fired). 4. Returns to **Thread mode** (if the EXC_RETURN was `0xFFFFFFF9` or `0xFFFFFFFD`). **Why This Design?** - The handler function does not need to know it is an ISR — it just needs to ensure the EXC_RETURN value is in LR (the hardware already did that). - This enables **tail-chaining** — back-to-back exceptions skip the unstack/stack overhead. - The Cortex-M3/M4 NVIC handles the return automatically; the ISR source code is clean and portable. > **[7 marks]** --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic (and how to avoid it):** > > 1. **Confusing MSP word with a code address.** The first vector table entry is the **MSP value**, NOT a function pointer. Examiners explicitly test this. **Always say "initial stack pointer"** for offset `0x00`. > > 2. **Wrong stack frame size.** Students often write "16 bytes" or "12 bytes" for the exception stack frame. The correct, hardware-pushed frame is exactly **8 words × 4 bytes = 32 bytes**. > > 3. **Forgetting xPSR is part of the stack frame.** The pushed registers are **xPSR, PC, LR, R12, R3, R2, R1, R0** — exactly 8, in that hardware order. Writing "general-purpose registers" loses marks. > > 4. **Miscounting exception numbers.** NMI is **exception 2** (not 1). Reset is exception 1. SysTick is exception 15. The 16 system exceptions are at offsets `0x00` to `0x3C` (16 × 4 = 64 bytes). > > 5. **Ignoring VTOR alignment.** If asked about relocating the vector table, the new base must be aligned to the next power of 2 ≥ table size. Saying "any address" is wrong. > > 6. **Spelling "EXC_RETURN" wrongly** or treating it as a normal return address. Examiners want to see you understand it is a magic constant. > > 7. **Stating the CPU reads the vector table "from the program"** — no! It is read by **hardware** via the **Code bus** during exception entry. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Vector table** = array of 32-bit words at memory start; first entry is **MSP value**, rest are **handler addresses**. - **Vector size** = 4 bytes per entry; address = `base + 4 × exception_number`. - **Cortex-M3/M4** uses **Thumb-2** instruction set; **32-bit ALU and registers**. - **Register file** = R0–R12 (GPR) + R13 (SP) + R14 (LR) + R15 (PC) + xPSR + special registers. - **SP is banked**: MSP (default) and PSP (RTOS task stacks). - **LR holds EXC_RETURN** on exception entry: `0xFFFFFFF1` (Handler/MSP), `0xFFFFFFF9` (Thread/MSP), `0xFFFFFFFD` (Thread/PSP). - **Stack frame on exception** = 8 words (32 bytes): R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR. - **Stack direction** = **Full Descending** (SP decrements on push). - **Reset sequence**: boot pins → read MSP from `0x00` → read PC from `0x04` → execute Reset_Handler. - **Reset_Handler** in startup file does: copy `.data`, zero `.bss`, init C runtime, call `main()`. - **Tail-chaining** saves 12 cycles per back-to-back exception transition. - **VTOR** = `SCB->VTOR` register; relocates vector table; must be **2ⁿ aligned**. - **System exceptions** = 16 (Reset, NMI, HardFault, MemManage, BusFault, UsageFault, 4 reserved, SVCall, DebugMonitor, 1 reserved, PendSV, SysTick). - **Priorities** are **lower number = higher urgency** (Reset = -3, NMI = -2, HardFault = -1, others configurable from 0 to 255). - **Exception entry** uses **Handler mode + MSP** (always privileged). - **Bit-band regions** in Cortex-M3/M4: SRAM `0x20000000`–`0x200FFFFF` and peripherals `0x40000000`–`0x400FFFFF`. - **JTAG / SWD** debug pins are used by the debugger to halt the CPU and inspect core registers. - **Vector table relocation** is **mandatory** for bootloaders that jump to a secondary application. - **HardFault_Handler** is the **default catch-all** — it should log the stacked PC, LR, and CFSR for crash analysis. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute KTU Revision Mantra:** > *"MSP first, then PC, then 32-byte push, then EXC_RETURN."* If you can recite this single line cold, you can answer 80% of Module 4 questions on CPU and Vector Table.

topics.title: C Run-Time Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT525 - ARM ARCHITECTURE, PROGRAMMING AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** C Run-Time Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **C Run-Time Environment (RTE)** refers to the complete set of software and hardware resources, memory regions, registers, and conventions that govern how a C program is translated, loaded, linked, and executed on a target processor. For ARM-based systems, the C RTE is standardized by the **AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard)** and the **ABI (Application Binary Interface)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the C Run-Time Environment encompasses:

1. **C Program Building Process** — Preprocessing, Compilation, Assembly, Linking, and Loading.
2. **Memory Organization** — Text, Data, BSS, Heap, and Stack segments.
3. **ARM AAPCS Calling Convention** — Register usage rules (R0–R15), stack frame construction, and argument passing.
4. **Position-Independent Code (PIC)** and **Scatter Loading** for ARM targets.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blueprint Workshop"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of writing a C program like designing a building:**

| Stage in C RTE | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Preprocessor** | An editor who removes draft comments and merges referenced material |
| **Compiler** | The architect who converts the design (C) into a structural plan (Assembly) |
| **Assembler** | The foreman who translates the plan into specific building instructions (machine code) |
| **Linker** | The project manager who combines all building modules and resolves dependencies |
| **Loader** | The construction crew that places the building on its foundation (memory) |
| **Stack** | A scratchpad the builder uses for temporary measurements during construction |
| **Heap** | A storage yard where extra materials are kept when more space is needed |

## 1.3 ARM-Specific Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key ARM AAPCS Register Convention (ARM 32-bit):**
> - **R0–R3**: Argument/Scratch registers (caller-saved) — `r0` also returns the result.
> - **R4–R11**: Variable registers (callee-saved) — must be preserved across function calls.
> - **R12 (IP)**: Intra-procedure-call scratch register.
> - **R13 (SP)**: Stack Pointer — **MUST** be 8-byte aligned.
> - **R14 (LR)**: Link Register — holds return address.
> - **R15 (PC)**: Program Counter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage C Program Build Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every C program undergoes **5 distinct translation stages** before execution on ARM hardware.

### Stage 1: Preprocessing
- **Input:** `source.c` (raw C code)
- **Output:** `source.i` (pure C with macros expanded)
- **Tasks:** Expands `#include`, `#define`, conditional compilation (`#ifdef`), and removes comments.
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-cpp` (GNU ARM toolchain).

### Stage 2: Compilation
- **Input:** `source.i`
- **Output:** `source.s` (ARM Assembly code)
- **Tasks:** Performs lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, optimization, and code generation targeting the ARM ISA.
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-gcc -S`.

### Stage 3: Assembly
- **Input:** `source.s`
- **Output:** `source.o` (Relocatable Object File — ELF format)
- **Tasks:** Translates mnemonics to machine code, builds the symbol table and relocation table.
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-as`.

### Stage 4: Linking
- **Input:** Multiple `.o` files + libraries (`libc.a`, `libm.a`).
- **Output:** `program.elf` (Executable and Linkable Format — single executable).
- **Tasks:**
  - **Symbol Resolution** — matches undefined symbols to definitions.
  - **Relocation** — adjusts addresses to final layout.
  - **Section Merging** — combines `.text`, `.data`, `.bss` from all object files.
- **Tool:** `arm-none-eabi-ld`.

### Stage 5: Loading
- **Input:** `program.elf`
- **Output:** Program running in target memory.
- **Tasks:** Program loader copies text/rodata from Flash to RAM, zeroes BSS, sets up stack pointer, and jumps to `_start` (or `Reset_Handler` for bare-metal).

## 2.2 Process Memory Layout on ARM

> [!NOTE]
> A loaded C program occupies **5 distinct memory regions** in the ARM address space.

```
Low Address  (0x00000000)   ← Typical for Flash on Cortex-M
    +-----------------------+
    |       .text           |  ← Executable machine code (Flash, read-only)
    +-----------------------+
    |       .rodata         |  ← Read-only data: const, string literals
    +-----------------------+
    |       .data           |  ← Initialized global/static variables (RAM)
    +-----------------------+
    |       .bss            |  ← Uninitialized globals (zeroed at startup)
    +-----------------------+
    |        Heap           |  ← Dynamic memory (malloc/free), grows ↑
    |       (free)          |
    |        Stack          |  ← Local variables, return addresses, grows ↓
    +-----------------------+
High Address (0xFFFFFFFF)
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU Exam 2024 Scheme**

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Description | Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stack Pointer Alignment | $\text{SP} \equiv 0 \pmod 8$ | AAPCS requires **8-byte alignment** at public interfaces | Bytes |
| Frame Pointer | $\text{FP} = \text{R11}$ | Points to old frame pointer (saved on stack) | Address |
| Return Address | $\text{LR} = \text{PC} + 4$ | Stores next instruction address on `BL` | Bytes offset |
| Stack Growth Direction | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - N$ | Full Descending (FD) on ARM | Bytes |
| Link Register Offset | $\text{LR} = \text{PC} + 4$ | Pipeline effect in ARM (1 instruction fetched ahead) | Bytes |
| BSS Zeroing Cost | $T_{\text{bss}} = \frac{\vert \text{bss\_size} \vert}{f_{\text{CPU}}} \cdot k$ | Time to zero BSS depends on clock frequency $f_{\text{CPU}}$ | Seconds |
| Heap Fragmentation | $F = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i^{\text{used}}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i^{\text{alloc}}}$ | Ratio of unusable heap to allocated heap | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| Code Size Estimate | $S_{\text{text}} \approx 1.1 \times S_{\text{assembly}}$ | Compiled code is ~10% larger than hand-written assembly | Bytes |
| Function Call Overhead | $C_{\text{call}} = T_{\text{BL}} + T_{\text{push}} + T_{\text{pop}} + T_{\text{ret}}$ | Cost of procedure call on ARM | Clock cycles |
| AAPCS Scratch Regs | $N_{\text{scratch}} = 4$ | R0–R3 are scratch (caller-saved) | Count |
| AAPCS Preserved Regs | $N_{\text{preserved}} = 8$ | R4–R11 are preserved (callee-saved) | Count |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The C Run-Time Environment knowledge is **critical** in:
- **Embedded Firmware Development** (bare-metal ARM Cortex-M): Manually placing ISR vectors, scatter-loading files.
- **RTOS Design** (FreeRTOS, Keil RTX): Stack overflow detection, context switching relies on AAPCS.
- **Compiler Optimization**: Understanding calling convention allows inline assembly and register allocation analysis.
- **Security Engineering**: Buffer-overflow attacks exploit stack frame layout knowledge.
- **System Programming**: Bootloaders initialize BSS, copy `.data` from Flash to RAM.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stack Frame Construction for an ARM Function Call

> [!NOTE]
> Consider the following C function:
> ```c
> int add(int a, int b) {
>     int result;
>     result = a + b;
>     return result;
> }
> ```
> When called from `main()`, ARM AAPCS dictates a specific stack frame and register usage.

### Step 1: Argument Passing via Registers

According to AAPCS, the first four 32-bit arguments are passed in **R0–R3**.

$$
\text{arg}_1 \rightarrow R_0, \quad \text{arg}_2 \rightarrow R_1, \quad \text{arg}_3 \rightarrow R_2, \quad \text{arg}_4 \rightarrow R_3
$$

For the call `add(5, 7)`:

$$
R_0 = 5, \quad R_1 = 7
$$

### Step 2: Branch with Link Instruction

The `BL` instruction saves the return address into LR and branches to the target.

$$
\text{LR} = \text{PC}_{\text{return}} = \text{PC}_{\text{after\_BL}} + 4
$$

The $+4$ accounts for ARM's 3-stage pipeline. The compiler emits:

```
BL  add
```

### Step 3: Stack Frame Setup (Function Prologue)

The callee `add` must preserve any caller-saved registers it uses. It pushes them onto the stack.

**Memory Transaction:**

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 8
$$

$$
\text{Memory}[\text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4] = \text{LR} \quad \text{(save return address)}
$$

$$
\text{Memory}[\text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 8] = \text{R11} \quad \text{(save frame pointer)}
$$

The corresponding ARM assembly is:

```
PUSH    {R11, LR}      ; Save frame pointer and link register
```

### Step 4: Frame Pointer Initialization

The frame pointer (FP = R11) is set to the current SP to mark the base of the frame.

$$
\text{R11} = \text{SP}_{\text{new}}
$$

```
MOV     R11, SP        ; Set frame pointer
```

### Step 5: Local Variable Allocation

The local variable `result` is allocated 4 bytes on the stack.

$$
\text{SP} = \text{SP} - 4
$$

```
SUB     SP, SP, #4     ; Allocate 4 bytes for 'result'
```

### Step 6: Execution of Function Body

The addition is performed and stored at the stack location.

$$
\text{Memory}[\text{SP}] = R_0 + R_1
$$

```
ADD     R0, R0, R1     ; result = a + b
STR     R0, [SP]       ; Store to stack
```

### Step 7: Result Return via R0

The result is returned in R0 (the standard return register).

$$
R_0^{\text{return}} = R_0 + R_1 = 5 + 7 = 12
$$

### Step 8: Function Epilogue and Return

Registers are restored and stack is deallocated.

```
ADD     SP, SP, #4     ; Deallocate 'result'
POP     {R11, PC}      ; Restore R11 and PC (from saved LR)
```

The `POP` into `PC` performs a **function return** (jumps to saved LR).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Memory Region Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive comparison of ARM C program segments**

| Segment | Contents | Writable? | Allocation Time | Size Determined By | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **`.text`** | Machine code, ISR vectors | No | Compile-time | Code complexity | `main()` compiled instructions |
| **`.rodata`** | `const` variables, string literals | No | Compile-time | Data values | `"Hello\r\n"`, `const int x = 5;` |
| **`.data`** | Initialized globals, `static` | Yes | Compile-time + Runtime | Initial values in source | `int g = 10;` |
| **`.bss`** | Uninitialized globals, `static` | Yes | Runtime (zeroed) | Type declarations | `int arr[100];` |
| **Heap** | `malloc`, `calloc`, `new` | Yes | Runtime (dynamic) | Program logic | `p = malloc(64);` |
| **Stack** | Locals, return addresses | Yes | Runtime (per call) | Function depth | `int temp;` in function |

## 3.3 Mathematical Analysis: Stack Memory Growth

For $N$ nested function calls with average local variable size $L$ bytes:

$$
\text{Stack}_{\text{depth}} = N \times (L + 8)
$$

Where $8$ bytes accounts for saved FP and LR per function.

For ARM, stack must satisfy:

$$
\text{SP} \bmod 8 = 0
$$

Therefore the actual frame size is rounded up to the next multiple of 8:

$$
\text{Frame}_{\text{aligned}} = 8 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{L + 8}{8} \right\rceil
$$

### Worked Example

For 3 nested function calls, each with $L = 12$ bytes of locals:

$$
L + 8 = 12 + 8 = 20
$$

$$
\text{Frame}_{\text{aligned}} = 8 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{20}{8} \right\rceil = 8 \cdot 3 = 24 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Total Stack} = 3 \times 24 = 72 \text{ bytes}
$$

## 3.4 Heap Allocation Time Complexity

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `malloc(n)` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ free-block | $O(n)$ scan |
| `free(p)` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ coalesce |
| `realloc` | $O(1)$ extend | $O(n)$ copy | $O(n)$ copy |

Where $n$ is the number of free blocks in the free list.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 C Program Build Pipeline (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[source.c] --> B[Preprocessor]
    B --> C[source.i]
    C --> D[Compiler]
    D --> E[source.s]
    E --> F[Assembler]
    F --> G[source.o]
    G --> H[Linker]
    I[libc.a] --> H
    J[libm.a] --> H
    H --> K[program.elf]
    K --> L[Loader]
    L --> M[ARM Memory]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style K fill:#ffe1e1
    style M fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.2 ARM AAPCS Register Roles (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0[R0 - Arg0 or Return Value]
    R1[R1 - Arg1]
    R2[R2 - Arg2]
    R3[R3 - Arg3]
    R4[R4 - Callee Saved]
    R5[R5 - Callee Saved]
    R6[R6 - Callee Saved]
    R7[R7 - Callee Saved]
    R8[R8 - Callee Saved]
    R9[R9 - Callee Saved]
    R10[R10 - Callee Saved]
    R11[R11 - Frame Pointer FP]
    R12[R12 - Intra-Procedure Scratch IP]
    R13[R13 - Stack Pointer SP]
    R14[R14 - Link Register LR]
    R15[R15 - Program Counter PC]

    R0 --- R1 --- R2 --- R3
    R3 --- R4 --- R5 --- R6
    R6 --- R7 --- R8 --- R9
    R9 --- R10 --- R11
    R11 --- R12 --- R13
    R13 --- R14 --- R15
```

## 4.3 Stack Frame Layout (Detailed Memory Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    direction TB
    A[Higher Address - Old Stack] --> B[Caller's Frame]
    B --> C[SP+0: Local Variable result]
    C --> D[SP+4: Saved R11 FP]
    D --> E[SP+8: Saved LR Return Address]
    E --> F[SP+12: Arguments 5+ on stack if more than 4]
    F --> G[Lower Address - Stack Grows Down]

    style A fill:#ffe1e1
    style G fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
```

## 4.4 ARM Bare-Metal Startup Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On Reset] --> B[Vector Table Read]
    B --> C[Reset_Handler Entry]
    C --> D[Copy .data from Flash to RAM]
    D --> E[Zero Initialize .bss in RAM]
    E --> F[Initialize SP to stack top]
    F --> G[Call static constructors C init]
    G --> H[Branch to main]
    H --> I[Execute User Application]
    I --> J{Return from main?}
    J --> K[Yes - Call exit handlers]
    J --> L[No - Continue execution]
    K --> M[Spin or Halt]

    style A fill:#ffe1e1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
    style M fill:#fff4e1
```

## 4.5 Heap vs Stack Allocation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Program Start] --> B[Static Regions Preallocated]
    B --> C[Stack - Automatic Variables]
    B --> D[Heap - Dynamic Variables]
    C --> E[Function Call Push]
    E --> F[Function Return Pop]
    D --> G[malloc Request]
    G --> H[Free Block Available?]
    H --> I[Yes - Allocate and Return Pointer]
    H --> J[No - SBRK Extend Heap]
    I --> K[Application Uses Memory]
    J --> K
    K --> L[free Call Releases Memory]
    L --> M[Block Returns to Free List]
```
